topics.title: Introduction to 8086 Microprocessor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT503 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to 8086 Microprocessor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Microprocessor?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **Microprocessor (µP/MPU)** is a single-chip, multipurpose, programmable, clock-driven, register-based, binary-compatible electronic device that reads binary instructions from an external memory, decodes them, and executes them by performing arithmetic, logical, and control operations on binary data fetched from memory or I/O devices, thereby functioning as the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** of a microcomputer system.

In simple words, a microprocessor is the **"brain"** of a computer system, manufactured on a single silicon chip using Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) technology.

### Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Post Office Sorting Room**
> - Think of a microprocessor as a **post office sorting clerk** who stands at a desk.
> - **Desk Drawers** = Registers (temporary storage of small notes).
> - **Incoming Mail Tray** = Input port (data coming in from outside).
> - **Outgoing Mail Tray** = Output port (results going out).
> - **Rule Book on the desk** = Memory (stored instructions telling the clerk what to do).
> - **The Bell (Ring = Clock Pulse)** = Clock signal — every time the bell rings, the clerk takes *one* step in his job.
> - The clerk cannot think on his own; he just **fetches** a rule, **decodes** it, and **executes** it. This is exactly the **Fetch–Decode–Execute (FDE) cycle** of a microprocessor.

---

## 1.2 Evolution / Generations of Microprocessors

The evolution of microprocessors is often classified by the **word size** (number of bits the ALU can process in one operation) and the technology used.

| Generation | Period | Word Size | Example Chips | Key Advancement |
|:----------:|:------:|:---------:|:-------------:|:----------------|
| **1st Gen** | 1971–1973 | **4-bit** | Intel 4004, 4040 | First commercial MPU; PMOS technology |
| **2nd Gen** | 1974–1977 | **8-bit** | Intel 8008, 8080, 8085; Motorola 6800 | NMOS; higher clock; assembly HLL emerging |
| **3rd Gen** | 1978–1985 | **16-bit** | **Intel 8086**, 8088; Motorola 68000 | HMOS; segmented memory; pipelined BIU |
| **4th Gen** | 1985–1995 | **32-bit** | Intel 80386, 80486; Motorola 68020 | Virtual memory; pipelined superscalar cores |
| **5th Gen** | 1995–Present | **64-bit** | Intel Pentium, Core, AMD Ryzen; ARM Cortex-A | Multi-core; out-of-order execution; IoT-optimized cores |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The 8086 belongs to the **3rd Generation** of microprocessors. It is a **16-bit** processor with a **16-bit Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** and a **20-bit address bus**, capable of accessing **2²⁰ = 1,048,576 bytes = 1 MB** of physical memory.

---

## 1.3 Salient Features of the 8086 Microprocessor

The Intel 8086, released in **June 1978**, introduced several architectural innovations that became the foundation of the entire **x86 family** still used in PCs today.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Features of 8086 (High-Yield for KTU):**
> 1. **16-bit Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** — operates on 16-bit data.
> 2. **16-bit Data Bus** — can transfer 16 bits in one bus cycle (8088 has 8-bit external data bus but 16-bit internal).
> 3. **20-bit Address Bus** — addresses up to **1,048,576 bytes (1 MB)** of physical memory.
> 4. **16-bit Registers** — General purpose and segment registers.
> 5. **Clock Frequency** — Originally **5 MHz, 8 MHz, and 10 MHz** variants; the 8086-2 version supported up to **8 MHz**.
> 6. **Pipelined Architecture** — uses separate **Bus Interface Unit (BIU)** and **Execution Unit (EU)** operating concurrently.
> 7. **Instruction Queue (Pre-fetch Buffer)** — a 6-byte FIFO queue allows pre-fetching of up to 6 instruction bytes.
> 8. **Memory Segmentation** — divides 1 MB memory into logical **64 KB × 16 segments**.
> 9. **Multiprocessing Support** — pins like `LOCK`, `RQ`/`GT` support co-processor (8087) and multiprocessor configurations.
> 10. **Addressing Modes** — supports **8 different addressing modes**, providing flexible data access.
> 11. **Instruction Set** — over **117 (≈ 135)** instructions, ranging from data transfer to string operations.
> 12. **Package** — **40-pin DIP (Dual In-line Package)** for the original 8086; the 8088 variant came in an 8-bit external version.

---

## 1.4 Comparison: 8085 vs 8086 (Frequently Asked in KTU)

| Parameter | Intel 8085 | Intel 8086 |
|:---------:|:----------:|:----------:|
| Data Bus | **8-bit** | **16-bit** |
| Address Bus | **16-bit** | **20-bit** |
| Memory | 2¹⁶ = **64 KB** | 2²⁰ = **1 MB** |
| ALU | 8-bit | 16-bit |
| Registers | 8-bit (B, C, D, E, H, L) + 16-bit (SP, PC) | 16-bit (AX, BX, CX, DX, SI, DI, BP, SP) + Segment registers |
| Clock | 3 MHz typical | 5–10 MHz |
| Pipeline | No (single bus unit) | Yes (BIU + EU) |
| Co-processor | None | Supports **8087** (Math) and **8089** (I/O) |
| Pins | 40 | 40 |
| Operating Modes | Single | **Minimum** and **Maximum** mode |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Unit Internal Architecture of 8086

The 8086 is functionally divided into **two independent processing units** that operate in parallel via a pipeline. This is the *most important* architectural concept for KTU.

### A. Bus Interface Unit (BIU) — The "Fetcher & Deliverer"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **BIU** is responsible for *all bus operations*: fetching instructions, reading operands from memory, and writing results back to memory or I/O. It handles *external* communication.

**Components of BIU:**
1. **Instruction Queue (6-byte FIFO buffer)** — stores pre-fetched instruction bytes.
2. **Instruction Pointer (IP)** — 16-bit register holding the **offset** of the next instruction within the current code segment. (Equivalent to Program Counter PC in 8085.)
3. **Segment Registers (CS, DS, SS, ES)** — four 16-bit registers holding the base (segment) address of the currently active code, data, stack, and extra segments.
4. **Address Generation Adder (Σ)** — a 20-bit adder that computes the **20-bit Physical Address** by combining a segment register and an offset using the formula:

$$PA \;=\; \text{Segment} \times 16 \;+\; \text{Offset} \;=\; \text{Segment} \ll 4 \;+\; \text{Offset}$$

5. **Bus Control Logic** — generates read/write control signals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** The BIU constantly *looks ahead* in memory and fills the queue, so when the EU needs the next instruction, it is already sitting in the queue. This is **pre-fetching**, the heart of 8086 pipelining.

---

### B. Execution Unit (EU) — The "Decoder & Worker"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **EU** is responsible for *decoding and executing* instructions. It operates *only* on data already inside the CPU and never touches the external bus.

**Components of EU:**
1. **Control Unit / Instruction Decoder** — translates the opcode into control signals.
2. **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** — 16-bit wide; performs +, −, AND, OR, XOR, shifts, rotates, etc.
3. **Flag Register (FR / PSW)** — 16-bit register with **9 active flags** (6 status + 3 control).
4. **General Purpose Registers** — AX, BX, CX, DX (each splittable into 8-bit H and L halves).
5. **Pointer and Index Registers** — SP, BP, SI, DI.
6. **Temporary Registers** — invisible to programmer; used internally by EU.

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Point:** EU has *no* direct connection to the external bus. All memory access is *delegated* to the BIU via an internal request — this is what enables simultaneous fetching (BIU) and execution (EU).

---

## 2.2 Register Organization of 8086

The 8086 has **fourteen 16-bit registers**, grouped as follows.

### A. General Purpose Registers (User-Accessible, 16-bit, 8-bit splittable)

| 16-bit | High Byte | Low Byte | Conventional Use |
|:------:|:---------:|:--------:|:-----------------|
| **AX** | AH | AL | **Accumulator** — primary for I/O, arithmetic, string ops |
| **BX** | BH | BL | **Base** — pointer to data memory (default base in addressing) |
| **CX** | CH | CL | **Counter** — count for loops, shifts, string repetition |
| **DX** | DH | DL | **Data / I/O port** — holds port address, high word of MUL/DIV |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each 16-bit register can be used as either **one 16-bit register** or **two separate 8-bit registers** (e.g., `MOV AL, 34h` and `MOV AH, 12h` independently). This dual capability was an 8086 design innovation for compatibility with 8-bit code.

### B. Pointer and Index Registers (16-bit, *cannot* be split)

| Register | Purpose |
|:--------:|:--------|
| **SP** | **Stack Pointer** — points to top of stack in current Stack Segment |
| **BP** | **Base Pointer** — points to base of data on stack (default SS) |
| **SI** | **Source Index** — source pointer in string operations |
| **DI** | **Destination Index** — destination pointer in string operations |

### C. Segment Registers (16-bit)

| Register | Default Base For |
|:--------:|:-----------------|
| **CS** | Code Segment — holds current code |
| **DS** | Data Segment — holds current data |
| **SS** | Stack Segment — holds the stack |
| **ES** | Extra Segment — alternate data / string destination |

### D. Instruction Pointer and Flag Register

- **IP (Instruction Pointer)** — 16-bit offset of next instruction within CS. **Programmer cannot access IP directly.**
- **FLAGS / PSW (16 bits, 9 active)** — see formula sheet below.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **MASTER FORMULA — Physical Address Generation**

The 20-bit physical address output on the address bus is computed by the BIU as:

$$PA = \text{Segment Register} \times 16 + \text{Effective Offset (EA)}$$

Equivalently, since `× 16` is a left shift by 4 bits, appending a hex `0`:

$$PA = (\text{Segment} \, \ll \, 4) + \text{Offset}$$

**Segment:Offset Representation:** `HHHH:OOOO` (4 hex digits + 4 hex digits = 20 bits).

---

### Memory Mapping Constraints

| Quantity | Formula | Value |
|:--------:|:--------|:------|
| Physical Address Space | $2^{20}$ bytes | **1,048,576 B = 1 MB** |
| Logical (Virtual) per Segment | $2^{16}$ bytes | **65,536 B = 64 KB** |
| Number of Segments | $2^{20} \div 2^{16}$ | **16 × possible overlapping segments** |
| Segment Boundary | Segment base is **always** at a 16-byte aligned address (lowest 4 bits = 0) | Divisible by 16 |
| Overlap Rule | Two segments overlap if their base addresses differ by < 64 KB | — |
| Maximum segments active at once | 4 (CS, DS, SS, ES) | — |

---

### Flag Register Layout (16 bits, 9 active)

| Bit | Flag | Type | Meaning When Set = 1 |
|:---:|:----:|:----:|:---------------------|
| 0 | **CF** (Carry) | Status | Carry/borrow out of MSB |
| 2 | **PF** (Parity) | Status | Low byte has even number of 1s |
| 4 | **AF** (Auxiliary Carry) | Status | Carry from bit 3 to bit 4 (BCD helper) |
| 6 | **ZF** (Zero) | Status | Result is zero |
| 7 | **SF** (Sign) | Status | MSB of result is 1 (negative in signed) |
| 8 | **TF** (Trap) | Control | Single-step for debugging |
| 9 | **IF** (Interrupt Enable) | Control | INTR pin enabled |
| 10 | **DF** (Direction) | Control | String ops go from high → low addr |
| 11 | **OF** (Overflow) | Status | Signed overflow occurred |

Bits 1, 3, 5, 12–15 are **reserved (= 0)**.

---

## 2.4 Why Memory Segmentation? — The Engineering Rationale

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Problem Segmentation Solves:**
> The 8086 has a **20-bit** physical address bus (to access 1 MB) but only **16-bit** registers (which can only hold 0–65,535). A single 16-bit register therefore *cannot* address 1 MB on its own.
>
> **The Solution:** Use **two 16-bit values** (a segment and an offset) to produce one 20-bit address via the formula above. The segment register provides the high-order 16 bits (after a 4-bit shift) and the offset fills the low-order 16 bits.

This is also why **physical addresses are conventionally written as `Segment:Offset`** (e.g., `F000:0005`).

### Real-World Utility of 8086 Architecture

- **Foundation of the IBM PC architecture (1981)** — the 8088 (8-bit external version of 8086) was the CPU of the original IBM PC.
- **Backward-compatible x86 family** — every modern Intel/AMD 64-bit CPU still executes the original 8086 instruction set in **real mode**.
- **Embedded education** — 8086 concepts (segmentation, BIU/EU, addressing) form the basis for teaching all later Intel processors (80186, 286, 386, 486, Pentium).
- **Industrial controllers** — 8086-based SBCs (Single Board Computers) are still used in legacy industrial systems and teaching labs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Physical Address Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** Segment register value = `1234h` and Offset value = `ABCDh`.  
> **Find:** The 20-bit Physical Address.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the formula:**

$$PA = (\text{Segment} \ll 4) + \text{Offset}$$

**Step 2 — Shift the segment left by 4 bits (multiply by 16).**  
Shifting a hex number left by 1 hex digit is equivalent to multiplying by 16:

$$1234h \ll 4 = 12340h$$

*Verification:* $0x1234 \times 16 = 0x12340$. ✓

**Step 3 — Add the offset to the shifted segment base:**

$$PA = 12340h + ABCDh$$

**Step 4 — Perform the hexadecimal addition column by column:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
12340h \\
+ \; ABCDh \\
\hline
\end{aligned}
$$

*Low digit:* $0 + D = D$  
*Next digit:* $4 + C = 10h \rightarrow$ write $0$, carry $1$  
*Next digit:* $3 + B + 1_{carry} = 3 + 11 + 1 = 15 = F$  
*Next digit:* $2 + A = C$  
*High digit:* $1 + 0 = 1$

**Result:**

$$PA = 1 \, CF0D h$$

**Step 5 — Convert to decimal for cross-check:**

$$1CF0Dh = (1 \times 16^4) + (C \times 16^3) + (F \times 16^2) + (0 \times 16^1) + (D \times 16^0)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
PA &= 65536 + 12(4096) + 15(256) + 0 + 13 \\
&= 65536 + 49152 + 3840 + 13 \\
&= 118{,}541_{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check:** Segment `1234h` and Offset `ABCDh`. Since offset is only 16 bits, it cannot exceed `FFFFh`. The physical address must lie in the range `[Segment*16, Segment*16 + FFFF] = [12340h, 12340h + FFFFh] = [12340h, 2233Fh]`. Our result `1CF0Dh` lies in this range. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Segment Boundary and 16-Byte Alignment

**Claim:** Every valid segment base address is a multiple of 16 (i.e., the lowest 4 bits of any physical segment base are 0).

**Proof:**

The segment base is computed as:

$$\text{Base} = \text{Segment Register} \times 16$$

In binary, multiplying by 16 = shifting left by 4 bits. Therefore the lowest 4 bits of the result are always **0000**, regardless of the segment register value.

**Consequence (KTU High-Yield):** A valid 20-bit segment base, when written in hexadecimal, **always ends in `0`**. For example, `F000`, `A000`, `12340`, `FFFF0` are all valid segment bases; `F001`, `A012` are **not**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Number of Segments in 1 MB Memory

**Claim:** There are exactly $2^{20} \div 2^{4} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536$ possible distinct segment bases, and any single 64 KB offset range can start at any of them.

**Proof:**

- Each segment is 64 KB = $2^{16}$ bytes.
- Total physical memory = $2^{20}$ bytes.
- A new segment can *begin* at any 16-byte aligned address.
- Number of such starting positions = $2^{20} \div 2^{4} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536$.

$$
N_{segments} \;=\; \frac{2^{20}}{2^{4}} \;=\; 2^{16} \;=\; 65{,}536
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** At any instant, only **4** of these 65,536 segments are *active* (CS, DS, SS, ES), but any one physical byte can be addressed by **overlapping segments**. For example, physical address `1234Ah` can be expressed as `1234:000A`, `1230:004A`, `1200:034A`, and so on — exactly $2^{12} = 4096$ valid segment:offset pairs.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Maximum Number of (Segment:Offset) Pairs Mapping to a Single Physical Address

**Claim:** A given 20-bit physical address $P$ can be generated by **4,096** different (Segment:Offset) pairs.

**Proof:**

We need $(S \ll 4) + O = P$ where $0 \le O \le FFFFh$.

For a fixed $P$, every value of $S$ such that $S \ll 4 \le P \le S \ll 4 + FFFF$ yields a valid offset. The condition on $S$ is:

$$S \ll 4 \le P \le (S \ll 4) + FFFF$$

Subtracting: $P - (S \ll 4) \le FFFF$, i.e., $S \ll 4 \ge P - FFFF$.

The number of integer multiples of 16 in the interval $[P - FFFF, P]$ is the count of valid segment bases:

$$
N \;=\; \frac{FFFF - 0}{10h} + 1 \;=\; \frac{65535}{16} + 1 \;=\; 4096 + 0
$$

Wait — recheck the interval width:

The interval of valid segment bases has width $FFFF$ (in hex) + 1 = $65536$ bytes; each base is 16-byte aligned, so the number of distinct bases is:

$$N \;=\; \frac{65536}{16} \;=\; 4096$$

For each such $S$, the offset $O = P - (S \ll 4)$ is unique. Hence exactly **4,096 = 2¹²** valid (S:O) pairs.

$$
\boxed{N_{\text{encodings}}(P) \;=\; 2^{12} \;=\; 4096}
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Calculating Stack Top (SS:SP) → Physical Address

**Step 1 — Suppose** `SS = 2000h` and `SP = 1456h`.  
**Step 2 — Compute the 20-bit physical address of the current stack top:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PA_{TOP} &= (SS \ll 4) + SP \\
&= 2000h \times 10h + 1456h \\
&= 20000h + 1456h \\
&= 21456h
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — After a `PUSH` operation:** SP is decremented by 2 (because stack grows downward) and the new top becomes `21454h`.

**Step 4 — After a `POP` operation:** Data at `21456h` is read, then SP is incremented by 2 back to `21456h`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always show the **20-bit** result with all 5 hex digits when computing physical addresses. Forgetting the carry (overflow into the 5th digit) is the **#1 most common error** in KTU 8086 address problems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 8086 Internal Block Diagram (BIU + EU Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BIU["BUS INTERFACE UNIT - BIU"]
        direction TB
        csreg["CS - Code Segment Reg 16 bit"]
        dsreg["DS - Data Segment Reg 16 bit"]
        ssreg["SS - Stack Segment Reg 16 bit"]
        esreg["ES - Extra Segment Reg 16 bit"]
        ipreg["IP - Instruction Pointer 16 bit"]
        adder["20 bit Physical Address Adder Sigma"]
        queue["6 byte Instruction Queue FIFO"]
        busctrl["Bus Control Logic"]
        seggrp["Segment Registers Group"]:::grp
        csreg --- seggrp
        dsreg --- seggrp
        ssreg --- seggrp
        esreg --- seggrp
        seggrp --> adder
        ipreg --> adder
        adder --> busctrl
        queue <--> busctrl
    end

    subgraph EU["EXECUTION UNIT - EU"]
        direction TB
        decoder["Instruction Decoder / Control Unit"]
        alu["16 bit Arithmetic Logic Unit"]
        flagsreg["FLAGS Register - 9 active"]
        gpr["GPR: AX, BX, CX, DX - 8/16 bit"]
        ptrreg["SP, BP, SI, DI - 16 bit"]
        tempreg["Temporary Registers - hidden"]
        decoder --> alu
        gpr --> alu
        ptrreg --> alu
        alu --> flagsreg
        tempreg --> alu
    end

    queue -- "opcode fetch via internal bus" --> decoder

    adder -- "20 bit PA on Address Bus" --> extbus["External Address Data and Control Bus"]
    busctrl -- "Read/Write Control Signals" --> extbus

    classDef grp fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:1px;
```

---

## 4.2 Physical Address Generation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["16 bit Segment Register S"] --> SH["Shift Left by 4 - multiply by 16"]
    SH --> B["20 bit Segment Base B = S x 16"]
    C["16 bit Effective Offset O"] --> ADD["20 bit Adder Sigma"]
    B --> ADD
    ADD --> D["20 bit Physical Address PA = B + O"]
    D --> E["Output on 20 bit Address Bus AD0 to AD19"]
    style A fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af
    style C fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534
    style D fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#854d0e
```

---

## 4.3 Memory Segmentation Model (1 MB = 4 Segments × 64 KB)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MEM["1 MB PHYSICAL MEMORY - 20 bit addressable"]
        direction LR
        subgraph CSSEG["CS Segment 64 KB - 00000h to 0FFFFh"]
            cs1["Code Bytes"]
        end
        subgraph DSSEG["DS Segment 64 KB - 10000h to 1FFFFh"]
            ds1["Data Variables"]
        end
        subgraph SSSEG["SS Segment 64 KB - 20000h to 2FFFFh"]
            ss1["Stack Frames"]
        end
        subgraph ESSEG["ES Segment 64 KB - 30000h to 3FFFFh"]
            es1["Extra / String Dest"]
        end
    end

    CSREG["CS:0000h to CS:FFFFh"] --> CSSEG
    DSREG["DS:0000h to DS:FFFFh"] --> DSSEG
    SSREG["SS:0000h to SS:FFFFh"] --> SSSEG
    ESREG["ES:0000h to ES:FFFFh"] --> ESSEG

    style MEM fill:#f5f5f4,stroke:#57534e
    style CSSEG fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af
    style DSSEG fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534
    style SSSEG fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d
    style ESSEG fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e
```

---

## 4.4 8086 Register Organization Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R["8086 - 14 x 16 bit Registers"]

    R --> G1["General Purpose - 16 bit splittable to 8 bit"]
    R --> G2["Pointer and Index - 16 bit only"]
    R --> G3["Segment Registers - 16 bit"]
    R --> G4["Special - IP and FLAGS"]

    G1 --> AX["AX = AH:AL - Accumulator"]
    G1 --> BX["BX = BH:BL - Base"]
    G1 --> CX["CX = CH:CL - Counter"]
    G1 --> DX["DX = DH:DL - Data and Port"]

    G2 --> SP["SP - Stack Pointer"]
    G2 --> BP["BP - Base Pointer"]
    G2 --> SI["SI - Source Index"]
    G2 --> DI["DI - Dest Index"]

    G3 --> CS["CS - Code Segment"]
    G3 --> DS["DS - Data Segment"]
    G3 --> SS["SS - Stack Segment"]
    G3 --> ES["ES - Extra Segment"]

    G4 --> IP["IP - Instruction Pointer"]
    G4 --> FR["FLAGS - 16 bit, 9 active"]

    style R fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px
    style G1 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af
    style G2 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534
    style G3 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e
    style G4 fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d
```

---

## 4.5 Default Segment:Offset Association Table (KTU High-Yield)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DEFAULT["DEFAULT SEGMENT for OFFSET TYPE"]
        D1["Instruction Fetch - CS:IP"]
        D2["Stack Operations - SS:SP"]
        D3["BP as Base - SS:BP"]
        D4["Data except string - DS:offset"]
        D5["Source String - DS:SI"]
        D6["Dest String - ES:DI"]
    end
    style DEFAULT fill:#fff7ed,stroke:#9a3412
```
