### 2.1.1 Data Transfer Instructions
These move data between registers, memory, and the stack **without modifying the data itself**.

- `MOV dest, src` — General-purpose copy.
- `PUSH src` / `POP dest` — Stack operations (SP is auto-decremented/incremented by 2).
- `XCHG op1, op2` — Swap contents.
- `IN` / `OUT` — I/O port communication (DX or immediate port address).
- `LEA reg, mem` — Load Effective Address (offset only, not data).
- `LDS` / `LES` — Load pointer to DS or ES.

### 2.1.2 Arithmetic Instructions
Operate on signed or unsigned numbers; modify the **FLAGS register**.

- `ADD`, `ADC` (Add with Carry), `SUB`, `SBB` (Subtract with Borrow).
- `CMP op1, op2` — Performs `op1 - op2` but **discards the result**, only sets flags.
- `MUL src` (unsigned 8/16-bit), `IMUL src` (signed).
- `DIV src` (unsigned), `IDIV src` (signed).
- `INC op`, `DEC op`, `NEG op` (two's complement negation).

### 2.1.3 Logical / Bit Manipulation Instructions
- `AND`, `OR`, `XOR`, `NOT` — Bitwise operations.
- `SHL/SHR` (Shift Left/Right **Logical**), `SAL/SAR` (Shift **Arithmetic**).
- `ROL/ROR/RCL/RCR` — Rotates.
- `TEST op1, op2` — Logical AND without storing result (sets ZF).

### 2.1.4 String Instructions
Operate on byte/word strings; auto-increment/decrement SI/DI.

- `MOVS`, `CMPS`, `SCAS`, `LODS`, `STOS`.
- All require a **prefix**: `REP`, `REPE`, `REPNE` (uses CX as counter).

### 2.1.5 Program Transfer (Control Flow) Instructions
- `JMP label` — Unconditional jump (near: ±32 KB, far: anywhere in 1 MB).
- **Conditional jumps**: `JE/JZ`, `JNE/JNZ`, `JG`, `JL`, `JGE`, `JLE`, `JA`, `JB`, etc. — Range: **-128 to +127 bytes** from IP.
- `CALL` / `RET` — Subroutine management.
- `LOOP label` — Decrements CX, jumps to label if CX ≠ 0.
- `INT n` — Software interrupt (e.g., `INT 21H` for DOS services).

### 2.1.6 Processor Control Instructions
- `STC`, `CLC`, `CMC` — Flag manipulation.
- `STD`, `CLD` — Direction flag (for string ops).
- `STI`, `CLI` — Interrupt enable/disable.
- `NOP` — No operation (1 byte, 3 clock cycles, often used for timing).
- `HLT` — Halt the processor.

## 2.2 Addressing Modes — The Heart of 8086 Programming

The 8086 supports **8 addressing modes**, determining how the operand's effective address is computed.

| # | Mode | Syntax Example | Effective Address (EA) Calculation |
|---|------|----------------|-------------------------------------|
| 1 | **Register** | `MOV AX, BX` | N/A (operand is in register) |
| 2 | **Immediate** | `MOV AX, 1234H` | Operand is part of instruction |
| 3 | **Direct** | `MOV AX, [2000H]` | EA = 2000H |
| 4 | **Register Indirect** | `MOV AX, [BX]` | EA = (BX) |
| 5 | **Based Relative** | `MOV AX, [BX+SI]` or `[BX+disp8]` | EA = (BX) + (SI) + disp |
| 6 | **Indexed Relative** | `MOV AX, [SI+disp8]` | EA = (SI) + disp |
| 7 | **Based Indexed** | `MOV AX, [BX+SI]` or `[BX+DI]` | EA = (BX) + (SI/DI) |
| 8 | **Based Indexed + Displacement** | `MOV AX, [BX+SI+disp16]` | EA = (BX) + (SI) + disp |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Default Segment Association:**
> - `[BX]` → Data Segment (DS)
> - `[BP]` → Stack Segment (SS)
> - `[SI]`, `[DI]` → Data Segment (DS)
> - `[BP+disp]` → Stack Segment (SS)
> 
> Override with segment prefix: `MOV AX, CS:[BX]`

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Range |
|---|---------|----------------|--------------|
| 1 | Physical Address | `PA = (Segment × 10H) + Offset` | 20-bit (00000H – FFFFFH) |
| 2 | Max Memory | `2²⁰ = 1,048,576 bytes = 1 MB` | Bytes |
| 3 | Segment Alignment | Segments always start on **16-byte (paragraph) boundary** | 16 bytes |
| 4 | Segment Range | 0000H – FFFFH (× 16) → 00000H – FFFFF0H | 20-bit |
| 5 | Near Jump Range | `-32768` to `+32767` bytes | 16-bit signed offset |
| 6 | Short Jump Range | `-128` to `+127` bytes | 8-bit signed offset |
| 7 | Conditional Jump Range | `-128` to `+127` bytes | 8-bit signed offset |
| 8 | Stack Pointer Behavior | `PUSH` decrements SP by 2; `POP` increments SP by 2 | Words |
| 9 | MUL 8-bit Result | Stored in **AH:AL** (16-bit) | 16-bit |
| 10 | MUL 16-bit Result | Stored in **DX:AX** (32-bit) | 32-bit |
| 11 | DIV 8-bit | `AX ÷ src = AL (quotient), AH (remainder)` | 8-bit ÷ 8-bit |
| 12 | DIV 16-bit | `DX:AX ÷ src = AX (quotient), DX (remainder)` | 16-bit ÷ 16-bit |
| 13 | Flags Affected by CMP | ZF, SF, OF, CF, AF, PF | 6 flags |
| 14 | Clock Cycles (typical) | `MOV = 2`, `ADD = 3`, `MUL = 70-77`, `DIV = 80-90` | T-states |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

8086 programming concepts form the foundation of:
- **Embedded System Design** (8051, ARM Cortex-M inherit the same register philosophy).
- **IoT Edge Devices** (sensor data acquisition, GPIO bit-manipulation).
- **Industrial Control Loops** (real-time interrupt-driven handlers).
- **x86 BIOS / Firmware** (every PC still boots through real-mode 8086 code).

The **segment:offset model** taught here directly explains **virtual memory paging** in modern OSes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Physical Address Calculation from Segment:Offset

### Problem Statement
Given: `DS = 2000H`, `SI = 0450H`. Find the physical address of the operand referenced by `MOV AX, [SI]`.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Identify the addressing mode.  
`[SI]` is **Register Indirect** mode. Default segment for SI is the **Data Segment (DS)**.

**Step 2:** Apply the physical address formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Physical Address} &= (\text{Segment} \times 10\text{H}) + \text{Offset} \\
&= (2000\text{H} \times 10\text{H}) + 0450\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the segment shift (multiply by 10H = 16 in decimal).

$$
\begin{aligned}
2000\text{H} \times 10\text{H} &= 20000\text{H} \\
&\quad \text{(Append a zero hex digit, equivalent to left shift by 4 bits)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Add the offset.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Physical Address} &= 20000\text{H} + 0450\text{H} \\
&= 20450\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Verify the range — `20450H` lies in `[20000H, 2FFFFH]`, which is inside segment `2000H` extended. ✅

> **Result:** The byte at physical address `20450H` is fetched and placed into `AL`, while `20451H` goes into `AH` (little-endian convention).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Instruction Size Calculation

### Problem Statement
Determine the total byte size of the following 8086 instruction:  
`MOV AX, [BX+SI+0100H]`

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Identify the opcode size.  
`MOV reg16, r/m16` uses opcode `8B` (1 byte).

**Step 2:** Identify the ModR/M byte structure.  
The `8B` instruction requires a **ModR/M** byte to specify operands:

$$
\text{ModR/M} = \underbrace{\text{Mod}}_{2\text{ bits}} \;\; \underbrace{\text{Reg}}_{3\text{ bits}} \;\; \underbrace{\text{R/M}}_{3\text{ bits}}
$$

- `Mod = 10` (16-bit displacement follows)
- `Reg = 000` (destination = AX)
- `R/M = 000` (BX + SI + disp16)

Combined: `ModR/M = 10 000 000 = 80H` (1 byte).

**Step 3:** The 16-bit displacement `0100H` follows (2 bytes).

**Step 4:** Sum the components.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Size} &= \text{Opcode} + \text{ModR/M} + \text{Displacement} \\
&= 1 + 1 + 2 = 4 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** Instruction occupies **4 bytes** in memory.

### Analytical Matrix — Common Instruction Sizes

| Instruction | Opcode Bytes | ModR/M | Displacement | Immediate | **Total Bytes** |
|-------------|--------------|--------|--------------|-----------|-----------------|
| `MOV AX, BX` | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | **2** |
| `MOV AX, [2000H]` | 1 | 1 | 2 | 0 | **4** |
| `MOV AX, [BX]` | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | **2** |
| `MOV AX, [BX+SI]` | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | **2** |
| `MOV AX, [BX+SI+0100H]` | 1 | 1 | 2 | 0 | **4** |
| `MOV AX, 1234H` | 1 | 1 | 0 | 2 | **3** |
| `ADD AL, 05H` | 2 | 0 | 0 | 1 | **3** |
| `MUL BX` | 2 | 1 | 0 | 0 | **2** |
| `NOP` | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | **1** |

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Execution Trace of an 8086 Assembly Program

### Program
```asm
MOV AX, 0005H
MOV BX, 0003H
ADD AX, BX
SUB AX, 0002H
HLT
```

### Trace Table

| Step | Instruction | AX (Before) | BX (Before) | Operation | AX (After) | BX (After) | Flags (ZF) |
|------|-------------|-------------|-------------|-----------|------------|------------|------------|
| 1 | `MOV AX, 0005H` | XXXX | 0003 | AX ← 5 | 0005 | 0003 | — |
| 2 | `MOV BX, 0003H` | 0005 | 0003 | BX ← 3 | 0005 | 0003 | — |
| 3 | `ADD AX, BX` | 0005 | 0003 | AX ← AX + BX | 0008 | 0003 | 0 (NZ) |
| 4 | `SUB AX, 0002H` | 0008 | 0003 | AX ← AX − 2 | 0006 | 0003 | 0 (NZ) |
| 5 | `HLT` | 0006 | 0003 | Halt | 0006 | 0003 | 0 |

### Mathematical Verification of Step 3

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AX}_{\text{new}} &= \text{AX}_{\text{old}} + \text{BX} \\
&= 0005\text{H} + 0003\text{H} \\
&= 0008\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Mathematical Verification of Step 4

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AX}_{\text{new}} &= \text{AX}_{\text{old}} - 0002\text{H} \\
&= 0008\text{H} - 0002\text{H} \\
&= 0006\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Result:** AX = `0006H`, BX = `0003H`, Zero Flag = 0 (not zero).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Conditional Jump Decision Logic

### Problem Statement
Will the jump execute in the following snippet?
```asm
MOV AL, 0F0H
CMP AL, 0F0H
JE  EQUAL
INC BL
EQUAL: HLT
```

### Step-by-Step Evaluation

**Step 1:** Execute `CMP AL, 0F0H`. Internally, ALU computes `AL - 0F0H = 0F0H - 0F0H = 00H`.

**Step 2:** Observe flag effect: Result is zero → **ZF = 1**.

**Step 3:** Decode `JE` (Jump if Equal) — jump if `ZF = 1`.

**Step 4:** Decision: **ZF = 1 → JUMP TAKEN** → IP loaded with address of `EQUAL:` label.

**Step 5:** Control reaches `HLT` directly, skipping `INC BL`. Therefore, **BL is not incremented**.

> **Result:** The jump executes, and `BL` retains its original value.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — 8086 Programming Model (Register Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef gen fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#0D47A1
    classDef seg fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#BF360C
    classDef ptr fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,color:#311B92
    classDef idx fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#1B5E20
    classDef spc fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F,color:#880E4F

    genA["AX AH AL"]:::gen
    genB["BX BH BL"]:::gen
    genC["CX CH CL"]:::gen
    genD["DX DH DL"]:::gen

    seg1["CS Code Segment"]:::seg
    seg2["DS Data Segment"]:::seg
    seg3["SS Stack Segment"]:::seg
    seg4["ES Extra Segment"]:::seg

    ptr1["SP Stack Pointer"]:::ptr
    ptr2["BP Base Pointer"]:::ptr

    idx1["SI Source Index"]:::idx
    idx2["DI Destination Index"]:::idx

    spc1["IP Instruction Pointer"]:::spc
    spc2["FLAGS Register"]:::spc

    genA --- genB --- genC --- genD
    seg1 --- seg2 --- seg3 --- seg4
    ptr1 --- ptr2
    idx1 --- idx2
    spc1 --- spc2
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid — Instruction Execution Flow in 8086

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Power ON / Reset]) --> fetch1[Fetch Opcode from CS:IP]
    fetch1 --> decode[Decode Instruction]
    decode --> branch{Instruction Type?}
    branch -->|Data Transfer| dt[Execute MOV PUSH POP XCHG]
    branch -->|Arithmetic| ar[Execute ADD SUB MUL DIV CMP]
    branch -->|Logical| lg[Execute AND OR XOR SHL SHR]
    branch -->|Control Flow| cf{Jump/CALL?}
    cf -->|YES| updateIP[Modify IP from displacement]
    cf -->|NO| updateIP2[Increment IP normally]
    branch -->|Processor Control| pc[Execute STC CLC HLT NOP]
    dt --> flags[Update FLAGS if needed]
    ar --> flags
    lg --> flags
    pc --> flags
    updateIP --> flags
    updateIP2 --> flags
    flags --> halt{HLT encountered?}
    halt -->|YES| stop([Processor Halted])
    halt -->|NO| fetch1
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid — Memory Segmentation Model

```mermaid
graph LR
    classDef seg fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1
    classDef off fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100
    classDef phy fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20

    segA["Segment Register: 2000H"]:::seg
    mul["x 10H = 20000H"]:::off
    off1["Offset: 0450H"]:::off
    add((ADD)):::off
    phys["Physical Address: 20450H"]:::phy

    segA --> mul
    mul --> add
    off1 --> add
    add --> phys
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid — Address Calculation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inst[Assembly Instruction with Memory Operand] --> parse[Parser Identifies Addressing Mode]
    parse --> mode1{Addressing Mode?}
    mode1 -->|Direct| ea1[EA = Displacement]
    mode1 -->|Register Indirect| ea2[EA = BX or SI or DI or BP]
    mode1 -->|Based| ea3[EA = BX or BP plus disp]
    mode1 -->|Indexed| ea4[EA = SI or DI plus disp]
    mode1 -->|Based Indexed| ea5[EA = BX or BP plus SI or DI plus disp]
    ea1 --> seg[Determine Default Segment]
    ea2 --> seg
    ea3 --> seg
    ea4 --> seg
    ea5 --> seg
    seg --> phys[Compute PA = Segment x 10H plus EA]
    phys --> fetch[Fetch Operand from Physical Address]
    fetch --> exec[Execute Instruction]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**List any six addressing modes of 8086 with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

The 8086 supports **8 addressing modes**:

1. **Register**: `MOV AX, BX` — Operand is in a register.
2. **Immediate**: `MOV CX, 0AH` — Operand is constant within the instruction.
3. **Direct**: `MOV AX, [2000H]` — Effective address is given directly.
4. **Register Indirect**: `MOV AX, [SI]` — Address is in SI.
5. **Based Relative**: `MOV AX, [BX+04H]` — Address = BX + displacement.
6. **Indexed Relative**: `MOV AX, [DI+10H]` — Address = DI + displacement.
7. **Based Indexed**: `MOV AX, [BX+SI]` — Address = BX + SI.
8. **Based Indexed with Displacement**: `MOV AX, [BX+DI+0200H]` — Address = BX + DI + 0200H.

**(3 marks: 0.5 per correct mode — any 6)**

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Explain the function of the following 8086 instructions: (a) LEA (b) XCHG (c) CMP.**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) LEA (Load Effective Address):**  
Loads the **offset (effective address)** of a memory operand into a 16-bit register, **without fetching the data** from memory.  
*Example:* `LEA BX, [SI+100H]` → BX = SI + 100H (the offset, not the memory content).

**(b) XCHG (Exchange):**  
Swaps the contents of the source and destination operands. Both operands must be the same size (byte/word), and a segment register cannot be one of them.  
*Example:* `XCHG AX, BX` → After: AX = old BX, BX = old AX.

**(c) CMP (Compare):**  
Performs `destination − source` internally, **discards the result**, but **sets the FLAGS** (ZF, CF, SF, OF, AF, PF) to reflect the comparison outcome. Used **before conditional jumps**.  
*Example:* `CMP AL, BL` → AL − BL performed, flags updated, AL/BL unchanged.

**(3 marks: 1 per correct explanation)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not forget the 10H shift** in physical address calculation. A very common error is `PA = Segment + Offset`. Always remember the shift-left-by-4-bits rule.
> - For `MUL` and `DIV`, **state where the result is stored** (AX/DX:AX) — students often lose 1–2 marks by omitting this.
> - In trace tables, **explicitly show the flags** updated, not just register values.
> - For `LOOP`, remember it **decrements CX first**, then checks. Mis-ordering loses marks.
> - When writing programs, **end with HLT or INT 3** — examiners deduct marks for "infinite execution".

---

### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023 — Modified Model]**

**(a)** Explain the 8086 programming model with a neat diagram. List the function of each register group. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the following addressing modes: (i) Register Indirect (ii) Based Indexed (iii) Based Indexed with Displacement. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

The **8086 programming model** comprises **14 sixteen-bit registers** organized into 4 groups:

**1. General Purpose Registers (4 × 16-bit, splittable into 8-bit):**
- **AX (Accumulator):** Used in arithmetic (MUL, DIV), I/O (IN, OUT), and string operations.
- **BX (Base):** Holds the base address in addressing modes.
- **CX (Counter):** Used as counter in LOOP, REP, and shift/rotate instructions.
- **DX (Data):** Used in MUL/DIV (upper half of result) and I/O port addressing.

**2. Segment Registers (4 × 16-bit):**
- **CS (Code Segment):** Points to the segment containing the program code.
- **DS (Data Segment):** Points to the segment containing data.
- **SS (Stack Segment):** Points to the stack segment.
- **ES (Extra Segment):** Used as an additional data segment in string operations.

**3. Pointer and Index Registers (4 × 16-bit):**
- **SP (Stack Pointer):** Points to the top of the stack.
- **BP (Base Pointer):** Used to access data within the stack frame.
- **SI (Source Index):** Source pointer in string operations.
- **DI (Destination Index):** Destination pointer in string operations.

**4. Special Registers (2 × 16-bit):**
- **IP (Instruction Pointer):** Holds the offset of the next instruction to fetch.
- **FLAGS Register:** Contains 9 active flags (6 status + 3 control).

**The 9 Flags:**

| Flag | Type | Purpose |
|------|------|---------|
| CF | Status | Carry flag |
| PF | Status | Parity flag |
| AF | Status | Auxiliary carry |
| ZF | Status | Zero flag |
| SF | Status | Sign flag |
| OF | Status | Overflow flag |
| TF | Control | Trap (single-step) |
| IF | Control | Interrupt enable |
| DF | Control | Direction (string ops) |

**Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram)*

**(7 marks: 2 for groups + 2 for individual register functions + 1 for diagram + 2 for flags table)**

#### Model Solution to (b):

**(i) Register Indirect Addressing:**  
The **Effective Address (EA) is the contents of a register** (BX, SI, DI, or BP). The register "indirectly" points to memory.

*Example:*
```asm
MOV AX, [SI]
```
- If `SI = 1000H` and `DS = 2000H`:
  - `PA = (2000H × 10H) + 1000H = 21000H`
  - `AX ← Word at address 21000H`

**(ii) Based Indexed Addressing:**  
EA is the **sum of a base register (BX or BP) and an index register (SI or DI)**.

*Example:*
```asm
MOV AX, [BX+SI]
```
- If `BX = 1000H`, `SI = 0500H`:
  - `EA = 1000H + 0500H = 1500H`
  - With `DS = 2000H`: `PA = 21500H`

**(iii) Based Indexed with Displacement:**  
EA = **base register + index register + 8-bit or 16-bit signed displacement**.

*Example:*
```asm
MOV AX, [BX+DI+0200H]
```
- If `BX = 0500H`, `DI = 0300H`:
  - `EA = 0500H + 0300H + 0200H = 0A00H`
  - With `DS = 4000H`: `PA = 40A00H`

This mode is **heavily used for array and structure access** in C compilers translated to assembly.

**(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 2 for each mode explained + 1 for numeric example)**

---

### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 — Modified Model]**

**(a)** Write an 8086 assembly language program to add two 16-bit numbers stored at memory locations `2000H:1000H` and `2000H:1002H`. Store the result at `2000H:1004H`. Show the trace. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the functions of the following 8086 instructions with examples: (i) PUSH and POP (ii) MUL and DIV (iii) AND, OR, XOR. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Program:**
```asm
ASSUME CS:CODE
CODE SEGMENT
START:  MOV AX, 2000H          ; Initialize DS
        MOV DS, AX

        MOV SI, 1000H          ; Point SI to first number
        MOV DI, 1002H          ; Point DI to second number

        MOV AX, [SI]           ; Load first 16-bit number
        MOV BX, [DI]           ; Load second 16-bit number

        ADD AX, BX             ; AX = AX + BX

        MOV [1004H], AX        ; Store result

        HLT                    ; Halt processor
CODE ENDS
END START
```

**Trace Table:**

| Step | Instruction | AX | BX | SI | Memory [1000H] | Memory [1002H] | Memory [1004H] |
|------|-------------|-----|-----|-----|----------------|----------------|----------------|
| Initial | — | XXXX | XXXX | 1000H | 1234H | 00A5H | XXXX |
| 1 | `MOV AX, 2000H` | 2000H | XXXX | 1000H | 1234H | 00A5H | XXXX |
| 2 | `MOV DS, AX` | 2000H | XXXX | 1000H | 1234H | 00A5H | XXXX |
| 3 | `MOV SI, 1000H` | 2000H | XXXX | 1000H | 1234H | 00A5H | XXXX |
| 4 | `MOV DI, 1002H` | 2000H | XXXX | 1002H | 1234H | 00A5H | XXXX |
| 5 | `MOV AX, [SI]` | 1234H | XXXX | 1002H | 1234H | 00A5H | XXXX |
| 6 | `MOV BX, [DI]` | 1234H | 00A5H | 1002H | 1234H | 00A5H | XXXX |
| 7 | `ADD AX, BX` | 12D9H | 00A5H | 1002H | 1234H | 00A5H | XXXX |
| 8 | `MOV [1004H], AX` | 12D9H | 00A5H | 1002H | 1234H | 00A5H | 12D9H |
| 9 | `HLT` | 12D9H | 00A5H | 1002H | 1234H | 00A5H | 12D9H |

**Mathematical Verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Result} &= 1234\text{H} + 00\text{A}5\text{H} \\
&= (1 \times 16^3 + 2 \times 16^2 + 3 \times 16 + 4) + (0 \times 16^3 + 0 \times 16^2 + 10 \times 16 + 5) \\
&= 4660 + 165 \\
&= 4825 \\
&= 12\text{D}9\text{H} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**(7 marks: 2 program + 3 trace + 2 verification)**

#### Model Solution to (b):

**(i) PUSH and POP — Stack Operations:**

`PUSH src`:  
- Decrements SP by 2.  
- Stores the 16-bit value of `src` at `SS:SP`.  
- *Example:* `PUSH AX` — Stores AX at `SS:[SP-2]`, SP ← SP − 2.

`POP dest`:  
- Loads a 16-bit value from `SS:SP` into `dest`.  
- Increments SP by 2.  
- *Example:* `POP BX` — BX ← Word at `SS:SP`, SP ← SP + 2.

**Use:** Subroutine return addresses, register preservation, local variables.

**(ii) MUL and DIV — Multiplication/Division:**

`MUL src` (unsigned):  
- 8-bit: `AX = AL × src` (result in AH:AL).  
- 16-bit: `DX:AX = AX × src` (32-bit result).  
- *Example:* `MUL BL` → AX = AL × BL.

`DIV src` (unsigned):  
- 8-bit: `AL = AX / src` (quotient), `AH = AX mod src` (remainder).  
- 16-bit: `AX = DX:AX / src` (quotient), `DX = DX:AX mod src`.  
- *Example:* `DIV BL` → AL = AX/BL, AH = AX%BL.

> [!WARNING]
> Division by **zero** causes a Type 0 interrupt (Divide Error) — a common pitfall.

**(iii) AND, OR, XOR — Logical Bitwise Operations:**

`AND dest, src`: Bitwise AND. Used to **mask bits** (clear specific bits).  
- *Example:* `AND AL, 0FH` → Keeps only lower 4 bits of AL (masking upper 4).

`OR dest, src`: Bitwise OR. Used to **set specific bits**.  
- *Example:* `OR AL, 80H` → Sets the MSB of AL.

`XOR dest, src`: Bitwise XOR. Used to **toggle bits** or **clear a register** (e.g., `XOR AX, AX` sets AX = 0 — faster than `MOV AX, 0`).

**(7 marks: 2 + 3 + 2 for the three groups)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **8086 has 14 registers** — 4 GP, 4 Segment, 4 Pointer/Index, 2 Special (IP + FLAGS).
- ✅ **Physical Address Formula:** `PA = (Segment × 10H) + Offset` — always 20 bits, max **1 MB** memory.
- ✅ **Segments always start on a 16-byte (paragraph) boundary** — low nibble of physical address = low nibble of offset.
- ✅ **Default segment:** DS for [BX], [SI], [DI]; SS for [BP].
- ✅ **Instruction sizes range from 1 to 6 bytes** — NOP is 1 byte; longest instructions use 16-bit displacement + 16-bit immediate.
- ✅ **Conditional jumps** are always **SHORT** (range −128 to +127).
- ✅ **LOOP** uses **CX as counter** and decrements **before** checking.
- ✅ **MUL result** is 2× the operand size (8→16, 16→32).
- ✅ **DIV inputs** the 2× size dividend (AX for 8-bit, DX:AX for 16-bit).
- ✅ **PUSH** decrements SP; **POP** increments SP — both by 2.
- ✅ **CMP** does `dest − src`, **discards result**, sets flags.
- ✅ **LEA** loads the offset (not the data) — common confusion with `MOV`.
- ✅ **Mnemonic colors:** `MOV = blue (safe)`, `CMP = yellow (silent)`, `MUL = red (wide result)`.
- ✅ **String instructions** require `REP`/`REPE`/`REPNE` prefix and use **CX** as counter.
- ✅ **XOR reg, reg = fastest way to clear** a register.
- ✅ **TEST** is to AND as CMP is to SUB (computes but discards).
- ✅ **Flags register has 9 active bits** — 6 status (CF, PF, AF, ZF, SF, OF) + 3 control (TF, IF, DF).

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** For any 8086 program, always include the **ASSUME directive, SEGMENT/ENDS, END label**, and a **HLT** at the end. Examiners award 1 mark for "well-structured program" as per KTU 2024 scheme rubrics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Features of 8051 Microcontroller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT503 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Features of 8051 Microcontroller

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Intel 8051 microcontroller** is an **8-bit**, single-chip, Harvard-architecture-based CMOS microcontroller originally introduced by Intel Corporation in **1980**. It integrates a CPU, RAM, ROM, I/O ports, timers/counters, a serial communication port, and an interrupt controller on a single silicon die, making it a true "System-on-Chip" (SoC) for its era. The 8051 family became the de-facto industry standard for embedded control and remains pedagogically foundational for modern ARM-based microcontrollers used in IoT.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The 8051 is examined under the broad heading *"Features of 8051 Microcontroller"*. The Board expects students to be fluent with: **architecture, register set, pin configuration, memory organization, addressing modes, instruction set, I/O ports, timers, interrupts, and serial communication.**

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

Imagine you are building a fully-automatic washing machine. You need:
- A **brain** to make decisions (CPU)
- A small **notebook** to remember the current wash cycle (RAM)
- A **recipe book** permanently stored with washing programs (ROM/Flash)
- **Buttons and display** to interact with the user (I/O ports)
- A **clock** to time the wash cycles (Timers)
- A **buzzer** to alert when wash completes (Interrupts)
- A way to call a **service technician** for diagnostics (Serial Port)

The **8051** is exactly this — *a tiny computer with all these "organs" baked into one single chip*. Unlike a general-purpose desktop CPU (e.g., Intel Core i7) which needs external RAM, ROM, and peripherals to function, the 8051 is **self-sufficient**. You just add power, a crystal oscillator, and your application-specific sensors/actuators, and you have a complete embedded system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A *microprocessor* (like 8086) is **just the CPU**. A *microcontroller* (like 8051) is a **CPU + Memory + I/O + Peripherals**, all on one chip. This integration is what makes microcontrollers ideal for IoT edge nodes where size, power, and cost matter.

### 1.3 Why the 8051? (Historical & Engineering Relevance)

| Property | Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Bus Width** | **8 bits** | Matches most sensor/control signal widths |
| **Address Bus Width** | **16 bits** | Can address **64 KB** of code + **64 KB** of data |
| **Operating Frequency** | **12 MHz** typical (up to 40 MHz variants) | Balances speed and power consumption |
| **Instruction Cycle** | **12 clock periods** | Each machine cycle = 1 µs at 12 MHz |
| **Technology** | **CMOS** | Low power, suitable for battery IoT nodes |
| **Instruction Set** | **CISC-style**, 255 instructions | Rich instructions simplify firmware |
| **MIPS Rating @ 12 MHz** | **1 MIPS** | 1 million instructions per second |
| **On-chip RAM** | **128 bytes** (256 in 8052) | Registers, bit-addressable, stack |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Salient Features of the 8051 Microcontroller

The 8051 contains the following integrated functional blocks. KTU Board questions frequently ask students to "List the features of 8051" for 3 marks or to "Draw and explain the architecture" for 7+ marks.

1. **8-bit ALU** — Performs arithmetic (ADD, SUBB, MUL, DIV) and logic (ANL, ORL, XRL) operations.
2. **8-bit Accumulator (A)** — Primary working register for most ALU operations.
3. **8-bit B Register** — Used by MUL AB and DIV AB; serves as a general-purpose register otherwise.
4. **Program Counter (PC)** — **16-bit** register; always points to the next instruction to fetch.
5. **Stack Pointer (SP)** — **8-bit** register; points to the top of the on-chip RAM stack. **Resets to 0x07**, so the first PUSH goes to 0x08.
6. **Data Pointer (DPTR)** — **16-bit** register (composed of DPH + DPL); used to access external data memory and look-up tables in program memory.
7. **Program Status Word (PSW)** — **8-bit** flag register containing Carry (CY), Auxiliary Carry (AC), User Flag (F0), Register Bank Select bits (RS1, RS0), Overflow (OV), and Parity (P).
8. **Four 8-bit I/O Ports** — P0, P1, P2, P3. **32 I/O lines** total. P3 has alternate functions (RXD, TXD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD).
9. **Two 16-bit Timers/Counters** — Timer 0 and Timer 1. Can operate in four modes (Mode 0, 1, 2, 3).
10. **Full-Duplex UART (Serial Port)** — One asynchronous serial communication channel.
11. **Interrupt Controller** — **5 interrupt sources** (2 external + 2 timer + 1 serial). The 8052 has 6.
12. **On-chip ROM (Program Memory)** — **4 KB** (in original 8051). Variants exist: 8031 (no ROM), 8751 (UV-EPROM), 89C51 (Flash).
13. **On-chip RAM (Data Memory)** — **128 bytes** organized as **32 working registers** (4 banks × 8) + **16 bit-addressable locations** (0x20–0x2F) + **80 general-purpose bytes** (0x30–0x7F).
14. **Oscillator and Clock Circuit** — On-chip oscillator; needs only an external quartz crystal.
15. **Control Signals** — $\overline{EA}$, $\overline{PSEN}$, ALE, $\overline{WR}$, $\overline{RD}$ for memory interfacing.

### 2.2 KTU Formula / Metric Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Numerical Formulas for 8051**

| Parameter | Formula / Rule | Unit / Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Machine Cycle (MC)** | $\text{MC} = \dfrac{12}{\text{Oscillator Frequency}}$ | seconds |
| **Time per MC @ 12 MHz** | $T_{\text{MC}} = \dfrac{12}{12 \times 10^6}$ | $1 \,\mu\text{s}$ |
| **Time per MC @ 11.0592 MHz** | $T_{\text{MC}} = \dfrac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6}$ | $\approx 1.085 \,\mu\text{s}$ *(exact for baud rates)* |
| **Program Memory Range** | $0x0000$ to $0xFFFF$ | $2^{16} = \mathbf{64\,KB}$ |
| **External Data Memory Range** | $0x0000$ to $0xFFFF$ | $2^{16} = \mathbf{64\,KB}$ |
| **Internal RAM Range** | $0x00$ to $0x7F$ | $\mathbf{128\,bytes}$ (00–7F hex) |
| **Bit-Addressable RAM** | $0x20$ to $0x2F$ | $\mathbf{128\,bits}$ (16 bytes × 8) |
| **Register Banks** | 4 banks × 8 registers | $4 \times 8 = \mathbf{32\,bytes}$ (0x00–0x1F) |
| **SFR Range** | $0x80$ to $0xFF$ | $\mathbf{128\,bytes}$ (only 21–26 used) |
| **Timer Count Formula** | $N = 2^{n} - \text{Delay} \times \dfrac{F_{\text{osc}}}{12}$ | For Mode 1: $n=16$ |
| **Baud Rate (Mode 1, Timer 1)** | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \dfrac{F_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$ | bps |
| **Stack Operation** | $\text{SP}_{\text{after PUSH}} = \text{SP}_{\text{before}} + 1$ | byte-wise |
| **Reset Vector Address** | $0x0000$ | PC loads with 0x0000 |
| **Interrupt Vectors (8051)** | INT0=0x0003, T0=0x000B, INT1=0x0013, T1=0x001B, SIO=0x0023 | 8-byte spacing |
| **PSW Bit Layout** | `CY AC F0 RS1 RS0 OV - P` | bit7 to bit0 |
| **MUL AB / DIV AB** | Result of MUL AB: B:A = product | A = quotient, B = remainder for DIV |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Safety Note:** In all markdown tables, the absolute-value / divide notation uses `\vert` and `\times` to avoid breaking the pipe-based table syntax.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility in IoT

The 8051's deterministic instruction timing, low power, and rich on-chip peripherals make it a workhorse for **cost-sensitive IoT edge nodes**: smart energy meters, home automation controllers, sensor data loggers, RFID readers, industrial PLCs, and automotive dashboards. Modern derivatives (e.g., **CC2530, C8051Fxxx, Nuvoton N76E003**) integrate 8051 cores with **RF transceivers, ADCs, and PWM** blocks — directly enabling **Zigbee / Thread / BLE-based IoT** deployments where ARM Cortex-M would be overkill.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivative 1 — Calculating the Timer Reload Value for a Given Delay

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** An 8051 system uses an **11.0592 MHz** crystal. Configure **Timer 0 in Mode 1** to generate a delay of exactly **5 ms**. Compute the value to be loaded into **TH0** and **TL0**.

#### Step 1: Identify the operating mode
Mode 1 uses a **16-bit timer**, so the maximum count is $2^{16} = 65536$. When the timer overflows from `FFFFh` to `0000h`, the **TF0 flag is set**, signalling end of delay.

#### Step 2: Compute the machine-cycle frequency

$$f_{\text{MC}} = \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12} = 921.6 \times 10^{3}\,\text{Hz}$$

#### Step 3: Compute the machine-cycle period

$$T_{\text{MC}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{MC}}} = \frac{1}{921.6 \times 10^{3}} \approx 1.0851 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s} = 1.0851\,\mu\text{s}$$

#### Step 4: Determine the required number of timer counts

$$\text{Number of counts} = \frac{\text{Desired Delay}}{T_{\text{MC}}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0851 \times 10^{-6}}$$

Performing the division:

$$\text{Number of counts} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0851 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{5}{1.0851} \times 10^{3} \approx 4608 \text{ counts}$$

#### Step 5: Calculate the timer preload (reload) value

Since the timer counts **up from the preload value to 65536** and then overflows, the preload value is:

$$\text{Preload} = 65536 - \text{Number of counts} = 65536 - 4608 = 60928$$

#### Step 6: Convert the 16-bit preload into TH0 and TL0 values

Express 60928 in hexadecimal:

$$60928 \div 16 = 3808 \text{ remainder } 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 0$$
$$3808 \div 16 = 238 \text{ remainder } 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 0$$
$$238 \div 16 = 14 \text{ remainder } 14 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{E}$$
$$14 \div 16 = 0 \text{ remainder } 14 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{E}$$

Reading the remainders bottom-up: $\text{0xEE00}$.

So:

$$\text{TH0} = \text{0xEE} = 238_{10}, \quad \text{TL0} = \text{0x00} = 0_{10}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Final count reached = $0\text{xEE00} + 4608 = 60928 + 4608 = 65536 = 0\text{x10000}$, which overflows correctly to $0\text{x0000}$, setting **TF0 = 1** after exactly $5\,$ms.

---

### 3.2 Derivative 2 — Computing UART Baud Rate Reload Value

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** With the same **11.0592 MHz** crystal and **SMOD = 0**, configure the **8051 UART in Mode 1 (8-bit UART)** to transmit at **9600 bps**. Find the **TH1 reload value**.

#### Step 1: Recall the Mode 1 baud rate equation

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

#### Step 2: Substitute known values (SMOD = 0 ⇒ 2^0 = 1)

$$9600 = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$$

#### Step 3: Rearrange to isolate (256 − TH1)

$$256 - \text{TH1} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{32 \times 12 \times 9600}$$

#### Step 4: Evaluate the denominator

$$32 \times 12 \times 9600 = 3\,686\,400$$

#### Step 5: Compute the numerator / denominator

$$256 - \text{TH1} = \frac{11\,059\,200}{3\,686\,400} = 3$$

#### Step 6: Solve for TH1

$$\text{TH1} = 256 - 3 = 253 = 0\text{xFD}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is exactly why **11.0592 MHz** crystals are preferred for 8051 serial communication — it produces an **integer divisor**, eliminating cumulative baud-rate error. Using a 12 MHz crystal for 9600 baud gives a ~8.5% error, which breaks reliable communication.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table (Frequently asked in KTU)

| Feature | 8051 | 8052 | 8031 | 89C51 (Modern Flash) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ROM** | 4 KB | 8 KB | 0 KB (external) | 4 KB Flash |
| **RAM** | 128 B | 256 B | 128 B | 128 B |
| **Timers** | 2 | 3 | 2 | 2 |
| **Interrupts** | 5 | 6 | 5 | 5 |
| **I/O Pins** | 32 | 32 | 32 | 32 |
| **Use case** | Standard | Enhanced | External ROM | In-system programmable |
| **Manufacturers** | Intel, Atmel, Nuvoton, Philips, Siemens | | | |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — 8051 vs 8086 Microprocessor

| Parameter | 8051 Microcontroller | 8086 Microprocessor |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Microcontroller (CPU+Peripherals) | Microprocessor (CPU only) |
| **Word Size** | 8-bit (data) / 16-bit (address) | 16-bit (data) / 20-bit (address) |
| **Memory Address Space** | 64 KB code + 64 KB data | 1 MB combined |
| **On-chip RAM** | 128 bytes | None |
| **On-chip ROM** | 4 KB | None |
| **Timers/Counters** | 2 on-chip | None on-chip |
| **Serial Port** | On-chip UART | None (needs 8251) |
| **Architecture** | Harvard | Von Neumann (modified) |
| **Typical Clock** | 12 MHz | 5–10 MHz |
| **Application** | Embedded control | Desktop computing |
| **Power Consumption** | Low (battery friendly) | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of the 8051 Microcontroller Architecture

The 8051's internal architecture is a **Harvard architecture**, meaning the program memory and data memory are accessed through **separate buses**. This allows simultaneous instruction fetch and data access in many operations.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU["8 BIT CPU ALU ACC B REG"]:::core
    PC["16 BIT PROGRAM COUNTER PC"]:::core
    SP["8 BIT STACK POINTER SP"]:::core
    DPTR["16 BIT DATA POINTER DPTR"]:::core
    PSW["PSW FLAG REGISTER"]:::core
    RAM["ON CHIP RAM 128 BYTES 00H 7FH"]:::mem
    ROM["ON CHIP ROM 4 KB 0000H 0FFFH"]:::mem
    SREG["SFR BLOCK 80H FFH 21 REGISTERS"]:::mem
    T0["TIMER 0 16 BIT"]:::peri
    T1["TIMER 1 16 BIT"]:::peri
    SIO["SERIAL PORT UART"]:::peri
    INTC["INTERRUPT CONTROLLER 5 SOURCES"]:::peri
    OSC["ON CHIP OSCILLATOR"]:::peri
    P0["PORT 0 P0 0 7"]:::io
    P1["PORT 1 P1 0 7"]:::io
    P2["PORT 2 P2 0 7"]:::io
    P3["PORT 3 P3 0 7 ALT FN"]:::io
    CTRLBUS["CONTROL BUS ALE PSEN EA WR RD"]:::core
    DATABUS["INTERNAL 8 BIT DATA BUS"]:::bus
    CODEBUS["INTERNAL 16 BIT PROGRAM ADDRESS BUS"]:::bus

    CPU --- DATABUS
    PC --- CODEBUS
    ROM --- CODEBUS
    RAM --- DATABUS
    SREG --- DATABUS
    T0 --- DATABUS
    T1 --- DATABUS
    SIO --- DATABUS
    INTC --- DATABUS
    P0 --- DATABUS
    P1 --- DATABUS
    P2 --- DATABUS
    P3 --- DATABUS
    OSC --- CPU
    CTRLBUS --- DATABUS

    classDef core fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mem fill:#9fc5e8,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef peri fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bus fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 8051 Pin Configuration (40-Pin DIP) — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PIN["8051 40 PIN DIP FUNCTIONAL MAP"]
        direction TB
        P1ROW["P1 0 P1 1 P1 2 P1 3 P1 4 P1 5 P1 6 P1 7 -- 8 BIT PORT 1"]
        P3ROW["P3 0 RXD P3 1 TXD P3 2 INT0 P3 3 INT1 P3 4 T0 P3 5 T1 P3 6 WR P3 7 RD -- 8 BIT PORT 3 ALT FN"]
        P0ROW["P0 0 AD0 TO P0 7 AD7 -- 8 BIT PORT 0 AD BUS"]
        P2ROW["P2 0 A8 TO P2 7 A15 -- 8 BIT PORT 2 HIGH ADDR"]
        CTRL["PSEN EA ALE RST XTAL1 XTAL2 VCC VSS -- CONTROL OSC POWER"]
    end
    P1ROW --> GENIO["GENERAL PURPOSE I O"]
    P3ROW --> SER["SERIAL TIMER INTERRUPT EXTERNAL MEMORY CONTROL"]
    P0ROW --> ADBUS["MULTIPLEXED LOW BYTE ADDRESS DATA BUS"]
    P2ROW --> HADR["HIGH BYTE ADDRESS BUS FOR EXTERNAL MEMORY"]
    CTRL --> SYSCTL["SYSTEM CLOCK RESET BOOT SELECT"]
```

### 4.3 8051 Internal RAM Organization Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RAMMAP["INTERNAL RAM 128 BYTES 00H TO 7FH"]
        direction TB
        RB0["00H 07H RB0 R0 TO R7"]
        RB1["08H 0FH RB1 R0 TO R7"]
        RB2["10H 17H RB2 R0 TO R7"]
        RB3["18H 1FH RB3 R0 TO R7"]
        BIT["20H 2FH 16 BYTES 128 BIT ADDRESSABLE 00H TO 7FH"]
        GEN["30H 7FH 80 BYTES GENERAL PURPOSE SCRATCH PAD"]
    end
    RB0 --> RSEL["SELECTED BY PSW RS1 RS0"]
    RB1 --> RSEL
    RB2 --> RSEL
    RB3 --> RSEL
    BIT --> BITOP["SETB CLR ANL ORL BIT OPS"]
    GEN --> STK["STACK AREA USED BY PUSH POP CALL"]
```

### 4.4 Port 3 Alternate Functions (Frequently Asked in KTU)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P3["PORT 3 PINS"]:::io
    P30["P3 0 RXD SERIAL INPUT"]:::io
    P31["P3 1 TXD SERIAL OUTPUT"]:::io
    P32["P3 2 INT0 EXTERNAL INT 0"]:::io
    P33["P3 3 INT1 EXTERNAL INT 1"]:::io
    P34["P3 4 T0 TIMER 0 EXT INPUT"]:::io
    P35["P3 5 T1 TIMER 1 EXT INPUT"]:::io
    P36["P3 6 WR EXTERNAL WRITE STROBE"]:::io
    P37["P3 7 RD EXTERNAL READ STROBE"]:::io

    P3 --> P30
    P3 --> P31
    P3 --> P32
    P3 --> P33
    P3 --> P34
    P3 --> P35
    P3 --> P36
    P3 --> P37

    classDef io fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any six salient features of the 8051 microcontroller.**

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

1. **8-bit CPU** with an 8-bit ALU, capable of arithmetic, logic, and Boolean bit operations.
2. **On-chip 4 KB ROM** for program storage (Flash in modern variants like 89C51).
3. **On-chip 128 bytes RAM** for data storage, including 4 register banks and 16 bytes bit-addressable memory.
4. **Four 8-bit I/O ports** (P0, P1, P2, P3) providing 32 digital I/O lines.
5. **Two 16-bit Timers/Counters** (Timer 0, Timer 1) for timing and event counting.
6. **Full-duplex UART** for serial communication with external devices.
7. *(Bonus, optional)* Five interrupt sources, 16-bit PC, DPTR, and 64 KB external memory addressing.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Harvard and Von Neumann architectures. Which one does the 8051 use?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Harvard | Von Neumann |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory** | Separate code & data memory | Single shared memory for code & data |
| **Buses** | Separate address + data buses for code and data | Common address + data bus |
| **Simultaneous access** | Yes (parallel fetch + data access) | No (bus contention) |
| **Speed** | Faster | Slower (von Neumann bottleneck) |
| **Example** | 8051, PIC, ARM Cortex | 8086, 80486, most desktop CPUs |

**The 8051 uses the Harvard architecture**, with separate program memory (64 KB code via 16-bit program address bus) and data memory (64 KB external + 128 B internal RAM via separate data address bus). This allows instruction prefetch and operand fetch to occur in parallel, increasing throughput. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — Module 1, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Draw the architectural block diagram of the 8051 microcontroller and explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Explain the internal RAM organization of the 8051. List the Special Function Registers (SFRs) and their functions. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Block Diagram & Functions (7 marks):**

*(Refer to Section 4.1 diagram for the block diagram. The student should draw and label.)*

Function of each block:
- **CPU / ALU (8-bit):** Performs arithmetic (+, −, ×, ÷ via MUL/DIV), logic (AND, OR, XOR), and bit-manipulation operations. Uses the **Accumulator (A)** as the primary operand and stores the result back in A.
- **Accumulator (A) & B Register:** A is the primary working register. B is used implicitly by `MUL AB` and `DIV AB` and as a general-purpose register.
- **Program Counter (PC, 16-bit):** Holds the address of the next instruction. Increments after every byte fetch. Reset to 0x0000.
- **Stack Pointer (SP, 8-bit):** Points to the top of the on-chip RAM stack. Reset value = 0x07. Increments before a PUSH, decrements after a POP.
- **Data Pointer (DPTR, 16-bit):** Made of DPH (high) and DPL (low). Used for accessing external data memory and look-up tables in program memory (via `MOVC A,@A+DPTR`).
- **Program Status Word (PSW):** Holds ALU flags: CY (Carry), AC (Auxiliary Carry), F0 (User flag), RS1, RS0 (Register bank select), OV (Overflow), P (Parity).
- **On-chip ROM (4 KB):** Stores the user program. Accessed via the program address bus.
- **On-chip RAM (128 bytes):** Stores data variables, register banks, bit-addressable memory, and stack.
- **SFR Block:** 21 special-function registers (ACC, B, PSW, SP, DPTR, P0–P3, TCON, TMOD, TL0, TH0, TL1, TH1, SCON, SBUF, PCON, IE, IP) mapped at addresses 0x80–0xFF.
- **Timers/Counters (T0, T1):** Two 16-bit timer/counter blocks for timing and event counting.
- **Serial Port (UART):** One full-duplex programmable serial communication channel.
- **Interrupt Controller:** Manages 5 interrupt sources (INT0, T0, INT1, T1, Serial).
- **Oscillator Circuit:** On-chip inverter + external crystal generates the system clock. At 12 MHz, machine cycle = 1 µs.
- **I/O Ports P0–P3:** Four 8-bit ports for digital I/O. P3 has alternate functions. P0 and P2 are used for external memory addressing.
- **Control Bus:** Provides $\overline{EA}$, $\overline{PSEN}$, ALE, $\overline{WR}$, $\overline{RD}$ to interface with external memory.

**(b) Internal RAM Organization & SFRs (7 marks):**

**Internal RAM is 128 bytes (0x00 – 0x7F):**

| Address Range | Function |
|---|---|
| **0x00 – 0x1F** | **32 bytes = 4 Register Banks × 8 Registers.** Each bank has R0–R7. Bank selected via PSW[RS1:RS0]. Bank 0 = 0x00–0x07, Bank 1 = 0x08–0x0F, Bank 2 = 0x10–0x17, Bank 3 = 0x18–0x1F. |
| **0x20 – 0x2F** | **16 bytes = 128 bit-addressable locations** (bits 0x00 to 0x7F). Supports bit-level SETB, CLR, ANL, ORL, JB, JNB, JBC. |
| **0x30 – 0x7F** | **80 bytes of general-purpose scratchpad RAM** for variables and stack. |

**Special Function Registers (SFRs), address 0x80 – 0xFF:**

| SFR | Address | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **ACC** | 0xE0 | Accumulator (bit-addressable) |
| **B** | 0xF0 | B register (used by MUL/DIV) |
| **PSW** | 0xD0 | Program Status Word (flags) |
| **SP** | 0x81 | Stack Pointer |
| **DPH / DPL** | 0x83 / 0x82 | Data Pointer high / low |
| **P0 / P1 / P2 / P3** | 0x80 / 0x90 / 0xA0 / 0xB0 | I/O Port latches |
| **TCON** | 0x88 | Timer/Counter control (flags + mode trigger) |
| **TMOD** | 0x89 | Timer mode register |
| **TL0 / TH0** | 0x8A / 0x8C | Timer 0 low / high byte |
| **TL1 / TH1** | 0x8B / 0x8D | Timer 1 low / high byte |
| **SCON** | 0x98 | Serial port control |
| **SBUF** | 0x99 | Serial data buffer (TX and RX) |
| **PCON** | 0x87 | Power control (SMOD bit) |
| **IE** | 0xA8 | Interrupt Enable |
| **IP** | 0xB8 | Interrupt Priority |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE:**

**(a)** Explain the **PSW register** of the 8051 with a neat bit diagram. How are the register banks selected? **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Compare the **8051 microcontroller** with the **8086 microprocessor** in terms of architecture, memory, peripherals, and applications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) PSW Register and Register Bank Selection (7 marks):**

The **Program Status Word (PSW)** is an 8-bit SFR at address **0xD0** and is **bit-addressable**.

| Bit | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **PSW.7 (CY)** | Carry Flag | Set if there is a carry-out from bit 7 during addition or borrow during subtraction. |
| **PSW.6 (AC)** | Auxiliary Carry | Set on carry from bit 3 to bit 4 (used for BCD arithmetic). |
| **PSW.5 (F0)** | User Flag | General-purpose flag available to the programmer. |
| **PSW.4 (RS1)** | Register Bank Select 1 | Together with RS0, selects one of 4 register banks. |
| **PSW.3 (RS0)** | Register Bank Select 0 | Together with RS1, selects one of 4 register banks. |
| **PSW.2 (OV)** | Overflow Flag | Set on signed arithmetic overflow. Used by MUL/DIV. |
| **PSW.1 (—)** | Reserved | User-defined in some derivatives. |
| **PSW.0 (P)** | Parity Flag | Set if the number of 1-bits in the accumulator is **odd** (hardware sets it = 1 for odd parity). |

**Register Bank Selection using RS1:RS0:**

| RS1 | RS0 | Bank Selected | Address Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | Bank 0 | 0x00 – 0x07 |
| 0 | 1 | Bank 1 | 0x08 – 0x0F |
| 1 | 0 | Bank 2 | 0x10 – 0x17 |
| 1 | 1 | Bank 3 | 0x18 – 0x1F |

*Example:* `SETB RS0` selects Bank 1; `CLR RS1` deselects Bank 2.

On **RESET**, RS1:RS0 = 00 → **Bank 0** is active, and SP = 0x07 (i.e., the first PUSH goes to 0x08).

**(b) Comparison: 8051 vs 8086 (7 marks):**

*(Refer to Section 3.4 table for the complete comparison. Key points below.)*

- **Type:** 8051 = Microcontroller (SoC); 8086 = Microprocessor (CPU only).
- **Word size:** 8051 = 8-bit data, 16-bit address; 8086 = 16-bit data, 20-bit address.
- **Memory addressing:** 8051 = 64 KB code + 64 KB data (Harvard); 8086 = 1 MB unified (modified Von Neumann).
- **On-chip RAM/ROM:** 8051 = 128 B RAM + 4 KB ROM; 8086 = None (external).
- **Timers/UART:** 8051 = 2 timers + 1 UART on-chip; 8086 = Needs external 8253/8251 chips.
- **I/O:** 8051 = 32 I/O lines on-chip; 8086 = Needs 8255 PPI.
- **Power consumption:** 8051 = mW range (battery IoT); 8086 = W range.
- **Application domain:** 8051 = Embedded control, IoT, sensor interfacing, automotive; 8086 = Desktop PCs, laptops, larger computing systems.
- **Complexity & cost:** 8051 = Simpler, cheaper, smaller; 8086 = Complex, expensive, larger.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting RESET values** — Always write the reset values of PC (0x0000), SP (0x07), ACC (0x00), PSW (0x00), P0–P3 (0xFF). Examiners specifically check this.
> 2. **Mixing up SP behavior** — SP *increments BEFORE PUSH* and *decrements AFTER POP* (or equivalently, PUSH stores at SP+1 and POP reads from SP+1 then decrements). Many students reverse this.
> 3. **Confusing internal vs external memory** — The 8051 can access up to 64 KB code and 64 KB data *externally*; internal is only 4 KB + 128 B.
> 4. **Bit-addressable memory limits** — Only **0x20–0x2F (16 bytes = 128 bits)** of internal RAM and the **11 bit-addressable SFRs** (ACC, B, PSW, TCON, SCON, IE, IP, P0, P1, P2, P3) are bit-addressable. Pasting "the entire RAM is bit-addressable" loses 1 mark.
> 5. **P3 alternate function confusion** — Writing only "P3 is an I/O port" without listing RXD, TXD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, $\overline{WR}$, $\overline{RD}$ is incomplete.
> 6. **Machine cycle formula sign error** — $T_{\text{MC}} = 12 / f_{\text{osc}}$ (not $f_{\text{osc}}/12$). Mixing up gives a 10x numerical error.
> 7. **Using 0x in subtraction-based questions** — Examiners check that SP and PUSH/POP addresses are written in **hexadecimal** for 8051 questions, matching the 8051 convention.
> 8. **Drawing block diagrams without labels** — Every block in the architecture diagram needs at least a 1-line function annotation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Summary — 8051 Features**

- ✅ 8051 = **8-bit, Harvard-architecture, single-chip CMOS microcontroller** by **Intel, 1980**.
- ✅ On-chip **4 KB ROM** (program memory) + **128 bytes RAM** (data memory) + **21 SFRs** at 0x80–0xFF.
- ✅ **16-bit PC** (reset to 0x0000), **8-bit SP** (reset to 0x07), **16-bit DPTR** (no default).
- ✅ **4 register banks** of 8 registers each at 0x00–0x1F; selected via **PSW.4 (RS1)** and **PSW.3 (RS0)**.
- ✅ **Bit-addressable RAM:** 0x20–0x2F = 128 bits. **Bit-addressable SFRs:** ACC, B, PSW, TCON, SCON, IE, IP, P0–P3.
- ✅ **4 × 8-bit I/O ports** (P0–P3) = 32 I/O lines. P3 has **alternate functions** (RXD, TXD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, $\overline{WR}$, $\overline{RD}$).
- ✅ **2 × 16-bit Timers/Counters** in 4 modes; Mode 1 = 16-bit, Mode 2 = 8-bit auto-reload.
- ✅ **1 full-duplex UART** with 4 modes; Mode 1 = 8-bit variable baud rate (most common).
- ✅ **5 interrupt sources** with fixed vector addresses (INT0 = 0x0003, T0 = 0x000B, INT1 = 0x0013, T1 = 0x001B, SIO = 0x0023).
- ✅ **Machine Cycle = 12 oscillator periods**; at 12 MHz, $T_{\text{MC}} = 1\,\mu\text{s}$; at 11.0592 MHz, $T_{\text{MC}} \approx 1.085\,\mu\text{s}$ (baud-friendly).
- ✅ **PSW bits (bit7 to bit0):** `CY AC F0 RS1 RS0 OV - P`.
- ✅ External memory: **64 KB code** (via $\overline{PSEN}$) + **64 KB data** (via $\overline{WR}/\overline{RD}$) = combined 128 KB external address space.
- ✅ **Derivative families to remember:** 8031 (no ROM), 8052 (8 KB ROM, 3 timers, 6 interrupts), 89C51 (Flash ROM, ISP).
- ✅ **Modern 8051 derivatives** (CC2530, N76E003, C8051Fxxx) integrate ADCs, PWM, RF transceivers — directly enabling **IoT** applications.
- ✅ **Key control signals:** $\overline{EA}$ (internal/external code access), $\overline{PSEN}$ (program store enable), ALE (address latch enable), $\overline{WR}$, $\overline{RD}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internal Block Diagram of 8051
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCGT503 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS FOR IOT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Internal Block Diagram of 8051 # Module 1 — Internal Block Diagram of 8051 ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **8051 Microcontroller** is an **8-bit**, single-chip, Von-Neumann/Princeton architecture-based CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer) microcontroller originally developed by **Intel** in **1980**. It integrates a **CPU**, **4 KB of on-chip ROM (Program Memory)**, **128 bytes of on-chip RAM (Data Memory)**, **four 8-bit bi-directional I/O ports (32 I/O lines)**, **two 16-bit Timers/Counters**, a **full-duplex programmable UART (Serial Port)**, a **5-vector interrupt structure with 2 priority levels**, and an **on-chip oscillator** — all fabricated on a single piece of silicon. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The 8051 is treated as the foundational *Harvard-architecture* microcontroller (with separate program and data memory spaces) widely used as the building block for IoT edge-node prototyping. For KTU, students must remember its **40-pin DIP** package, **8-bit ALU**, and **16-bit Program Counter (PC)** capable of addressing **64 KB** of external memory. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The 8051 as a Self-Contained Office" Think of the 8051 as a **tiny, self-sufficient office housed inside a single room**: | 8051 Component | Office Analogy | Function | |---|---|---| | **CPU / ALU** | The office worker (brain) | Performs all arithmetic, logic, and decision-making | | **Accumulator (A)** | The worker's desk | Where active calculations are performed | | **Program Memory (ROM)** | The instruction manual | Stores permanent, read-only programs (the rules) | | **Data Memory (RAM)** | The worker's notepad | Holds temporary variables and results | | **I/O Ports (P0–P3)** | The office doors & windows (4 doors, 8 windows each) | Communication with the outside world | | **Timers/Counters** | The wall clock & stopwatch | Tracks time intervals and counts external events | | **Serial Port (UART)** | The telephone line | Sends/receives data bit-by-bit to a remote office | | **Interrupt Logic** | The doorbell | Alerts the worker to urgent external events | | **Oscillator / Clock** | The heartbeat | Provides the rhythm for synchronized operation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Takeaway:** Unlike a microprocessor (which is *only* a CPU and needs *external* memory/IO chips to function), a **microcontroller is a "system-on-a-chip"** — the CPU, memory, and I/O all live together. The 8051 is the classic textbook example of this philosophy and the conceptual ancestor of every modern IoT MCU (ESP32, STM32, Arduino's ATmega328P). ### 1.3 Core Physical & Electrical Constants - **Data Bus Width:** **8 bits** - **Address Bus Width (External):** **16 bits** → can address $2^{16} = \mathbf{65{,}536 = 64\text{ KB}}$ - **On-chip Program ROM:** **4 KB** (address range $0000\text{H} - 0FFF\text{H}$) - **On-chip Data RAM:** **128 bytes** (address range $00\text{H} - 7F\text{H}$) - **Number of I/O Pins:** **32** (4 ports × 8 bits) - **Operating Frequency Range:** **1.2 MHz to 12 MHz** (crystal oscillator) - **Operating Voltage:** **+5 V DC** (standard) / **2.7 V – 5.5 V** (low-voltage variants) - **Package:** **40-pin DIP** (Dual In-line Package) or 44-pin PLCC/QFP --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet ## 2.1 Block-Level Functional Decomposition The 8051 architecture can be functionally decomposed into **seven major building blocks** that interact through three internal buses: ### 2.1.1 Central Processing Unit (CPU) The CPU is the "thinking" unit. It contains the following sub-modules: - **Accumulator (A / ACC):** The single most-used 8-bit register. Almost every ALU operation passes through `A`. Example: `ADD A, R0` adds the value of `R0` to the accumulator. - **B Register:** Used implicitly by `MUL AB` and `DIV AB` instructions. It holds the upper byte of multiplication or the remainder of division. Otherwise, it acts as a general-purpose scratch register. - **Program Status Word (PSW):** An **8-bit flag register** that stores the status of the most recent ALU operation. - **Stack Pointer (SP):** An 8-bit register that points to the top of the stack in internal RAM. **Reset value = 07H**, so the stack conventionally starts at 08H. - **Data Pointer (DPTR):** A **16-bit register** (composed of `DPH` + `DPL`) used to access external data memory and lookup tables via `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`. - **Program Counter (PC):** A **16-bit counter** that always points to the **next instruction** to be fetched. Reset value = **0000H**. ### 2.1.2 Oscillator & Clock Circuit The 8051 requires an **external quartz crystal** connected between **pins XTAL1 (pin 19)** and **XTAL2 (pin 18)** with two load capacitors (typically **30 pF**) to ground. The on-chip oscillator generates a **clock signal** that drives every internal operation. > [!NOTE] > **One Machine Cycle = 12 Oscillator Periods (Clock Cycles).** This is a **high-yield KTU fact**. If a 12 MHz crystal is used, one machine cycle = 12 / 12 MHz = **1 microsecond (µs)**. ### 2.1.3 Program Memory (ROM / Code Memory) - **Size:** 4 KB on-chip (expandable to 64 KB externally). - **Type:** Non-volatile, read-only in normal operation. - **Address Range:** `0000H` to `0FFFH` (on-chip) and `1000H` to `FFFFH` (external). - **Accessed by:** `MOVC` (Move Code) instructions using the 16-bit PC or DPTR. - **Special Addresses:** - `0000H` → Reset vector (execution begins here). - `0003H` → External Interrupt 0 (INT0) ISR. - `000BH` → Timer 0 Overflow ISR. - `0013H` → External Interrupt 1 (INT1) ISR. - `001BH` → Timer 1 Overflow ISR. - `0023H` → Serial Port ISR. ### 2.1.4 Data Memory (RAM) Divided into two distinct regions: | Region | Size | Address Range | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | **Lower RAM** | 128 B | `00H–7FH` | User variables + Bit-addressable area + Register Banks | | **SFR Space** | 128 B | `80H–FFH` | Special Function Registers (control/status) | The **lower 128 bytes** are further subdivided into: - **Register Banks (R0–R7):** Four banks of 8 registers each (`00H–1FH`). Bank selected via PSW bits RS0 and RS1. - **Bit-Addressable Area:** 16 bytes (`20H–2FH`) → 128 individually addressable bits. Useful for boolean flags. - **General-Purpose RAM:** 80 bytes (`30H–7FH`) for scratchpad use. ### 2.1.5 Special Function Registers (SFRs) These are the **control panel** of the 8051. Each SFR is an internal register mapped into the upper RAM address space (`80H–FFH`). Important ones: | SFR | Address | Function | |---|---|---| | `A` (ACC) | `E0H` | Accumulator | | `B` | `F0H` | B register | | `PSW` | `D0H` | Program Status Word | | `SP` | `81H` | Stack Pointer | | `DPH/DPL` | `83H / 82H` | Data Pointer (high/low) | | `P0`–`P3` | `80H, 90H, A0H, B0H` | Port 0–3 latches | | `IE` | `A8H` | Interrupt Enable | | `IP` | `B8H` | Interrupt Priority | | `TCON` | `88H` | Timer/Counter Control | | `TMOD` | `89H` | Timer/Counter Mode | | `TH0, TL0` | `8CH, 8AH` | Timer 0 high/low | | `TH1, TL1` | `8DH, 8BH` | Timer 1 high/low | | `SCON` | `98H` | Serial Control | | `SBUF` | `99H` | Serial Data Buffer | | `PCON` | `87H` | Power Control | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Not all addresses `80H–FFH` are defined as SFRs.** Undefined addresses must not be accessed, or the behavior is undefined. This is a frequent KTU exam pitfall. ### 2.1.6 I/O Ports (P0, P1, P2, P3) - **Port 0 (P0, pins 32–39):** **8-bit**, open-drain, **bidirectional**. Used as the **multiplexed low-byte address/data bus** in external memory mode. - **Port 1 (P1, pins 1–8):** **8-bit**, quasi-bidirectional, no alternate function. **General-purpose I/O only.** - **Port 2 (P2, pins 21–28):** **8-bit**, quasi-bidirectional, provides the **high-byte of the address bus** in external memory mode. - **Port 3 (P3, pins 10–17):** **8-bit**, quasi-bidirectional, has **alternate functions** for all 8 pins (RXD, TXD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD). ### 2.1.7 Interrupt, Timer & Serial Subsystems - **Two 16-bit Timers/Counters (T0 & T1):** Configurable in **4 modes** via TMOD. Used for timing, event counting, or baud-rate generation. - **Full-Duplex UART:** One serial port via `SBUF` and `SCON`, configurable in **4 modes** (Mode 1 — 8-bit UART — is most common). - **5 Interrupt Sources:** INT0, T0, INT1, T1, Serial. Each has a fixed vector address and individual enable + priority control. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Table > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates every formula, constant, and fact you must memorize for KTU 2024 ESE/IA questions on this topic. | # | Parameter / Formula | Value / Expression | KTU Significance | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Data Bus Width** | $\mathbf{8\text{ bits}}$ | Defines ALU word size | | 2 | **Address Bus Width** | $\mathbf{16\text{ bits}}$ | Enables $2^{16} = 64\text{ KB}$ addressing | | 3 | **Max External Memory** | $2^{16} = \mathbf{65{,}536\text{ bytes} = 64\text{ KB}}$ | Direct calculation question | | 4 | **On-chip ROM** | $\mathbf{4\text{ KB}}$ | Address `0000H–0FFFH` | | 5 | **On-chip RAM** | $\mathbf{128\text{ bytes}}$ | Address `00H–7FH` | | 6 | **Total RAM (incl. SFRs)** | $128 + 128 = \mathbf{256\text{ bytes}}$ | Combined data memory space | | 7 | **I/O Lines** | $4 \times 8 = \mathbf{32}$ | 4 ports × 8 bits | | 8 | **Timers/Counters** | $\mathbf{2 \times 16\text{-bit}}$ | T0, T1 | | 9 | **Interrupt Sources** | $\mathbf{5}$ | INT0, T0, INT1, T1, Serial | | 10 | **Interrupt Priority Levels** | $\mathbf{2}$ | High / Low (via IP register) | | 11 | **Register Banks** | $\mathbf{4}$ | Selected by PSW[RS1:RS0] | | 12 | **Registers per Bank** | $\mathbf{8}$ (R0–R7) | 4 × 8 = 32 bytes total | | 13 | **Bit-Addressable RAM** | $16 \times 8 = \mathbf{128\text{ bits}}$ | Byte addresses `20H–2FH` | | 14 | **SFR Count (defined)** | $\mathbf{21}$ common ones | Located at `80H–FFH` | | 15 | **Stack Pointer Reset Value** | $\mathbf{07H}$ | First PUSH writes to `08H` | | 16 | **PC Reset Value** | $\mathbf{0000H}$ | First instruction fetched from `0000H` | | 17 | **Oscillator Range** | $\mathbf{1.2\text{ MHz} – 12\text{ MHz}}$ | External crystal | | 18 | **Machine Cycle Formula** | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | 12 clock periods per machine cycle | | 19 | **Machine Cycle @ 12 MHz** | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = \mathbf{1\ \mu s}$ | Standard for KTU numeric problems | | 20 | **Power Supply** | $\mathbf{+5\text{ V DC}}$ | Pin 40 = Vcc, Pin 20 = GND | | 21 | **Package** | $\mathbf{40\text{-pin DIP}}$ | 44-pin variants also exist | | 22 | **Reset Pin** | $\mathbf{\text{Pin 9 (RST)}}$ | Active HIGH, min 2 machine cycles | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > The 8051 is the **direct ancestor** of every IoT-capable microcontroller you will study in this course (CC2530, CC3200, ESP8266, ESP32). Understanding its block diagram provides the **mental scaffolding** for understanding *any* modern MCU. In industry, 8051 cores are still embedded as **IP cores** inside SoCs for low-cost applications: **washing machines, microwave ovens, remote controls, automotive ECUs, RFID readers, and toy electronics**. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Signal-Flow Analysis > Since the 8051 internal block diagram is fundamentally a **descriptive architectural topic** rather than a purely mathematical one, this section presents an **exhaustive, step-by-step analytical walkthrough** of how the seven functional blocks interact during a typical instruction fetch–decode–execute cycle. This is the form of "derivation" most relevant to KTU board exam answers. ## 3.1 Analytical Goal We will trace the complete signal flow when the 8051 **resets** and then **executes the first instruction** stored at program memory address `0000H`, identifying the role of **every block** in the internal block diagram. ## 3.2 Step-by-Step Walkthrough ### Step 1 — Power-On / Reset Signal Applied The user applies a **HIGH pulse on pin 9 (RST)** for a minimum of **2 machine cycles**. Internally: $$ \text{Reset Pulse} \xrightarrow{\text{Reset Circuit}} \text{Clears PC, SFRs, sets SP} $$ **Specifically:** - `PC ← 0000H` (Program Counter loaded with reset vector) - `ACC ← 00H`, `B ← 00H`, `SP ← 07H` - All port latches (`P0`–`P3`) ← `FFH` (ready as inputs) - `PSW ← 00H` (Register Bank 0 selected) - `TCON ← 00H`, `TMOD ← 00H`, `SCON ← 00H`, `IE ← 00H`, `IP ← 00H` (Timers/Serial/Interrupts disabled) - `SBUF ← Indeterminate` (unaffected by reset) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Tip:** Always list **at least 6 SFRs** when describing the 8051 reset state. The most commonly missed one is **DPTR**, which is reset to `0000H`. ### Step 2 — Oscillator Starts The crystal oscillator (between **XTAL1 pin 19** and **XTAL2 pin 18**) begins oscillating at frequency $f_{osc}$ (typically **11.0592 MHz** or **12 MHz**). The on-chip clock generator divides this by 12 to produce the **machine cycle clock**. $$ f_{MC} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12} $$ The clock is distributed to **all internal blocks** (CPU, Timers, Serial Port, Interrupt logic) to synchronize their operations. ### Step 3 — PC Addresses Program Memory The Program Counter currently holds `0000H`. The CPU asserts: - `PSEN̅` (Program Store Enable, pin 29) → **LOW** to enable external program memory read *(or internal ROM is used directly when `EA̅ = HIGH`)* - The **16-bit address `0000H`** is sent out: **low byte on P0** (multiplexed), **high byte on P2** ### Step 4 — Instruction Fetch The program memory (internal ROM, $4\text{ KB}$ at `0000H–0FFFH`) places the **opcode byte** of the first instruction onto the data bus. The CPU **latches** this byte into the **Instruction Register (IR)** inside the CPU block. ### Step 5 — Instruction Decode The **Instruction Decoder / Timing & Control Unit** decodes the opcode and generates the appropriate micro-control signals. It may also fetch **one or two additional operand bytes** in subsequent machine cycles. ### Step 6 — Execution by ALU / CPU Sub-modules Depending on the decoded opcode, the execution routes to: - **ALU** for arithmetic/logic (uses `A`, `B`, internal data bus) - **Internal RAM** (via `R0`–`R7` of selected bank) for data movement - **SFR block** for configuring peripherals (Timers, Serial, Interrupts, Ports) - **External bus controller** for accessing external data memory (`MOVX` instructions) ### Step 7 — PC Auto-Increment After the instruction is fully fetched (1–3 bytes), the PC is **incremented by the instruction length** so it points to the next instruction. The cycle **repeats indefinitely** — this is the **fetch–decode–execute loop**. ## 3.3 KTU-Style Memory-Map Calculation A frequent KTU 14-mark question asks: *"Calculate the total addressable memory and draw the memory map of the 8051."* Here is the step-by-step logic: **Step (i):** The 16-bit address bus can access: $$ \text{Total Addressable Memory} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ KB} $$ **Step (ii):** Of this, 4 KB is on-chip ROM, the rest (60 KB) is external: $$ \text{Internal ROM} = 4 \text{ KB} = 4096 \text{ bytes at } 0000\text{H} - 0FFF\text{H} $$ $$ \text{External Program Memory} = 64 - 4 = 60 \text{ KB at } 1000\text{H} - FFFF\text{H} $$ **Step (iii):** Data memory space is **separate** (Harvard architecture). The data bus is **8 bits wide**, so the on-chip data memory is 128 bytes. The lower 128 bytes are user RAM, the upper 128 bytes are SFRs. **Step (iv):** External data memory (accessed via `MOVX`) also occupies the 64 KB address space (`0000H–FFFFH`), **separate from program memory**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Common Student Mistake:** Treating program and data memory as the **same** 64 KB space. The 8051 uses a **modified Harvard architecture**: program and data memory are **logically and physically separate** even though both can be 64 KB in size. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — 8051 Internal Architecture > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete internal block-level functional architecture** of the 8051 microcontroller, showing how the CPU, memory, I/O, and peripherals interconnect through the internal buses. ```mermaid flowchart TB OSC["OSCILLATOR AND CLOCK CIRCUIT
XTAL1 XTAL2 External Crystal"] CPU["CPU CORE"] ALU["8 BIT ALU"] ACC["ACCUMULATOR A"] BR["B REGISTER"] PSW["PSW FLAGS"] SP["STACK POINTER SP"] PC["PROGRAM COUNTER PC 16 BIT"] DPTR["DATA POINTER DPTR 16 BIT"] IR["INSTRUCTION REGISTER"] DEC["INSTRUCTION DECODER"] CTRL["TIMING AND CONTROL UNIT"] ROM["PROGRAM ROM 4 KB
0000H to 0FFFH"] RAM["DATA RAM 128 BYTES
00H to 7FH"] SFR["SFR BLOCK 128 BYTES
80H to FFH"] REGB["REGISTER BANKS R0 to R7
4 Banks x 8 Registers"] BIT["BIT ADDRESSABLE AREA
20H to 2FH"] P0["PORT 0 P0
8 LINES Multiplexed AD0 to AD7"] P1["PORT 1 P1

8 LINES Alternate Functions"] T0["TIMER COUNTER T0 16 BIT"] T1["TIMER COUNTER T1 16 BIT"] UART["SERIAL PORT UART
Full Duplex"] INT["INTERRUPT CONTROLLER
5 Sources 2 Priority Levels"] BUS_INT["INTERNAL DATA BUS 8 BIT"] BUS_ADDR["INTERNAL ADDRESS BUS 16 BIT"] EXT_CTRL["EXTERNAL BUS CONTROL
ALE PSEN EA WR RD"] OSC --> CPU CPU --> ALU CPU --> IR CPU --> DEC CPU --> CTRL CPU --> ACC CPU --> BR CPU --> PSW CPU --> SP CPU --> PC CPU --> DPTR ALU --- BUS_INT ACC --- BUS_INT BR --- BUS_INT PSW --- BUS_INT SP --- BUS_INT DPTR --- BUS_INT PC --- BUS_ADDR DEC --- BUS_INT CTRL --- BUS_INT BUS_INT --- ROM BUS_INT --- RAM BUS_INT --- SFR RAM --- REGB RAM --- BIT SFR --- P0 SFR --- P1 SFR --- P2 SFR --- P3 SFR --- T0 SFR --- T1 SFR --- UART SFR --- INT SFR --- EXT_CTRL EXT_CTRL --- BUS_ADDR ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — 8051 Memory Organization ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PGMMEM["PROGRAM MEMORY 64 KB MAX"] ROM_INT["INTERNAL ROM 4 KB
0000H to 0FFFH"] ROM_EXT["EXTERNAL ROM 60 KB
1000H to FFFFH"] end subgraph DATAMEM["DATA MEMORY LOGICAL"] subgraph LOWRAM["LOWER 128 BYTES 00H to 7FH"] RB0["BANK 0 R0 to R7
00H to 07H"] RB1["BANK 1 R0 to R7
