10H to 17H"] RB3["BANK 3 R0 to R7
18H to 1FH"] BITA["BIT ADDRESSABLE
20H to 2FH"] GPR["GENERAL PURPOSE RAM
30H to 7FH"] end subgraph SFRMEM["SFR SPACE 80H to FFH"] SFRL["21 DEFINED SFRs
ACC B PSW SP DPTR
P0 P1 P2 P3 TCON TMOD
SCON SBUF IE IP PCON etc"] end end EXTDM["EXTERNAL DATA MEMORY
64 KB via MOVX
0000H to FFFFH"] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Port 3 Alternate Functions ```mermaid flowchart TB P3["PORT 3 P3 PINS 10 TO 17"] P30["P3.0 Pin 10 RXD"] P31["P3.1 Pin 11 TXD"] P32["P3.2 Pin 12 INT0"] P33["P3.3 Pin 13 INT1"] P34["P3.4 Pin 14 T0"] P35["P3.5 Pin 15 T1"] P36["P3.6 Pin 16 WR"] P37["P3.7 Pin 17 RD"] P3 --> P30 P3 --> P31 P3 --> P32 P3 --> P33 P3 --> P34 P3 --> P35 P3 --> P36 P3 --> P37 P30 --> URX["SERIAL INPUT"] P31 --> UTX["SERIAL OUTPUT"] P32 --> IE0["EXTERNAL INTERRUPT 0"] P33 --> IE1["EXTERNAL INTERRUPT 1"] P34 --> TC0["TIMER 0 INPUT"] P35 --> TC1["TIMER 1 INPUT"] P36 --> WRE["EXTERNAL WRITE STROBE"] P37 --> RDE["EXTERNAL READ STROBE"] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define the 8051 microcontroller and list its salient features. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]` **Model Answer:** The **8051** is an **8-bit, single-chip, Harvard-architecture CISC microcontroller** developed by Intel in 1980. It integrates a CPU, memory, and I/O peripherals on a single silicon die. **Salient Features:** 1. **8-bit ALU** and **8-bit data bus** 2. **16-bit address bus** → addressable memory = $2^{16} = \mathbf{64\text{ KB}}$ 3. **On-chip Program ROM:** 4 KB (`0000H–0FFFH`) 4. **On-chip Data RAM:** 128 bytes (`00H–7FH`) 5. **On-chip SFR space:** 128 bytes (`80H–FFH`) 6. **Four 8-bit I/O ports** → **32 I/O lines** 7. **Two 16-bit Timers/Counters** (T0, T1) 8. **Full-duplex programmable UART** 9. **5 interrupt sources** with **2 priority levels** 10. **On-chip oscillator** with external crystal (1.2–12 MHz) 11. **40-pin DIP** package, **+5 V** supply --- ### Question 2: Differentiate between the Harvard and Von-Neumann architectures as applied to the 8051. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]` **Model Answer:** | Parameter | **Harvard Architecture (8051)** | **Von-Neumann Architecture** | |---|---|---| | **Memory Structure** | Separate program and data memories | Single shared memory for both | | **Buses** | Separate address and data buses for code and data | Common address and data buses | | **Fetching** | Can fetch instruction and data **simultaneously** | Must fetch instruction **or** data sequentially | | **Speed** | **Faster** (parallel access) | Slower (bus contention) | | **Complexity** | More complex hardware | Simpler hardware | | **Example** | **8051, AVR, PIC, ARM Cortex-M** | 8085, 8086, general-purpose CPUs | > **Note:** The 8051 is technically a **modified Harvard** because although it has separate internal program and data memory, the external memory buses (P0, P2) can be configured to access *either* external program or external data memory in a multiplexed fashion. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice means you must answer *either* Choice A *or* Choice B *in full*. --- ### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) #### Q.A(a) — Draw and explain the internal block diagram of the 8051 microcontroller in detail. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Solution:** **Introduction (1 mark):** The 8051 is an 8-bit, single-chip microcontroller. Its internal block diagram consists of **seven major functional blocks** interconnected by an 8-bit internal data bus, a 16-bit address bus, and a control bus. **The Seven Major Blocks:** 1. **CPU (Central Processing Unit)** (1 mark) - Contains the **8-bit ALU**, **Accumulator (A)**, **B Register**, **Program Status Word (PSW)**, **Stack Pointer (SP)**, **Data Pointer (DPTR)**, **Program Counter (PC)**, **Instruction Register**, **Instruction Decoder**, and **Timing & Control Unit**. - The ALU performs arithmetic (`ADD`, `SUBB`, `MUL`, `DIV`), logic (`ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`), and shift/rotate operations. 2. **Oscillator & Clock Circuit** (0.5 marks) - Uses an external crystal between **XTAL1 (pin 19)** and **XTAL2 (pin 18)**. - Generates the master clock. **One machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods.** 3. **Program Memory (ROM)** (0.5 marks) - **4 KB on-chip ROM** (addresses `0000H–0FFFH`). - Holds the user's program. Accessed via `MOVC` instructions. 4. **Data Memory (RAM)** (1 mark) - **128 bytes on-chip** (`00H–7FH`) — user RAM, register banks, bit-addressable area. - **128 bytes SFR space** (`80H–FFH`) — control/status registers. - Expandable to **64 KB external data memory** via `MOVX`. 5. **I/O Ports** (1 mark) - **P0** (open-drain, multiplexed AD bus), **P1** (general purpose), **P2** (high-address bus), **P3** (alternate functions: RXD, TXD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR̅, RD̅). 6. **Timers/Counters** (0.5 marks) - **Two 16-bit timers** (T0, T1) usable as timers or event counters in 4 modes. 7. **Serial Port (UART) + Interrupt Controller** (1 mark) - **Full-duplex UART** via `SBUF` and `SCON` (4 modes). - **5 interrupt sources**: INT0, T0, INT1, T1, Serial. Two priority levels via `IP`. 8. **External Bus Control** (0.5 marks) - Pins `ALE` (pin 30), `PSEN̅` (pin 29), `EA̅` (pin 31), `WR̅` (pin 16), `RD̅` (pin 17) for external memory interfacing. **Conclusion (0.5 marks):** The integration of CPU, memory, and I/O on a single chip makes the 8051 ideal for embedded and IoT applications requiring low cost, low power, and real-time control. --- #### Q.A(b) — Explain the memory organization of the 8051 with a neat diagram. List the SFRs and their functions. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **Model Solution:** **Data Memory Organization (4 marks):** The 8051 data memory is logically divided into two parts: - **Lower 128 bytes (00H–7FH):** User-accessible RAM. - **Register Banks 0–3 (00H–1FH):** Four banks of 8 registers each (`R0–R7`). Bank selected by `PSW.4` (RS0) and `PSW.3` (RS1). - **Bit-Addressable Area (20H–2FH):** 16 bytes → 128 individually addressable bits (e.g., `SETB 20H.0`). - **General-Purpose RAM (30H–7FH):** 80 bytes for scratch variables and the stack. - **Upper 128 bytes (80H–FFH):** **SFR space.** Only defined addresses return valid registers; undefined addresses must not be accessed. **Program Memory Organization (1 mark):** - 4 KB on-chip ROM (0000H–0FFFH), expandable externally to 64 KB. - Special addresses: `0000H` (Reset), `0003H` (INT0), `000BH` (T0), `0013H` (INT1), `001BH` (T1), `0023H` (Serial). **Important SFRs and Functions (2 marks):** | SFR | Address | Function | |---|---|---| | `ACC` | `E0H` | Accumulator — main ALU operand | | `B` | `F0H` | Used by `MUL` and `DIV` | | `PSW` | `D0H` | Contains flags: CY, AC, F0, RS1, RS0, OV, –, P | | `SP` | `81H` | Stack pointer (reset = 07H) | | `DPH/DPL` | `83H/82H` | 16-bit data pointer | | `P0/P1/P2/P3` | `80H/90H/A0H/B0H` | Port latches | | `TMOD` | `89H` | Timer mode select | | `TCON` | `88H` | Timer control flags | | `TH0, TL0` | `8CH, 8AH` | Timer 0 high/low byte | | `TH1, TL1` | `8DH, 8BH` | Timer 1 high/low byte | | `SCON` | `98H` | Serial control | | `SBUF` | `99H` | Serial data buffer | | `IE` | `A8H` | Interrupt enable | | `IP` | `B8H` | Interrupt priority | | `PCON` | `87H` | Power control | --- ### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) #### Q.B(a) — With a neat block diagram, explain the function of the CPU, ALU, and oscillator in the 8051. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Model Solution:** **1. Central Processing Unit (CPU) (3 marks):** The CPU is the brain of the 8051. It consists of: - **Accumulator (A) — 8 bits:** The primary operand for all ALU operations. Most data-movement and arithmetic instructions use `A` as a source or destination. Example: `ADD A, R1` adds R1's content to A. - **B Register — 8 bits:** Used implicitly by `MUL AB` and `DIV AB`. After `MUL AB`, the high byte of the 16-bit product is stored in B. Otherwise, it is a general-purpose scratch register. - **Program Status Word (PSW) — 8 bits:** Holds flags — Carry (CY), Auxiliary Carry (AC), Flag 0 (F0), Register bank select (RS1, RS0), Overflow (OV), and Parity (P). - **Stack Pointer (SP) — 8 bits:** Points to the top of the stack. Reset value = `07H`. - **Data Pointer (DPTR) — 16 bits:** Used to access external data memory and lookup tables. Composed of `DPH` (high byte) and `DPL` (low byte). - **Program Counter (PC) — 16 bits:** Holds the address of the next instruction to fetch. Reset value = `0000H`. - **Instruction Register & Decoder:** Latches and decodes each opcode. - **Timing & Control Unit:** Generates all internal and external control signals. **2. Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) (2 marks):** The ALU is an **8-bit arithmetic and logical processing unit**. It supports: - **Arithmetic:** `ADD`, `ADDC`, `SUBB`, `MUL`, `DIV`, `INC`, `DEC`, `DA` (decimal adjust). - **Logic:** `ANL` (AND), `ORL` (OR), `XRL` (XOR), `CPL` (complement), `CLR` (clear). - **Bit manipulation** and **rotate** (`RL`, `RR`, `RLC`, `RRC`). - It updates the **PSW flags** (CY, AC, OV, P) after every operation. **3. Oscillator Circuit (2 marks):** The 8051 contains an **on-chip oscillator** that requires only an **external quartz crystal** and **two 30 pF capacitors** connected between **XTAL1 (pin 19)** and **XTAL2 (pin 18)** to ground. - The oscillator generates a sine wave at the crystal's resonant frequency $f_{osc}$. - This is internally amplified and used as the **master clock** for the entire chip. - **One machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods**, i.e., $$ T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} $$ - For example, with $f_{osc} = 11.0592\text{ MHz}$ (commonly chosen for **9600 baud** serial communication), $T_{MC} \approx 1.085\ \mu s$. --- #### Q.B(b) — Explain the four I/O ports of the 8051. List the alternate functions of Port 3. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]` **Model Solution:** **1. Port 0 (P0) — Pins 32–39 (1.5 marks):** - 8-bit, **bidirectional, open-drain** port. - Has **no internal pull-up resistors**, so external pull-ups (typically 10 kΩ) are required when used as a general-purpose output. - **Alternate function:** Acts as the **multiplexed low-byte address/data bus (AD0–AD7)** when external memory is accessed. The address low byte is latched externally using `ALE` (pin 30). **2. Port 1 (P1) — Pins 1–8 (1 mark):** - 8-bit, **quasi-bidirectional** port with **internal pull-ups**. - Has **no alternate functions** — used purely as general-purpose I/O. - Can drive 4 LS TTL loads. **3. Port 2 (P2) — Pins 21–28 (1 mark):** - 8-bit, **quasi-bidirectional** port with internal pull-ups. - **Alternate function:** Provides the **high-byte of the address bus (A8–A15)** when accessing external memory (>256 bytes). **4. Port 3 (P3) — Pins 10–17 (1.5 marks):** - 8-bit, **quasi-bidirectional** port. - Every pin has a dedicated **alternate function** used when the corresponding bit in the **P3 SFR** is set to 1. **Alternate Functions of Port 3 (2 marks):** | P3 Pin | Pin No. | Alternate Function | Description | |---|---|---|---| | `P3.0` | 10 | `RXD` | Serial data **receive** input | | `P3.1` | 11 | `TXD` | Serial data **transmit** output | | `P3.2` | 12 | `INT0̅` | External interrupt 0 (active low) | | `P3.3` | 13 | `INT1̅` | External interrupt 1 (active low) | | `P3.4` | 14 | `T0` | Timer/Counter 0 external input | | `P3.5` | 15 | `T1` | Timer/Counter 1 external input | | `P3.6` | 16 | `WR̅` | External data memory **write** strobe | | `P3.7` | 17 | `RD̅` | External data memory **read** strobe | --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here!):** > > 1. **Forgetting to mention that P0 is open-drain** and requires external pull-ups. Many students write "P0 is bidirectional" without this critical detail → loses 1 mark. > 2. **Confusing Program Counter width with Data Bus width.** PC is **16-bit**; data bus is **8-bit**. KTU examiners test this distinction routinely. > 3. **Stating "8051 has 4 KB RAM" instead of 4 KB ROM.** RAM is 128 bytes, ROM is 4 KB. This is the single most common factual error. > 4. **Writing "PSW contains 5 flags" — wrong.** PSW has **8 bits** but only **7 are used** (bit 1 is reserved/undefined). Always state this nuance. > 5. **Forgetting the Stack Pointer reset value (07H).** KTU questions on "8051 reset state" almost always ask this. > 6. **Confusing `PSEN̅` with `RD̅`.** `PSEN̅` is for **program memory**, `RD̅` is for **external data memory** — they are *different* signals. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam!** - ☐ **8051** = 8-bit, single-chip, Harvard-architecture CISC microcontroller by **Intel (1980)** - ☐ **40-pin DIP**, **+5 V** supply (Vcc pin 40, GND pin 20) - ☐ **8-bit data bus**, **16-bit address bus** → max **64 KB** addressable memory (per memory space) - ☐ **4 KB on-chip ROM** (`0000H–0FFFH`), **128 B on-chip RAM** (`00H–7FH`) - ☐ **SFR space:** `80H–FFH` — 21 commonly-used SFRs - ☐ **32 I/O lines:** P0 (8), P1 (8), P2 (8), P3 (8) - ☐ **P0 is open-drain** (needs pull-ups); P1, P2, P3 are quasi-bidirectional (internal pull-ups) - ☐ **P3 alternate functions:** RXD, TXD, INT0̅, INT1̅, T0, T1, WR̅, RD̅ - ☐ **CPU sub-blocks:** ALU, A, B, PSW, SP, DPTR, PC, IR, Decoder, Timing & Control - ☐ **PC reset value:** `0000H`; **SP reset value:** `07H` - ☐ **Oscillator pins:** XTAL1 (pin 19), XTAL2 (pin 18) — external crystal 1.2–12 MHz - ☐ **One machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods:** $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ - ☐ At **12 MHz** crystal → **1 µs** machine cycle - ☐ **Two 16-bit Timers/Counters** (T0, T1) — 4 operating modes - ☐ **One full-duplex UART** (Modes 0, 1, 2, 3; Mode 1 most common) - ☐ **5 interrupt sources:** INT0, T0, INT1, T1, Serial - ☐ **Interrupt vector addresses:** `0003H, 000BH, 0013H, 001BH, 0023H` - ☐ **2 priority levels** controlled by `IP` register - ☐ **External control pins:** `ALE` (30), `PSEN̅` (29), `EA̅` (31), `RST` (9) - ☐ **Modified Harvard architecture** — separate *internal* program/data, multiplexed *external* buses - ☐ **Register Banks:** 4 banks × 8 registers (R0–R7) at `00H–1FH`; selected by `PSW[RS1:RS0]` - ☐ **Bit-addressable RAM:** 16 bytes at `20H–2FH` → 128 individually addressable bits - ☐ **Application domains:** consumer electronics, automotive, IoT edge nodes, industrial control, education (Arduino Uno is based on ATmega328P — a modern 8051 descendant)

topics.title: Memory Organization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT503 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Memory Organization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
**Memory Organization** refers to the structural design and hierarchical arrangement of various memory units within a computer system or embedded device, governing how data is stored, addressed, accessed, and transferred between the processor, cache, main memory, and secondary storage. In the context of **IoT microcontrollers and microprocessors**, memory organization defines the address space layout, internal bus architecture, register maps, and the techniques used (such as caching, paging, banking, and interleaving) to optimize the **latency**, **throughput**, and **power consumption** of data retrieval operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 1**
> Memory Organization is a foundational topic that directly enables students to understand how IoT edge devices (ESP32, STM32, Arduino, ARM Cortex-M) manage limited on-chip SRAM, Flash, and external memory expansions using buses such as **AMBA AHB/APB** and **QSPI**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a **large busy library**:
- The librarian's **desk (Registers)** holds the few books currently being read — accessed in **nanoseconds** but only a handful at a time.
- The **reference shelf near the desk (Cache Memory)** holds recently returned popular books — accessed in tens of nanoseconds.
- The **main reading hall stacks (Main Memory / RAM)** hold thousands of books — accessed in hundreds of nanoseconds.
- The **basement archives (Secondary Storage — Flash, SSD, HDD)** hold millions of books — accessed in **milliseconds**, but never lost during a power failure.

The Memory Organization is the **library's entire management system** — its catalog (addressing), checkout rules (caching policy), and floor layout (hierarchy) — all engineered to minimize the time the librarian (CPU) spends waiting.

### 1.3 Key Terminology and Physical Metrics
- **Access Time ($T_a$)**: The time delay between a memory request and the delivery of data, measured in **nanoseconds (ns)**.
- **Cycle Time ($T_c$)**: Minimum time between two successive memory operations.
- **Bandwidth**: Amount of data transferred per unit time, in **MB/s or GB/s**.
- **Hit Ratio ($H$)**: Fraction of memory accesses served from the faster memory, dimensionless between **0 and 1** (often expressed as a percentage).
- **Miss Penalty**: Extra time incurred when data is not found in the faster memory level.
- **Volatility**: Property determining whether memory loses data when power is removed — **volatile (RAM)** vs **non-volatile (Flash, ROM)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Reminder**: For 2024-scheme problems, the formula $T_s = T_1 + (1 - H) \cdot T_2$ is the canonical expression for a **two-level memory average access time**, where $T_s$ is the system access time, $T_1$ is the cache access time, and $T_2$ is the main memory access time.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Memory Hierarchy Pyramid
Modern computing systems (including IoT edge nodes) arrange memory into a strict hierarchy governed by three competing constraints: **speed**, **cost per bit**, and **capacity**.

| Level | Memory Type | Typical Capacity | Access Time | Cost / Bit | Volatility |
|------|------------|------------------|-------------|------------|------------|
| L0 | CPU Registers | 32 – 64 × 8/32/64 bits | **≈ 1 ns** | Very High | Volatile |
| L1 | L1 Cache (on-chip) | 16 KB – 128 KB | **≈ 1 – 3 ns** | High | Volatile |
| L2 | L2 Cache (on-chip) | 256 KB – 8 MB | **≈ 5 – 10 ns** | Medium | Volatile |
| L3 | L3 Cache (shared) | 4 MB – 64 MB | **≈ 10 – 20 ns** | Medium | Volatile |
| L4 | Main Memory (DRAM) | 1 GB – 64 GB | **≈ 50 – 100 ns** | Low | Volatile |
| L5 | SSD / eMMC / Flash | 128 GB – 4 TB | **≈ 50 – 500 µs** | Very Low | Non-volatile |
| L6 | HDD / Cloud Storage | TB – PB scale | **≈ 5 – 10 ms** | Lowest | Non-volatile |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight for IoT**: An ESP32 module has only **520 KB SRAM** and **4 MB Flash**. Memory organization in IoT is therefore a *resource-constrained* problem — cache design is replaced by **DMA (Direct Memory Access)** and **paging from SPI Flash** using techniques like **memory-mapped flash (MMF)** and **PSRAM** expansion.

### 2.2 Classification of Memory
- **Primary Memory** (directly accessed by CPU):
  - **RAM** (Random Access Memory) — Read/Write, Volatile.
    - *SRAM* (Static RAM): Uses 6 transistors per bit, no refresh, fast, expensive.
    - *DRAM* (Dynamic RAM): Uses 1 capacitor + 1 transistor per bit, needs periodic refresh, slower, cheaper.
  - **ROM** (Read-Only Memory) — Non-volatile.
    - *MROM* (Masked ROM), *PROM*, *EPROM*, *EEPROM*, *Flash ROM*.
- **Secondary Memory** — Bulk storage (magnetic, optical, flash).
- **Cache Memory** — High-speed buffer between CPU and main memory.
- **Associative Memory (CAM)** — Content-addressable; used in TLB lookups and networking.
- **Virtual Memory** — Abstraction allowing execution of programs larger than physical RAM.

### 2.3 Cache Memory Organization — The Three Mapping Techniques
A cache is logically divided into **lines (or blocks)**. Each main memory block maps to a cache line according to a specific rule:

1. **Direct Mapping**: Block $j$ of main memory maps to line $(j \bmod N)$ of cache, where $N$ is the number of cache lines. Simple but suffers from conflict misses.
2. **Fully Associative Mapping**: Any main memory block can be placed in any cache line. Requires simultaneous comparison against all tags — implemented using **CAM** (Content Addressable Memory). Eliminates conflict misses but is hardware-expensive.
3. **Set-Associative Mapping** (K-way): Cache is divided into $S$ sets, each holding $K$ lines. Block $j$ maps to set $(j \bmod S)$ and may occupy any of the $K$ lines in that set. This is the **industry-standard compromise** used in modern ARM Cortex cores.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Description |
|-------------|------------------------|-------------|
| Average Access Time (Two-Level) | $T_s = H \cdot T_1 + (1-H) \cdot T_2$ | Weighted average based on hit ratio $H$ |
| Extended Two-Level | $T_s = T_1 + (1-H) \cdot T_2$ | When miss penalty is $T_2$ (not $T_1 + T_2$) |
| Three-Level Memory | $T_s = H_1 T_1 + H_2 T_2 + (1-H_1-H_2) T_3$ | L1, L2, Main memory |
| Memory Capacity | $C = 2^n$ bytes (with $n$ address lines) | $n$-bit address bus |
| Direct Mapping Index | $\text{Index} = j \bmod N$ | $N$ = number of cache lines |
| Number of Tag Bits | $t = n - s - w$ | $n$ = address bits, $s$ = index bits, $w$ = word offset |
| Number of Sets | $S = N / K$ | $K$ = associativity |
| Number of Index Bits | $s = \log_2 S$ | For set-associative |
| Miss Rate | $M = 1 - H$ | Complementary to hit rate |
| Effective CPI | $\text{CPI}_{\text{eff}} = \text{CPI}_{\text{base}} + \text{Memory Stall Cycles}$ | For pipelined analysis |
| Interleaving Banks | Consecutive words assigned to consecutive banks | $n$-way low-order interleaving |
| Power-of-2 Boundary | $2^k$ words → $k$ address bits | Used in all capacity problems |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility**: In IoT firmware (e.g., FreeRTOS on STM32), these formulas determine **DMA burst sizes**, **cache line alignments for XIP (Execute-In-Place)**, and **stack/heap partitioning** in linker scripts (`.ld` files).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) for a Two-Level Hierarchy
**Problem Setup**: Consider a system with an L1 cache of access time $T_1 = 5$ ns and a main memory of access time $T_2 = 100$ ns. The cache hit ratio is $H = 0.90$. Derive the expression for the average memory access time $T_s$.

**Step 1 — Define the Two Outcomes**
On every memory reference, exactly one of two events occurs:
- **Event A (Hit)**: Probability = $H$, Time cost = $T_1$.
- **Event B (Miss)**: Probability = $1 - H$, Time cost = $T_1 + T_2$ (we still pay $T_1$ to discover the miss, then $T_2$ to fetch from main memory).

**Step 2 — Compute the Expected Access Time**
By the law of total expectation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_s &= H \cdot (\text{time on hit}) + (1-H) \cdot (\text{time on miss}) \\
    &= H \cdot T_1 + (1-H) \cdot (T_1 + T_2) \\
    &= T_1 \bigl[H + (1-H)\bigr] + (1-H) \cdot T_2 \\
    &= T_1 + (1-H) \cdot T_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Numerical Values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_s &= 5 + (1 - 0.90) \cdot 100 \\
    &= 5 + 0.10 \cdot 100 \\
    &= 5 + 10 \\
    &= 15 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**: The system appears to behave like a 15 ns memory, which is a **6.67× speedup** over the 100 ns main memory. The miss penalty is effectively amortized because 90% of accesses are caught by the cache.

> [!NOTE]
> If the KTU problem states that the *miss penalty* is $T_2$ (i.e., $T_1$ is *not* paid on a miss), the formula reduces to $T_s = H \cdot T_1 + (1-H) \cdot T_2$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation: Address Partitioning in a Direct-Mapped Cache
**Problem Statement**: A 32-bit system has a main memory of **16 MB** and a **direct-mapped cache of 256 KB** with a **block (line) size of 16 words**, where each word is **4 bytes**. Compute the number of **tag bits**, **index bits**, and **block offset bits** in the physical address.

**Step 1 — Total Physical Address Bits**
The address bus must be wide enough to address 16 MB.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Capacity} &= 16 \text{ MB} = 16 \times 2^{20} = 2^4 \times 2^{20} = 2^{24} \text{ bytes} \\
\Rightarrow n &= 24 \text{ address bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Word Offset Bits ($w$)**
A block contains 16 words, and each word is 4 bytes. The total block size in bytes is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Block size} &= 16 \text{ words} \times 4 \text{ bytes/word} = 64 \text{ bytes} = 2^6 \text{ bytes} \\
\Rightarrow w &= 6 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Index Bits ($s$)**
The cache holds 256 KB of data. Number of blocks in the cache:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of lines } (N) &= \frac{\text{Cache size}}{\text{Block size}} = \frac{256 \text{ KB}}{64 \text{ B}} = \frac{2^{18}}{2^6} = 2^{12} \\
\Rightarrow s &= 12 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Tag Bits ($t$)**
By conservation of address bits:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= n - s - w \\
  &= 24 - 12 - 6 \\
  &= 6 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Sanity Check & Summary Table**

| Field | Tag | Index (Line #) | Block Offset |
|------|-----|---------------|--------------|
| Bit Width | 6 | 12 | 6 |
| Addresses Bits | 23 – 18 | 17 – 6 | 5 – 0 |
| Function | Identifies which MM block is resident | Selects the cache line | Selects the byte inside the block |

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Techniques — Side-by-Side Comparison

| Parameter | Direct Mapping | Fully Associative | K-way Set Associative |
|----------|---------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| **Placement rule** | Fixed line $(j \bmod N)$ | Any line | Set $(j \bmod S)$, any of $K$ lines |
| **Hardware complexity** | Lowest | Highest | Moderate |
| **Comparison logic** | 1 comparator | $N$ comparators (CAM) | $K$ comparators per set |
| **Conflict misses** | Many | None | Reduced by factor $K$ |
| **Capacity misses** | Same | Same | Same |
| **Tag bits** | $n - \log_2 N$ | $n$ (full tag) | $n - \log_2 S$ |
| **Replacement policy** | None (forced) | LRU / FIFO / Random | LRU / Pseudo-LRU within set |
| **Used in** | Older x86 L1, simple MCUs | TLB, small fully-assoc caches | Modern ARM Cortex, Intel Core |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall**: In a 4-way set-associative cache of 64 KB with 32-byte blocks, students often confuse the number of sets with the number of lines. The number of *lines* is $64 \text{ KB} / 32 \text{ B} = 2048$, and the number of *sets* is $2048 / 4 = 512$. The index field then uses $\log_2 512 = 9$ bits, **not** $\log_2 2048 = 11$ bits.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Speedup Due to Cache
**Setup**: $T_m = 100$ ns (main memory), $T_c = 5$ ns (cache), $H = 0.95$.

**Step 1 — Compute AMAT**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_s &= T_c + (1-H) \cdot T_m \\
    &= 5 + (1 - 0.95) \cdot 100 \\
    &= 5 + 5 = 10 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Speedup**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{T_m}{T_s} = \frac{100}{10} = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation**: With a 95% hit rate, the cache delivers a **10× effective speedup**, even though the cache itself is 20× faster than main memory. This demonstrates the *non-linear* benefit of caching in IoT edge processing.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Memory Hierarchy Pyramid (Functional Block Architecture)
The following Mermaid diagram presents a *Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow* of the memory hierarchy, illustrating the data path, control signals, and the role of the **Memory Management Unit (MMU)** or **Memory Protection Unit (MPU)** in IoT microcontrollers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cpuCore[CPU Core] -->|Fetch Decode Execute| mmuCtrl[MMU MPU Controller]
    mmuCtrl -->|Virtual to Physical Address Translation| tlbCache[TLB Translation Lookaside Buffer]
    tlbCache -->|Cache Hit Fast Path| l1Cache[L1 Cache SRAM 32 KB]
    l1Cache -->|On Miss Lookup| l2Cache[L2 Cache SRAM 256 KB]
    l2Cache -->|On Miss Transfer Block| mainRAM[Main Memory DRAM 512 MB]
    mainRAM -->|DMA Channel 1| dmaCtrl[DMA Controller]
    mainRAM -->|Paged From| flashStorage[Flash Memory 16 MB Non Volatile]
    flashStorage -->|SPI QSPI Bus| externalFlash[External SPI Flash 64 MB]
    dmaCtrl -->|Block Transfer 64 bytes| ioPeripherals[IO Peripherals UART SPI I2C ADC]
    mainRAM -->|Refresh Signal Every 64 ms| refreshLogic[DRAM Refresh Logic]
    l1Cache -->|Write Back Policy| writeBuffer[Write Buffer 8 entries]
    writeBuffer -->|Coalesced Write| mainRAM
    classDef fast fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mid fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef slow fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class cpuCore,mmuCtrl,tlbCache,l1Cache,writeBuffer fast
    class l2Cache,dmaCtrl,refreshLogic mid
    class mainRAM,flashStorage,externalFlash,ioPeripherals slow
```

### 4.2 Direct-Mapped Cache Address Decomposition (Schematic Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Address 32 bits] --> B[Tag t bits]
    A --> C[Index s bits]
    A --> D[Block Offset w bits]
    C --> E[Cache Line Selection 0 to 2 power s minus 1]
    E --> F{Tag Comparison with Stored Tag}
    B --> F
    F -->|Match| G[Cache Hit Output 1 of 2 power w bytes]
    F -->|Mismatch| H[Cache Miss Fetch Block from Main Memory]
    H --> I[Update Cache Line with New Tag and Data]
    I --> G
    classDef hit fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef miss fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class G hit
    class H,I miss
```

### 4.3 Memory Interleaving — 4-Way Low-Order Interleaving Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    addrBus[Address Bus A0 to A31] --> decoder[Low Order 2 Bits Decoder]
    decoder -->|00| bank0[Memory Bank 0]
    decoder -->|01| bank1[Memory Bank 1]
    decoder -->|10| bank2[Memory Bank 2]
    decoder -->|11| bank3[Memory Bank 3]
    bank0 --> mux[Output Multiplexer]
    bank1 --> mux
    bank2 --> mux
    bank3 --> mux
    mux --> dataBus[Data Bus to CPU]
    classDef bank fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class bank0,bank1,bank2,bank3 bank
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Interleave?** Sequential word addresses (0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 …) are distributed across 4 banks. The CPU can read word 0 from bank 0 *and simultaneously* initiate word 1 from bank 1, effectively overlapping access times and boosting **bandwidth by 4×** for sequential access patterns — a critical optimization for instruction fetch in microcontrollers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`
**Differentiate between SRAM and DRAM with respect to construction, speed, refresh requirement, and typical use case.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:

| Feature | SRAM (Static RAM) | DRAM (Dynamic RAM) |
|--------|------------------|--------------------|
| **Basic cell** | 6 transistors (flip-flop) | 1 transistor + 1 capacitor |
| **Refresh** | **Not required** | **Required every ~64 ms** |
| **Speed (Access time)** | Faster (≈ 1–10 ns) | Slower (≈ 50–100 ns) |
| **Density / Cost per bit** | Lower density, **expensive** | Higher density, **cheaper** |
| **Power consumption** | Higher (continuous current) | Lower in standby |
| **Typical use** | **Cache memory**, registers | **Main memory** (PC RAM, mobile DDR) |

**Conclusion**: SRAM is used where speed is paramount (cache); DRAM is used where capacity and cost dominate (main memory).

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`
**What is virtual memory? Why is it important in modern operating systems, and how does the MMU participate in its operation?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks)**:
Virtual memory is a memory management technique that creates an **illusion of a large, contiguous address space** for each process, even when the physical RAM is smaller than the program's working set.

- It allows programs **larger than physical RAM** to execute by **paging** or **segmentation**.
- The **MMU (Memory Management Unit)** translates **virtual addresses** issued by the CPU into **physical addresses** in main memory, using page tables (often cached in the **TLB** for speed).
- It provides **process isolation**, **memory protection**, and efficient **multi-programming**, which is essential for OS-level multitasking in modern embedded Linux (used in gateways, Raspberry Pi IoT nodes).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **Module Choice Declaration**: From Module 1, the student is offered a choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. Both questions carry **14 marks** and consist of two sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Module 1]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Consider a computer system with a **cache of 32 KB**, **main memory of 1 MB**, and a **block size of 16 bytes**. The CPU generates **32-bit addresses**. Assuming **direct-mapped** cache:
**(i)** Calculate the number of **tag bits**, **index bits**, and **block offset bits**.
**(ii)** Determine the number of **bits required to build the tag directory** (i.e., the total tag storage in bits).

**(b) [7 Marks]** A system has an L1 cache access time of **10 ns** and a main memory access time of **200 ns**. The hit ratio is **0.92**.
**(i)** Compute the **average memory access time (AMAT)**.
**(ii)** If a new **L2 cache of 20 ns** is inserted with a **global hit ratio of 0.96** at L2 (i.e., L1 misses served by L2 with $H_2 = 0.96$), recompute AMAT and discuss the **speedup**.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**Part (a) — Address Partitioning**

**Step 1 — Physical address width (n)**
Main memory = 1 MB = $2^{20}$ bytes. So $n = 20$ bits.

**Step 2 — Block offset bits (w)**
Block size = 16 bytes = $2^4$ bytes. So $w = 4$ bits.

**Step 3 — Number of cache lines and index bits (s)**
Number of lines = $32 \text{ KB} / 16 \text{ B} = 2^{15} / 2^4 = 2^{11}$. So $s = 11$ bits.

**Step 4 — Tag bits (t)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= n - s - w = 20 - 11 - 4 = 5 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Tag directory size**
The tag directory stores one tag per cache line. Number of entries = $2^{11} = 2048$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total tag storage} &= 2048 \times 5 \text{ bits} = 10{,}240 \text{ bits} \approx 1.25 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Field | Tag | Index | Block Offset |
|------|-----|-------|--------------|
| Width | 5 bits | 11 bits | 4 bits |

---

**Part (b) — AMAT Calculations**

**Step 1 — AMAT for single-level cache**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_s &= T_1 + (1 - H) \cdot T_2 \\
    &= 10 + (1 - 0.92) \cdot 200 \\
    &= 10 + 0.08 \cdot 200 \\
    &= 10 + 16 = 26 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — AMAT for two-level (L1 + L2 + Main)**
Given: $T_1 = 10$ ns, $T_2 = 20$ ns, $T_m = 200$ ns, $H_1 = 0.92$ (L1 hit rate), $H_2 = 0.96$ (L2 hit rate *given L1 missed*).

For hierarchical caches, AMAT is computed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_s &= T_1 + (1 - H_1) \cdot \bigl[T_2 + (1 - H_2) \cdot T_m\bigr] \\
    &= 10 + (1 - 0.92) \cdot \bigl[20 + (1 - 0.96) \cdot 200\bigr] \\
    &= 10 + 0.08 \cdot \bigl[20 + 0.04 \cdot 200\bigr] \\
    &= 10 + 0.08 \cdot \bigl[20 + 8\bigr] \\
    &= 10 + 0.08 \cdot 28 \\
    &= 10 + 2.24 \\
    &= 12.24 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Speedup**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{26}{12.24} \approx 2.12
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion**: Adding an L2 cache reduces AMAT from 26 ns to 12.24 ns, yielding a **2.12× speedup** over the single-level cache design. This is the rationale behind the multi-level cache hierarchies in modern microcontrollers such as the **ARM Cortex-M7**, which features an L1 + optional L2 architecture.

---

### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Module 1]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the three cache mapping techniques — **Direct, Fully Associative, and K-way Set Associative** — with neat block diagrams (described in words) and a comparative table.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A computer has a **4-way set-associative cache** of **64 KB**, with a **block size of 32 bytes**. The CPU address is **32 bits**.
**(i)** Find the number of **sets**, **index bits**, **block offset bits**, and **tag bits**.
**(ii)** If the main memory access time is **80 ns** and the cache access time is **8 ns**, with a **hit ratio of 0.94**, compute the AMAT and effective speedup.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**Part (a) — Cache Mapping Techniques**

1. **Direct Mapping**: Main memory block $j$ maps to cache line $(j \bmod N)$. Simple hardware: one comparator per line. Suffers from high conflict misses.

2. **Fully Associative Mapping**: Any block can go into any line. Uses **Content Addressable Memory (CAM)** with $N$ parallel comparators. Best hit rate but most expensive.

3. **K-way Set Associative Mapping**: Cache divided into $S$ sets, each with $K$ lines. Block $j$ goes to set $(j \bmod S)$, occupying any of the $K$ ways. Industry-standard trade-off (used in all modern CPUs).

**Comparison Table**:

| Property | Direct | Fully Associative | K-way Set Associative |
|----------|--------|-------------------|------------------------|
| Placement | Fixed | Any | Within a set |
| # Comparators | 1 | N | K per set |
| Conflict misses | High | None | Reduced |
| Hardware cost | Low | High | Moderate |
| Used in | Simple MCUs | TLB | Modern ARM, x86 |

---

**Part (b) — Set-Associative Cache Parameters**

**Step 1 — Total address bits (n)** = 32 (given).

**Step 2 — Block offset bits (w)**
Block size = 32 bytes = $2^5$ bytes → $w = 5$ bits.

**Step 3 — Total cache lines**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lines} &= \frac{64 \text{ KB}}{32 \text{ B}} = \frac{2^{16}}{2^5} = 2^{11} = 2048
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Number of sets (associativity K = 4)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{\text{Lines}}{K} = \frac{2048}{4} = 512 = 2^9 \\
\Rightarrow s &= 9 \text{ index bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Tag bits (t)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= n - s - w = 32 - 9 - 5 = 18 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — AMAT Calculation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_s &= T_c + (1-H) \cdot T_m \\
    &= 8 + (1 - 0.94) \cdot 80 \\
    &= 8 + 0.06 \cdot 80 \\
    &= 8 + 4.8 = 12.8 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Speedup**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{T_m}{T_s} = \frac{80}{12.8} = 6.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Summary Table**:

| Field | Tag | Index (Set #) | Block Offset |
|------|-----|---------------|--------------|
| Width | 18 bits | 9 bits | 5 bits |
| Range | 0 to $2^{18}-1$ | 0 to 511 | 0 to 31 |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost**
> 1. **Confusing *miss penalty* and *miss time***: The formula $T_s = H \cdot T_1 + (1-H) \cdot T_2$ is used when the miss *replaces* the cache access; the formula $T_s = T_1 + (1-H) \cdot T_2$ is used when the miss is in *addition* to it. **Read the problem statement carefully.**
> 2. **In set-associative caches, students forget to divide the number of lines by $K$** before computing the index bits, leading to off-by-$K$ errors.
> 3. **Forgetting to multiply the tag storage** by the number of cache lines in tag-directory sizing problems.
> 4. **Using non-power-of-2 numbers**: Cache sizes, block sizes, and memory capacities are *always* powers of 2 in KTU problems. If a student gets a non-integer bit count, the parameters have been misread.
> 5. **Confusing the block offset (byte-level) with the word offset** when the problem specifies "X words per block of Y bytes" — always reduce to bytes first.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memory Organization**

- **Memory Hierarchy**: Registers → L1 Cache → L2 Cache → L3 Cache → Main Memory (DRAM) → Secondary Storage (Flash/SSD) → Tertiary. Each step down: **slower, larger, cheaper per bit**.
- **SRAM vs DRAM**: SRAM = 6T, no refresh, fast, used in **cache**. DRAM = 1T + 1 cap, needs **periodic refresh** (≈ 64 ms), used in **main memory**.
- **ROM Variants**: MROM → PROM → EPROM → EEPROM → **Flash**. All non-volatile; Flash is the dominant choice in IoT.
- **Cache Mapping**:
  - *Direct*: index = $j \bmod N$, tag bits = $n - \log_2 N - w$.
  - *Fully Associative*: any line, tag = full address minus offset.
  - *K-way Set Associative*: index = $j \bmod (N/K)$, tag = $n - \log_2(N/K) - w$.
- **AMAT (Two-Level)**: $T_s = T_1 + (1-H) \cdot T_2$. **Three-Level**: $T_s = T_1 + (1-H_1)\bigl[T_2 + (1-H_2) T_3\bigr]$.
- **Speedup**: $S = T_m / T_s$ (where $T_s$ is the *system* AMAT).
- **Memory Interleaving**: Distributes consecutive addresses across $k$ banks → boosts sequential bandwidth by $k$×.
- **Virtual Memory**: Requires **MMU + TLB + Page Table**; provides illusion of large RAM, isolation, and protection.
- **Address Bits Invariant**: $n = t + s + w$ for direct-mapped; $n = t + s + w$ for set-associative (with $s = \log_2 S$, $S = N/K$).
- **IoT Context (KTU 2024 emphasis)**: Embedded MCUs (ARM Cortex-M, ESP32, STM32) use **memory-mapped Flash**, **DMA**, **PSRAM expansion**, and **QSPI XIP (Execute-In-Place)** instead of bulky DRAM hierarchies.
- **Power-of-2 Discipline**: All capacities, offsets, and indices are powers of 2. Any non-integer bit result = a setup error.
- **Units**: Access times in **ns**, capacity in **bytes/KB/MB/GB**, hit ratio as a fraction between 0 and 1 (or %).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Timer and Counters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT503 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Timer and Counters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Timer)**: A *Timer* in a microcontroller is a hardware peripheral that generates precise time delays by counting internal clock pulses derived from the system oscillator. Each count corresponds to one **machine cycle**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Counter)**: A *Counter* is essentially the same hardware peripheral, but instead of counting internal oscillator pulses, it counts **external events** applied to dedicated input pins.

In the 8051 family of microcontrollers (which is the canonical reference for KTU PCCGT503 Module 1), there are **two 16-bit Timer/Counter registers** named **Timer 0** and **Timer 1**. Each timer is physically composed of two 8-bit Special Function Registers (SFRs):

| Timer | High Byte Register | Low Byte Register | External Pin |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Timer 0** | **TH0** | **TL0** | **P3.4 (T0)** |
| **Timer 1** | **TH1** | **TL1** | **P3.5 (T1)** |

The high and low bytes together form a 16-bit counter that **increments by 1** on every count pulse until it **overflows** from `FFFFH` to `0000H`, at which point the associated **Timer Flag (TFx)** in the TCON register is set to logic 1.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Kitchen Stopwatch" Model)

Imagine a **mechanical egg-timer** sitting on a kitchen counter:

- The **spring tension knob** that you wind up is the **Initial Value (THx:TLx)** you load into the timer registers. A small wind = large delay, a big wind = small delay.
- The **ticking mechanism inside** is the **internal oscillator clock** (in Timer mode) or the **door-bell button** (in Counter mode). Each tick or each press = one count.
- The **bell that rings when the sand runs out** is the **Timer Overflow Flag (TFx)**, telling the CPU "Time's up!"
- The **start/stop lever on the side** is the **TRx (Timer Run) control bit** in the TCON register.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight**: The same hardware block serves as BOTH a Timer AND a Counter. The selection is done via **one single bit** in the TMOD register, namely the **C/T bit** (Counter/Timer select bit).

## 1.3 Why Timers/Counters are Critical for IoT

In IoT embedded systems, timers are indispensable for:
1. **Generating precise delays** (debouncing switches, blinking LEDs at fixed intervals).
2. **Producing Baud Rates** for serial UART communication (Timer 1 is the standard baud-rate generator in the 8051).
3. **Sampling sensors** at fixed periodic intervals.
4. **Generating PWM signals** for motor speed control, LED dimming, and servo control.
5. **Counting external events** (e.g., pulses from a flow meter, revolutions of a wheel, people passing through an IR sensor).
6. **Implementing Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS)** with task scheduling.

## 1.4 Operational Modes at a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 8051 supports **4 operating modes (Mode 0, 1, 2, 3)**. Mode 1 (16-bit) and Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload) are the most frequently asked in KTU exams.

| Mode | Size | Auto-Reload? | Common Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mode 0** | 13-bit | ❌ No | Legacy 8048 compatibility (rarely used) |
| **Mode 1** | 16-bit | ❌ No | Long, variable delays (most common) |
| **Mode 2** | 8-bit | ✅ Yes (THx only) | Serial UART baud-rate generation |
| **Mode 3** | 8-bit × 2 | ❌ No | Split Timer (Timer 0 only) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Control Registers: TMOD and TCON

### A) TMOD Register — Timer MODE Register (Address: 89H, Not Bit-Addressable)

TMOD configures the **MODE** and **OPERATION TYPE** of both timers. It is an 8-bit register divided into two identical 4-bit nibbles:

```
  Bit 7    Bit 6    Bit 5    Bit 4   |  Bit 3    Bit 2    Bit 1    Bit 0
  ─────── TIMER 1 ───────             |  ─────── TIMER 0 ───────
   GATE     C/T      M1      M0      |   GATE     C/T      M1      M0
```

| Bit | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **GATE** | Gating Control | When **GATE = 1**, timer runs only when TRx = 1 **AND** the external INTx pin is HIGH. Used for pulse-width measurement. When **GATE = 0**, timer runs whenever TRx = 1 (software control). |
| **C/T** | Counter / Timer Select | **C/T = 0** → **Timer mode** (internal oscillator). **C/T = 1** → **Counter mode** (external pin T0/T1). |
| **M1, M0** | Mode Selector | (0,0) = Mode 0, (0,1) = Mode 1, (1,0) = Mode 2, (1,1) = Mode 3. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Default TMOD value on RESET = 00H**, meaning both timers are in Mode 0 (13-bit) and gated by software only (GATE=0, C/T=0).

### B) TCON Register — Timer CONtrol Register (Address: 88H, Bit-Addressable)

```
  Bit 7    Bit 6    Bit 5    Bit 4   |  Bit 3    Bit 2    Bit 1    Bit 0
  ─────── TIMER FLAGS ──────         |  ─────── INT FLAGS ───────
   TF1      TR1      TF0     TR0     |   IE1      IT1      IE0      IT0
```

| Bit | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **TF1, TF0** | Timer Overflow Flag | **Set to 1 automatically by hardware** when the corresponding timer overflows. Must be **cleared by software**. |
| **TR1, TR0** | Timer Run Control | **TRx = 1** → Start the timer. **TRx = 0** → Stop the timer. Controlled by software. |
| **IE1, IE0** | External Interrupt Edge Flag | Set when an external interrupt is detected. |
| **IT1, IT0** | Interrupt Type Control | **ITx = 1** → Falling-edge triggered. **ITx = 0** → Low-level triggered. |

## 2.2 Clock Source Architecture

The clock source for the timers depends on the mode selected by the C/T bit:

**🔹 Timer Mode (C/T = 0):**
The internal oscillator frequency is divided by **12** before being fed to the timer. This divided clock is the **machine cycle clock**.

$$f_{timer} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12}$$

$$T_{machine\_cycle} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{1}{f_{timer}}$$

**🔹 Counter Mode (C/T = 1):**
The timer counts **external pulses** applied to pin **P3.4 (for T0)** or **P3.5 (for T1)**. The maximum external count frequency is $f_{osc}/24$ (i.e., the external pulse must be at least **2 machine cycles wide** to be reliably detected).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake**: Students often forget that the input pin must remain HIGH for at least 1 machine cycle and LOW for at least 1 machine cycle. Sampling happens once every machine cycle.

## 2.3 The Core Delay Formulas (KTU Formula Sheet)

The timer is essentially a "down-counted" register — we **preload** it with a value, and it counts UP to overflow. The **delay** is the time it takes to roll over from the preload to 0.

### For Mode 1 (16-bit Timer, Range: 0000H to FFFFH):

$$\text{Delay} = (65536 - X_{initial}) \times T_{machine\_cycle}$$

Solving for the initial value (this is the **most common KTU question**):

$$X_{initial} = 65536 - \frac{\text{Delay}}{T_{machine\_cycle}} = 65536 - \frac{\text{Delay} \times f_{osc}}{12}$$

### For Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload, Range: 00H to FFH):

$$\text{Delay} = (256 - X_{initial}) \times T_{machine\_cycle}$$

$$X_{initial} = 256 - \frac{\text{Delay}}{T_{machine\_cycle}}$$

The 8-bit reload value is loaded into **THx** while **TLx** performs the actual counting. When TLx overflows, THx's value is **automatically copied** into TLx — that's the "auto-reload" feature.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Table

| Parameter | Mode 0 (13-bit) | Mode 1 (16-bit) | Mode 2 (8-bit AR) | Mode 3 (Split) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Counter Size** | 13 bits (8192 counts) | 16 bits (65536 counts) | 8 bits (256 counts) | 8+8 bits |
| **Max Delay** | $8192 \times T_{mc}$ | $65536 \times T_{mc}$ | $256 \times T_{mc}$ | $256 \times T_{mc}$ (each) |
| **Reload?** | Manual | Manual | Automatic (THx → TLx) | Manual |
| **Initial Value Formula** | $X = 8192 - \dfrac{D}{T_{mc}}$ | $X = 65536 - \dfrac{D}{T_{mc}}$ | $X = 256 - \dfrac{D}{T_{mc}}$ | Each half: 8-bit |
| **Where to load** | THx(5 bits) + TLx(8 bits) | THx(8 bits) + TLx(8 bits) | THx holds reload, TLx counts | TH0/TL0 each 8-bit |
| **Typical Use** | Legacy 8048 | Long variable delays | UART baud rates | Extra 8-bit timer |

Where $T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$ is the **machine cycle time** (e.g., for 11.0592 MHz crystal, $T_{mc} = 12/11.0592 \times 10^6 \approx 1.085~\mu s$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crystal Frequency Note**: For UART baud-rate generation, the **11.0592 MHz** crystal is preferred over 12 MHz because it produces exact integer division for standard baud rates (9600, 4800, 1200). At 11.0592 MHz: $T_{mc} = 1.085~\mu s$, and Timer 1 reload value for 9600 baud = 253 (-3 in 2's complement).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Solved Problem 1: Calculating Timer Initial Value (Mode 1)

> **Problem (KTU Standard)**: An 8051 system uses an **11.0592 MHz** crystal. Write a C program to generate a delay of **50 ms** using **Timer 0 in Mode 1**. Calculate the values to be loaded into TH0 and TL0.

### Step 1: Compute the Machine Cycle Time

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6}$$

$$T_{mc} = 1.085~\mu s$$

### Step 2: Compute the Number of Timer Counts Required

$$N_{counts} = \frac{\text{Desired Delay}}{T_{mc}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$N_{counts} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 46{,}082.94$$

Since the count must be an integer, we round **down** to **46,082** counts. (This gives a slightly shorter delay of $46{,}082 \times 1.085~\mu s = 49.999~\text{ms} \approx 50~\text{ms}$.)

### Step 3: Compute the Initial 16-bit Timer Value

$$X_{initial} = 65536 - N_{counts} = 65536 - 46082$$

$$X_{initial} = 19{,}454 \quad (\text{in decimal})$$

### Step 4: Convert to Hexadecimal and Split into TH0 and TL0

$$19{,}454_{10} = 4BFE_{16}$$

Verifying the hex conversion:

$$\begin{aligned}
19{,}454 \div 4096 &= 4 \quad \text{remainder} \quad 19{,}454 - 16{,}384 = 3{,}070 \\
3{,}070 \div 256 &= 11 \;(= B) \quad \text{remainder} \quad 3{,}070 - 2{,}816 = 254 \;(= FE) \\
\text{Therefore:} \quad 19{,}454_{10} &= 4BFE_{16}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final register split:**
- **TH0 = 0x4B** (high byte)
- **TL0 = 0xFE** (low byte)

### Step 5: Complete 8051 C Program (Keil/SDCC Syntax)

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void Timer0_Delay_50ms(void) {
    TMOD = 0x01;     // Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit), software gated, timer mode
    TH0  = 0x4B;     // Load high byte
    TL0  = 0xFE;     // Load low byte
    TR0  = 1;        // Start Timer 0
    
    while (TF0 == 0); // Wait here until overflow flag is set
    
    TR0  = 0;        // Stop Timer 0
    TF0  = 0;        // Manually clear overflow flag
}

void main(void) {
    while (1) {
        P1 = 0xFF;   // LED ON
        Timer0_Delay_50ms();
        P1 = 0x00;   // LED OFF
        Timer0_Delay_50ms();
    }
}
```

## 3.2 Solved Problem 2: Auto-Reload Mode (Mode 2) Calculation

> **Problem**: Generate a delay of **200 µs** using Timer 1 in **Mode 2 (auto-reload)** with the same 11.0592 MHz crystal.

### Step 1: Calculate Required Counts

$$N_{counts} = \frac{200 \times 10^{-6}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 184.33$$

Round down to **184 counts** (small error of ~$0.33 \times 1.085 \approx 0.36~\mu s$).

### Step 2: Compute 8-bit Initial Value

$$X_{initial} = 256 - 184 = 72_{10} = 48_{16}$$

### Step 3: Register Assignment

- **TH1 = 0x48** ← Holds the reload value permanently
- **TL1 = 0x48** ← Initial count starts here, but auto-reloads from TH1 on every overflow

> [!NOTE]
> In Mode 2, only **TH1** is loaded once. **TL1** should be initialized to the same value (or 0) at the start, and the hardware automatically copies TH1 → TL1 on every overflow, giving a **continuous, jitter-free** periodic interrupt or delay.

## 3.3 Solved Problem 3: Counter Mode Application

> **Problem**: A bottle-filling machine drops one bottle past an IR sensor every time a pulse is generated. Connect the IR sensor output to **P3.4 (T0 pin)**. Configure the 8051 to count **100 bottles** and then stop the conveyor (assume conveyor control is at P1.0).

### Configuration:
- **C/T bit for Timer 0 = 1** (Counter mode)
- **Mode 1 (16-bit)** for largest count range
- **Initial value** so that the timer overflows on the 100th pulse:
  $$X_{initial} = 65536 - 100 = 65436 = FF9C_{16}$$
- TH0 = 0xFF, TL0 = 0x9C
- GATE = 0 (software start/stop only)

### C Program:

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit CONVEYOR = P1^0;  // Conveyor motor control

void main(void) {
    TMOD = 0x05;     // Timer 0: C/T=1 (Counter), M1M0=01 (Mode 1), GATE=0
    TH0  = 0xFF;
    TL0  = 0x9C;
    CONVEYOR = 1;    // Start conveyor
    TR0  = 1;        // Start counting external pulses on T0 pin
    
    while (TF0 == 0);  // Wait until 100 pulses counted
    
    TR0  = 0;          // Stop counter
    CONVEYOR = 0;      // Stop conveyor
    TF0  = 0;          // Clear flag
    while(1);          // Done
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TMOD = 0x05** is the key: binary `0000 0101` → Timer 0 in Mode 1, C/T=1 (counter), GATE=0. Timer 1 remains at Mode 0 but is unused.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Mode Should I Use?"

| Requirement | Recommended Mode | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Single, one-shot delay (e.g., 500 ms blink) | **Mode 1 (16-bit)** | Largest range; software reloads each time |
| Continuous periodic delay (e.g., UART tick) | **Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload)** | No software overhead between overflows |
| Counting more than 256 events | **Mode 1 (16-bit)** | 65,536 max count |
| Need two independent 8-bit timers from Timer 0 | **Mode 3 (Split)** | TL0 + TH0 act as two separate 8-bit timers |
| Legacy 8048 hardware emulation | **Mode 0 (13-bit)** | Backward compatibility only |
| Gating by an external pin (pulse width measurement) | **Mode 1 + GATE=1** | Timer runs only while INTx pin is HIGH |

## 3.5 Common KTU Pitfalls in Numerical Problems

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1**: Forgetting to **convert crystal frequency** to **machine cycle time**. Always divide by 12 first.
>
> **Pitfall 2**: Mixing up **THx** (high byte) and **TLx** (low byte). If value is `0x4BFE`, then THx = 0x4B, TLx = 0xFE (NOT the other way around).
>
> **Pitfall 3**: Forgetting to **manually clear the TFx flag** in Mode 0/1. Hardware only sets it.
>
> **Pitfall 4**: In **Mode 2**, students often forget to set **both THx and TLx** initially, but only THx needs the reload value at the start.
>
> **Pitfall 5**: Using the **wrong hex to decimal conversion** — always double-check with a calculator.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of 8051 Timer/Counter Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OSC["Crystal Oscillator f_osc"] --> DIV["Divide by 12 Prescaler"]
    DIV --> MC["Machine Cycle Clock T_mc"]
    MC --> MUX{"MUX C/T bit = 0?"}

    EXT["External Pin P3.4 T0 or P3.5 T1"] --> MUX

    MUX -->|"Yes Timer"| CNT["16-bit Up Counter THx TLx"]
    MUX -->|"No Counter"| CNT

    CNT -->|"Overflow FFFF to 0000"| OVF["Overflow Detector"]
    OVF -->|"Sets flag"| TF["TFx in TCON register"]
    TF -->|"Triggers interrupt if enabled"| ISR["Timer ISR"]

    GATE["GATE bit + INTx pin"] --> AND["AND gate"]
    TR["TRx bit in TCON"] --> AND
    AND -->|"Enable signal"| CNT

    TMOD_CFG["TMOD Register GATE C/T M1 M0"] -.->|"Configures"| MUX
    TMOD_CFG -.->|"Configures"| GATE
    TMOD_CFG -.->|"Sets mode 0 1 2 3"| CNT

    TF -->|"Software clears"| CLR["User clears TFx = 0"]
```

## 4.2 Mode 1 (16-bit) and Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Mode1["Mode 1 Sixteen Bit Timer"]
        TH1_16["TH0 8 bits"] --- TL1_16["TL0 8 bits"]
        TH1_16 --> OV1["Overflow at FFFF"]
        TL1_16 --> OV1
        OV1 --> TF1_OUT["Sets TF0"]
    end

    subgraph Mode2["Mode 2 Eight Bit Auto Reload"]
        TH2_AR["TH0 8 bit reload register"]
        TL2_AR["TL0 8 bit active counter"]
        TH2_AR -->|"Auto copy on overflow"| TL2_AR
        TL2_AR --> OV2["Overflow at FF"]
        OV2 --> TF2_OUT["Sets TF0"]
        OV2 -->|"Reloads from TH0"| TL2_AR
    end
```

## 4.3 Timer Programming Sequence (Software Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Step 1 Configure TMOD register mode and C/T bit"] --> B["Step 2 Load initial value into THx and TLx"]
    B --> C["Step 3 Set TRx = 1 to start the timer"]
    C --> D{"Step 4 Monitor TFx in a loop or use interrupt"}

    D -->|"TFx = 0 keep counting"| D
    D -->|"TFx = 1 overflow occurred"| E["Step 5 Stop timer TRx = 0"]
    E --> F["Step 6 Clear the flag TFx = 0"]
    F --> G{"Step 7 Repeat?"}
    G -->|"Yes repeat delay"| B
    G -->|"No continue main program"| H["Step 8 Return to main loop"]
```
