# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Timer mode and Counter mode of the 8051 microcontroller. Which SFR bit is used to switch between them?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Timer Mode | Counter Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Clock Source** | Internal oscillator (divided by 12) | External pin T0 (P3.4) or T1 (P3.5) |
| **Counting** | Machine cycles (time) | External events (pulses) |
| **C/T Bit in TMOD** | C/T = 0 | C/T = 1 |
| **Application** | Generating delays, baud rates | Counting events (rotations, pulses) |
| **Max Frequency** | Fixed by oscillator | Limited to $f_{osc}/24$ |

The switching is controlled by the **C/T bit** in the **TMOD register** (bit 6 for Timer 1, bit 2 for Timer 0).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List the four operating modes of Timer 0 in the 8051. Which mode is used for UART baud rate generation and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four operating modes are:
1. **Mode 0** — 13-bit timer (8048 legacy compatibility)
2. **Mode 1** — 16-bit timer (range 0000H to FFFFH)
3. **Mode 2** — 8-bit auto-reload timer
4. **Mode 3** — Split timer (Timer 0 only; TL0 + TH0 as two 8-bit timers)

**Mode 2 is used for UART baud rate generation** because:
- It provides an **automatic hardware reload** of the 8-bit value from THx into TLx on every overflow.
- This ensures **continuous, jitter-free periodic timing** with no software overhead between counts, which is critical for accurate serial bit timing.
- Reloading THx once avoids timing errors caused by software latency.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Timer/Counter logic** of the 8051 microcontroller. Describe the role of the **TMOD and TCON registers** in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An 8051 system uses a **12 MHz crystal**. Write an 8051 C program to generate a delay of exactly **2 ms** using **Timer 1 in Mode 1**. Compute the values to be loaded into TH1 and TL1. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📘 MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A:

### Part (a) — Solution (7 marks):

**Block Diagram (1 mark)**:

```
   ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │             8051 TIMER/COUNTER BLOCK         │
   │                                              │
   │   f_osc → ÷12 → MUX ←── External Pin (T0/T1)│
   │              ↓                               │
   │         [C/T bit]                            │
   │              ↓                               │
   │      ┌──────────────┐                        │
   │      │ 16-bit Counter│                        │
   │      │  THx  │  TLx │                        │
   │      └──────┬───────┘                        │
   │             ↓ (overflow)                     │
   │      ┌──────────────┐                        │
   │      │  TFx flag in │                        │
   │      │  TCON reg.   │                        │
   │      └──────────────┘                        │
   │                                              │
   │   GATE/INTx ──┐                              │
   │               ├──AND──> Run Control          │
   │   TRx ────────┘                              │
   └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Explanation (6 marks):**

1. **Clock Source Selection** (1 mark): The C/T bit in TMOD determines the source. When C/T = 0, the timer is driven by the internal machine cycle clock (oscillator ÷ 12). When C/T = 1, it counts external pulses on pin T0 (P3.4) or T1 (P3.5).

2. **Counting Mechanism** (1 mark): The 16-bit counter is formed by pairing THx and TLx. It increments by 1 on every count pulse. On overflow from FFFFH to 0000H, the corresponding TFx flag in TCON is set to 1.

3. **TMOD Register Function** (2 marks): It is an 8-bit, **non-bit-addressable** SFR at address 89H. Upper nibble configures Timer 1, lower nibble configures Timer 0. Each nibble contains the GATE, C/T, M1, and M0 bits that control hardware gating, mode of operation (0/1/2/3), and Timer/Counter selection.

4. **TCON Register Function** (2 marks): It is an 8-bit, **bit-addressable** SFR at address 88H. Bits TR0/TR1 start or stop the respective timer under software control. Bits TF0/TF1 are overflow flags set by hardware and cleared by software. The lower nibble is dedicated to external interrupt control (IE0/IE1/IT0/IT1).

### Part (b) — Solution (7 marks):

**Step 1**: Calculate machine cycle time (1 mark):

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1~\mu s$$

**Step 2**: Number of counts required (1 mark):

$$N_{counts} = \frac{\text{Delay}}{T_{mc}} = \frac{2000~\mu s}{1~\mu s} = 2000 \text{ counts}$$

**Step 3**: 16-bit initial value (2 marks):

$$X_{initial} = 65536 - 2000 = 63536_{10} = F830_{16}$$

Hex verification:

$$\begin{aligned}
63{,}536 \div 65{,}536 &= 0.9697... \\
63{,}536 \div 256 &= 248 \;(= F8_{16}) \quad \text{remainder} = 48 \;(= 30_{16})
\end{aligned}$$

**Therefore: $X_{initial} = 0xF830$**

**Register split (1 mark):**
- **TH1 = 0xF8**
- **TL1 = 0x30**

**Complete C program (2 marks):**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void Timer1_Delay_2ms(void) {
    TMOD = 0x10;     // 0001 0000b: Timer 1, Mode 1, software gated, timer mode
    TH1  = 0xF8;
    TL1  = 0x30;
    TR1  = 1;        // Start Timer 1
    while (TF1 == 0); // Wait for overflow
    TR1  = 0;        // Stop Timer 1
    TF1  = 0;        // Clear overflow flag
}

void main(void) {
    while (1) {
        P1 = 0xFF;
        Timer1_Delay_2ms();
        P1 = 0x00;
        Timer1_Delay_2ms();
    }
}
```

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the four operating modes of the 8051 Timer in detail. Compare **Mode 1** and **Mode 2** in terms of range, reload mechanism, and typical application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An industrial conveyor uses an **IR sensor** connected to pin **P3.4** to count bottles. Write an 8051 C program to count **500 bottles** using **Timer 0 in Counter Mode (Mode 1)** with an 11.0592 MHz crystal. After 500 counts, an LED at **P1.7** must glow. Compute the values of TH0 and TL0. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📘 MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B:

### Part (a) — Solution (7 marks):

**Mode 0 — 13-bit Timer (1 mark):** Compatible with the older 8048 microcontroller. Uses 13 bits (5 in THx and all 8 in TLx). Range: 0000H to 1FFFH (8192 counts). Not commonly used in modern designs.

**Mode 1 — 16-bit Timer (2 marks):** Uses the full 16 bits (THx and TLx). Range: 0000H to FFFFH (65,536 counts). Maximum delay = $65536 \times T_{mc}$. After overflow, the timer restarts from 0000H, and software must reload the initial value if a repeated delay is needed. Most widely used for long, non-repeating delays.

**Mode 2 — 8-bit Auto-Reload (2 marks):** Only TLx is the active counter. THx holds the reload value. On overflow, THx is **automatically copied into TLx** by hardware. Range: 256 counts max. Ideal for **continuous periodic events** like UART baud-rate generation.

**Mode 3 — Split Timer (1 mark):** Timer 0 is split into two independent 8-bit timers (TL0 uses Timer 0 controls, TH0 uses Timer 1's TR1/TF1). Timer 1 in Mode 3 simply stops. Used when three timers are needed.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Feature | Mode 1 | Mode 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Counter Size | 16-bit | 8-bit |
| Max Counts | 65,536 | 256 |
| Reload | Manual (by software) | Automatic (THx → TLx) |
| Typical App | Long delays, one-shot | UART baud rates, periodic |

### Part (b) — Solution (7 marks):

**Step 1**: Counter mode selection (1 mark):
- Timer 0 in Mode 1 with C/T = 1 (counter mode)
- TMOD = 0x05 (binary 0000 0101)
- External pulses counted on pin P3.4 (T0)

**Step 2**: Initial value calculation (2 marks):

We need the timer to overflow after exactly 500 pulses.

$$X_{initial} = 65536 - 500 = 65036_{10}$$

**Step 3**: Hex conversion (2 marks):

$$\begin{aligned}
65{,}036 \div 65{,}536 &= 0.9924... \\
65{,}036 \div 256 &= 254 \;(= FE_{16}) \quad \text{remainder} = 12 \;(= 0C_{16})
\end{aligned}$$

$$X_{initial} = FE0C_{16}$$

**Register split:**
- **TH0 = 0xFE**
- **TL0 = 0x0C**

**Step 4**: C Program (2 marks):

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit LED = P1^7;

void main(void) {
    LED   = 0;
    TMOD  = 0x05;     // Timer 0: Counter Mode, Mode 1, software gated
    TH0   = 0xFE;
    TL0   = 0x0C;
    TR0   = 1;        // Start counting external pulses on P3.4
    
    while (TF0 == 0);  // Wait for 500 pulses
    
    TR0   = 0;         // Stop counter
    TF0   = 0;         // Clear flag
    LED   = 1;         // Turn ON LED on P1.7
    
    while (1);         // Stay here
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing THx and TLx positions** — If the value is `FE0CH`, TH0 = FE, TL0 = 0C, NOT TL0 = FE, TH0 = 0C. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Forgetting to clear the TFx flag** — Hardware only sets it. If you don't clear it, the program behaves erratically on the next iteration.
> 3. **Wrong TMOD value** — For Counter Mode, Mode 1, the value is **0x05**, not 0x01. The 5th bit (C/T) must be 1.
> 4. **Crystal frequency vs machine cycle confusion** — Always remember $T_{mc} = 12/f_{osc}$. At 12 MHz, $T_{mc} = 1~\mu s$. At 11.0592 MHz, $T_{mc} \approx 1.085~\mu s$.
> 5. **Counter mode cannot be used for time delays** — A common conceptual error. C/T = 1 means you are counting external events, not internal time.
> 6. **GATE bit confusion** — When GATE = 1, the timer only runs when the corresponding INTx pin is HIGH; this is for **hardware pulse width measurement**, not normal software delays.

---

## 5.3 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> **High-Density Summary — Read This the Night Before the Exam:**

- ⏱ **8051 has 2 timers**: Timer 0 and Timer 1, each **16-bit wide** (THx:TLx). Reset value: 0000H.
- 🔧 **TMOD register (89H)**: Configures mode. Fields per timer: GATE | C/T | M1 | M0.
- 🚦 **TCON register (88H)**: Bit-addressable. TRx starts/stops, TFx is overflow flag (set by HW, cleared by SW).
- 📐 **Clock source**: Timer = $f_{osc}/12$ (machine cycle). Counter = external pin T0 (P3.4) / T1 (P3.5).
- 📊 **4 Modes**: 0 (13-bit), 1 (16-bit), 2 (8-bit auto-reload), 3 (split — Timer 0 only).
- 🧮 **Master Formula (Mode 1)**: $\text{Delay} = (65536 - X_{initial}) \times T_{mc}$
- 🧮 **Master Formula (Mode 2)**: $\text{Delay} = (256 - X_{initial}) \times T_{mc}$
- 🔁 **C/T bit = 0** → Timer mode. **C/T bit = 1** → Counter mode.
- 🚪 **GATE = 0** → Software control. **GATE = 1** → Hardware control via INTx pin.
- 🔁 **Mode 2 auto-reload**: THx → TLx automatically on every overflow. No software overhead. Used for UART.
- 🔢 **At 11.0592 MHz crystal**: $T_{mc} = 1.085~\mu s$ (this crystal is the gold standard for UART work).
- 📡 **UART baud rate generator**: Timer 1, Mode 2, with $f_{osc} = 11.0592$ MHz.
- ⚠ **Always manually clear TFx** after overflow in Mode 0/1. Hardware will not do it for you.
- 🎯 **Counter mode max frequency** = $f_{osc}/24$ — external pulse must be at least 2 machine cycles wide.
- 🛠 **Standard crystal frequencies**: 11.0592 MHz (UART), 12 MHz (general), 16 MHz (Arduino-clone 8051).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: I/O Ports
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT503 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** I/O Ports

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **I/O Port** (Input/Output Port) is a physical interface on a microcontroller (MCU) consisting of a group of bidirectional pins (typically organized in groups of 8 bits, called a *port*) that enables the microcontroller to communicate with external peripheral devices, sensors, and actuators. In the context of the **AVR ATmega328P** architecture (the standard reference MCU for KTU PCCGT503), each I/O port is controlled by three dedicated 8-bit Special Function Registers (SFRs):

- **DDRx Register** (Data Direction Register) — Configures pin direction (Input/Output).
- **PORTx Register** (Port Output Register) — Controls output state or enables internal pull-up resistors.
- **PINx Register** (Port Input Register) — Reads the logical state of physical pins.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Focus:** The I/O Port is a *programmable digital interface*; its behavior at any instant is the combined state of the above three registers. A pin's electrical role is NOT fixed by hardware — it is dynamically reconfigurable via software.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The Mailbox System)

Think of an **I/O Port as a row of 8 mailboxes** belonging to a microcontroller (the postmaster). Each mailbox (pin) has three properties:

1. **A Direction Label (DDRx):** Is this mailbox for *sending letters out* (Output) or *receiving letters in* (Input)?
2. **A Letter Slot (PORTx):** When sending out, what *letter* (HIGH/LOW signal) do I drop? When receiving, do I want a *default spring-loaded door* (pull-up resistor) that keeps the box shut if no one pushes?
3. **A Window to Peek (PINx):** When configured for input, I can *look through this window* to see what voltage is present on the external pin.

Just as a postmaster can relabel a mailbox from "outgoing" to "incoming" at any time, the programmer can flip a pin's direction mid-program by writing to the DDRx register.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Metric:** The ATmega328P I/O pins are capable of sourcing/sinking up to **40 mA** per pin (DC current) and operate at a logic level of **5V (TTL)** for HIGH and **0V (GND)** for LOW. The absolute maximum ratings must never be exceeded, or the pin will suffer *latch-up* damage.

## 1.3 Physical Pin Grouping in ATmega328P

The ATmega328P exposes **3 primary I/O ports**, providing a total of **23 usable GPIO (General Purpose Input/Output) pins**:

| Port | Pins Available | Common Arduino UNO Mapping | KTU Notation |
|:----:|:--------------:|:--------------------------:|:------------:|
| **Port B** | PB0 – PB5 (6 pins) | Digital Pins 8–13 | `DDRB`, `PORTB`, `PINB` |
| **Port C** | PC0 – PC6 (7 pins) | Analog Pins A0–A5 | `DDRC`, `PORTC`, `PINC` |
| **Port D** | PD0 – PD7 (8 pins) | Digital Pins 0–7 | `DDRD`, `PORTD`, `PIND` |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Registers Governing Each Port

Every port (B, C, or D) is governed by a *triad* of registers. Understanding the logical relationship between them is the single most important concept for KTU exams on this topic.

### 2.1.1 DDRx — The Direction Switch
- **Size:** 8 bits (one bit per pin of that port).
- **Function:** Selects whether a pin is **INPUT** (bit = 0) or **OUTPUT** (bit = 1).
- **Reset State:** All bits = 0 → All pins default to **High-Impedance Input** (Tri-state).

### 2.1.2 PORTx — The Output Driver / Pull-Up Activator
The function of `PORTx` is **context-dependent** on `DDRx`:

| DDRx Bit | PORTx Bit | Resulting Pin Behavior |
|:--------:|:---------:|:----------------------:|
| 0 (Input) | 0 | High-Impedance (Hi-Z) — Pin floats |
| 0 (Input) | 1 | Input with **Internal Pull-Up Resistor** enabled (~20–50 kΩ) |
| 1 (Output) | 0 | Output LOW (0V, sinks current) |
| 1 (Output) | 1 | Output HIGH (5V, sources current) |

### 2.1.3 PINx — The Read Window
- **Size:** 8 bits.
- **Function:** Allows software to *sample* the actual physical voltage on the pin.
- **Critical Rule:** When `DDRx` = 1 (Output mode), writing to `PORTx` changes the output *and* `PINx` reflects that same value. When `DDRx` = 0 (Input mode), `PINx` reads the *external* physical world.

## 2.2 Why This Tri-Register Design? (The Engineering Rationale)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why three registers instead of one?**
> 1. **Hardware Sharing:** The same physical silicon pad must serve dual roles (input sensing & output driving). A separate direction register prevents electrical contention.
> 2. **Atomic Operations:** Splitting concerns allows the CPU to reconfigure direction without disturbing the current output value, and vice versa.
> 3. **Pull-up Resistor Software Control:** A single PORTx bit cleverly performs *two* jobs (output drive or pull-up enable) depending on direction — this saves silicon area.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Boundary Conditions

| Parameter | Symbol | Value / Formula | Unit | Notes |
|:---------:|:------:|:---------------:|:----:|:------|
| Logic HIGH voltage | $V_{IH}$ | $\geq 0.6 \cdot V_{CC}$ | V | Minimum input recognized as HIGH |
| Logic LOW voltage | $V_{IL}$ | $\leq 0.3 \cdot V_{CC}$ | V | Maximum input recognized as LOW |
| Output HIGH voltage | $V_{OH}$ | $\geq 4.2$ | V | At $I_{OH} = 20$ mA, $V_{CC} = 5$ V |
| Output LOW voltage | $V_{OL}$ | $\leq 0.7$ | V | At $I_{OL} = 20$ mA, $V_{CC} = 5$ V |
| DC Current per pin | $I_{pin}$ | $\leq 40$ | mA | Absolute maximum |
| Pull-up resistor | $R_{PU}$ | $20$ – $50$ | kΩ | Internal, software-selectable |
| Pin capacitance | $C_{pin}$ | $\leq 10$ | pF | Affects rise/fall time |
| Number of ports (ATmega328P) | $N_{ports}$ | 3 | — | B, C, D |
| Total GPIO pins | $N_{GPIO}$ | 23 | — | 2 pins reserved for XTAL |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in IoT

In IoT systems, I/O ports are the **physical bridge between the digital brain (MCU) and the analog world**:

- **Output Mode:** Driving an LED, activating a relay, sending a control pulse to a motor driver, toggling a buzzer, communicating with sensors via protocols like 1-Wire or simple bit-banged SPI.
- **Input Mode:** Reading a push-button, sampling a digital PIR motion sensor, monitoring a thermostat's open-drain output, capturing pulse-width signals from an ultrasonic sensor (HC-SR04).
- **Pull-up Mode:** Connecting a button between pin and GND (rather than $V_{CC}$) — the internal pull-up holds the line HIGH when the button is open, eliminating the need for an external 10 kΩ resistor.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Reading a Push-Button with Pull-Up

This is a classic KTU analytical question: *"Explain how a pin is configured to read a push-button using an internal pull-up resistor, and derive the logic state seen by PINx."*

### Step 1 — Hardware Setup
A momentary push-button is connected between the **ATmega328P pin (e.g., PD2)** and **GND**. The internal pull-up resistor $R_{PU}$ is enabled.

### Step 2 — Register Configuration
The programmer writes the following to initialize the pin as input with pull-up:

$$
\text{DDRD bit 2} = 0 \quad \text{(set as INPUT)}
$$

$$
\text{PORTD bit 2} = 1 \quad \text{(activate internal pull-up)}
$$

### Step 3 — Equivalent Circuit Analysis
With pull-up enabled, the pin is internally connected to $V_{CC}$ through $R_{PU}$. The circuit is a simple voltage divider between $R_{PU}$ and the switch resistance $R_{sw}$:

$$
V_{pin} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_{sw}}{R_{PU} + R_{sw}}
$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate for Button RELEASED (open circuit)
When the button is open, the switch path is broken. The only path is through $R_{PU}$, meaning $R_{sw} \to \infty$:

$$
\lim_{R_{sw} \to \infty} V_{pin} = \lim_{R_{sw} \to \infty} V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_{sw}}{R_{PU} + R_{sw}} = V_{CC} \cdot \lim_{R_{sw} \to \infty} \frac{1}{\frac{R_{PU}}{R_{sw}} + 1} = V_{CC}
$$

Since $V_{CC} = 5\text{ V} \geq V_{IH} = 0.6 \cdot 5 = 3\text{ V}$, the pin reads **HIGH (logic 1)**. Thus `PIND` bit 2 = **1**.

### Step 5 — Evaluate for Button PRESSED (short to GND)
When the button is pressed, $R_{sw} \approx 0\ \Omega$ (ideal switch):

$$
V_{pin} = 5 \cdot \frac{0}{R_{PU} + 0} = 0 \text{ V}
$$

Since $0\text{ V} \leq V_{IL} = 0.3 \cdot 5 = 1.5\text{ V}$, the pin reads **LOW (logic 0)**. Thus `PIND` bit 2 = **0**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Logic Table (Active-Low):** Button Released → PINx reads **1**; Button Pressed → PINx reads **0**. This is the standard *active-low* input convention used in nearly all embedded systems.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: The 2² Configuration Truth Table

The behavior of any single pin is completely determined by the 4 possible combinations of two bits. The following exhaustive table is **high-yield for KTU viva and 3-mark questions**:

| DDRx | PORTx | Mode | Electrical Behavior | PINx Reads |
|:----:|:-----:|:-----|:--------------------|:----------:|
| 0 | 0 | Input, No Pull-up | Hi-Z (tri-state); pin floats | External state |
| 0 | 1 | Input, Pull-up | Pin weakly pulled to $V_{CC}$ | External state |
| 1 | 0 | Output LOW | Drives 0V (sinks current) | Returns 0 |
| 1 | 1 | Output HIGH | Drives $V_{CC}$ (sources current) | Returns 1 |

## 3.3 Derivation: Current-Limiting Resistor for an LED Output

A common KTU 14-mark question asks to design an LED-driving circuit. Given:
- LED forward voltage: $V_F = 2.0\text{ V}$ (typical red LED)
- Desired LED current: $I_F = 15\text{ mA}$
- Supply voltage: $V_{CC} = 5\text{ V}$

**Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the output pin → resistor → LED → GND loop:**

$$
V_{CC} = V_{R} + V_F
$$

**Solve for the resistor voltage drop:**

$$
V_{R} = V_{CC} - V_F = 5 - 2.0 = 3.0 \text{ V}
$$

**Apply Ohm's Law to find the required series resistance:**

$$
R = \frac{V_{R}}{I_{F}} = \frac{3.0 \text{ V}}{15 \text{ mA}} = \frac{3.0}{0.015} = 200\ \Omega
$$

**To ensure safety margin, choose the next standard E12 value:** $R = 220\ \Omega$. This yields a final current of:

$$
I_{F,actual} = \frac{3.0}{220} = 13.6 \text{ mA}
$$

This is well within the **40 mA absolute maximum** of the ATmega328P pin and provides a safe brightness for a standard indicator LED.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of an ATmega328P I/O Pin

The following block diagram maps the internal tri-register structure and how a single physical pin (e.g., PD2) is electrically shared between input sensing, output driving, and pull-up functions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU_BUS["Internal CPU Data Bus"]
        BUS[8-bit Data Bus]
    end

    subgraph REG_TRIAD["Tri-Register Control Logic"]
        DDRx["DDRx Bit\n(Direction)"]
        PORTx["PORTx Bit\n(Output/Pull-up)"]
        PINx["PINx Bit\n(Input Read)"]
    end

    subgraph PIN_CELL["Pin Cell Hardware (e.g., PD2)"]
        MUX["Direction MUX"]
        PULL["Pull-up Resistor\n20-50 kΩ"]
        DRV["Output Driver\n(Push-Pull Stage)"]
        PAD["Physical PAD\n(Pin PD2)"]
    end

    subgraph EXT_WORLD["External Circuit"]
        LED["LED + 220Ω Resistor"]
        BTN["Push Button to GND"]
    end

    BUS --> DDRx
    BUS --> PORTx
    BUS --> PINx

    DDRx -->|selects path| MUX
    PORTx --> MUX
    PORTx -->|enables| PULL
    MUX --> DRV
    PULL --> PAD
    DRV --> PAD
    PAD --> PINx

    PAD --> LED
    PAD --> BTN

    style REG_TRIAD fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style PIN_CELL fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    style EXT_WORLD fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Logical Flow: Configuring a Pin (Initialization Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Pin PD2 needs configuration] --> B{What is the role?}
    B -->|Output| C[Set DDRD bit 2 = 1]
    C --> D{What logic level?}
    D -->|HIGH| E[Set PORTD bit 2 = 1\nDrives 5V on pin]
    D -->|LOW| F[Set PORTD bit 2 = 0\nDrives 0V on pin]

    B -->|Input| G[Set DDRD bit 2 = 0]
    G --> H{Need pull-up?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Set PORTD bit 2 = 1\nActivates 20-50 kΩ pull-up]
    H -->|No| J[Set PORTD bit 2 = 0\nHi-Z floating input]
    I --> K[Read PIND bit 2 to sample pin]
    J --> K

    E --> L[Done: Pin Active]
    F --> L
    K --> L
```

## 4.3 Register Bit-Map Visualization for Port D (Example)

| Bit Position | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|:------------:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| **DDRD** | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| **PORTD** | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| **Pin Role** | IN | IN | **OUT-HI** | IN | IN | **OUT-HI** | IN | IN |

*Reading:* PD5 and PD2 are configured as **outputs driving HIGH**; all others are **inputs with no pull-up**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between the functions of DDRx, PORTx, and PINx registers of an ATmega microcontroller.**

**Model Answer:**
- **DDRx (Data Direction Register):** Determines whether each pin of port x acts as an **input** (bit = 0) or an **output** (bit = 1). It does not produce any external signal — it is a *control* register.
- **PORTx (Port Data Register):** Has a *dual role* depending on DDRx. If DDRx = 1, writing to PORTx drives the pin HIGH (1) or LOW (0). If DDRx = 0, writing 1 to PORTx enables the **internal pull-up resistor**.
- **PINx (Port Input Register):** Used *only for reading*. It reflects the current logic state of the physical pin, allowing the CPU to sample external signals.

> [!NOTE]
> The fundamental distinction is: **DDRx = direction selector, PORTx = output writer / pull-up enabler, PINx = input reader.**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is the purpose of an internal pull-up resistor in an AVR microcontroller? When is it activated?**

**Model Answer:**
The internal pull-up resistor (typically **20–50 kΩ**) weakly ties an input pin to $V_{CC}$, preventing it from *floating* when no external driver is connected. A floating input can pick up electrical noise and cause spurious logic transitions.

**Activation Condition:** The pull-up is activated when the pin is configured as **input (DDRx bit = 0)** AND **PORTx bit = 1**. This is the standard technique for interfacing a push-button connected between the pin and GND, eliminating the need for an external 10 kΩ resistor.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — ESE Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the internal structure of a single I/O pin of the ATmega328P microcontroller. Describe the role of DDRx, PORTx, and PINx registers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a circuit to interface an LED to pin PB5 of an ATmega328P. The LED has a forward voltage $V_F = 2.2\text{ V}$ and requires a forward current of 20 mA. Calculate the required series resistance and write down the register-level configuration steps. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Internal Structure of an I/O Pin:**

The internal structure consists of three major sub-blocks controlled by software:

1. **Direction Control MUX** — Driven by the corresponding DDRx bit. When DDRx = 0, the output driver is *disconnected* (Hi-Z) and the pin is in input mode. When DDRx = 1, the output driver is connected.

2. **Pull-up Resistor Block** — A CMOS transistor acting as a resistor ($R_{PU} \approx 20$–$50\text{ k}\Omega$) between $V_{CC}$ and the pin pad. Activated only when DDRx = 0 AND PORTx = 1.

3. **Output Push-Pull Driver** — A pair of complementary MOSFETs that actively drive the pin to $V_{CC}$ (PORTx = 1) or GND (PORTx = 0) when DDRx = 1.

The **PINx** register is connected directly to the input of the Schmitt trigger, which samples the pin voltage and synchronizes it to the CPU clock.

**(b) LED Interface Design:**

**Step 1 — Apply KVL around the loop:**

$$
V_{CC} = V_R + V_F
$$

$$
V_R = 5 - 2.2 = 2.8 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Ohm's Law:**

$$
R = \frac{V_R}{I_F} = \frac{2.8}{0.020} = 140\ \Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Choose standard E12 resistor:** Closest higher value = **$150\ \Omega$** (or $180\ \Omega$ for additional safety margin).

**Step 4 — Final actual current:**

$$
I_{F,actual} = \frac{2.8}{150} = 18.67 \text{ mA}
$$

This is **safe** (well below 40 mA pin limit).

**Step 5 — Register Configuration (Conceptual Description):**

- Set **DDRB bit 5 = 1** → Configure PB5 as **OUTPUT**.
- Set **PORTB bit 5 = 1** → Drive pin **HIGH (5V)** to switch the LED ON.
- To switch LED OFF: Set **PORTB bit 5 = 0**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the four possible configurations of an AVR I/O pin using the DDRx and PORTx registers. Construct a complete truth table and describe the electrical behavior in each case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Interface a push-button to pin PD3 of an ATmega328P using the internal pull-up resistor. Draw the circuit, derive the logic levels at PINx for both button states, and explain why this is called an "active-low" input. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) The Four Configurations:**

*(See the comprehensive Analytical Matrix in Section 3.2 — reproduce all four rows.)*

The table lists: (1) Input/Hi-Z, (2) Input/Pull-up, (3) Output-LOW, (4) Output-HIGH. For each, the student must describe: a) the electrical role, b) the typical use case, and c) what PINx will read.

**(b) Push-Button Interface with Internal Pull-Up:**

**Circuit:** The push-button is connected between **PD3** and **GND**. No external resistor is needed.

**Register Settings:**
- DDRD bit 3 = 0 (configure as INPUT)
- PORTD bit 3 = 1 (enable pull-up)

**Derivation of Logic Levels (using the Section 3.1 derivation):**

- **Button Released:** $V_{pin} = V_{CC} = 5\text{ V}$ → PINx reads **1 (HIGH)**
- **Button Pressed:** $V_{pin} = 0\text{ V}$ → PINx reads **0 (LOW)**

**Why "Active-Low"?** The *active event* (button press) corresponds to a **logic LOW (0)** state. The *inactive* (resting) state is HIGH (1). This naming convention is universal in embedded systems because mechanical switches are typically wired to GND for safety and noise immunity (the pull-up keeps the line at a defined, noise-immune HIGH level when open).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in I/O Port Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing PORTx and PINx roles:** Students often write "PORTx reads the input" — this is WRONG. PINx reads; PORTx writes.
> 2. **Forgetting the active-low convention:** When asked about a button interface, students frequently assume "pressed = 1" — this costs 2 marks in design questions.
> 3. **Ignoring the 40 mA pin limit:** Designing an LED with $R = 10\ \Omega$ and not justifying the current limit will lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Not specifying DDR/PORT/PIN prefixes:** Always write `DDRD`, `PORTD`, `PIND` — not just "the registers".
> 5. **Skipping the pull-up activation rule:** The pull-up works *only* when DDRx = 0. Many students think "writing 1 to PORTx in input mode is a no-op" — it actually enables the pull-up.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — I/O Ports (ATmega328P):**
> - **3 Registers, 1 Pin:** Every I/O pin is governed by **DDRx (direction)**, **PORTx (output/pull-up)**, and **PINx (input read)**.
> - **DDRx = 1 → Output Mode; DDRx = 0 → Input Mode** (reset default).
> - **PORTx dual role:** Output drive (when DDR=1) OR pull-up enable (when DDR=0).
> - **PINx is read-only from software's perspective** for sampling external signals.
> - **23 GPIO pins** in ATmega328P across **3 ports** (B: 6, C: 7, D: 8). 2 pins are reserved for crystal.
> - **Current limits:** 40 mA absolute max per pin; 200 mA total across all pins.
> - **Internal pull-up resistance:** 20–50 kΩ, software-activated.
> - **Logic levels:** $V_{IH} \geq 0.6 V_{CC}$, $V_{IL} \leq 0.3 V_{CC}$.
> - **Active-low convention** is the industry standard for button interfaces.
> - **LED current-limiting resistor formula:** $R = (V_{CC} - V_F) / I_F$.
> - **Floating inputs are dangerous** — always use pull-ups for mechanical switches or unused pins.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interrupt Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT503 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Interrupt Structure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Interrupt**: An **asynchronous external or internal event** that temporarily suspends the execution of the main program (foreground process) so that the processor can execute a special routine called an **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** or **Interrupt Handler** to service the event. After the ISR completes, the processor returns to the exact point in the main program where it was interrupted using the saved **Program Counter (PC)** value on the stack.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for *PCCGT503 – Microprocessors and Microcontrollers for IoT*, the term **Interrupt Structure** refers to the complete hardware-software mechanism inside the microcontroller that supports interrupt-driven operation. For the classical **Intel 8051** family (the most-tested microcontroller in KTU papers), the interrupt structure consists of:

- **5 interrupt sources** (in standard 8051)
- **2 priority levels** (High and Low)
- **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** — namely the **Interrupt Enable (IE) Register** and the **Interrupt Priority (IP) Register**
- **Fixed vector addresses** in program memory for each ISR
- **Interrupt flag bits** scattered across the **TCON** and **SCON** registers

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cooking Analogy"

Imagine you are **cooking dinner** (the main program is running). Suddenly, the **doorbell rings** (an external interrupt INT0 fires). What do you do?

1. You **pause** the cooking (saves the current step on the stack → CPU pushes PC).
2. You **answer the door** (executes the ISR for INT0).
3. Once done, you **resume cooking from the exact same step** (pops PC and continues the main program).

If the **phone rings** (Timer 1 overflow interrupt) while answering the door, you must decide **which is more important** — this is exactly what the **Interrupt Priority Register (IP)** does! It defines a hierarchy: High priority interrupts can interrupt low priority ISRs, but **not vice versa**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 1):**
> The Interrupt Structure is *not* just a list of interrupts. It is a complete system comprising:
> **Interrupt Sources → Interrupt Flags → Interrupt Enable → Interrupt Priority → Vector Address → ISR Execution → Return via RETI.**

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Number of interrupt sources in 8051:** **5** (Reset is technically the 6th, but is *not* maskable or vectored like the others).
- **Vector address spacing:** **8 bytes** between consecutive interrupt vectors.
- **Two priority levels:** **0 (Low)** and **1 (High)**.
- **Interrupt latency (8051, 12 MHz crystal):** **3 to 7 machine cycles** (3 µs to 7 µs per machine cycle at 1 µs each).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8051 Interrupt Sources (Exhaustive Breakdown)

The standard 8051 microcontroller supports the following **5 maskable interrupts**:

| # | Interrupt | Flag Bit | SFR | Vector Address | Natural Priority |
|---|-----------|----------|-----|----------------|------------------|
| 1 | **External Interrupt 0 (INT0)** | IE0 | TCON.1 | 0003H | Highest |
| 2 | **Timer 0 Overflow** | TF0 | TCON.5 | 000BH | ↓ |
| 3 | **External Interrupt 1 (INT1)** | IE1 | TCON.3 | 0013H | ↓ |
| 4 | **Timer 1 Overflow** | TF1 | TCON.7 | 001BH | ↓ |
| 5 | **Serial Port (TI or RI)** | TI / RI | SCON.1 / SCON.0 | 0023H | Lowest |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reset** (vector 0000H) is a separate, **non-maskable** system reset and is *not* considered part of the 5 maskable interrupts. It has the absolute highest priority in the system.

## 2.2 Interrupt Enable Register (IE) — Address 0A8H

The **IE register** acts as the master gatekeeper. Every bit is a switch; the interrupt will be recognized **only if both** the source's local enable bit **AND** the global **EA (Enable All)** bit are set to **1**.

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name | **EA** | — | — | **ES** | **ET1** | **EX1** | **ET0** | **EX0** |
| Function | Global Enable | Reserved | Reserved | Serial Port | Timer 1 | External 1 | Timer 0 | External 0 |

- **EA = 1** → All individual enables take effect.
- **EA = 0** → All interrupts are disabled (masked), no matter what the other bits say.

### KTU High-Yield Example — Enabling External Interrupt 0:
To enable only **INT0**, write `10000001` in binary to the IE register.

$$IE = EA \cdot 2^{7} + ES \cdot 2^{4} + ET1 \cdot 2^{3} + EX1 \cdot 2^{2} + ET0 \cdot 2^{1} + EX0 \cdot 2^{0}$$

$$IE = 1 \cdot 128 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 1 \cdot 1 = 129_{10} = 81_{H}$$

In assembly: `MOV IE, #81H`

## 2.3 Interrupt Priority Register (IP) — Address 0B8H

The IP register assigns **High (1)** or **Low (0)** priority to each interrupt.

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name | — | — | — | **PS** | **PT1** | **PX1** | **PT0** | **PX0** |
| Function | Reserved | Reserved | Reserved | Serial | Timer 1 | External 1 | Timer 0 | External 0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Two-Level Priority" Rule:**
> 1. A **high-priority** interrupt can interrupt a **low-priority** ISR.
> 2. A **low-priority** interrupt **CANNOT** interrupt a **high-priority** ISR.
> 3. If two interrupts of the **same priority** occur simultaneously, the **natural hardware priority order** (INT0 > TF0 > INT1 > TF1 > Serial) is used to break the tie.

## 2.4 Interrupt Vector Addresses — Memory Map

Each interrupt jumps to a **fixed, hard-wired address** in code memory. Notice the spacing:

$$V_{n+1} - V_{n} = 8 \text{ bytes} \quad \forall n \in \{1,2,3,4\}$$

This means the default space allotted to each ISR is only **8 bytes** of program memory. If the ISR is longer than 8 bytes, the code will "overflow" into the next interrupt's vector area, causing system corruption. The standard professional solution is to place an **LJMP (Long Jump)** at the vector address to the actual ISR elsewhere in memory.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Unit | Purpose |
|-----------|-----------------|------|---------|
| Vector address spacing | $V_{n+1} - V_{n} = 8$ | Bytes | Memory layout for ISRs |
| Maximum ISR size (default) | $S_{max} = 8$ | Bytes | Without using LJMP |
| IE register value to enable $k$ | $\text{IE} = 128 \cdot EA + \sum_{i \in k} 2^{i}$ | Decimal | Register programming |
| Interrupt latency (12 MHz) | $T_{latency} = 3 \text{ to } 7$ | µs | Response time analysis |
| Crystal period (11.0592 MHz) | $T_{clk} = 1/11.0592$ | µs | UART baud-rate accuracy |
| Machine cycle (12 MHz) | $T_{mc} = 12 \times T_{clk} = 1$ | µs | Per instruction timing |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **IoT applications** (the focus of PCCGT503), interrupt structures are mission-critical. For example:

- A **smart weather station** uses **Timer interrupts** to sample temperature sensors every 100 ms without halting the main HTTP communication loop.
- A **wireless sensor node** uses **External Interrupts** to wake up the MCU from **power-down mode** the instant a packet arrives on the radio.
- A **UART-based LoRa/GSM module** uses **Serial Port Interrupts** to handle incoming data byte-by-byte without polling, drastically reducing CPU load and power consumption.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Calculating the IE Register Value to Enable INT1, Timer 0, and Serial Port (Global Enable ON)

**Problem:** Write the value to be loaded into the **IE register** to enable **External Interrupt 1 (EX1)**, **Timer 0 (ET0)**, and the **Serial Port (ES)**, with global enable **EA = 1**.

**Step 1 — Identify which bits of IE must be set to 1:**

$$EA = IE.7 = 1, \quad ES = IE.4 = 1, \quad ET1 = IE.3 = 0, \quad EX1 = IE.2 = 1, \quad ET0 = IE.1 = 1, \quad EX0 = IE.0 = 0$$

**Step 2 — Express the value as a sum of weighted bits:**

$$IE = (1 \cdot 2^{7}) + (0 \cdot 2^{4}) + (0 \cdot 2^{3}) + (1 \cdot 2^{2}) + (1 \cdot 2^{1}) + (0 \cdot 2^{0})$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each power of 2:**

$$IE = 128 + 0 + 0 + 4 + 2 + 0$$

**Step 4 — Final summation:**

$$IE = 134_{10}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to hexadecimal (KTU standard):**

$$134 \div 16 = 8 \text{ remainder } 6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 134_{10} = 86_{H}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{IE = 86_{H}, \quad \text{Assembly code: } MOV \ IE, \ \#86H}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Verifying the Vector Address Spacing

**Problem:** Show mathematically that the vector addresses differ by exactly 8 bytes.

Given vector addresses:
$$V_{1} = 0003_{H}, \quad V_{2} = 000B_{H}, \quad V_{3} = 0013_{H}, \quad V_{4} = 001B_{H}, \quad V_{5} = 0023_{H}$$

**Step 1 — Compute $V_{2} - V_{1}$:**

$$V_{2} - V_{1} = 000B_{H} - 0003_{H} = 0008_{H} = 8_{10} \text{ bytes} \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Compute $V_{3} - V_{2}$:**

$$V_{3} - V_{2} = 0013_{H} - 000B_{H} = 0008_{H} = 8_{10} \text{ bytes} \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — General pattern:**

$$V_{n+1} - V_{n} = 8_{H} \quad \text{ for } n = 1,2,3,4$$

**Step 4 — Total memory reserved (from 0003H to 002AH end of serial ISR):**

$$S_{total} = 002B_{H} - 0003_{H} = 0028_{H} = 40_{10} \text{ bytes} \text{ (5 ISRs} \times \text{8 bytes each)}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Interrupt Latency Bound

**Problem:** Calculate the **worst-case interrupt latency** for the 8051 running with a **11.0592 MHz crystal**.

**Step 1 — Machine cycle frequency:**

$$f_{mc} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12} = \frac{11.0592 \text{ MHz}}{12} = 0.9216 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 2 — Machine cycle period:**

$$T_{mc} = \frac{1}{f_{mc}} = \frac{1}{0.9216 \text{ MHz}} \approx 1.085 \, \mu s$$

**Step 3 — The 8051 completes the current instruction (1–4 machine cycles) and then adds 2 machine cycles for the hardware response:**

$$T_{latency} = N_{inst} \cdot T_{mc} + 2 \cdot T_{mc}$$

**Step 4 — Worst case (4-cycle instruction, e.g., MUL/DIV):**

$$T_{latency,max} = (4 + 2) \cdot T_{mc} = 6 \cdot 1.085 \, \mu s \approx 6.51 \, \mu s$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing Trigger Mode (Level vs. Edge)

| Criterion | **Level-Triggered (IT0/IT1 = 0)** | **Edge-Triggered (IT0/IT1 = 1, default)** |
|-----------|-----------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| Activation condition | Low level held | Falling edge (1 → 0) |
| Flag behavior | Not latched; CPU re-enters ISR if pin is still low | Flag auto-cleared when ISR is vectored |
| Re-trigger safety | **Unsafe** — may cause infinite re-entry | **Safe** — one interrupt per edge |
| Use case | Wired-OR shared interrupt lines | Pulsed sources, push-buttons |
| KTU recommendation | Avoid unless mandatory | **Use this by default** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the 8051 Interrupt Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Interrupt Sources] --> B[Interrupt Flag Bits in TCON and SCON]
    B --> C{IE Register: Local Enables EX0 ET0 EX1 ET1 ES}
    C --> D{Global Enable EA = 1?}
    D -- No --> Z1[Interrupt Masked, No Action]
    D -- Yes --> E{IP Register: High or Low Priority?}
    E --> F[Priority Encoder and Arbiter]
    F --> G[Vector Generator: PC loaded with Vector Address]
    G --> H[Push current PC onto Stack]
    H --> I[Execute ISR at Vector Address]
    I --> J{RETI instruction executed?}
    J -- No --> I
    J -- Yes --> K[Pop PC from Stack]
    K --> L[Resume Main Program]
```

## 4.2 Vector Memory Layout Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[0000H Reset Vector] --> A1[0003H INT0 ISR 8 bytes]
    A1 --> A2[000BH TF0 ISR 8 bytes]
    A2 --> A3[0013H INT1 ISR 8 bytes]
    A3 --> A4[001BH TF1 ISR 8 bytes]
    A4 --> A5[0023H Serial ISR 8 bytes]
    A5 --> A6[002BH General Program Code]
```

## 4.3 Interrupt Enable Programming Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Set EA = 0 to safely configure] --> S2[Step 2: Configure IP register for priorities]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Configure TCON bits IT0 and IT1 for trigger mode]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Set local enable bits EX0 ET0 EX1 ET1 ES as required]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Set EA = 1 to globally activate]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Clear the flag bit by software if edge triggered]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: ISR uses RETI to return]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the Interrupt Structure of 8051 microcontroller. List its interrupt sources with their vector addresses.**

**Model Answer:**

The 8051 interrupt structure is a hardware-software mechanism that allows the CPU to respond to asynchronous events. The 8051 supports **5 maskable interrupt sources**, each with a fixed vector address in program memory:

| Interrupt | Flag | Vector Address |
|-----------|------|----------------|
| External Interrupt 0 (INT0) | IE0 (TCON.1) | **0003H** |
| Timer 0 Overflow | TF0 (TCON.5) | **000BH** |
| External Interrupt 1 (INT1) | IE1 (TCON.3) | **0013H** |
| Timer 1 Overflow | TF1 (TCON.7) | **001BH** |
| Serial Port (TI/RI) | TI/RI (SCON.1/0) | **0023H** |

Two SFRs control the structure: **IE (Interrupt Enable)** and **IP (Interrupt Priority)**. The global enable bit **EA** must be set for any interrupt to be recognized. The IP register provides two priority levels, with a high-priority interrupt capable of interrupting a low-priority ISR but not vice versa. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between level-triggered and edge-triggered external interrupts in 8051.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Level-Triggered (ITx = 0) | Edge-Triggered (ITx = 1) |
|-----------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| Activation | Low level on INTx pin | Falling edge (1 → 0) |
| Flag IE0/IE1 | Not latched (not generated) | Latched, auto-cleared on ISR entry |
| Re-trigger risk | **High** — CPU may re-enter if level persists | **Low** — one interrupt per edge |
| Controlled by | TCON.0 (IT0) / TCON.2 (IT1) | TCON.0 (IT0) / TCON.2 (IT1) |
| Typical use | Wired-OR interrupt sharing | Push-buttons, sensors |

Edge-triggered is the safer default mode for most IoT applications. (3 marks)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Draw the Interrupt Enable (IE) register of 8051 and explain the function of each bit. Write the value of IE to enable Timer 0 overflow, External Interrupt 1, and Serial Port interrupt with global enable ON. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw the Interrupt Priority (IP) register of 8051 and explain the two-level priority scheme. Describe what happens when two interrupts of the same priority occur simultaneously. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) IE Register Explanation:**
- The IE register is at SFR address **A8H** and is bit-addressable.
- **EA (IE.7)**: Global Enable — must be 1 for any interrupt to be recognized.
- **ES (IE.4)**: Enables Serial Port interrupt.
- **ET1 (IE.3)**: Enables Timer 1 overflow interrupt.
- **EX1 (IE.2)**: Enables External Interrupt 1.
- **ET0 (IE.1)**: Enables Timer 0 overflow interrupt.
- **EX0 (IE.0)**: Enables External Interrupt 0.
- Bits IE.6 and IE.5 are **reserved** (undefined for standard 8051).

**Calculation for Timer 0, External 1, and Serial with EA = 1:**

$$IE = 2^{7} \cdot EA + 2^{4} \cdot ES + 2^{2} \cdot EX1 + 2^{1} \cdot ET0$$

$$IE = 128 \cdot 1 + 16 \cdot 1 + 4 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 1 = 150_{10} = 96_{H}$$

**Assembly code:** `MOV IE, #96H` **(7 marks)**

**(b) IP Register and Priority Scheme:**
- IP is at SFR address **B8H**, bit-addressable.
- Bits: **PS (IP.4)**, **PT1 (IP.3)**, **PX1 (IP.2)**, **PT0 (IP.1)**, **PX0 (IP.0)**.
- Setting a bit to 1 → High priority; 0 → Low priority.
- **Rules:**
  1. A high-priority ISR cannot be interrupted by a low-priority interrupt.
  2. A high-priority ISR *can* be interrupted by another high-priority interrupt.
  3. A low-priority ISR *can* be interrupted by a high-priority interrupt.
  4. A low-priority ISR *cannot* be interrupted by another low-priority interrupt.
- **Simultaneous same-priority conflict:** The CPU uses the **natural hardware priority**: **INT0 > TF0 > INT1 > TF1 > Serial**. The interrupt with the highest natural order is serviced first. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the complete **interrupt handling sequence** in 8051 from the moment an interrupt occurs to the moment execution returns to the main program. Mention the role of the stack and the **RETI** instruction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **vector address memory layout** of 8051 interrupts. Why is the 8-byte spacing important, and how is the problem of overflow handled? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Interrupt Handling Sequence (Step-by-Step):**
1. The interrupt source sets its **flag bit** (e.g., IE0, TF0).
2. The corresponding **local enable bit** (EX0, ET0, etc.) and the **EA bit** are checked by hardware. If both are 1, the request is accepted.
3. The CPU **completes the current instruction**.
4. The current **16-bit PC is pushed onto the stack** (8 bytes used; one for high byte, one for low byte).
5. The CPU loads the PC with the **fixed vector address** of the highest-priority active interrupt.
6. The ISR executes. Inside the ISR, the **flag must be cleared** (for edge-triggered external interrupts, hardware clears it; for timers and serial, software must clear TFx/TI/RI).
7. The ISR ends with the **RETI** instruction.
8. RETI **pops the saved PC** from the stack back into the program counter, resuming the main program exactly where it left off.
9. Crucially, **RETI also signals the interrupt controller** that the ISR has ended (unlike a plain RET, which would not re-enable the interrupt logic). **(7 marks)**

**(b) Vector Address Memory Layout:**
The vector memory layout was illustrated in **Section 4.2** above. Key points:
- The 8-byte spacing is a **fixed architectural decision** of Intel for 8051.
- Since 8 bytes is only enough for 2–3 small instructions, **most ISRs do not fit**.
- The standard solution is to place an **`LJMP target_address`** at the vector address, redirecting execution to the actual ISR located elsewhere in code memory.
- Example for INT0:

```assembly
ORG 0003H        ; Vector address for INT0
LJMP ISR_INT0    ; Long jump to actual ISR
ORG 0030H        ; Place actual ISR here
ISR_INT0:
   ; ... ISR body ...
RETI             ; Return and re-enable
```

This pattern is essential for KTU 8051 assembly programming questions. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting EA = 1**: Students often set the local enable bits but leave EA = 0. The interrupt will never fire. KTU examiners specifically deduct 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Using RET instead of RETI**: Using RET does not signal the interrupt controller that the ISR has ended, leading to malfunction on subsequent interrupts. Always use **RETI**.
> 3. **Ignoring the 8-byte limit**: Placing the entire ISR body at the vector address without LJMP will overwrite the next interrupt's vector.
> 4. **Wrong bit position**: Remember that ES is **IE.4** (not IE.5), and the reserved bits IE.6 and IE.5 must be written as 0. KTU evaluators check this carefully.
> 5. **Not clearing flag bits**: For Timer 0/1 and Serial Port interrupts, the flag (TF0, TF1, TI, RI) must be **cleared by software** inside the ISR; otherwise, the interrupt will re-trigger immediately after RETI.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **8051 has 5 maskable interrupts** with vector addresses at **0003H, 000BH, 0013H, 001BH, 0023H**.
- **Reset (0000H)** is a separate non-maskable system event.
- **Two control SFRs**: **IE (A8H)** for enabling, **IP (B8H)** for prioritizing.
- **Global enable bit EA** (IE.7) must be 1 for any interrupt to function.
- **Two priority levels** only; natural hardware priority breaks same-priority ties.
- **Edge-triggered** is the default and safest mode for IT0 and IT1.
- **8-byte vector spacing** requires the **LJMP technique** for longer ISRs.
- Always end the ISR with **RETI** — never RET.
- Clear **TF0, TF1, TI, RI** flags by software inside the ISR; edge-triggered external flags are auto-cleared.
- Interrupt latency ≈ **3–7 machine cycles**; relevant for real-time IoT design.
- IE programming formula: $\text{IE} = 128 \cdot EA + \sum \text{local enables as } 2^n$.
- IP programming formula: Set the bit of the desired source to 1 for High priority.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ARM Microcontroller Architecture and Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT503 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** ARM Microcontroller Architecture and Programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**ARM (Advanced RISC Machine)** is a family of reduced instruction set computing (RISC) architectures for computer processors, configured for a range of environments. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme, an **ARM Microcontroller** refers to a single-chip embedded computing device built around an ARM processor core (commonly the **ARM7TDMI-S** in the LPC2148 used in the syllabus), integrating CPU, memory, peripherals, and I/O on a single die.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course focuses on the **ARM7TDMI-S** processor core inside the **NXP LPC2148** microcontroller. Students must master both the architectural theory and the assembly-level programming model.

**Key Architectural Properties of ARM:**
- **32-bit RISC architecture** with a **Load/Store** programming model.
- **Fixed instruction length of 32 bits** in ARM state (16 bits in Thumb state).
- **37 registers** in the physical register file, of which **31 are visible at any time** depending on processor mode.
- **Von Neumann architecture** with a unified instruction and data bus (modified Harvard in some variants).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Smart Workshop Manager" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a highly efficient factory floor managed by a strict foreman (the ARM core):
> - The foreman **never stops to think long** — every instruction is a fixed, simple command (RISC philosophy).
> - The foreman keeps tools (data) on a **separate workbench (registers)**, away from the raw materials (memory). The foreman only fetches materials when explicitly told to do so (**Load/Store architecture**).
> - A small **clipboard** tracks the current job status (the **Program Status Register — CPSR**) — holding flags like "negative result," "zero result," or "carry occurred."
> - When an emergency occurs (an exception, like a power failure or an external interrupt), the foreman **temporarily switches to a special emergency role** (Exception mode) and handles it before resuming.
> - The foreman can work in **two shifts**: a full detailed shift (**ARM state, 32-bit instructions**) and a compact fast shift (**Thumb state, 16-bit instructions**) for power savings.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Conventions

- **Instruction Length:** **32 bits** (ARM state) | **16 bits** (Thumb state)
- **Register Width:** **32 bits**
- **Operating Voltage (LPC2148):** **3.0 V to 3.6 V** (typical 3.3 V)
- **Clock Speed (LPC2148):** Up to **60 MHz**
- **Programmable Counter Width:** 32 bits (4 GB addressable space)
- **Endianness:** **Little Endian** by default for LPC2148

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for ARM modes:** "**USR FIQ IRQ SVC UND ABT SYS**" — remember as **"U FISH AS"** (Unified, FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Undefined, Abort, System).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ARM7TDMI-S Processor Core

The **ARM7TDMI-S** is a 32-bit RISC processor core widely used in embedded and IoT applications. The suffix decoding is critical for KTU exams:

| Suffix | Meaning | Significance |
|--------|---------|--------------|
| **T** | **Thumb** | Supports 16-bit compressed instruction set |
| **D** | **Debug** | Contains hardware debug extensions (J-TAG) |
| **M** | **Multiplier** | Enhanced multiplier (produces 64-bit results) |
| **I** | **EmbeddedICE** | In-circuit emulator hardware for breakpoints |
| **-S** | **Synthesizable** | Soft core, can be synthesized on FPGA/ASIC |

## 2.2 Processor Modes of Operation (7 Modes)

ARM7TDMI-S supports **seven operating modes**, organized by exception type. Mode switching is performed by writing to the CPSR.

1. **User (USR)** — Normal program execution; **most application code runs here**; cannot switch to privileged modes directly.
2. **Fast Interrupt (FIQ)** — High-priority interrupt; designed for **fast data transfer**.
3. **Interrupt (IRQ)** — Normal external hardware interrupt.
4. **Supervisor (SVC)** — Entered on **reset** or **software interrupt (SWI)**; the protected OS kernel mode.
5. **Abort (ABT)** — Used by memory system on **failed instruction or data fetch**.
6. **Undefined (UND)** — Entered when an **undefined instruction** is executed.
7. **System (SYS)** — Privileged mode using **User mode registers**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Five modes are privileged (FIQ, IRQ, SVC, ABT, UND), one is user (USR), and SYS is a privileged mode sharing USR register banks.

## 2.3 The Register File (37 Registers)

The ARM7TDMI-S has **37 physical 32-bit registers**, of which **31 are visible at any instant**, grouped by mode.

### 2.3.1 General-Purpose Registers (R0–R12)
- **R0–R12:** All-purpose registers used for data manipulation.
- **R13 (SP):** Stack Pointer — points to the top of the stack.
- **R14 (LR):** Link Register — stores the **return address** after a subroutine call (BL instruction).
- **R15 (PC):** Program Counter — holds the address of the **next instruction to execute (PC + 8)** in ARM state due to pipelining.

### 2.3.2 Banked Registers (Mode-Specific)
- Each privileged mode has its **own R13 (SP) and R14 (LR)**.
- **FIQ additionally banks R8–R14**, allowing fast context switching without pushing registers to memory.

## 2.4 Program Status Registers (CPSR & SPSR)

The **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)** is the heart of ARM's conditional execution model. Every arithmetic, logical, comparison, or move operation updates these flags.

| Bit | Flag | Meaning | Set When |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| **31** | **N** | Negative | Result bit 31 is 1 (negative signed) |
| **30** | **Z** | Zero | Result is zero |
| **29** | **C** | Carry | Unsigned overflow out of bit 31, or borrow in subtraction |
| **28** | **V** | oVerflow | Signed overflow occurred |
| **7** | **I** | IRQ disable | 1 = IRQ disabled |
| **6** | **F** | FIQ disable | 1 = FIQ disabled |
| **5** | **T** | Thumb state | 1 = Thumb, 0 = ARM |
| **4:0** | **M[4:0]** | Mode bits | Select current operating mode |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Saved Program Status Register (SPSR)** is a per-mode register that **saves a copy of CPSR** when an exception occurs. The CPSR is restored from SPSR on exception return.

## 2.5 Conditional Execution — ARM's Superpower

ARM instructions can be **conditionally executed** by appending a 2-letter condition code. The processor checks the CPSR flags and **skips the instruction** if the condition is false (no branch penalty!).

| Code | Suffix | Condition Checked |
|:----:|:------:|:------------------|
| **0000** | EQ | Z = 1 (Equal) |
| **0001** | NE | Z = 0 (Not equal) |
| **0010** | CS/HS | C = 1 (Carry set / Unsigned higher or same) |
| **0011** | CC/LO | C = 0 (Carry clear / Unsigned lower) |
| **0100** | MI | N = 1 (Negative) |
| **0101** | PL | N = 0 (Positive or zero) |
| **0110** | VS | V = 1 (Overflow) |
| **0111** | VC | V = 0 (No overflow) |
| **1000** | HI | C = 1 AND Z = 0 (Unsigned higher) |
| **1001** | LS | C = 0 OR Z = 1 (Unsigned lower or same) |
| **1010** | GE | N = V (Signed greater or equal) |
| **1011** | LT | N ≠ V (Signed less than) |
| **1100** | GT | Z = 0 AND N = V (Signed greater than) |
| **1101** | LE | Z = 1 OR N ≠ V (Signed less or equal) |
| **1110** | AL | Always executed |
| **1111** | NV | Never (historically for unconditional) |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** ARM's conditional execution **eliminates short branches**, reducing pipeline flushes and improving IoT energy efficiency.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|:---|:---|:---|
| ARM instruction size | $L_{inst} = 32 \text{ bits}$ | ARM state |
| Thumb instruction size | $L_{inst} = 16 \text{ bits}$ | Thumb state |
| Code density ratio (Thumb/ARM) | $\rho \approx 0.65$ | Thumb saves ~35% memory |
| Effective PC (ARM state) | $PC_{eff} = PC_{current} + 8$ | Pipeline of 3 stages |
| Throughput (3-stage pipeline) | $CPI_{avg} = 1 + \text{stalls}$ | Ideal = 1 instruction/cycle |
| Branch Penalty (unconditional) | $P_{branch} = 1 \text{ cycle}$ | Pipeline flush |
| Branch Penalty (conditional not taken) | $P_{branch} = 0$ | Conditional exec advantage |
| Carry flag (addition) | $C = \lfloor (A + B + C_{in}) / 2^{32} \rfloor$ | Bit 31 carry out |
| Overflow flag (signed) | $V = (A_{31} \oplus B_{31} \oplus R_{31}) \cdot \overline{C_{in\_borrow}}$ | $A_{31} = B_{31} \neq R_{31}$ rule |
| Address bus width (LPC2148) | $N_{addr} = 32$ | 4 GB addressable |
| Memory map (Flash) | $0x0000\,0000$ to $0x0007\,FFFF$ | 512 KB on LPC2148 |
| Memory map (SRAM) | $0x4000\,0000$ to $0x4000\,FFFF$ | 32 KB on LPC2148 |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effective PC Value in ARM State

A common exam question asks: "Why does ARM show `PC = current + 8`?" The answer lies in the **3-stage pipeline** (Fetch, Decode, Execute).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stage 1 (Fetch):} &\quad \text{Instruction at } PC_{old} \text{ is being fetched} \\
\text{Stage 2 (Decode):} &\quad \text{Instruction at } PC_{old} + 4 \text{ is being decoded} \\
\text{Stage 3 (Execute):} &\quad \text{Instruction at } PC_{old} + 8 \text{ is executing} \\
\text{When CPU reads R15 (PC):} &\quad \text{It returns the address of the instruction 2 ahead} \\
\therefore PC_{read} &= PC_{current\,execute} + 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Logic:** Because pipelining means the CPU is always 2 instructions ahead, reading R15 (PC) returns the address of the instruction **two words** (2 × 4 bytes = 8 bytes) ahead. This is why `MOV R0, PC` actually loads `current+8` in ARM state and `current+4` in Thumb state.

## 3.2 Derivation: Carry and Overflow Flag Logic

For an unsigned addition `A + B = Result`:

$$
C_{out} = \text{Bit 31 carry from bit position 31}
$$

Algebraically, for two 32-bit unsigned integers $A$ and $B$:

$$
C = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } (A + B) \geq 2^{32} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

For the **V (overflow) flag** in signed arithmetic, overflow occurs when the sign bit of the result is **incorrect**:

$$
V = A_{31} \cdot B_{31} \cdot \overline{R_{31}} + \overline{A_{31}} \cdot \overline{B_{31}} \cdot R_{31}
$$

In words: **V = 1 if both operands have the same sign but the result has the opposite sign.**

### Worked Example: $0x7FFFFFFF + 0x00000001$ (signed)

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 0x7\text{FFFF\,FFFF} = +2{,}147{,}483{,}647 \quad (A_{31} = 0) \\
B &= 0x0000\,0001 = +1 \quad (B_{31} = 0) \\
R &= 0x8000\,0000 = -2{,}147{,}483{,}648 \quad (R_{31} = 1) \\
N &= R_{31} = 1 \quad \text{(Negative flag set)} \\
Z &= 0 \\
C &= 0 \quad \text{(no unsigned overflow, but...)} \\
V &= A_{31} \cdot B_{31} \cdot \overline{R_{31}} + \overline{A_{31}} \cdot \overline{B_{31}} \cdot R_{31} \\
V &= 0 \cdot 0 \cdot 0 + 1 \cdot 1 \cdot 1 = 1 \quad \text{(SIGNED OVERFLOW!)} \\
\text{CPSR} &= 0x3000\,0000 \quad (N=1, V=1)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** "C is for Carry (unsigned), V is for oVerflow (signed)."

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: ARM vs Thumb

| Parameter | ARM State | Thumb State | Trade-off |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Instruction width | **32 bits** | **16 bits** | Thumb saves memory |
| Register access | All 16 visible | R0–R7 only (R8–R15 limited) | ARM has more flexibility |
| Conditional execution | All 16 conditions | Only `B` (conditional branch) | ARM = branchless code |
| PC offset when read | +8 bytes | +4 bytes | Pipelining artifact |
| Memory bandwidth | 1 instruction = 4 bytes | 1 instruction = 2 bytes | Thumb = 2× density |
| Performance (MHz) | Higher per instruction | Lower per instruction | ARM = faster code |
| Best use case | Performance-critical | Memory-constrained IoT | Designer choice |

## 3.4 Addressing Modes of ARM7

ARM supports **9 addressing modes** — a high-yield KTU topic.

| Mode | Syntax Example | Effective Address | Use |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Immediate** | `MOV R0, #0x25` | Value is constant in instruction | Loading constants |
| **Register** | `MOV R0, R1` | Operand is in R1 | Direct copy |
| **Register (shifted)** | `ADD R0, R1, R2, LSL #2` | $R1 + (R2 \ll 2)$ | Multiply by 4 (array index) |
| **Register indirect** | `LDR R0, [R1]` | Memory at address in R1 | Pointer dereference |
| **Pre-indexed** | `LDR R0, [R1, #4]` | $R1 + 4$ → read, **R1 unchanged** | Array access |
| **Pre-indexed (writeback)** | `LDR R0, [R1, #4]!` | $R1 + 4$ → read, **R1 = R1 + 4** | Pointer increment |
| **Post-indexed** | `LDR R0, [R1], #4` | Read from R1, **then R1 = R1 + 4** | Pointer post-update |
| **PC-relative** | `LDR R0, [PC, #offset]` | $PC + 8 + \text{offset}$ | Loading literals from pool |
| **Multiple register** | `LDMIA R0!, {R1–R4}` | Block transfer | Stack push/pop |

## 3.5 LPC2148 Memory Map (Analytical Layout)

The LPC2148 uses a **unified 4 GB address space** divided into functional regions:

| Address Range | Size | Function |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| `0x0000 0000 – 0x0007 FFFF` | 512 KB | On-chip Flash (program memory) |
| `0x4000 0000 – 0x4000 7FFF` | 32 KB | On-chip SRAM (data memory) |
| `0x8000 0000 – 0x80FF FFFF` | 16 MB | Boot block (remapped) |
| `0xE000 0000 – 0xEFFF FFFF` | 256 MB | APB peripherals (UART, I2C, SPI, ADC) |
| `0xFFFF 0000 – 0xFFFF FFFF` | 64 KB | AHB peripherals (Vectored Interrupt Controller) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Pearl:** Boot pins **P0.14** (ISP entry) and **P0.31** determine whether the LPC2148 boots from Flash (`0x0000 0000`) or ISP/IAP. A pull-down on P0.14 selects Flash boot.

## 3.6 GPIO Programming Model on LPC2148

The **General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO)** peripheral occupies specific APB registers. Each pin has 4 controlling registers:

| Register | Address (P0) | Function | Bit Direction |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---:|
| **IO0PIN** | `0xE002 8000` | Read/Write pin state | R/W |
| **IO0SET** | `0xE002 8004` | Set pin high (1 = drive high) | W |
| **IO0CLR** | `0xE002 8008` | Clear pin low (1 = drive low) | W |
| **IO0DIR** | `0xE002 800C` | Direction (1 = output, 0 = input) | R/W |

### Step-by-Step: Blinking P0.0 LED using GPIO

**Step 1 — Set direction (output):**
Write `1` to bit 0 of `IO0DIR`:
$$
IO0DIR \mathrel{|}= 0x00000001
$$

**Step 2 — Drive pin HIGH:**
$$
IO0SET = 0x00000001
$$

**Step 3 — Drive pin LOW (after delay):**
$$
IO0CLR = 0x00000001
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Writing to **IO0PIN** is valid but has a "read-modify-write" hazard. Always prefer **IO0SET** and **IO0CLR** for atomic bit-set/bit-clear operations to prevent race conditions in ISRs.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ARM7TDMI-S Internal Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[External Address Bus A31-A0] --> B[Address Register]
    C[External Data Bus D31-D0] --> D[Write Data Register]
    D --> E[Read Data Register]
    E --> C
    B --> F[Barrel Shifter]
    F --> G[ALU 32-bit]
    G --> H[Multiplier Booth 32x8]
    H --> I[Register Bank R0-R15]
    I --> F
    I --> J[Instruction Decoder]
    J --> K[Control Logic State Machine]
    K --> B
    K --> D
    I --> L[CPSR Current Program Status Register]
    I --> M[SPSR Saved Program Status Register]
    N[Exception Vector Table] --> K
    O[EmbeddedICE Logic] --> I
    P[JTAG TAP Controller] --> O
    subgraph core_units
        G
        H
        F
        I
        L
        M
    end
```

## 4.2 ARM Processor Mode and Register Visibility Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USR
        U_R0[R0] --- U_R1[R1] --- U_R2[R2] --- U_R3[R3] --- U_R4[R4] --- U_R5[R5] --- U_R6[R6] --- U_R7[R7] --- U_R8[R8] --- U_R9[R9] --- U_R10[R10] --- U_R11[R11] --- U_R12[R12] --- U_R13_USR[R13 SP] --- U_R14_USR[R14 LR] --- U_R15[R15 PC]
    end
    subgraph SVC
        S_R13[R13 SP_svc] --- S_R14[R14 LR_svc] --- S_SPSR[SPSR_svc]
    end
    subgraph IRQ
        I_R13[R13 SP_irq] --- I_R14[R14 LR_irq] --- I_SPSR[SPSR_irq]
    end
    subgraph FIQ
        F_R8[R8_fiq] --- F_R9[R9_fiq] --- F_R10[R10_fiq] --- F_R11[R11_fiq] --- F_R12[R12_fiq] --- F_R13[R13 SP_fiq] --- F_R14[R14 LR_fiq] --- F_SPSR[SPSR_fiq]
    end
    subgraph ABT
        A_R13[R13 SP_abt] --- A_R14[R14 LR_abt] --- A_SPSR[SPSR_abt]
    end
    subgraph UND
        Un_R13[R13 SP_und] --- Un_R14[R14 LR_und] --- Un_SPSR[SPSR_und]
    end
    USR -.same physical register.-> SVC
    USR -.same physical register.-> IRQ
    USR -.same physical register.-> FIQ
```

## 4.3 ARM 3-Stage Pipeline Timing Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1["Cycle 1"] --> A1["F: I1 instr fetch"]
    C2["Cycle 2"] --> A2["D: I1 decode | F: I2 fetch"]
    C3["Cycle 3"] --> A3["E: I1 execute | D: I2 decode | F: I3 fetch"]
    C4["Cycle 4"] --> A4["E: I2 execute | D: I3 decode | F: I4 fetch"]
    C5["Cycle 5"] --> A5["E: I3 execute | D: I4 decode | F: I5 fetch"]
    A1 ~~~ A2 ~~~ A3 ~~~ A4 ~~~ A5
```

**Pipeline Behavior Notes:**
- **F = Fetch** | **D = Decode** | **E = Execute**
- After the pipeline is **filled** (3 cycles), one instruction completes **per cycle** (CPI = 1).
- A **branch instruction** causes a **pipeline flush** (3-cycle penalty unless conditionally skipped).

## 4.4 Exception Handling Flow (Vectored Entry)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Exception Occurs e.g. IRQ, SWI, Reset] --> B[Save CPSR into SPSR of new mode]
    B --> C[Set LR_mode = return address link register]
    C --> D[Force PC to vector address 0x0000_0018 for IRQ]
    D --> E[Disable further IRQs by setting I bit in CPSR]
    E --> F[Execute exception handler from vector table]
    F --> G[On return MOVS PC, LR14 restores CPSR from SPSR]
    G --> H[Resume normal execution in previous mode]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Vector Table for LPC2148:** Reset=`0x0000_0000`, Undefined=`0x0000_0004`, SWI=`0x0000_0008`, Prefetch Abort=`0x0000_000C`, Data Abort=`0x0000_0010`, IRQ=`0x0000_0018`, FIQ=`0x0000_001C`.
