# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Paper)]
**"List and briefly explain any three fundamental building blocks of an IoT system with one real-world example for each."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

1. **Sensors and Actuators** — A *sensor* converts a physical quantity (e.g., temperature, humidity, motion) into an electrical signal. *Actuators* convert electrical signals back into physical action. **Example:** A DHT22 temperature/humidity sensor in a smart thermostat and a servo motor in a smart curtain.

2. **Microcontrollers / Microprocessors (Processing Core)** — Acts as the brain of the IoT device, executing firmware, performing local data processing, and managing peripheral interfaces. **Example:** An ESP32 microcontroller inside a Wi-Fi smart plug.

3. **Communication Modules** — Enable wireless data exchange between the IoT device and the network/gateway using protocols like Wi-Fi, BLE, ZigBee, or LoRa. **Example:** A LoRa module inside a soil-moisture sensor node in a smart farm.

*(Alternative valid answers: Cloud/Edge Computing, Power Management, Security Subsystem)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Differentiate between a sensor and an actuator. Give one IoT application where both are used together."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Sensor | Actuator |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Direction of Energy** | Physical → Electrical | Electrical → Physical |
| **Function** | *Measures* a quantity | *Acts* upon a quantity |
| **Output** | Analog voltage / digital word | Motion / heat / light / sound |
| **Examples** | Thermistor, LDR, accelerometer | DC motor, relay, buzzer, solenoid |

**Combined Application:** A **smart air conditioner** — a temperature sensor (thermistor) measures room temperature; the microcontroller compares it to a setpoint; if hot, it signals a relay/actuator to switch on the compressor. This is a **closed-loop IoT control system** in a single device.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**(a) Explain the architecture of an IoT system in terms of its five building blocks with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each block with suitable examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The IoT system is built on five fundamental building blocks, each performing a distinct function:

**1. Sensors and Actuators (Transducer Block):**
This is the **front-line interface** between the physical world and the digital world. Sensors (LM35 for temperature, MQ-135 for gas, ADXL345 for acceleration) gather raw data; actuators (relays, DC motors, solenoids, LEDs) act upon the environment based on decisions. **Example:** A MQ-135 gas sensor in an industrial safety alarm.

**2. Processing Block (Microcontroller/Microprocessor):**
The "brain" runs the firmware, executes control algorithms, performs ADC conversion, and coordinates all peripherals. For end-nodes, a low-power MCU (e.g., **ESP32, STM32, ATmega328**) is preferred; for gateways, a higher-power MPU (e.g., **Raspberry Pi 4, Intel Atom**) is used. **Example:** ESP32 in a Wi-Fi smart bulb.

**3. Communication Block (Connectivity):**
Handles the wireless (or wired) data transfer between the device and the network. Includes radio modules (ESP32's integrated Wi-Fi/BLE, external LoRa SX1276, NB-IoT modules like Quectel BC95). **Example:** BLE module in a heart-rate strap.

**4. Cloud / Edge Computing Block (Data Plane):**
Provides scalable storage, analytics, and visualization. Edge computing pushes processing closer to the device for **low-latency** and **bandwidth-saving** reasons. **Example:** AWS IoT Core ingesting temperature data from 10,000 sensors globally.

**5. Power Management & Security Block (Support Plane):**
The PMIC (e.g., TI BQ24075) regulates battery voltage; sleep modes reduce average power to μA. Security primitives (AES, SHA, secure boot, TLS) protect data integrity and confidentiality. **Example:** Secure-boot-enabled firmware on a medical IoT infusion pump.

**Block Diagram (text form):**

```
   [SENSOR] -- signal --> [MCU] -- data --> [COMM MODULE] -- radio --> [CLOUD]
                                |                                              |
                                v                                              v
                           [ACTUATOR] <-- command --------------------[DASHBOARD]
                               
   [POWER SUPPLY] energizes entire chain
   [SECURITY MODULE] protects MCU and COMM at every step
```

---

**(b) Compare Microprocessor (MPU) and Microcontroller (MCU) based on architecture, power, OS support, and IoT applicability. Why is an MCU preferred over an MPU for battery-powered IoT end-nodes? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Microprocessor (MPU) | Microcontroller (MCU) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Architecture** | CPU only; RAM/ROM/peripherals are **external** | CPU + RAM + ROM + peripherals on a **single chip (SoC)** |
| **Clock Speed** | High (1–5 GHz) | Moderate (16–240 MHz) |
| **Power Consumption** | High (1–50 W) | Low (μW to mW) |
| **Operating System** | Full OS (Linux, Windows, Android) | RTOS (FreeRTOS) or **bare-metal** |
| **Cost** | Higher (>$10) | Low ($0.50–$5) |
| **Footprint** | Large PCB required | Compact single-board designs |
| **IoT Applicability** | Edge gateways, local servers | Battery-powered end-nodes |
| **Examples** | Intel Core, ARM Cortex-A, RPi SoC | ESP32, STM32, ATmega328, MSP430 |

**Why MCU is preferred for battery-powered IoT end-nodes:**

1. **Ultra-low Sleep Current:** MCUs offer multiple sleep modes (e.g., ESP32 deep sleep at **10 μA**), allowing battery life of **months to years**. MPUs in contrast, consume watts even in idle states.

2. **Integrated Peripherals:** Built-in ADC, PWM, I²C, SPI, UART, and timers eliminate the need for external ICs, reducing **BOM cost** and PCB area — critical for miniaturized IoT nodes.

3. **Fast Wake-up Time:** MCUs wake from sleep in **microseconds**, allowing aggressive duty-cycling. MPUs boot in **seconds**, making them unsuitable for intermittent operation.

4. **Cost and Scalability:** A $2 MCU enables deployment of thousands of nodes (smart agriculture, smart metering). An $50 MPU cannot scale economically.

5. **Determinism:** Bare-metal or RTOS-based MCU firmware delivers **real-time, deterministic response** essential for industrial control loops, whereas OS-based MPUs have non-deterministic scheduling latency.

**Conclusion:** The MCU is the **de-facto processing block of battery-powered IoT end-nodes**, while MPUs are reserved for **gateway-class devices** that aggregate data from many MCUs and need full OS support for protocol translation and local analytics.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the IoT protocol stack. Compare MQTT and CoAP application-layer protocols in terms of transport, message model, and suitability for constrained devices. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**IoT Protocol Stack (5-Layer Reference Model):**

```
   +-------------------------------------+
   |   APPLICATION LAYER (MQTT, CoAP,    |
   |   HTTP, WebSocket)                  |
   +-------------------------------------+
   |   TRANSPORT LAYER (TCP, UDP, DTLS)  |
   +-------------------------------------+
   |   NETWORK LAYER (IPv6, 6LoWPAN,     |
   |   RPL)                              |
   +-------------------------------------+
   |   DATA LINK LAYER (IEEE 802.15.4,   |
   |   BLE, LTE MAC)                     |
   +-------------------------------------+
   |   PHYSICAL LAYER (Sub-1 GHz,        |
   |   2.4 GHz, 5 GHz RF)                |
   +-------------------------------------+
```

The IoT stack adapts the classical OSI model by merging or omitting certain layers. For instance, **6LoWPAN** allows IPv6 packets to be carried over low-power IEEE 802.15.4 frames, enabling direct Internet addressing for constrained devices.

**Comparison: MQTT vs CoAP**

| Feature | MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport) | CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Standard** | OASIS Standard (ISO/IEC 20922) | IETF RFC 7252 |
| **Transport** | TCP (port 1883 / 8883 for TLS) | UDP (port 5683 / 5684 for DTLS) |
| **Message Model** | **Publish/Subscribe** (asynchronous) | **Request/Response** (synchronous, RESTful) |
| **Header Size** | 2 bytes minimum | 4 bytes minimum |
| **Broker Required** | Yes (central broker: Mosquitto, HiveMQ) | No (peer-to-peer) |
| **Reliability** | 3 QoS levels (0, 1, 2) | Confirmable (CON) and Non-confirmable (NON) messages |
| **Suitability** | High-latency, intermittent, many-to-many | Low-latency, one-to-one, REST-style |
| **Use Case** | Telemetry, sensor data to cloud | Smart energy, lighting control, M2M |
| **Constrained Devices** | Requires TCP stack (more RAM ~30 KB) | UDP-friendly (works with ~10 KB RAM) |

**Conclusion:** MQTT is **best for cloud-centric telemetry** where devices are behind NAT/firewalls. CoAP is **best for resource-constrained, peer-to-peer constrained networks** that need RESTful semantics.

---

**(b) Define Nyquist sampling theorem. An IoT temperature node uses a 12-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V. Calculate (i) the resolution per LSB, (ii) the quantization step in °C if the sensor spans 0–100 °C over the full ADC range, and (iii) the minimum sampling rate to capture temperature variations up to 10 Hz. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of Nyquist Theorem:**
The Nyquist sampling theorem states that to accurately reconstruct a **band-limited analog signal** with maximum frequency $f_{max}$, the sampling rate $f_s$ must be **at least twice** the maximum frequency:

$$
f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}
$$

The minimum sampling rate $2 \cdot f_{max}$ is called the **Nyquist rate**.

**(i) Resolution per LSB:**

$$
V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{3.3}{2^{12} - 1} = \frac{3.3}{4095} \approx 805.86 \text{ μV}
$$

**(ii) Quantization step in °C:**

The full-scale range corresponds to 100 °C spread across $2^{12} - 1 = 4095$ steps.

$$
\Delta T = \frac{T_{FS}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{100 \text{ °C}}{4095} \approx 0.0244 \text{ °C/LSB}
$$

**(iii) Minimum sampling rate:**

$$
f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max} = 2 \times 10 \text{ Hz} = 20 \text{ Hz}
$$

In practice, an oversampling factor of 4–10× is used for noise reduction, so a sampling rate of 100 Hz is recommended for this 10 Hz signal.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in "IoT Building Blocks" Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing layers with blocks:** Students often write "Perception Layer, Network Layer, Application Layer" when asked for *building blocks*. The KTU 2024 Module 2 expects the **five hardware/software component blocks** (Sensors, MCU, Communication, Cloud/Edge, Power + Security). Marks lost: 2–3.
> 
> 2. **MQTT vs HTTP confusion:** Writing "MQTT uses HTTP" is a fatal error. MQTT uses TCP directly. CoAP mimics REST over UDP. Marks lost: 2.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting units in derivations:** A formula like $V_{LSB} = V_{ref}/2^n$ without numerical substitution gets **partial credit only**. Always compute the final value with units.
> 
> 4. **Writing "Microcontroller = CPU":** This is **wrong**. An MCU is a CPU + memory + peripherals on one chip. Marks lost: 1.
> 
> 5. **Skipping the "Why" in design questions:** When asked "Why is BLE used in wearables?", saying "because it is wireless" gets 1/3 marks. The expected answer is "because BLE has **2 Mbps data rate, ultra-low sleep current (μA), and short range sufficient for body-area networks**."
> 
> 6. **Forgetting Security:** Security is **not a separate block** in the KTU 2024 view but a **cross-cutting concern**. Stating "the 6th block is security" loses a mark; correct framing is "security is embedded within all five blocks."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — IoT Building Blocks (Print This Before Exam)**

- [ ] **IoT = Interconnected physical devices with sensors, software, and connectivity.**
- [ ] **Five building blocks:** Sensors/Actuators, MCU/MPU, Communication, Cloud/Edge, Power/Security.
- [ ] **Sensor** = Physical → Electrical; **Actuator** = Electrical → Physical.
- [ ] **MCU** integrates CPU + RAM + ROM + peripherals; **MPU** has CPU only. MCUs are for end-nodes; MPUs for gateways.
- [ ] **Nyquist rate:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. ADC resolution: $V_{LSB} = V_{ref}/(2^n - 1)$.
- [ ] **Wi-Fi:** High bandwidth, high power, short range. **LoRa:** Long range, low bandwidth, ultra-low power. **BLE:** Short range, ultra-low power, 2 Mbps PHY.
- [ ] **MQTT:** TCP-based, Publish/Subscribe, broker-based. **CoAP:** UDP-based, Request/Response, RESTful. **MQTT for cloud, CoAP for constrained peer-to-peer.**
- [ ] **Edge computing** reduces latency and bandwidth by processing near the data source.
- [ ] **Sleep current** (μA) dominates battery life in duty-cycled IoT nodes.
- [ ] **Security is cross-cutting** — secure boot, TLS/DTLS, hardware root of trust, AES/SHA accelerators.
- [ ] **6 dB per bit rule:** Each extra ADC bit adds ~6 dB SQNR.
- [ ] **Three-Layer Model** = Perception + Network + Application. **Five-Layer Model** adds Transport + Processing + Business. **KTU uses Five Blocks view.**
- [ ] **Remember the Friis equation** for link budget: $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda/4\pi d)^2$ — essential for 7-mark design questions.
- [ ] **Use $\vert$ not $\mid$ in Markdown tables** — not in exam, but in your notes!

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT Boards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT503 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** IoT Boards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — IoT Boards: Section 1

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

An **IoT Board** is a compact, integrated printed-circuit assembly that combines a microprocessor or microcontroller unit (MCU/MPU), memory (volatile + non-volatile), wireless/wired communication transceivers, power regulation circuitry, and standardized general-purpose input/output (GPIO) headers on a single PCB. These boards act as the **edge-computing substrate** of any Internet of Things (IoT) deployment, executing the sensing, conditioning, computation, and actuation tasks that bridge the physical world to cloud infrastructure.

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus (PCCGT503), IoT boards are formally classified under two architectural families:

1. **Microcontroller-based Boards** (CISC/RISC MCU + on-chip radio): Arduino Uno, Arduino Mega, NodeMCU (ESP8266), ESP32 DevKit, STM32 Nucleo.
2. **Microprocessor-based Single-Board Computers (SBC)** (full MPU + OS + radio): Raspberry Pi 4/5, BeagleBone Black, ASUS Tinker Board.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Distinction Note:** The examiner specifically tests whether students can differentiate between a *microcontroller-class* board (resource-constrained, deterministic, RTOS-friendly) and a *microprocessor-class* SBC (rich OS, multi-tasking, higher power). Misclassifying an ESP32 as a "microprocessor" or a Raspberry Pi as a "microcontroller" is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction** in the valuation key.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Postman" Model

Think of an IoT board as a **highly skilled postman working in a small village** (the *edge* of a network):

- The **MCU/MPU** is the postman's *brain* — decides which letter (data packet) to carry.
- The **Sensors** (temperature, humidity, motion) are the *villagers* creating the letters.
- The **Wi-Fi/Bluetooth radio** is the postman's *bicycle* — transports letters to the city (the Cloud).
- The **GPIO pins** are the *hooks* on his bag — to hang parcels (actuators, LEDs, relays).
- The **Voltage Regulator** is his *uniform* — keeps him functioning properly in any weather (supply fluctuation from **3.3 V** to **5 V** to **12 V**).

Unlike a heavy postal truck (a full PC), this postman is small, cheap, low-power, and purpose-built for one route — exactly the philosophy behind every IoT board from Arduino to ESP32.

> [!NOTE]
> The term **"Board"** (as opposed to "chip") emphasizes the *ready-to-deploy* nature — power input, USB programming, and pin headers are already soldered, making IoT boards the de-facto choice for prototyping under the KTU lab curriculum.

### 1.3 Why IoT Boards Matter in the 2024 Scheme

The 2024 NEP-aligned KTU syllabus mandates IoT-board literacy because:

- They expose students to **bare-metal embedded programming** (register-level C, Arduino sketches, MicroPython).
- They support the **CO2 outcome**: *"Design IoT systems using appropriate microcontrollers and communication protocols."*
- They integrate with the **EST 300 / EST 310** lab tracks, where students physically deploy sensor nodes.

Standard metrics a student MUST commit to memory (frequently asked in Part A):

| Metric | Typical Range in IoT Boards |
|---|---|
| Operating Voltage | **3.3 V** or **5 V** |
| Clock Frequency | **8 MHz – 1.8 GHz** |
| Flash Memory | **32 KB – 32 MB** |
| SRAM | **2 KB – 8 GB** |
| Active Current | **20 mA – 700 mA** |
| Sleep Current | **< 10 µA** (ESP32 deep-sleep) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 2 — IoT Boards: Section 2

## 2.1 Deep Theoretical Analysis of the IoT Board Architecture

Every IoT board — whether a $4 NodeMCU or a $75 Raspberry Pi 5 — implements the same canonical **five-block functional architecture**. Mastering this block decomposition is a *high-yield* KTU scoring area.

### Block 1: The Processing Core
- **Microcontroller boards** (Arduino, ESP32): A single chip containing ALU, registers, program counter, and on-chip Flash/SRAM. Examples: ATmega328P (8-bit AVR), Xtensa LX6/LX7 (32-bit), ARM Cortex-M4.
- **Microprocessor boards** (RPi, BeagleBone): A full application processor (Cortex-A72) requiring external RAM and storage; runs a full OS (Raspberry Pi OS / Debian).

### Block 2: Memory Subsystem
- **Non-volatile storage**: Flash (code + data); on-chip for MCUs, microSD/SSD for SBCs.
- **Volatile storage**: SRAM (runtime variables), DRAM (for SBCs).
- **EEPROM**: Tiny byte-addressable non-volatile area (e.g., ATmega328P provides **1024 bytes**) for configuration parameters.

### Block 3: Communication Interfaces
- **Wired**: UART, I²C (TWI), SPI, USB, CAN, Ethernet.
- **Wireless**: Wi-Fi (802.11 b/g/n), Bluetooth/BLE, LoRa, Zigbee, NB-IoT (board-dependent).

### Block 4: Power Management Unit (PMU)
- Linear regulators (LDO): AMS1117-3.3 / LM7805.
- Switching regulators (buck/boost): For battery-powered boards (e.g., ESP32 battery shield).
- Sleep-mode controllers: Critical for battery-life engineering.

### Block 5: GPIO and Peripheral Expansion
- Digital I/O, ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter), PWM (Pulse Width Modulation), DAC (Digital-to-Analog Converter), timers, interrupts.

> [!NOTE]
> **The KTU Board Selection Litmus Test** — Before designing any IoT node, the 2024 scheme mandates that students justify board selection using three questions: *(1) Does the board have on-board radio? (2) What is its deep-sleep current? (3) Can it run the target protocol stack?* These are repeatedly tested in Part B (14-mark) design questions.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CPU Cycle Time** | $T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}}$ | Seconds; e.g., 16 MHz Arduino → **62.5 ns** |
| 2 | **Instruction Throughput** | $MIPS = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$ | Millions of Instructions/sec |
| 3 | **Average Power** | $P_{avg} = V_{DD} \times I_{active} \times t_{active} + V_{DD} \times I_{sleep} \times t_{sleep}$ | Watts; used for battery-life estimation |
| 4 | **Battery Life** | $t_{life} = \dfrac{C_{batt} \times V_{batt}}{P_{avg}} \times \eta$ | Hours; $\eta$ = regulator efficiency (typ. 0.85) |
| 5 | **ADC Resolution** | $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}-1}$ | Volts/LSB; $n$ = ADC bits (10/12-bit) |
| 6 | **Nyquist Sampling** | $f_{s} \geq 2 \times f_{max}$ | Hz; minimum sampling rate |
| 7 | **Wi-Fi Data Rate (theoretical)** | $R = N_{SC} \times N_{bits\_per\_symbol} \times \dfrac{1}{T_{sym}}$ | bps; OFDM-based |
| 8 | **Free-Space Path Loss** | $FSPL = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{c}\right)$ | dB; $d$ in m, $f$ in Hz |
| 9 | **PWM Duty Cycle** | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on}+T_{off}} \times 100\%$ | %; used for LED dimming, motor control |
| 10 | **Energy per Sample** | $E_{sample} = V_{DD} \times I_{active} \times \dfrac{1}{f_{s}}$ | Joules; edge-AI design metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the markdown table above, the absolute-value / division bar uses `\dfrac{}{}` instead of a single pipe `|` character. **Never** write `$|x|$` inside a KTU table row — the markdown parser will break the cell. The same rule applies to all subsequent tables.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

IoT boards are the **first-mile hardware** of every smart system:
- **Precision agriculture**: ESP32 + soil-moisture sensor, LoRa backhaul.
- **Industrial Predictive Maintenance**: STM32 + vibration sensor → MQTT broker.
- **Smart Health Wearables**: Arduino Nano 33 BLE + heart-rate sensor → BLE phone gateway.
- **Home Automation**: Raspberry Pi as the home gateway, with ESP8266 nodes for switches.

The 2024 KTU syllabus emphasizes that **selection of the right board is itself 30% of the design mark** — students must defend the choice in terms of power budget, connectivity, and compute class.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# MODULE 2 — IoT Boards: Section 3

## 3.1 Exhaustive Analytical Derivations

The derivations below are the **highest-weight numerical questions** the KTU board sets under "IoT Boards" / "Performance Analysis."

### 3.1.1 Derivation of Battery Life for an ESP32 NodeMCU Sensor Node

> **KTU-Style Problem (similar to Dec 2023 model):**
> An IoT node uses an ESP32 DevKit (operating voltage **3.3 V**). It is powered by a single 18650 Li-ion battery rated at **3000 mAh** and **3.7 V** nominal. The node wakes every 60 s, takes a sensor reading, transmits a 2 KB packet over Wi-Fi, and returns to deep-sleep. Active current = **120 mA**, active duration = **3 s**. Deep-sleep current = **10 µA**. LDO efficiency $\eta$ = 0.85. Compute the **battery life in days**.

**Step 1 — Convert Battery Capacity to Coulombs**

$$
C_{batt} = 3000 \text{ mAh} = 3.0 \text{ Ah} = 3.0 \times 3600 \text{ C} = 10800 \text{ C}
$$

Text: The 18650 cell stores 3.0 Ampere-hours; converting to Coulombs (1 Ah = 3600 C) yields the total charge available.

**Step 2 — Identify Duty Cycle and Effective Average Current**

$$
t_{active} = 3 \text{ s},\quad t_{cycle} = 60 \text{ s},\quad t_{sleep} = 57 \text{ s}
$$

Text: Of every 60-second period, only 3 seconds are spent actively running and transmitting.

$$
I_{avg} = I_{active}\cdot\frac{t_{active}}{t_{cycle}} + I_{sleep}\cdot\frac{t_{sleep}}{t_{cycle}}
$$

$$
I_{avg} = 120\text{ mA}\cdot\frac{3}{60} + 0.01\text{ mA}\cdot\frac{57}{60}
$$

$$
I_{avg} = 6.000 \text{ mA} + 0.0095 \text{ mA} = 6.0095 \text{ mA}
$$

Text: The active phase dominates despite being brief — this is the classic IoT power trade-off.

**Step 3 — Account for Regulator Efficiency**

$$
I_{batt} = \frac{I_{avg}}{\eta} = \frac{6.0095 \text{ mA}}{0.85} = 7.070 \text{ mA}
$$

Text: The LDO burns 15% of the energy as heat, so the battery must deliver slightly more current.

**Step 4 — Battery Life in Hours**

$$
t_{life\_h} = \frac{3000 \text{ mAh}}{7.070 \text{ mA}} = 424.33 \text{ h}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Days**

$$
t_{life\_days} = \frac{424.33}{24} \approx 17.68 \text{ days}
$$

Text: A real-world ESP32 sensor node on a single 18650 cell therefore lasts roughly **17–18 days** — a figure the KTU examiner loves because it requires correct unit-conversion discipline.

### 3.1.2 Derivation of ADC Resolution for an Arduino Analog Pin

The Arduino Uno's ATmega328P features a **10-bit** successive-approximation ADC with a default reference voltage of **5.0 V**.

**Step 1 — Compute Voltage per LSB**

$$
V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1} = \frac{5.0}{2^{10} - 1} = \frac{5.0}{1023} = 4.888 \text{ mV/LSB}
$$

Text: Each step of the 10-bit output (0–1023) corresponds to ≈ 4.89 mV on the analog pin.

**Step 2 — Input Voltage for a Digital Reading of $D$**

$$
V_{in} = D \times V_{LSB}
$$

Text: For example, a digital reading of **512** implies $V_{in} = 512 \times 4.888 \text{ mV} \approx 2.502 \text{ V}$.

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Required Sampling Rate (Nyquist-Shannon)

A vibration sensor outputs a maximum frequency of **1.2 kHz**. The board's ADC must satisfy:

$$
f_{s} \geq 2 \times f_{max} = 2 \times 1200 = 2400 \text{ samples/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Practical Multiplier (Anti-aliasing headroom)**

$$
f_{s,\text{practical}} = k \times 2 f_{max}, \quad k = 2.5
$$

$$
f_{s,\text{practical}} = 2.5 \times 2400 = 6000 \text{ samples/s}
$$

Text: A 2×–3× oversampling factor is industry-standard to relax anti-aliasing filter design. An ESP32 ADC easily handles this; an Arduino Uno (≈ 15 kS/s) also qualifies.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Major IoT Boards

The KTU valuation key awards full 14 marks only to answers that include a **justified comparison**. Below is the canonical table students should reproduce (memorize the **bolded** figures):

| Parameter | **Arduino Uno R3** | **NodeMCU (ESP8266)** | **ESP32 DevKit V1** | **Raspberry Pi 4 B** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Processor | ATmega328P | Tensilica L106 | Xtensa LX6 (dual) | BCM2711 Cortex-A72 (quad) |
| Architecture | **8-bit AVR** | 32-bit RISC | **32-bit RISC** | **64-bit ARM** |
| Clock | 16 MHz | 80–160 MHz | 80–240 MHz | 1.5–1.8 GHz |
| Flash | **32 KB** | 1–4 MB | 4 MB | microSD up to **2 TB** |
| SRAM | 2 KB | 80 KB | 320 KB | 1/2/4/8 GB LPDDR4 |
| Wi-Fi | ✗ | **802.11 b/g/n** | **802.11 b/g/n** | **802.11 ac + 5 GHz** |
| Bluetooth | ✗ | ✗ | **BLE 4.2** | **BLE 5.0** |
| GPIO Pins | 14 digital + 6 analog | 11 | 34 | 40 |
| Operating Voltage | **5 V** | **3.3 V** | **3.3 V** | **5 V (USB-C)** |
| Active Current | ~45 mA | ~70 mA | ~160 mA | ~600 mA |
| Sleep Current | ~1 mA | ~10 µA | **~10 µA** | ~200 mA |
| OS | None (bare-metal) | None / RTOS | None / FreeRTOS | **Linux** |
| Cost (approx.) | ₹ 450 | ₹ 250 | ₹ 400 | ₹ 4 500 |
| Best Use | Education, simple sensing | Low-cost Wi-Fi nodes | **Modern IoT flagship** | Edge gateway, vision AI |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Students frequently write "ESP32 has 4 MB *of RAM*". This is wrong. ESP32 has **320 KB of SRAM** and **4 MB of external SPI Flash** used for code storage. Mixing these up is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction in the 2024 valuation key.

## 3.3 Engineering Decision Flow

A rigorous answer to a KTU design question (e.g., *"Select a board for a battery-powered agricultural soil sensor"*) should follow this logic:

1. **Compute needs** (e.g., reading one soil-moisture ADC value every 15 min) → Arduino-class is sufficient.
2. **Connectivity** (must reach a gateway 500 m away) → Wi-Fi on NodeMCU is too short-range; ESP32 + LoRa shield is justified.
3. **Power** (battery must last 6 months) → Sleep current < 20 µA is non-negotiable → ESP32 deep-sleep is appropriate.
4. **Cost** (target BOM < ₹ 500) → ESP32 fits; Raspberry Pi does not.
5. **Final Selection:** ESP32 with LoRa, solar trickle-charged 18650 cell.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# MODULE 2 — IoT Boards: Section 4

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic IoT Board

The Mermaid diagram below maps the **canonical five-block architecture** discussed in Section 2.1. This is the diagram most often demanded in a 14-mark KTU "explain the architecture" question.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pwr["Power Supply 5V or 3.3V DC"] --> ldo["LDO Voltage Regulator 3.3V"]
    bat["Battery Li-ion or USB"] --> pwr
    ldo --> core["Processing Core MCU or MPU"]
    sensor["External Sensors Temp, Humidity, Motion"] --> adc["ADC and Signal Conditioning"]
    adc --> core
    core --> flash["Non-Volatile Memory Flash and EEPROM"]
    core --> sram["Volatile Memory SRAM or DRAM"]
    core --> radio["Wireless Transceiver Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa"]
    core --> gpio["GPIO Bank Digital PWM UART I2C SPI"]
    gpio --> act["Actuators Relay, Motor, LED"]
    radio --> ant["Antenna PCB trace or SMA"]
    ant --> cloud(("Cloud or Gateway"))
    core --> dbg["USB to UART Bridge Programming and Debug"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram uses purely alphanumeric node IDs (`pwr`, `ldo`, `core`, etc.) and clean uppercase labels — no `**bold**` or HTML inside the quoted labels, per the Mermaid Compilation Safeguard.

## 4.2 Decision Flow: Selecting the Right IoT Board

This flow chart should be drawn verbatim in the exam (5–6 marks) when a "justify your board selection" question appears.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["Design Brief"]) --> q1{"Battery Powered?"}
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{"Range greater than 100 m?"}
    q1 -- No --> q3{"Heavy computation or vision?"}
    q3 -- Yes --> rpi["Raspberry Pi 4 or 5"]
    q3 -- No --> uno["Arduino Uno or Mega"]
    q2 -- Yes --> lora["ESP32 plus LoRa Shield"]
    q2 -- No --> q4{"BLE required?"}
    q4 -- Yes --> esp32["ESP32 DevKit"]
    q4 -- No --> nodemcu["NodeMCU ESP8266"]
    rpi --> end1(["Selected Board"])
    uno --> end1
    lora --> end1
    esp32 --> end1
    nodemcu --> end1
```

## 4.3 Power-State State Machine of an IoT Node

A second high-yield diagram is the **active-sleep state machine** of any battery-powered IoT board. It maps directly to the battery-life derivation in Section 3.1.1.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> DeepSleep
    DeepSleep --> Active : RTC Timer fires
    Active --> TX : Sensor read completed
    TX --> DeepSleep : ACK received
    Active --> DeepSleep : Sensor read failed
    DeepSleep --> [*] : Battery removed
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In `stateDiagram-v2`, use the simple `stateName` rule (alphanumeric) for every state — never name a state `end` or `subgraph`, as they are reserved Mermaid keywords and will cause a parser failure.

## 4.4 Functional Partitioning of an ESP32 (Internal Block View)

Because the KTU 2024 scheme places special emphasis on the ESP32, the internal partitioning diagram is a recurring high-value question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cpu0["CPU 0 Xtensa LX6"] --> mac["MAC and Baseband"]
    cpu1["CPU 1 Xtensa LX6"] --> mac
    mac --> radio["Radio 2.4 GHz"]
    radio --> ant["Chip Antenna"]
    cpu0 --> rom["ROM 448 KB"]
    cpu0 --> sram["SRAM 320 KB"]
    cpu0 --> rtc["RTC Memory 8 KB"]
    cpu0 --> gpio["GPIO Matrix 34 pins"]
    cpu1 --> peri["Peripherals I2C SPI UART ADC"]
    rtc --> deep["Deep Sleep Controller"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# MODULE 2 — IoT Boards: Section 5

## 5.1 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

### PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Distinguish between a microcontroller-based IoT board and a microprocessor-based IoT board. Give one example of each.**

*Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):*
- A **microcontroller-based** IoT board (e.g., **ESP32 DevKit**) integrates CPU, RAM, ROM, and peripherals on a *single chip*; runs bare-metal firmware or an RTOS; consumes **tens of milliamps**; and boots in < 100 ms.
- A **microprocessor-based** IoT board (e.g., **Raspberry Pi 4 B**) uses a full application processor that requires **external RAM, storage, and a Linux-class OS**; consumes hundreds of milliamps; and supports heavy compute (vision, ML).
- *Key contrast*: deterministic real-time behaviour (MCU) vs. multitasking richness (MPU).

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is the deep-sleep current of an ESP32, and why is this metric critical for IoT battery-life design?**

*Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):*
- The ESP32 deep-sleep current is **≈ 10 µA** (with RTC memory retention).
- It is critical because the node is asleep for > 99 % of its duty cycle in typical IoT applications; hence the *total energy budget* is dominated by sleep current, not active current. Reducing sleep current by 50 % can double battery life.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: Select ONE)

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a generic IoT board. Highlight the role of the PMU and the wireless transceiver. (7 marks)*

*Model Solution:*

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** An IoT board integrates compute, memory, communication, power, and I/O on a single PCB.
2. **Block Diagram (3 marks):** (Draw the five-block diagram from Section 4.1 of these notes — PMU, Processing Core, Memory, Communication, GPIO.)
3. **PMU Role (1.5 marks):** Conditions the input voltage (USB, battery, solar) to stable 3.3 V / 5 V rails; provides reverse-polarity and over-current protection; enables deep-sleep gating.
4. **Wireless Transceiver Role (1.5 marks):** Implements the physical + MAC layers of Wi-Fi/BLE/LoRa; converts digital packets to modulated RF; supports antenna diversity on boards like ESP32.

**(b)** *Compare Arduino Uno, NodeMCU (ESP8266), and ESP32 on the following parameters: processor, clock, flash, RAM, Wi-Fi, sleep current, and operating voltage. Which board is most suitable for a battery-powered Wi-Fi sensor node and why? (7 marks)*

*Model Solution:*

| Parameter | Arduino Uno | NodeMCU (ESP8266) | ESP32 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Processor | ATmega328P (8-bit) | Tensilica L106 (32-bit) | Xtensa LX6 dual (32-bit) |
| Clock | **16 MHz** | 80–160 MHz | 80–240 MHz |
| Flash | 32 KB | 1–4 MB | 4 MB |
| RAM | 2 KB | 80 KB | 320 KB |
| Wi-Fi | None | 802.11 b/g/n | 802.11 b/g/n |
| Sleep Current | ~1 mA | ~10 µA | **~10 µA** |
| Operating Voltage | 5 V | 3.3 V | 3.3 V |

**Selection Justification (3 marks):** The **ESP32** is the most suitable because (i) it has on-board 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi + BLE, (ii) the lowest deep-sleep current (~10 µA) extends battery life to weeks, (iii) 320 KB SRAM supports TLS stacks and JSON parsing, and (iv) 34 GPIO pins accommodate multiple sensors/actuators. The Arduino Uno lacks Wi-Fi; the NodeMCU has fewer peripherals and no BLE.

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** *Explain the architecture of the ESP32 DevKit board. Discuss the role of its two CPU cores and RTC memory. (7 marks)*

*Model Solution:*

1. **Overview (1 mark):** The ESP32 is a dual-core 32-bit SoC with integrated Wi-Fi + BLE, manufactured by Espressif on TSMC 40 nm.
2. **CPU Cores (3 marks):** The two Xtensa LX6 cores can run independent tasks — typically, *Core 0* runs the Wi-Fi/BLE stack (protocol handling) and *Core 1* runs user application code. This separation prevents radio interrupts from corrupting sensor timing.
3. **RTC Memory (2 marks):** The 8 KB RTC slow memory is retained during deep-sleep, allowing the firmware to store wake-up counters, calibration data, and last-known sensor state without external EEPROM.
4. **Power Modes (1 mark):** Active, Modem-Sleep, Light-Sleep, Deep-Sleep, Hibernation — each mode trades wake-up latency for current consumption.

**(b)** *An IoT node uses an ESP32 in deep-sleep mode, waking every 120 s to transmit a 1 KB LoRa packet. Active current is 80 mA for 2 s; sleep current is 10 µA. Battery is a 2000 mAh, 3.7 V Li-ion cell with regulator efficiency 0.9. Calculate the battery life in days. (7 marks)*

*Model Solution:*

**Step 1 — Compute average current.**

$$
I_{avg} = 80\text{ mA}\cdot\frac{2}{120} + 0.01\text{ mA}\cdot\frac{118}{120}
$$

$$
I_{avg} = 1.333 \text{ mA} + 0.00983 \text{ mA} = 1.343 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply regulator efficiency.**

$$
I_{batt} = \frac{1.343}{0.9} = 1.492 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute life in hours.**

$$
t_{life\_h} = \frac{2000 \text{ mAh}}{1.492 \text{ mA}} = 1340 \text{ h}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to days.**

$$
t_{life\_days} = \frac{1340}{24} \approx 55.8 \text{ days}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** In battery-life questions, students frequently forget to (a) divide active current by the duty cycle, (b) convert hours to days, or (c) include the regulator efficiency. Each omission is **-2 marks** under the 2024 valuation key. Always state the formula and substitute *all* numerical values explicitly.

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MCU vs. MPU**: ESP32 is a *microcontroller-class* IoT board; Raspberry Pi is a *microprocessor-class* SBC. **Never confuse them.**
- **Memory Mnemonic**: ESP32 → **320 KB SRAM + 4 MB Flash (external SPI).** Arduino Uno → **2 KB SRAM + 32 KB Flash + 1 KB EEPROM.**
- **Sleep current is the design king** for battery nodes. Target < 20 µA.
- **Wi-Fi range** indoors ≈ 30–50 m; for > 100 m, choose **LoRa** on the ESP32 with a transceiver shield (SX1276, 868/915 MHz).
- **Voltage Levels**: Arduino = 5 V logic; ESP32 = 3.3 V logic. **Never** connect a 5 V output directly to an ESP32 GPIO — it will damage the chip.
- **Core Concept**: The five-block architecture (Power, Core, Memory, Comm, I/O) applies to *every* IoT board — draw it once, use it everywhere.
- **Nyquist Rule**: Sample at least $2 f_{max}$; in practice oversample at **2.5–3×** to relax analog-filter design.
- **PWM frequency** for LED dimming on Arduino default = **490 Hz** (pins 3, 9, 10, 11) and **980 Hz** (pins 5, 6) — remember the standard 8-bit range (0–255).
- **Free-Space Path Loss** grows as $20 \log_{10}(d)$ — doubling distance adds ≈ 6 dB of loss, an ESSENTIAL insight for any wireless IoT design.
- **KTU 2024 Project Tip**: A 14-mark Part-B answer that includes a *table* + a *block diagram* + a *numerical example* consistently scores in the top decile.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Arduino
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT503 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Overview of Arduino

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Arduino** is an **open-source electronics prototyping platform** based on **easy-to-use hardware (microcontroller-based boards)** and **software (the Arduino Integrated Development Environment - IDE)**. It is designed to make embedded systems, interactive prototyping, and IoT application development accessible to students, hobbyists, and engineers without extensive knowledge of low-level electronics or complex toolchains.

The Arduino ecosystem consists of three tightly-coupled components:

1. **Hardware** – A physical programmable microcontroller board (e.g., Arduino Uno R3) that can read inputs (light on a sensor, finger on a button, Twitter message) and turn them into outputs (activating a motor, lighting an LED, publishing data online).
2. **Software** – The Arduino IDE, a cross-platform application written in Java, used to write, compile, and upload code (called **"sketches"**) to the board.
3. **Community & Libraries** – A massive ecosystem of open-source **shields** (add-on boards), **libraries** (pre-written code modules), and **forums** that accelerate development.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Arduino falls under the **"Open-Source Hardware Platform"** category in Module 2. Students are expected to know the **board architecture, pin mapping, memory organization, and the bootloading process** of the ATmega328P (the heart of Arduino Uno).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an Arduino board as a **miniature, simplified laboratory assistant (a "tinkering lab on a chip")**:

- Imagine you have a **universal remote control** that can sense the world (temperature, light, touch, motion) and react to it (blink LEDs, spin motors, send SMS, tweet). 
- Now imagine that you are allowed to **reprogram every single button** of that remote in plain English-like instructions.
- The Arduino is that universal remote control. The **microcontroller** is the brain, the **pins** are the fingers/eyes, and the **USB cable** is the mouth through which you teach it new tricks.

> [!TIP]
> A more formal analogy: *Arduino is to embedded systems what "Python scripting" is to programming — it abstracts the heavy lifting (register-level configuration, complex linker scripts) so that the user can focus on the **logic of interaction** rather than the **mechanics of the silicon**.*

## 1.3 Why Arduino Matters in IoT

In the KTU 2024 IoT curriculum, Arduino acts as the **edge-node** or **end-device** layer of the IoT stack:

| IoT Layer | Role of Arduino |
|---|---|
| **Perception Layer** | Reads physical signals via analog/digital sensors |
| **Network Layer** | Interfaces with Wi-Fi (ESP8266), Bluetooth (HC-05), LoRa shields |
| **Application Layer** | Logs data to cloud platforms (ThingSpeak, Blynk) |

---

## 1.4 Key Physical & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Arduino Uno R3 Specifications (KEEPER VALUES):**
> - **Microcontroller IC:** ATmega328P (8-bit AVR RISC architecture)
> - **Operating Voltage:** **5 V DC**
> - **Input Voltage (recommended):** **7 V – 12 V DC**
> - **Digital I/O Pins:** **14** (of which **6** provide **PWM** output)
> - **Analog Input Pins:** **6** (10-bit ADC → **0 to 1023** resolution)
> - **Flash Memory:** **32 KB** (0.5 KB used by bootloader)
> - **SRAM:** **2 KB**
> - **EEPROM:** **1 KB**
> - **Clock Speed:** **16 MHz**
> - **USB Interface:** Type-B (for Uno), Micro-USB (for Nano)

---

## 1.5 The Arduino Family (KTU Exam-Relevant Boards)

| Board | MCU | Flash | Digital I/O | Analog In | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Arduino Uno R3** | ATmega328P | 32 KB | 14 (6 PWM) | 6 | **Beginners, Lab experiments** |
| **Arduino Nano** | ATmega328P | 32 KB | 14 (6 PWM) | 8 | Breadboard-friendly projects |
| **Arduino Mega 2560** | ATmega2560 | 256 KB | 54 (15 PWM) | 16 | Large-scale robotics |
| **Arduino Leonardo** | ATmega32U4 | 32 KB | 20 (7 PWM) | 12 | HID (Keyboard/Mouse) emulation |
| **Arduino Due** | ATSAM3X8E (ARM) | 512 KB | 54 (12 PWM) | 12 | High-performance 32-bit apps |

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Module 2**, the **Arduino Uno R3** is the **de facto reference board** and the most commonly tested. Memorize its pin map and ATmega328P block diagram.

---

## 1.6 Historical Context (Board Examiner's Favorite)

- **2005**: Created at the **Interaction Design Institute Ivrea (IDII)**, Italy, by **Massimo Banzi** and **David Cuartielles**.
- **Goal**: Provide a low-cost, easy tool for non-engineers (designers, artists) to build interactive prototypes.
- **Name origin**: Named after **"Arduin of Ivrea"**, a historical Italian king.
- **2013**: Forked into **Arduino.cc** and **Arduino.org**; later re-merged.
- **2020s**: Introduction of **Portenta** and **Nano RP2040** boards for industrial IoT.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block-Level Architecture of Arduino Uno R3

The Arduino Uno is **not just a microcontroller** — it is a **complete development board** that packages the ATmega328P with supporting circuitry. Let us dissect it layer by layer:

### Layer 1: The Microcontroller Core (ATmega328P)

The ATmega328P is an **8-bit AVR RISC-based CMOS microcontroller** manufactured by **Atmel** (now **Microchip Technology**). Its internal architecture consists of:

1. **CPU Core (AVR RISC)**
   - **131 instructions**, most executed in a **single clock cycle**.
   - **32 × 8-bit General Purpose Working Registers** (directly accessible by ALU — this is what makes AVR fast).
   - **ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit)** connected to all 32 registers in a single-cycle fashion.

2. **Program Memory (Flash)**
   - **32 KB** of in-system reprogrammable Flash.
   - **0.5 KB reserved for the Bootloader** (a small program that listens on the UART/USB for new code uploads).
   - Endurance: **10,000 write/erase cycles**.

3. **Data Memory (SRAM)**
   - **2 KB** of SRAM for runtime variables, stack, and heap.
   - **Volatile** — data lost on power-off.

4. **EEPROM**
   - **1 KB** of electrically erasable read-only memory.
   - Used to store non-volatile user data (e.g., calibration values, passwords).
   - Endurance: **100,000 write/erase cycles** (so don't write to it in tight loops!).

5. **Peripherals**
   - **Two 8-bit Timer/Counters** (Timer0, Timer2).
   - **One 16-bit Timer/Counter** (Timer1).
   - **6-channel ADC** (10-bit resolution).
   - **1 UART**, **1 SPI**, **1 I²C (TWI)**.
   - **Watchdog Timer** with separate on-chip oscillator.

### Layer 2: The Arduino Board Glue Logic

The board itself adds several components that the bare MCU does not have:

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Voltage Regulator (AMS1117-5.0 / LM7805)** | Converts 7–12 V input → stable 5 V for MCU |
| **3.3 V Regulator (LP2985)** | Powers 3.3 V shields and sensors |
| **16 MHz Crystal Oscillator** | Provides the master clock to ATmega328P |
| **USB-to-Serial Converter (ATmega16U2)** | Acts as bridge between USB port and MCU's UART |
| **Power LED (Green)** | Indicates board is powered |
| **L LED (Pin 13)** | Connected to digital pin 13 via op-amp driver |
| **Reset Button** | Resets the MCU (and triggers bootloader) |
| **ICSP Header (2×3)** | In-Circuit Serial Programming for burning bootloader |

## 2.2 Power Tree of Arduino Uno

Arduino Uno can be powered via **three** sources, and the on-board logic automatically selects the highest voltage:

1. **DC Barrel Jack** (7 – 12 V recommended; absolute max 20 V)
2. **USB Type-B Connector** (5 V from PC/laptop)
3. **VIN Pin** (when using shield-derived power)

```
DC Jack  →──┐
            ├──► Auto-Selector (MOSFET) ──► 5V Regulator ──► 5V Rail
USB Port ──┘                                          └──► 3.3V Regulator ──► 3.3V Rail
VIN Pin ── (Direct to 5V regulator input)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The **5V pin** is **NOT a power input** for normal use; it is an **output** of the on-board regulator. Supplying 5V directly to the 5V pin bypasses the regulator and can damage the board if voltage fluctuates.

## 2.3 Pin Map (The Most Tested Diagram in KTU)

Arduino Uno exposes **20 physical pins** in addition to power and analog:

### Digital Pins (D0 – D13)

| Pin | Special Function | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **D0, D1** | **UART (RX, TX)** | Used for serial communication; **avoid during upload** |
| **D2, D3** | External Interrupts (INT0, INT1) | Triggered by LOW, CHANGE, RISING, FALLING |
| **D3, D5, D6, D9, D10, D11** | **PWM (~)** | 8-bit PWM, default ~490 Hz (D5/D6 ~980 Hz) |
| **D10, D11, D12, D13** | **SPI (SS, MOSI, MISO, SCK)** | Hardware SPI bus |
| **D13** | Built-in LED (L) | SCK also routed here; **do not use as input** |

### Analog Pins (A0 – A5)

| Pin | Special Function | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **A0–A5** | 10-bit ADC (0–1023) | Default reference: **5 V** |
| **A4, A5** | **I²C (SDA, SCL)** | TWI bus; needs **4.7 kΩ pull-up resistors** |
| **A6, A7*** | Only on **Nano/Pro Mini** | Not present on Uno |

### Power Pins

| Pin | Voltage | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **VIN** | 7–12 V (unregulated) | Alternative power input |
| **5V** | 5 V regulated | Output of on-board regulator |
| **3.3V** | 3.3 V regulated | Max current: **~50 mA** |
| **GND** | 0 V | Common ground (there are 3 GND pins) |
| **RESET** | — | Pull LOW to reset MCU |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly applicable to KTU numerical questions** on Arduino timer/ADC calculations.

### 2.4.1 Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) Resolution

$$
V_{in} = \frac{ADC \times V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}
$$

where:
- $V_{in}$ = actual analog input voltage (Volts)
- $ADC$ = digital output value (0 to $2^n - 1$)
- $V_{ref}$ = reference voltage (default = **5 V** for Uno)
- $n$ = resolution in bits (**10** for Uno)

**Step size (LSB voltage):**
$$
V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{5}{1024} \approx 4.88 \text{ mV}
$$

### 2.4.2 PWM Frequency Calculation (Phase-Correct PWM Mode)

$$
f_{PWM} = \frac{f_{clk}}{N \times 510}
$$

where:
- $f_{clk}$ = clock source frequency (default = **16 MHz** for Uno)
- $N$ = prescaler value (1, 8, 64, 256, or 1024)

**For default Arduino analogWrite() on D9, D10, D11 (Timer1, prescaler = 64):**
$$
f_{PWM} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{64 \times 510} \approx 490 \text{ Hz}
$$

### 2.4.3 PWM Duty Cycle to Output Voltage

$$
V_{out,avg} = \frac{DC}{255} \times 5 \text{ V}
$$

where $DC$ = value passed to `analogWrite()` (0 to 255).

### 2.4.4 ADC Conversion Time

$$
T_{conv} = (13 + \text{prescaler division}) \times T_{clk}
$$

For default prescaler = 128 and $f_{clk}$ = 16 MHz:
$$
T_{conv} \approx \frac{13 + 128}{16 \times 10^6} \approx 8.81 \text{ µs}
$$

### 2.4.5 Maximum ADC Sampling Rate

$$
f_{sample,max} = \frac{1}{T_{conv}} \approx 113 \text{ kHz}
$$

(In practice, `analogRead()` is far slower due to library overhead: ~100 µs per read.)

### 2.4.6 Timer Overflow Frequency (for delay())

The `millis()` and `delay()` functions rely on **Timer0** overflowing at a specific rate:

$$
f_{overflow} = \frac{f_{clk}}{N \times 256} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{64 \times 256} \approx 976.56 \text{ Hz}
$$

The timer is preloaded with 6, so effective frequency becomes **exactly 1000 Hz** → `millis()` increments every 1 ms.

### 2.4.7 Memory Map Summary

| Memory Type | Size | Used For | Volatile? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flash (Program) | 32 KB | Sketches + bootloader | No |
| SRAM | 2 KB | Variables, stack | **Yes** |
| EEPROM | 1 KB | Persistent settings | No |
| **Total addressable I/O registers** | 64 | Hardware control | Yes |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Arduino's principles of abstraction are the foundation of all modern embedded platforms:

- **Industry 4.0**: Industrial IoT gateways use a similar "shield + baseboard" modular architecture.
- **Rapid Prototyping → Product**: Companies like **Particle (Photon)**, **Espressif (ESP32)**, and **Adafruit (Feather)** are direct commercial descendants of the Arduino concept.
- **Education**: KTU itself uses Arduino in PCCGT503 labs to teach **sensor interfacing, PWM motor control, I²C/SPI communication, and IoT cloud integration** without the steep learning curve of bare-metal STM32/8051 programming.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> Since Arduino is fundamentally an **electronic + embedded systems** topic, the "derivations" here translate to **worked-out analytical problems** that KTU examiners love to set.

---

## 3.1 Worked Problem 1: ADC Reading to Voltage Conversion

**Problem:** A temperature sensor outputs 1.25 V when connected to analog pin A0 of Arduino Uno (with default 5 V reference). What digital value will the 10-bit ADC return?

**Given:**
- $V_{in} = 1.25$ V
- $V_{ref} = 5$ V
- $n = 10$ bits

**Formula:**
$$
ADC = \frac{V_{in} \times (2^n - 1)}{V_{ref}}
$$

**Step 1:** Compute $2^n - 1$:
$$
2^{10} - 1 = 1024 - 1 = 1023
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into formula:
$$
ADC = \frac{1.25 \times 1023}{5}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute numerator:
$$
1.25 \times 1023 = 1278.75
$$

**Step 4:** Divide by denominator:
$$
ADC = \frac{1278.75}{5} = 255.75
$$

**Step 5:** Truncate to integer (since ADC returns whole number):
$$
ADC \approx 256
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** The ADC returns a digital value of **256** (out of 1023 max). The LSB voltage step is 4.88 mV, so the actual precision is roughly 5 mV per bit.

---

## 3.2 Worked Problem 2: PWM Duty Cycle to Average Voltage

**Problem:** An LED is connected to pin D9 of Arduino Uno. If `analogWrite(9, 128)` is executed, what is the average voltage across the LED?

**Given:**
- $DC = 128$ (duty cycle value)
- $V_{cc} = 5$ V

**Formula:**
$$
V_{out,avg} = \frac{DC}{255} \times 5
$$

**Step 1:** Compute duty cycle ratio:
$$
\frac{128}{255} = 0.5020
$$

**Step 2:** Multiply by supply voltage:
$$
V_{out,avg} = 0.5020 \times 5 = 2.510 \text{ V}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** The average voltage is approximately **2.51 V**, which corresponds to a **~50.2% duty cycle** PWM signal at **~490 Hz**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Problem 3: Custom PWM Frequency using Timer1

**Problem:** Design a sketch to generate a **2 kHz** PWM signal on pin D9 (Timer1 of ATmega328P) using Fast PWM mode, prescaler = 8. Calculate the required TOP value (ICR1 register).

**Given:**
- $f_{clk} = 16 \times 10^6$ Hz
- Target $f_{PWM} = 2000$ Hz
- Prescaler $N = 8$

**Formula (Fast PWM with ICR1 as TOP):**
$$
f_{PWM} = \frac{f_{clk}}{N \times (1 + TOP)}
$$

**Step 1:** Rearrange for TOP:
$$
1 + TOP = \frac{f_{clk}}{N \times f_{PWM}}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:
$$
1 + TOP = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{8 \times 2000} = \frac{16{,}000{,}000}{16{,}000} = 1000
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for TOP:
$$
TOP = 1000 - 1 = 999
$$

**Step 4:** Assign to ICR1 register:
$$
ICR1 = 999
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** Setting `ICR1 = 999` with prescaler = 8 will generate a 2 kHz Fast PWM signal on pin D9. The duty cycle is then controlled via `OCR1A` (0 to 999).

---

## 3.4 Worked Problem 4: ADC Sampling Time vs. Signal Frequency (Nyquist)

**Problem:** An audio signal of maximum frequency 4 kHz is to be sampled by Arduino's ADC. Determine whether the default sampling rate of Arduino is sufficient, and if not, suggest a prescaler value.

**Step 1: Calculate Nyquist rate**
$$
f_{Nyquist} = 2 \times f_{max} = 2 \times 4000 = 8000 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2: Compute actual Arduino sampling time per conversion**
With prescaler = 128 (default):
$$
T_{conv} = \frac{13 + 128}{16 \times 10^6} = \frac{141}{16 \times 10^6} \approx 8.81 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate max sampling rate**
$$
f_{sample} = \frac{1}{8.81 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 113.5 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 4: Compare to Nyquist**
$$
113.5 \text{ kHz} \gg 8 \text{ kHz} \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** The default Arduino ADC configuration is **fully sufficient** to sample a 4 kHz audio signal (with theoretical oversampling factor of ~14×). However, in practice, the Arduino's `analogRead()` function has library overhead and achieves only ~9–10 kHz real-world sampling, which is still adequate.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Arduino Boards (For KTU 2-Mark Questions)

| Parameter | Uno R3 | Nano | Mega 2560 | Due |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| MCU | ATmega328P | ATmega328P | ATmega2560 | ATSAM3X8E |
| Architecture | 8-bit AVR | 8-bit AVR | 8-bit AVR | **32-bit ARM** |
| Clock | 16 MHz | 16 MHz | 16 MHz | **84 MHz** |
| Flash | 32 KB | 32 KB | **256 KB** | 512 KB |
| SRAM | 2 KB | 2 KB | 8 KB | **96 KB** |
| EEPROM | 1 KB | 1 KB | 4 KB | 0 (emulated) |
| Digital I/O | 14 | 14 | **54** | 54 |
| Analog In | 6 | 8 | 16 | 12 (12-bit!) |
| Operating V | 5 V | 5 V | 5 V | **3.3 V** |
| Best For | Learning | Compact builds | Robotics | DSP/Signal Proc |

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Communication Protocols Supported

| Protocol | Pins Used | Speed | Devices on Bus | Master/Slave |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **UART** | D0 (RX), D1 (TX) | Up to 1 Mbps | 1-to-1 | Peer-to-peer |
| **I²C (TWI)** | A4 (SDA), A5 (SCL) | 100 kHz / 400 kHz | **Up to 127** | Master/Slave |
| **SPI** | D10–D13 (SS, MOSI, MISO, SCK) | Up to 8 MHz (Uno) | Limited by CS pins | Master/Slave |
| **SoftwareSerial** | Any 2 digital pins | Up to 115200 bps | 1-to-1 | Peer-to-peer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** I²C is a **multi-master, multi-slave** protocol. Arduino Uno's I²C bus typically uses a **4.7 kΩ pull-up resistor** on SDA and SCL to 5 V (some sensors have built-in pull-ups).

---

## 3.7 Bootloading Process (A Common Theory Question)

When you press the **Upload** button in the Arduino IDE, the following sequence occurs:

1. IDE **compiles** the sketch into **Intel HEX** format.
2. IDE triggers a **reset** of the ATmega328P via the **DTR pin** of the USB-Serial chip.
3. The **bootloader** (a 512-byte program pre-flashed in the high memory region) starts.
4. Bootloader listens on **UART (D0/D1)** for new HEX data using the **STK500 protocol**.
5. If valid data arrives within 1 second, it is written to Flash memory.
6. If no data arrives, bootloader jumps to the **user program** (address 0x0000).

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Layout:** Bootloader resides at the **end of Flash (0x7E00 – 0x7FFF for Uno)**, while user code begins at **0x0000**. The **fuse bits** of ATmega328P redirect the reset vector to start at the bootloader section.

---

## 3.8 Analytical Reasoning: Why 16 MHz and Not 20 MHz?

| Clock Speed | Pros | Cons | Why Arduino chose 16 MHz |
|---|---|---|---|
| 8 MHz | Lower power, less EMI | Slower ADC, math | Sufficient for Uno, but Mega also 16 MHz |
| 16 MHz | Balanced perf/power, 5 V safe | — | **Industry standard for AVR hobby boards** |
| 20 MHz | Faster | Requires tight PCB, EMI issues, USB timing tighter | Marginal benefit |

> The **16 MHz crystal with 5 V Vcc** is the **sweet spot** for the ATmega328P — it allows **single-cycle instruction execution** while staying well within the MCU's electrical specifications.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (2 marks each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Part A questions are direct, definition-style, requiring 2–3 sentence answers.

### Question 1: Define Arduino. Mention its three major components.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**
Arduino is an **open-source electronics prototyping platform** based on easy-to-use hardware and software. It consists of three major components:
1. **Hardware** – microcontroller-based boards (e.g., Arduino Uno R3 with ATmega328P).
2. **Software** – the Arduino IDE used to write and upload sketches.
3. **Community & Libraries** – open-source shields, libraries, and documentation that accelerate development.

It is widely used in IoT, robotics, and embedded systems education.

---

### Question 2: What is the role of the bootloader in an Arduino board?
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**
The **bootloader** is a small program (typically **0.5 KB**) pre-flashed in the high memory region of the ATmega328P. Its role is to:
- **Listen for new code** on the UART (via the USB-Serial bridge) when the board resets.
- **Receive and write the new sketch** into Flash memory using the **STK500 protocol**.
- **Jump to the user program** (address 0x0000) if no upload is detected within ~1 second.

This eliminates the need for an external hardware programmer for routine uploads.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** For 14-mark questions, students often lose marks by:
> 1. **Forgetting the unit** in numerical answers (e.g., writing "490" instead of "490 Hz").
> 2. **Confusing A4/A5** as analog-only pins (they are also I²C pins).
> 3. **Mislabelling PWM frequencies** — D5 and D6 use Timer0 (~980 Hz), others use Timer1/Timer2 (~490 Hz).
> 4. **Not drawing clear block diagrams** — examiners expect at least one well-labeled diagram in a 14-mark question.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Architecture & Operation

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of Arduino Uno R3**. Identify all major ICs, their functions, and the power supply scheme.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Introduction**
Arduino Uno R3 is an open-source microcontroller board based on the **ATmega328P (8-bit AVR RISC, 16 MHz)**. It integrates a USB-serial interface, voltage regulators, and a 16 MHz crystal into a single PCB.

**Step 2: Major ICs and Their Functions**

| IC | Function |
|---|---|
| **ATmega328P (Main MCU)** | Executes user sketches; has 32 KB Flash, 2 KB SRAM, 1 KB EEPROM, 6× ADC, 3 timers |
| **ATmega16U2 (USB-Serial)** | Converts USB signals to UART for the main MCU; can be reprogrammed as HID |
| **AMS1117-5.0 (5 V Regulator)** | Steps down 7–12 V input to 5 V for the MCU and digital logic |
| **LP2985 (3.3 V Regulator)** | Provides 3.3 V for shields (max ~50 mA) |
| **16 MHz Crystal (HC-49S)** | Main clock source for ATmega328P |

**Step 3: Power Supply Scheme**

- **DC Barrel Jack (7–12 V)** and **USB (5 V)** feed into a **MOSFET-based auto-selector** that picks the higher voltage.
- This selected voltage goes to the **5 V regulator**, which powers the MCU and the 5 V pin header.
- A sub-regulator produces **3.3 V** for shields.
- The **VIN pin** allows alternate power input from shields.

**Step 4: Block Diagram Reference**
*(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram for visual.)*

**Step 5: Conclusion**
Arduino Uno R3 is a self-contained prototyping platform whose integration of MCU, USB-serial, and power management makes it ideal for embedded systems and IoT education.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **pin configuration of Arduino Uno** with a table covering digital, analog, and power pins. List the special functions of pins D9, D10, D11, A4, and A5.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Digital Pins (D0 – D13)**

| Pin | Special Function |
|---|---|
| **D0** | UART RX |
| **D1** | UART TX |
| **D2, D3** | External Interrupts (INT0, INT1) |
| **D3, D5, D6, D9, D10, D11** | **8-bit PWM** output (~) |
| **D10, D11, D12, D13** | SPI (SS, MOSI, MISO, SCK) |
| **D13** | Built-in LED (L) |

**Step 2: Analog Pins (A0 – A5)**

| Pin | Function |
|---|---|
| **A0–A3** | 10-bit ADC channels only |
| **A4** | ADC + **I²C SDA** |
| **A5** | ADC + **I²C SCL** |

**Step 3: Power Pins**

| Pin | Function |
|---|---|
| **VIN** | 7–12 V unregulated input |
| **5V** | 5 V regulated output |
| **3.3V** | 3.3 V regulated output |
| **GND** | Common ground (×3) |
| **RESET** | Pull LOW to reset MCU |

**Step 4: Special Functions of Selected Pins**

- **D9**: PWM (Timer1) — ideal for servo motor control. Frequency ≈ 490 Hz.
- **D10**: PWM + **SPI SS** (Slave Select). Used when Arduino is SPI slave.
- **D11**: PWM + **SPI MOSI** (Master Out Slave In). Carries data from master to slave.
- **A4**: **I²C SDA** (Serial Data). Used to connect I²C sensors (e.g., MPU6050, BMP180). Needs 4.7 kΩ pull-up.
- **A5**: **I²C SCL** (Serial Clock). Carries the clock signal from the I²C master.

**Step 5: Conclusion**
The Arduino Uno pin layout is highly versatile, supporting digital IO, analog input, PWM, and three major communication protocols (UART, SPI, I²C) on shared pins.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): ADC, PWM & Memory Deep Dive

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **ADC and PWM subsystems** of Arduino Uno. For a 10-bit ADC with 5 V reference, what digital value corresponds to 2.5 V? Calculate the LSB voltage.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: ADC Overview**
The ATmega328P has a **successive approximation (SAR) ADC** with:
- **10-bit resolution** → output range **0 to 1023**
- **6 channels** (A0–A5) multiplexed to a single ADC
- **Default reference: 5 V** (from internal Vcc; can be switched to 1.1 V internal or external AREF)

**Step 2: ADC Formulas**
$$
V_{in} = \frac{ADC \times V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}
$$
$$
V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{5}{1024} = 4.88 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 3: Calculation for 2.5 V Input**
$$
ADC = \frac{2.5 \times 1023}{5} = \frac{2557.5}{5} = 511.5 \approx 512
$$

**Step 4: PWM Overview**
Arduino's `analogWrite()` produces **8-bit PWM** with duty cycle range 0 to 255. The default PWM frequency is:
- **~490 Hz** for D9, D10, D11 (Timer1) and D3, D9 (Timer2 — wait, D3 is Timer2B at ~490 Hz)
- **~980 Hz** for D5, D6 (Timer0)

**Step 5: PWM Formula**
$$
V_{out,avg} = \frac{DC}{255} \times 5 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 6: Conclusion**
The ADC and PWM subsystems allow Arduino to interface with continuous analog signals (sensors) and generate analog-like outputs (motor speed, LED brightness) respectively.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **memory architecture** of Arduino Uno (Flash, SRAM, EEPROM). Explain the bootloading process. How is the 0.5 KB bootloader memory protected from being overwritten by user code?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Memory Types**

| Memory | Size | Purpose | Volatile? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Flash** | 32 KB | Stores sketch + bootloader | No |
| **SRAM** | 2 KB | Runtime variables, stack, heap | **Yes** |
| **EEPROM** | 1 KB | Long-term data storage (calibration, passwords) | No |

**Step 2: Harvard Architecture**
ATmega328P uses a **modified Harvard architecture** — program and data memories are physically separate, with separate buses, allowing simultaneous instruction fetch and data access.

**Step 3: Memory Layout**
- **0x0000 – 0x7FFF**: 32 KB Flash
  - **0x0000 – 0x7DFF**: User sketch area (32 KB − 0.5 KB)
  - **0x7E00 – 0x7FFF**: Bootloader section (0.5 KB / 512 bytes)
- **0x0100 – 0x08FF**: SRAM (2 KB)
- **0x0000 – 0x03FF**: EEPROM (1 KB) (logical address space; actual access via library)

**Step 4: Bootloading Process**
1. User clicks "Upload" in Arduino IDE.
2. IDE compiles sketch → Intel HEX file.
3. IDE toggles **DTR line** of USB-Serial chip, which (via 100 nF cap) creates a pulse on the MCU's **RESET pin**.
4. ATmega328P resets; control jumps to the bootloader section (due to fuse bit configuration: `BOOTRST` = 1).
5. Bootloader initializes UART at **115200 baud** and listens for STK500 protocol packets.
6. New sketch data is received and written to Flash (page-by-page, 128 bytes at a time).
7. After upload, bootloader executes a **JMP 0x0000** instruction, transferring control to the user code.

**Step 5: Protection Mechanism**
- The **BOOTSZ1:0 fuse bits** are programmed to reserve the top 512 bytes of Flash for the bootloader.
- The **lock bits (LB1, LB2)** can be set to prevent further reads/writes to the bootloader section in application mode.
- The compiler/linker places user code in the lower region (0x0000 – 0x7DFF), and any overflow is rejected at link time.

**Step 6: Conclusion**
The memory architecture of ATmega328P, with its dedicated bootloader section and Harvard bus design, enables both fast execution and safe in-system reprogramming.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Print this before exams!):**

### Hardware Basics
- Arduino Uno R3 uses the **ATmega328P** (8-bit AVR, **16 MHz**, 5 V).
- **32 KB Flash** (0.5 KB bootloader), **2 KB SRAM**, **1 KB EEPROM**.
- **14 digital pins** (6 PWM), **6 analog pins** (10-bit ADC), **3 GND pins**.

### Pin Special Functions
- **D0, D1**: UART (RX, TX) — avoid during upload.
- **D2, D3**: External interrupts (INT0, INT1).
- **D3, D5, D6, D9, D10, D11**: PWM output.
- **D10–D13**: SPI (SS, MOSI, MISO, SCK).
- **A4, A5**: I²C (SDA, SCL) — need 4.7 kΩ pull-ups.

### Critical Formulas
- **ADC**: $V_{in} = \frac{ADC \times V_{ref}}{1023}$; $V_{LSB} = 4.88$ mV (5 V ref).
- **PWM**: $V_{out,avg} = \frac{DC}{255} \times 5$ V; $f_{PWM,default} \approx 490$ Hz.
- **Bootloader size**: **0.5 KB** at the top of Flash.
- **ADC conversion time**: ~8.81 µs (prescaler = 128).

### Power Supply
- **Recommended input**: 7–12 V (via DC jack or VIN).
- **Operating voltage**: 5 V (regulated).
- **3.3 V pin** max current: **~50 mA**.

### Communication Protocols
| Protocol | Pins | Speed | Topology |
|---|---|---|---|
| UART | D0, D1 | Up to 1 Mbps | 1-to-1 |
| I²C | A4, A5 | 100/400 kHz | Multi-master |
| SPI | D10–D13 | Up to 8 MHz | Master-slave |

### Common KTU Pitfalls (Avoid These!)
- ❌ Saying Arduino has an "operating voltage of 3.3 V" — that's **Due/Zero**. Uno is **5 V**.
- ❌ Forgetting the **bootloader** when discussing Flash memory.
- ❌ Confusing **D5/D6** (~980 Hz) with **D9/D10** (~490 Hz) PWM frequencies.
- ❌ Calling A4/A5 "analog only" — they double as I²C pins.
- ❌ Saying the 5V pin can supply "unlimited current" — limit is **~500 mA** (regulator-dependent).
- ❌ Mixing up **I²C** (A4, A5) and **SPI** (D10–D13) pin numbers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** For any 14-mark question on Arduino, ALWAYS include:
> 1. A **neat block diagram** (free 2 marks guaranteed).
> 2. A **table** comparing at least 2 boards or 2 protocols.
> 3. At least **one numerical calculation** (ADC/PWM/Timer).
> 4. Proper **units** in every numerical answer.
> 5. A concluding sentence linking the topic to **IoT applications**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arduino Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT503 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Arduino Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Arduino Architecture** refers to the **complete hardware and software blueprint** of the Arduino open-source electronics prototyping platform, which integrates an **8-bit AVR RISC-based microcontroller (typically ATmega328P)**, standardized power management circuitry, multi-protocol digital/analog I/O subsystems, on-chip memory hierarchy, and a cross-platform Integrated Development Environment (IDE) that abstracts low-level register manipulation into simplified C/C++ function calls.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Arduino architecture is studied as a **representative embedded system-on-board (SoB)** that demonstrates how a single-chip microcontroller, when paired with regulated power, clock sources, and a bootloader, becomes a self-sufficient computation node — a foundational building block for **Internet of Things (IoT)** edge devices.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Swiss Army Knife of Embedded Systems" Analogy**

Imagine a fully equipped **Swiss Army knife**. Each tool (blade, scissors, screwdriver, corkscrew) is a separate *specialized function*, but they all share the **same handle (the knife body)**, fit into the **same leather pouch (the platform)**, and are operated using the **same grip pattern (the IDE/library abstraction)**.

- The **knife body** → the **ATmega328P microcontroller die** (CPU + memory + peripherals etched onto one silicon chip).
- The **individual tools** → the **peripherals** (ADC, UART, SPI, I2C, Timers, PWM, GPIO).
- The **leather pouch with belt loop** → the **Arduino PCB**, which standardizes pin headers so that any "tool" (sensor/actuator) can be attached the same way.
- The **grip pattern you learned once** → the **Arduino IDE + Wiring/Arduino library**, which lets you call `analogRead()` or `digitalWrite()` without writing AVR assembly.
- The **pouch's standardized size** → the **Arduino Shield form factor**, which guarantees that accessories (Wi-Fi, motor driver, GPS) from any third party snap onto any Arduino board.

> [!TIP]
> **Takeaway for Students:** Arduino is *not* a microcontroller; it is a **complete abstraction layer** sitting on top of one. Understanding the architecture means understanding **what sits between your `setup()` function and the silicon**.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants

The following **standard, board-agnostic metrics** apply to the canonical Arduino Uno (ATmega328P) and are highlighted in **bold** for board-exam recall:

| Metric | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| **Microcontroller** | **ATmega328P (AVR 8-bit RISC)** |
| **Operating Voltage (logic)** | **5 V DC** |
| **Input Voltage (recommended)** | **7 V – 12 V DC** |
| **Digital I/O Pins** | **14 (of which 6 provide PWM)** |
| **Analog Input Pins (ADC)** | **6 (10-bit resolution)** |
| **Flash Memory (Program)** | **32 KB (0.5 KB used by bootloader)** |
| **SRAM (Runtime Data)** | **2 KB** |
| **EEPROM (Non-volatile Data)** | **1 KB** |
| **Clock Frequency** | **16 MHz** |
| **DC Current per I/O Pin** | **40 mA (absolute max)** |
| **ADC Reference Voltages** | **AREF, AVcc, or internal 1.1 V** |

> [!NOTE]
> The 16 MHz crystal is the **primary system clock** driving every instruction cycle. The AVR is a *single-cycle RISC* core, meaning most instructions execute in **1 clock cycle = 62.5 ns** at 16 MHz.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Layer Architecture of an Arduino System

The Arduino architecture is best understood as **five cooperating layers**, from the highest-level user code down to the physical silicon.

### Layer 1 — Application / Sketch Layer (User Space)
- The `.ino` file containing `setup()` and `loop()`.
- Compiled on the host PC into AVR-GCC object code.

### Layer 2 — Arduino Core / Wiring API (Abstraction Layer)
- Provides digital/analog convenience functions: `pinMode()`, `digitalWrite()`, `analogRead()`, `Serial.begin()`.
- Implemented in `cores/arduino/` (C/C++ source).
- Translates friendly function calls into register-level operations.

### Layer 3 — AVR-libc + Toolchain (System Layer)
- Standard C runtime, GCC compiler, `avr-objcopy` for Intel HEX generation.
- Linker inserts the **bootloader** at a fixed memory offset.

### Layer 4 — Bootloader (Optiboot)
- A small (~512 B) pre-flashed program occupying the high end of Flash.
- Listens on **UART (D0 RX / D1 TX)** at **115200 baud** (for Optiboot) for STK500 protocol commands.
- Allows in-system programming (ISP) without an external programmer.

### Layer 5 — ATmega328P Silicon (Hardware Layer)
- CPU core, memory, and peripherals physically etched onto the chip die.

---

## 2.2 On-Chip Memory Hierarchy

The ATmega328P uses a **Harvard architecture** — program and data memories are *physically separate buses*.

| Memory Type | Size | Persistence | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Flash (Program)** | 32 KB | Non-volatile | Stores sketch + bootloader |
| **SRAM** | 2 KB | Volatile (lost on power-off) | Stack, heap, `static`/`global` variables |
| **EEPROM** | 1 KB | Non-volatile | Long-term key-value storage (e.g., calibration) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Harvard split** is critical: it allows the CPU to fetch the *next* instruction from Flash on the same clock cycle it reads/writes a data byte in SRAM. This is what makes AVR "single-cycle" execution possible.

---

## 2.3 I/O Pin Architecture (Digital)

Each of the 14 digital pins (D0–D13) on the Uno is multiplexed:
- **DDRxn** register bit → selects Input (0) or Output (1).
- **PORTxn** register bit → drives HIGH/LOW when in Output mode; enables/disables internal pull-up when in Input mode.
- **PINxn** register → reads the physical pin state.

The Arduino API hides this behind:
- `pinMode(pin, OUTPUT)` → `DDRxn = 1`
- `digitalWrite(pin, HIGH)` → `PORTxn = 1`
- `digitalRead(pin)` → returns `PINxn`

---

## 2.4 Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) Subsystem

- **10-bit successive-approximation ADC**.
- 6 input channels (A0–A5) multiplexed into a single Sample-and-Hold.
- Reference voltage (`Vref`) selectable: **AREF pin, AVcc, or internal 1.1 V bandgap**.
- Conversion time ≈ 13 ADC clock cycles (first conversion 25 cycles).

---

## 2.5 Communication Peripherals

| Protocol | Pins (Uno) | Library | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **UART (TTL Serial)** | D0 (RX), D1 (TX) | `HardwareSerial` | PC debugging, GPS, Bluetooth (HC-05) |
| **I²C (TWI)** | A4 (SDA), A5 (SCL) | `Wire.h` | Sensors, RTC, OLED displays |
| **SPI** | D10–D13 (SS, MOSI, MISO, SCK) | `SPI.h` | SD cards, RFID, high-speed peripherals |

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Memorize this table — it accounts for >70% of numerical questions in Part A and Part B.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Instruction cycle time | $T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{osc}}$ | seconds | $f_{osc} = 16\ \text{MHz} \Rightarrow T_{clk} = 62.5\ \text{ns}$ |
| 2 | ADC resolution (step size) | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}-1}$ | volts | $n=10,\ V_{ref}=5\text{V} \Rightarrow \Delta V \approx 4.88\ \text{mV}$ |
| 3 | ADC digital output | $D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^{n}-1) \right\rfloor$ | integer | $D \in [0,\ 1023]$ |
| 4 | Timer prescaled frequency | $f_{timer} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{N}$ | Hz | $N$ = prescaler (1, 8, 64, 256, 1024) |
| 5 | PWM frequency (Fast PWM, 8-bit) | $f_{PWM} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{N \cdot 256}$ | Hz | Timer0 default: $f_{PWM} \approx 976\ \text{Hz}$ |
| 6 | PWM duty cycle | $D\% = \dfrac{OCR + 1}{256} \cdot 100$ | % | `analogWrite(pin, v)` ⇒ $OCR = v$ |
| 7 | UART baud rate | $Baud = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{16 \cdot (UBRR+1)}$ | bps | $UBRR = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{16 \cdot Baud} - 1$ |
| 8 | UART frame bits | $T_{frame} = 1 + D + P + S$ | bits | 8N1 ⇒ 10 bits/frame |
| 9 | Timer overflow period | $T_{ovf} = \dfrac{N \cdot (TOP+1)}{f_{osc}}$ | seconds | For 16-bit Timer1: $TOP=65535$ |
| 10 | Power dissipation (pin) | $P = V \cdot I$ | W | Max: $5\text{V} \times 20\text{mA} = 100\ \text{mW per pin}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These equations are the bedrock of embedded firmware. Industrial PLCs, automotive ECUs, and IoT edge nodes all use the *same* ADC-resolution, prescaler, and baud-rate formulas. Mastering them here transfers directly to STM32, ESP32, and PIC platforms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section exhaustively derives the four most frequently tested numerical quantities in KTU examinations on Arduino architecture. **Every algebraic step is shown explicitly** — no shortcuts.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — ADC Resolution (Step Size)

**Problem statement:** The ATmega328P ADC is configured with a reference voltage $V_{ref} = 5.0\ \text{V}$. Compute the resolution (LSB weight) and the digital output for an input of $V_{in} = 2.5\ \text{V}$.

### Step 1 — Identify ADC parameters from the silicon
The ATmega328P datasheet specifies a **10-bit successive-approximation ADC**. Therefore the number of discrete output codes is:
$$N_{codes} = 2^{n} = 2^{10} = 1024$$
The output range of the digital code is therefore:
$$D \in \left[0,\ 2^{n}-1\right] = \left[0,\ 1023\right]$$

### Step 2 — Apply the general ADC step-size formula
The voltage represented by one least-significant bit (LSB) is the reference voltage divided across all codes:
$$\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute numerical values
$$\Delta V = \dfrac{5.0\ \text{V}}{2^{10} - 1} = \dfrac{5.0}{1023}\ \text{V}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the decimal result
$$\Delta V = 0.004887586\ \text{V} \approx 4.888\ \text{mV}$$

### Step 5 — Compute digital output for $V_{in} = 2.5\ \text{V}$
$$D = \text{round}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^{n} - 1)\right)$$

$$D = \text{round}\!\left(\dfrac{2.5}{5.0} \cdot 1023\right) = \text{round}(511.5) = 512$$

### Step 6 — Final boxed result
$$\boxed{\Delta V \approx 4.888\ \text{mV/LSB}, \quad D = 512\ (\text{for } V_{in}=2.5\ \text{V})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Common error:** Students often write $2^{n}$ in the denominator instead of $(2^{n}-1)$. The correct denominator is $(2^{n}-1)$ because the range of $n$-bit codes is $0$ to $2^{n}-1$ (a total of $2^{n}$ codes, but only $2^{n}-1$ intervals between them).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — PWM Output Frequency (Timer0, Fast PWM Mode)

**Problem statement:** Arduino Uno's Timer0 drives pins D5 and D6. The prescaler is set to $N = 64$. Compute the PWM output frequency.

### Step 1 — Recall the system clock
The Uno's ATmega328P runs from a 16 MHz external crystal:
$$f_{osc} = 16\ \text{MHz} = 16 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Hz}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the prescaler to obtain the timer tick rate
The prescaler divides the system clock before it enters the timer counter register:
$$f_{timer} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{N} = \dfrac{16 \times 10^{6}}{64} = 250\,000\ \text{Hz} = 250\ \text{kHz}$$

### Step 3 — In 8-bit Fast PWM mode the counter counts from 0 to 255 (TOP = 255)
One full PWM period therefore lasts **256 timer ticks**:
