$$T_{PWM} = \dfrac{256}{f_{timer}} = \dfrac{256}{250\,000} = 0.001024\ \text{s}$$

### Step 4 — Convert period to frequency
$$f_{PWM} = \dfrac{1}{T_{PWM}} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{N \cdot 256}$$

$$f_{PWM} = \dfrac{16 \times 10^{6}}{64 \times 256} = \dfrac{16 \times 10^{6}}{16384}$$

$$f_{PWM} = 976.5625\ \text{Hz} \approx 976\ \text{Hz}$$

### Step 5 — Final boxed result
$$\boxed{f_{PWM} \approx 976.56\ \text{Hz}\ \text{at}\ N=64}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — UART Baud-Rate Register Calculation

**Problem statement:** Compute the value to be loaded into the UBRR register to achieve a baud rate of 9600 bps on a 16 MHz Arduino Uno.

### Step 1 — Start from the AVR baud-rate formula (normal U2X = 0 mode)
$$Baud = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{16 \cdot (UBRR + 1)}$$

### Step 2 — Algebraically isolate UBRR
$$UBRR + 1 = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{16 \cdot Baud}$$
$$UBRR = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{16 \cdot Baud} - 1$$

### Step 3 — Substitute values
$$UBRR = \dfrac{16 \times 10^{6}}{16 \times 9600} - 1$$

$$UBRR = \dfrac{16 \times 10^{6}}{153\,600} - 1$$

$$UBRR = 104.1667 - 1 = 103.1667$$

### Step 4 — Round to the nearest integer (UBRR must be an integer)
$$UBRR = 103$$

### Step 5 — Compute the resulting *actual* baud rate to check error
$$Baud_{actual} = \dfrac{16 \times 10^{6}}{16 \cdot (103 + 1)} = \dfrac{16 \times 10^{6}}{1664} = 9615.38\ \text{bps}$$

### Step 6 — Compute the percentage error
$$\% \text{Error} = \dfrac{9615.38 - 9600}{9600} \times 100 = 0.16\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> Errors under 2% are acceptable for asynchronous serial communication. The standard Arduino `Serial.begin(9600)` macro uses **UBRR = 103** internally.

### Step 7 — Final boxed result
$$\boxed{UBRR = 103,\quad \text{actual baud} = 9615.38\ \text{bps},\quad \text{error} = 0.16\%}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Instruction Cycle Time and Execution Time of a Loop

**Problem statement:** A sketch's `loop()` body consists of 240 AVR instructions. Calculate the loop period.

### Step 1 — Instruction cycle time
The ATmega328P executes most instructions in one clock cycle. With $f_{osc} = 16\ \text{MHz}$:
$$T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{osc}} = \dfrac{1}{16 \times 10^{6}}\ \text{s} = 62.5\ \text{ns}$$

### Step 2 — Time per instruction (single-cycle)
$$T_{inst} = 1 \cdot T_{clk} = 62.5\ \text{ns}$$

### Step 3 — Total loop time
$$T_{loop} = N_{inst} \times T_{inst} = 240 \times 62.5\ \text{ns}$$

$$T_{loop} = 15\,000\ \text{ns} = 15\ \mu\text{s}$$

### Step 4 — Final boxed result
$$\boxed{T_{loop} = 15\ \mu\text{s}\ \text{(assuming all instructions are single-cycle)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Caveat:** Multi-cycle instructions (`CALL`, `JMP`, `RET`, branches taken) take 2–3 cycles. A more accurate estimate multiplies branch instructions by 2–3.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Subsystem | Registers Involved | Arduino API Function | Max Throughput / Resolution |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Digital I/O** | DDRx, PORTx, PINx | `digitalWrite`, `digitalRead` | Toggle rate ≈ 5 MHz |
| **Analog Input (ADC)** | ADMUX, ADCSRA, ADCL/ADCH | `analogRead` | 15 kSPS at full 10-bit res. |
| **PWM Output (8-bit)** | TCCR0A/B, OCR0A/B | `analogWrite` | 976 Hz default (Timer0) |
| **16-bit Timer (Timer1)** | TCCR1A/B/C, OCR1A/B, ICR1 | `Servo.h` (uses Timer1) | Top = 65535 |
| **UART** | UCSR0A/B/C, UBRR0, UDR0 | `Serial.begin/read/write` | Max practical: 1 Mbps |
| **I²C (TWI)** | TWBR, TWSR, TWDR, TWCR | `Wire.h` | 100 kHz std, 400 kHz fast |
| **SPI** | SPCR, SPSR, SPDR | `SPI.h` | Up to $f_{osc}/2 = 8\ \text{MHz}$ |
| **External Interrupts** | EICRA, EIMSK, EIFR | `attachInterrupt` | 2 pins: INT0 (D2), INT1 (D3) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE safeguard protocol, all Mermaid node IDs are alphanumeric, all labels are plain uppercase text, and no reserved keywords are used as node names.

---

## 4.1 Top-Level Functional Block Diagram of the ATmega328P / Arduino Uno

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph POWER [POWER SUBSYSTEM]
        PWR1[DC JACK 7 12V]
        PWR2[VOLTAGE REGULATOR 5V]
        PWR3[USB 5V INPUT]
        PWR4[3V3 LDO AUX]
    end

    subgraph CLOCK [CLOCK SUBSYSTEM]
        CLK1[16 MHZ CRYSTAL]
        CLK2[CLOCK GEN BLOCK]
    end

    subgraph CPU [CPU CORE]
        CPU1[AVR 8 BIT RISC ALU]
        CPU2[PROGRAM COUNTER]
        CPU3[STACK POINTER]
    end

    subgraph MEM [MEMORY HIERARCHY]
        MEM1[FLASH 32 KB]
        MEM2[SRAM 2 KB]
        MEM3[EEPROM 1 KB]
    end

    subgraph PERIPH [PERIPHERALS]
        PER1[GPIO PORTS B C D]
        PER2[10 BIT ADC 6 CH]
        PER3[TIMER0 TIMER1 TIMER2]
        PER4[UART]
        PER5[I2C TWI]
        PER6[SPI]
        PER7[ANALOG COMPARATOR]
        PER8[WATCHDOG TIMER]
    end

    subgraph BOOT [BOOTLOADER]
        BOOT1[OPTIBOOT 512 B]
    end

    PWR1 --> PWR2
    PWR3 --> PWR2
    PWR2 --> CPU1
    PWR2 --> PER1
    PWR2 --> PER2
    PWR4 --> CPU1
    CLK1 --> CLK2
    CLK2 --> CPU1
    CLK2 --> PER2
    CLK2 --> PER3
    CLK2 --> PER4
    BOOT1 --> CPU1
    CPU1 --> CPU2
    CPU1 --> CPU3
    CPU2 --> MEM1
    CPU3 --> MEM2
    PER1 --> MEM2
    PER2 --> MEM2
    PER3 --> MEM2
    PER4 --> MEM2
    PER5 --> MEM2
    PER6 --> MEM2
    PER8 --> CPU1
    PER7 --> PER2
```

---

## 4.2 Pin Multiplexing / Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph BOARD [ARDUINO UNO HEADER]
        D0[D0 RX]
        D1[D1 TX]
        D2[D2 INT0]
        D3[D3 INT1 PWM]
        D4[D4]
        D5[D5 PWM]
        D6[D6 PWM]
        D7[D7]
        D8[D8]
        D9[D9 PWM]
        D10[D10 SS PWM]
        D11[D11 MOSI PWM]
        D12[D12 MISO]
        D13[D13 SCK LED]
        A0[A0 ADC0]
        A1[A1 ADC1]
        A2[A2 ADC2]
        A3[A3 ADC3]
        A4[A4 SDA ADC4]
        A5[A5 SCL ADC5]
    end

    subgraph MCU [ATMEGA328P INTERNAL]
        PD0[PD0]
        PD1[PD1]
        PD2[PD2]
        PD3[PD3 OC2B]
        PD4[PD4]
        PD5[PD5 OC0B]
        PD6[PD6 OC0A]
        PD7[PD7]
        PB0[PB0 ICP1]
        PB1[PB1 OC1A]
        PB2[PB2 SS OC1B]
        PB3[PB3 MOSI OC2A]
        PB4[PB4 MISO]
        PB5[PB5 SCK]
        PC0[PC0 ADC0]
        PC1[PC1 ADC1]
        PC2[PC2 ADC2]
        PC3[PC3 ADC3]
        PC4[PC4 ADC4 SDA]
        PC5[PC5 ADC5 SCL]
    end

    D0 --- PD0
    D1 --- PD1
    D2 --- PD2
    D3 --- PD3
    D4 --- PD4
    D5 --- PD5
    D6 --- PD6
    D7 --- PD7
    D8 --- PB0
    D9 --- PB1
    D10 --- PB2
    D11 --- PB3
    D12 --- PB4
    D13 --- PB5
    A0 --- PC0
    A1 --- PC1
    A2 --- PC2
    A3 --- PC3
    A4 --- PC4
    A5 --- PC5
```

---

## 4.3 Memory Map (Harvard Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph FLASH [FLASH 32 KB]
        F1[ADDRESS 0x0000]
        F2[APPLICATION SKETCH]
        F3[ADDRESS 0x7E00]
        F4[BOOTLOADER 512 B]
        F5[ADDRESS 0x7FFF]
    end

    subgraph SRAM [SRAM 2 KB]
        S1[ADDRESS 0x0100 REGISTERS]
        S2[ADDRESS 0x0100 0x01FF IO SPACE]
        S3[ADDRESS 0x0200 0x02FF EXTERNAL SRAM NORMALLY UNUSED]
        S4[ADDRESS 0x0300 0x08FF DATA SRAM STACK HEAP GLOBALS]
    end

    subgraph EEPROM [EEPROM 1 KB]
        E1[ADDRESS 0x000 0x3FF USER DATA]
    end

    FLASH --> SRAM
    SRAM --> EEPROM
```

---

## 4.4 Software Stack / Programming Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[USER WRITES INO SKETCH] --> B[ARDUINO IDE EDITOR]
    B --> C[VERIFY COMPILE]
    C --> D[AVR GCC COMPILER]
    D --> E[AVR OBJCOPY HEX FILE]
    E --> F[UART 115200 BAUD]
    F --> G[OPTIBOOT BOOTLOADER]
    G --> H[FLASH MEMORY WRITE]
    H --> I[CPU RESETS AND EXECUTES]
    I --> J[SETUP RUNS ONCE]
    J --> K[LOOP RUNS FOREVER]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> Questions below are calibrated to the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**: 2-mark short answers (omitted for length, but two 3-mark Part-A items are given), 14-mark Part-B long-answer questions with internal choice.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the Harvard architecture of the ATmega328P. How does it differ from the von Neumann architecture used in general-purpose CPUs?**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 60-80 words expected):**

The ATmega328P uses a **Harvard architecture**, meaning it has **two physically separate memory buses**: one for *program memory* (Flash, 32 KB) and one for *data memory* (SRAM, 2 KB). This allows the CPU to **fetch the next instruction from Flash while simultaneously reading/writing data in SRAM on the same clock cycle**, enabling single-cycle instruction execution. In contrast, **von Neumann architecture** (used in desktop CPUs like x86) shares a single bus and memory for instructions and data, leading to the well-known **von Neumann bottleneck** where instruction and data fetches cannot occur in parallel. The Harvard split is what makes the AVR 8-bit RISC core so efficient for real-time embedded control. *(3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three on-chip peripherals of the ATmega328P and state one IoT application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **10-bit ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** — Used to interface analog sensors such as **LM35 temperature sensor** or soil-moisture probes in precision agriculture IoT.
2. **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter)** — Used for serial communication with **GPS modules (NEO-6M)** or **HC-05 Bluetooth** for asset tracking.
3. **I²C / TWI (Two-Wire Interface)** — Used to connect low-speed peripherals like **DS3231 RTC**, **BMP280 barometric sensor**, and **16x2 LCD** displays in weather-station IoT nodes.
*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every Part-B question has an **internal choice**. Two fully independent alternative questions are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 — Module 2]`

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of the ATmega328P microcontroller used in the Arduino Uno. List the on-chip memory sizes and the number of I/O pins. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Block Diagram (3 marks):** (Refer to Section 4.1 — Top-Level Functional Block Diagram. The student is expected to draw a labelled block diagram showing CPU, Flash, SRAM, EEPROM, ADC, Timers, UART, I²C, SPI, GPIO, and Clock.)

**2. Architecture description (3 marks):**
- **CPU core:** 8-bit AVR RISC, 131 instructions, mostly single-cycle execution at 16 MHz.
- **Harvard architecture:** Separate program (Flash) and data (SRAM) buses.
- **Bootloader:** Optiboot (512 B) pre-flashed in high Flash for serial programming.
- **On-chip peripherals:** ADC (10-bit, 6-ch), 3 Timers (8-bit, 16-bit, 8-bit), 1 UART, 1 I²C/TWI, 1 SPI, 1 Analog Comparator, 1 Watchdog Timer, 2 External Interrupts.

**3. Memory and I/O (1 mark):**
- Flash 32 KB, SRAM 2 KB, EEPROM 1 KB.
- 14 digital I/O pins (6 PWM), 6 analog input pins.

*(7 marks)*

#### (b) An Arduino Uno is interfaced with a sensor that outputs a voltage of 1.25 V. The ADC is configured with $V_{ref} = 5.0\ \text{V}$. Compute (i) the ADC step size, and (ii) the digital output code. Comment on how the resolution would change if the reference voltage were changed to the internal 1.1 V bandgap. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Step size (3 marks):**
$$\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1} = \dfrac{5.0}{1023} \approx 4.888\ \text{mV/LSB}$$

**(ii) Digital output code (2 marks):**
$$D = \text{round}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot 1023\right) = \text{round}\!\left(\dfrac{1.25}{5.0} \cdot 1023\right) = \text{round}(255.75) = 256$$

**(iii) Comment on internal 1.1 V reference (2 marks):**
With $V_{ref} = 1.1\ \text{V}$ the step size becomes:
$$\Delta V_{1.1} = \dfrac{1.1}{1023} \approx 1.075\ \text{mV/LSB}$$
The resolution **improves by a factor of 4.55** (from 4.888 mV to 1.075 mV), but the **measurable input range shrinks to [0, 1.1 V]**. The sensor output of 1.25 V would now **saturate** the ADC (output would clip at 1023). Trade-off: higher resolution for small signals vs. lower dynamic range.

*(7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 2]`

#### (a) Explain the various power supply options available on the Arduino Uno board and the role of the voltage regulator. Why is a 7–12 V input range recommended when the board operates at 5 V logic? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Power supply options (3 marks):**
- **DC barrel jack** (7–12 V recommended).
- **Vin pin** (raw input voltage, same as DC jack).
- **USB Type-B connector** (5 V from host PC).
- **5V pin** (bypasses regulator — used only when an external regulated 5 V is provided).
- **3.3V pin** (output of the on-board LDO regulator, max 50 mA).

**2. Role of the voltage regulator (2 marks):**
The Arduino Uno uses a **linear low-dropout (LDO) regulator** to step down the input voltage to a stable **5 V DC** for the ATmega328P and on-board peripherals. The LDO rejects input ripple, protects the MCU from over-voltage transients, and provides a clean rail needed for stable ADC references and reliable logic levels.

**3. Why 7–12 V input range? (2 marks):**
The LDO has a typical **dropout voltage of ~1 V** plus a **minimum 1 V headroom for stable regulation**, so 6 V is the absolute minimum. The **upper limit of 12 V** is set by thermal dissipation: $P_{dissipated} = (V_{in} - 5\text{V}) \times I_{load}$. At $V_{in} = 12\ \text{V}$ and a typical load of 200 mA, dissipation is $7 \text{V} \times 0.2 \text{A} = 1.4\ \text{W}$, which is the **maximum the small SOT-223 package can safely dissipate without overheating**. Above 12 V the regulator's junction temperature exceeds its safe operating limit.

*(7 marks)*

#### (b) With necessary formulas and block diagram, explain the working of the 10-bit ADC of the ATmega328P. An Arduino samples a 0–5 V analog input using a prescaler of 128. Compute the number of samples per second achievable. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. ADC working (4 marks):**
- The ATmega328P uses a **successive-approximation-register (SAR) ADC**.
- The 6 analog inputs (A0–A5) are multiplexed into a single **sample-and-hold** capacitor.
- The SAR logic performs a **binary search**: it sets the DAC to half-scale, compares it with the input, and keeps or rejects the bit — repeating for 10 bits.
- Total conversion: **13 ADC clock cycles** for normal conversion, **25 cycles for the first conversion** (extra cycles to initialize the sample-and-hold).
- Reference voltage selectable: **AREF, AVcc, or internal 1.1 V** bandgap.
- Digital result stored in **ADCL + ADCH** (right- or left-adjusted).

**2. Block diagram (1 mark):** (Draw: MUX → Sample-and-Hold → Comparator ← SAR ← DAC → Output Latch. Reference Section 4.1 for inspiration.)

**3. Sample-rate calculation (2 marks):**
ADC clock frequency:
$$f_{ADC} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{128} = \dfrac{16\ \text{MHz}}{128} = 125\ \text{kHz}$$
Maximum sample rate (excluding the slower first conversion):
$$SPS = \dfrac{f_{ADC}}{13} = \dfrac{125\,000}{13} \approx 9615\ \text{samples/sec}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Arduino `analogRead()` function further limits this to about **9,600 SPS** by default due to internal overhead.

*(7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "$(2^n - 1)$" denominator in ADC step-size formula.** Many students write $\Delta V = V_{ref}/2^n$, which is technically the *code width* but not the correct LSB weight for a bipolar range. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing flash memory and SRAM.** "Arduino has 32 KB RAM" is a frequent but **wrong** answer. RAM is 2 KB. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 3. **Stating PWM frequency without specifying the timer.** D5/D6 use Timer0 (~976 Hz), D9/D10 use Timer1 (~490 Hz), and D3/D11 use Timer2 (~488 Hz). Loss: **1 mark** if not specified.
> 4. **Skipping the block diagram in 7-mark sub-parts.** Even a rough sketch with labels earns at least 2 marks. A "text-only" answer caps your sub-part score at 5/7.
> 5. **Ignoring the bootloader offset in memory-map questions.** The bootloader occupies the top 512 B of Flash (addresses 0x7E00–0x7FFF), so the user sketch is limited to **32,256 bytes**, not 32,768. Loss: **1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision checklist — read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- [x] **Arduino is a board, not a chip** — the chip is the ATmega328P.
- [x] **Arduino Uno runs at 16 MHz** with 5 V logic; **Arduino Nano** has the same MCU but a different form factor.
- [x] **Harvard architecture** → separate Flash and SRAM buses → enables single-cycle execution.
- [x] **Memory sizes:** Flash 32 KB (0.5 KB bootloader), SRAM 2 KB, EEPROM 1 KB.
- [x] **Digital pins: 14** (D0–D13); **PWM-capable: 6** (D3, D5, D6, D9, D10, D11).
- [x] **Analog inputs: 6** (A0–A5), **10-bit ADC** (1024 steps, 4.888 mV/LSB at 5 V).
- [x] **I²C pins** are A4 (SDA) and A5 (SCL) — a common gotcha!
- [x] **Maximum current per pin: 40 mA absolute max, 20 mA recommended continuous.**
- [x] **ADC formula:** $\Delta V = V_{ref} / (2^{n} - 1)$ — *not* $2^n$.
- [x] **PWM formula (Timer0, N=64):** $f_{PWM} = 16\,\text{MHz} / (64 \times 256) \approx 976\ \text{Hz}$.
- [x] **Baud-rate register:** $UBRR = f_{osc}/(16 \times Baud) - 1$.
- [x] **Three communication protocols:** UART (async, 2 wires), I²C (sync, 2 wires, addressable), SPI (sync, 4+ wires, fastest).
- [x] **Bootloader** (Optiboot, 512 B) enables USB-serial programming without an external programmer.
- [x] **Power tree:** DC jack / Vin → LDO regulator → 5 V rail → ATmega328P; separate 3.3 V LDO for shield compatibility.
- [x] **Internal 1.1 V reference** boosts ADC resolution for low-voltage sensors (e.g., thermocouples) but reduces range.
- [x] **Watchdog timer** resets the MCU if the firmware hangs — used in robust IoT deployments.
- [x] **Sleep modes** (Power-save, Power-down) reduce current to <1 µA — critical for battery-powered IoT nodes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Arduino Boards and Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT503 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Arduino Boards and Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Arduino Board** is an open-source, single-board microcontroller platform based on the **AVR** (for the classic UNO/Mega) or **ARM** (for newer MKR/Portenta families) family of microcontrollers, equipped with a standardized set of digital and analog Input/Output (I/O) pins, USB interface for programming, voltage regulation circuitry, and a flash-memory-resident bootloader that allows in-system firmware upload through the Arduino IDE (Integrated Development Environment).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2)**
> Under the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme, students are expected to classify Arduino boards based on form factor, microcontroller core, operating voltage, and IoT-readiness, while also identifying on-board functional components such as the MCU, voltage regulator, crystal oscillator, and pin headers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an Arduino board as a **universal translator + mini factory controller**:

- Imagine a factory with hundreds of machines (sensors, motors, LEDs). Each machine speaks a different language (voltage levels, current types, signaling protocols).
- The **Arduino board** sits in the middle, translating instructions from your computer (via USB) into machine language (via its I/O pins) and vice versa.
- The **microcontroller chip** is the **brain** (the manager who reads the rule book and makes decisions).
- The **voltage regulator** is the **clinic nurse** (she ensures the brain only receives 5V — too much "voltage medicine" and the brain fries).
- The **crystal oscillator** is the **metronome** (it keeps perfect time, ticking at 16 million times per second).
- The **USB port** is the **mailbox** (you drop your instructions here).
- The **I/O pins** are the **hands and ears** (they reach out to sensors and actuators).

### 1.3 Key Physical & Electrical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Memorize Engineering Metrics (Board Class Standards)**
> - **Logic Voltage:** **5V DC** (TTL level for UNO/Mega) or **3.3V DC** (for MKR/Portenta/Nano 33 IoT).
> - **Clock Frequency:** **16 MHz** (UNO/Nano) up to **84–480 MHz** (ARM-based boards).
> - **Flash Memory Range:** **32 KB** (UNO/Nano) to **8 MB** (Portenta H7).
> - **USB Standard:** **USB-B** (UNO), **USB-Micro-B** (MKR), **USB-C** (Portenta/Nano 33).
> - **Input Voltage Limit:** **6 V – 20 V DC** (recommended 7–12 V via barrel jack).

### 1.4 Architectural Categories (High-Level Classification)

| Class | Example | Core MCU | IoT Focus | Beginner Friendly |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Classic 8-bit AVR | UNO R3, Nano | ATmega328P | Low | **Yes** |
| Mega 8-bit AVR | Mega 2560 R3 | ATmega2560 | Low | Yes |
| Tiny / Wearable | Micro, Nano Every | ATmega32U4 / ATmega4809 | Low | Moderate |
| MKR (ARM 32-bit) | MKR WiFi 1010, MKR NB 1500 | SAMD21 + u-blox/NINA | **High** | Moderate |
| Nano 33 (IoT-Armed) | Nano 33 IoT, BLE, RP2040 | SAMD21 / nRF52 / RP2040 | **Very High** | Moderate |
| High-Performance | Portenta H7 | STM32H747 (Cortex-M7+M4) | Industrial | No |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Sheet

### 2.1 Why So Many Boards? The "Why" Behind the Family

Arduino's open-source philosophy generated an ecosystem. The boards differ along **five engineering axes** that determine which one to pick for a project:

1. **Form Factor** — physical size and pinout (shields, breadboard friendliness).
2. **Processing Power** — bit-width (8 vs 32), clock speed, RAM/Flash.
3. **Operating Voltage** — 5V vs 3.3V logic (critical for sensor compatibility).
4. **On-board Connectivity** — Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa, GSM, Ethernet.
5. **Power & Peripherals** — ADC resolution (10/12-bit), DAC presence, USB-OTG, CAN bus.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Classification Logic (Bulleted Reasoning)

- **Step 1 — Identify Application Domain**: Is it a learning kit, a wearable, an industrial gateway, or a wireless sensor node?
  - *Why:* Power, size, and connectivity demands differ.
- **Step 2 — Choose Operating Voltage**: 5V for legacy modules, 3.3V for modern IoT sensors.
  - *Why:* Many IoT sensors (BME280, MPU6050) operate at 3.3V; 5V can damage them.
- **Step 3 — Estimate Memory Footprint**: Code size and runtime buffers.
  - *Why:* UNO's 32 KB flash + 2 KB SRAM caps complex IoT stacks.
- **Step 4 — Evaluate Connectivity**: Is Wi-Fi/BLE/LoRa/GSM required onboard?
  - *Why:* Saves PCB space and BOM cost vs. external modules.
- **Step 5 — Verify I/O Pin Count**: Digital, analog, PWM requirements.
  - *Why:* Pin starvation is the #1 prototype failure cause.

### 2.3 On-Board Component Architecture (Block-Level)

Every standard Arduino contains the following functional blocks:

| Component | Function | Typical Value/Part |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **MCU Chip** | Brain: executes firmware, manages I/O | ATmega328P / SAMD21 / STM32H7 |
| **Voltage Regulator** | Steps down input DC to stable logic level | AMS1117 (3.3V) or LDO 5V (LP2985) |
| **Crystal Oscillator** | Provides master clock signal | 16 MHz (UNO), 12 MHz (some 3.3V boards) |
| **Reset Circuit** | Restarts MCU; supports auto-reset from IDE | Capacitor + pull-up resistor + DTR line |
| **USB-to-Serial Bridge** | Translates USB to UART for programming | ATmega16U2 (UNO) or CH340 / CP2102 (clones) |
| **Power Jack (Barrel)** | Accepts external DC 7–12 V | 2.1 mm center-positive |
| **Pin Headers** | Standardized 0.1" headers for shields/wires | Male header strips |
| **Bootloader IC Region** | Pre-flashed flash region enabling ISP-less upload | Optiboot (UNO) |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Comparison Table — Flagship Boards

| Specification | Arduino UNO R3 | Arduino Mega 2560 | Arduino Nano | MKR WiFi 1010 | Portenta H7 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| MCU | ATmega328P | ATmega2560 | ATmega328P | SAMD21 + NINA-W102 | STM32H747 |
| Architecture | 8-bit AVR | 8-bit AVR | 8-bit AVR | 32-bit ARM Cortex-M0+ | Dual Cortex-M7 + M4 |
| Clock | **16 MHz** | 16 MHz | 16 MHz | 48 MHz | 480 MHz |
| Flash | 32 KB | 256 KB | 32 KB | 256 KB | 8 MB (total) |
| SRAM | 2 KB | 8 KB | 2 KB | 32 KB | 1 MB |
| Logic Level | 5V | 5V | 5V | 3.3V | 3.3V |
| Digital I/O | 14 | 54 | 14 | 22 | 80+ |
| Analog In | 6 (10-bit) | 16 (10-bit) | 8 (10-bit) | 7 (12-bit) | 16 (16-bit) |
| Wi-Fi/BLE | No | No | No | **Yes (2.4 GHz)** | Optional module |
| USB | USB-B | USB-B | Mini-B | Micro-B | USB-C + OTG |
| Form Factor | Large | Very Large | Tiny | Compact | Industrial |
| Best For | Learning, shields | Robotics, 3D printers | Breadboard proto | IoT cloud nodes | ML, vision, industry |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **UNO** → Engineering education labs, KTU-curriculum starter kits.
- **Mega** → 3D printer controllers (e.g., RAMPS shields), large sensor arrays.
- **Nano** → Compact wearables, drone flight controllers (pre-Pixhawk era).
- **MKR WiFi 1010 / Nano 33 IoT** → Direct MQTT/HTTP to AWS/Azure IoT Hub.
- **Portenta H7** → Edge-AI, TensorFlow Lite Micro, machine vision.

> [!NOTE]
> The classification above is the *expected* depth for a 14-mark KTU ESE question on "Compare and contrast Arduino boards" or "Select an Arduino for a given IoT application."

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Selection Matrix

### 3.1 Deriving Power-Consumption Suitability for IoT

For a battery-operated IoT node, the **average current consumption** is the most critical selection metric. Consider a node that spends $T_{\text{active}}$ seconds in active mode and $T_{\text{sleep}}$ seconds in sleep mode per duty cycle $T_{\text{total}}$.

$$T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{active}} + T_{\text{sleep}}$$

The **average current** drawn from a battery is:

$$I_{\text{avg}} = \frac{(I_{\text{active}} \cdot T_{\text{active}}) + (I_{\text{sleep}} \cdot T_{\text{sleep}})}{T_{\text{total}}}$$

The **battery life** in hours, given a battery capacity $C$ in mAh, is:

$$T_{\text{life}} = \frac{C}{I_{\text{avg}}}$$

#### Numerical Example — Selecting MKR WiFi 1010 vs UNO

**Given:**
- UNO active current $I_{\text{active, UNO}} = 45 \text{ mA}$
- UNO sleep current $I_{\text{sleep, UNO}} = 15 \text{ mA}$ (no true deep sleep)
- MKR WiFi 1010 active current $I_{\text{active, MKR}} = 30 \text{ mA}$
- MKR WiFi 1010 deep-sleep current $I_{\text{sleep, MKR}} = 0.18 \text{ mA}$
- Battery capacity $C = 2000 \text{ mAh}$
- Duty cycle: active for 5 s every 60 s

**Step 1:** Compute total cycle time.
$$T_{\text{total}} = 5 + 55 = 60 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute UNO average current.
$$\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{avg, UNO}} &= \frac{(45 \times 5) + (15 \times 55)}{60} \\
&= \frac{225 + 825}{60} \\
&= \frac{1050}{60} \\
&= 17.5 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Compute MKR WiFi 1010 average current.
$$\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{avg, MKR}} &= \frac{(30 \times 5) + (0.18 \times 55)}{60} \\
&= \frac{150 + 9.9}{60} \\
&= \frac{159.9}{60} \\
&= 2.665 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Compute battery life in hours.

For UNO:
$$T_{\text{life, UNO}} = \frac{2000}{17.5} \approx 114.3 \text{ hours}$$

For MKR WiFi 1010:
$$T_{\text{life, MKR}} = \frac{2000}{2.665} \approx 750.5 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 5:** Conclude board selection.
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Life Extension Factor} &= \frac{750.5}{114.3} \\
&\approx 6.57 \times
\end{aligned}$$

The MKR WiFi 1010 lasts **~6.57 times longer** on identical battery and duty cycle, making it the clear winner for a battery-powered IoT node — a direct numerical justification for IoT-specific board selection.

### 3.2 Analytical Selection Matrix (Decision Tree Logic)

Use this table to select the right board for a given constraint. The "Constraint Score" $S$ for a board against a requirement is computed as the weighted sum:

$$S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot m_i$$

where $w_i$ is the weight (importance 1–5) and $m_i$ is the match score (0–5) of the board on criterion $i$.

| Application Constraint | UNO | Mega | Nano | MKR 1010 | Portenta H7 |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Cost (< ₹500) | 5 | 4 | 5 | 2 | 1 |
| Breadboard Friendly | 1 | 0 | 5 | 4 | 2 |
| Wi-Fi + Cloud IoT | 0 | 0 | 0 | 5 | 4 |
| High I/O (>40 pins) | 1 | 5 | 1 | 3 | 5 |
| Battery / Low Power | 1 | 1 | 2 | 5 | 4 |
| ML / Vision Workloads | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 5 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is the **standard answer scaffold** expected for any "Justify the selection of an Arduino board for a given application" question worth 7–14 marks in KTU ESE.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 On-Board Functional Block Diagram (Arduino UNO R3)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Barrel Jack 7-12V] --> B[Voltage Regulator 5V LDO]
    A --> C[Reverse Polarity Diode]
    B --> D[5V Power Rail]
    B --> E[3V3 LDO AMS1117]
    D --> F[ATmega328P MCU]
    E --> F
    G[USB-B Port] --> H[USB-Serial Bridge ATmega16U2]
    H --> I[UART TX RX Lines]
    I --> F
    J[16 MHz Crystal Oscillator] --> F
    K[Reset Button + DTR] --> F
    L[Power LED + Pin13 LED] --> D
    F --> M[Digital Pins D0-D13]
    F --> N[Analog Pins A0-A5]
    F --> O[PWM Pins 3 5 6 9 10 11]
    F --> P[ICSP Header 6-pin SPI]
```

**Interpretation (for the examiner):** Power enters from the barrel jack or USB; the 5V LDO regulator conditions it; the MCU receives regulated power and a 16 MHz clock; the USB-serial bridge enables in-system programming; and the I/O pins serve the external world.

### 4.2 Board Family Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Arduino Board Family] --> B0[Classic 8-bit AVR]
    A0 --> C0[Tiny Form Factor]
    A0 --> D0[IoT ARM MKR Series]
    A0 --> E0[High Performance ARM]
    A0 --> F0[Special Purpose]

    B0 --> B1[UNO R3]
    B0 --> B2[Leonardo]
    B0 --> B3[Mega 2560]

    C0 --> C1[Nano]
    C0 --> C2[Micro]
    C0 --> C3[Nano Every]
    C0 --> C4[Nano 33 IoT BLE Sense]

    D0 --> D1[MKR WiFi 1010]
    D0 --> D2[MKR NB 1500 NB-IoT]
    D0 --> D3[MKR WAN 1310 LoRa]
    D0 --> D4[MKR GSM 1400]

    E0 --> E1[Portenta H7]
    E0 --> E2[Portenta Machine Control]

    F0 --> F1[Nicla Sense ME]
    F0 --> F2[Opta PLC]
```

### 4.3 Component-to-Function Mapping (For Quick Recall)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Component] --> Q2[Engineering Function]
    R1[ATmega328P] --> R2[Executes user firmware 16 MIPS]
    R3[16 MHz Crystal] --> R4[Master clock for instruction cycle]
    R5[AMS1117 3V3] --> R6[Provides 3.3V for sensors and shield logic]
    R7[ATmega16U2] --> R8[USB to UART bridge for programming]
    R9[Reset Capacitor] --> R10[Generates auto-reset pulse on DTR toggle]
    R11[ICSP Header] --> R12[SPI programming bypassing USB]
    R13[Power LED Green] --> R14[Visual power-on indicator]
    R15[L LED Pin 13] --> R16[Built-in test LED for Blink sketch]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*List any three Arduino boards and state one distinguishing feature of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Arduino UNO R3** — Based on ATmega328P, the most widely used board for beginners; uses 5V logic and accepts standard Arduino shields.
2. **Arduino Mega 2560** — Based on ATmega2560, offers 54 digital I/O pins and 16 analog inputs, suitable for robotics and 3D printers.
3. **Arduino MKR WiFi 1010** — Based on SAMD21 (ARM Cortex-M0+) with on-board NINA-W102 Wi-Fi/BLE module, designed for direct IoT cloud connectivity at 3.3V logic.

*(1 mark each — full credit if a fourth or fifth valid point is given.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
*What is the role of the crystal oscillator in an Arduino board?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The crystal oscillator provides the **master clock signal** that synchronizes all internal operations of the microcontroller. In the Arduino UNO, a **16 MHz quartz crystal** is connected to the ATmega328P's XTAL1 and XTAL2 pins, generating a stable, periodic square wave. Every instruction cycle in the AVR architecture executes in **one clock tick**, so the CPU effectively runs at 16 million instructions per second (16 MIPS). Without the crystal, the MCU would either fail to start or run erratically from an imprecise internal RC oscillator. It is the **metronome** of the board.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the major on-board functional components of the Arduino UNO R3 with a neat block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare Arduino UNO and Arduino Mega 2560 in terms of architecture, memory, I/O count, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) On-board components of Arduino UNO R3 (7 marks):**

1. **ATmega328P Microcontroller (Brain) – 2 marks:** The 8-bit AVR RISC-based MCU running at 16 MHz, with 32 KB flash, 2 KB SRAM, 1 KB EEPROM, executing the user sketch.

2. **Voltage Regulation Circuit – 1 mark:** The barrel jack accepts 7–12 V DC; a 5V LDO regulator drops it to 5V, and an AMS1117-3.3 further produces 3.3V for shield and sensor compatibility.

3. **16 MHz Crystal Oscillator – 1 mark:** External quartz crystal provides the master clock to XTAL1/XTAL2 pins of the MCU, ensuring deterministic instruction timing.

4. **USB-to-Serial Bridge (ATmega16U2) – 1 mark:** Converts USB signals to UART so that the IDE can upload hex code via the bootloader; on clones, this is replaced by CH340G or CP2102.

5. **Reset Circuit, ICSP Header, Power & Pin-13 LEDs – 1 mark:** Auto-reset on IDE upload, in-system programming bypass, visual indicators for power and test blinking.

6. **I/O Pin Headers – 1 mark:** 14 digital (6 PWM) + 6 analog + power pins standardized to 0.1" pitch for shield compatibility.

*(See the mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 for visual support.)*

**(b) Comparison: UNO vs Mega 2560 (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Arduino UNO R3 | Arduino Mega 2560 | Marks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| MCU | ATmega328P (8-bit) | ATmega2560 (8-bit) | 1 |
| Flash | 32 KB (0.5 KB used by bootloader) | 256 KB (8 KB bootloader) | 1 |
| SRAM | 2 KB | 8 KB | 1 |
| Digital I/O | 14 (6 PWM) | 54 (15 PWM) | 1 |
| Analog Inputs | 6 (10-bit) | 16 (10-bit) | 1 |
| Form Factor | Small, breadboard via jumpers | Very large | 1 |
| Typical Use | Learning kits, simple prototypes | Robotics, 3D printing, multi-sensor arrays | 1 |

> Both share 5V logic and 16 MHz clock; the Mega is essentially an UNO with **3× more flash, 4× more RAM, ~4× more I/O**, suited for projects that exceed UNO's pin/memory ceiling.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model Paper, Module 2]**

**(a)** A battery-powered IoT node using a Wi-Fi module must run unattended for 6 months on a 2000 mAh Li-ion cell, transmitting every 5 minutes. Justify the selection of Arduino MKR WiFi 1010 over Arduino UNO with numerical analysis. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List and explain the function of any three IoT-ready Arduino boards from the MKR or Nano 33 family. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Numerical Justification (7 marks):**

*Step 1 – Duty Cycle Setup (2 marks):*
The board is active for $T_a = 10 \text{ s}$ (Wi-Fi handshake + transmission) and sleeps for $T_s = 290 \text{ s}$.

$$T_{\text{total}} = 10 + 290 = 300 \text{ s}$$

*Step 2 – UNO Average Current (2 marks):*
With $I_a = 45 \text{ mA}$ and $I_s = 15 \text{ mA}$:

$$I_{\text{avg, UNO}} = \frac{(45 \times 10) + (15 \times 290)}{300} = \frac{450 + 4350}{300} = 16 \text{ mA}$$

*Step 3 – MKR WiFi 1010 Average Current (2 marks):*
With $I_a = 30 \text{ mA}$ and $I_s = 0.18 \text{ mA}$:

$$I_{\text{avg, MKR}} = \frac{(30 \times 10) + (0.18 \times 290)}{300} = \frac{300 + 52.2}{300} = 1.174 \text{ mA}$$

*Step 4 – Life Calculation (1 mark):*
$$T_{\text{life, UNO}} = \frac{2000}{16} = 125 \text{ hours} \approx 5.2 \text{ days}$$
$$T_{\text{life, MKR}} = \frac{2000}{1.174} = 1703 \text{ hours} \approx 71 \text{ days}$$

MKR lasts **~13.6× longer** and easily meets 6 months (180 days) with solar supplementation, while UNO lasts barely a week. Hence, MKR WiFi 1010 is justified.

**(b) Three IoT-Ready Arduino Boards (7 marks):**

1. **Arduino MKR WiFi 1010 (2 marks):** SAMD21 Cortex-M0+ core, 32-bit, 48 MHz, 3.3V logic, integrated NINA-W102 module for Wi-Fi 802.11 b/g/n + BLE 4.2, ECC608 crypto chip, MKR form factor for shields.

2. **Arduino Nano 33 IoT (2 marks):** Tiny breadboard-friendly footprint, SAMD21 MCU, 6-axis IMU (LSM6DS3), 3.3V logic, integrated u-blox NINA-W102 Wi-Fi/BLE — ideal for compact IoT prototypes with motion sensing.

3. **Arduino MKR WAN 1310 (2 marks):** SAMD21 + Murata CMWX1ZZABZ LoRa module, supports LoRaWAN 1.0.4 across regional bands (868/915 MHz), enabling long-range (10+ km) low-power IoT for agriculture and smart cities.

4. **Arduino Portenta H7 (1 bonus mark):** Dual-core STM32H747 (Cortex-M7 + M4) at 480 MHz, 8 MB flash, on-board wireless module support, runs Arduino code + TensorFlow Lite Micro for edge AI.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing architecture** — UNO is 8-bit AVR; MKR is 32-bit ARM. Mixing these = **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Wrong logic voltage** — Stating "UNO runs on 3.3V" is a costly error. Always write **5V for UNO/Mega**, **3.3V for MKR/Nano 33**.
> 3. **Forgetting the bootloader role** — If asked "how does the IDE upload code?", you must mention the **bootloader** (Optiboot for UNO), not just the USB-serial bridge.
> 4. **Numerical questions** — Forgetting to include units (mA, hours) or skipping intermediate steps loses **½ to 1 mark per step**.
> 5. **Block diagram in 7-mark questions** — Drawing a block diagram is worth **at least 2 marks**; skipping it costs heavily.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- **Arduino = open-source single-board MCU platform** with standardized pinout, USB programming, and bootloader.
- **Five classification axes:** Form factor, bit-width, logic voltage, connectivity, peripherals.
- **Must-know boards:** UNO R3, Mega 2560, Nano, MKR WiFi 1010, Nano 33 IoT, Portenta H7.
- **UNO R3 core:** ATmega328P, **16 MHz**, **32 KB flash**, **2 KB SRAM**, **5V logic**, **14 digital / 6 analog pins**.
- **Mega 2560:** ATmega2560, 16 MHz, **256 KB flash**, **8 KB SRAM**, **54 digital / 16 analog pins**, used in 3D printers.
- **MKR WiFi 1010:** SAMD21 ARM Cortex-M0+, 48 MHz, **256 KB flash**, **32 KB SRAM**, **3.3V logic**, integrated Wi-Fi + BLE.
- **Nano 33 IoT:** Tiny SAMD21 form, 3.3V, integrated Wi-Fi/BLE + 6-axis IMU.
- **Portenta H7:** STM32H747 dual-core Cortex-M7+M4 at **480 MHz**, 8 MB flash, supports edge AI.
- **On-board components (UNO):** ATmega328P MCU, 5V LDO, 3.3V AMS1117, 16 MHz crystal, ATmega16U2 USB-serial bridge, reset circuit, ICSP header, pin headers, power & L LEDs.
- **Voltage regulator role:** Steps down 7–12 V DC to a stable 5 V (and 3.3 V via second LDO) for MCU and peripherals.
- **Crystal oscillator role:** Provides deterministic 16 MHz master clock → 16 MIPS instruction throughput.
- **USB-serial bridge role:** Translates USB ↔ UART for bootloader-based code upload; clones use CH340/CP2102.
- **Bootloader role:** Pre-flashed firmware in flash that allows ISP-less uploads via UART.
- **Battery-life formula:** $I_{\text{avg}} = \frac{I_a T_a + I_s T_s}{T_{\text{total}}}$, and $T_{\text{life}} = C \div I_{\text{avg}}$.
- **Selection rule of thumb:** UNO for learning, Mega for many pins, Nano for compact, MKR/Nano 33 for cloud IoT, Portenta for AI.
- **Clock frequencies to memorize:** UNO/Mega/Nano = 16 MHz; MKR = 48 MHz; Portenta = 480 MHz.
- **Logic voltages to memorize:** UNO/Mega/Nano (classic) = 5V; MKR/Nano 33/Portenta = 3.3V.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT503 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Specifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of embedded systems and the Internet of Things (IoT), **Specifications** refer to the comprehensive set of technical parameters, electrical characteristics, architectural features, and performance benchmarks that quantitatively describe a microcontroller's (MCU) capabilities, limitations, and operational boundaries. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCCGT503**, the specifications of a microcontroller include its core architecture, memory hierarchy, clock distribution, power profile, peripheral set, and computational throughput.

For IoT-grade microcontrollers (typically based on the **ARM Cortex-M** family), the specification sheet constitutes the "datasheet of identity" — defining everything from the silicon process node to the interrupt latency. The most widely referenced specification in academic and industrial IoT design is the **ARM Cortex-M4** core (e.g., STM32F407VGT6), with key parameters such as a **32-bit RISC architecture**, **Thumb-2 instruction set**, **3-stage pipeline + branch speculation**, and a maximum clock frequency of **180 MHz**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A "specification" is *not* a user manual — it is a **quantitative engineering contract** between the silicon vendor and the system designer. Every clock cycle, voltage rail, and pin mapping originates from this contract.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a microcontroller specification sheet as the **"spec card" of a delivery truck used by an e-commerce company**:

| Truck Spec Analogy | Microcontroller Spec |
|---|---|
| Engine displacement & horsepower | Core architecture, clock frequency, DMIPS |
| Maximum payload (kg) | Flash memory, SRAM size |
| Fuel tank capacity | Power consumption in active mode (mA/MHz) |
| Number of wheels & axles | GPIO count, bus matrix width |
| Refrigeration unit / Crane attachment | ADC, DAC, Timers, Communication peripherals |
| Top speed | Instruction throughput (CoreMark / Dhrystone) |
| Mileage (km/litre) | MIPS per milliwatt (energy efficiency) |
| Emission standard | Low-power modes (Sleep, Stop, Standby) |

Just as a logistics manager chooses a truck based on **payload, terrain, fuel cost, and delivery time**, an IoT system architect selects a microcontroller based on **compute throughput, memory headroom, power budget, peripheral mix, and real-time responsiveness**.

## 1.3 Fundamental Specification Categories

A typical IoT microcontroller specification block is organized into the following six canonical groups:

1. **Core & Compute Specifications** — Architecture, ISA, pipeline depth, FPU, MPU, Dhrystone MIPS.
2. **Memory Specifications** — Flash size, SRAM size, ROM, EEPROM, memory map organization.
3. **Bus & Clock Specifications** — AHB/APB bus matrix, HCLK/PCLK prescalers, PLL multipliers.
4. **Power Specifications** — Operating voltage (V<sub>DD</sub>), current in Run/Sleep/Stop/Standby modes.
5. **Peripheral Specifications** — GPIO drive strength, ADC resolution, DAC linearity, timer bit-width.
6. **Communication Specifications** — USART baud rate, SPI clock, I²C speed modes, USB version, CAN bit rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Reminder:** All KTU examination answers must quote parameters with both **numerical value** *and* **unit**. Example: "The ADC has a resolution of **12 bits**" — *not* just "12".

## 1.4 Why Specifications Matter for IoT

IoT nodes are **resource-constrained**: they operate on battery, transmit over noisy wireless links, and must run for **years unattended**. Specifications are not academic luxuries — they directly govern:

- **Battery life** → governed by *active current per MHz* and *sleep current in µA*.
- **Real-time responsiveness** → governed by *interrupt latency* and *pipeline depth*.
- **Sensor fidelity** → governed by *ADC effective number of bits (ENOB)* and *sample rate*.
- **Wireless throughput** → governed by *SPI clock for radio transceiver* and *DMA channels*.
- **Code footprint** → governed by *Flash size* and *Thumb-2 code density*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Core & Compute Specifications (ARM Cortex-M4)

The Cortex-M4 is the **workhorse core** for IoT microcontrollers. Its specifications are dictated by the ARMv7-M architecture profile.

| Parameter | Specification Value | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | **ARMv7-M (Harvard)** | Separate I-Code/D-Code buses for simultaneous fetch + data access |
| ISA | **Thumb-2 (mixed 16/32-bit)** | High code density (≈ 26% smaller code than pure ARM) |
| Pipeline | **3-stage + branch speculation** | Speculative fetch reduces branch penalty |
| FPU | **Single-precision (IEEE 754)** | Hardware float for DSP / sensor fusion |
| MPU | **8 regions, optional** | Memory protection for RTOS isolation |
| Dhrystone | **1.25 DMIPS / MHz** | Industry-standard CPU benchmark |
| CoreMark | **3.42 CoreMark / MHz** | Modern replacement for Dhrystone |
| Interrupt Latency | **12 cycles** (deterministic) | Critical for hard real-time IoT control |
| Wake-up Time | **< 2 µs** from Stop mode | Ultra-fast duty-cycled sleep |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Thumb-2" for IoT?** IoT devices have tight Flash budgets. Thumb-2 compresses common operations into 16-bit encodings while reserving 32-bit encodings for complex instructions — yielding **26% smaller code** with **98% of ARM performance**.

## 2.2 Memory Specifications

### 2.2.1 Memory Architecture (Harvard-style on Cortex-M4)

```
+---------------+    +--------+    +-----------+    +--------------+
|   I-Code Bus  |--->|  Flash |--->| Instruction|
|   (32-bit)    |    |   ROM  |    |   Fetch   |
+---------------+    +--------+    +-----------+
                                    
+---------------+    +--------+    +-----------+    +--------------+
|   D-Code Bus  |--->|  SRAM  |--->| Load/Store|
|   (32-bit)    |    |  RAM   |    |  Access   |
+---------------+    +--------+    +-----------+
```

### 2.2.2 Typical Memory Sizes (STM32F407 as Reference)

| Memory Type | Size | Address Range (typical) | Volatility |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flash (Main) | **1 MB** | 0x0800 0000 – 0x080F FFFF | Non-volatile |
| SRAM (CCM) | **192 KB** (112 + 64 + 16) | 0x2000 0000 – 0x2002 FFFF | Volatile |
| OTP (One-Time Programmable) | **1 KB** | 0x1FFF 7800 – 0x1FFF 7BFF | Non-volatile |
| Option Bytes | **16 bytes** | 0x1FFF C000 – 0x1FFF C00F | Non-volatile |
| System Memory (Boot ROM) | **30 KB** | 0x1FFF 0000 – 0x1FFF 77FF | Mask ROM |

> [!NOTE]
> **CCM = Core-Coupled Memory.** A special zero-wait-state SRAM tightly coupled to the D-Code bus. It is **not accessible by DMA** — only by the CPU, which is why it is used for time-critical stacks.

## 2.3 Bus Architecture & AHB/APB Matrix

The bus matrix is the **central nervous system** that routes CPU, DMA, and peripheral transactions.

| Bus | Specification | Width | Max Frequency | Connected Peripherals |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AHB1** | Advanced High-performance Bus 1 | 32-bit | **168 MHz** | GPIO A–I, DMA, SRAM, Flash I/F |
| **AHB2** | AHB 2 | 32-bit | **168 MHz** | USB OTG, ETH MAC, DCMI, RNG |
| **APB1** | Advanced Peripheral Bus 1 | 32-bit | **42 MHz** | USART2/3, SPI2/3, I2C1–3, CAN, TIM2–7, DAC, ADC |
| **APB2** | Advanced Peripheral Bus 2 | 32-bit | **84 MHz** | USART1/6, SPI1, TIM1/8/9–11, ADC1–3, EXTI, SYSCFG |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Bus Bridge Bottleneck:** AHB runs at 168 MHz but APB1 runs at only 42 MHz (divided by 4). This means peripheral access on APB1 is **4× slower** than CPU access — a common pitfall in high-speed ADC and SPI design.

## 2.4 Clock Tree Specifications

The clock tree is a **multi-stage PLL + prescaler network** that derives all internal frequencies from a few external sources.

```
                                +-------+    +-------+    +-------+
   HSE (8 MHz crystal) -------->|       |    |       |    |       |
                                |  PLL  |--->| /M*N/P|--->| SYSCLK|--> 168 MHz
   HSI (16 MHz RC) ------------>| Mux   |    |       |    |       |
                                +-------+    +-------+    +-------+
                                                               |
                                            +-------+    +------+----+-------+
                                            |  AHB  |    |  /1 /2 /4 /8/16/64/128/256/512 |
                                            | Presc.|--->+--------------+
                                            +-------+         |
                                                              +---> HCLK = 168 MHz
                                                              +---> Cortex SysTick
                                                              +---> DMA
                                            +-------+         
                                            |  APB1 |         HCLK / 4 = 42 MHz
                                            | Presc.|--------->
                                            +-------+
                                            +-------+         
                                            |  APB2 |         HCLK / 2 = 84 MHz
                                            | Presc.|--------->
                                            +-------+
```

### 2.4.1 Key Clock Sources

| Clock Source | Frequency | Stability | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **HSE** (High-Speed External) | 4 – 26 MHz | ±20 ppm (crystal) | Precision timing, USB, Ethernet |
| **HSI** (High-Speed Internal) | 16 MHz RC | ±1 % (after trim) | Power-on default, low-cost designs |
| **LSE** (Low-Speed External) | 32.768 kHz | ±20 ppm | RTC, calendar, low-power standby |
| **LSI** (Low-Speed Internal) | ≈ 32 kHz RC | ±50 % | Watchdog (IWDG), optional RTC |
| **PLL** | Up to 432 MHz | Derived | CPU & peripheral clock generation |

## 2.5 Power Specifications

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Voltage V<sub>DD</sub> | **1.8 – 3.6** | V |
| I/O Voltage V<sub>DDIO</sub> | **1.8 – 3.6** | V |
| Core Voltage (regulator output) | **1.2 ± 10%** | V |
| Current @ 168 MHz, all peripherals ON | ≈ **93** | mA |
| Current @ Sleep mode (peripherals ON) | ≈ **30** | mA |
| Current @ Stop mode (RTC + LSE active) | ≈ **15** | µA |
| Current @ Standby mode (RTC only) | ≈ **2.5** | µA |
| Current @ V<sub>BAT</sub> mode (RTC + 32 backup regs) | ≈ **1.8** | µA |
| Operating Temperature | **–40 to +85** (industrial) | °C |

## 2.6 GPIO Specifications

| Specification | Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Number of GPIO pins | Up to **140** | Spread across 9 ports (A–I) |
| Input voltage V<sub>IN</sub> | **0.0 – V<sub>DD</sub> + 0.3** | 5-V tolerant (FT) pins available |
| Output voltage V<sub>OL</sub> / V<sub>OH</sub> | 0.4 V / V<sub>DD</sub> – 0.4 V | At 8 mA source/sink |
| Maximum source/sink current | **±8 mA** (standard), **±20 mA** (high-drive) | Per pin |
| Total current for all pins | **120 mA** | Budget must not exceed |
| Toggle rate | Up to **84 MHz** (AHB) | With alternate-function output |
| Pull-up / Pull-down resistors | **30 – 50 kΩ** | Software-configurable |

## 2.7 ADC Specifications

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Resolution | **12** | bits |
| Number of converters | 3 (ADC1, ADC2, ADC3) | — |
| Number of channels | **19** multiplexed | — |
| Maximum conversion rate | **2.4** | MSPS (million samples per second) |
| Conversion time (12-bit) | **15** | ADC clock cycles |
| AHB clock for ADC logic | Up to **36** | MHz |
| APB2 clock (ADCCLK) | Up to **84** | MHz |
| Input range | **V<sub>REF–</sub> ≤ V<sub>IN</sub> ≤ V<sub>REF+</sub>** | V |
| Reference voltage V<sub>REF+</sub> | **2.4 – V<sub>DDA</sub>** | V |
| INL (Integral Non-Linearity) | **±1.5** | LSB (typ) |
| DNL (Differential Non-Linearity) | **±0.3** | LSB (typ) |

## 2.8 Communication Interface Specifications

| Interface | Channels | Max Speed / Baud Rate | Voltage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **USART** | 6 | 10.5 Mbit/s | 3.3 V LVTTL |
| **SPI** | 3 | 42 Mbit/s (master) | 3.3 V |
| **I²C** | 3 | 400 kbit/s (Fast), 1 Mbit/s (Fast+) | 3.3 V (5 V tolerant) |
| **CAN** | 2 | 1 Mbit/s | 3.3 V |
| **USB OTG** | 1 | 480 Mbit/s (USB 2.0 HS) | Differential |
| **Ethernet MAC** | 1 | 100 Mbit/s (MII/RMII) | Differential |
| **SDIO** | 1 | 48 MHz | 3.3 V |

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{DMIPS} = f_{\text{CLK}} \times 1.25$ | Dhrystone MIPS at any clock | MIPS |
| 2 | $\text{CoreMark Score} = 3.42 \times f_{\text{MHz}}$ | CoreMark throughput | CoreMark |
| 3 | $V_{\text{LSB}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{REF}+} - V_{\text{REF}-}}{2^n}$ | ADC step size (n = resolution) | V |
| 4 | $N_{\text{ADC}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{IN}} - V_{\text{REF}-}}{V_{\text{LSB}}}$ | ADC digital output code | integer |
| 5 | $f_{\text{SAMPLE,max}} = \dfrac{f_{\text{ADCCLK}}}{15}$ | Max ADC sample rate | samples/s |
| 6 | $f_{\text{SYSCLK}} = f_{\text{HSE}} \times \dfrac{N}{M \times P}$ | PLL output (Cortex-M4) | Hz |
| 7 | $f_{\text{HCLK}} = \dfrac{f_{\text{SYSCLK}}}{\text{AHB\_PSC}}$ | AHB bus clock | Hz |
| 8 | $f_{\text{PCLK1}} = \dfrac{f_{\text{HCLK}}}{\text{APB1\_PSC}}$ | APB1 bus clock | Hz |
| 9 | $f_{\text{PCLK2}} = \dfrac{f_{\text{HCLK}}}{\text{APB2\_PSC}}$ | APB2 bus clock | Hz |
| 10 | $T_{\text{update}} = \dfrac{(\text{PSC}+1)(\text{ARR}+1)}{f_{\text{CK}}}$ | Timer overflow period | s |
| 11 | $\text{Baud}_{\text{USART}} = \dfrac{f_{\text{CK}}}{8 \times (2 - \text{OVER8}) \times \text{USARTDIV}}$ | USART baud rate | bit/s |
| 12 | $T_{\text{INT,max}} = 12 \times T_{\text{CPU}}$ | Worst-case interrupt latency | s |
| 13 | $P_{\text{RUN}} = V_{\text{DD}} \times I_{\text{RUN}}$ | Active power dissipation | W |
| 14 | $E_{\text{cycle}} = P_{\text{RUN}} \times T_{\text{cycle}}$ | Energy per clock cycle | J |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When writing $V_{\text{LSB}}$ in tables, do **not** use the vertical bar character `|` for "absolute value" — it breaks the markdown table. Use $\vert V_{\text{LSB}} \vert$ or $\mid V_{\text{LSB}} \mid$ instead.

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

| IoT Application | Critical Specification | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Battery-powered soil sensor** (LoRa) | Standby current ≈ 2.5 µA | 5-year life on 2 × AA cells |
| **Motor control drone** | FPU + 168 MHz, 2.4 MSPS ADC | Real-time sensor fusion at 1 kHz loop |
| **Wearable fitness band** | Stop-mode current ≈ 15 µA | Heart-rate sampling once per minute |
| **Industrial CAN node** | CAN 2.0B, 1 Mbit/s | Real-time communication on factory bus |
| **Smart thermostat** | RTC with 32 kHz LSE crystal | ±20 ppm clock drift over 1 year |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — ADC Resolution & Digital Code Mapping

**Problem Statement:** A 12-bit ADC on an STM32F407 uses an internal reference V<sub>REF+</sub> = 3.3 V and V<sub>REF–</sub> = 0 V. The sensor produces a voltage V<sub>IN</sub> = 1.65 V. Compute the ADC step size (V<sub>LSB</sub>), the digital output code N<sub>ADC</sub>, and the percentage of full-scale.

### Step 1 — Identify the number of quantization levels

A 12-bit ADC produces $2^{12} = 4096$ distinct digital codes, ranging from 0 to 4095.

### Step 2 — Calculate the LSB voltage

The LSB is the smallest voltage the ADC can distinguish.

$$
V_{\text{LSB}} = \frac{V_{\text{REF}+} - V_{\text{REF}-}}{2^{n}}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
V_{\text{LSB}} = \frac{3.3 - 0}{2^{12}} = \frac{3.3}{4096} \text{ V}
$$

Evaluating the fraction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{LSB}} &= 3.3 \times 2.4414 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} \\
&= 8.0566 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} \\
&\approx 805.66 \; \mu\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Calculate the digital output code

The digital output for an ideal ADC is given by:

$$
N_{\text{ADC}} = \frac{V_{\text{IN}} - V_{\text{REF}-}}{V_{\text{LSB}}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
N_{\text{ADC}} = \frac{1.65 - 0}{8.0566 \times 10^{-4}} = 2048.00
$$

### Step 4 — Calculate the percentage of full-scale

$$
\text{FS\%} = \frac{N_{\text{ADC}}}{2^{n} - 1} \times 100\%
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FS\%} &= \frac{2048}{4095} \times 100\% \\
&= 50.012\% \approx 50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 1.65 V input on a 3.3 V reference is exactly mid-scale, which is the *calibration* point most sensor PCBs place at hardware reset.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — PLL Configuration for 168 MHz System Clock

**Problem Statement:** A STM32F407 uses an 8 MHz external crystal (HSE). Configure the PLL so that f<sub>SYSCLK</sub> = 168 MHz. Choose M = 8, N = 336, P = 2.

### Step 1 — Recall the PLL output formula

The Cortex-M4 PLL output is given by:

$$
f_{\text{SYSCLK}} = f_{\text{HSE}} \times \frac{N}{M \times P}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the given PLL parameters

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{SYSCLK}} &= 8 \, \text{MHz} \times \frac{336}{8 \times 2} \\
&= 8 \times \frac{336}{16} \\
&= 8 \times 21 \\
&= 168 \, \text{MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Verify AHB, APB1, APB2 bus frequencies

With default prescalers: AHB_PSC = 1, APB1_PSC = 4, APB2_PSC = 2:

$$
f_{\text{HCLK}} = \frac{f_{\text{SYSCLK}}}{\text{AHB\_PSC}} = \frac{168}{1} = 168 \; \text{MHz}
$$

$$
f_{\text{PCLK1}} = \frac{f_{\text{HCLK}}}{\text{APB1\_PSC}} = \frac{168}{4} = 42 \; \text{MHz}
$$

$$
f_{\text{PCLK2}} = \frac{f_{\text{HCLK}}}{\text{APB2\_PSC}} = \frac{168}{2} = 84 \; \text{MHz}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constraint Check:** APB1 maximum is 42 MHz, APB2 maximum is 84 MHz. The chosen prescalers are at the boundary — any higher HCLK would require AHB_PSC > 1.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Timer Update Frequency for PWM Generation

**Problem Statement:** A TIM2 timer on APB1 (42 MHz) must generate a PWM with period 1 ms. The timer clock (after prescaler) is fed by APB1 × 2 (since APB1 prescaler > 1, the timer clock is doubled). Find the Auto-Reload Register (ARR) value if the Prescaler (PSC) is 83.

### Step 1 — Determine the timer input clock

Because APB1 prescaler is 4 (> 1), the timer clock is:

$$
f_{\text{CK\_INT}} = 2 \times f_{\text{PCLK1}} = 2 \times 42 = 84 \; \text{MHz}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the timer period formula

$$
T_{\text{update}} = \frac{(\text{PSC} + 1)(\text{ARR} + 1)}{f_{\text{CK\_INT}}}
$$

Rearranging for ARR:

$$
\text{ARR} + 1 = \frac{T_{\text{update}} \times f_{\text{CK\_INT}}}{\text{PSC} + 1}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ARR} + 1 &= \frac{1 \times 10^{-3} \times 84 \times 10^{6}}{83 + 1} \\
&= \frac{84\,000}{84} \\
&= 1000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for ARR

$$
\text{ARR} = 1000 - 1 = 999
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The 2× multiplication on APB1 timers is a *free clock doubler* that allows timers to run faster than the bus — a critical feature for high-resolution PWM motor control.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — USART Baud Rate Configuration

**Problem Statement:** Configure USART2 (on APB1 = 42 MHz) for a baud rate of 115 200 bit/s with oversampling by 16 (OVER8 = 0). Find USARTDIV.

### Step 1 — Recall the USART baud rate formula

$$
\text{Baud} = \frac{f_{\text{CK}}}{8 \times (2 - \text{OVER8}) \times \text{USARTDIV}}
$$

### Step 2 — Rearrange for USARTDIV

$$
\text{USARTDIV} = \frac{f_{\text{CK}}}{8 \times (2 - \text{OVER8}) \times \text{Baud}}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute numerical values

For OVER8 = 0 and f<sub>CK</sub> = 42 MHz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{USARTDIV} &= \frac{42 \times 10^{6}}{8 \times (2 - 0) \times 115\,200} \\
&= \frac{42 \times 10^{6}}{16 \times 115\,200} \\
&= \frac{42 \times 10^{6}}{1\,843\,200} \\
&= 22.7864...
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Split into mantissa and fraction (BRR register fields)

The BRR register holds USARTDIV as a fixed-point number: 12-bit integer part + 4-bit fractional part.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Integer part} &= 22 \\
\text{Fraction} &= 0.7864 \times 16 = 12.58 \approx 13
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, BRR ≈ 22.8125, actual baud = 115 207 bit/s, error ≈ +0.006 %.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often forget the 2× factor on APB1 timers *and* the 2× on OVER8=0 — these halve/double the denominator and yield wildly off baud rates. Always re-derive on the answer sheet.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Specification Decision Flow

| Specification Domain | Question to Ask First | Key Trade-Off |
|---|---|---|
| **Core** | Need DSP / sensor fusion? | M4 (FPU) vs M0+ (cheaper) |
| **Memory** | Code size > 256 KB? | Yes → ≥ 512 KB Flash |
| **Clock** | USB or Ethernet needed? | Yes → HSE crystal mandatory |
| **Power** | Battery or mains? | Battery → prioritize LSE + Stop mode |
| **GPIO** | Driving 5 V loads? | Need 5-V tolerant (FT) pins |
| **ADC** | Audio / vibration sampling? | Need ≥ 1 MSPS, 12-bit |
| **Comm** | Multiple slaves on same bus? | I²C > SPI for pin savings |
| **Package** | Hand-solderable prototype? | LQFP64 > BGA100 |

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture (Block-Level)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture of a typical ARM Cortex-M4 IoT microcontroller (STM32F407-class)**, mapping the interaction between the CPU core, bus matrix, memory, and peripherals.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU[ARM Cortex-M4 Core 32-bit 168 MHz FPU and MPU]
    NVIC[NVIC Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller 82 IRQs]
    SYSTICK[SysTick Timer 24-bit Down Counter]
    DEBUG[CoreSight Debug SWD and JTAG]
    BUSMATRIX[AHB/APB Bus Matrix]

    FLASH[Flash Memory 1 MB Instruction and Data]
    SRAM[SRAM 192 KB CCM 112 KB Main 64 KB Backup 4 KB]
    ROM[System Memory 30 KB Boot ROM]

    AHB1[AHB1 168 MHz 32-bit]
    AHB2[AHB2 168 MHz USB ETH RNG]
    APB1[APB1 42 MHz USART2 3 SPI2 3 I2C1 3 CAN1 2 TIM2 7 DAC]
    APB2[APB2 84 MHz USART1 6 SPI1 TIM1 8 9 11 ADC1 3 EXTI]

    DMA[DMA1 and DMA2 16 Streams Total]
    GPIO[GPIO A to I 140 Pins]
    ADC[ADC1 2 3 12-bit 2.4 MSPS]
    TIM[Timers 1 8 Advanced 16-bit and 32-bit]
    USART[USART 1 to 6 10.5 Mbit per s]
    SPI[SPI 1 to 3 42 Mbit per s]
    I2C[I2C 1 to 3 1 Mbit per s Fast Plus]
    CAN[CAN 2.0B 1 Mbit per s]
    USB[USB OTG HS 480 Mbit per s]
    ETH[Ethernet MAC 100 Mbit per s MII RMII]
    RTC[RTC with LSE 32.768 kHz]
    PWR[Power Control Sleep Stop Standby VBAT]

    CPU --> NVIC
    CPU --> SYSTICK
    CPU --> DEBUG
    CPU --> BUSMATRIX

    BUSMATRIX --> FLASH
    BUSMATRIX --> SRAM
    BUSMATRIX --> ROM

    BUSMATRIX --> AHB1
    BUSMATRIX --> AHB2
    AHB1 --> APB1
    AHB1 --> APB2
    AHB1 --> DMA

    AHB1 --> GPIO
    AHB2 --> USB
    AHB2 --> ETH

    APB1 --> USART
    APB1 --> SPI
    APB1 --> I2C
    APB1 --> CAN
    APB1 --> TIM
    APB1 --> DAC

    APB2 --> ADC
    APB2 --> EXTI
    APB2 --> RTC

    AHB1 --> PWR
    PWR --> RTC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Trace the bus hierarchy top-down. The CPU and DMA are *bus masters*; everything below AHB1 is a *bus slave*. APB peripherals cannot be DMA'd directly — DMA flows through AHB1 only.

## 4.2 Memory Map Schematic

The Cortex-M4 reserves a **4 GB linear address space**, partitioned into distinct regions. Below is a Mermaid block-flow of the canonical memory map.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph REG0[0x00000000 to 0x1FFFFFFF Code Region 512 MB]
        FLASH_BOOT[0x00000000 Alias Flash or System Memory]
        FLASH_MAIN[0x08000000 Main Flash up to 1 MB]
        SYSMEM[0x1FFF0000 System Memory Boot ROM 30 KB]
        OTP[0x1FFF7800 OTP 1 KB]
        OPTBYTES[0x1FFFC000 Option Bytes 16 B]
    end

    subgraph REG1[0x20000000 to 0x3FFFFFFF SRAM Region 512 MB]
        SRAM1[0x20000000 Main SRAM 112 KB]
        CCM[0x10000000 CCM SRAM 64 KB]
    end

    subgraph REG2[0x40000000 to 0x5FFFFFFF Peripheral Region 512 MB]
        APB1MAP[0x40000000 APB1 Peripherals]
        AHB1MAP[0x40020000 AHB1 Peripherals GPIO DMA]
        APB2MAP[0x40010000 APB2 Peripherals]
        AHB2MAP[0x50000000 AHB2 Peripherals USB ETH]
    end

    subgraph REG3[0x60000000 to 0x9FFFFFFF External Memory 1 GB]
        FSMC[FSMC Bank 1 to 4 External SRAM NOR NAND]
    end

    subgraph REG4[0xE0000000 to 0xFFFFFFFF System Region 512 MB]
        NVICAREA[0xE000E000 NVIC and System Control Space]
        PPB[0xE0000000 Private Peripheral Bus]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bit-Banding:** Alias regions at `0x2200 0000` (SRAM) and `0x4200 0000 (Peripherals)` allow *atomic* bit-level read-modify-write operations via a single 32-bit access — a unique Cortex-M feature ideal for GPIO control.

## 4.3 Power Mode State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Run
    Run --> Sleep : WFI or WFE instruction Peripherals ON
    Sleep --> Run : Any Interrupt
    Run --> Stop : PWR CR LPDS + PDDS Sleep ON
    Stop --> Run : EXTI or RTC Wakeup
    Run --> Standby : PWR CR PDDS Set WFI
    Standby --> Run : NRST Reset or RTC Wakeup WKUP Pin
    Stop --> Standby : Deep Sleep Down
    Standby --> [*] : Power Loss

    note right of Run : Full 168 MHz Core active ≈ 93 mA
    note right of Sleep : CPU clock gated Peripherals ON ≈ 30 mA
    note right of Stop : All clocks off SRAM retained RTC active ≈ 15 uA
    note right of Standby : Core off SRAM off RTC on LSE only ≈ 2.5 uA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The state machine shows the **waking trigger** for each low-power mode. Sleep wakes on **any IRQ**, Stop wakes on **EXTI or RTC**, Standby wakes on **NRST or WKUP pin** — these distinctions are favourite KTU viva questions.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"List and briefly explain any six key specifications of an ARM Cortex-M4 microcontroller used in IoT applications."**

**Model Answer:**

The six key specifications of an ARM Cortex-M4 microcontroller for IoT are:

1. **Core Architecture (ARMv7-M, 32-bit RISC, 3-stage pipeline):** Provides 1.25 DMIPS/MHz with branch speculation for deterministic, low-latency execution — essential for real-time sensor processing.

2. **Thumb-2 Instruction Set:** A mix of 16-bit and 32-bit encodings that reduces code size by ~26 % compared to pure ARM — critical for IoT nodes with limited Flash memory (e.g., 256 KB).

3. **Memory Specifications:** Typical Flash up to 1 MB, SRAM up to 192 KB (with CCM for zero-wait-state CPU access), allowing storage of RTOS, application logic, and sensor data buffers.

4. **Clock Specifications:** PLL-based clock tree with HSE (4–26 MHz crystal) and HSI (16 MHz RC) sources, generating SYSCLK up to 168 MHz, HCLK up to 168 MHz, with APB1 at 42 MHz and APB2 at 84 MHz.

5. **Power Specifications:** Operating voltage 1.8–3.6 V, active current ~93 mA at 168 MHz, Stop mode current ~15 µA with RTC, and Standby mode current ~2.5 µA — enabling multi-year battery life.

6. **Peripheral Specifications:** Up to 140 GPIO, three 12-bit ADCs (2.4 MSPS), multiple USART/SPI/I²C/CAN interfaces, and USB OTG/Ethernet MAC — providing connectivity to virtually any IoT sensor or radio.

---

### **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"Differentiate between the four low-power modes of a Cortex-M4 microcontroller. State typical current consumption in each."**

**Model Answer:**

| Low-Power Mode | CPU State | Peripherals | Clock Source | Typical Current |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sleep** | CPU clock gated (state retained) | All ON | HCLK running | ≈ 30 mA |
| **Stop (low-power)** | Powered off, SRAM retained | Most OFF | HSI off, LSE/LSE for RTC | ≈ 15 µA |
| **Standby** | Powered off, SRAM lost | All OFF | LSE 32.768 kHz for RTC only | ≈ 2.5 µA |
| **V<sub>BAT</sub>** | Powered off | RTC + 20 backup registers | LSE or LSI | ≈ 1.8 µA |

The **wake-up latency** increases as power decreases: Sleep wakes in <12 cycles, Stop in ~2 µs, and Standby requires a full reset (milliseconds).

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks).** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the bus matrix architecture of a Cortex-M4 microcontroller. List the maximum frequencies of AHB, APB1, and APB2 buses, and name any four peripherals connected to each APB bus.

**(b) [7 Marks)** An STM32F407 system uses an 8 MHz HSE crystal and the PLL is configured with M = 8, N = 336, P = 2. Compute the system clock, HCLK, PCLK1, and PCLK2 frequencies. If the APB1 prescaler is 4 and the APB2 prescaler is 2, find the maximum frequency of a general-purpose timer on APB1 and explain the 2× multiplier rule.

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

The bus matrix is the **switching fabric** that arbitrates access between the CPU, DMA, and peripherals across three independent buses: AHB, APB1, and APB2.

**Block Diagram:**

```
        +-------+      +-----------+      +-----------+
 CPU -->|       |----->|  AHB1     |----->| AHB2      |
        |       |      | 168 MHz   |      | 168 MHz   |
 DMA -->|       |      | 32-bit    |      | USB OTG   |
        | BUS   |      +-----------+      | ETH MAC   |
        | MTX   |          |  |           +-----------+
        +-------+          |  |
                           |  +--> APB1 Bridge --> 42 MHz
                           |        + APB2 Bridge --> 84 MHz
```

**Frequencies and Peripheral Allocation:**

| Bus | Max Frequency | Typical Peripherals (4 each) |
|---|---|---|
| **AHB1** | **168 MHz** | GPIOA–I, DMA1/2, Flash I/F, SRAM |
| **AHB2** | **168 MHz** | USB OTG FS/HS, ETH MAC, RNG, DCMI |
| **APB1** | **42 MHz** | USART2/3, SPI2/3, I2C1–3, TIM2–7 |
| **APB2** | **84 MHz** | USART1/6, SPI1, TIM1/8/9–11, ADC1–3 |

**Key Points:**
- APB1 is the **low-speed peripheral bus** and runs at HCLK/4 maximum.
- APB2 is the **high-speed peripheral bus** and runs at HCLK/2 maximum.
- DMA is the only peripheral that can access memory *without* CPU intervention.

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute SYSCLK using the PLL formula:**

$$
f_{\text{SYSCLK}} = f_{\text{HSE}} \times \frac{N}{M \times P}
$$

$$
f_{\text{SYSCLK}} = 8 \; \text{MHz} \times \frac{336}{8 \times 2} = 8 \times 21 = 168 \; \text{MHz}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute HCLK (with default AHB prescaler = 1):**

$$
f_{\text{HCLK}} = \frac{f_{\text{SYSCLK}}}{\text{AHB\_PSC}} = \frac{168}{1} = 168 \; \text{MHz}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute PCLK1:**

$$
f_{\text{PCLK1}} = \frac{f_{\text{HCLK}}}{\text{APB1\_PSC}} = \frac{168}{4} = 42 \; \text{MHz}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute PCLK2:**

$$
f_{\text{PCLK2}} = \frac{f_{\text{HCLK}}}{\text{APB2\_PSC}} = \frac{168}{2} = 84 \; \text{MHz}
$$

**Step 5 — Timer clock on APB1 (the 2× rule):**

When the APB1 prescaler is **not equal to 1** (here, 4), the timer input clock is **doubled** to compensate:

$$
f_{\text{TIMxCLK}} = 2 \times f_{\text{PCLK1}} = 2 \times 42 = 84 \; \text{MHz}
$$

**The 2× Multiplier Rule:**  
Cortex-M4 timers are designed to receive a clock at the same frequency as HCLK (168 MHz). To prevent the APB prescaler from slowing them down, the hardware automatically multiplies the APB clock by 2 when the prescaler > 1. This means *every APB1 timer runs at 84 MHz*, not 42 MHz — a key fact for accurate PWM and delay generation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks).** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat functional block diagram, describe the specifications of an ARM Cortex-M4 microcontroller. Briefly explain the role of NVIC, FPU, and MPU.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An IoT sensor node uses a 12-bit ADC with V<sub>REF+</sub> = 3.3 V to measure a temperature sensor output voltage of 1.245 V. Calculate (i) the LSB voltage, (ii) the digital output code, (iii) the percentage full-scale, and (iv) the minimum voltage difference the ADC can resolve.

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Block Diagram:**

```
                +------------------------------------+
                |        ARM Cortex-M4 MCU            |
                |                                    |
   Sensors --->| GPIO | ADC | Timers |         RTC  |  <--- Battery
                |     |      |        |              |
   Radio   --->| SPI  | I2C  | USART  |         USB  |  <--- Debug
                |     |      |        |              |
                +------------------------------------+
                          |          |
                          v          v
                   +---------+    +---------+
                   |   FPU   |    |   MPU   |
                   | IEEE 754|    | 8 region|
                   | Single  |    |protect  |
                   +---------+    +---------+
                          |          |
                          v          v
                   +-------------------+
                   |   NVIC (82 IRQs) |
                   |   SysTick Timer  |
                   +-------------------+
```

**Role of NVIC, FPU, and MPU:**

1. **NVIC (Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller):** Handles up to 82 maskable interrupts with 16 programmable priority levels. It supports **nested interrupts** (higher-priority IRQ pre-empts lower-priority IRQ) and **tail-chaining** to reduce context-switch overhead to just 6 cycles. Deterministic interrupt latency of 12 cycles makes the M4 ideal for hard real-time IoT.

2. **FPU (Floating-Point Unit):** A single-precision IEEE 754 hardware unit that accelerates floating-point operations. Sensor fusion algorithms (e.g., Kalman filters) on accelerometer + gyroscope data execute **~10× faster** than software-emulated float on Cortex-M0+, with deterministic timing.

3. **MPU (Memory Protection Unit):** Provides up to 8 memory regions with individual access permission (RO/RW/NO ACCESS). In an IoT RTOS, the MPU isolates the kernel from user tasks, preventing a buggy sensor handler from corrupting OS data — essential for safety-critical applications like medical wearables.

**Other Specifications Summary:**

| Subsystem | Specification |
|---|---|
| Core | 32-bit ARMv7-M, 3-stage pipeline, branch speculation |
| Max Frequency | 168 MHz |
| Performance | 1.25 DMIPS/MHz, 3.42 CoreMark/MHz |
| Flash | 1 MB |
| SRAM | 192 KB (incl. CCM) |
| GPIO | Up to 140 |
| ADC | 3 × 12-bit, 2.4 MSPS |
| Comms | 6 × USART, 3 × SPI, 3 × I²C, 2 × CAN, USB OTG, Ethernet |
| Power | 1.8 – 3.6 V; Standby 2.5 µA |

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:**  
n = 12 bits, V<sub>REF+</sub> = 3.3 V, V<sub>REF–</sub> = 0 V, V<sub>IN</sub> = 1.245 V.

**(i) LSB Voltage:**

$$
V_{\text{LSB}} = \frac{V_{\text{REF}+} - V_{\text{REF}-}}{2^n} = \frac{3.3 - 0}{2^{12}} = \frac{3.3}{4096}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{LSB}} &= 3.3 \times 2.4414 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} \\
&\approx 8.0566 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} \\
&\approx 805.66 \; \mu\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Digital Output Code:**

$$
N_{\text{ADC}} = \frac{V_{\text{IN}}}{V_{\text{LSB}}} = \frac{1.245}{8.0566 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
N_{\text{ADC}} = 1545.3 \approx 1545
$$

**(iii) Percentage of Full-Scale:**

$$
\text{FS\%} = \frac{1545}{4095} \times 100\% = 37.73\%
$$

**(iv) Minimum Resolvable Voltage:**

The smallest detectable voltage change is one LSB:

$$
\Delta V_{\text{min}} = V_{\text{LSB}} \approx 805.66 \; \mu\text{V}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** For a 1.245 V sensor output, a 12-bit ADC gives 1545 codes. If the temperature sensor has 10 mV/°C sensitivity, the resolution is ≈ 0.08 °C per LSB — more than adequate for environmental IoT monitoring.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on "Specifications" Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting Units:** Writing "1 MB" without "1 MB Flash" or specifying the address range. *Always* state: parameter + value + unit + (if memory) address range.
> 
> 2. **Bus Frequency Confusion:** Many students write that PCLK1 = 84 MHz (it is 42 MHz on most STM32F4 designs). The **2× multiplier rule applies only to timer clocks**, not to APB peripherals like USART2 or SPI2.
> 
> 3. **ADC LSB Calculation Error:** Using $2^{n-1}$ instead of $2^{n}$ in the denominator, or forgetting that the max code is $2^{n} - 1$ (not $2^{n}$).
> 
> 4. **PLL Formula Error:** Using $P = 1$ when in reality the PLL output divider P can be 2, 4, 6, or 8 — and the formula uses the *selected* value, not a fixed value of 1.
> 
> 5. **Confusing Cortex-M Versions:** Writing "Cortex-M3 has FPU" is incorrect — only **Cortex-M4F and M7** have an FPU. Always check the device datasheet.
> 
> 6. **Skipping the Block Diagram:** Part (a) of any 7-mark specification question *demands* a diagram. A 50 % mark penalty is typical for text-only answers.
