5. **Open-Drain Mode:** The pin can only actively pull LOW; an external pull-up is required for HIGH.

> [!NOTE]
> **Schmitt Trigger Hysteresis:** A Schmitt trigger uses two distinct threshold voltages — $V_{T+}$ (upper) and $V_{T-}$ (lower). The output transitions HIGH only when input crosses $V_{T+}$, and transitions LOW only when input falls below $V_{T-}$. The difference $V_{H} = V_{T+} - V_{T-}$ is the **hysteresis voltage**, typically **0.5 V – 1.5 V** in 5V CMOS logic.

### 2.2 Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) – Operational Theory

The ADC performs three fundamental operations:
1. **Sampling** – Captures the continuous signal at discrete time intervals.
2. **Quantization** – Rounds each sample to the nearest representable digital level.
3. **Encoding** – Converts the quantized value into a binary code.

#### 2.2.1 Types of ADC Architectures (KTU High-Yield)

| ADC Type | Speed | Resolution | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Flash ADC** | Very Fast ($\le 1\text{ GS/s}$) | 6 – 8 bits | Oscilloscopes, video |
| **Successive Approximation (SAR)** | Medium (1 MS/s) | 8 – 18 bits | IoT sensors, microcontrollers |
| **Sigma-Delta ($\Sigma$-$\Delta$)** | Slow | 16 – 24 bits | Audio, precision measurement |
| **Dual-Slope Integrating** | Slow | High (16+ bits) | Digital multimeters |
| **Pipeline ADC** | Fast (100+ MS/s) | 10 – 14 bits | Communications, radar |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Most IoT microcontrollers (ESP32, Arduino Uno) use SAR ADCs** due to their excellent trade-off between speed, power consumption, and resolution.

### 2.3 Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) – The "Pseudo-Analog" Output

PWM does not produce a true analog voltage. Instead, it generates a **square wave** whose **duty cycle** ($D$) is varied to control the average power delivered to a load.

$$V_{avg} = D \times V_{peak} = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} \times V_{peak}$$

A low-pass RC filter applied to the PWM output produces a smooth DC voltage approximately equal to $V_{avg}$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master the following formulas – they appear repeatedly in KTU ESE questions for PCCGT503.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ADC Resolution (LSB) | $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^n}$ | Smallest detectable voltage step |
| 2 | Digital Output Code | $D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{LSB}} \right\rfloor$ | Decimal equivalent of ADC reading |
| 3 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ | Minimum sampling to avoid aliasing |
| 4 | PWM Duty Cycle | $D = \dfrac{T_{ON}}{T_{period}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of time signal is HIGH |
| 5 | PWM Average Voltage | $V_{avg} = D \times V_{peak}$ | DC equivalent of PWM after filtering |
| 6 | PWM Frequency | $f_{PWM} = \dfrac{1}{T_{period}}$ | Inverse of total period |
| 7 | Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio | $SQNR = 6.02n + 1.76 \text{ dB}$ | Theoretical quality of ADC |
| 8 | Settling Time of RC Filter | $\tau = RC$ | Time to reach 63.2% of final value |
| 9 | Hysteresis Voltage | $V_H = V_{T+} - V_{T-}$ | Noise immunity margin |
| 10 | Throughput Rate (SAR) | $f_{throughput} = \dfrac{f_{CLK}}{n+1}$ | For an n-bit SAR ADC |

**Real-world Engineering Utility:**
- **Precision Agriculture:** Soil moisture sensors (analog) are read by an ADC and thresholded digitally to control irrigation pumps.
- **Industrial Automation:** PWM drives variable-speed conveyor belts from a microcontroller that has no built-in DAC.
- **Wearables:** Heart-rate sensors with Sigma-Delta ADCs deliver 16-bit precision at ultra-low power.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 – ADC Resolution and Digital Code Mapping

**Problem Statement:** A 10-bit ADC in an ESP32 has a reference voltage $V_{REF} = 3.3\text{ V}$. An analog input $V_{in} = 1.65\text{ V}$ is applied. Determine:
(a) The LSB voltage (resolution)
(b) The digital output code (decimal and binary)
(c) The quantization error in volts.

**Step 1 – Identify the number of quantization levels**

For an $n$-bit ADC, the total number of discrete digital codes is:
$$N = 2^n$$

Substituting $n = 10$:
$$N = 2^{10} = 1024 \text{ codes}$$

**Step 2 – Calculate the LSB voltage (resolution)**

The smallest voltage change that the ADC can distinguish is:
$$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^n}$$

Substituting the given values:
$$V_{LSB} = \frac{3.3\text{ V}}{1024} = 0.003223\text{ V} \approx 3.223\text{ mV}$$

**Step 3 – Compute the decimal output code**

The output code is the integer part of the input voltage divided by the LSB:
$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{LSB}} \right\rfloor$$

Substituting $V_{in} = 1.65\text{ V}$:
$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{1.65}{0.003223} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 511.94 \right\rfloor = 511$$

**Step 4 – Convert decimal 511 to 10-bit binary**

We perform successive division by 2 (or recognize $511 = 2^9 - 1$):
$$511_{10} = 0111111111_2$$

**Step 5 – Quantization error calculation**

The ideal voltage represented by code 511 is:
$$V_{ideal} = D \times V_{LSB} = 511 \times 0.003223 = 1.6469\text{ V}$$

The quantization error is the deviation of the ideal representation from the true input:
$$e_q = V_{in} - V_{ideal} = 1.65 - 1.6469 = 0.0031\text{ V}$$

By design, the maximum quantization error is bounded by:
$$\vert e_q \vert \le \frac{V_{LSB}}{2} = \pm 1.61\text{ mV}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** The quantization error is *inherent* and *irreducible* for a given resolution. To halve this error, the resolution must be doubled (e.g., from 10-bit to 11-bit) **OR** the reference voltage must be halved (which also reduces dynamic range).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 – PWM Duty Cycle for Required Average Voltage

**Problem Statement:** A DC motor requires an average voltage of $2.4\text{ V}$ from a $5.0\text{ V}$ PWM signal to operate at the desired speed. Determine the required duty cycle and the ON-time if the PWM frequency is $1\text{ kHz}$.

**Step 1 – Compute the total period**

$$T = \frac{1}{f_{PWM}} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1\text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 – Solve for duty cycle**

Starting from the average voltage equation:
$$V_{avg} = D \times V_{peak}$$

Rearranging for $D$:
$$D = \frac{V_{avg}}{V_{peak}}$$

Substituting the given values:
$$D = \frac{2.4}{5.0} = 0.48 = 48\%$$

**Step 3 – Compute the ON-time**

$$T_{ON} = D \times T = 0.48 \times 1\text{ ms} = 480\text{ \textmu s}$$

**Step 4 – Compute the OFF-time**

$$T_{OFF} = T - T_{ON} = 1000\text{ \textmu s} - 480\text{ \textmu s} = 520\text{ \textmu s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Many students confuse $T_{ON}$ with the *total period*. The ON-time is **strictly less than** the total period for any duty cycle below 100%. Always verify: $T_{ON} + T_{OFF} = T$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 – Nyquist Sampling Theorem Application

**Problem Statement:** An audio signal has a maximum frequency component of $f_{max} = 8\text{ kHz}$. Determine the minimum sampling frequency to avoid aliasing. If a 12-bit ADC is used with $V_{REF} = 5\text{ V}$, calculate the SQNR.

**Step 1 – Apply the Nyquist criterion**

$$f_s \ge 2 \times f_{max}$$

Substituting:
$$f_s \ge 2 \times 8000 = 16000\text{ Hz} = 16\text{ kHz}$$

**Step 2 – Standard oversampling choice**

In practice, audio systems use $f_s = 44.1\text{ kHz}$ (CD quality) — about 2.76× the Nyquist rate. This is a **guard band** that allows practical anti-aliasing filters to roll off cleanly.

**Step 3 – Calculate SQNR for 12-bit ADC**

$$SQNR = 6.02n + 1.76 \text{ dB}$$

Substituting $n = 12$:
$$SQNR = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.0\text{ dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb:** Each additional bit of resolution contributes approximately **6 dB** of signal-to-quantization-noise ratio. This is why 16-bit audio CDs achieve ~98 dB SQNR, while 24-bit studio recordings achieve ~146 dB.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Choosing the Right I/O Type

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a high-yield KTU question pattern. Memorize the decision logic.**

| System Requirement | Recommended Interface | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Read ON/OFF push button | Digital Input with pull-up | Simple binary state, debounce in software |
| Measure room temperature | Analog Input + ADC (10-bit) | LM35 produces continuous analog voltage |
| Drive an LED with brightness control | PWM Output | No true analog needed; PWM is energy-efficient |
| Generate a sine wave audio output | DAC (8/12-bit) | PWM has too much harmonic distortion for audio |
| Read a high-speed vibration sensor | External SAR/Flash ADC | MCU's internal ADC may be too slow |
| Control a relay (ON/OFF) | Digital Output | Relay is a binary actuator |
| Read a rotary encoder | Digital Input (interrupt) | Produces quadrature pulses, not analog levels |
| Battery voltage monitoring | ADC with voltage divider | Battery voltage may exceed $V_{REF}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram – Microcontroller I/O Subsystem Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU[CPU Core] --> BR[Bus Matrix and AHB APB]
    BR --> GPIOA[GPIO Port A]
    BR --> GPIOB[GPIO Port B]
    BR --> ADC1[ADC Unit 12 bit]
    BR --> TIM1[Timer 1 PWM]
    BR --> DAC1[DAC Unit 8 bit]
    
    GPIOA --> PIN1[PA0 Push Pull]
    GPIOA --> PIN2[PA1 Open Drain]
    GPIOA --> PIN3[PA2 Input Pull Up]
    GPIOB --> PIN4[PB0 Schmitt Input]
    
    ADC1 --> CH1[Channel 0 Temp Sensor]
    ADC1 --> CH2[Channel 1 Potentiometer]
    ADC1 --> CH3[Channel 2 LDR]
    
    TIM1 --> PWM1[PWM Channel 1 LED Dimming]
    TIM1 --> PWM2[PWM Channel 2 Motor Speed]
    
    DAC1 --> OUT1[Analog Audio Out]
    
    PIN1 --> LED1[External LED]
    PIN2 --> REL1[External Relay]
    TIM1 --> M1[DC Motor]
    DAC1 --> SPK[Speaker]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The **Bus Matrix** acts as a central highway, connecting the CPU to all peripheral blocks. Each peripheral (GPIO, ADC, Timer, DAC) has its own configuration registers, which the CPU programs via the bus.

---

### 4.2 Functional Flow – Analog-to-Digital Conversion Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Continuous Analog Input Vin] --> B[Anti Aliasing LPF]
    B --> C[Sample and Hold S/H]
    C --> D[Quantizer 2 to the power n levels]
    D --> E[Encoder Binary Output]
    E --> F[Digital Code D 0 to 2n minus 1]
    F --> G[Microcontroller Register]
    G --> H[CPU Reads and Processes]
    
    VREF[Reference Voltage VREF] --> D
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style F fill:#fff4e1
    style H fill:#e1ffe1
```

**Block-by-Block Explanation:**

1. **Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter (LPF):** Removes frequency components above $f_s/2$ to prevent aliasing. Typically a 2nd-order Butterworth or Sallen-Key active filter.
2. **Sample and Hold (S/H):** A switch and capacitor hold the input voltage *constant* during conversion (otherwise small input changes during quantization corrupt the result).
3. **Quantizer:** Divides $V_{REF}$ range into $2^n$ discrete levels.
4. **Encoder:** Outputs the binary equivalent of the quantized level.

---

### 4.3 Functional Flow – PWM Generation and Filtering for DAC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CPU writes CCR value] --> B[Timer Counter CNT]
    B --> C{Compare CNT with CCR}
    C -->|CNT less CCR| D[Output HIGH]
    C -->|CNT greater CCR| E[Output LOW]
    D --> F[PWM Waveform]
    E --> F
    F --> G[RC Low Pass Filter]
    G --> H[Smooth DC Voltage Vavg]
    H --> I[Load Speaker or Motor]
    
    style F fill:#ffe1e1
    style H fill:#e1ffe1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Relationship:** The **Capture/Compare Register (CCR)** value directly controls the duty cycle:
> $$D = \frac{CCR}{ARR + 1} \times 100\%$$
> where **ARR** is the Auto-Reload Register (period setting) of the timer.

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram – Signal Conditioning Chain for Sensor Interfacing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Physical Sensor] --> SG[Signal Generation]
    SG --> AMP[Amplifier Gain stage]
    AMP --> FLT[Filter LPF or HPF]
    FLT --> LMT[Voltage Limiter Zener Clamp]
    LMT --> MC[Microcontroller ADC Pin]
    MC --> REG[Digital Register Value]
    REG --> CPU[CPU Processing]
    
    style S fill:#fff4e1
    style MC fill:#e1f5ff
    style CPU fill:#e1ffe1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Signal Conditioning is Mandatory:** Most real-world sensors produce signals that are either too small (e.g., thermocouples: $\mu$V), too noisy, or outside the ADC's voltage range. The conditioning chain **amplifies**, **filters**, and **clamps** the signal to a usable form.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
Define **Digital I/O** and **Analog I/O**. Give **two examples** of each from a typical IoT sensor node.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Digital I/O** is the binary signaling interface where a microcontroller reads or writes one of two discrete logic levels — HIGH (typically $V_{DD}$, e.g., 3.3 V) or LOW (0 V). Digital inputs are read by GPIO pins configured in input mode; digital outputs drive GPIO pins in output mode. **Examples:** A push button (digital input) and an LED indicator (digital output). **[1.5 marks]**
- **Analog I/O** handles continuously varying signals. Analog inputs (e.g., from a potentiometer or LM35 temperature sensor) are sampled by the on-chip ADC and represented as multi-bit binary codes. Analog outputs (e.g., audio) are produced by a DAC or reconstructed from PWM through a low-pass filter. **Examples:** Potentiometer wiper voltage (analog input) and DC motor speed control via PWM (analog-equivalent output). **[1.5 marks]**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
What is the **Schmitt trigger** in a GPIO input buffer? Why is it preferred over a simple logic gate for noisy digital inputs?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Schmitt trigger** is a comparator with **hysteresis** — it uses two distinct threshold voltages: an upper threshold $V_{T+}$ (for HIGH transition) and a lower threshold $V_{T-}$ (for LOW transition). The output only changes state when the input crosses the *corresponding* threshold. **[1.5 marks]**
It is preferred because the **hysteresis band** $V_H = V_{T+} - V_{T-}$ provides **noise immunity**. If a noisy input oscillates around a single threshold (as it would with a simple gate), it causes multiple false transitions at the output. The Schmitt trigger's two-level threshold ensures the input must travel a wider voltage range before the output switches, eliminating spurious toggles. **[1.5 marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

**Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an **ADC** in a microcontroller. List the **three fundamental operations** performed by an ADC and identify which type of ADC is most commonly used in **IoT-class microcontrollers**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 12-bit ADC in an ESP32-based IoT node is configured with $V_{REF} = 3.3\text{ V}$. An analog input voltage of $1.234\text{ V}$ is applied. Calculate the **(i)** LSB resolution, **(ii)** digital output code in decimal and binary, and **(iii)** maximum theoretical quantization error. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution – Question A

**(a) Block diagram and explanation (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Analog Input Vin] --> B[Sample and Hold]
    B --> C[Comparator Bank]
    REF[Reference VREF] --> C
    C --> D[SAR Logic n bit]
    D --> E[Output Register]
    E --> F[Digital Code D 0 to 4095]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style F fill:#e1ffe1
```

**Three Fundamental Operations of an ADC:**

1. **Sampling** – The continuous input signal is measured at discrete time instants determined by the sampling clock $f_s$. The sample-and-hold circuit freezes the voltage during the conversion period. **[1 mark]**
2. **Quantization** – Each sampled voltage is mapped to the nearest of $2^n$ discrete levels. This step introduces an unavoidable **quantization error** of up to $\pm V_{LSB}/2$. **[1 mark]**
3. **Encoding** – The quantized level is converted into a binary code ($n$-bit), which is read by the CPU. **[1 mark]**

**Most Common ADC in IoT Microcontrollers:**
The **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** is the dominant choice for IoT microcontrollers (ESP32, STM32, Arduino Uno's ATmega328P). Reasons: **(i)** Low power consumption (suitable for battery-operated IoT nodes), **(ii)** moderate resolution (8–12 bits, sufficient for most sensors), **(iii)** medium speed (1 MS/s, more than adequate for sensor sampling), and **(iv)** small silicon area. **[1.5 marks]**

**SAR Working Principle:** The SAR logic performs a binary search. For a 12-bit ADC, the MSB is set first; the comparator checks if $V_{in} > V_{DAC}/2$. If yes, the MSB stays at 1; if no, it resets to 0. This repeats for each successive bit, completing conversion in exactly $n$ clock cycles. **[2.5 marks for diagram + principle]**

---

**(b) Numerical calculation (7 marks):**

**(i) LSB Resolution:**

$$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^n} = \frac{3.3}{2^{12}} = \frac{3.3}{4096} = 8.0566 \times 10^{-4}\text{ V} = 0.8057\text{ mV}$$

**[2 marks]**

**(ii) Digital output code:**

Decimal:
$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{LSB}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{1.234}{0.0008057} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 1531.6 \right\rfloor = 1531$$

**[1.5 marks]**

Binary conversion of 1531:
$$1531 = 1024 + 512 - 5 = 1024 + 256 + 128 + 64 + 32 + 16 + 8 + 2 + 1$$
$$= 2^{10} + 2^8 + 2^7 + 2^6 + 2^5 + 2^4 + 2^3 + 2^1 + 2^0$$
$$1531_{10} = 010111111011_2$$

**[1.5 marks]**

**(iii) Maximum theoretical quantization error:**

$$\vert e_q \vert_{max} = \frac{V_{LSB}}{2} = \frac{0.8057\text{ mV}}{2} = 0.4028\text{ mV}$$

**[2 marks]**

---

**Question B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` (ALTERNATIVE)**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)**. With a neat timing diagram, describe how varying the **duty cycle** controls the average voltage across a DC load. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An IoT smart-fan controller uses PWM at $f_{PWM} = 25\text{ kHz}$ from a $3.3\text{ V}$ GPIO to drive a DC motor through a MOSFET. The motor is required to run at "half speed" (average voltage $= 1.65\text{ V}$). Calculate the **(i)** required duty cycle, **(ii)** ON-time and OFF-time per PWM cycle, and **(iii)** explain why the motor's mechanical inertia acts as a natural low-pass filter for the PWM signal. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution – Question B

**(a) PWM Concept and Timing Diagram (7 marks):**

**Definition:** Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) is a technique to generate a **variable average DC voltage** by rapidly switching a digital output between HIGH and LOW states. The fraction of time the signal stays HIGH is called the **duty cycle (D)**. **[1 mark]**

**Timing Diagram Description:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOW["D = 25 percent Vavg = 0.825 V"]
    A1[HIGH for 10us] --> A2[LOW for 30us]
    end
    subgraph MID["D = 50 percent Vavg = 1.65 V"]
    B1[HIGH for 20us] --> B2[LOW for 20us]
    end
    subgraph HIGH["D = 75 percent Vavg = 2.475 V"]
    C1[HIGH for 30us] --> C2[LOW for 10us]
    end
    style A1 fill:#90EE90
    style A2 fill:#FFB6C1
    style B1 fill:#90EE90
    style B2 fill:#FFB6C1
    style C1 fill:#90EE90
    style C2 fill:#FFB6C1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Waveform Structure:** In all three cases, the **period (T) remains constant** (here, $40\text{ \textmu s}$), but the **ON-time varies**. A higher duty cycle → larger $T_{ON}$ → higher average voltage → brighter LED / faster motor. **[2 marks for diagram]**

**Mathematical Foundation:**
$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^{T_{ON}} V_{peak}\,dt = \frac{T_{ON}}{T} \cdot V_{peak} = D \cdot V_{peak}$$

**Three key properties:** **[2 marks]**
- Frequency $f_{PWM} = 1/T$ is held constant.
- Only $D$ (or equivalently, $T_{ON}$) is varied.
- The peak amplitude $V_{peak}$ is unchanged (always at the supply rail).
- **Efficiency:** Because the output is either fully ON (low $V_{DS}$ across the MOSFET → low power loss) or fully OFF (no current → no power loss), PWM is extremely power-efficient — typically 90%+ for motor control.

**Applications in IoT:** LED dimming, servo motor angle control, DC motor speed, audio buzzers, and (with filtering) crude DAC functionality. **[2 marks]**

---

**(b) Smart-Fan PWM Calculation (7 marks):**

**(i) Required Duty Cycle:**

$$D = \frac{V_{avg}}{V_{peak}} = \frac{1.65}{3.3} = 0.5 = 50\%$$

**[1.5 marks]**

**(ii) ON-time and OFF-time:**

Total period:
$$T = \frac{1}{f_{PWM}} = \frac{1}{25000} = 40\text{ \textmu s}$$

ON-time:
$$T_{ON} = D \times T = 0.5 \times 40 = 20\text{ \textmu s}$$

OFF-time:
$$T_{OFF} = T - T_{ON} = 40 - 20 = 20\text{ \textmu s}$$

**[1.5 + 1.5 = 3 marks]**

**(iii) Motor Inertia as Natural LPF (2 marks):**

A DC motor's rotor has significant **mechanical inertia** (angular momentum $J \cdot \omega$). It cannot instantaneously change its rotational speed. The 25 kHz PWM cycle (40 µs period) is *thousands of times faster* than the motor's mechanical time constant (typically 10–100 ms). Therefore:
- During $T_{ON} = 20\text{ \textmu s}$, the motor receives a current pulse that imparts a small angular impulse.
- During $T_{OFF} = 20\text{ \textmu s}$, the motor's inertia carries it forward.
- The mechanical system **averages out** the rapid electrical pulses, behaving as if it were receiving a steady 1.65 V DC.
- The motor's inductance (and back-EMF) further smooths the current ripple.

This is why **no external low-pass filter is required** when PWM drives a motor — the *load itself* acts as the filter.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout – Digital & Analog I/O**
> 1. **Do not confuse PWM frequency with the modulated signal frequency.** A 25 kHz PWM driving a 1 kHz audio buzzer will still produce a 25 kHz switching signal — the *load* must filter it.
> 2. **Forgetting the $2^n$ factor.** Many students write $V_{LSB} = V_{REF}/n$ instead of $V_{REF}/2^n$. This single error loses **2 marks** instantly.
> 3. **Skipping the floor function in ADC code computation.** If $V_{in}/V_{LSB}$ is not an integer, the code is the *integer part* (floor), not the rounded value.
> 4. **Naming the wrong ADC type.** Saying "Flash ADC" for an IoT microcontroller is incorrect — Flash ADCs are used in oscilloscopes, not in ESP32/Arduino boards. The correct answer is **SAR ADC**.
> 5. **Confusing pull-up vs. pull-down.** A pull-up keeps the pin HIGH when floating; a pull-down keeps it LOW. Choosing the wrong one inverts your input logic.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this checklist before the exam — it is your high-yield summary.**

- [x] **Digital I/O** = binary two-level signaling (HIGH/LOW). Implemented via GPIO pins with push-pull, open-drain, and pull-up/pull-down options.
- [x] **Analog I/O** = continuous signal handling. Input uses **ADC**; output uses **DAC** or **PWM + LPF**.
- [x] **ADC resolution formula** is the single most important equation: $V_{LSB} = V_{REF}/2^n$. Memorize it.
- [x] **SAR ADC** is the workhorse of IoT microcontrollers (low power, 10–12 bit, 1 MS/s).
- [x] **Nyquist theorem**: $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$. Sample at least twice the highest signal frequency.
- [x] **Quantization error** is bounded by $\pm V_{LSB}/2$ and is **inherent** — cannot be eliminated, only reduced by increasing $n$ or decreasing $V_{REF}$.
- [x] **SQNR** = $6.02n + 1.76$ dB. Each extra bit adds ~6 dB of dynamic range.
- [x] **PWM duty cycle** $D = T_{ON}/T$. Average voltage $V_{avg} = D \times V_{peak}$. PWM is *not* true analog — it is a switched signal.
- [x] **Schmitt trigger hysteresis** prevents false triggering from noise on digital inputs.
- [x] **Signal conditioning chain**: Sensor → Amplifier → Filter → Limiter → ADC.
- [x] **PWM frequency for motor control**: typically 20–25 kHz (above audible range, below switching losses).
- [x] **Voltage divider** is mandatory when a sensor output exceeds the MCU's $V_{REF}$ (e.g., 5 V sensor into 3.3 V ADC).
- [x] **Open-drain outputs** need an external pull-up; they can only actively pull LOW.
- [x] **Anti-aliasing filter** must precede the ADC sampler with cutoff $\le f_s/2$.
- [x] **Most exam marks are lost** on: wrong ADC type, missing $2^n$ factor, forgetting floor function, and not labeling PWM timing diagrams clearly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensor and Actuator Interfacing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT503 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sensor and Actuator Interfacing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Sensor and Actuator Interfacing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensor Interfacing** is the systematic process of physically and electronically coupling an input transducer (sensor) to a microcontroller or microprocessor so that the physical phenomenon being measured is converted into a digital quantity that the processor can interpret, store, and act upon.
>
> **Actuator Interfacing** is the converse process of coupling an output transducer (actuator) to the processor, enabling the controller to convert digital decisions into real-world physical actions such as motion, light, heat, or fluid flow.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **PCCGT503**, the term *interfacing* covers three distinct engineering layers:

1. **Electrical Layer** – voltage level translation, current drive capability, and isolation (e.g., optocouplers, level shifters).
2. **Logical Layer** – register configuration, timing diagrams, and protocol handling (GPIO, ADC, PWM, I²C, SPI, UART).
3. **Functional Layer** – the algorithm that maps sensed data to actuator commands.

> [!NOTE]
> A **Sensor** is a *passive* device that converts a physical stimulus (temperature, light, pressure) into an electrical signal. An **Actuator** is an *active* device that converts an electrical signal into a physical action.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body

Imagine the microcontroller as the **human brain** sitting inside a dark skull:

- The **senses** (eyes, ears, skin, nose, tongue) are the **sensors**. They convert external stimuli (light, sound, pressure, smell, taste) into nerve signals.
- The **muscles and glands** are the **actuators**. They receive signals from the brain and produce motion, speech, or hormonal release.
- The **nervous system** is the *interfacing circuitry* — it carries signals, amplifies them, conditions them, and routes them between brain and body.
- The **spinal cord and reflexes** represent *interrupt-driven* responses, bypassing full brain processing for speed.

Just as the brain cannot directly "see" light without the eyes, a microcontroller cannot directly "feel" temperature without a sensor like the **LM35** or **DHT11**. Likewise, the brain cannot "push" a lever without muscles — the processor cannot "rotate a motor" without a driver like the **L293D** or a MOSFET.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** Sensor = "Sense and report"; Actuator = "Accept command and act".

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold for Emphasis)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Transducer** | A device that converts energy from one form to another. Sensors and actuators are both transducers. |
| **Signal Conditioning** | Amplification, filtering, and linearization applied to a raw sensor signal before ADC conversion. |
| **Resolution** | The smallest change in input that an ADC can detect, determined by the number of bits *n*. |
| **Driver Circuit** | An electronic circuit (transistor, relay, H-bridge) that provides the current/voltage an actuator requires. |
| **GPIO** | **G**eneral **P**urpose **I**nput/**O**utput pin on a microcontroller. |
| **ADC** | **A**nalog-to-**D**igital **C**onverter. |
| **PWM** | **P**ulse-**W**idth **M**odulation. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Sensors

Sensors can be classified along three orthogonal axes relevant to KTU examinations:

### 2.1.1 By Output Signal Type
- **Analog Sensors** – produce a continuous voltage/current proportional to the measurand.
  - Examples: LM35 (temperature), TMP36, photodiode with transimpedance amplifier, potentiometer, NTC thermistor.
- **Digital Sensors** – produce a discrete bit pattern (binary, PWM, or serial).
  - Examples: DHT11/DHT22 (temperature + humidity over single-wire), DS18B20 (1-Wire), MPU6050 (I²C accelerometer/gyro), HC-SR04 (ultrasonic, gives time-of-flight pulse width).

### 2.1.2 By Power Requirement
- **Active Sensors** – require external excitation (e.g., RTD, strain gauge in a Wheatstone bridge).
- **Passive Sensors** – self-generating (e.g., thermocouple, piezoelectric sensor, solar cell).

### 2.1.3 By Measurand (most common in KTU questions)
| Measurand | Common Sensor | Output Type |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | LM35, DS18B20 | Analog / Digital |
| Humidity | DHT11, DHT22 | Digital |
| Light | LDR, photodiode, BH1750 | Analog / I²C |
| Distance | HC-SR04, VL53L0X | Digital (pulse / I²C) |
| Gas / Air Quality | MQ-2, MQ-135 | Analog |
| Motion / Acceleration | PIR, MPU6050 | Digital / I²C |
| Pressure | BMP180, MPX4115 | I²C / Analog |

## 2.2 Classification of Actuators

| Actuator | Energy Domain | Typical Interface to MCU |
|---|---|---|
| DC Motor | Rotational mechanical | H-Bridge (L293D, L298N) + PWM |
| Servo Motor | Positional rotational | PWM (50 Hz, 1–2 ms pulse) |
| Stepper Motor | Precise rotational | Driver IC (ULN2003, A4988) + GPIO sequence |
| Relay / Contactor | Switching AC mains | Transistor (BC547) + flyback diode |
| Solenoid | Linear mechanical | MOSFET + flyback diode |
| LED / Laser Diode | Optical | Current-limiting resistor / MOSFET |
| Buzzer / Speaker | Acoustic | GPIO / PWM |
| Peltier / Heater | Thermal | MOSFET + PWM |
| LCD / OLED | Visual display | I²C (PCF8574) / SPI / parallel GPIO |

## 2.3 The Three Interfacing Paradigms

### Paradigm A — Direct GPIO Interfacing
Used when the sensor/actuator is already logic-compatible (3.3 V or 5 V CMOS).
- **Sensor side:** a digital sensor (e.g., PIR) drives the GPIO pin high/low.
- **Actuator side:** the GPIO sinks/source current to a small load (LED, small buzzer).
- **Limitation:** most MCUs source/sink only **4–20 mA** per pin — too low for motors, relays, or high-brightness LEDs.

### Paradigm B — Analog Sensor via ADC
The most heavily examined paradigm in KTU Module 2.
1. The analog sensor outputs a voltage $V_{in}$ in the range $[0, V_{ref}]$.
2. The internal **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** samples $V_{in}$ and produces an *n*-bit digital code $D$.
3. The firmware reads $D$ and converts it back to engineering units.

### Paradigm C — Actuator via PWM + Driver
1. The MCU generates a PWM waveform (variable duty cycle $D_{PWM}$, fixed frequency $f_{PWM}$).
2. The PWM signal is fed to a *driver* (transistor, MOSFET, or H-bridge) that handles the high-current/voltage load.
3. The actuator responds to the **average** voltage $\bar{V} = D_{PWM} \cdot V_{supply}$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Master the five equations below — they account for roughly **70 % of numerical marks** in KTU Module 2 questions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ADC step size (resolution) | $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1}$ | $V_{ref}$ = reference voltage, $n$ = ADC bits | Volts |
| 2 | ADC output code (rounded) | $D = \mathrm{round}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{LSB}}\right)$ | $V_{in}$ = input voltage | unitless |
| 3 | ADC output code (truncated) | $D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{LSB}} \right\rfloor$ | same as above | unitless |
| 4 | Nyquist sampling rate | $f_{s} \ge 2 \cdot f_{\max}$ | $f_{s}$ = sampling frequency, $f_{\max}$ = highest signal frequency | Hz |
| 5 | PWM average voltage | $\bar{V} = D_{PWM} \cdot V_{s}$ | $D_{PWM} = t_{on}/T$, $V_{s}$ = supply | Volts |
| 6 | PWM duty cycle (%) | $D_{\%} = \dfrac{t_{on}}{T} \times 100$ | $t_{on}$ = high-time, $T$ = period | % |
| 7 | Sensor sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta \text{measurand}}$ | change in output per unit measurand | V / unit |
| 8 | Resolution of an n-bit ADC in % | $\%FS = \dfrac{1}{2^{n}-1}\times 100$ | full-scale percentage | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strict LaTeX rule:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` for any absolute-value or divide symbol in markdown tables — never the raw pipe `|`.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Smart Agriculture:** soil-moisture sensor + relay-driven water pump forms a classic sensor-actuator feedback loop.
- **Wearable Health:** MAX30102 (PPG + SpO₂) → ESP32 → vibration motor for alert.
- **Industrial IoT:** PT100 RTD → signal conditioner → 4–20 mA current loop → ADC.
- **Smart Home:** PIR + LDR → Arduino/NodeMCU → relay module → lamp/fan.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — ADC Resolution (Least Significant Bit Voltage)

The ADC is fundamentally a *voltage-divider* of the reference voltage $V_{ref}$ into $2^{n}$ equal intervals.

**Step 1:** An ideal *n*-bit ADC has $2^{n}$ distinct output codes, ranging from $0$ to $2^{n}-1$.

**Step 2:** The total input range that the ADC can quantize is from $0$ V to $V_{ref}$.

**Step 3:** Therefore, the voltage difference between two adjacent codes is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{LSB} &= \frac{V_{ref} - 0}{(2^{n} - 1) - 0} \\[4pt]
V_{LSB} &= \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** The minus-1 in the denominator arises because there are $2^{n}-1$ *intervals* between $2^{n}$ discrete codes, not $2^{n}$ intervals. A 2-bit ADC has codes 00, 01, 10, 11 → 4 codes → 3 intervals.

**Conversion to engineering units (temperature example with LM35):**
LM35 outputs $10\,\text{mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$. If the firmware reads ADC code $D$ from a 10-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 5\,\text{V}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{in} &= D \cdot V_{LSB} = D \cdot \frac{5}{2^{10}-1} \\[4pt]
T(^{\circ}\text{C}) &= \frac{V_{in}}{0.010}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem

**Step 1:** A band-limited analog signal $x(t)$ contains no frequency components above $f_{\max}$.

**Step 2:** If we sample $x(t)$ at instants $t = k/f_{s}$ for integer $k$, the sampled signal is:

$$
x_{s}(t) = x(t) \cdot \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta\!\left(t - \frac{k}{f_{s}}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** Multiplication in the time domain corresponds to *convolution* in the frequency domain. The spectrum of $x_{s}(t)$ is a periodic replication of $X(f)$ with period $f_{s}$:

$$
X_{s}(f) = f_{s} \cdot \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_{s})
```

**Step 4:** To prevent the spectral replicas from *overlapping* (aliasing), the gap between adjacent replicas must be non-negative. Each replica extends from $-f_{\max}$ to $+f_{\max}$, so its total width is $2 f_{\max}$. Two adjacent replicas are spaced $f_{s}$ apart, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{s} &\ge 2 f_{\max} \\[4pt]
\therefore f_{s,\min} &= 2 f_{\max}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Practical engineering always chooses a slightly higher rate — typically $f_{s} = 5 f_{\max}$ to $10 f_{\max}$ — to allow for the finite roll-off of the anti-aliasing filter (a low-pass RC or active filter placed before the ADC).

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-aliasing filter cutoff rule:** place a low-pass filter with $-3\,\text{dB}$ cutoff at $f_{c} \le f_{s}/2$ *before* the ADC input. Otherwise, high-frequency noise folds back into the baseband.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — PWM Average Voltage and Power

**Step 1:** A PWM signal is a rectangular pulse train with period $T$, on-time $t_{on}$, and off-time $t_{off} = T - t_{on}$.

**Step 2:** The duty cycle is defined as:

$$
D = \frac{t_{on}}{T}
$$

**Step 3:** The average voltage delivered to a slow-responding load (e.g., DC motor, heater filament) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{V} &= \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v(t)\,dt \\[4pt]
\bar{V} &= \frac{1}{T}\left[ V_{s} \cdot t_{on} + 0 \cdot t_{off} \right] \\[4pt]
\bar{V} &= V_{s} \cdot \frac{t_{on}}{T} = V_{s} \cdot D
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Average power delivered to a resistive load $R$:

$$
P_{avg} = \frac{\bar{V}^{\,2}}{R} = \frac{(D \cdot V_{s})^{2}}{R}
$$

This is the foundational equation for **DC motor speed control** and **LED dimming** in IoT firmware.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Interfacing Decision Table

| Sensor / Actuator | Output / Input Type | MCU Pin Used | External Component Required |
|---|---|---|---|
| LM35 (analog temp) | 0–1.5 V analog | ADC pin | None (or op-amp buffer) |
| DHT11 (digital temp-hum) | Single-wire protocol | Any GPIO | 10 kΩ pull-up resistor |
| HC-SR04 (ultrasonic) | 10 µs trigger, pulse-echo | Two GPIO | Voltage divider on Echo pin (5 V → 3.3 V) |
| MPU6050 (IMU) | I²C | SDA, SCL | 4.7 kΩ pull-ups on SDA, SCL |
| MQ-2 (gas) | 0–5 V analog | ADC pin | 5 V load resistor on sensor board |
| DS18B20 (temp) | 1-Wire | Any GPIO | 4.7 kΩ pull-up on DQ |
| DC Motor | — | PWM + direction GPIO | L293D H-bridge + flyback diodes |
| Servo Motor | — | PWM (50 Hz) | Direct 3-wire connection (signal, V+, GND) |
| 5 V Relay | — | GPIO | BC547 NPN transistor + 1N4007 flyback diode |
| 16×2 LCD | Parallel / I²C | 6 GPIO or 2 GPIO (I²C) | PCF8574 backpack for I²C mode |
| Relay (12 V) | — | GPIO | N-channel MOSFET (IRF540N) + gate resistor |
| 1 W Power LED | — | GPIO | MOSFET + sense resistor |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Level Sensor-Actuator Interfacing Architecture

The Mermaid diagram below maps the full data flow from physical world to the MCU and back out, showing every functional block a KTU answer should mention.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical World Stimulus] --> B[Analog Sensor]
    C[Digital Sensor] --> B
    B --> D[Signal Conditioner]
    D --> E[Anti-Aliasing Filter]
    E --> F[ADC inside MCU]
    F --> G[Microcontroller Core]
    G --> H[Decision Firmware]
    H --> I[PWM Generator]
    H --> J[Digital GPIO]
    I --> K[Driver Stage MOSFET H-Bridge]
    J --> L[Transistor Relay]
    K --> M[Actuator DC Motor]
    L --> N[Actuator Lamp Solenoid]
    M --> O[Physical Action]
    N --> O
    G --> P[Display UART I2C]
    P --> Q[User / Cloud]
```

**How to read it:**
- *Left-to-right* flow: physical stimulus → sensor → conditioner → ADC → MCU.
- *Right branch*: MCU → PWM/driver → actuator → physical action.
- *Bottom branch*: telemetry back to the user/cloud (closes the IoT loop).

## 4.2 Detailed ADC Subsystem Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph sensor_channel [Analog Sensor Channel]
        s1[LM35 Temperature] --> s2[RC Low-Pass Filter 1 kHz]
        s2 --> s3[Op-Amp Buffer]
    end
    sensor_channel --> mux[Analog Multiplexer 8 to 1]
    mux --> sh[Sample and Hold Switch + Capacitor]
    sh --> sar[SAR n-bit ADC Core]
    sar --> ctrl[Control Logic]
    ctrl --> dr[Digital Result Register 10-bit]
    dr --> cpu[CPU Firmware]
    vref[Voltage Reference 5 V or 3.3 V] --> sar
```

**Key takeaways for KTU answers:**
- The **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** block freezes the analog voltage during conversion — without it, a high-frequency input would change value mid-conversion, causing bit errors.
- The **SAR (Successive Approximation Register)** is the most common architecture in microcontrollers like ATmega328P, STM32, and ESP32 because of its balance of speed, area, and power.

## 4.3 PWM-Driven DC Motor Interfacing Schematic (Block Form)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mcu[MCU PWM Output 3.3 V] --> drv[L293D H-Bridge Driver]
    drv --> m[DC Motor]
    m --> fb[Back-EMF]
    fb --> opt[Optional Isolator]
    opt --> mcu
    mcu2[MCU Direction GPIO] --> drv
    psu[External 12 V Supply] --> drv
    psu --> m
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why an H-bridge is mandatory for a DC motor:** a single transistor can only spin the motor in one direction. An H-bridge uses four switches arranged in an "H" around the motor; flipping which diagonal pair is ON reverses the current, hence the rotation direction.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Differentiate between a **sensor** and an **actuator**. Give one example of each used in IoT applications.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Sensor | Actuator |
|---|---|---|
| Direction of energy | Physical → Electrical | Electrical → Physical |
| Role in system | Input device | Output device |
| Example | DHT11 (temperature + humidity) | L293D-driven DC motor (fan) |
| Power | Usually passive | Requires driver for high current |

A *sensor* senses a physical quantity and converts it into an electrical signal readable by a microcontroller. An *actuator* accepts an electrical command and produces a physical effect. (1 mark each for definition + 1 mark for examples with explanation.)

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is **signal conditioning**? List any two operations performed during signal conditioning of an analog sensor signal.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Signal conditioning is the manipulation of a raw sensor output to make it suitable for the ADC input range. Operations include: (i) **amplification** (using instrumentation amplifiers for thermocouples / strain gauges), (ii) **filtering** (low-pass RC filter to remove high-frequency noise), (iii) **linearization** (for thermistor's exponential R-T curve), (iv) **isolation** (using optocouplers for high-voltage sensing), (v) **level shifting** (e.g., 5 V sensor to 3.3 V MCU). (1 mark for definition + 2 marks for any two operations explained.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional blocks involved in interfacing an **analog temperature sensor (LM35)** to an **ATmega328P** microcontroller. Discuss the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An 8-bit ADC is supplied with $V_{ref} = 5.0\,\text{V}$. The LM35 outputs $10\,\text{mV/}^{\circ}\text{C}$ and the ADC reads code $D = 102$. Compute (i) the ADC step size, (ii) the input voltage, and (iii) the corresponding temperature. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

The functional block chain is:

1. **LM35 sensor** (1 mark) — produces an analog voltage proportional to temperature: $V_{LM35} = 10\,\text{mV/}^{\circ}\text{C} \times T$.
2. **RC low-pass filter** (1 mark) — attenuates high-frequency EMI/noise; typical values $R = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C = 100\,\text{nF}$ give $f_{c} \approx 1.6\,\text{kHz}$.
3. **Optional op-amp buffer / instrumentation amplifier** (1 mark) — prevents the ADC's sample-and-hold capacitor from loading the high-impedance sensor output, especially for long cables.
4. **ATmega328P ADC channel** (1 mark) — internal MUX selects ADC0..ADC7, gated by ADMUX and ADCSRA registers.
5. **SAR ADC core** (1 mark) — performs 8-/10-bit successive approximation using internal bandgap reference or external $V_{ref}$.
6. **CPU / Firmware** (1 mark) — reads ADCH:ADCL, computes $V_{in} = D \cdot V_{LSB}$, divides by $0.010$ to get ${}^{\circ}\text{C}$.
7. **Display / Communication block** (1 mark) — outputs temperature via UART to PC or 16×2 LCD.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Number of codes (1 mark):**
An 8-bit ADC produces codes $0$ to $2^{8}-1 = 255$.

**Step 2 — Step size $V_{LSB}$ (2 marks):**

$$
V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1} = \frac{5.0}{255} = 0.019608\,\text{V} \approx 19.61\,\text{mV}
$$

**Step 3 — Input voltage from code $D = 102$ (2 marks):**

$$
V_{in} = D \cdot V_{LSB} = 102 \times 0.019608 = 2.0\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 4 — Temperature conversion (2 marks):**

$$
T = \frac{V_{in}}{0.010} = \frac{2.0}{0.010} = 200\,^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — don't lose marks here:**
> - Using $2^{n}$ instead of $2^{n}-1$ in the denominator loses 1 full mark.
> - Confusing LM35 with LM34 (LM34 outputs 10 mV/°F, not °C) loses 1 mark.
> - Not stating units (V, mV, °C) loses 0.5 mark for "presentation".

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **PWM-based speed control** of a DC motor using a microcontroller. Derive the expression for the average voltage applied to the motor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A servo motor is controlled by a 50 Hz PWM signal. The pulse width range for $0^{\circ}$ to $180^{\circ}$ rotation is $1.0\,\text{ms}$ to $2.0\,\text{ms}$. Calculate (i) the PWM period, (ii) the duty cycle range, and (iii) the pulse width required for $90^{\circ}$ rotation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks

PWM (Pulse-Width Modulation) is a technique in which the MCU generates a digital waveform of fixed frequency but variable on-time $t_{on}$. The motor's mechanical inertia makes it respond to the *average* voltage rather than the instantaneous switching. (2 marks for concept)

**Driver-stage necessity:** MCUs cannot source the 200 mA–2 A required by small DC motors directly. Hence an H-bridge driver (L293D / L298N) is used. The MCU's PWM pin is fed to the *enable* input of the H-bridge. (2 marks for driver explanation)

**Derivation of average voltage (3 marks):**

$$
\bar{V} = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v(t)\,dt = \frac{V_{s} \cdot t_{on}}{T} = D \cdot V_{s}
$$

where $D = t_{on}/T$ is the duty cycle. As $D$ varies from 0 % to 100 %, the motor speed varies from 0 to maximum.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — PWM period (1 mark):**

$$
T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{50} = 0.02\,\text{s} = 20\,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Duty cycle range (3 marks):**

For $0^{\circ}$ ($t_{on} = 1.0\,\text{ms}$):

$$
D_{0} = \frac{1.0}{20} \times 100 = 5\,\%
$$

For $180^{\circ}$ ($t_{on} = 2.0\,\text{ms}$):

$$
D_{180} = \frac{2.0}{20} \times 100 = 10\,\%
$$

**Step 3 — Pulse width for $90^{\circ}$ (3 marks):**
Linear interpolation between the two endpoints:

$$
t_{on}(90^{\circ}) = 1.0 + \frac{(2.0 - 1.0) \times 90}{180} = 1.0 + 0.5 = 1.5\,\text{ms}
$$

Corresponding duty cycle:

$$
D_{90} = \frac{1.5}{20} \times 100 = 7.5\,\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — don't lose marks here:**
> - Forgetting that servo PWM is **50 Hz** (20 ms period), *not* kHz. Many students mistakenly use 1 kHz or 50 kHz.
> - Using $2^{n}$ instead of $2^{n}-1$ in the denominator of any ADC equation.
> - Failing to mention the **driver IC** between MCU and motor. A circuit diagram showing a direct MCU-to-motor connection loses 2 marks.
> - Omitting units (ms, %, Hz) in the final answers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — go through this 10 minutes before the exam:**

- ☐ A **sensor** converts *physical → electrical*; an **actuator** converts *electrical → physical*.
- ☐ ADC resolution formula: $V_{LSB} = V_{ref}/(2^{n}-1)$ — note the **minus one**.
- ☐ Nyquist rate: $f_{s} \ge 2 f_{\max}$ — in practice, sample at $5 f_{\max}$ to $10 f_{\max}$.
- ☐ PWM average: $\bar{V} = D \cdot V_{s}$ where $D = t_{on}/T$.
- ☐ Servo motor PWM: 50 Hz, pulse width 1–2 ms for 0–180° rotation.
- ☐ L293D is a **dual H-bridge** driver — needed for bi-directional DC motor control.
- ☐ Always include a **flyback diode** across inductive loads (relay coil, motor, solenoid) to protect the driver transistor.
- ☐ For 5 V sensors feeding a 3.3 V MCU (e.g., HC-SR04 echo), use a **voltage divider** (1 kΩ + 2 kΩ) on the signal line.
- ☐ Pull-up resistors are mandatory for **open-drain** buses: 4.7 kΩ for I²C, 10 kΩ for single-wire (DHT11).
- ☐ The **signal conditioning chain** is: Sensor → Amplifier → Filter → Level-Shift → ADC.
- ☐ The **actuator chain** is: MCU → PWM/GPIO → Driver → Actuator → Flyback protection.
- ☐ Know at least three I²C sensors and three digital GPIO actuators for the KTU viva.
- ☐ Memorize the formula for percentage resolution: $\%FS = \frac{1}{2^{n}-1} \times 100$.
- ☐ A 10-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 5\,\text{V}$ has $V_{LSB} \approx 4.88\,\text{mV}$ — this is a KTU-favorite numerical.
- ☐ Remember: the **LM35** outputs 10 mV/°C; the **TMP36** outputs 10 mV/°C with a 500 mV offset (so 0 °C → 0.5 V).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Communication Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT503 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Communication Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Communication Protocols?

A **Communication Protocol** is a formally defined set of rules, data formats, signal timing specifications, and procedural conventions that govern the exchange of digital information between two or more electronic devices. In the context of **Internet of Things (IoT)**, communication protocols form the linguistic backbone that allows resource-constrained microcontrollers, sensors, actuators, and gateways to exchange data reliably, deterministically, and often in real-time.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, communication protocols are categorized across the **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) reference model**, but for embedded IoT systems, the most critical layers are:

| Layer | Concern | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical / Data Link** | Wires, voltages, bit timing, framing | UART, SPI, I2C, CAN |
| **Network / Transport** | Addressing, routing, reliability | TCP/IP, UDP, 6LoWPAN |
| **Application** | Message semantics for IoT | MQTT, CoAP, HTTP, AMQP |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Focus (Module 2):** This module emphasizes the **hardware-level serial communication protocols (UART, SPI, I2C, CAN)** and the **wireless personal/local area network protocols (Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, ZigBee, LoRa)** that microcontrollers use to talk to sensors, actuators, and the cloud.

## 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy — A Postal System for Chips

Imagine a microcontroller is a **post office**, and every sensor or peripheral is a **different house in a neighborhood**.

- **UART** is like two people shouting letters to each other across a fence. There is no clock keeping them in sync — they must both agree beforehand (via **baud rate**) how fast to speak. It is **asynchronous** and **point-to-point**.
- **SPI** is like a **headmaster with a microphone** addressing one student at a time. The headmaster (Master) controls a clock line (the bell) and uses a dedicated "chip select" line to pick which student (slave) is allowed to listen. It is **synchronous**, **full-duplex**, and **fast**.
- **I2C** is like a **shared conference call** where only one person speaks at a time, identified by their name (address). Only two wires run through the entire conference room. It is **synchronous**, **half-duplex**, and supports **multiple masters**.
- **CAN** is like a **round-table discussion** in a car where every ECU (Electronic Control Unit) listens to every other ECU, and the most urgent message wins the bus automatically. Built for **noise immunity** and **safety-critical** systems.
- **Bluetooth / Wi-Fi / ZigBee / LoRa** are the **wireless equivalents** — invisible radio waves replacing the copper wires.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Vocabulary to Anchor:**
> - **Master / Controller:** The device that initiates and controls the clock (in SPI/I2C).
> - **Slave / Peripheral / Target:** The device that responds to the master.
> - **Simplex:** One-way only.
> - **Half-Duplex:** Both ways, but not simultaneously.
> - **Full-Duplex:** Both ways simultaneously.
> - **Baud Rate (bps):** Symbols per second; for binary protocols, equals bits per second.
> - **Framing:** Start bit + Data bits + (Parity) + Stop bit structure.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 UART — Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter

### Operational Logic
1. **No shared clock.** Both ends must agree on a **baud rate** (bits per second) in advance, configured identically in firmware.
2. The line rests at logic **HIGH (idle state = 1)**.
3. Transmission begins with a **START bit (logic LOW)** — this alerts the receiver.
4. **Data bits (5, 6, 7, 8, or 9 bits)** are sent LSB-first.
5. An optional **parity bit** (Even/Odd) provides single-bit error detection.
6. Transmission ends with **1, 1.5, or 2 STOP bits** (logic HIGH), which also return the line to idle.
7. **Baud rate tolerance** must be within ±2% across the link, otherwise bit-sampling drifts and **framing errors** occur.

### UART Frame Structure (8N1 — the most common)
```
Idle (1) | Start (0) | D0 D1 D2 D3 D4 D5 D6 D7 | Stop (1)
```
**Total frame width = 1 (start) + 8 (data) + 1 (stop) = 10 bits**

### KTU Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Frame time** | $T_{frame} = \dfrac{N_{bits}}{Baud}$ | $N_{bits} = 1 + n_{data} + n_{parity} + n_{stop}$ |
| **Throughput (8N1)** | $\eta = \dfrac{8}{10} \times 100\% = 80\%$ | Only 8 of 10 bits carry payload |
| **Effective data rate** | $R_{eff} = 0.8 \times Baud$ | For 8N1, 115200 baud → 115200 × 0.8 = 92160 bps |
| **Baud rate divisor (16MHz clock)** | $Divisor = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{16 \times Baud}$ | Common values: 9600, 19200, 38400, 115200 |
| **Standard baud rates** | 1200, 2400, 4800, 9600, **19200**, **38400**, **57600**, **115200** | All multiples of crystal/oscillator base |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard baud rate** used in KTU labs: **9600 bps** or **115200 bps**. Default values in Arduino `Serial.begin()` and ESP32 boot messages.

### Real-World Utility
Used in **GPS modules (NMEA 0183)**, **HC-05/HC-06 Bluetooth modules**, **debug console output (printf-style)**, **Xbee radio modules**, and **legacy industrial RS-232/RS-485 equipment**.

## 2.2 SPI — Serial Peripheral Interface

### Operational Logic
SPI uses **4 logical wires (minimum)**:
- **SCLK (Serial Clock):** Generated by master.
- **MOSI (Master Out, Slave In):** Data from master to slave.
- **MISO (Master In, Slave Out):** Data from slave to master.
- **SS/CS (Slave Select / Chip Select):** Active-low line per slave; master pulls it LOW to select a device.

### Clock Polarity (CPOL) & Clock Phase (CPHA)
The four SPI modes arise from the combination:

| Mode | CPOL | CPHA | Clock Idle | Sampling Edge |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | 0 | 0 | LOW | Rising (leading) |
| **1** | 0 | 1 | LOW | Falling (trailing) |
| **2** | 1 | 0 | HIGH | Falling (leading) |
| **3** | 1 | 1 | HIGH | Rising (trailing) |

### KTU Formula Sheet — SPI

| Parameter | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of wires** | $N = 3 + N_{slaves}$ | 1 SCLK + 1 MOSI + 1 MISO + 1 SS per slave |
| **Throughput** | $R = f_{SCLK} \times n_{bits}$ | e.g., 10 MHz × 8 bits = 80 Mbps peak |
| **Max practical speed** | Up to **50 MHz** (short PCB traces) | Modern MCUs support 80+ MHz |
| **Daisy-chain bit length** | $n_{chain} = N_{slaves} \times n_{bits}$ | One long shift register through all devices |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pros:** Fastest of the three (UART/SPI/I2C), full-duplex, simple hardware, no addressing overhead. **Cons:** More pins (4+), no built-in acknowledgment, no hot-plug.

### Real-World Utility
**SD cards**, **TFT/OLED displays**, **Flash memory (W25Q)**, **ADC/DAC (MCP3008, MCP4725)**, **NRF24L01+ radio modules**, **gyroscopes/accelerometers (MPU6050)**.

## 2.3 I2C — Inter-Integrated Circuit

### Operational Logic
I2C uses only **2 wires**, pulled HIGH via pull-up resistors:
- **SDA (Serial Data):** Bidirectional data line.
- **SCL (Serial Clock):** Generated by master.

Both lines are **open-drain** — devices can only pull the line LOW. The pull-up resistor ($\approx 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$ for 100 kHz, $\approx 2.2 \text{ k}\Omega$ for 400 kHz) restores HIGH. This is what makes I2C **multi-master safe** — wired-AND logic ensures no bus contention.

### Addressing
Every slave has a **7-bit address** (or 10-bit extended). Common addresses: `0x27` (LCD), `0x68` (DS1307 RTC), `0x76/0x77` (BMP280).

### I2C Data Format
```
S | Slave Addr (7-bit) | R/W (1) | ACK | Data Byte | ACK | ... | P
```
**S** = Start condition (SDA falls while SCL is HIGH)  
**P** = Stop condition (SDA rises while SCL is HIGH)  
**ACK** = SDA pulled LOW by receiver on the 9th clock pulse  
**NACK** = SDA remains HIGH on 9th pulse (e.g., end of read)

### KTU Formula Sheet — I2C

| Parameter | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard speed** | 100 kHz | "Standard-mode" |
| **Fast mode** | 400 kHz | "Fast-mode" |
| **Fast mode plus** | 1 MHz | "Fm+" |
| **High speed** | 3.4 MHz | "Hs-mode" (rare, requires bus mux) |
| **Pull-up resistor (min)** | $R_{pull-up(min)} = \dfrac{V_{DD} - V_{OL}}{I_{OL}}$ | Use $V_{OL} = 0.4$ V, $I_{OL} = 3$ mA |
| **Pull-up resistor (max)** | $R_{pull-up(max)} = \dfrac{t_{r}}{0.8473 \times C_{bus}}$ | $t_r$ = rise time, $C_{bus}$ in pF |
| **Max nodes** | **127** (7-bit addr) | Practical limit ~10–20 due to capacitance |
| **Address space** | $2^7 = 128$ (7-bit), $2^{10} = 1024$ (10-bit) | Reserved addresses: 0000 0× and 1111 1× |

### Real-World Utility
**EEPROM (24Cxx)**, **RTC (DS1307/DS3231)**, **temperature sensors (LM75, BMP280)**, **GPIO expanders (PCF8574)**, **OLED displays (SSD1306)**, **audio codecs**.

## 2.4 CAN — Controller Area Network

### Operational Logic
Designed by **Bosch (1986)** for automotive ECUs.
- **Differential signalling** on CANH and CANL lines (high noise immunity).
- **NRZ (Non-Return-to-Zero) bit encoding** with **bit stuffing** (a stuff bit is inserted after 5 consecutive identical bits to maintain synchronization).
- **CSMA/CR — Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Resolution:** If two nodes transmit simultaneously, the one with the **lower arbitration ID (dominant 0 bits)** wins; the other automatically backs off. **No data is destroyed** — this is the magic of CAN.
- Frame types: **Data frame**, **Remote frame (RTR)**, **Error frame**, **Overload frame**.

### CAN Frame (Standard 11-bit ID)
```
SOF | Arbitration (11-bit ID + RTR) | Control (IDE, r0, DLC) | Data (0–8 bytes) | CRC | ACK | EOF
```

### KTU Formula Sheet — CAN

| Parameter | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Bit stuffing efficiency** | $\eta_{stuff} = \dfrac{N}{N + \lceil (N-1)/4 \rceil}$ | Worst case: 1 stuff bit per 4 data bits |
| **Bus length vs bit rate** | At 1 Mbps: $\le$ 40 m; at 125 kbps: $\le$ 500 m | |
| **Termination resistors** | **120 Ω** at each end of bus | Resistor divides differential voltage |
| **Standard speeds** | 125 kbps, 250 kbps, 500 kbps, **1 Mbps** | CAN FD up to 5 Mbps |
| **Max payload (Classic CAN)** | **8 bytes** | CAN FD: up to 64 bytes |

### Real-World Utility
**Automotive ECUs (engine, ABS, airbag)**, **industrial automation (CANopen, DeviceNet)**, **aerospace**, **elevator controllers**, **EV battery management (BMS)**.

## 2.5 Wireless Protocols Comparison

| Protocol | Standard | Frequency | Range | Data Rate | Topology | Power |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bluetooth (BLE)** | IEEE 802.15.1 | 2.4 GHz | ~10–100 m | 1–2 Mbps | Star (piconet) | Low (BLE) |
| **Wi-Fi** | IEEE 802.11 b/g/n | 2.4 / 5 GHz | ~50 m indoor | Up to 600 Mbps | Star (AP-based) | High |
| **ZigBee** | IEEE 802.15.4 | 2.4 GHz | ~10–100 m | 250 kbps | Mesh | Very low |
| **LoRa** | Semtech proprietary, LoRaWAN | 433/868/915 MHz | 2–10 km (rural) | 0.3–50 kbps | Star-of-stars | Ultra low |
| **NB-IoT** | 3GPP LTE Cat-NB1/NB2 | Cellular bands | ~10–15 km | 26 kbps DL / 62 kbps UL | Cellular | Low |
| **Z-Wave** | ITU-T G.9959 | 908/868 MHz | ~30 m | 100 kbps | Mesh | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Distinction:**
> - **Bluetooth / BLE:** Short-range, smartphone-friendly. Used in wearables, beacons, smart locks.
> - **Wi-Fi:** High throughput for IP cameras, video streaming, firmware OTA updates.
> - **ZigBee / Thread:** Mesh networking for smart home, lighting (Philips Hue), industrial sensors.
> - **LoRa / LoRaWAN:** Long-range, ultra-low-power for agriculture, smart metering, asset tracking.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 UART Timing Derivation

**Problem:** A microcontroller with a 16 MHz system clock is configured to communicate with a GPS module at **115200 bps** using **8N1** framing. The UART peripheral divides the system clock by 16 to oversample each bit. Calculate the required baud-rate divisor register value, the time to transmit a single character ('A' = ASCII 0x41), and the effective throughput.

### Step 1 — Determine the baud-rate divisor formula

The UART samples each bit 16 times to align with the bit-center. The divisor $D$ into the peripheral clock is:

$$D = \frac{f_{clk}}{16 \times Baud}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the values

Given: $f_{clk} = 16 \text{ MHz} = 16 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$, $Baud = 115200$ bps.

$$D = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{16 \times 115200}$$

$$D = \frac{10^6}{115200}$$

$$D = 8.6805...$$

Since the divisor must be an integer, we round to the nearest valid value: $D = 9$ gives an actual baud of $\frac{10^6}{9} = 111111$ bps (≈ 3.5% error — too high).

The correct value for 115200 bps on a 16 MHz clock is **D = 8.6805**, but most MCUs require an exact integer and accept a small tolerance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world resolution:** Boards use **quartz crystals that are integer multiples of 115200** — e.g., 8 MHz × 14.4 = 115.2 MHz, divided to give an exact 115200 bps. Common KTU boards (Arduino Uno) use a 16 MHz crystal and accept 115200 with a 16-bit fractional baud generator (UBRRn = 8) producing 115207 bps — within UART tolerance.

### Step 3 — Frame size for 8N1

$$N_{frame} = 1 \text{ (start)} + 8 \text{ (data)} + 0 \text{ (parity)} + 1 \text{ (stop)} = 10 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 4 — Time to transmit one character

$$T_{char} = \frac{N_{frame}}{Baud} = \frac{10}{115200}$$

$$T_{char} = 86.8056 \text{ μs}$$

### Step 5 — Effective throughput

$$\eta = \frac{n_{data}}{N_{frame}} = \frac{8}{10} = 0.8 = 80\%$$

$$R_{eff} = 0.8 \times 115200 = 92{,}160 \text{ bps} = 11.52 \text{ KB/s}$$

### Final Answer
$$\boxed{D = 8.68 \text{ (rounded to } 8 \text{ or } 9\text{)}, \quad T_{char} = 86.81 \text{ μs}, \quad R_{eff} = 92.16 \text{ kbps}}$$

## 3.2 I2C Pull-up Resistor Calculation

**Problem:** An ESP32 (3.3 V logic) communicates with a BMP280 pressure sensor over I2C at **400 kHz** (Fast-mode). The bus capacitance $C_{bus} = 100$ pF. Calculate the maximum allowable pull-up resistor value.

### Step 1 — Fast-mode rise time specification

From the I2C specification (NXP UM10204), Fast-mode requires:

$$t_{r} \leq 300 \text{ ns}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the maximum pull-up formula

$$R_{pull-up(max)} = \frac{t_{r}}{0.8473 \times C_{bus}}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute values

$$R_{pull-up(max)} = \frac{300 \times 10^{-9}}{0.8473 \times 100 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$R_{pull-up(max)} = \frac{300 \times 10^{-9}}{84.73 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$R_{pull-up(max)} = 3540 \text{ Ω} \approx 3.53 \text{ kΩ}$$

### Step 4 — Choose a standard value below the maximum

To leave margin for temperature, component tolerance, and additional capacitance from multiple slaves, we use the next lower standard E12 value:

$$\boxed{R_{pull-up} = 2.2 \text{ kΩ} \text{ (typical for Fast-mode I2C)}}$$

## 3.3 CAN Bus Arbitration — Worked Example

**Problem:** Two CAN nodes, **Node A (ID = 0x123 = 001 0010 0011)** and **Node B (ID = 0x122 = 001 0010 0010)**, transmit simultaneously on a CAN bus. Determine which one wins arbitration and explain why no data is lost.

### Step 1 — Align the IDs (11 bits, MSB first)

- Node A: `0 0 1 0 0 1 0 0 0 1 1`
- Node B: `0 0 1 0 0 1 0 0 0 1 0`

### Step 2 — Bit-by-bit comparison (Wired-AND logic: 0 is dominant)

| Bit Position | Node A | Node B | Bus (AND) | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (MSB) | 0 | 0 | 0 | Match (both recessive-bus dominant) |
| 2 | 0 | 0 | 0 | Match |
| 3 | 1 | 1 | 1 | Match (recessive) |
| 4 | 0 | 0 | 0 | Match |
| 5 | 0 | 0 | 0 | Match |
| 6 | 1 | 1 | 1 | Match |
| 7 | 0 | 0 | 0 | Match |
| 8 | 0 | 0 | 0 | Match |
| 9 | 0 | 0 | 0 | Match |
| 10 | **1** | **1** | **1** | Match |
| 11 (LSB) | **1** | **0** | **0** | **CONFLICT — Node B sends 0 (dominant)** |

### Step 3 — Resolution

Node B's bit 11 is `0` (dominant); Node A's bit 11 is `1` (recessive). The bus reads `0`. Node A, seeing its recessive `1` overwritten by a dominant `0`, knows it has lost arbitration and immediately releases the bus. **Node B continues transmitting** the rest of its frame uninterrupted.

$$\boxed{\text{Node B (ID 0x122) wins arbitration. Node A retries after the current frame finishes.}}$$

This non-destructive arbitration is the cornerstone of CAN's determinism — guaranteed worst-case message latency.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | UART | SPI | I2C | CAN |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Synchronization** | Async (no clock) | Sync (SCLK) | Sync (SCL) | Sync (bit stuffing) |
| **Wires** | 2 (TX, RX) | 4+ (SCLK, MOSI, MISO, $\overline{\text{CS}}$) | 2 (SDA, SCL) | 2 (CANH, CANL) differential |
| **Duplex** | Full-duplex | Full-duplex | Half-duplex | Half-duplex |
| **Max devices** | 2 (point-to-point) | Limited by MCU pins (CS) | 127 (theoretical) | Practically ~30 |
| **Addressing** | None | None (chip select) | 7-/10-bit | 11-/29-bit ID |
| **Speed** | Up to 1 Mbps typical | Up to 50+ Mbps | 100 kHz / 400 kHz / 1 MHz / 3.4 MHz | 125 kbps – 1 Mbps (CAN FD: 5 Mbps) |
| **Error detection** | Optional parity | None (app-level) | ACK/NACK | CRC, ACK, bit stuffing, ack slot |
| **Multi-master** | No | No | Yes (arbitration by address) | Yes (arbitration by ID) |
| **Power consumption** | Low | Moderate | Low (with low clock) | Moderate |
| **Typical use** | Console, GPS, BT modules | Display, SD, Flash | Sensors, RTC, EEPROM | Automotive, industrial |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 UART Master-Slave Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph MCU1
        A1[UART TX Pin] --> A2[Shift Register]
        A3[Clock Generator] --> A2
        A2 --> A4[Frame Formatter]
        A4 --> A5[Line Driver]
    end
    A5 -- "TX line 1" --> B5
    B5[Line Receiver] --> B4[Frame Parser]
    B4 --> B3[Shift Register]
    B3 --> B1[UART RX Pin]
    B1 --> MCU2[MCU2]
    B5 -- "RX line 2" --> A5
    classDef hw fill:#cfd8ff,stroke:#3a3a8c,color:#0a0a3a
    classDef sw fill:#ffe9b3,stroke:#a07200,color:#3a2a00
    class A2,A3,B3 hw
    class A1,B1,A4,B4 sw
```

## 4.2 SPI Master with Three Slaves — Wiring & Selection

```mermaid
graph TB
    MCLK[SCLK]:::clk --> SCLK1[SCLK1]
    MCLK --> SCLK2[SCLK2]
    MCLK --> SCLK3[SCLK3]
    MMOSI[MOSI]:::mosi --> MOSI1[MOSI1]
    MMOSI --> MOSI2[MOSI2]
    MMOSI --> MOSI3[MOSI3]
    MISO1[MISO1] --> MMISO[MISO]:::miso
    MISO2 --> MMISO
    MISO3 --> MMISO
    SS1[SS1 Active Low]:::cs --> SEL1[Slave 1]
    SS2[SS2 Active Low]:::cs --> SEL2[Slave 2]
    SS3[SS3 Active Low]:::cs --> SEL3[Slave 3]
    classDef clk fill:#fff3a8,stroke:#8a7b00,color:#2a2500
    classDef mosi fill:#c6f0c6,stroke:#1a7a1a,color:#083808
    classDef miso fill:#f0c6e0,stroke:#7a1a5a,color:#380818
    classDef cs fill:#f5b8b8,stroke:#8a0a0a,color:#2a0202
```

**KTU Insight:** Note how each slave has a **dedicated chip-select line**; only one is LOW at a time, preventing bus conflicts. MISO is **tri-stated** when the slave's CS is HIGH.

## 4.3 I2C Multi-Master Arbitration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start Condition SDA falls while SCL is HIGH]) --> B{Master Transmit Address and R/W}
    B --> C{All Slaves Compare to Own Address}
    C -->|Match Found| D[Addressed Slave ACKs by pulling SDA LOW on 9th clock]
    C -->|No Match| E[Other Slaves Wait for Next Start or STOP]
    D --> F{Read or Write}
    F -->|Read| G[Master Sends Repeated Start or STOP]
    F -->|Write| H[Master Clocks Data Byte]
    H --> I[Slave Returns ACK or NACK]
    I -->|ACK and more data| H
    I -->|ACK and last byte| J[Master sends NACK]
    I -->|NACK| K[Slave may reset or drop]
    J --> L([Stop Condition SDA rises while SCL is HIGH])
    K --> L
    E --> A
    G --> L
    classDef start fill:#a8e0a8,stroke:#1a6b1a,color:#021a02
    classDef stop fill:#f0a8a8,stroke:#8a1a1a,color:#2a0202
    classDef act fill:#b8c8f0,stroke:#1a3a8a,color:#020a2a
    class A,L start
    class B,C,D,F,H,I,J act
```

## 4.4 CAN Bus Topology

```mermaid
graph LR
    ECU1[Engine ECU]:::ecu --> BUS((CAN BUS\n120 ohm termination at both ends)):::bus
    ECU2[ABS ECU]:::ecu --> BUS
    ECU3[Airbag ECU]:::ecu --> BUS
    ECU4[Infotainment]:::ecu --> BUS
    ECU5[Body Control]:::ecu --> BUS
    BUS --- TERM1[120 ohm]:::term
    BUS --- TERM2[120 ohm]:::term
    classDef ecu fill:#d4c2f0,stroke:#3a1a8a,color:#0a0220
    classDef bus fill:#ff8c5a,stroke:#7a3a0a,color:#2a0a02
    classDef term fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#5a5a5a,color:#1a1a1a
```

**KTU Insight:** CAN is a **multi-drop bus** — all ECUs see all messages, and they filter by ID. The 120 Ω termination at both ends prevents signal reflections at high bit rates (1 Mbps needs 40 m max length with these terminations).

## 4.5 Wireless Protocol Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New IoT Application] --> B{Range Required?}
    B -->|under 10 m| C{Throughput Required?}
    B -->|10 to 100 m| D{Mesh Required?}
    B -->|over 1 km| E[LoRa or NB-IoT or Cellular LTE-M]
    C -->|under 1 Mbps| F[BLE Bluetooth Low Energy]
    C -->|over 10 Mbps| G[Wi-Fi 802.11 b/g/n]
    D -->|Yes| H[ZigBee or Thread]
    D -->|No| I[BLE or Wi-Fi or ZigBee]
    E --> J{Already Have Cellular Coverage?}
    J -->|Yes| K[NB-IoT or LTE-M]
    J -->|No| L[LoRaWAN with Gateway]
    classDef tech fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#1a5a8a,color:#021a2a
    classDef decision fill:#fff5d0,stroke:#8a7a1a,color:#2a2202
    class F,G,H,I,K,L tech
    class A,B,C,D,J decision
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]
**Differentiate between UART and SPI communication protocols in terms of clock usage, number of wires, and speed.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | UART | SPI |
|---|---|---|
| **Clock** | Asynchronous; no shared clock line; both ends must agree on baud rate | Synchronous; master generates a dedicated SCLK line |
| **Wires** | Minimum 2 (TX, RX), plus common ground | Minimum 4 (SCLK, MOSI, MISO, $\overline{\text{CS}}$) + ground |
| **Speed** | Limited, typically up to **1 Mbps** in practice | Very high, up to **10–50 MHz** in modern MCUs |
| **Devices** | Point-to-point only (1-to-1) | Multi-slave via separate chip-select lines (1-to-N) |
| **Duplex** | Full-duplex | Full-duplex |

> **Distribution:** 1 mark for clock, 1 mark for wires/duplex, 1 mark for speed/devices.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**List the two signals used in the I2C bus and explain the START and STOP conditions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The two signals of the I2C bus are:
1. **SDA (Serial Data Line)** — carries the actual data.
2. **SCL (Serial Clock Line)** — carries the clock generated by the master.

Both lines are **open-drain**, requiring external pull-up resistors to VCC.

- **START (S) condition:** Generated by the master by pulling the **SDA line from HIGH to LOW while SCL is HIGH**. It signals all slaves that a new transfer is beginning.
- **STOP (P) condition:** Generated by the master by pulling the **SDA line from LOW to HIGH while SCL is HIGH**. It signals the end of the transfer and releases the bus.

> **Distribution:** 1 mark for naming SDA/SCL, 1 mark for START, 1 mark for STOP.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark ESE Module Internal Choice Questions

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the SPI protocol with a neat block diagram. Discuss its four operating modes based on CPOL and CPHA. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a suitable timing diagram, explain the UART frame format for 8N1. Calculate the time required to transmit a 50-character string at 115200 bps. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Block Diagram (4 marks):** Refer to the SPI block diagram in SECTION 4.2.

**Key Components:**
- **Master Controller** (e.g., microcontroller).
- **Clock Generator** that produces SCLK.
- **MOSI shift register** in master + each slave.
- **MISO tri-state buffers** in each slave (active only when its $\overline{\text{CS}}$ is LOW).
- **Slave Select Decoder** that asserts exactly one $\overline{\text{CS}}$ at a time.

**Four Modes (3 marks):**

| Mode | CPOL | CPHA | Idle Clock | Sampling Edge | Shifting Edge |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | 0 | 0 | LOW | Rising (1st) | Falling |
| **1** | 0 | 1 | LOW | Falling (2nd) | Rising |
| **2** | 1 | 0 | HIGH | Falling (1st) | Rising |
| **3** | 1 | 1 | HIGH | Rising (2nd) | Falling |

Mode **0 (CPOL=0, CPHA=0)** is the **most common default** in KTU lab peripherals (SD card, MPU6050, W25Q flash).

**Operation:** Master pulls slave's $\overline{\text{CS}}$ LOW → generates SCLK → on each clock edge, one bit is shifted out on MOSI and one bit is shifted in on MISO simultaneously → 8 clocks transfer 1 byte → master raises $\overline{\text{CS}}$ HIGH to end transaction.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**UART 8N1 Frame Diagram (3 marks):**
```
Idle 1 | Start 0 | D0 D1 D2 D3 D4 D5 D6 D7 | Stop 1
```
- 1 start bit (LOW)
- 8 data bits (LSB first)
- No parity
- 1 stop bit (HIGH)
- Line returns to idle HIGH

**Calculation (4 marks):**

Frame size for 8N1:
$$N_{frame} = 1 + 8 + 1 = 10 \text{ bits per character}$$

Total bits for 50 characters:
$$N_{total} = 50 \times 10 = 500 \text{ bits}$$

Time per bit at 115200 bps:
$$T_{bit} = \frac{1}{115200} = 8.68 \text{ μs}$$

Total time:
$$T_{total} = 500 \times 8.68 \text{ μs} = 4340 \text{ μs}$$

$$\boxed{T_{total} \approx 4.34 \text{ ms}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Alternate]**

**(a)** Explain the I2C bus protocol with a block diagram. Discuss the role of open-drain configuration, pull-up resistors, and bus arbitration. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the CAN protocol. Explain the concept of non-destructive bit-wise arbitration with an example involving two nodes with IDs 0x110 and 0x108. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

**I2C Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to SECTION 4.3. Two open-drain transistors (one each for SDA and SCL) with pull-up resistors to VDD.

**Open-Drain & Pull-up (2 marks):**
- Each I2C device has an **N-channel MOSFET (or open-collector transistor)** that can only pull the line LOW.
- A pull-up resistor (typically 4.7 kΩ at 100 kHz, 2.2 kΩ at 400 kHz) restores the line to HIGH.
- This creates a **wired-AND bus** — any device pulling LOW wins, no short circuit.
- Allows **multiple masters** and **hot-plug** without damage.

**Bus Arbitration (3 marks):**
- If two masters start simultaneously, both monitor SDA while clocking.
- When one sends `1` (releases line) and reads back `0` (the other pulled it LOW), it has lost arbitration and immediately stops.
- The winning master continues uninterrupted. **No data loss, no retransmission required for the winner.**
- Arbitration is performed on each bit until only one master remains — the master with the **lowest address** wins.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**CAN Protocol Description (3 marks):**
- **Bosch CAN standard (ISO 11898)** for in-vehicle networking.
- **Differential signalling** on CANH and CANL — robust against EMI.
- **Bit stuffing:** After 5 consecutive identical bits, a stuff bit of opposite polarity is inserted (used to maintain synchronization).
- **Frame types:** Data, Remote (RTR), Error, Overload.
- **11-bit standard ID** (CAN 2.0A) or **29-bit extended ID** (CAN 2.0B).
- **CRC-15** for error detection, plus ACK bit, plus error counters (TEC/REC).

**Non-Destructive Arbitration Example (4 marks):**

| Bit Pos (MSB→LSB) | Node 0x110 | Node 0x108 | Bus (Wired-AND) |
|---|---|---|---|
| | `001 0001 0000` | `001 0000 1000` | |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 ✓ Match |
| 2 | 0 | 0 | 0 ✓ Match |
| 3 | 1 | 1 | 1 ✓ Match |
| 4 | 0 | 0 | 0 ✓ Match |
| 5 | 0 | 0 | 0 ✓ Match |
| 6 | 0 | 0 | 0 ✓ Match |
| 7 | 0 | 0 | 0 ✓ Match |
| 8 | **1** | **0** | 0 — **Node 0x110 loses** |

When both nodes send bit 8, Node 0x110 sends recessive `1` but reads back dominant `0`. It immediately aborts transmission. **Node 0x108 wins** because its ID has a `0` (dominant) in an earlier position. Node 0x110 will retry after the bus becomes idle.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Valuation Pitfalls (KTU Examiner Warnings):**
> 1. **Confusing "dominant" and "recessive"** — always remember 0 is dominant in CAN (it's the active state of the bus). Losing students say "0 = recessive" or "higher ID wins" — both are **wrong**.
> 2. **Wrong frame format** — UART must be drawn with **idle HIGH**, **start LOW**, **LSB first** in data. Drawing MSB first = 1 mark deduction.
> 3. **I2C open-drain confusion** — writing "I2C uses push-pull drivers" loses 1 mark immediately. The pull-up resistor must be **explicitly shown** in the diagram.
> 4. **SPI mode mnemonic missing** — students often write the four modes but forget to say which is most common. Always state **Mode 0 is the default for >90% of peripherals**.
> 5. **Forgetting ground in SPI wire count** — saying "SPI uses 4 wires" loses 0 marks, but saying "UART uses 2 wires" without including ground loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **UART** = asynchronous, 2 wires (TX, RX), 8N1 most common, **9600 / 115200 bps** standard, used for console and Bluetooth SPP.
- **SPI** = synchronous, 4 wires minimum, **Mode 0 (CPOL=0, CPHA=0)** most common, fastest of the three, dedicated chip-select per slave.
- **I2C** = synchronous, **2 wires (SDA, SCL)** only, **open-drain** with pull-ups, **7-bit addressing**, **100 kHz / 400 kHz / 1 MHz** modes.
- **CAN** = differential bus (**CANH, CANL**), **120 Ω termination** at both ends, **non-destructive arbitration** (lower ID wins), built for **safety-critical** automotive/industrial use.
- **Bluetooth/BLE** = short-range, smartphone-friendly, beacon, wearables.
- **Wi-Fi** = high bandwidth, IP-based, requires access point, higher power.
- **ZigBee / Thread** = **mesh networking**, low power, smart home/industrial sensors.
- **LoRa / LoRaWAN** = **kilometre range**, ultra-low power, sub-kHz data rate, agriculture/metering.
- **Golden formulas:**
  - UART frame time: $T_{frame} = \frac{N_{bits}}{Baud}$
  - UART effective rate: $R_{eff} = 0.8 \times Baud$ (for 8N1)
  - I2C pull-up max: $R_{max} = \frac{t_r}{0.8473 \times C_{bus}}$
  - CAN speed-distance tradeoff: 1 Mbps ≤ 40 m; 125 kbps ≤ 500 m
- **Memory trick:** "**S**PI is **S**imple and **S**peedy; I²C is **I**ntelligent with **a**ddresses; **C**AN is for **C**ars and **C**riticality."
- **Exam mantra:** Always include a **block diagram with labelled pins/wires** in 7-mark questions — diagrams are mandatory and carry 2–3 marks in KTU valuations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hardware Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCGT503 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS FOR IOT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Hardware Components # Module 3: Hardware Components for IoT Systems ## 1.
