Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hardware Components in IoT Context** — Refers to the physical electronic building blocks that enable an IoT device to **sense**, **process**, **communicate**, and **actuate**. In the KTU 2024 PCCGT503 framework, these components are classified into four functional tiers: **Sensing Subsystems**, **Processing Cores**, **Communication Interfaces**, and **Power Management & Actuator Units**. In an IoT node, hardware components form the **physical instantiation** of the perception–computation–communication–action chain. The KTU syllabus specifically emphasizes the **intersection between microcontrollers and external peripherals** — meaning every hardware component discussed here ultimately interfaces with a processor via standardized protocols (GPIO, I²C, SPI, UART, ADC channels). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body Model Think of an IoT device as a **miniature digital organism**: | IoT Component | Human Body Equivalent | Function | |---------------|----------------------|----------| | Sensors | Eyes, Ears, Skin | Sense the environment | | Microcontroller | Brain | Process & decide | | Communication Module | Mouth, Nerves | Talk to others | | Actuators | Hands, Legs | Affect the world | | Power Supply | Heart & Lungs | Provide energy | | Memory | Long-term memory banks | Store data | > [!NOTE] > Just as a human cannot function without all organs working in harmony, an IoT node requires *every* hardware tier to be operational. A brilliant processor is useless without sensors (deaf & blind), and sensors are useless without communication (mute). ### 1.3 The Four-Tier Hardware Architecture The KTU 2024 syllabus structures IoT hardware into a **hierarchical four-tier model**: **Tier 1 — Sensing Subsystem (Input Layer)** - Environmental sensors (temperature, humidity, pressure) - Motion & position sensors (accelerometer, gyroscope, GPS) - Chemical & biological sensors (gas, pH, biosensors) - Image & audio sensors (CMOS cameras, MEMS microphones) **Tier 2 — Processing Core (Brain Layer)** - Microcontrollers (Arduino, ESP32, STM32, MSP430) - Microprocessors (Raspberry Pi class — ARM Cortex-A) - System on Chip (SoC) integration - Coprocessors (AI accelerators, crypto engines) **Tier 3 — Communication Interfaces (Network Layer)** - Short-range: Bluetooth/BLE, Zigbee, NFC, RFID - Medium-range: Wi-Fi (802.11 b/g/n/ac) - Long-range: LoRa, Sigfox, NB-IoT, LTE-M - Wired: Ethernet, CAN, RS-485 **Tier 4 — Power & Actuation (Energy/Output Layer)** - Battery technologies (Li-ion, Li-Po, NiMH) - Energy harvesting (solar, piezoelectric, thermal) - Voltage regulators (LDO, DC-DC buck/boost) - Actuators (motors, relays, solenoids, LEDs) ### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Examinations > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Standard Metrics You Must Memorize for KTU Exams:** > - **Supply Voltage:** Typically **3.3 V** or **5 V** for IoT nodes > - **Clock Frequency:** Ranges from **8 MHz** (low-power) to **240 MHz** (ESP32) > - **ADC Resolution:** **10-bit to 12-bit** standard (1024 to 4096 discrete levels) > - **Current Consumption:** Sleep currents often **< 10 μA**, active **40–240 mA** > - **Wireless Range:** BLE ~10 m, Wi-Fi ~50 m, LoRa ~10 km > - **Data Rates:** BLE 1 Mbps, Wi-Fi 54+ Mbps, LoRa 0.3–50 kbps # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Sensing Subsystem — Operational Theory A **sensor** is a transducer that converts a physical phenomenon (temperature, light, pressure) into an electrical signal (voltage, current, resistance, or capacitance) that a microcontroller can interpret. ### 2.1.1 Sensor Classification by Output Type - **Analog Sensors:** Output a continuous voltage proportional to the measured quantity (e.g., TMP36 temperature sensor, LM35). - **Digital Sensors:** Output discrete binary or protocol-based data (e.g., DHT22 over single-wire protocol). - **Smart Sensors:** Include on-chip signal conditioning, ADC, calibration, and digital interface (e.g., BME280 over I²C/SPI). ### 2.1.2 Operational Steps of an Analog Sensor Interface 1. **Physical Phenomenon Detection** — The sensor element (thermistor, photodiode, strain gauge) experiences the physical variable. 2. **Signal Conversion** — Physical change is converted to a measurable electrical change (resistance, voltage). 3. **Signal Conditioning** — Amplification (e.g., instrumentation amplifier), filtering (RC low-pass), and linearization. 4. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)** — The microcontroller's internal ADC quantizes the analog voltage into binary codes. 5. **Digital Interpretation** — Software converts the binary code to engineering units (°C, lux, Pa). > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Questions on "interfacing an LM35 with ATmega328" or "explain ADC operation in ESP32" require you to mention the full chain above. Simply writing "sensor gives temperature" loses marks. ### 2.1.3 Critical Sensor Specifications (Board Exam Favorites) - **Accuracy:** Deviation from true value (e.g., ±0.5°C) - **Precision:** Repeatability of measurement - **Resolution:** Smallest detectable change - **Sensitivity:** Output change per unit input change - **Dynamic Range:** Ratio between largest and smallest measurable input - **Response Time:** Time to reach 90% of final output ## 2.2 Processing Core — Theory of Operation ### 2.2.1 Microcontroller vs. Microprocessor in IoT | Parameter | Microcontroller (MCU) | Microprocessor (MPU) | |-----------|----------------------|---------------------| | Integration | CPU + RAM + ROM + I/O on one chip | CPU only — external peripherals needed | | Power | **Low (μW to mW range)** | Higher (W range) | | Cost | Low ($1–$10) | Higher ($35+) | | OS | Bare-metal / RTOS | Full Linux/Windows | | IoT Use Case | ESP32, Arduino, STM32 | Raspberry Pi, BeagleBone | | Real-time | Excellent (deterministic) | Not deterministic | ### 2.2.2 Core Components Inside a Microcontroller A modern IoT MCU contains: - **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** — Executes instructions - **Flash Memory (ROM)** — Stores program code (typically **32 KB to 4 MB**) - **SRAM** — Volatile runtime data (typically **4 KB to 520 KB**) - **EEPROM / FRAM** — Non-volatile parameter storage - **GPIO Pins** — General-purpose digital I/O - **Timers/Counters** — For PWM, event timing - **Communication Peripherals** — UART, SPI, I²C, I²S, CAN - **ADC/DAC** — Analog interfaces - **Watchdog Timer (WDT)** — System reset on hang ### 2.2.3 The "Why" Behind Choosing an MCU for IoT The choice depends on the **power budget**, **computation requirement**, **communication needs**, and **cost ceiling**. For battery-powered sensors, MSP430 or ATmega328L (8 MHz @ 3.3 V) is preferred. For Wi-Fi/BLE nodes, ESP32 dominates due to integrated radio. For edge AI, STM32 with Cortex-M4F or ESP32-S3 with vector instructions is selected. ## 2.3 Communication Interfaces — Protocol Theory ### 2.3.1 Short-Range Wireless **Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE)** - Operates in the **2.4 GHz ISM band** - Uses **40 channels** (3 advertising + 37 data) - Modulation: **GFSK** with 1 Mbps data rate - Range: **~10 m** in open air - Power: **0.01–0.5 W** - Use case: Wearables, smart home sensors, beacons **Zigbee (IEEE 802.15.4)** - Also **2.4 GHz** (868 MHz in Europe, 915 MHz in USA) - Mesh networking support — up to **65,000 nodes** - Data rate: **250 kbps** - Range: **10–100 m** - Use case: Industrial automation, smart lighting ### 2.3.2 Medium-Range Wireless — Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11) - Bands: **2.4 GHz** and **5 GHz** - Standards: 802.11 b/g/n/ac/ax - Data rate: **54 Mbps (g) to 1.2 Gbps (ax)** - Range: **~50 m indoors** - Power: **High (0.5–2 W active)** - Use case: Video streaming IoT, home gateways ### 2.3.3 Long-Range Low-Power — LoRaWAN - Sub-GHz band (**433/868/915 MHz** depending on region) - **Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS) modulation** - Data rate: **0.3 to 50 kbps** - Range: **2–10 km (rural)**, 1–3 km (urban) - Battery life: **up to 10 years** on 2× AA - Use case: Smart agriculture, asset tracking, smart city ### 2.3.4 Wired Industrial Protocols - **CAN Bus:** Robust differential signaling, used in automotive - **RS-485:** Multi-drop, up to 32 nodes, 1200 m - **I²C:** 2-wire (SDA, SCL), 7-bit addressing, up to 127 devices - **SPI:** 4-wire (MOSI, MISO, SCK, CS), high speed, full duplex ## 2.4 Power Management & Actuation ### 2.4.1 Battery Technologies for IoT | Battery Type | Nominal Voltage | Energy Density | Rechargeable | IoT Suitability | |-------------|----------------|----------------|--------------|-----------------| | Alkaline AA | 1.5 V | 100 Wh/kg | No | Low-power sensors | | Li-ion | 3.7 V | 150–250 Wh/kg | Yes | Smartphones, gateways | | Li-Po | 3.7 V | 130–200 Wh/kg | Yes | Wearables | | Li-SOCl₂ | 3.6 V | 700 Wh/kg | No | Remote metering | | NiMH | 1.2 V | 60–120 Wh/kg | Yes | Toys, low-cost | ### 2.4.2 Voltage Regulation - **LDO (Low Dropout Regulator):** Simple, low noise, but inefficient (excess voltage → heat) - **Buck Converter:** Step-down DC-DC, efficiency **>90%** - **Boost Converter:** Step-up DC-DC for low-voltage sources - **Buck-Boost:** Either step-up or step-down ### 2.4.3 Actuators - **DC Motors / Servos:** Rotary motion control - **Stepper Motors:** Precise angular positioning - **Relays:** Electromagnetic switches for high-power loads - **Solenoids:** Linear motion - **Piezo Buzzers / Speakers:** Audio output - **LEDs / LCD / OLED:** Visual feedback ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **THE KTU BOARD EXAM FORMULA SHEET — HARDWARE COMPONENTS** | Concept | Formula / Equation | Units | Application | |---------|-------------------|-------|-------------| | ADC Conversion | $$D = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^n - 1)$$ | Dimensionless | Digital output code | | ADC Voltage Recovery | $$V_{in} = \frac{D \times V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$$ | Volts (V) | Reconstruct voltage | | Resolution (LSB size) | $$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$ | Volts (V) | Smallest detectable step | | Sensor Sensitivity | $$S = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta x_{input}}$$ | V/unit | Calibration | | Power Dissipation | $$P = V \times I$$ | Watts (W) | Power budget | | Battery Life (hours) | $$t = \frac{C_{battery}}{I_{load}}$$ | Hours | Energy planning | | Energy Stored | $$E = V \times C$$ | Watt-hours (Wh) | Battery capacity | | Ohm's Law | $$V = I \times R$$ | V, A, Ω | Circuit analysis | | Resistor Divider | $$V_{out} = V_{in} \times \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$ | V | Sensor scaling | | RC Time Constant | $$\tau = R \times C$$ | Seconds (s) | Filter design | | PWM Duty Cycle | $$D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{period}} \times 100\%$$ | Percent | Motor speed, LED dimming | | Link Budget (dB) | $$L = P_{tx} - P_{rx_{min}}$$ | dBm | Wireless range | | Free-Space Path Loss | $$FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$$ | dB | (d in km, f in MHz) | | SNR | $$SNR = 10\log_{10}\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}$$ | dB | Communication quality | | Ohm's Law Power | $$P = I^2 R = \frac{V^2}{R}$$ | W | Power loss | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Reminder for KTU 2024 Examiners:** When asked to "calculate ADC output for X volts," show the full substitution. Marks are awarded for each mathematical transition, not just the final answer. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: ADC Digital Code from Analog Input This is a **board-exam favorite derivation**. We derive the formula that converts an analog voltage to the corresponding digital code. ### Problem Statement Given an ADC with reference voltage $V_{ref} = 5\text{ V}$ and resolution $n = 10$ bits, derive the general formula and compute the digital output when the analog input voltage is $V_{in} = 2.45\text{ V}$. ### Step 1 — Define the Quantization Concept An ADC divides the reference voltage range into $2^n$ equal discrete levels, called **Least Significant Bits (LSB)**. Each LSB corresponds to: $$ \Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} $$ **Explanation:** The reference voltage $V_{ref}$ is the maximum representable input. An $n$-bit ADC can resolve $2^n$ distinct codes (from $0$ to $2^n - 1$). The resolution per step is the total range divided by the total number of steps. ### Step 2 — Derive the LSB Step Size Substituting $V_{ref} = 5\text{ V}$ and $n = 10$: $$ \Delta V = \frac{5}{2^{10}} = \frac{5}{1024} $$ $$ \Delta V = 0.0048828125 \text{ V} \approx 4.88 \text{ mV} $$ **Explanation:** This means our ADC can detect a voltage change as small as **4.88 mV**. Any input change smaller than this is lost in quantization noise. ### Step 3 — Express the Digital Output Formula The digital code $D$ corresponding to an input $V_{in}$ is the ratio of $V_{in}$ to $\Delta V$, rounded to the nearest integer: $$ D = \text{round}\left(\frac{V_{in}}{\Delta V}\right) = \text{round}\left(\frac{V_{in} \times 2^n}{V_{ref}}\right) $$ **Explanation:** Since each LSB represents $\Delta V$ volts, the number of LSBs that "fit" into $V_{in}$ is the integer count $D$. The full expression is: $$ D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^n - 1) + 0.5 \right\rfloor $$ where $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ is the floor function and the $+0.5$ performs rounding. ### Step 4 — Substitute and Compute Substituting $V_{in} = 2.45\text{ V}$, $V_{ref} = 5\text{ V}$, $n = 10$: $$ D = \text{round}\left(\frac{2.45 \times 1024}{5}\right) $$ $$ D = \text{round}\left(\frac{2508.8}{5}\right) $$ $$ D = \text{round}(501.76) = 502 $$ **Explanation:** The ADC outputs the binary equivalent of 502. Converting to binary: $502_{10} = 111110110_2$. A 10-bit result is often left-justified in 16-bit registers: $502 \times 64 = 32128$ (for 10-bit ADC aligned to 16-bit, multiplication by $2^6$). ### Step 5 — Verification (Reverse Calculation) To verify, reconstruct the analog voltage from the digital code: $$ V_{in\_reconstructed} = D \times \Delta V = 502 \times 0.0048828125 $$ $$ V_{in\_reconstructed} = 2.451171875 \text{ V} $$ The quantization error is: $$ e_q = \vert 2.45 - 2.451171875 \vert = 0.001171875 \text{ V} \approx 1.17 \text{ mV} $$ **Explanation:** The error is always less than $\pm 0.5 \times \Delta V = \pm 2.44\text{ mV}$. This is the **quantization noise floor** — an unavoidable consequence of digital sampling. ### Final Result $$ \boxed{D = 502, \quad V_{in} = 2.45 \text{ V}, \quad \Delta V = 4.88 \text{ mV}, \quad e_q = 1.17 \text{ mV}} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Battery Lifetime for an IoT Node ### Problem Statement An IoT sensor node is powered by a **3.7 V, 2400 mAh Li-ion battery**. The node operates in two modes: - **Active Mode:** Current = 80 mA, Duration = 5 seconds per measurement - **Sleep Mode:** Current = 8 μA, Duration = rest of the cycle - **Measurement cycle:** every 60 seconds Calculate the average current consumption and total battery life in days. ### Step 1 — Calculate Active Mode Charge per Cycle The charge consumed in active mode per cycle: $$ Q_{active} = I_{active} \times t_{active} = 80 \text{ mA} \times 5 \text{ s} $$ Converting seconds to hours: $$ t_{active} = \frac{5}{3600} \text{ h} = 0.0013889 \text{ h} $$ $$ Q_{active} = 80 \times 0.0013889 = 0.11111 \text{ mAh per cycle} $$ **Explanation:** In 5 seconds, the node draws 0.111 mAh from the battery. ### Step 2 — Calculate Sleep Mode Charge per Cycle Sleep duration per cycle: $$ t_{sleep} = 60 - 5 = 55 \text{ s} = \frac{55}{3600} \text{ h} = 0.015278 \text{ h} $$ Sleep current: $I_{sleep} = 8\ \mu A = 0.008\ mA$ $$ Q_{sleep} = 0.008 \times 0.015278 = 0.00012222 \text{ mAh per cycle} $$ **Explanation:** Sleep mode contributes a tiny but non-zero charge draw — this is why ultra-low sleep currents are critical in IoT design. ### Step 3 — Total Charge per Cycle $$ Q_{cycle} = Q_{active} + Q_{sleep} = 0.11111 + 0.00012222 = 0.111233 \text{ mAh} $$ **Explanation:** Sleep is negligible here (0.1% of active), confirming that the dominant power drain is the active phase. ### Step 4 — Calculate Average Current $$ I_{avg} = \frac{Q_{cycle}}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{0.111233 \text{ mAh}}{\frac{60}{3600} \text{ h}} = \frac{0.111233}{0.01667} $$ $$ I_{avg} = 6.674 \text{ mA} $$ **Explanation:** The time-averaged current (6.67 mA) is much lower than the peak active current (80 mA). This is the key benefit of duty-cycled IoT operation. ### Step 5 — Calculate Battery Lifetime $$ t_{life} = \frac{C_{battery}}{I_{avg}} = \frac{2400 \text{ mAh}}{6.674 \text{ mA}} = 359.6 \text{ hours} $$ Converting to days: $$ t_{life} = \frac{359.6}{24} = 14.98 \text{ days} \approx 15 \text{ days} $$ ### Final Result $$ \boxed{t_{life} \approx 15 \text{ days on a 2400 mAh battery with 5 s/60 s duty cycle}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Real-world correction:** In practice, you must also subtract 20–30% for battery self-discharge, DC-DC converter inefficiency, and end-of-life cutoff. So practical lifetime ≈ **10–12 days**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Sensor Scaling Using Resistor Divider ### Problem Statement A photoresistor (LDR) has a resistance range of **1 kΩ (bright) to 1 MΩ (dark)**. It is connected in a voltage divider with $R_1 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ fixed resistor and $V_{in} = 3.3\text{ V}$. Derive the output voltage range. ### Step 1 — Voltage Divider Formula $$ V_{out} = V_{in} \times \frac{R_{LDR}}{R_1 + R_{LDR}} $$ ### Step 2 — Compute for Bright Condition When $R_{LDR} = 1\text{ k}\Omega$: $$ V_{out\_bright} = 3.3 \times \frac{1000}{1000 + 10000} = 3.3 \times \frac{1}{11} = 0.30 \text{ V} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute for Dark Condition When $R_{LDR} = 1\text{ M}\Omega = 1{,}000{,}000\ \Omega$: $$ V_{out\_dark} = 3.3 \times \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{10{,}000 + 1{,}000{,}000} = 3.3 \times \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{1{,}010{,}000} $$ $$ V_{out\_dark} = 3.3 \times 0.9901 = 3.267 \text{ V} $$ ### Step 4 — Output Range Summary $$ \boxed{V_{out} \in [0.30 \text{ V (bright)}, 3.267 \text{ V (dark)}]} $$ **Explanation:** The LDR's output is **non-linear** (logarithmic with light intensity), so software linearization is required for accurate lux measurement. # 4. 4.2 Sensor Interfacing Schematic (Analog + Digital) This diagram shows the standard interfacing pattern for both analog sensors (LDR via voltage divider) and digital sensors (DHT22 via single-wire protocol) to a microcontroller. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph AnalogSensorCluster[Analog Sensor Cluster] A1[LDR Light Dependent Resistor] --> A2[Fixed Resistor 10kOhm] A2 --> A3[Voltage Divider Output to ADC Pin] end subgraph DigitalSensorCluster[Digital Sensor Cluster] B1[DHT22 Temperature Humidity] --> B2[Single Wire Data Line] B2 --> B3[Pullup Resistor 10kOhm to 3.3V] B3 --> B4[GPIO Pin on MCU] end subgraph I2CSensorCluster[I2C Sensor Cluster] C1[BME280 Pressure Humidity Temp] --> C2[SDA Line] C3[BH1750 Light Sensor] --> C2 C2 --> C4[SCL Line] C4 --> C5[Pullup Resistors 4.7kOhm to 3.3V] C5 --> C6[I2C Bus on MCU] end A3 --> MCU1[Microcontroller ADC Pin] B4 --> MCU1 C6 --> MCU1 MCU1 --> PROC[Processing and Decision Logic] PROC --> OUTPUT[Output to Communication or Display] classDef ana fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#B8860B classDef dig fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B classDef i2c fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#CD5C5C classDef cpu fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4 class A1,A2,A3,AnalogSensorCluster ana class B1,B2,B3,B4,DigitalSensorCluster dig class C1,C2,C3,C4,C5,C6,I2CSensorCluster i2c class MCU1,PROC,OUTPUT cpu ``` ### Why This Diagram Matters for KTU Exams > [!NOTE] > When asked "explain how you would interface multiple sensors to an ESP32," **this exact layered architecture** is what examiners expect. Showing analog + digital + I²C clustering with proper pull-up resistors and bus sharing demonstrates complete understanding. --- ## 4.3 Power Management Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD SRC1[Li-ion Battery 3.7V 2400mAh] --> PMIC[Power Management IC] SRC2[USB 5V Input] --> CHRG[Charging Circuit TP4056] CHRG --> SRC1 SRC3[Solar Panel 6V] --> CHRG PMIC --> LDO1[LDO 3.3V Output to MCU] PMIC --> LDO2[LDO 1.8V Output to Radio] PMIC --> BOOST[Boost Converter to 5V for Sensors] LDO1 --> MCUP[Microcontroller Core] LDO2 --> RADP[Radio Module Power] BOOST --> SENSP[Sensor Power Rail] MCUP --> PWROK[Power Good Signal] PWROK --> GATE[P-Channel MOSFET Load Switch] GATE --> OPT[Optional Peripheral Power] classDef batt fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#8B4513 classDef conv fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082 classDef load fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400 class SRC1,SRC2,SRC3,CHRG batt class PMIC,LDO1,LDO2,BOOST conv class MCUP,RADP,SENSP,PWROK,GATE,OPT load ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Architectural Principle:** The MCU's main 3.3 V rail is **always on** to retain sleep mode and RTC. The radio and sensor rails are **gated** through MOSFETs to enable complete power-down via software load switching — this is how IoT nodes achieve sub-10 μA sleep currents. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question] **Q: List any six commonly used hardware components in an IoT system and state the function of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks – expect 6 points × 0.5 each, or 3 categories × 1 mark each):** The six commonly used hardware components in an IoT system are: 1. **Sensors** (e.g., DHT22) — Sense physical parameters like temperature and humidity and convert them to electrical signals. 2. **Microcontroller** (e.g., ESP32) — Acts as the processing brain, executes the application logic, and controls peripherals. 3. **Communication Module** (e.g., Wi-Fi/BLE radio) — Enables wireless data transmission to gateways, cloud servers, or mobile devices. 4. **Actuators** (e.g., Relay, DC motor) — Perform physical actions in response to processed sensor data (e.g., switch on a fan). 5. **Power Supply** (e.g., Li-ion battery with LDO regulator) — Provides regulated DC voltage to all subsystems. 6. **Memory** (e.g., SPI Flash, EEPROM) — Stores program code, logged data, and configuration parameters persistently. --- ### Question A2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question] **Q: Differentiate between analog and digital sensors with two examples each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Analog Sensor | Digital Sensor | |-----------|---------------|----------------| | Output Type | Continuous voltage or current | Discrete binary or protocol data | | ADC Required | Yes — must be digitized by MCU ADC | No — direct digital read | | Examples | LM35 (temperature), LDR (light), MQ-2 (gas) | DHT22 (temp/humidity), DS18B20 (temperature) | | Interfacing Complexity | Simpler hardware, complex calibration | More complex protocol, simpler software | | Noise Susceptibility | High (analog noise) | Low (digital thresholding) | > [!NOTE] > **Note on marking:** Award 1 mark for clear definition of each, 0.5 mark per correct example, 0.5 mark for the interfacing/noise comparison. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 ESE Part B Pattern: Two sub-parts, each 7 marks. Internal choice between Question A and Question B is provided. ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Pattern] **Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the major hardware components of an IoT system and their functions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An IoT node uses a 12-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 3.3\text{ V}$. Compute (i) the LSB step size, (ii) the digital output code for an analog input of $1.75\text{ V}$, and (iii) the quantization error. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question A (a) — 7 Marks **Introduction (1 mark):** An IoT system comprises four major hardware tiers working in concert: **Sensing**, **Processing**, **Communication**, and **Power/Actuation**. **Sensing Subsystem (1.5 marks):** - **Sensors** like DHT22 (temperature/humidity), MQ-135 (gas), PIR (motion), LDR (light) detect physical/environmental variables. - Analog sensors output continuous voltages; digital sensors output protocol data (I²C, SPI, single-wire). - **Signal conditioning** (amplification, filtering) is often needed before ADC. **Processing Core (1.5 marks):** - **Microcontroller** (ESP32, STM32, Arduino) is the central processing unit, executing application firmware. - Contains CPU, RAM, ROM (Flash), GPIO, ADC, timers, and communication peripherals. - For complex edge processing, **microprocessors** (Raspberry Pi class) with Linux are used. **Communication Interface (1.5 marks):** - Short-range: **BLE, Zigbee, NFC** (≤100 m, low power) - Medium-range: **Wi-Fi** (≤50 m, high bandwidth) - Long-range: **LoRa, NB-IoT, Sigfox** (≥2 km, very low power) - Wired: **Ethernet, CAN, RS-485** for industrial IoT **Power and Actuation (1 mark):** - **Power supply** via batteries (Li-ion, Li-Po) or energy harvesters (solar, piezo), regulated to 3.3 V or 5 V. - **Actuators** like relays, motors, solenoids perform physical actions based on processed decisions. **Block Diagram (0.5 mark):** Draw the four-tier layered architecture showing MCU at the center, sensors feeding in, communication/actuation branching out, and power feeding all layers. --- ### Model Solution for Question A (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** $V_{ref} = 3.3\text{ V}$, $n = 12\text{ bits}$, $V_{in} = 1.75\text{ V}$ **(i) LSB Step Size (2 marks):** $$ \Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{3.3}{2^{12}} = \frac{3.3}{4096} $$ $$ \Delta V = 0.00080566 \text{ V} \approx 0.806 \text{ mV} $$ **(ii) Digital Output Code (3 marks):** $$ D = \text{round}\left(\frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times 2^n\right) = \text{round}\left(\frac{1.75}{3.3} \times 4096\right) $$ $$ D = \text{round}(0.53030 \times 4096) = \text{round}(2172.12) = 2172 $$ Converting to binary for completeness: $2172_{10} = 100001111100_2$ **(iii) Quantization Error (2 marks):** $$ V_{reconstructed} = D \times \Delta V = 2172 \times 0.00080566 = 1.74989 \text{ V} $$ $$ e_q = \vert V_{in} - V_{reconstructed} \vert = \vert 1.75 - 1.74989 \vert = 0.000107 \text{ V} $$ $$ e_q = 0.107 \text{ mV} $$ **Final Answer:** $\Delta V = 0.806 \text{ mV}$, $D = 2172$, $e_q \approx 0.107 \text{ mV}$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall (1–2 marks lost):** Students often forget to *round* the digital code and write $2172.12$ — this is wrong because ADC output is always an integer. Also, students forget to convert $V_{in}/V_{ref}$ properly before multiplying by $2^n$. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative — KTU 2024 Pattern] **Q: (a)** Explain the four-tier hardware architecture of an IoT system. Compare **microcontroller** vs. **microprocessor** for IoT applications with five points. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A wireless sensor node is powered by a **3.7 V, 1500 mAh Li-ion battery**. It operates at **40 mA active for 3 seconds** every **30 seconds**, with **5 μA sleep current** during the rest period. Calculate the average current and total battery life in days, considering a 20% derating factor. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question B (a) — 7 Marks **Four-Tier Architecture (3 marks):** Already covered in Question A(a). Summarize: Sensing → Processing → Communication → Power/Actuation, with the microcontroller as the central hub. **MCU vs MPU Comparison Table (4 marks):** | Parameter | Microcontroller (MCU) | Microprocessor (MPU) | |-----------|----------------------|---------------------| | Integration | CPU + RAM + ROM + I/O on-chip | CPU only — needs external chips | | Power | Low (μW to mW) | High (Watts) | | Cost | $1–$10 | $35 and above | | Real-Time | Deterministic, hard real-time | Not deterministic, soft real-time | | IoT Use Case | ESP32, Arduino, STM32 | Raspberry Pi, BeagleBone | | OS Support | Bare-metal, RTOS (FreeRTOS) | Full Linux (Raspbian, Ubuntu) | | ADC | On-chip | External ADC required | > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Note:** Award 0.8 marks per well-explained point (5 × 0.8 = 4 marks). --- ### Model Solution for Question B (b) — 7 Marks **Given:** $V = 3.7\text{ V}$, $C = 1500\text{ mAh}$, $I_{active} = 40\text{ mA}$, $t_{active} = 3\text{ s}$, $T = 30\text{ s}$, $I_{sleep} = 5\ \mu A$ **Step 1: Active charge per cycle (1.5 marks):** $$ Q_{active} = 40 \text{ mA} \times \frac{3}{3600} \text{ h} = 0.03333 \text{ mAh} $$ **Step 2: Sleep charge per cycle (1.5 marks):** $$ t_{sleep} = 30 - 3 = 27 \text{ s} = 0.0075 \text{ h} $$ $$ Q_{sleep} = 0.005 \text{ mA} \times 0.0075 \text{ h} = 0.0000375 \text{ mAh} $$ **Step 3: Total charge per cycle and average current (2 marks):** $$ Q_{cycle} = 0.03333 + 0.0000375 = 0.03337 \text{ mAh} $$ $$ I_{avg} = \frac{Q_{cycle}}{T/3600} = \frac{0.03337}{30/3600} = \frac{0.03337}{0.00833} = 4.005 \text{ mA} $$ **Step 4: Raw battery life (1 mark):** $$ t_{raw} = \frac{1500}{4.005} = 374.5 \text{ h} = 15.6 \text{ days} $$ **Step 5: Apply 20% derating (1 mark):** $$ t_{actual} = 15.6 \times 0.8 = 12.5 \text{ days} $$ **Final Answer:** $I_{avg} \approx 4 \text{ mA}$, battery life ≈ **12.5 days** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:** > 1. Forgetting to convert seconds to hours (loses 1 mark). > 2. Forgetting to apply the 20% derating factor (loses 1 mark). > 3. Confusing "active time per cycle" with "active time per day." > 4. Writing $I_{avg} = (40 + 0.005)/2$ — this is WRONG. Average current requires **time-weighted integration**, not arithmetic mean. > 5. Forgetting that sleep current is in μA and must be converted to mA. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — HARDWARE COMPONENTS** ### A. Architecture & Tiers - ✅ IoT hardware is organized into **four tiers**: Sensing, Processing, Communication, Power/Actuation. - ✅ The **microcontroller is the central hub** — every other component interfaces with it. - ✅ Analog sensors need **ADC**; digital sensors use **protocols** (I²C, SPI, UART, 1-Wire). ### B. Sensor Subsystem - ✅ **Analog sensors**: LM35, LDR, MQ-2 — output continuous voltage. - ✅ **Digital sensors**: DHT22, DS18B20, BME280 — output digital codes. - ✅ **Signal conditioning** chain: Sensor → Amplifier → Filter → ADC → MCU. - ✅ Key specifications: Accuracy, precision, resolution, sensitivity, dynamic range. ### C. Processing Core - ✅ **MCU** = CPU + RAM + ROM + Peripherals on one chip (low power, real-time). - ✅ **MPU** = CPU only, needs external peripherals (high power, full OS). - ✅ Popular IoT MCUs: **ESP32, STM32, ATmega328, MSP430, PIC**. - ✅ On-chip peripherals: GPIO, ADC, DAC, Timers, UART, SPI, I²C, WDT. ### D. Communication Interfaces - ✅ **Short-range (≤100 m)**: BLE (1 Mbps), Zigbee (250 kbps), NFC, RFID. - ✅ **Medium-range (~50 m)**: Wi-Fi 802.11 b/g/n/ac/ax (54 Mbps to 9.6 Gbps). - ✅ **Long-range (≥2 km)**: LoRa (0.3–50 kbps), NB-IoT, Sigfox, LTE-M. - ✅ **Wired protocols**: I²C, SPI, UART, CAN, RS-485, Ethernet. - ✅ Frequency bands: 2.4 GHz (BLE/Wi-Fi/Zigbee), Sub-GHz (LoRa/Zigbee). ### E. Power Management - ✅ **Battery types**: Alkaline, Li-ion, Li-Po, Li-SOCl₂, NiMH. - ✅ **Voltage regulators**: LDO (low noise, inefficient), Buck (step-down, >90% efficient), Boost (step-up). - ✅ **Energy harvesting**: Solar (PV), piezoelectric (vibration), thermoelectric (heat). - ✅ Battery life formula: $t = C_{battery} / I_{avg}$ — but apply **20–30% derating**. ### F. Critical Formulas (Must Memorize) - ✅ ADC code: $D = (V_{in}/V_{ref}) \times 2^n$ - ✅ LSB size: $\Delta V = V_{ref}/2^n$ - ✅ Power: $P = V \times I = I^2 R = V^2/R$ - ✅ Battery life (h): $t = C / I_{avg}$ - ✅ Resistor divider: $V_{out} = V_{in} \times R_2/(R_1+R_2)$ - ✅ PWM duty: $D\% = (T_{on}/T_{period}) \times 100$ - ✅ RC time constant: $\tau = R \times C$ - ✅ Free-space path loss: $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ (d in km, f in MHz) ### G. KTU Exam Writing Tips - ✅ Always draw a **labeled block diagram** for architecture questions. - ✅ For ADC questions, show **all substitution steps** — partial marking depends on this. - ✅ For comparison questions, use a **table format** with at least 5 points. - ✅ Mention **real-world examples** (e.g., ESP32, DHT22) to demonstrate applied knowledge. - ✅ Use correct **units** throughout (V, mA, mAh, kbps, dBm). - ✅ State **assumptions** explicitly (e.g., "assuming ideal ADC, no noise"). ### H. Common Numerical Values to Remember - ✅ Standard ADC resolutions: 8, 10, 12, 16 bits → 256, 1024, 4096, 65536 levels. - ✅ Common supply voltages: 1.8 V, 3.3 V, 5 V, 12 V. - ✅ Common frequencies: 8 MHz, 16 MHz, 80 MHz, 240 MHz. - ✅ Common data rates: UART 9600/115200 baud, I²C 100/400 kHz, SPI 1–10 MHz. - ✅ Wireless ISM bands: 433 MHz, 868 MHz, 915 MHz, 2.4 GHz, 5 GHz. > [!NOTE] > **End of Module 3 — Hardware Components Notes** > These notes cover the KTU 2024 Scheme PCCGT503 Module 3 syllabus requirements. Pair them with the official textbook and laboratory manual for comprehensive preparation. Practice the ADC and battery life derivations — they appear in nearly every KTU exam cycle.

topics.title: Setting Up Raspberry Pi
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT503 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Setting Up Raspberry Pi

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Raspberry Pi?

**Raspberry Pi** is a series of small, single-board computers (SBCs) developed by the **Raspberry Pi Foundation** (UK) to promote the teaching of basic computer science in schools and developing countries. For IoT, it acts as a **powerful edge-computing node**, capable of running a full-fledged Operating System (OS), processing sensor data, controlling actuators, and acting as a gateway between local IoT devices and the cloud.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):**
> "Raspberry Pi is a credit-card sized, low-cost, single-board computer based on a Broadcom ARM-based System on Chip (SoC), designed to run Linux-based operating systems and widely adopted as a processing unit for Internet of Things (IoT) applications, prototyping, and embedded development."

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **full desktop computer compressed into the size of a credit card** — that is a Raspberry Pi.

- **The SoC (System on Chip)** is the **brain** — it contains the CPU, GPU, and RAM all in one tiny package.
- **The GPIO pins** are its **hands and fingers** — they reach out to physically touch the real world (sensors, LEDs, motors).
- **The OS (Raspberry Pi OS)** is its **consciousness** — it understands commands and decides what to do.
- **The microSD card** is its **memory and notebook** — it stores the OS, files, and your programs.

When you "Set Up" a Raspberry Pi, you are essentially **waking it up, teaching it how to talk to its surroundings, and giving it a job to do** — the same way you would set up a new employee: give them a desk (power), tools (peripherals), a manual (OS), and a task (IoT application).

### Key Hardware Components at a Glance

| Component | Standard Metric | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **SoC** | Broadcom BCM2711 (Quad-core Cortex-A72 @ **1.8 GHz**) | Main Processor |
| **RAM** | 1 GB / 2 GB / 4 GB / 8 GB LPDDR4 | Volatile Memory |
| **Storage** | microSD card slot | OS + persistent data |
| **GPIO** | 40-pin header (2.54 mm pitch) | Hardware interfacing |
| **USB** | 2x USB 3.0 + 2x USB 2.0 | Peripherals |
| **Network** | Gigabit Ethernet, Wi-Fi (802.11ac), Bluetooth 5.0 | Connectivity |
| **Display** | 2x micro-HDMI (4K @ 60 Hz) | Video output |
| **Power** | USB-C, **5.1 V / 3 A** | Power input |

> [!NOTE]
> For the KTU 2024 syllabus, the **Raspberry Pi 4 Model B** is the most commonly referenced variant, though the **Pi 3 B+** and **Pi 5** may appear in newer questions.

## 1.2 What Does "Setting Up" Mean in the IoT Context?

"Setting Up Raspberry Pi" refers to the **end-to-end bootstrap process** that transforms a freshly unpacked board into a fully functional IoT-ready compute node. It includes:

1. **Hardware assembly** (board, case, peripherals).
2. **OS installation** on the microSD card.
3. **Initial configuration** (locale, password, network).
4. **Remote access** (SSH / VNC) for headless IoT deployment.
5. **GPIO and library installation** for sensor interfacing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU emphasizes this topic:** Module 3 bridges the gap between microprocessor theory and real-world IoT deployment. Examiners expect students to demonstrate **procedural knowledge** (how to set up) and **analytical knowledge** (why each step matters in IoT).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 The Setting-Up Workflow — Structured Logic

Setting up a Raspberry Pi follows a **sequential, dependency-driven pipeline**. Each step is a prerequisite for the next.

### Step 1: Hardware Inventory Check
- Verify board, **5.1 V / 3 A** USB-C power supply, microSD card (**Class 10**, ≥ **16 GB**), HDMI cable, keyboard, mouse, monitor, and Ethernet cable (optional).
- **Why?** Undervoltage causes throttling and system instability — a **lightning bolt icon** appears on screen.

### Step 2: OS Image Flashing
- Download **Raspberry Pi Imager** (official tool from the Foundation).
- Flash the chosen OS (default: **Raspberry Pi OS (64-bit, Desktop)**) onto the microSD card.
- **Why?** The Pi has no internal storage; the microSD is its hard disk.

### Step 3: First Boot & Peripheral Connection
- Insert the flashed microSD card.
- Connect HDMI → monitor, USB → keyboard/mouse.
- Connect USB-C power (last step — the Pi has no power button).

### Step 4: Initial Configuration Wizard (raspi-config)
- Set **country, language, timezone**.
- Change default password (`pi / raspberry` is publicly known).
- Connect to **Wi-Fi** (SSID + password).
- Enable **SSH** (Secure Shell) and **VNC** (Virtual Network Computing) for remote access.
- Update the system via `sudo apt update && sudo apt full-upgrade`.

### Step 5: Headless / Remote Access Setup
- For IoT, the Pi usually runs **without a monitor** ("headless").
- Access via **SSH** (command-line) or **VNC** (graphical desktop) over LAN.
- Find the Pi's IP using `hostname -I` or your router's DHCP lease table.

### Step 6: GPIO and IoT Library Installation
- Install Python (preinstalled) and the **RPi.GPIO** or **gpiozero** library.
- Test GPIO with a simple LED-blink script.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> The "Setting Up" topic is **procedural**, not formula-heavy. However, the following **boundary conditions and metrics** are frequently tested in KTU exams.

| Parameter | Standard Value | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Input Voltage | **5.1 V DC** | Lower values cause undervoltage warnings |
| Input Current | **3.0 A** (Pi 4/5) | Power budget for USB peripherals |
| microSD Capacity (min) | **8 GB** (Class 10) | Below this, OS install fails |
| GPIO Pin Voltage | **3.3 V** logic | **NOT 5 V tolerant** — a common exam pitfall |
| GPIO Max Current per pin | **16 mA** (recommended $\leq$ 8 mA) | Exceeding damages the SoC |
| Default Username | `pi` | Replaced in newer OS releases |
| Default Password | `raspberry` | Must be changed on first boot |
| Configuration Tool | `raspi-config` | CLI tool for system settings |
| Network Discovery | `ping raspberrypi.local` (mDNS) | Works on Pi 3B+ and later |
| Remote Port (SSH) | **22** | Default TCP port |
| Remote Port (VNC) | **5900** | Default TCP port |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **industrial IoT (IIoT)** deployments, the "Setting Up" phase determines:

- **Boot reliability** in unattended environments.
- **Security posture** (default credentials = compromised node).
- **Maintainability** (headless access allows remote firmware updates).
- **Determinism** (a properly configured Pi can run 24/7 as an edge gateway aggregating Modbus, MQTT, and HTTP data).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Examiners often ask *"Why is it recommended to change the default password immediately?"* — Answer: The default `pi / raspberry` credentials are public knowledge and indexed by automated botnets like **Mirai**, which scan the internet for unsecured IoT devices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedural Analysis

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough — Standard Desktop Setup

The following is a **complete, copy-examinable procedure** for setting up a Raspberry Pi. Each step is annotated with the underlying **"Why"**.

### Procedure: Desktop Setup with Monitor

**Step 1 — Download Raspberry Pi Imager**
- Visit the official Raspberry Pi website.
- Install the Imager on a host PC (Windows / macOS / Linux).
- **Why?** It is the only tool endorsed by the Foundation and it verifies image integrity via SHA-256 checksums.

**Step 2 — Flash the microSD Card**
- Insert the microSD card (≥ **16 GB**, Class 10) into the host PC.
- Open Raspberry Pi Imager.
- Select: *Device* → *Raspberry Pi 4* → *Operating System* → *Raspberry Pi OS (64-bit)* → *Storage* → *microSD card*.
- Click **Write**.
- **Why 64-bit?** The 64-bit kernel enables access to the full **8 GB RAM** (32-bit is limited to ~ 3 GB usable per process).

**Step 3 — Pre-Configure (Optional but Recommended)**
- Click the **gear icon** (or Ctrl+Shift+X) in Imager before writing.
- Set:
  - **Hostname:** `iot-pi-01`
  - **Enable SSH:** Use password authentication
  - **Set username and password:** e.g., `iotuser / StrongP@ss123`
  - **Configure Wi-Fi:** SSID + password + country `IN`
  - **Set locale:** Asia/Kolkata, en-IN keyboard
- **Why?** This eliminates the need for a monitor entirely (headless from boot).

**Step 4 — First Boot**
- Eject the microSD card, insert it into the Pi.
- Connect HDMI, keyboard, mouse, Ethernet (if not using Wi-Fi).
- Plug in the USB-C power supply **last**.
- The Pi will boot to the desktop in **≈ 25 seconds**.

**Step 5 — Run `raspi-config` (CLI) or Use GUI Preferences**
- Update `apt` repositories:

```bash
sudo apt update
```

Upgrade installed packages:

```bash
sudo apt full-upgrade -y
```

- **Why?** A freshly imaged OS may have outdated security patches; Mirai-class botnets exploit unpatched OpenSSL vulnerabilities.

**Step 6 — Enable Interfaces**
- Open `raspi-config` → *Interface Options* → Enable:
  - **SSH** (port 22)
  - **VNC** (port 5900)
  - **I2C** (for sensors like MPU6050, BMP280)
  - **SPI** (for displays, high-speed ADC)
  - **1-Wire** (for DS18B20 temperature sensors)
  - **GPIO remote access** (for `gpiozero` over network)

**Step 7 — Verify the Setup**
- Check IP address:

```bash
hostname -I
```

- Test GPIO with a Python script (LED blink):

```python
from gpiozero import LED
from time import sleep

led = LED(17)        # Pin 11 on the board
while True:
    led.on()
    sleep(1)
    led.off()
    sleep(1)
```

- **Why pin 17?** Because `gpiozero` uses **Broadcom (BCM) numbering**, not physical pin position. BCM 17 = physical pin 11.

## 3.2 Headless Setup — Analytical Breakdown

A **headless** Raspberry Pi runs **without a monitor, keyboard, or mouse** — ideal for IoT deployments. The setup can be analyzed in three distinct phases:

### Phase A — Pre-Boot Configuration
- The microSD card, when flashed, contains a **boot partition** readable on any PC.
- Create an empty file named `ssh` (no extension) in the boot partition to enable SSH on first boot.
- Create / edit `wpa_supplicant.conf` to pre-configure Wi-Fi:

```ini
country=IN
ctrl_interface=DIR=/var/run/wpa_supplicant GROUP=netdev
update_config=1
network={
    ssid="YourWiFiSSID"
    psk="YourWiFiPassword"
}
```

### Phase B — Network Discovery
- Insert the card, power the Pi, wait **≈ 30 seconds**.
- From your PC, discover the Pi via:
  - **mDNS:** `ping raspberrypi.local`
  - **Router admin page:** Look for new DHCP leases
  - **Network scanner:** Tools like `nmap -sn 192.168.1.0/24`

### Phase C — Remote Login
- **SSH (command-line):**

```bash
ssh iotuser@raspberrypi.local
```

- **VNC (graphical):** Open VNC Viewer, enter `raspberrypi.local`, log in.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Analytical Question Pattern:** *"Compare SSH and VNC for IoT deployment. Which is preferred for a battery-powered sensor node?"* — Answer: **SSH** is preferred for low-bandwidth, headless, energy-constrained nodes because VNC's continuous screen-refresh traffic consumes significant power and bandwidth.

## 3.3 Network Configuration — Static IP Derivation

For a stable IoT gateway, a **static IP** is essential (so that MQTT brokers, dashboards, and remote sensors know exactly where to send data).

**Step 1 — Identify the current IP and gateway:**

```bash
ip a
ip route
```

**Step 2 — Edit the DHCP client configuration:**

```bash
sudo nano /etc/dhcpcd.conf
```

**Step 3 — Append the static configuration (example):**

```bash
interface wlan0
static ip_address=192.168.1.50/24
static routers=192.168.1.1
static domain_name_servers=8.8.8.8 8.8.4.4
```

**Step 4 — Reboot and verify:**

```bash
sudo reboot
ping 8.8.8.8
```

**Why these specific values?**

- `192.168.1.50` — A free address in the typical home/office subnet.
- `192.168.1.1` — Default gateway (most home routers).
- `8.8.8.8` — Google Public DNS (reliable, censorship-free).
- `/24` — Subnet mask `255.255.255.0` (allows 254 hosts).

## 3.4 OS Variants — Comparative Analysis

| OS Variant | Size | Best Use Case | KTU Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Raspberry Pi OS (Full)** | ≈ 2.8 GB | Desktop + development | Default for KTU labs |
| **Raspberry Pi OS (Lite)** | ≈ 0.5 GB | Headless servers, IoT gateways | Preferred for low-memory Pi Zero |
| **Ubuntu Server (ARM64)** | ≈ 1.2 GB | Cloud-native, Docker, K8s | Emerging KTU topic |
| **Home Assistant OS** | ≈ 2.0 GB | Smart-home automation | Industry-relevant |
| **RetroPie** | ≈ 1.5 GB | Gaming emulation | Not for KTU exams |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Raspberry Pi Setup Flow

The following Mermaid block illustrates the **complete decision-and-action pipeline** for setting up a Raspberry Pi, including the conditional branching required for headless vs. desktop deployment.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Unboxed Pi] --> B[Download Raspberry Pi Imager on Host PC]
    B --> C[Insert microSD Card into Host PC]
    C --> D[Select OS: Raspberry Pi OS 64-bit]
    D --> E[Optional: Pre-configure SSH + Wi-Fi via Gear Icon]
    E --> F[Write Image to microSD]
    F --> G{Eject microSD and Insert into Pi}
    G --> H{Connect Peripherals}
    H -->|Desktop Setup| I[Connect HDMI, USB Keyboard, USB Mouse]
    H -->|Headless Setup| J[Skip Peripherals, Rely on SSH]
    I --> K[Plug USB-C 5.1V 3A Power]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Pi Boots in 25 Seconds]
    L --> M{First Boot Configuration}
    M --> N[Set Locale, Timezone, Keyboard]
    N --> O[Change Default Password]
    O --> P[Connect to Wi-Fi or Ethernet]
    P --> Q[Run sudo apt update && sudo apt full-upgrade]
    Q --> R[Enable Interfaces via raspi-config]
    R --> S[SSH Port 22]
    R --> T[VNC Port 5900]
    R --> U[I2C, SPI, 1-Wire]
    S --> V[Install gpiozero / RPi.GPIO Library]
    T --> V
    U --> V
    V --> W[Test GPIO with LED Blink Program]
    W --> X[Setup Complete: Pi is IoT Ready]
    X --> Y[Optional: Configure Static IP for Gateway Role]
```

## 4.2 Raspberry Pi GPIO Pinout (BCM Numbering Map)

The following diagram shows the **40-pin GPIO header** with both **physical pin numbers** (left) and **Broadcom BCM numbers** (right). Only the most commonly used pins for IoT are highlighted.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PiGPIO["Raspberry Pi 40-Pin GPIO Header"]
        direction LR
        P1[Pin 1: 3.3V Power] --- P2[Pin 2: 5V Power]
        P3[Pin 3: GPIO2 I2C SDA] --- P4[Pin 4: 5V Power]
        P5[Pin 5: GPIO3 I2C SCL] --- P6[Pin 6: Ground]
        P7[Pin 7: GPIO4 GPCLK0] --- P8[Pin 8: GPIO14 UART TX]
        P9[Pin 9: Ground] --- P10[Pin 10: GPIO15 UART RX]
        P11[Pin 11: GPIO17] --- P12[Pin 12: GPIO18 PWM]
        P13[Pin 13: GPIO27] --- P14[Pin 14: Ground]
        P15[Pin 15: GPIO22] --- P16[Pin 16: GPIO23]
        P17[Pin 17: 3.3V Power] --- P18[Pin 18: GPIO24]
        P19[Pin 19: GPIO10 SPI MOSI] --- P20[Pin 20: Ground]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked *"On which pin would you connect an LED to blink it via BCM numbering 17?"* — Answer: **Physical pin 11**. This is a classic trap question; many students write "pin 17" and lose 2 marks.

## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture of Raspberry Pi as an IoT Node

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ExternalWorld["External IoT Environment"]
        Sensor1[Temperature Sensor DS18B20]
        Sensor2[Humidity Sensor DHT22]
        Actuator1[Relay Module]
        Cloud[Cloud Server MQTT Broker]
    end

    subgraph RaspberryPi["Raspberry Pi IoT Node"]
        Power[USB-C 5.1V 3A Power Supply]
        SoC[SoC BCM2711 Quad Core ARM Cortex A72]
        RAM[LPDDR4 RAM 4GB]
        Storage[microSD Card Raspberry Pi OS]
        GPIO[GPIO Header 40 pins 3.3V logic]
        Network[Wi-Fi and Ethernet and Bluetooth]
        USB[USB Ports for Camera and Storage]
    end

    Power --> SoC
    Storage --> SoC
    RAM --> SoC
    SoC --> GPIO
    SoC --> Network
    SoC --> USB
    GPIO --> Sensor1
    GPIO --> Sensor2
    GPIO --> Actuator1
    Network --> Cloud
```

## 4.4 Boot Sequence of Raspberry Pi

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power On USB-C Plugged] --> B[SoC Reads First Stage Bootloader from ROM]
    B --> C[GPU Loads bootcode.bin from microSD FAT32 Partition]
    C --> D[GPU Loads start.elf Initialization Firmware]
    D --> E[GPU Reads config.txt and cmdline.txt]
    E --> F[GPU Hands Over Control to ARM CPU]
    F --> G[Linux Kernel vmlinuz Loaded into RAM]
    G --> H[Kernel Mounts rootfs Partition]
    H --> I[Systemd Starts as PID 1]
    I --> J[Services Activated: SSH, VNC, cron, networking]
    J --> K[Login Prompt or Desktop Environment Ready]
    K --> L[Pi Fully Booted and IoT Operational]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the GPU load the bootloader, not the CPU?**
> This is a deliberate design decision by Broadcom — the GPU's firmware handles the lowest-level hardware initialization, freeing the ARM CPU from platform-specific boot logic. This is also why **closed-source `start.elf` files** are required and distributed only by the Raspberry Pi Foundation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**Q1. List any three essential hardware components required for the first-time setup of a Raspberry Pi 4 Model B.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **microSD card** (Class 10, ≥ 16 GB) — to store the OS and persistent data.
2. **USB-C power supply** rated **5.1 V / 3 A** — to power the board and connected peripherals.
3. **micro-HDMI to HDMI cable** — to connect the Pi to a display for the first boot.

*(Acceptable alternatives: USB keyboard, USB mouse, Ethernet cable, case for physical protection.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

**Q2. What is the purpose of the `raspi-config` tool in Raspberry Pi setup?**

**Model Answer:**

`raspi-config` is a **command-line configuration utility** provided by the Raspberry Pi OS that allows the user to modify system-level settings without manually editing configuration files. It is used to:

- Change the default password.
- Enable/disable hardware interfaces (SSH, VNC, I2C, SPI, 1-Wire, Camera).
- Configure locale, timezone, and keyboard layout.
- Set hostname and network options (static IP, Wi-Fi country).
- Expand the filesystem to use the full capacity of the microSD card.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure to set up a Raspberry Pi in **headless mode** for an IoT application. Include the methods for enabling remote access. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the significance of configuring a **static IP address** and enabling **SSH/VNC** services on a Raspberry Pi deployed as an IoT gateway. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Headless Setup Procedure (7 Marks)

A **headless** setup is one in which the Raspberry Pi operates **without a dedicated monitor, keyboard, or mouse**, controlled entirely over the network. The procedure is:

**Step 1 — Flash the OS (2 marks)**

- Insert a microSD card (≥ 16 GB, Class 10) into the host PC.
- Open **Raspberry Pi Imager**, select *Raspberry Pi OS Lite (64-bit)* to minimize footprint.
- Click the **gear icon** and pre-configure:
  - Hostname: `iot-gateway-01`
  - Enable SSH with password authentication
  - Set username `iotuser` and a strong password
  - Configure Wi-Fi SSID, password, and country `IN`
- Click **Write** and verify the SHA-256 checksum.

**Step 2 — First Boot Without Peripherals (2 marks)**

- Insert the microSD card into the Pi.
- Connect an Ethernet cable (more reliable than Wi-Fi for first boot).
- Plug in the USB-C power supply.
- The Pi boots in approximately **25 seconds**; the green ACT LED flickers during boot and stays steady when ready.

**Step 3 — Remote Access (3 marks)**

- From the host PC, discover the Pi via **mDNS**:

```bash
ping iot-gateway-01.local
```

- Establish an **SSH** session:

```bash
ssh iotuser@iot-gateway-01.local
```

- (Optional) For graphical access, enable VNC via `sudo raspi-config` → *Interface Options* → *VNC* → *Yes*, then connect using **RealVNC Viewer** on the host.
- SSH uses **port 22**; VNC uses **port 5900**.

#### Part (b) — Static IP and SSH/VNC Significance (7 Marks)

**Static IP Configuration (3.5 marks)**

- A **dynamic IP** (assigned by DHCP) may change after reboot, breaking any IoT clients that hardcode the Pi's address.
- A **static IP** ensures the Pi is reachable at a **fixed, predictable address** — essential when it acts as:
  - An **MQTT broker** (publishers and subscribers need a stable host).
  - A **data dashboard server** (web clients bookmark the URL).
  - A **Modbus/OPC-UA gateway** (PLCs and SCADA systems require deterministic addressing).
- Configured in `/etc/dhcpcd.conf`:

```bash
interface wlan0
static ip_address=192.168.1.50/24
static routers=192.168.1.1
static domain_name_servers=8.8.8.8 8.8.4.4
```

**SSH and VNC Significance (3.5 marks)**

- **SSH (Secure Shell):** Provides **encrypted, command-line remote access** over port 22. Used for headless administration, running scripts, installing packages, and editing files. Lightweight (≈ a few KB/s).
- **VNC (Virtual Network Computing):** Provides **graphical desktop access** over port 5900. Useful for IoT developers running GUI-based IDEs (Thonny, Visual Studio Code) on the Pi. Heavier (~ 100 KB/s to several MB/s).
- **Combined benefit:** SSH + VNC eliminate the need for a physical monitor in production deployments, allowing the Pi to be installed in remote, inaccessible, or hazardous IoT locations (e.g., factory floors, agricultural fields, rooftops) while remaining fully manageable.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of a Raspberry Pi** as an IoT processing node, identifying the role of the SoC, GPIO, and network interfaces. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **headless** and **desktop** setup approaches of a Raspberry Pi in terms of power consumption, security, and suitability for IoT deployment. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Raspberry Pi Architecture (7 Marks)

The Raspberry Pi architecture as an IoT node can be represented as follows:

**Block-Level Architecture (5 marks for diagram + 2 marks for explanation):**

*(Refer to the Block-Level Architecture Mermaid diagram in Section 4.3 for the visual.)*

- **SoC (Broadcom BCM2711):** Houses the **quad-core ARM Cortex-A72 CPU @ 1.8 GHz**, the **VideoCore VI GPU**, and the **LPDDR4 RAM controller** on a single die. It executes the OS, runs IoT applications, processes sensor data, and manages network communication.

- **GPIO Header (40 pins):** Provides **3.3 V logic-level** bidirectional digital I/O pins that interface directly with sensors (DS18B20, DHT22, MPU6050) and actuators (relays, motors, LEDs). Special-function pins support **I2C, SPI, UART, and PWM** protocols.

- **Network Interfaces:** The **Wi-Fi (802.11ac)** and **Gigabit Ethernet** modules enable the Pi to communicate with cloud MQTT brokers, REST APIs, and other IoT nodes. **Bluetooth 5.0** allows short-range communication with BLE sensors and beacons.

#### Part (b) — Headless vs. Desktop Setup Comparison (7 Marks)

| Parameter | Desktop Setup | Headless Setup |
|---|---|---|
| **Peripherals Required** | Monitor, keyboard, mouse | None |
| **Power Consumption** | Higher (≈ **3.0–3.5 W** idle, up to **7.5 W** under load) | Lower (≈ **2.5–3.0 W** idle) |
| **Security** | Lower (physical access to peripherals, default passwords often left unchanged) | Higher (no physical input devices, SSH key-based auth recommended) |
| **Boot Time** | **25–30 s** (full desktop) | **20–25 s** (CLI only) |
| **OS Footprint** | Raspberry Pi OS Full (≈ 2.8 GB) | Raspberry Pi OS Lite (≈ 0.5 GB) |
| **RAM Usage at Idle** | ≈ 400–500 MB | ≈ 80–150 MB |
| **IoT Suitability** | Limited (good for development only) | **High** (ideal for production deployment) |
| **Remote Management** | Optional | Mandatory via SSH/VNC |
| **Physical Footprint** | Larger (requires display space) | Minimal (fits in enclosures) |

**Verdict (2 marks):** For **production IoT deployments** (smart agriculture, industrial monitoring, edge gateways), the **headless setup is strongly preferred** because it offers lower power consumption, smaller OS footprint, higher security, and easier remote management. The desktop setup is best reserved for **initial development and debugging**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones in "Setting Up Raspberry Pi" Questions:**
>
> 1. **GPIO Voltage Confusion (–2 marks):** Students often write "GPIO is 5 V" because the Pi has a 5 V rail. **WRONG** — the GPIO pins are **3.3 V logic only**. Connecting 5 V to a GPIO pin **permanently damages the SoC**.
> 2. **Pin Numbering Mix-up (–2 marks):** Mixing up **physical pin numbers** (1–40) with **BCM (Broadcom) GPIO numbers** (GPIO2, GPIO17, etc.). The `gpiozero` library uses BCM; the `RPi.GPIO` library can use either depending on the `mode` setting.
> 3. **Power Supply Specs (–1 mark):** Writing "5 V / 2 A" instead of the correct **5.1 V / 3 A** for Pi 4. The slightly higher voltage compensates for cable drop.
> 4. **Confusing `raspi-config` with `apt` (–1 mark):** `raspi-config` is a **configuration tool**, not a package manager. Use `apt` for installing software.
> 5. **Forgetting to enable interfaces (–1 mark):** I2C, SPI, and 1-Wire are **disabled by default**. Students forget that a sensor connected to I2C pins will not work until I2C is enabled in `raspi-config`.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Setting Up Raspberry Pi" (Module 3):**
>
> - **Raspberry Pi 4 Model B** uses a **Broadcom BCM2711 SoC** with a **quad-core Cortex-A72** CPU clocked at **1.8 GHz**.
> - Power input is **USB-C, 5.1 V / 3 A**. Insufficient power triggers a **lightning bolt warning** and CPU throttling.
> - The Pi has **no internal storage** — the **microSD card** is mandatory (Class 10, ≥ 8 GB minimum, 16 GB recommended).
> - The **default username/password** (`pi / raspberry`) must be **changed immediately** for security.
> - **GPIO pins are 3.3 V logic** and **NOT 5 V tolerant**; max current per pin is **16 mA** (recommended ≤ 8 mA).
> - The **40-pin GPIO header** supports **I2C, SPI, UART, PWM, and 1-Wire** protocols on dedicated pins.
> - **Raspberry Pi Imager** is the official tool to flash OS images; it supports pre-configuration of SSH and Wi-Fi via a **gear icon**.
> - **`raspi-config`** is the CLI tool for system configuration: locale, password, interfaces, hostname, boot options.
> - **Headless setup** requires creating an empty `ssh` file in the boot partition and (optionally) a `wpa_supplicant.conf` for Wi-Fi.
> - **Remote access** is via **SSH (port 22)** for command-line and **VNC (port 5900)** for graphical desktop.
> - The Pi can be discovered on a network via **mDNS** (`hostname.local`) on systems that support it.
> - A **static IP** is essential for IoT gateways acting as MQTT brokers or data servers — configured in `/etc/dhcpcd.conf`.
> - For IoT, the **Lite** variant of Raspberry Pi OS is preferred (smaller footprint, lower RAM usage, faster boot).
> - The **boot sequence** involves the GPU loading `bootcode.bin` → `start.elf` → reading `config.txt` → handing control to the ARM CPU → loading the Linux kernel → starting **systemd** as PID 1.
> - Always run **`sudo apt update && sudo apt full-upgrade`** after first boot to patch security vulnerabilities.
> - **Common Python libraries** for IoT on the Pi: `gpiozero` (high-level GPIO), `RPi.GPIO` (low-level GPIO), `paho-mqtt` (MQTT client), `Flask` (web server).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programming on Raspberry Pi
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT503 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Programming on Raspberry Pi

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Programming on Raspberry Pi** refers to the systematic process of writing, compiling/interpretating, and executing software code on the **Raspberry Pi single-board computer (SBC)** to perform computational tasks, control external hardware through **General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO)** pins, and implement **Internet of Things (IoT)** applications. The Raspberry Pi primarily runs on the **Raspberry Pi OS** (a Debian-based Linux distribution) and supports multiple programming languages, with **Python** being the de-facto standard due to its simplicity and rich library ecosystem (e.g., `RPi.GPIO`, `gpiozero`).

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, programming the Raspberry Pi is positioned as the **practical implementation layer** that bridges high-level IoT logic (sensing, actuation, networking, cloud communication) with the physical BCM2837/BCM2711 SoC hardware. The standard language for examination purposes is **Python 3**, while **C/C++** is used for performance-critical and real-time applications via the **WiringPi** or **pigpio** libraries.

### 1.2 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **RPi** | Raspberry Pi — a credit-card-sized SBC developed by the Raspberry Pi Foundation |
| **GPIO** | General Purpose Input/Output — programmable pins on the Pi header |
| **SoC** | System on Chip (e.g., BCM2711 in Pi 4, BCM2837 in Pi 3) |
| **BCM** | Broadcom pin numbering — refers to the SoC's internal GPIO channel numbers |
| **BOARD** | Physical pin numbering — refers to the actual position on the 40-pin header |
| **PWM** | Pulse Width Modulation — used to simulate analog output from a digital pin |
| **I²C** | Inter-Integrated Circuit — two-wire serial protocol (SDA, SCL) |
| **SPI** | Serial Peripheral Interface — four-wire full-duplex protocol |
| **UART** | Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter — serial communication |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Raspberry Pi as a "Swiss Army Knife of IoT"**
> 
> Imagine you have a **tiny, fully functional desktop computer** that fits in your palm, costs less than a pizza, and exposes a set of **"magic buttons and levers"** on its edge. These buttons (GPIO pins) can be told to either **send electricity out** (output mode — like switching on an LED) or **listen for electricity coming in** (input mode — like reading a push button). 
> 
> Now imagine writing a **recipe (program)** in a language the computer understands (Python). When you give it the recipe:
> - *Every half second, send power to Pin 17 to blink an LED.*
> - *If Button on Pin 27 is pressed, turn on a buzzer connected to Pin 22.*
> - *Read the temperature from the sensor on Pin 4 and post it to the cloud every minute.*
> 
> The Raspberry Pi acts like a **very obedient robot chef** that follows your recipe **24×7**, talking to sensors, motors, lights, and the internet. This combination of **computational power + physical-world control + network connectivity** is exactly what makes it the cornerstone of IoT prototyping.

### 1.4 Why Raspberry Pi for IoT Programming?

> [!NOTE]
> **Three pillars** that make the Pi the preferred IoT programming platform in academic and industrial settings:
> 
> 1. **Full OS Support** — Unlike microcontrollers (Arduino), the Pi runs a **full Linux OS**, enabling multitasking, networking stacks (TCP/IP, MQTT, HTTP), file systems, and shell scripting.
> 2. **High-Level Language Support** — Python, C/C++, Java, Node.js, and even Scratch (for beginners).
> 3. **Rich GPIO Header** — **40-pin** header (26 usable GPIO + power/ground pins) supporting digital I/O, PWM, I²C, SPI, UART.

### 1.5 Raspberry Pi Models at a Glance

| Model | SoC | RAM | GPIO | Recommended Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Raspberry Pi 4B** | BCM2711 (Cortex-A72, 1.5 GHz quad-core) | 2/4/8 GB | 40-pin | Heavy IoT gateways, ML edge |
| **Raspberry Pi 3B+** | BCM2837B0 (Cortex-A53, 1.4 GHz) | 1 GB | 40-pin | General IoT, education |
| **Raspberry Pi Zero W** | BCM2835 (ARM11, 1 GHz) | 512 MB | 40-pin | Low-power wearable IoT |
| **Raspberry Pi Pico** | RP2040 (dual Cortex-M0+) | 264 KB | 26-pin | Microcontroller-class (uses MicroPython/C) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Raspberry Pi Software Stack

Programming on the Raspberry Pi is best understood by examining its **layered software architecture**:

> **Layer 1 — Hardware (SoC + GPIO + Peripherals)**
> ↓ controlled by
> **Layer 2 — Linux Kernel (Raspberry Pi OS, kernel v6.x)**
> ↓ exposes standard interfaces through
> **Layer 3 — Device Drivers & `/dev` filesystems (`/dev/gpiomem`, `/dev/i2c-1`)**
> ↓ accessed via
> **Layer 4 — User-Space Libraries (`RPi.GPIO`, `gpiozero`, `pigpio`, `WiringPi`)**
> ↓ used in
> **Layer 5 — Application Code (Python 3, C/C++)**

### 2.2 GPIO Pin Numbering Schemes — The #1 Exam Trap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Exam Pitfall #1:** Confusing **BCM** and **BOARD** numbering. Always explicitly mention the numbering scheme in code comments. Mismatched pin numbers can short-circuit hardware.

The Raspberry Pi 40-pin header exposes pins, but they are referenced in **two different ways**:

| Pin Number on Header (Physical) | BCM GPIO Number | WiringPi Number | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pin 11 | **GPIO17** | 0 | General Purpose |
| Pin 13 | **GPIO27** | 2 | General Purpose |
| Pin 15 | **GPIO22** | 3 | General Purpose |
| Pin 16 | **GPIO23** | 4 | General Purpose |
| Pin 18 | **GPIO24** | 5 | General Purpose |
| Pin 22 | **GPIO25** | 6 | General Purpose |
| Pin 29 | **GPIO5** | 21 | General Purpose |
| Pin 31 | **GPIO6** | 22 | General Purpose |
| Pin 32 | **GPIO12** | 26 | PWM0 |
| Pin 33 | **GPIO13** | 23 | PWM1 |
| Pin 35 | **GPIO19** | 24 | SPI MISO |
| Pin 36 | **GPIO16** | 27 | SPI CE2 |
| Pin 37 | **GPIO26** | 25 | General Purpose |
| Pin 38 | **GPIO20** | 28 | SPI MOSI |
| Pin 40 | **GPIO21** | 29 | SPI SCLK |

- **`GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)`** → uses Broadcom channel numbers (e.g., GPIO17).
- **`GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BOARD)`** → uses physical header positions (e.g., Pin 11 = GPIO17).

### 2.3 Setting Up the Programming Environment

**Step 1: OS Installation**
Flash the **Raspberry Pi Imager** tool to write **Raspberry Pi OS (64-bit)** onto a microSD card (minimum **8 GB**, recommended **32 GB Class 10**).

**Step 2: Enable Interfaces**
```bash
sudo raspi-config
# Navigate: Interface Options → Enable I2C, SPI, Serial, 1-Wire, Remote GPIO
```

**Step 3: Install Python GPIO Library**
```bash
sudo apt update
sudo apt install python3-rpi.gpio python3-gpiozero
```

**Step 4: Verify Python**
```bash
python3 --version    # Expect: Python 3.11.x or higher
```

### 2.4 Core Python Library: `RPi.GPIO`

The `RPi.GPIO` library is the **canonical library** for KTU examinations. Its API has four essential operations:

| Function | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `GPIO.setmode(mode)` | Choose `GPIO.BCM` or `GPIO.BOARD` |
| `GPIO.setup(pin, direction)` | Configure pin as `GPIO.IN` or `GPIO.OUT` |
| `GPIO.output(pin, state)` | Drive pin `GPIO.HIGH` (3.3 V) or `GPIO.LOW` (0 V) |
| `GPIO.input(pin)` | Read digital state — returns `True` (HIGH) or `False` (LOW) |
| `GPIO.cleanup()` | Reset all pins to safe state (mandatory before exit) |

### 2.5 Core Python Library: `gpiozero`

The **modern, beginner-friendly** library recommended by the Raspberry Pi Foundation:

```python
from gpiozero import LED, Button
from signal import pause

led = LED(17)            # Refers to BCM GPIO17
button = Button(27)      # Refers to BCM GPIO27

button.when_pressed = led.on
button.when_released = led.off

pause()
```

> [!NOTE]
> `gpiozero` uses **BCM numbering by default** and does **not require `cleanup()`** — making it safer for beginners. However, `RPi.GPIO` is more flexible and is **expected in KTU answer scripts**.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Standard | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| GPIO Output Voltage (HIGH) | $V_{OH} = 3.3 \text{ V}$ | Volts | **NEVER drive 5 V into a Pi GPIO** — it damages the SoC |
| GPIO Output Voltage (LOW) | $V_{OL} = 0.0 \text{ V}$ | Volts | Logic LOW is ground |
| GPIO Source/Sink Current | $I_{GPIO} \le 16 \text{ mA}$ per pin | mA | Total current across all pins $\le 50 \text{ mA}$ |
| PWM Frequency Range | $f_{PWM} = 1 \text{ Hz to } 125 \text{ MHz}$ (hardware) | Hz | Software PWM typically up to 1 kHz |
| PWM Duty Cycle | $D = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} \times 100\%$ | % | Controls average voltage / LED brightness |
| Average PWM Voltage | $V_{avg} = V_{OH} \times D$ | Volts | Linear relation with duty cycle |
| I²C Clock Frequency | $f_{SCL} \in \{100 \text{ kHz}, 400 \text{ kHz}, 1 \text{ MHz}\}$ | Hz | Standard, Fast, Fast-Plus modes |
| SPI Clock Frequency | $f_{SPI} \le 125 \text{ MHz}$ | Hz | Configurable via `spidev` |
| UART Baud Rate | $9600, 115200$ (typical) | bps | Set identically on both ends |
| LED Resistor Calculation | $R = \frac{V_{supply} - V_{LED}}{I_{LED}}$ | Ohms | $V_{LED} \approx 2.0$ V (red), 3.2 V (blue) |
| Blinky Frequency | $f = \frac{1}{2 \cdot t_{delay}}$ | Hz | For symmetric HIGH/LOW periods |
| Sampling Rate (ADC) | $f_s = \frac{1}{T_s}$ | Hz | Pi has **no native ADC** — uses external MCP3008 |

### 2.7 PWM Theory on Raspberry Pi

> [!NOTE]
> The Raspberry Pi has **two hardware PWM channels** (PWM0 on GPIO12, GPIO18; PWM1 on GPIO13, GPIO19). Other pins support **software PWM** (less precise, CPU-intensive).

For an LED with supply voltage $V_{supply} = 3.3 \text{ V}$, forward voltage $V_f = 2.0 \text{ V}$, and desired current $I_f = 10 \text{ mA}$:

$$R = \frac{V_{supply} - V_f}{I_f} = \frac{3.3 - 2.0}{0.010} = \frac{1.3}{0.010} = 130 \text{ }\Omega$$

A standard **220 Ω** resistor is typically chosen to limit current safely below 16 mA.

### 2.8 Communication Protocols Summary

| Protocol | Pins Used | Max Speed | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **UART** | TX (GPIO14), RX (GPIO15) | ~1 Mbps | Serial console, GPS, Bluetooth modules |
| **I²C** | SDA (GPIO2), SCL (GPIO3) | 1 MHz (Fast-Plus) | Sensors (BME280, MPU6050), OLED displays |
| **SPI** | MOSI (GPIO10), MISO (GPIO9), SCLK (GPIO11), CE0 (GPIO8), CE1 (GPIO7) | 125 MHz | Fast ADC, displays, SD cards |
| **1-Wire** | GPIO4 | ~16 kbps | DS18B20 temperature sensor |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: LED Blinking Logic

The simplest "Hello World" of embedded systems is **blinking an LED**. Below is the complete logical derivation from circuit to code.

**Hardware Setup:**
- LED anode → **GPIO17 (Pin 11)** via **220 Ω resistor** → LED cathode → **GND (Pin 6)**
- This is called **current-sinking** when the GPIO is LOW (LED off) and **current-sourcing** when HIGH (LED on).

**Logical Algorithm:**

```
Step 1: Import RPi.GPIO library
Step 2: Set numbering mode to BCM
Step 3: Disable GPIO warnings (best practice)
Step 4: Configure GPIO17 as OUTPUT
Step 5: Loop forever:
         a. Set GPIO17 HIGH → LED ON
         b. Wait t seconds
         c. Set GPIO17 LOW  → LED OFF
         d. Wait t seconds
Step 6: On Ctrl+C, call GPIO.cleanup() to reset pins
```

**Reference Implementation (Python 3):**

```python
import RPi.GPIO as GPIO
import time

LED_PIN = 17           # BCM GPIO17 (Physical Pin 11)
DELAY   = 1.0          # seconds

GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)
GPIO.setwarnings(False)
GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT)

try:
    while True:
        GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.HIGH)   # LED ON  (3.3 V)
        time.sleep(DELAY)
        GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.LOW)    # LED OFF (0 V)
        time.sleep(DELAY)
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    pass
finally:
    GPIO.cleanup()        # CRITICAL: resets pins to safe state
```

**Verification of Blinky Frequency:**

For a chosen $t_{delay} = 0.5 \text{ s}$, one complete HIGH+LOW cycle takes $T = 2 \times 0.5 = 1.0 \text{ s}$.

$$f_{blink} = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{1.0} = 1.0 \text{ Hz}$$

The LED therefore blinks **once per second** — a useful "heartbeat" indicator in IoT devices.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Button-Controlled LED (Digital Input)

To read a **push button** connected between **GPIO27 (Pin 13)** and **GND**, we need a **pull-up resistor** to prevent a *floating input*. Modern Raspberry Pi SoCs have **internal pull-up/pull-down resistors** that can be enabled in software.

**Hardware Setup:**
- Button → **GPIO27 (Pin 13)** and **GND (Pin 14)**
- Internal pull-up enabled → GPIO27 reads HIGH (1) when button is released
- Button pressed → GPIO27 reads LOW (0) (shorted to ground)

**Algorithm:**

```
Step 1: Configure GPIO17 as OUTPUT (LED)
Step 2: Configure GPIO27 as INPUT with internal pull-UP
Step 3: Loop forever:
         a. Read state of GPIO27
         b. If state == LOW (button pressed) → LED ON
         c. Else (button released)            → LED OFF
Step 4: Cleanup on exit
```

**Implementation:**

```python
import RPi.GPIO as GPIO
import time

LED_PIN    = 17
BUTTON_PIN = 27

GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)
GPIO.setwarnings(False)
GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT)
GPIO.setup(BUTTON_PIN, GPIO.IN, pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_UP)

try:
    while True:
        if GPIO.input(BUTTON_PIN) == GPIO.LOW:
            GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.HIGH)   # Button pressed → LED ON
        else:
            GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.LOW)    # Button released → LED OFF
        time.sleep(0.05)                      # Debounce delay
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    pass
finally:
    GPIO.cleanup()
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **50 ms debounce delay** is critical. Mechanical buttons "bounce" (oscillate between HIGH/LOW for ~5–20 ms) when pressed. Without debounce, a single press can register as 5–10 events.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: PWM-Based LED Brightness Control

To control LED brightness, we vary the **duty cycle** of a PWM signal on a pin that supports hardware or software PWM.

**Duty Cycle Derivation:**

Suppose we want the LED to appear at **60% brightness**. We set duty cycle $D = 60\%$.

PWM Period at default frequency $f = 800 \text{ Hz}$:

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{800} = 1.25 \text{ ms}$$

Therefore:

$$T_{ON} = D \times T = 0.60 \times 1.25 \text{ ms} = 0.75 \text{ ms}$$

$$T_{OFF} = T - T_{ON} = 1.25 - 0.75 = 0.50 \text{ ms}$$

Average output voltage:

$$V_{avg} = V_{OH} \times D = 3.3 \times 0.60 = 1.98 \text{ V}$$

**Implementation:**

```python
import RPi.GPIO as GPIO

LED_PIN = 18           # GPIO18 supports hardware PWM0
FREQ    = 800          # Hz

GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)
GPIO.setwarnings(False)
GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT)

pwm = GPIO.PWM(LED_PIN, FREQ)
pwm.start(0)           # Start with 0% duty cycle

try:
    while True:
        for duty in range(0, 101, 5):     # Ramp UP: 0% → 100%
            pwm.ChangeDutyCycle(duty)
            time.sleep(0.05)
        for duty in range(100, -1, -5):   # Ramp DOWN: 100% → 0%
            pwm.ChangeDutyCycle(duty)
            time.sleep(0.05)
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    pass
finally:
    pwm.stop()
    GPIO.cleanup()
```

### 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation: Reading an I²C Sensor (BME280 Example)

The **BME280** is a popular temperature/humidity/pressure sensor that communicates over **I²C** at address **0x76** (or 0x77). It requires the `smbus2` library.

**I²C Address Configuration:**

The Pi's I²C bus is exposed as `/dev/i2c-1`. Each slave device on the bus has a **7-bit address**.

| Hex Address | Decimal | Device Type |
|---|---|---|
| 0x76 | 118 | BME280 (SDO pin to GND) |
| 0x77 | 119 | BME280 (SDO pin to VDD) |
| 0x68 | 104 | MPU6050 IMU |

**Read Sequence:**

```
Step 1: Open I²C bus: bus = SMBus(1)
Step 2: Send configuration commands:
         - Write 0x01 to register 0xF4 (humidity oversampling)
         - Write 0x01 to register 0xF5 (config register)
Step 3: Burst-read 8 bytes from register 0xF7 (data block)
Step 4: Apply calibration coefficients (stored in registers 0x88-0xA1, 0xE1-0xE7)
Step 5: Convert raw ADC readings to engineering units:
         T (°C) = (raw_T / 5120.0) - calibration_offset
         P (hPa) = raw_P / 256.0
         H (%) = raw_H / 1024.0
```

**Implementation Sketch:**

```python
from smbus2 import SMBus
import time

I2C_ADDR = 0x76
bus = SMBus(1)

bus.write_byte_data(I2C_ADDR, 0xF4, 0x01)   # Set humidity oversampling
bus.write_byte_data(I2C_ADDR, 0xF5, 0x01)   # Set config

time.sleep(0.5)

raw = bus.read_i2c_block_data(I2C_ADDR, 0xF7, 8)
# raw[0:3] = pressure, raw[3:5] = temperature, raw[6:8] = humidity

print(f"Raw bytes: {raw}")
```

### 3.5 Exhaustive Derivation: Multi-Tasking IoT Program

A common KTU exam question asks how to implement a **multi-task IoT program** that **simultaneously** reads a sensor, controls an actuator, and publishes data to the cloud. The solution is **Python `threading`**.

**Threading Model:**

```
Main Thread  ──→  Spawns Worker Threads:
   │
   ├── Thread-1: Sensor Reader   (every 1 s)
   ├── Thread-2: LED Heartbeat   (every 0.5 s)
   └── Thread-3: Cloud Publisher (every 5 s)
```

**Implementation:**

```python
import RPi.GPIO as GPIO
import threading, time, requests

LED_PIN    = 17
SENSOR_PIN = 27

data_lock = threading.Lock()
shared_value = 0

def sensor_thread():
    global shared_value
    while True:
        with data_lock:
            shared_value += 1
        time.sleep(1)

def led_thread():
    GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)
    GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT)
    while True:
        GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.HIGH)
        time.sleep(0.25)
        GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.LOW)
        time.sleep(0.25)

def publish_thread():
    while True:
        with data_lock:
            payload = {"counter": shared_value}
        try:
            requests.post("https://example.com/api/data", json=payload)
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"Publish error: {e}")
        time.sleep(5)

t1 = threading.Thread(target=sensor_thread, daemon=True)
t2 = threading.Thread(target=led_thread, daemon=True)
t3 = threading.Thread(target=publish_thread, daemon=True)

t1.start()
t2.start()
t3.start()

while True:
    time.sleep(1)
```

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Library Selection Guide

| Scenario | Recommended Library | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Quick prototype, simple LED/button | `gpiozero` | High-level, no cleanup needed |
| Exam-style code, explicit control | `RPi.GPIO` | Industry standard, KTU-expected |
| Real-time PWM / servo control | `pigpio` | Hardware-timed pulses, remote GPIO |
| Legacy projects (pre-2016) | `WiringPi` | Deprecated but still used in textbooks |
