| Performance-critical (C code) | `WiringPi` (C) / `libgpiod` | Direct register access, no GIL bottleneck |
| I²C device communication | `smbus2` | Pythonic wrapper over Linux `i2c-dev` |
| SPI device communication | `spidev` | Standard Linux SPI user-space API |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Raspberry Pi Software Stack — Layered Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Application Layer: Python C++ Java NodeJS"] --> B["Library Layer: RPi.GPIO gpiozero pigpio smbus2 spidev"]
    B --> C["Device Files: /dev/gpiomem /dev/i2c-1 /dev/spidev0.0 /dev/ttyAMA0"]
    C --> D["Linux Kernel: GPIO Subsystem I2C Subsystem SPI Subsystem UART Driver"]
    D --> E["Hardware: BCM2711 SoC GPIO Controller I2C Controller SPI Controller UART"]
    E --> F["External Devices: LEDs Buttons Sensors Actuators Displays"]
```

### 4.2 GPIO Programming Workflow — Setup to Cleanup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step 1: Import RPi.GPIO and time"] --> B["Step 2: setmode BCM or BOARD"]
    B --> C["Step 3: setwarnings False"]
    C --> D["Step 4: setup pin IN or OUT"]
    D --> E{"Pin Direction"}
    E -->|OUTPUT| F["Drive pin HIGH or LOW"]
    E -->|INPUT| G["Read pin state"]
    F --> H["Sleep or Process"]
    G --> H
    H --> I{"Continue Loop?"}
    I -->|Yes| E
    I -->|No KeyboardInterrupt| J["GPIO.cleanup"]
    J --> K["Exit Safely"]
```

### 4.3 LED + Button + PWM IoT Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENSOR["Input Section"]
        BTN["Push Button on GPIO27"]
    end
    subgraph PROCESSING["Raspberry Pi Python Program"]
        CODE["Main Loop with RPi.GPIO"]
        PWM["PWM Generator on GPIO18"]
    end
    subgraph ACTUATOR["Output Section"]
        LED1["Status LED on GPIO17"]
        LED2["Brightness LED on GPIO18"]
    end
    BTN -->|Digital LOW on press| CODE
    CODE -->|Digital HIGH or LOW| LED1
    CODE -->|PWM signal with duty cycle D| PWM
    PWM -->|Variable brightness| LED2
```

### 4.4 Multi-Threaded IoT Program Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MAIN["Main Thread main.py startup"] --> T1["Thread 1: sensor_reader - 1s interval"]
    MAIN --> T2["Thread 2: led_heartbeat - 0.5s interval"]
    MAIN --> T3["Thread 3: cloud_publisher - 5s interval"]
    MAIN --> JOIN["Main Thread join sleep loop"]
    T1 --> SHARED["Shared Data with Lock"]
    T2 --> SHARED
    T3 --> SHARED
    SHARED --> T3
    JOIN --> MAIN
```

### 4.5 I²C Communication Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PI as Raspberry Pi Master
    participant SENSOR as BME280 Sensor Slave
    PI->>PI: Open SMBus(1)
    PI->>SENSOR: Write config to register 0xF4
    PI->>SENSOR: Write config to register 0xF5
    Note over PI: Wait 500 ms for conversion
    PI->>SENSOR: Burst read 8 bytes from 0xF7
    SENSOR-->>PI: Raw ADC bytes
    PI->>PI: Apply calibration coefficients
    PI->>PI: Convert to T C and P hPa
    PI-->>PI: Print or publish to cloud
```

### 4.6 Program Execution Lifecycle on Raspberry Pi

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Boot: Power On
    Boot --> Kernel: Load Linux Kernel from SD card
    Kernel --> Init: Mount root filesystem
    Init --> Services: Start systemd services
    Services --> User: Login or auto-start program
    User --> Idle: Program not running
    User --> Run: python3 myprogram.py
    Run --> Active: GPIO setup and main loop
    Active --> Active: Process I/O events
    Active --> Cleanup: Ctrl+C or exit
    Cleanup --> [*]: GPIO.cleanup pins reset
```

### 4.7 Decision Matrix — Library Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1{"Need hardware-timed PWM?"}
    Q1 -->|Yes| PIGPIO["Use pigpio library"]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{"Writing exam or quick prototype?"}
    Q2 -->|Exam| RPIGPIO["Use RPi.GPIO - KTU standard"]
    Q2 -->|Quick prototype| GPIOZERO["Use gpiozero - beginner friendly"]
    PIGPIO --> DONE["Selected"]
    RPIGPIO --> DONE
    GPIOZERO --> DONE
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the difference between BCM and BOARD pin numbering schemes on the Raspberry Pi. Why is it important to set the numbering mode explicitly in every program?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Raspberry Pi 40-pin GPIO header can be referenced in **two ways**:

1. **BCM (Broadcom SOC Channel) Numbering** — uses the **internal GPIO channel numbers** of the BCM2837/BCM2711 SoC (e.g., `GPIO17`).
2. **BOARD Numbering** — uses the **physical position** of the pin on the header (e.g., `Pin 11` for GPIO17).

It is important to set the numbering mode explicitly because **BCM Pin 17 corresponds to Physical Pin 11**, and confusing them can lead to driving the **wrong pin** — potentially short-circuiting hardware. The standard practice is:

```python
GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)   # or GPIO.BOARD
GPIO.setwarnings(False)  # suppress "channel already in use" warnings
```

Always documenting the chosen scheme in comments prevents ambiguity and debugging errors in IoT applications. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the role of `GPIO.cleanup()` in a Raspberry Pi Python program? What happens if it is omitted?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

`GPIO.cleanup()` is a function in the `RPi.GPIO` library that **resets all GPIO pins used by the program back to their default input state** with no pull-up/pull-down resistors. This prevents the pins from remaining in a **last-set state** (HIGH or LOW) after the program terminates, which could:

- Keep an LED permanently on, draining power.
- Leave an output pin driving a circuit unintentionally, potentially damaging components.
- Cause a "channel already in use" warning when the program is re-run.

Best practice is to place `GPIO.cleanup()` inside a `finally:` block so it executes even if the program is interrupted by `Ctrl+C` (KeyboardInterrupt). **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **step-by-step procedure** to set up a Raspberry Pi for GPIO programming using the Python language. Include OS installation, enabling interfaces, installing libraries, and verifying the setup.

**(b) [7 marks]** Write a complete Python program using `RPi.GPIO` to **read a push button** connected to **GPIO27** and **toggle an LED** connected to **GPIO17** accordingly. Use internal pull-up resistors and include proper cleanup.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Step-by-Step Raspberry Pi Setup for GPIO Programming:**

| Step | Action | Command / Detail |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Hardware Assembly** | Insert microSD (≥8 GB) into Pi, connect HDMI, keyboard, mouse, 5V/3A power supply. | Use official **Raspberry Pi 15W USB-C** supply for Pi 4. |
| **2. OS Flashing** | Download **Raspberry Pi Imager** from `raspberrypi.com`. Select **Raspberry Pi OS (64-bit)** and write to SD card. | Optional: pre-configure Wi-Fi, SSH, username. |
| **3. First Boot** | Insert SD, power on, complete OS setup wizard (locale, password, Wi-Fi). | Update password from default `raspberry`. |
| **4. Update System** | Open terminal and run: `sudo apt update && sudo apt full-upgrade -y` | Keeps kernel and packages current. |
| **5. Enable Interfaces** | Run `sudo raspi-config` → `Interface Options` → Enable **I2C, SPI, Serial, 1-Wire, Remote GPIO**. | Reboot after enabling. |
| **6. Install GPIO Library** | `sudo apt install python3-rpi.gpio python3-gpiozero python3-smbus2 python3-spidev` | This covers 90% of IoT programming needs. |
| **7. Verify Python** | `python3 --version` should show **Python 3.11+**. | `python3` opens REPL. |
| **8. Test GPIO Access** | Run a test script to blink an LED on GPIO17. | Confirms user is in `gpio` group. |

If the test fails with `RuntimeError: No access to /dev/gpiomem`, add the user to the `gpio` group:

```bash
sudo usermod -aG gpio $USER
sudo reboot
```

**Marks Distribution (7 marks):**
- OS installation and imaging → **1.5 marks**
- `raspi-config` interface enabling → **1.5 marks**
- Library installation commands → **2 marks**
- Verification and user permissions → **2 marks**

**(b) Python Program: Button-Controlled LED Toggle**

```python
import RPi.GPIO as GPIO
import time

# ---------- Pin Configuration (BCM Numbering) ----------
LED_PIN    = 17      # BCM GPIO17 → Physical Pin 11
BUTTON_PIN = 27      # BCM GPIO27 → Physical Pin 13
DEBOUNCE   = 0.20    # Debounce delay in seconds

# ---------- GPIO Setup ----------
GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)
GPIO.setwarnings(False)

GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT)
GPIO.setup(BUTTON_PIN, GPIO.IN, pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_UP)

# ---------- State Variable ----------
led_state = False    # Tracks current LED status

print("Button-Controlled LED Program Started.")
print("Press CTRL+C to exit.\n")

# ---------- Main Loop ----------
try:
    while True:
        if GPIO.input(BUTTON_PIN) == GPIO.LOW:    # Button pressed
            time.sleep(DEBOUNCE)                  # Debounce
            if GPIO.input(BUTTON_PIN) == GPIO.LOW:
                led_state = not led_state         # Toggle
                GPIO.output(LED_PIN, led_state)
                print(f"LED is now {'ON' if led_state else 'OFF'}")
                while GPIO.input(BUTTON_PIN) == GPIO.LOW:
                    time.sleep(0.01)              # Wait for release
except KeyboardInterrupt:
    print("\nProgram interrupted by user.")
finally:
    GPIO.cleanup()
    print("GPIO cleaned up. Exiting safely.")
```

**Explanation of Code Logic:**
1. `GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)` — selects BCM numbering for clarity.
2. `pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_UP` — enables internal pull-up; pin reads HIGH when button is open.
3. Button press → pin pulled to GND → reads LOW → toggles `led_state`.
4. The inner `while` loop waits for button release to prevent multiple toggles from one press.
5. `finally:` ensures `GPIO.cleanup()` runs even on `Ctrl+C`.

**Marks Distribution (7 marks):**
- Correct library imports and pin setup → **2 marks**
- Pull-up configuration and input reading → **2 marks**
- Toggle logic with debounce → **2 marks**
- `try/except/finally` cleanup block → **1 mark**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **four key communication protocols** supported by the Raspberry Pi GPIO header: **UART, I²C, SPI, and 1-Wire**. Compare their pin usage, speed, and typical IoT applications.

**(b) [7 marks]** Write a Python program to **read temperature data** from a **DS18B20 1-Wire temperature sensor** connected to **GPIO4** and print the value every 2 seconds. Include the necessary kernel module loading and device file reading steps.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Comparison of Raspberry Pi Communication Protocols:**

| Protocol | Pins Used (BCM) | No. of Wires | Max Speed | Topology | Typical IoT Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **UART** | TX: GPIO14, RX: GPIO15 | 2 (+ GND) | ~1 Mbps | Point-to-point | GPS modules, Bluetooth HC-05, serial console |
| **I²C** | SDA: GPIO2, SCL: GPIO3 | 2 (+ GND, +V) | 1 MHz (Fast-Plus) | Multi-master, multi-slave (up to 127 devices) | Sensors (BME280, MPU6050), OLED displays, EEPROM |
| **SPI** | MOSI: GPIO10, MISO: GPIO9, SCLK: GPIO11, CE0: GPIO8, CE1: GPIO7 | 4 (+ GND) | 125 MHz | Master-slave, multiple chip-selects | Fast ADC (MCP3008), SD cards, TFT displays |
| **1-Wire** | DQ: GPIO4 | 1 (+ GND) | ~16 kbps | Single master, multi-slave (up to 100 devices with unique 64-bit ROM IDs) | DS18B20 temperature, iButton, identification |

**Key Distinctions:**
- **UART** is asynchronous (no shared clock); both ends must agree on baud rate.
- **I²C** uses only 2 wires but requires pull-up resistors (4.7 kΩ typical).
- **SPI** is the **fastest** of the four, ideal for displays and high-throughput sensors.
- **1-Wire** uses the **fewest wires**, making it ideal for long-distance, low-speed sensing.

**Marks Distribution (7 marks):**
- Four protocols with correct pin mappings → **3 marks**
- Speed and topology comparison → **2 marks**
- IoT application examples → **2 marks**

**(b) Python Program: DS18B20 1-Wire Temperature Sensor**

**Step 1: Enable 1-Wire Interface**
```bash
sudo raspi-config
# Interface Options → 1-Wire → Enable
sudo reboot
```

**Step 2: Identify Device File**
```bash
ls /sys/bus/w1/devices/
# Output: 28-00000xxxxxxx  (28- prefix = DS18B20 family code)
```

**Step 3: Read Raw Sensor Data**
```bash
cat /sys/bus/w1/devices/28-00000xxxxxxx/w1_slave
# Returns: crc yes\n t=23187  (temperature in milli-Celsius)
```

**Step 4: Python Program**

```python
import time
import os

# ---------- Configuration ----------
DEVICE_FOLDER = "/sys/bus/w1/devices/"
SLAVE_FILE    = "w1_slave"
SCAN_INTERVAL = 2     # seconds

# ---------- Discover DS18B20 Devices ----------
def find_ds18b20():
    devices = []
    for entry in os.listdir(DEVICE_FOLDER):
        if entry.startswith("28-"):           # DS18B20 family code
            devices.append(entry)
    return devices

# ---------- Read Temperature ----------
def read_temperature(device_id):
    device_file = DEVICE_FOLDER + device_id + "/" + SLAVE_FILE
    with open(device_file, "r") as f:
        lines = f.readlines()
    
    # Verify CRC
    if lines[0].strip()[-3:] != "YES":
        raise RuntimeError("CRC check failed for sensor " + device_id)
    
    # Extract t= value
    equals_pos = lines[1].find("t=")
    if equals_pos != -1:
        temp_string = lines[1][equals_pos + 2:]
        temp_celsius = float(temp_string) / 1000.0
        return temp_celsius
    else:
        raise RuntimeError("Temperature data not found.")

# ---------- Main Program ----------
def main():
    devices = find_ds18b20()
    if not devices:
        print("No DS18B20 sensors detected. Check wiring.")
        return
    
    print(f"Found {len(devices)} sensor(s): {devices}")
    print("Reading temperature every 2 seconds. Press CTRL+C to stop.\n")
    
    try:
        while True:
            for dev in devices:
                temp = read_temperature(dev)
                temp_f = (temp * 9.0 / 5.0) + 32.0
                print(f"[{dev}] {temp:.2f} °C  |  {temp_f:.2f} °F")
            print("-" * 50)
            time.sleep(SCAN_INTERVAL)
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        print("\nExiting program.")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

**Output Example:**
```
Found 1 sensor(s): ['28-00000abc1234']
Reading temperature every 2 seconds. Press CTRL+C to stop.

[28-00000abc1234] 24.31 °C  |  75.76 °F
--------------------------------------------------
[28-00000abc1234] 24.37 °C  |  75.87 °F
```

**Marks Distribution (7 marks):**
- 1-Wire interface enabling and device discovery → **2 marks**
- CRC verification logic → **2 marks**
- Temperature parsing and main loop → **2 marks**
- Multi-sensor support and clean output → **1 mark**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Missing `GPIO.cleanup()`** — Examiners explicitly check for it. Without it, the GPIO pins remain in the last state, which is a **-1 mark penalty**.
> 2. **No `try/except/finally` Block** — A program that crashes without graceful exit handling loses **1–2 marks** on robustness.
> 3. **Forgetting to set pin numbering mode** — `GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)` is **mandatory**; using bare pin numbers without mode is a syntax error.
> 4. **Wrong library import** — Using `from RPi import GPIO` (incorrect) instead of `import RPi.GPIO as GPIO` (correct) results in **0 marks** for the code section.
> 5. **5 V into GPIO** — Never connect 5 V signals directly to Pi GPIO pins. Use a **level shifter** or **voltage divider** for 5 V devices.
> 6. **No comments in code** — Code without `#` comments explaining pin numbers and logic loses marks on "documentation" criteria.
> 7. **Confusing PWM pins** — Only GPIO18 (PWM0) and GPIO19 (PWM1) are hardware PWM by default. Other pins use software PWM (less accurate).
> 8. **I²C address mistakes** — Always verify the I²C address with `sudo i2cdetect -y 1` before writing code. Wrong address = `OSError` at runtime.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Programming on Raspberry Pi**
> 
> **Architecture & Setup:**
> - Raspberry Pi is a **Linux-based SBC** (not a microcontroller like Arduino).
> - Default OS: **Raspberry Pi OS (64-bit, Debian Bookworm)**.
> - **40-pin GPIO header**: 26 usable GPIO + power/ground pins.
> - GPIO logic levels: **3.3 V** (NOT 5 V tolerant).
> - Max current per pin: **16 mA**; total across all pins: **50 mA**.
> 
> **Pin Numbering:**
> - **BCM mode**: uses SoC channel numbers (e.g., GPIO17).
> - **BOARD mode**: uses physical pin position (e.g., Pin 11 = GPIO17).
> - Always call `GPIO.setmode()` first.
> 
> **Libraries:**
> - `RPi.GPIO` — standard, KTU-expected, requires `cleanup()`.
> - `gpiozero` — modern, beginner-friendly, BCM-only, no cleanup needed.
> - `pigpio` — hardware-timed PWM, supports remote GPIO over network.
> - `smbus2` — I²C communication.
> - `spidev` — SPI communication.
> 
> **Protocols:**
> - **UART**: GPIO14 (TX), GPIO15 (RX); baud-rate dependent.
> - **I²C**: GPIO2 (SDA), GPIO3 (SCL); 7-bit addresses; 4.7 kΩ pull-ups.
> - **SPI**: GPIO9-11 + CE0/CE1; fastest protocol on Pi.
> - **1-Wire**: GPIO4; DS18B20 family code starts with `28-`.
> 
> **PWM:**
> - Hardware PWM on **GPIO18** and **GPIO19** only.
> - Duty cycle $D = T_{ON} / T_{period} \times 100\%$.
> - Average voltage $V_{avg} = V_{OH} \times D$.
> 
> **Best Practices:**
> - Use `try/except/finally` with `GPIO.cleanup()` in the `finally` block.
> - Use internal pull-ups with `GPIO.PUD_UP` or `GPIO.PUD_DOWN`.
> - Debounce mechanical buttons with 20–50 ms delays.
> - Comment pin numbers and modes at the top of every script.
> - For IoT, use `threading` for concurrent sensing/actuation/cloud publishing.
> - Never drive 5 V into a 3.3 V GPIO pin — use a level shifter.
> 
> **Key Formulas:**
> - LED resistor: $R = (V_{supply} - V_{LED}) / I_{LED}$.
> - Blinky frequency: $f = 1 / (2 \times t_{delay})$.
> - PWM average: $V_{avg} = V_{OH} \times D$.
> - Sampling rate: $f_s = 1 / T_s$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Python Basics for Raspberry Pi
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT503 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Python Basics for Raspberry Pi

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Python for Raspberry Pi** refers to the implementation of the **Python 3.x** high-level, interpreted, general-purpose programming language on the **Raspberry Pi Single Board Computer (SBC)** running a Linux-based OS (typically **Raspberry Pi OS**, formerly Raspbian), leveraging its rich standard library and hardware-specific modules (notably `RPi.GPIO`, `gpiozero`, and `picamera2`) to interface with the board's **GPIO (General Purpose Input/Output)** pins, sensors, and actuators for IoT prototyping.

Python 3 is the **de facto standard language** for Raspberry Pi programming because of:
1. **Readable Syntax** – Minimal use of braces (`{}`) and semicolons (`;`).
2. **Dynamic Typing** – Variables do not require explicit type declaration.
3. **Interpreted Execution** – No compilation step; the interpreter parses and executes line-by-line.
4. **Extensive Standard Library** – Modules for file I/O, networking, math, and OS interaction.
5. **Hardware Abstraction Libraries** – `RPi.GPIO`, `gpiozero`, `smbus2`, `spidev`, etc.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — "Python on Raspberry Pi is like giving a Calculator a Pair of Hands and Ears"**

Imagine the Raspberry Pi's **ARM Cortex processor** is a brilliant mathematician who can only solve equations on paper (pure computation). Without Python and its GPIO libraries, this mathematician is deaf and paralyzed — it cannot **sense** the real world (read sensors) nor **act** upon it (control motors/LEDs).

**Python** acts as the **translator + nervous system**:
- **Interpreter (python3)** = The translator that converts human-readable English-like commands (Python syntax) into machine code the ARM CPU understands.
- **GPIO Libraries** = The "hands and ears" – they physically connect software instructions to the **40-pin GPIO header** on the Pi, allowing it to read a button press (input) or blink an LED (output).

So, when you write `GPIO.output(17, GPIO.HIGH)`, you are essentially **whispering** to the mathematician: "Send 3.3V out of Pin 17", and the Pi complies within microseconds.

---

## 1.3 Why Python Was Chosen for Raspberry Pi

| Feature | Engineering Benefit for IoT |
|---|---|
| **REPL (Read-Eval-Print Loop)** | Rapid prototyping – test code live via SSH/terminal |
| **Batteries-included** philosophy | Built-in `os`, `time`, `socket`, `json` modules |
| **GPIO & I²C/SPI/UART support** | Direct hardware control without writing C drivers |
| **Cross-platform** | Same script runs on Pi 3, Pi 4, Pi 5, Pi Zero |
| **Vast Community** | Millions of tutorials, libraries, and StackOverflow answers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant / Standard Metric**:
> - Raspberry Pi GPIO Logic Level = **3.3 V** (NOT 5V — applying 5V damages the SoC permanently)
> - Python 3 official version on Raspberry Pi OS (Bookworm, 2024) = **Python 3.11.x**
> - IDLE (Integrated Development and Learning Environment) is bundled by default.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Python Language Fundamentals for Raspberry Pi

### 2.1.1 Variables and Dynamic Typing
Python variables are **references (pointers)** to objects in memory. Type is determined at runtime.

```python
led_pin = 17            # int
temperature = 23.5      # float
device_name = "Pi_4B"   # str
is_active = True        # bool
sensor_reading = None   # NoneType (sentinel)
```

### 2.1.2 Core Data Types

| Data Type | Example | Mutable? | Use in Raspberry Pi |
|---|---|---|---|
| `int` | `42` | No | Pin numbers, counters |
| `float` | `3.1415` | No | ADC readings, temperature |
| `str` | `"Hello"` | No | Logging, MQTT payloads |
| `bool` | `True` / `False` | No | Conditional logic |
| `list` | `[1, 2, 3]` | Yes | Sensor buffer |
| `tuple` | `(17, 27, 22)` | No | Pin configuration tuples |
| `dict` | `{"temp": 25}` | Yes | JSON IoT payloads |
| `set` | `{1, 2, 3}` | Yes | Unique device IDs |
| `bytes` | `b'\x00\xFF'` | No | I²C/SPI raw register data |

---

## 2.2 Operators and Expressions

### 2.2.1 Arithmetic Operators
Python supports standard mathematical operators used heavily in sensor data processing:

$$ a + b \quad (\text{Addition})$$
$$ a - b \quad (\text{Subtraction})$$
$$ a \times b \quad (\text{Multiplication})$$
$$ a \; / \; b \quad (\text{True Division — always returns float})$$
$$ a \; // \; b \quad (\text{Floor Division — discards remainder})$$
$$ a \; \% \; b \quad (\text{Modulo — useful for cyclic GPIO patterns})$$
$$ a \; ** \; b \quad (\text{Exponentiation})$$

### 2.2.2 Comparison & Logical Operators
- **Comparison**: `==`, `!=`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=`
- **Logical**: `and`, `or`, `not`
- **Bitwise**: `&`, `|`, `^`, `~`, `<<`, `>>` — critical for **GPIO register-level manipulation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Bitwise Operators Matter in Pi Programming**:
> When controlling multiple GPIO pins in one write operation, you OR the pin masks together. E.g., setting pins 17 AND 27 simultaneously:
> `GPIO.output(17 | 27, GPIO.HIGH)` — this is a real-world engineering use of bitwise OR.

---

## 2.3 Control Flow Structures

### 2.3.1 Conditional Statements
```python
if temperature > 30.0:
    fan.ON()
elif temperature < 15.0:
    heater.ON()
else:
    fan.OFF()
    heater.OFF()
```

### 2.3.2 Loops
```python
# For loop — iterate over GPIO pin list
for pin in [17, 27, 22]:
    GPIO.setup(pin, GPIO.OUT)
    GPIO.output(pin, GPIO.HIGH)

# While loop — continuous sensor polling
while True:
    reading = read_sensor()
    if reading > THRESHOLD:
        break
    time.sleep(0.1)
```

### 2.3.3 Loop Control Keywords
- `break` — terminates the innermost loop.
- `continue` — skips remaining statements, jumps to next iteration.
- `pass` — null operation; acts as a syntactic placeholder.

---

## 2.4 Functions and Modular Programming

Functions in Python are defined using the `def` keyword. Raspberry Pi scripts benefit enormously from modular design — separating GPIO control, networking, and main logic.

```python
def read_temperature(sensor_pin):
    """Returns temperature in Celsius from an analog sensor."""
    raw = adc.read_adc(sensor_pin)
    voltage = raw * 3.3 / 1023.0          # 10-bit ADC mapping
    temperature = (voltage - 0.5) * 100   # LM35 formula
    return round(temperature, 2)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lambda Functions** — Anonymous single-expression functions used in `gpiozero` callbacks:
> ```python
> led = LED(17)
> button = Button(27)
> button.when_pressed = lambda: led.on()
> button.when_released = lambda: led.off()
> ```

---

## 2.5 File I/O for IoT Data Logging

IoT applications frequently log sensor data to local files for later upload.

```python
# Writing sensor data to a CSV log file
import datetime

with open("sensor_log.csv", "a") as f:
    timestamp = datetime.datetime.now().isoformat()
    f.write(f"{timestamp},{temperature},{humidity}\n")
```

The `with` statement guarantees the file handle is properly closed even if an exception occurs — this is **Context-Managed I/O**, a Python best practice.

---

## 2.6 Exception Handling in Hardware Programming

GPIO operations can fail (loose wires, short circuits, missing sensors). Robust Pi code MUST use `try-except-finally` blocks:

```python
import RPi.GPIO as GPIO

try:
    GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)
    GPIO.setup(17, GPIO.OUT)
    GPIO.output(17, GPIO.HIGH)
except RuntimeError as e:
    print(f"GPIO error: {e}")
finally:
    GPIO.cleanup()   # CRITICAL — resets all pins to safe state
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Real-World Engineering Consequence**: Forgetting `GPIO.cleanup()` leaves pins in their last state. If an LED was on, it stays on. If a motor was driving, it keeps driving — potentially causing damage when the script exits. **Always call `GPIO.cleanup()` in `finally`**.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Construct | Syntax | Example | Real-World Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Variable | `name = value` | `pin = 17` | Store pin number |
| Print | `print(x)` | `print("Temp:", t)` | Debug sensor values |
| Input | `input("prompt")` | `cmd = input(">>")` | REPL-style control |
| List | `[a, b, c]` | `pins = [17, 27]` | Multi-pin control |
| Dict | `{key: val}` | `{"id": "pi01"}` | JSON-MQTT payload |
| For loop | `for x in seq:` | `for i in range(5):` | Iterate 5 times |
| While | `while cond:` | `while running:` | Event loop |
| Function | `def f(x):` | `def read(pin):` | Modular code |
| Try/except | `try: ... except:` | `try: GPIO.output(...)` | Hardware safety |
| Import | `import mod` | `import RPi.GPIO as GPIO` | Load GPIO lib |
| With | `with open(...) as f:` | `with open("log") as f:` | Safe file I/O |
| Slice | `lst[a:b]` | `pins[0:2]` | Sub-list access |
| F-string | `f"{var}"` | `f"Pin {p} = HIGH"` | Formatted logging |

---

## 2.8 Why This Knowledge Matters in IoT Engineering

In professional IoT product development, Python on the Raspberry Pi is the **edge-computing layer**:
- It reads sensors via I²C/SPI/GPIO.
- It performs local analytics (e.g., moving average filtering of noisy data).
- It pushes data to the cloud via **MQTT** (`paho-mqtt` library) or **HTTP** (`requests` library).
- It runs an **MQTT broker** (Mosquitto) for local network control.

A solid grasp of Python basics is therefore **non-negotiable** for any IoT engineer working on Raspberry Pi-based gateways.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Numerical Example: Analog-to-Digital Conversion Mapping

This is one of the **most common computational problems** in Raspberry Pi interfacing — converting a 10-bit ADC reading (0 to 1023) into a real-world physical quantity like voltage or temperature.

### Problem Statement
A Raspberry Pi is connected to an **MCP3008** 10-bit ADC via SPI. The ADC reads an **LM35** temperature sensor whose output voltage is **linearly proportional** to temperature with a scale factor of **10 mV/°C** (i.e., 0.5 V at 50°C, 0 V at 0°C, though in practice LM35 has a 0.5 V offset).

**Given**:
- ADC resolution: $n = 10$ bits
- Reference voltage: $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V
- Raw ADC value: $D = 512$

**Derive**:
1. The measured voltage $V_{in}$.
2. The temperature in °C.

---

### Step 1: Recall the General ADC Transfer Function

An ADC converts an analog input voltage into a discrete digital code. The fundamental equation is:

$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^n - 1) \right\rfloor$$

Rearranging for $V_{in}$:

$$V_{in} = \frac{D}{2^n - 1} \cdot V_{ref}$$

This is the **inverse transfer function** of the ADC.

---

### Step 2: Substitute the Known Values

We are given:
- $D = 512$
- $n = 10$, so $2^{10} = 1024$, hence $2^n - 1 = 1023$
- $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V

Plugging into the equation:

$$V_{in} = \frac{512}{1023} \cdot 3.3 \text{ V}$$

---

### Step 3: Compute the Numerical Result

$$\frac{512}{1023} = 0.500488759...$$

Multiplying:

$$V_{in} = 0.500488759 \times 3.3 = 1.6516... \text{ V}$$

Rounding to 4 decimal places:

$$\boxed{V_{in} \approx 1.6516 \text{ V}}$$

---

### Step 4: Apply the LM35 Sensor Equation

The LM35 outputs a voltage proportional to Celsius temperature:

$$T(^{\circ}C) = \frac{V_{in}}{10 \text{ mV/}^{\circ}C} = \frac{V_{in}}{0.01 \text{ V/}^{\circ}C}$$

(Note: Some LM35 variants require a $-0.5$ V offset adjustment. We assume the **standard** non-offset model here.)

Substituting $V_{in} = 1.6516$ V:

$$T = \frac{1.6516}{0.01} = 165.16^{\circ}C$$

---

### Step 5: Sanity Check

The LM35 is rated for **2°C to 150°C** in the basic TO-92 package. A reading of 165°C is **outside the operating range**, suggesting either:
1. The sensor is the **high-temperature** LM35H variant (rated up to 200°C), OR
2. The reference voltage $V_{ref}$ is incorrect (LM35 cannot source 1.65 V at room temperature).

This sanity check teaches a **critical engineering lesson**: numerical results must always be cross-validated against physical constraints.

---

### Step 6: Full Python Implementation on Raspberry Pi

```python
import spidev

# Initialize SPI bus for MCP3008
spi = spidev.SpiDev()
spi.open(0, 0)            # Bus 0, Device 0 (CE0)
spi.max_speed_hz = 1350000

def read_adc(channel):
    """Read MCP3008 ADC channel (0-7). Returns 10-bit integer 0-1023."""
    if channel < 0 or channel > 7:
        raise ValueError("Channel must be 0-7")
    adc = spi.xfer2([1, (8 + channel) << 4, 0])
    raw = ((adc[1] & 3) << 8) + adc[2]
    return raw

def voltage_to_temperature(adc_value, vref=3.3, bits=10):
    """Convert raw ADC value to temperature in Celsius for LM35."""
    max_val = (1 << bits) - 1   # Equivalent to 2^n - 1
    vin = (adc_value / max_val) * vref
    temperature = vin / 0.01
    return vin, temperature

# Main loop
if __name__ == "__main__":
    for _ in range(5):
        raw = read_adc(channel=0)
        v, t = voltage_to_temperature(raw)
        print(f"Raw={raw}  V_in={v:.4f} V  T={t:.2f} °C")
```

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Operator Precedence (High-Yield for KTU)

When Python evaluates expressions like `GPIO.output(17 | 27, GPIO.HIGH)`, the order of operations is critical.

| Precedence | Operator | Description | Associativity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (Highest) | `()` | Parentheses | Left-to-right |
| 2 | `**` | Exponentiation | Right-to-left |
| 3 | `+x`, `-x`, `~x` | Unary | Right-to-left |
| 4 | `*`, `/`, `//`, `%` | Multiplicative | Left-to-right |
| 5 | `+`, `-` | Additive | Left-to-right |
| 6 | `<<`, `>>` | Shifts | Left-to-right |
| 7 | `&` | Bitwise AND | Left-to-right |
| 8 | `^` | Bitwise XOR | Left-to-right |
| 9 | `\|` | Bitwise OR | Left-to-right |
| 10 (Lowest) | `==`, `!=`, `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=` | Comparisons | Left-to-right |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Associativity**: The bitwise shift operators `<<` and `>>` have **left-to-right** associativity. This is non-obvious and is a common source of bugs. For example:
> `1 << 2 << 1` = `(1 << 2) << 1` = `4 << 1` = `8` — NOT `1 << (2 << 1)` = `1 << 4` = `16`.

---

## 3.3 Memory Model — Mutable vs Immutable

| Type | Immutable? | Passed to Function As | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| `int`, `float`, `str`, `tuple`, `frozenset` | **Yes** | Value (effectively) | `x = 5` |
| `list`, `dict`, `set`, `bytearray` | **No** | Reference | `lst = [1,2]` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication**: When you pass a `list` (e.g., a sensor buffer) to a function and modify it inside, the **original** list is also modified. This is a common bug in multi-threaded IoT code (e.g., sensor reading thread modifying the same list as the MQTT publishing thread). Always use a `threading.Lock()` to prevent **race conditions**.

---

## 3.4 Control Flow Execution Trace

| Step | Line | State of `x` | Output | Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `x = 0` | 0 | — | — |
| 2 | `while x < 3:` | 0 | — | `0 < 3` → True |
| 3 | `print(x)` | 0 | `0` | — |
| 4 | `x += 1` | 1 | — | — |
| 5 | (back to while) | 1 | — | `1 < 3` → True |
| 6 | `print(x)` | 1 | `1` | — |
| 7 | `x += 1` | 2 | — | — |
| 8 | (back to while) | 2 | — | `2 < 3` → True |
| 9 | `print(x)` | 2 | `2` | — |
| 10 | `x += 1` | 3 | — | — |
| 11 | (back to while) | 3 | — | `3 < 3` → False |
| 12 | Loop exits | 3 | — | — |

This trace is a **standard KTU exam question** — always practice dry-running loops by hand.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the difference between `BOARD` and `BCM` pin numbering modes in Raspberry Pi GPIO programming. Which one is recommended and why?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

**BOARD numbering**: Refers to the **physical pin position** on the 40-pin header (pins numbered 1 to 40 from top-left). It is **hardware-oriented** — if the physical pin changes location in a future Pi revision, the code may break.

**BCM numbering**: Refers to the **Broadcom SoC GPIO channel number** (e.g., `GPIO17`, `GPIO27`). It is **processor-oriented** and remains consistent across Pi models (as long as the same SoC is used).

**Recommendation**: **`BCM` numbering is recommended** in professional and KTU lab work because it directly maps to the SoC documentation, is the same convention used in `datasheets`, and is what the `gpiozero` library uses by default. It makes code portable across different physical Pi revisions, provided the same BCM pin is wired to the same physical component.

> Each mode explanation: 1 mark; Recommendation with reason: 1 mark.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the purpose of `GPIO.cleanup()` in a Python script using the `RPi.GPIO` library? When should it be called?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

`GPIO.cleanup()` is a function that **resets all GPIO pins used by the script back to their default input state** (high-impedance), effectively releasing them. It should be called:
1. **Before exiting the script** (typically inside a `finally:` block of a `try-except`).
2. **When switching the pin numbering mode** (`GPIO.setmode`) — to avoid a `RuntimeError: The GPIO channel has already been set up`.
3. **To prevent hardware damage** — if a pin was set HIGH (e.g., driving a motor), leaving it HIGH after the script ends can cause the connected hardware to remain powered indefinitely, leading to overheating or battery drain.

> Definition: 1 mark; Where to call: 1 mark; Engineering consequence: 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks)

#### (a) Write a Python program to blink an LED connected to GPIO 17 of a Raspberry Pi at 1 Hz (1 second ON, 1 second OFF) using the `RPi.GPIO` library. Explain the code line by line. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

```python
import RPi.GPIO as GPIO
import time

# --- Configuration ---
LED_PIN = 17              # Use BCM pin 17 (physical pin 11)
BLINK_INTERVAL = 1.0      # seconds

# --- Setup ---
GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)                    # Use Broadcom channel numbering
GPIO.setwarnings(False)                   # Suppress channel-already-in-use warnings
GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT)             # Configure pin 17 as output

# --- Main Loop ---
try:
    while True:
        GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.HIGH)   # Turn LED ON (3.3V on pin)
        time.sleep(BLINK_INTERVAL)        # Wait 1 second
        GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.LOW)    # Turn LED OFF (0V on pin)
        time.sleep(BLINK_INTERVAL)        # Wait 1 second

except KeyboardInterrupt:
    print("Program stopped by user.")     # Graceful exit on Ctrl+C

finally:
    GPIO.cleanup()                        # Reset all pins to safe state
```

**Line-by-Line Explanation (7 marks distributed as below):**

| Line(s) | Explanation | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| `import RPi.GPIO as GPIO` | Imports the GPIO library and aliases it as `GPIO` for brevity | 1 |
| `GPIO.setmode(GPIO.BCM)` | Selects BCM numbering; **must be called before any other GPIO function** | 1 |
| `GPIO.setup(LED_PIN, GPIO.OUT)` | Configures pin 17 as an **output** pin; this allocates the pin resource | 1 |
| `GPIO.output(LED_PIN, GPIO.HIGH)` | Sets pin 17 to 3.3V → LED lights up (current flows through the LED + resistor to GND) | 1 |
| `time.sleep(1.0)` | Pauses execution for 1 second; **without this, the blink would be too fast to see** (kHz range) | 1 |
| `try / except KeyboardInterrupt` | Catches `Ctrl+C` so the script doesn't crash with a stack trace | 1 |
| `finally: GPIO.cleanup()` | **Critical** — resets pin 17 to input mode so it's not left in an undefined state | 1 |

---

#### (b) Explain the difference between **lists**, **tuples**, and **dictionaries** in Python. Give one real-world Raspberry Pi use case for each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

| Feature | List `[]` | Tuple `()` | Dictionary `{}` |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mutability** | Mutable (can add/remove/change) | Immutable (read-only) | Mutable (values can change, keys cannot) |
| **Syntax** | `[1, 2, 3]` | `(1, 2, 3)` | `{"key": "value"}` |
| **Ordered?** | Yes (Python 3.7+) | Yes | Yes (insertion-ordered) |
| **Indexing** | `lst[0]` | `tup[0]` | `dict["key"]` (not by index) |
| **Duplicates?** | Allowed | Allowed | Keys must be unique |
| **Performance** | Slower than tuple | Faster (fixed size) | Fast O(1) lookup by key |

**Real-World Raspberry Pi Use Cases (1 mark each, plus 1 mark per explanation = 7 total):**

1. **List — Sensor Data Buffer** (2 marks):
   A temperature monitoring script that keeps the last 60 readings (1 minute of data at 1 Hz):
   ```python
   readings = []                      # start empty
   for _ in range(60):
       readings.append(read_temp())   # append every second
   avg = sum(readings) / len(readings)  # moving average
   ```
   Lists are perfect because we constantly **add** new readings and **remove** old ones.

2. **Tuple — GPIO Pin Configuration** (2 marks):
   A tuple holds pin numbers that should **never change** at runtime — the hardware wiring is fixed:
   ```python
   SENSOR_PINS = (17, 27, 22)         # immutable — prevents accidental modification
   for pin in SENSOR_PINS:
       GPIO.setup(pin, GPIO.IN, pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_UP)
   ```
   Using a tuple here is a **defensive programming** technique — the script will raise a `TypeError` if anyone tries `SENSOR_PINS[0] = 99`.

3. **Dictionary — JSON Payload for MQTT** (3 marks):
   IoT devices publish data to MQTT brokers as JSON objects. A `dict` maps directly to JSON:
   ```python
   import json
   payload = {
       "device_id": "pi_lab_01",
       "temperature": 24.7,
       "humidity": 58.2,
       "timestamp": "2024-12-15T10:30:00Z"
   }
   mqtt_client.publish("sensors/lab01", json.dumps(payload))
   ```
   Dicts provide **O(1) key-based lookup**, which is essential when you have dozens of sensor fields.

---

### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE

#### (a) Describe the Python data types `int`, `float`, `str`, `bool`, `list`, and `dict`. For each, give **one Raspberry Pi IoT application** where it is naturally used. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

| Data Type | Definition | Raspberry Pi IoT Application | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| `int` | Integer — whole numbers, arbitrary precision in Python 3 | **GPIO pin number** (must be integer for `GPIO.setup`) | `pin = 17` |
| `float` | Floating-point — decimal numbers (IEEE 754 double precision) | **Temperature reading from DHT22 sensor** | `temp = 24.7` |
| `str` | Immutable Unicode string | **MQTT topic or WiFi SSID** | `topic = "home/sensor1"` |
| `bool` | Logical `True` or `False` | **Motion sensor state** | `motion_detected = True` |
| `list` | Ordered, mutable sequence | **Buffer of last N sensor readings** | `buffer = [22.1, 22.3, 22.5]` |
| `dict` | Key-value mapping (hash table) | **JSON payload for cloud upload** | `{"temp": 24.7, "id": "pi01"}` |

**Grading**: 0.5 mark for correct definition + 0.5 mark for valid IoT example + 0.2 mark for code snippet = **~1.0–1.2 marks each × 6 = 7 marks total**.

---

#### (b) Write a Python program that reads a button connected to GPIO 27 (with pull-up resistor enabled) and toggles an LED on GPIO 17 every time the button is pressed. Use the `gpiozero` library for cleaner code. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

```python
from gpiozero import LED, Button
from signal import pause

# --- Hardware Setup ---
led = LED(17)                    # LED on GPIO 17
button = Button(27, pull_up=True) # Button on GPIO 27 with internal pull-up

# --- State Variable ---
led_state = {"on": False}        # Use dict to allow modification in nested function

def toggle_led():
    """Toggle the LED each time the button is pressed."""
    led_state["on"] = not led_state["on"]
    if led_state["on"]:
        led.on()
        print("LED turned ON")
    else:
        led.off()
        print("LED turned OFF")

# --- Event Binding ---
button.when_pressed = toggle_led  # Call toggle_led() on every press

# --- Keep the script running ---
print("Press Ctrl+C to exit.")
pause()                            # Blocks the main thread indefinitely
```

**Explanation Table (7 marks):**

| Construct | Purpose | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| `from gpiozero import LED, Button` | Imports modern, high-level GPIO abstraction (replaces older `RPi.GPIO` for simple use cases) | 1 |
| `LED(17)` / `Button(27, pull_up=True)` | Object-oriented interface — each pin is an object with methods like `.on()`, `.off()`, `.wait_for_press()` | 1 |
| `led_state = {"on": False}` | Mutable state holder; using a dict allows the nested function to modify the outer variable (a Python closure requirement — see note below) | 1 |
| `def toggle_led():` | Callback function that flips the state and updates the LED | 1 |
| `button.when_pressed = toggle_led` | **Event-driven programming** — instead of polling in a loop, the library calls `toggle_led` automatically when the button is pressed (interrupt-based on the SoC) | 2 |
| `pause()` | Suspends the main thread so the script doesn't exit; required because `gpiozero` runs callbacks in a background thread | 1 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Why use `{"on": False}` instead of just `on = False`?**
> In Python, assigning to a variable inside a nested function creates a **new local variable** in that function's scope — it does NOT modify the outer variable. The classic workaround is either:
> 1. `global on` declaration (works but is considered poor style), or
> 2. Wrap the state in a mutable container (a list or dict) — as done above.
> 3. In Python 3, use `nonlocal on` inside the nested function.
> Failing to handle this correctly is one of the **most common KTU exam and lab mistakes**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting `GPIO.cleanup()`** — When the script ends, pins remain in their last state. **Always use `try/finally`**. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Mixing BOARD and BCM numbering** — Code crashes with `RuntimeError: Please set pin numbering mode`. Pick ONE at the start. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Using `=` instead of `==` in conditions** — `if x = 5:` is a **SyntaxError** in Python (unlike C/C++). (–1 mark)
> 4. **Indentation errors** — Python uses indentation to define blocks; mixing tabs and spaces causes `IndentationError`. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Not handling exceptions** — In hardware programs, ALWAYS wrap GPIO calls in `try/except` to avoid crashes on wire disconnects. (–2 marks)
> 6. **Using `print()` for debugging in production** — Use the `logging` module instead for proper log levels (INFO, WARNING, ERROR). (–1 mark)
> 7. **Forgetting to declare `global` or `nonlocal`** for variables modified in nested functions (–1 mark)
> 8. **Confusing `time.sleep()` with `time.delay()`** — The latter does NOT exist in Python; only `time.sleep(seconds)` is valid. (–1 mark)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision — Python Basics for Raspberry Pi**

- ✅ **Python 3** is the default on Raspberry Pi OS (Bookworm); version is **3.11+**.
- ✅ **Two numbering modes**: `GPIO.BOARD` (physical 1–40) and `GPIO.BCM` (SoC channel numbers). Pick one via `GPIO.setmode()`.
- ✅ **GPIO voltage level is 3.3 V** — never apply 5 V to a Pi GPIO pin.
- ✅ **Three GPIO setup directions**: `OUT`, `IN`, and `IN` with `pull_up_down=GPIO.PUD_UP` / `GPIO.PUD_DOWN`.
- ✅ **Always use `try / except / finally`** with `GPIO.cleanup()` in the `finally` block.
- ✅ **Core data types**: `int`, `float`, `str`, `bool`, `list`, `dict`, `tuple`, `set`, `bytes`, `NoneType`.
- ✅ **Mutable types**: `list`, `dict`, `set`, `bytearray`. **Immutable**: `int`, `float`, `str`, `tuple`, `frozenset`, `bool`.
- ✅ **Loops**: `for item in iterable:` and `while condition:`. Use `break` to exit, `continue` to skip.
- ✅ **Functions**: Defined with `def name(args):`. May have default args, `*args`, `**kwargs`, and a `return` value.
- ✅ **File I/O**: Prefer `with open("file", "r") as f:` over manual `.close()` calls.
- ✅ **Bitwise operators** (`&`, `|`, `^`, `<<`, `>>`) are essential for multi-pin GPIO masking.
- ✅ **F-strings** `f"value: {x}"` are the modern way to format strings (Python 3.6+).
- ✅ **gpiozero library** is the **modern, recommended** abstraction over `RPi.GPIO` for beginners — object-oriented and event-driven.
- ✅ **ADC formula** to remember: $V_{in} = \dfrac{D}{2^n - 1} \times V_{ref}$.
- ✅ **LM35 scale factor**: 10 mV/°C → $T = V_{in} / 0.01$.
- ✅ **Exception hierarchy**: `KeyboardInterrupt` (Ctrl+C), `RuntimeError` (GPIO conflicts), `ValueError` (bad arg), `FileNotFoundError` (missing file).
- ✅ **Indentation is syntax**, not style — 4 spaces per level is the PEP 8 standard.
- ✅ **Shebang line** for executable scripts: `#!/usr/bin/env python3` at the top of the file.
- ✅ **Virtual environments** (`python3 -m venv myenv`) isolate Pi project dependencies — important when using pip on the system Python.
- ✅ **Pin 17** (BCM) = **Physical Pin 11** on the 40-pin header — memorize this mapping for the KTU lab exam.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: GPIO Interfacing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT503 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** GPIO Interfacing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**GPIO (General Purpose Input/Output)** refers to a generic, software-controllable pin on an Integrated Circuit (IC), most commonly a microcontroller, microprocessor, or System on Chip (SoC), whose behavior — whether it acts as a digital input or digital output, and whether it drives a logic HIGH or logic LOW — can be dynamically configured and reconfigured at runtime via firmware.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context of **PCCGT503 (Microprocessors and Microcontrollers for IoT)**, GPIO pins form the *fundamental physical bridge* between the digital computational domain (CPU, registers, ALU) and the analog/physical external world (sensors, actuators, displays, communication buses). Each GPIO pin is internally tied to a configurable **multiplexer**, a **direction control register**, an **output driver stage** (typically a CMOS push-pull pair), and optionally an **input Schmitt trigger**, **pull-up resistor**, **pull-down resistor**, and **alternate function logic** (for UART, SPI, I2C, PWM, ADC, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** GPIO is treated as the *lowest-level hardware abstraction* before moving to high-level protocols (I2C, SPI, UART). Students MUST master register-level configuration, electrical characteristics (VIH, VIL, VOH, VOL, IOH, IOL), and standard interfacing circuits (LED with current-limiting resistor, switch with pull-up/pull-down).

## 1.2 The Pin Function Multiplexing Reality

Modern IoT microcontrollers (e.g., **ESP32**, **STM32**, **Raspberry Pi RP2040**, **Arduino Uno ATmega328P**) do not have dedicated pins for every peripheral. Instead, each physical pad (e.g., GPIO17 on ESP32) can be routed internally to multiple peripheral functions through a **pin matrix / crossbar switch**. The *GPIO function* is just one of many possible mappings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Clarification:** A pin operating in "GPIO mode" means it has been disconnected from any peripheral (UART, SPI, ADC, etc.) and is directly under software control via the data register (e.g., `GPIO_OUT_REG` in ESP32, `PORTx` in AVR).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Electrical Socket"

Imagine a wall socket in your house. By default, it has no function assigned — it just *waits*. When you plug in a lamp, it becomes an **output** (electricity flows OUT to the appliance). When you plug in a sensor that detects whether the switch is on or off, it becomes an **input** (information flows IN to your monitoring system).

A **GPIO pin behaves exactly like this universal socket**:

| Analogy Element | GPIO Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Wall socket | Physical pin on microcontroller |
| Plugged-in lamp (load) | LED / Buzzer / Relay |
| Plugged-in sensor | Push button / Limit switch / Sensor |
| Decision to use as output | Setting `pinMode(pin, OUTPUT)` |
| Decision to use as input | Setting `pinMode(pin, INPUT)` |
| On/Off switch on wall | Software `digitalWrite(HIGH/LOW)` |
| Reading the state | `digitalRead(pin)` |

The *role is not fixed at manufacturing time* — it is *defined by the firmware you write*. This flexibility is the essence of "**General Purpose**".

## 1.4 Core GPIO Operational Modes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Favourite:** Memorize the following four pin modes — they appear almost every year in Part A (3-mark) questions.

1. **INPUT (High-Impedance / Hi-Z):** Pin is configured to *listen*. Internal drivers are disabled. Voltage on the pin is sampled by the Schmitt trigger and latched into the input register. Must NOT be left floating — requires external or internal pull-up/pull-down.
2. **OUTPUT (Push-Pull):** Pin actively drives the line to **VCC (logic HIGH, typically 3.3 V)** or **GND (logic LOW, 0 V)** using internal CMOS transistors. Can source current (when HIGH) or sink current (when LOW).
3. **INPUT_PULLUP:** INPUT mode with an internal resistor (typically 30 kΩ–50 kΩ) connected to VCC, weakly holding the pin at logic HIGH until an external connection pulls it LOW.
4. **INPUT_PULLDOWN:** INPUT mode with an internal resistor connected to GND, weakly holding the pin at logic LOW until externally pulled HIGH.

## 1.5 Electrical Logical Voltage Levels

For most IoT-grade 3.3 V microcontrollers (ESP32, RP2040, STM32):

- **VIH (Input HIGH Voltage):** ≥ **0.75 × VCC ≈ 2.475 V**
- **VIL (Input LOW Voltage):** ≤ **0.25 × VCC ≈ 0.825 V**
- **VOH (Output HIGH Voltage):** ≈ **VCC (3.3 V)** at minimal source current
- **VOL (Output LOW Voltage):** ≈ **0 V (GND)** at minimal sink current
- **Source Current (IOH):** typically **20 mA** (per pin), **40 mA** (per bank) — **ESP32 is rated for only 12 mA max**
- **Sink Current (IOL):** typically **20 mA**

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Damage Rule:** Never connect an LED directly between a GPIO pin and GND without a current-limiting resistor. Doing so will source excessive current, leading to thermal damage of the internal MOSFET driver.

## 1.6 Why GPIO is the Foundation of IoT

Every IoT node — from a soil-moisture sensor in a smart farm to a smart bulb in a home — ultimately relies on a microcontroller reading digital inputs (sensor states) and writing digital outputs (actuator commands). GPIO is the *atomic unit* of all physical interaction. Higher-level protocols (I2C, SPI, UART, 1-Wire) are essentially *multiplexed, protocol-wrapped GPIO operations* on multiple pins simultaneously.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Architecture of a Single GPIO Pin (Block-Level View)

Every modern GPIO pin has the following internal sub-blocks (mapped from the silicon die of ATmega328P, ESP32, STM32 families):

1. **Pad (Physical Bond Wire):** Externalized metal contact on the IC package.
2. **ESD Protection Diodes:** Two clamp diodes to VCC and GND to divert electrostatic discharge away from internal CMOS gates.
3. **Pull-Up / Pull-Down Resistors (Rpu, Rpd):** Weak (~30–50 kΩ) MOSFET-based resistors, software-enableable.
4. **Input Schmitt Trigger (Hysteresis Buffer):** Converts the slow/noisy analog input into a clean digital logic level. Provides noise immunity via VH = 2.0 V and VL = 0.8 V thresholds (hysteresis ~1.2 V).
5. **Input Data Register (GPIO_IN):** The sampled digital value, readable by the CPU.
6. **Output Data Register (GPIO_OUT):** The value to be driven when the pin is in OUTPUT mode.
7. **Direction / Mode Register (GPIO_ENABLE / DDR):** Selects INPUT (Hi-Z) or OUTPUT (drive mode).
8. **Output Driver (Push-Pull CMOS Pair):** A P-MOSFET (pulls to VCC) and N-MOSFET (pulls to GND). Only one is ON at a time.
9. **Alternate Function Multiplexer (MUX):** Routes the pad to peripherals (UART TX, SPI CLK, I2C SDA, PWM, ADC channel).
10. **Slew-Rate Control & Drive-Strength Selector:** Configurable edge transition speed and current capability.

## 2.2 Operational Theory: How an LED is Interfaced (Output Mode)

When a GPIO pin is set to OUTPUT and `digitalWrite(HIGH)` is executed:

1. The CPU writes a `1` to the GPIO_OUT bit.
2. The P-MOSFET turns ON; N-MOSFET turns OFF.
3. The pad is actively driven to **VCC = 3.3 V**.
4. Current flows from VCC → through the **external current-limiting resistor R** → through the **LED (anode → cathode)** → into the GPIO pad → into the internal N-MOSFET → to GND (since the P-MOSFET side is OFF at the chip's VCC rail relative to the LED forward voltage).

Actually, the correct current path is: **VCC (3.3 V) → Internal P-MOSFET (ON) → Pad (output HIGH) → External LED (anode→cathode) → External Resistor R → GND**.

The current is governed by **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)**:

$$V_{CC} = V_F + V_R$$

where $V_F$ is the LED forward voltage drop (~2.0 V for red, ~3.2 V for blue/white) and $V_R$ is the voltage drop across the series resistor.

$$I_{LED} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F}{R}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Exam Tip:** Always pick R so that $I_{LED}$ ≤ the GPIO's rated source current (typically ≤ 20 mA for AVR, ≤ 12 mA for ESP32). A safe nominal value is **5 mA–10 mA**.

## 2.3 Operational Theory: How a Push-Button is Interfaced (Input Mode)

A mechanical switch cannot be left "floating" — when open, the input pin would pick up stray electromagnetic noise (50 Hz mains hum, RF, static) and produce random HIGH/LOW transitions. Two standard solutions exist:

### (a) External Pull-Down Resistor (Active-HIGH Logic)

- Switch open → pin pulled to GND through R → reads **LOW (0)**.
- Switch closed → pin shorted to VCC → reads **HIGH (1)**.

### (b) External Pull-Up Resistor (Active-LOW Logic — Most Common)

- Switch open → pin pulled to VCC through R → reads **HIGH (1)**.
- Switch closed → pin shorted to GND → reads **LOW (0)**.

The value of R is chosen by balancing two competing constraints:
- **Too small (e.g., 100 Ω):** Wastes current when switch is pressed ($I = V_{CC}/R$); also overpowers the internal pull-up.
- **Too large (e.g., 10 MΩ):** Picks up noise; slow rise time due to parasitic capacitance.

A typical compromise is **10 kΩ** for external; internal pull-ups (~30–50 kΩ) are usually adequate.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **every formula you must memorize** for GPIO interfacing numericals. These are high-yield for both **ESE (End Semester)** and **internal lab viva**.

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_{LED}}$ | Current-limiting resistor for LED | Ohms (Ω) | LED interfacing |
| 2 | $P_R = I_{LED}^{2} \cdot R$ | Power dissipated in series resistor | Watts (W) | LED thermal analysis |
| 3 | $I_{switch} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_{pullup}}$ | Current through pull-up when switch closed | Amperes (A) | Button interfacing |
| 4 | $V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Voltage divider output (sensor scaling) | Volts (V) | Analog sensor input |
| 5 | $t_{RC} = R \cdot C \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_{CC}}{V_{threshold}}\right)$ | RC debounce time constant | Seconds (s) | Switch debouncing |
| 6 | $f_{PWM} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{N \cdot (TOP + 1)}$ | PWM frequency from timer | Hertz (Hz) | LED dimming / motor speed |
| 7 | $D = \dfrac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}}$ | Duty cycle of PWM | Unitless (0 to 1) | PWM applications |
| 8 | $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{CC}$ | Average DC voltage of PWM signal | Volts (V) | Motor speed control |
| 9 | $P_{GPIO} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{total}$ | Power consumed by GPIO bank | Watts (W) | Power budget calculations |
| 10 | $I_{sink,max} \geq I_{load}$ | Sink current must exceed load | Amperes (A) | Relay driver design |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

GPIO interfacing is not academic — it is the foundation of:

- **Smart Home Automation:** Reading door sensors, controlling relays for lights.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Reading limit switches on conveyor belts, driving solenoid valves.
- **Wearable Electronics:** Driving haptic feedback motors, reading capacitive touch buttons.
- **Automotive Embedded Systems:** Reading brake-pedal switches, driving dashboard LEDs.
- **Robotics:** Reading IR obstacle sensors, driving DC motors via H-bridge (via GPIO → driver IC).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Insight:** In professional embedded firmware, GPIO pins are rarely manipulated directly via `digitalWrite()` in production code. Instead, **HAL (Hardware Abstraction Layer)** functions and **Device Tree overlays** (in Linux-based SoCs like Raspberry Pi) are used to make code portable across microcontrollers.

## 2.6 Switch Debouncing — A Critical Real-World Concept

Mechanical push-buttons exhibit **contact bounce** — when pressed, the metal contacts physically vibrate for 1–20 ms before settling, producing multiple spurious edges that the microcontroller sees as 5–50 rapid transitions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Viva Question:** "Why do we need to debounce a switch?" **Answer:** Because mechanical contacts bounce for 1–20 ms; without debouncing, a single press may register as 5–50 false presses, breaking counters, toggles, and state machines.

Two debouncing strategies:

1. **Hardware Debouncing:** Add an RC low-pass filter (e.g., 10 kΩ + 100 nF → τ = 1 ms) followed by a Schmitt trigger (e.g., 74HC14).
2. **Software Debouncing:** After detecting an edge, ignore further changes for a **debounce delay** (typically 5–50 ms) using a timer or `delay()`.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: LED Current-Limiting Resistor Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style Numerical):**

> *Interface a red LED (forward voltage $V_F = 2.0$ V, max continuous current $I_{LED} = 15$ mA) to an ESP32 GPIO pin. The GPIO drives 3.3 V in HIGH state. Calculate the value of the series current-limiting resistor, the nearest standard E12 value, and the power dissipated in the resistor.*

### Given Data

- Supply voltage: $V_{CC} = 3.3$ V (ESP32 GPIO logic HIGH)
- LED forward voltage: $V_F = 2.0$ V (typical red LED)
- Desired LED current: $I_{LED} = 15$ mA $= 0.015$ A

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the series loop (VCC → Resistor R → LED → GND):

$$V_{CC} = V_R + V_F$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the known voltage drops:

$$3.3 \text{ V} = V_R + 2.0 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the voltage drop across the resistor:

$$V_R = 3.3 \text{ V} - 2.0 \text{ V} = 1.3 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4:** Apply Ohm's Law ($V = I \cdot R$) to find the resistance:

$$R = \frac{V_R}{I_{LED}} = \frac{1.3 \text{ V}}{0.015 \text{ A}}$$

**Step 5:** Perform the numerical division:

$$R = \frac{1.3}{0.015} = \frac{1300}{15} \approx 86.67 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 6:** Select the nearest **E12 standard value** (12 values per decade: 10, 12, 15, 18, 22, 27, 33, 39, 47, 56, 68, 82, 100…). The nearest standard value above 86.67 Ω is **100 Ω** (which gives slightly lower current) or **82 Ω** (which gives slightly higher current). For safety margin, choose **100 Ω**.

**Step 7:** Recompute the actual LED current with $R = 100$ Ω:

$$I_{LED,actual} = \frac{V_R}{R} = \frac{1.3 \text{ V}}{100 \text{ }\Omega} = 0.013 \text{ A} = 13 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 8:** Compute power dissipated by the resistor:

$$P_R = I_{LED,actual}^{2} \cdot R = (0.013)^{2} \cdot 100$$

$$P_R = 0.000169 \cdot 100 = 0.0169 \text{ W} \approx 16.9 \text{ mW}$$

**Step 9:** Select resistor power rating (standard: 1/8 W = 125 mW, 1/4 W = 250 mW):

$$P_R = 16.9 \text{ mW} \ll 125 \text{ mW (1/8 W)}$$

A **1/8 W resistor** is safely adequate.

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{R = 100 \text{ }\Omega \text{ (E12 standard)}, \quad I_{LED} = 13 \text{ mA}, \quad P_R \approx 17 \text{ mW (use 1/8 W resistor)}}$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: Relay Driver GPIO Interfacing (Transistor Switch)

**Problem Statement:**

> *A 5 V electromagnetic relay coil (coil resistance $R_{coil} = 200$ Ω, requiring $I_{coil} = 25$ mA) must be driven by an ESP32 GPIO pin. ESP32 GPIO can source/sink only 12 mA at 3.3 V. Design a transistor driver circuit and compute the base resistor value. Use a 2N2222 NPN BJT with $\beta_{DC} = 100$, $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V.*

### Given Data

- Relay coil: $V_{relay} = 5$ V, $R_{coil} = 200$ Ω, $I_{coil} = 25$ mA
- GPIO: $V_{CC} = 3.3$ V, $I_{GPIO,max} = 12$ mA
- Transistor: 2N2222 NPN, $\beta = 100$, $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V, $V_{CE,sat} \approx 0.2$ V

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Verify the relay current requirement exceeds GPIO capability:

$$I_{coil} = 25 \text{ mA} > I_{GPIO,max} = 12 \text{ mA} \quad \Rightarrow \text{ A driver is required.}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the base current required to saturate the transistor:

$$I_B = \frac{I_C}{\beta_{DC}} = \frac{25 \text{ mA}}{100} = 0.25 \text{ mA}$$

To ensure **saturation** (hard ON), apply an overdrive factor of 2 (rule of thumb):

$$I_{B,safe} = 2 \times I_B = 2 \times 0.25 \text{ mA} = 0.5 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 3:** Apply KVL to the base-emitter loop (GPIO pin → $R_B$ → Base-Emitter junction → GND):

$$V_{CC} = V_{R_B} + V_{BE}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $V_{R_B}$:

$$V_{R_B} = V_{CC} - V_{BE} = 3.3 \text{ V} - 0.7 \text{ V} = 2.6 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5:** Apply Ohm's Law to find $R_B$:

$$R_B = \frac{V_{R_B}}{I_{B,safe}} = \frac{2.6 \text{ V}}{0.5 \text{ mA}} = \frac{2.6}{0.0005} = 5200 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 6:** Select the nearest E24 standard value: **5.1 kΩ** (or use 4.7 kΩ for slight overdrive).

**Step 7:** Place a **flyback diode** (e.g., 1N4007) in reverse-biased parallel with the relay coil (cathode at +5 V, anode at collector) to protect the transistor from the inductive kick (back-EMF $\approx 50$–200 V) when the relay de-energizes.

**Step 8:** Compute power in $R_B$:

$$P_{R_B} = I_{B,safe}^{2} \cdot R_B = (0.5 \text{ mA})^{2} \cdot 5100 \text{ }\Omega = 1.275 \text{ }\mu\text{W} \text{ (negligible)}$$

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{R_B = 5.1 \text{ k}\Omega, \quad I_B = 0.5 \text{ mA}, \quad \text{Flyback diode} = 1\text{N4007 in parallel with coil}}$$

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3: Voltage Divider for 5 V Sensor to 3.3 V GPIO

**Problem Statement:**

> *An ultrasonic sensor HC-SR04 outputs a 5 V logic HIGH on its Echo pin. This must be connected to an ESP32 GPIO (max input voltage = 3.3 V, $V_{IH} \geq 2.475$ V). Design a voltage divider using $R_1 = 10$ kΩ. Calculate $R_2$, the actual output voltage when input is 5 V, and verify it lies within ESP32's safe input range.*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Apply the voltage divider formula:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 2:** Set the target output to **3.0 V** (safety margin below 3.3 V absolute max, well above 2.475 V $V_{IH}$):

$$3.0 = 5.0 \cdot \frac{R_2}{10000 + R_2}$$

**Step 3:** Cross-multiply:

$$3.0 \cdot (10000 + R_2) = 5.0 \cdot R_2$$

$$30000 + 3.0 \cdot R_2 = 5.0 \cdot R_2$$

**Step 4:** Isolate $R_2$:

$$30000 = 5.0 \cdot R_2 - 3.0 \cdot R_2 = 2.0 \cdot R_2$$

$$R_2 = \frac{30000}{2.0} = 15000 \text{ }\Omega = 15 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 5:** Verify by back-substitution:

$$V_{out} = 5.0 \cdot \frac{15000}{10000 + 15000} = 5.0 \cdot \frac{15000}{25000} = 5.0 \cdot 0.6 = 3.0 \text{ V} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6:** Verify ESP32 safe input range:

$$V_{out} = 3.0 \text{ V} \begin{cases} \leq 3.3 \text{ V (VCC)} & \text{ SAFE} \\ \geq 2.475 \text{ V (VIH)} & \text{ Recognized as HIGH} \end{cases}$$

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega, \quad R_2 = 15 \text{ k}\Omega, \quad V_{out} = 3.0 \text{ V (SAFE for ESP32)}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Pin Modes

| Pin Mode | Internal Pull-Up | Internal Pull-Down | Output Drive | Read-Back | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| INPUT (Hi-Z) | OFF | OFF | Disabled (Hi-Z) | Yes | External signal with explicit biasing |
| INPUT_PULLUP | ON | OFF | Disabled (Hi-Z) | Yes | Active-LOW button (pressed = 0) |
| INPUT_PULLDOWN | OFF | ON | Disabled (Hi-Z) | Yes | Active-HIGH button (pressed = 1) |
| OUTPUT (Push-Pull, LOW) | OFF | OFF | Sink (N-MOS ON) | Yes | Drive LED cathode, sink current |
| OUTPUT (Push-Pull, HIGH) | OFF | OFF | Source (P-MOS ON) | Yes | Drive LED anode, source current |
| OUTPUT (Open-Drain) | OFF | OFF | Sink only | Yes | I2C SDA/SCL, multi-node buses |
| ANALOG INPUT | OFF | OFF | Disabled | Via ADC | Potentiometer, LDR, temperature |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a GPIO Pin (Mermaid)

The following diagram maps the internal silicon-level architecture and external interfacing pathways of a single GPIO pin. Because Mermaid cannot render transistor-level schematics, this is a **block-level functional architecture flow** as mandated by the protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU["CPU / Core Logic"]
    DIR_REG["Direction Register DDR"]
    OUT_REG["Output Data Register OUT"]
    IN_REG["Input Data Register IN"]
    PULL_REG["Pull-Up / Pull-Down Enable Register"]
    MUX["Pin Function Multiplexer"]
    DRIVER["Push-Pull CMOS Driver P-MOS and N-MOS"]
    SCHMITT["Schmitt Trigger Hysteresis Buffer"]
    ESD["ESD Protection Diodes to VCC and GND"]
    PAD["Physical Pad External Pin"]
    LOAD["External Load LED or Relay"]
    BTN["External Input Push Button or Sensor"]

    CPU --> DIR_REG
    CPU --> OUT_REG
    CPU --> IN_REG
    CPU --> PULL_REG
    DIR_REG --> MUX
    OUT_REG --> MUX
    PULL_REG --> MUX
    MUX --> DRIVER
    MUX --> SCHMITT
    DRIVER --> PAD
    SCHMITT --> IN_REG
    PAD --> ESD
    PAD --> LOAD
    PAD --> BTN
```

**Interpretation:** The CPU writes configuration to DIR_REG (selects INPUT/OUTPUT), writes the output bit to OUT_REG (logic level to drive), and reads IN_REG (sampled logic level from the pad). The MUX routes either the output driver or the input Schmitt trigger to the pad. ESD diodes clamp voltage transients. The PAD is physically bonded out of the IC package and connects to external loads or inputs.

## 4.2 LED Interfacing Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCC["VCC 3.3 V"]
    PIN["GPIO Pin Output HIGH"]
    R["Current Limiting Resistor R"]
    LED["LED Anode to Cathode"]
    GND["GND 0 V"]

    PIN --> R
    R --> LED
    LED --> GND
    VCC -.Internal P-MOS ON.-> PIN
```

**Current Flow When GPIO=HIGH:** VCC → Internal P-MOSFET → PAD (output pin) → External Resistor R → LED (anode → cathode) → GND.

## 4.3 Push-Button Interfacing with Pull-Up (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VCC["VCC 3.3 V"]
    RPU["Pull-Up Resistor 10 kOhm"]
    PIN["GPIO Pin INPUT PULLUP"]
    BTN["Push Button NO"]
    GND["GND"]

    VCC --> RPU
    RPU --> PIN
    PIN --> BTN
    BTN --> GND
```

**Operational Logic:**
- Button **OPEN** → Current path: VCC → RPU → into pin (Hi-Z) → reads **HIGH (1)**.
- Button **CLOSED** → Current path: VCC → RPU → directly to GND (shorted through button) → pin reads **LOW (0)**.

This is the classic **active-LOW** logic convention used in 90% of embedded button designs.

## 4.4 Relay Driver Interfacing with BJT (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GPIO["GPIO Pin 3.3 V"]
    RB["Base Resistor RB 5.1 kOhm"]
    BJT["2N2222 NPN BJT"]
    COIL["Relay Coil 5 V 200 Ohm"]
    DIODE["Flyback Diode 1N4007 Reverse Biased"]
    VREL["Relay Supply 5 V"]

    GPIO --> RB
    RB --> BJT
    BJT --> COIL
    COIL --> VREL
    DIODE -.parallel reverse.-> COIL
```

**Operational Logic:**
- GPIO **HIGH** → $I_B$ flows → BJT saturates → collector current $I_C$ energizes coil → relay contacts close.
- GPIO **LOW** → BJT cutoff → coil de-energizes → relay contacts open.
- The flyback diode absorbs the inductive kickback (Lenz's law) when the BJT switches OFF, protecting the transistor from a $-V$ spike of $50$–$200$ V.

## 4.5 Inter-Module Data Flow (Sensor → GPIO → Cloud)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENS["Physical Sensor Temperature or Motion"]
    GPIO_IN["GPIO Pin Configured as INPUT"]
    MCU["Microcontroller CPU Reads digitalRead"]
    PROC["Processing Threshold Compare Filter"]
    GPIO_OUT["GPIO Pin Configured as OUTPUT"]
    ACT["Actuator LED or Relay or Buzzer"]
    COMM["Communication Module UART or WiFi or LoRa"]

    SENS --> GPIO_IN
    GPIO_IN --> MCU
    MCU --> PROC
    PROC --> GPIO_OUT
    GPIO_OUT --> ACT
    PROC --> COMM
```

**Interpretation:** This is the canonical IoT data acquisition and actuation loop. GPIO serves as both the input (sensor reading) and output (actuator control) gateway.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define GPIO. List any four pin modes supported by a typical microcontroller.**

**Model Answer:**

**GPIO (General Purpose Input/Output)** is a software-configurable digital signal pin on a microcontroller whose direction (input or output) and logic level (HIGH or LOW) can be dynamically controlled via firmware. Each GPIO pin is internally connected to a direction register, an output data register, an input data register, and a configurable pull-up/pull-down resistor network.

The four standard pin modes are:

1. **INPUT** — Pin is in high-impedance state, used to read external digital signals. Requires external or internal biasing.
2. **OUTPUT** — Pin actively drives HIGH (VCC) or LOW (GND) using internal CMOS push-pull transistors.
3. **INPUT_PULLUP** — INPUT mode with an internal pull-up resistor (~30–50 kΩ) to VCC, used for active-LOW button interfacing.
4. **INPUT_PULLDOWN** — INPUT mode with an internal pull-down resistor to GND, used for active-HIGH button interfacing.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Explain the need for a current-limiting resistor when interfacing an LED to a GPIO pin. What happens if it is omitted?**

**Model Answer:**

An LED is a **non-ohmic, current-driven** device. Its I-V curve is exponential — a small increase in voltage beyond the forward voltage $V_F$ causes a disproportionately large increase in current, potentially exceeding the LED's maximum rated current (typically 20–30 mA for standard 5 mm LEDs).

If a GPIO pin (say, 3.3 V) is connected directly to an LED without a resistor, the LED will draw excessive current, leading to:

1. **Thermal runaway** — junction temperature rises, further increasing current → catastrophic LED burnout.
2. **GPIO pin damage** — the internal CMOS driver may source/sink more current than its rated 20 mA, causing metallization migration and eventual open-circuit failure.

A **current-limiting resistor R** in series with the LED ensures that the current remains at a safe value, calculated as:

$$R = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_{LED,desired}}$$

For example, with $V_{CC} = 3.3$ V, $V_F = 2.0$ V, and $I_{LED} = 10$ mA, $R = 130$ Ω (nearest standard 150 Ω).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In GPIO interfacing questions, students frequently lose marks by:
> 1. **Forgetting to mention the current-limiting resistor** for LEDs (2 marks deducted).
> 2. **Forgetting the flyback diode** across relay coils (2 marks deducted).
> 3. **Not justifying why pull-up/pull-down resistors are needed** for floating inputs (1 mark).
> 4. **Skipping unit conversions** (e.g., writing mA as A) — examiner penalizes 0.5 marks per error.
> 5. **Confusing INPUT_PULLUP logic** (button pressed = LOW, not HIGH) — this is a 1-mark conceptual loss.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain how a **Light Emitting Diode (LED)** is interfaced to a microcontroller GPIO pin. Derive the expression for the current-limiting resistor value. A red LED ($V_F = 2.0$ V, $I_{LED} = 15$ mA) is to be driven from a 3.3 V GPIO. Calculate the resistor value and its power rating. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the interfacing of a **push-button switch** to a GPIO pin using an external pull-up resistor. Explain why pull-up/pull-down resistors are mandatory for floating inputs. Briefly describe **contact bounce** and how it can be eliminated in software. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question A**

### Part (a) — LED Interfacing [7 Marks]

**Circuit Diagram Description (2 marks):**

The GPIO pin is connected to the **anode** of the LED through a series resistor R. The **cathode** of the LED is connected to GND. When the GPIO is driven HIGH (3.3 V), current flows from the pin → through R → through the LED (anode → cathode) → to GND.

**Derivation of Current-Limiting Resistor (3 marks):**

Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL):

$$V_{CC} = V_R + V_F$$

$$V_R = V_{CC} - V_F$$

Applying Ohm's Law:

$$I_{LED} = \frac{V_R}{R}$$

Rearranging:

$$R = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_{LED}}$$

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

Substituting values:

$$R = \frac{3.3 \text{ V} - 2.0 \text{ V}}{15 \text{ mA}} = \frac{1.3 \text{ V}}{0.015 \text{ A}} = 86.67 \text{ }\Omega$$

Nearest E12 standard value: **100 Ω**.

Power dissipation:

$$P_R = I_{LED}^{2} \cdot R = (0.015)^{2} \cdot 100 = 0.0225 \text{ W} = 22.5 \text{ mW}$$

Safe resistor power rating: **1/8 W (125 mW)**.

---

### Part (b) — Push-Button Interfacing [7 Marks]

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**

The push-button (normally-open, NO) is connected between the GPIO pin and GND. A pull-up resistor R (typically 10 kΩ) connects the GPIO pin to VCC (3.3 V). When the button is open, the pin is pulled to VCC and reads HIGH. When pressed, the pin is shorted to GND and reads LOW.

**Need for Pull-Up/Pull-Down Resistors (2 marks):**

A GPIO pin in INPUT mode is **high-impedance** (Hi-Z) — it draws no current and behaves like an open circuit. If left floating (no pull-up or pull-down), the input voltage is undefined and picks up electromagnetic noise (50 Hz mains hum, RF interference, static charge), causing spurious random transitions. A pull-up or pull-down resistor provides a **defined default logic level** when no external source is driving the pin.

**Contact Bounce and Software Debouncing (3 marks):**

Mechanical push-buttons use metal contacts that **physically vibrate** for 1–20 ms after closing, producing multiple spurious HIGH↔LOW transitions (bouncing). This causes the microcontroller to register a single press as 5–50 false presses, corrupting counters and state machines.

**Software Debouncing Algorithm:**

1. On detecting the first edge (e.g., a falling edge from HIGH to LOW), record the timestamp $t_0$.
2. Ignore any further edge transitions for a **debounce delay** $t_d$ (typically 5–50 ms).
3. After $t_d$ has elapsed, re-sample the pin and confirm the stable state.
4. If the state matches the originally detected edge, accept it as a valid press.

A simpler delay-based approach is to call `delay(50)` after the first detection before processing the input, though this blocks other tasks.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### **[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the interfacing of a **5 V electromagnetic relay** to a 3.3 V GPIO pin of an ESP32 microcontroller. Why can't the GPIO directly drive the relay? Design the driver circuit using an NPN BJT (2N2222, $\beta = 100$, $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V) and compute the base resistor. Given: relay coil resistance $= 200$ Ω, supply $= 5$ V, coil current $= 25$ mA. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **voltage-level shifting** using a resistive voltage divider. A 5 V sensor output must be connected to a 3.3 V ESP32 GPIO. Using $R_1 = 10$ kΩ, calculate $R_2$ such that the output does not exceed 3.0 V. Verify the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question B**

### Part (a) — Relay Driver Interfacing [7 Marks]

**Why GPIO Cannot Directly Drive the Relay (2 marks):**

A 5 V relay coil at 200 Ω draws:

$$I_{coil} = \frac{V_{coil}}{R_{coil}} = \frac{5 \text{ V}}{200 \text{ }\Omega} = 25 \text{ mA}$$

ESP32 GPIO can source/sink only **12 mA maximum**, and operates at 3.3 V (not 5 V). Direct connection would:
1. **Exceed the current rating** of the GPIO driver, causing thermal damage.
2. **Fail to energize the coil**, since the GPIO voltage (3.3 V) is insufficient to drive the 5 V relay to its rated pull-in current.

Hence, a **transistor driver circuit** is required.

**Driver Circuit Design (2 marks):**

A 2N2222 NPN BJT is used in common-emitter configuration:
- **Collector** → Relay coil → 5 V supply
- **Emitter** → GND
- **Base** → GPIO pin via base resistor $R_B$
- **Flyback diode** (1N4007) in reverse-biased parallel with the coil (cathode at +5 V, anode at collector)

**Base Resistor Calculation (3 marks):**

Required base current for saturation (with $\beta = 100$):
