$$I_B = \frac{I_C}{\beta} = \frac{25 \text{ mA}}{100} = 0.25 \text{ mA}$$

For reliable saturation, apply overdrive factor of 2:

$$I_{B,safe} = 0.5 \text{ mA}$$

Apply KVL (GPIO → $R_B$ → $V_{BE}$ → GND):

$$V_{CC} = I_{B,safe} \cdot R_B + V_{BE}$$

$$R_B = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{I_{B,safe}} = \frac{3.3 - 0.7}{0.5 \text{ mA}} = \frac{2.6 \text{ V}}{0.0005 \text{ A}} = 5200 \text{ }\Omega$$

Nearest standard value: **$R_B = 5.1$ kΩ**.

---

### Part (b) — Voltage Divider Level Shifter [7 Marks]

**Concept (2 marks):**

A **resistive voltage divider** scales a higher voltage down to a lower voltage using two resistors in series. It is a simple, low-cost method to interface a 5 V device with a 3.3 V microcontroller, but it is suitable only for low-frequency, low-current signals (e.g., sensor outputs, not high-speed buses).

**Circuit Diagram (1 mark):**

The 5 V sensor output connects to $R_1$. The other end of $R_1$ connects to $R_2$ (which goes to GND) AND to the ESP32 GPIO pin (junction node). The output voltage at the GPIO is:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

Given $V_{in} = 5.0$ V, $V_{out} = 3.0$ V (target), $R_1 = 10$ kΩ:

$$3.0 = 5.0 \cdot \frac{R_2}{10000 + R_2}$$

$$3.0 \cdot (10000 + R_2) = 5.0 \cdot R_2$$

$$30000 + 3.0 R_2 = 5.0 R_2$$

$$30000 = 2.0 R_2$$

$$R_2 = 15000 \text{ }\Omega = 15 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Verification (1 mark):**

$$V_{out} = 5.0 \cdot \frac{15000}{10000 + 15000} = 5.0 \cdot 0.6 = 3.0 \text{ V} \quad \checkmark$$

This is below ESP32's $V_{CC}$ (3.3 V) and well above $V_{IH}$ (2.475 V), so the GPIO will reliably register a logic HIGH.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for GPIO Interfacing**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- [ ] **GPIO = General Purpose Input/Output** — software-configurable digital pin.
- [ ] Each pin has internal **direction register, output register, input register, pull-up, pull-down, Schmitt trigger, ESD diodes**.
- [ ] Pin modes: **INPUT, OUTPUT, INPUT_PULLUP, INPUT_PULLDOWN** (memorize all four).
- [ ] CMOS push-pull output can **source** (when HIGH) and **sink** (when LOW) current.

### ⚡ Electrical Limits
- [ ] Standard GPIO current limit: **20 mA** (AVR), **12 mA** (ESP32) per pin.
- [ ] 3.3 V logic levels: $V_{IH} \geq 2.475$ V, $V_{IL} \leq 0.825$ V.
- [ ] Never connect an inductive load (relay, motor) without a **flyback diode**.

### 💡 LED Interfacing Rule
- [ ] Always use a **series current-limiting resistor**: $R = (V_{CC} - V_F) / I_{LED}$.
- [ ] Typical LED current: **5–15 mA**.

### 🔘 Button Interfacing Rule
- [ ] Floating inputs are **forbidden** — use pull-up or pull-down.
- [ ] **INPUT_PULLUP** is most common → button pressed reads **LOW (0)**.
- [ ] **Debounce** mechanical switches in hardware (RC + Schmitt) or software (delay).

### 🔌 Relay Driving Rule
- [ ] GPIO cannot directly drive a relay → use a **BJT driver** (2N2222, BC547) or **MOSFET** (IRF540N, AO3400).
- [ ] Always include a **flyback diode** (1N4007 / 1N5819) across the coil.

### 🔀 Voltage Level Shifting Rule
- [ ] 5 V → 3.3 V: use **resistive voltage divider** for slow signals or a **level shifter IC** (TXS0108E, BSS138) for high-speed signals.
- [ ] Never feed > 3.3 V directly into an ESP32 GPIO — it will damage the IC.

### 📚 Examiner's Pet Questions
- [ ] "Define GPIO with internal block diagram." (3 marks — every year)
- [ ] "Calculate the current-limiting resistor for an LED." (numerical)
- [ ] "Explain the need for pull-up resistors." (3 marks)
- [ ] "Interface a relay to a microcontroller — explain with circuit." (7 marks)
- [ ] "What is contact bounce? How is it eliminated?" (3–7 marks)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfacing with Peripherals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCGT503 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS FOR IOT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Interfacing with Peripherals # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Peripheral Interfacing** is the engineering discipline of electrically and logically connecting external Input/Output (I/O) devices — such as sensors, actuators, displays, and communication modules — to a microcontroller or microprocessor through its General-Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) pins, specialized hardware peripherals, and standardized bus protocols so that data and control signals can be exchanged in a controlled, timely, and reliable manner. In the context of **PCCGT503 (Microprocessors & Microcontrollers for IoT)**, peripheral interfacing forms the critical bridge between the digital processing core (CPU/MCU) and the physical world. Every IoT node — whether a smart thermostat, a wearable fitness band, or an industrial gateway — is fundamentally a microcontroller **interfaced** to one or more peripherals. ### 1.1.1 Categories of Peripherals | Category | Function | Examples | |----------|----------|----------| | **Input Peripherals** | Sense the physical world | Push buttons, Keypads, Temperature sensors (LM35, DHT11), LDR, IR receiver | | **Output Peripherals** | Affect the physical world | LEDs, 7-segment displays, LCDs, Buzzers, DC/Stepper/Servo motors, Relays | | **Communication Peripherals** | Exchange data with other devices | UART (Bluetooth HC-05), SPI (SD card), I2C (RTC DS1307), CAN | | **Storage Peripherals** | Retain data persistently | External EEPROM (24Cxx), SD cards, Flash memory | | **Mixed-Signal Peripherals** | Convert between analog and digital domains | DAC, ADC, PWM-controlled devices | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Body-Brain Metaphor > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Analogy:** Think of the microcontroller as the **human brain** and the peripherals as the **senses (eyes, ears, skin) and limbs (hands, legs, mouth)**. > > - **GPIO pins** → neurons carrying tiny electrical signals. > - **Sensors** → sensory organs (eyes for light/light sensors, skin for temperature/temperature sensors). > - **Actuators** → muscles and limbs (motors, LEDs, relays). > - **Communication modules** → the vocal cords and ears (UART, SPI, I2C) that let one brain "talk" to another. > - **Drivers / interfacing circuits** → the nervous system that amplifies the weak signal from the brain to a strength strong enough to move a muscle. > > Just as your brain cannot directly "see" — it needs eyes — a microcontroller cannot "know" the temperature without a sensor. **Interfacing** is the act of connecting the two together so meaningful information flows between them. ## 1.3 Why Interfacing Matters in IoT > [!IMPORTANT] > An IoT device is essentially a **three-layer stack**: > 1. **Sensing layer** (peripherals + sensors) > 2. **Processing layer** (MCU/MPU) > 3. **Communication layer** (wireless/wired transceivers) > > The *sensing* and *communication* layers are entirely made of peripherals. A brilliant MCU is useless without correctly interfaced peripherals. ## 1.4 Core Technical Vocabulary (KTU High-Yield) - **GPIO (General-Purpose Input/Output):** A software-configurable pin that can be programmed as input or output. - **Current Sourcing vs. Sinking:** Sourcing = MCU supplies current to load; Sinking = MCU absorbs current from load. - **Pull-up / Pull-down Resistor:** A resistor used to define a known default logic level on a floating input line (typical value: **10 kΩ**). - **Open-Drain / Open-Collector Output:** An output that can only pull the line LOW; needs an external pull-up to go HIGH (used in I2C). - **Sink Current (I_OL):** Maximum current a pin can absorb when LOW (typical: **20 mA** for most MCUs). - **Source Current (I_OH):** Maximum current a pin can supply when HIGH (typical: **20 mA**). - **Fan-out:** The number of digital inputs that a single output can reliably drive. - **Memory-Mapped I/O vs. Isolated I/O:** Two methods of addressing peripherals in the address space. - **Handshaking:** Two-way coordination between MCU and peripheral (using Ready/Busy or ACK lines). # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The GPIO — Heart of All Interfacing A modern microcontroller GPIO pin is **not just a wire**. Internally, it contains: 1. A **tri-state buffer** to drive the line HIGH, LOW, or Hi-Z. 2. **Configurable pull-up and pull-down resistors** (typically 30 kΩ – 50 kΩ internal, 10 kΩ external reference). 3. **Schmitt trigger input** for noise immunity with defined switching thresholds (V_IL, V_IH). 4. **Drive-strength control** (2 mA / 8 mA / 12 mA modes in many ARM Cortex-M MCUs). 5. **Alternate function multiplexer** to route peripheral functions (UART TX, SPI MOSI, I2C SDA, ADC channel, etc.) onto the pin. ### 2.1.1 Logical Flow of a GPIO Read Operation 1. CPU executes `READ GPIOx_IDR` instruction. 2. The Input Data Register (IDR) latches the *current* logic level on the pin (after the Schmitt trigger). 3. The value is returned to the CPU in a single clock cycle. 4. The CPU acts on this value (e.g., `if (button == PRESSED) ...`). ### 2.1.2 Logical Flow of a GPIO Write Operation 1. CPU executes `WRITE GPIOx_ODR = 1`. 2. The Output Data Register (ODR) bit is set. 3. The output driver (push-pull) drives the pin HIGH (≈ V_DD) or LOW (≈ 0 V). 4. The connected peripheral reacts (e.g., LED turns ON, relay energizes). ## 2.2 Electrical Considerations — The Three Golden Rules > [!NOTE] > **Rule 1 — Voltage Compatibility:** V_OH of MCU ≥ V_IH of peripheral, and V_OL of MCU ≤ V_IL of peripheral. > > **Rule 2 — Current Limiting:** Never exceed the pin's rated I_OL / I_OH. Always size the load resistor to keep current within safe limits. > > **Rule 3 — Protection:** Use series resistors, clamp diodes, or optocouplers when interfacing with inductive loads (relays, motors) or higher-voltage circuits. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield for ESE Problems) | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units / Typical Value | Used For | |---|---------------------|------------|----------------------|----------| | 1 | **LED Current-Limiting Resistor** | $R = \dfrac{V_{DD} - V_{F}}{I_{LED}}$ | Ω (V_F ≈ 1.8 V for red, 3.2 V for white) | Driving an LED from a GPIO | | 2 | **Power Dissipated in Resistor** | $P_{R} = I_{LED}^{2} \cdot R$ | W | Selecting resistor wattage | | 3 | **Pull-up / Pull-down Resistor** | $R_{PU} = \dfrac{V_{DD} - V_{IL}}{I_{LEAK}}$ | Ω (typical 10 kΩ) | Defining a default logic level | | 4 | **ADC Resolution** | $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^{n}}$ | Volts per LSB | Quantization step of n-bit ADC | | 5 | **ADC Digital Output** | $D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{IN}}{V_{LSB}} \right\rfloor$ | Decimal count (0 to 2^n − 1) | Converting analog to digital | | 6 | **Baud Rate Divisor (UART)** | $BR_{div} = \dfrac{f_{CLK}}{16 \cdot \text{BaudRate}}$ | Integer (or fraction) | Setting UART speed | | 7 | **PWM Duty Cycle** | $D\% = \dfrac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} \times 100$ | Percent (%) | Motor speed / LED brightness | | 8 | **PWM Average Voltage** | $V_{avg} = D\% \cdot V_{DD}$ | Volts | Equivalent DC seen by motor | | 9 | **RC Time Constant** | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | Seconds (s) | Debounce delay for switches | | 10 | **Power in Load (DC Motor)** | $P = V \cdot I$ | Watts | Motor driver selection | | 11 | **Relay Coil Current** | $I_{coil} = \dfrac{V_{coil}}{R_{coil}}$ | A (e.g., 30 mA for 5 V relay) | Sizing the driver transistor base resistor | | 12 | **Voltage Divider (Sensor Scaling)** | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_{2}}{R_{1} + R_{2}}$ | Volts | Scaling down a sensor voltage to ADC range | | 13 | **Thermistor Beta Equation** | $T = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{T_{0}} + \dfrac{1}{\beta} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{R}{R_{0}}\right)}$ | Kelvin | Converting thermistor resistance to temperature | | 14 | **I2C Pull-up Sizing** | $R_{PU(min)} = \dfrac{V_{DD} - 0.4\text{ V}}{3\text{ mA}}$ | Ω | Minimum I2C bus pull-up resistor | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility of These Equations:** Every one of these formulas appears in at least one KTU university exam question. In IoT product development, the LED resistor and ADC resolution equations are used **daily** by embedded firmware engineers. ## 2.4 Communication Peripheral Protocols — Quick Comparison | Protocol | Wires | Speed | Topology | Distance | Used For | |----------|-------|-------|----------|----------|----------| | **UART** | 2 (TX, RX) | Up to 1 Mbps (typical 9600–115200 baud) | Point-to-point | Short (<15 m) | Debug console, Bluetooth (HC-05), GPS | | **SPI** | 4 (MOSI, MISO, SCK, CS) | Up to 10+ Mbps | Master–multi-slave (with multiple CS) | Short PCB-level | SD cards, displays, fast sensors | | **I2C** | 2 (SDA, SCL) | 100 kbps (Standard), 400 kbps (Fast), 3.4 Mbps (High-Speed) | Multi-master, multi-slave (up to 127 devices) | Short PCB-level | EEPROM, RTC, low-speed sensors | | **CAN** | 2 (CANH, CANL) | 1 Mbps | Multi-master bus | Long (with twisted pair) | Automotive, industrial | | **1-Wire** | 1 (Data) | 16 kbps | Master–multi-slave | Medium | DS18B20 temperature sensor | > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for IoT:** Most IoT peripherals (BME280, MPU6050, SSD1306 OLED, DS1307 RTC, AT24C EEPROM) use **I2C or SPI**. A KTU student must be able to draw the bus connections and describe the start/stop/acknowledge conditions. ## 2.5 Memory-Mapped I/O vs Isolated I/O | Feature | Memory-Mapped I/O | Isolated I/O | |---------|-------------------|--------------| | Address Space | Peripherals share the memory address space | Separate I/O address space | | Instructions | Any memory-access instruction (MOV, LDR, STR) | Special IN/OUT instructions (x86) | | Flexibility | High — full instruction set usable | Limited | | Address Space Size | Reduced for memory | Full memory available | | Example Architectures | ARM (Cortex-M), AVR, PIC, RISC-V | 8085, 8086 (in 16-bit mode) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Highlight:** The 8085 microprocessor uses **Isolated I/O** with dedicated `IN` and `OUT` instructions. The ARM Cortex-M and most modern microcontrollers use **Memory-Mapped I/O**. This is a **frequently asked two-mark question**. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: LED Current-Limiting Resistor > **Problem:** A red LED with forward voltage V_F = 1.8 V and rated forward current I_LED = 15 mA must be driven from a 3.3 V GPIO pin of an ESP32/ARM MCU. Calculate the series current-limiting resistor and the power dissipated in it. ### Given Data - Supply voltage: $V_{DD} = 3.3 \text{ V}$ - LED forward voltage: $V_{F} = 1.8 \text{ V}$ - Required LED current: $I_{LED} = 15 \text{ mA} = 0.015 \text{ A}$ ### Solution — Step by Step **Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the loop (V_DD → Resistor → LED → GND).** $$ V_{DD} - V_{R} - V_{F} = 0 $$ Therefore the voltage drop across the resistor is: $$ V_{R} = V_{DD} - V_{F} $$ $$ V_{R} = 3.3 - 1.8 = 1.5 \text{ V} $$ **Step 2 — Apply Ohm's Law to compute the required resistance.** $$ R = \dfrac{V_{R}}{I_{LED}} $$ $$ R = \dfrac{1.5}{0.015} = 100 \; \Omega $$ **Step 3 — Choose the next higher standard E12 value → 100 Ω (exact match) or 120 Ω for safety margin.** **Step 4 — Recalculate the actual LED current with the chosen 100 Ω resistor.** $$ I_{LED(actual)} = \dfrac{V_{DD} - V_{F}}{R} = \dfrac{1.5}{100} = 15 \text{ mA} $$ **Step 5 — Compute the power dissipation in the resistor.** $$ P_{R} = I_{LED}^{2} \cdot R $$ $$ P_{R} = (0.015)^{2} \cdot 100 = 0.000225 \cdot 100 = 0.0225 \text{ W} $$ **Step 6 — Choose the next standard wattage rating (rule of thumb: use 2× the calculated value).** $$ P_{rated} \geq 2 \cdot P_{R} = 0.045 \text{ W} \Rightarrow \textbf{Use a 1/8 W (0.125 W) resistor} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answer:** **R = 100 Ω, 1/8 W**. > This is the canonical KTU-style answer — students often forget the **wattage rating**, costing 1 mark. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: ADC Resolution and Digital Output > **Problem:** A 10-bit ADC is used with a reference voltage V_REF = 3.3 V. The analog input is V_IN = 1.25 V. Calculate the resolution (voltage per LSB) and the resulting digital output code. ### Given Data - ADC resolution: $n = 10$ bits - Reference voltage: $V_{REF} = 3.3 \text{ V}$ - Analog input: $V_{IN} = 1.25 \text{ V}$ ### Solution — Step by Step **Step 1 — Compute the total number of quantization levels.** $$ N_{levels} = 2^{n} = 2^{10} = 1024 $$ These correspond to codes **0** (representing 0 V) through **1023** (representing V_REF). **Step 2 — Compute the voltage represented by 1 LSB (the resolution).** $$ V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^{n}} $$ $$ V_{LSB} = \dfrac{3.3}{1024} = 0.003223 \text{ V} \approx 3.22 \text{ mV per LSB} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the digital output code for V_IN = 1.25 V.** $$ D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{IN}}{V_{LSB}} \right\rfloor $$ $$ D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{1.25}{0.003223} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 387.6 \right\rfloor = 387 $$ **Step 4 — Convert to binary (12-bit conversion is for 12-bit, here 10-bit):** $$ 387_{10} = \mathbf{0b110000011} \; (10\text{-bit}) = 0x183 $$ **Step 5 — Quantization error bound (always present).** $$ E_{q} \le \pm \dfrac{V_{LSB}}{2} = \pm 1.61 \text{ mV} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering insight:** Doubling the ADC bits (10 → 12) reduces V_LSB from 3.22 mV to 0.806 mV, which is why audio/precision sensors use 12, 16, or 24-bit ADCs. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: UART Baud-Rate Register Setting > **Problem:** Configure the UART peripheral of an STM32-like MCU for **9600 baud, 8N1** operation, given a peripheral clock f_CLK = 16 MHz. Compute the USARTDIV value (ignoring fractional oversampling for simplicity). ### Given Data - Clock: $f_{CLK} = 16 \text{ MHz} = 16 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}$ - Baud rate: $BR = 9600 \text{ bps}$ - Oversampling: 16 (default) ### Solution — Step by Step **Step 1 — Use the baud-rate formula.** $$ USARTDIV = \dfrac{f_{CLK}}{16 \cdot BR} $$ $$ USARTDIV = \dfrac{16 \times 10^{6}}{16 \cdot 9600} = \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000}{9600} = 104.1667 $$ **Step 2 — Split into integer and fractional parts.** $$ USARTDIV = 104 + 0.1667 $$ **Step 3 — Convert the fractional part to the 4-bit register field (multiply by 16).** $$ 0.1667 \times 16 = 2.667 \approx 3 $$ So the **BRR register = 0x0683** (integer = 104 = 0x68, fraction = 3). **Step 4 — Verify the actual achieved baud rate.** $$ BR_{actual} = \dfrac{f_{CLK}}{16 \cdot \left(104 + \dfrac{3}{16}\right)} = \dfrac{16 \times 10^{6}}{16 \cdot 104.1875} = 9596.16 \text{ bps} $$ **Step 5 — Compute the percent error.** $$ \% \text{ Error} = \dfrac{9600 - 9596.16}{9600} \times 100 = 0.04\,\% $$ This is **well within** the UART receiver tolerance of ±2 %, so the configuration is valid. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Trap:** A common student error is to forget the **÷16 oversampling factor**. Always check whether the MCU uses 16× or 8× oversampling mode — the divisor changes by a factor of 2. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Switch Debounce RC Time Constant > **Problem:** A tactile push-button is connected to a GPIO with a 10 kΩ pull-up. Calculate the capacitor value needed to achieve a 20 ms debounce time constant. ### Given Data - Pull-up resistor: $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega = 10{,}000 \; \Omega$ - Desired time constant: $\tau = 20 \text{ ms} = 0.020 \text{ s}$ ### Solution — Step by Step **Step 1 — Use the RC time constant formula.** $$ \tau = R \cdot C $$ **Step 2 — Solve for C.** $$ C = \dfrac{\tau}{R} = \dfrac{0.020}{10{,}000} = 2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F} = 2 \; \mu F $$ **Step 3 — Verify the charge/discharge behavior.** The voltage on the capacitor follows: $$ V_{C}(t) = V_{final} + (V_{initial} - V_{final}) \cdot e^{-t/\tau} $$ After $5\tau = 100 \text{ ms}$, the capacitor is **> 99 % charged**, far longer than the typical 5–10 ms mechanical bounce of a tactile switch. > [!NOTE] > **Alternative (preferred in modern firmware):** Skip the RC filter entirely and **debounce in software** using a 20–50 ms timer-based polling routine. This is more reliable and saves an external component. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Interfacing Block Diagram — IoT Node Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB MCU["Microcontroller Core
CPU + RAM + Flash"] GPIO["GPIO Controller
8 to 100+ pins"] UART["UART Module
Asynchronous Serial"] SPI["SPI Module
4-wire synchronous"] I2C["I2C Module
2-wire bus"] ADC["ADC Module
10 to 12-bit"] PWM["PWM / Timer Module"] EXTMEM["External Memory Bus
FSMC / EBI"] LED["LED Output
Indicator / Status"] BTN["Push Button Input
User Control"] KEYPAD["4x4 Keypad
Matrix"] LCD["16x2 Character LCD
HD44780"] OLED["OLED Display SSD1306
I2C 128x64"] MOTOR["DC / Stepper Motor
via Driver L298N"] SERVO["Servo Motor SG90
PWM control"] RELAY["Relay Module
5V coil"] SENS_T["Temperature LM35
Analog"] SENS_D["DHT11 Digital
Temp + Humid"] SENS_L["LDR / Light Sensor
Analog"] SENS_M["MPU6050 IMU
I2C 6-axis"] BT["Bluetooth HC-05
UART"] ESP["ESP8266 WiFi
UART bridge"] SD["SD Card
SPI"] RTC["RTC DS1307
I2C"] EEP["EEPROM AT24C256
I2C"] MCU --- GPIO MCU --- UART MCU --- SPI MCU --- I2C MCU --- ADC MCU --- PWM MCU --- EXTMEM GPIO --- LED GPIO --- BTN GPIO --- KEYPAD GPIO --- LCD GPIO --- RELAY GPIO --- MOTOR GPIO --- SERVO I2C --- OLED I2C --- SENS_M I2C --- RTC I2C --- EEP SPI --- SD UART --- BT UART --- ESP ADC --- SENS_T ADC --- SENS_L GPIO --- SENS_D ``` ## 4.2 LED + Current-Limiting Resistor Interface (Schematic Representation) ```mermaid flowchart LR PIN["MCU GPIO Pin
3.3 V or 5 V"] R["R = 100 ohm
Current Limiter"] LED["LED
V_F = 1.8 V
I_F = 15 mA"] GND["GND"] PIN --> R R --> LED LED --> GND ``` > **Operating principle:** When the GPIO is driven HIGH, current flows from the pin through the resistor and LED to ground. The resistor drops the excess voltage (V_DD − V_F) so that the LED receives its rated current. ## 4.3 Relay Interfacing Circuit (MCU → Transistor → Relay Coil) ```mermaid flowchart TB MCU_PIN["MCU GPIO Pin
3.3 V logic"] RB["R_B = 1 kohm
Base Resistor"] Q["NPN Transistor
BC547 / 2N2222"] D["1N4007 Flyback Diode
across coil"] COIL["Relay Coil
5 V, 70 ohm"] VCC["V_CC = 5 V"] GND["GND"] SW["Relay Contacts
Switch AC/DC load"] MCU_PIN --> RB RB --> Q Q --> COIL COIL --> VCC Q --> GND COIL --- D SW --- COIL ``` > **Operating principle:** > - When the GPIO is HIGH → base current flows → transistor saturates → coil energizes → contacts close → AC/DC load is switched. > - The **flyback diode** (D) protects the transistor from the reverse-EMF spike when the coil is de-energized. **Never omit it.** ## 4.4 I2C Bus Topology with Multiple Peripherals ```mermaid flowchart LR MCU_SDA["MCU SDA Pin"] MCU_SCL["MCU SCL Pin"] RPU1["R_PU 4.7 kohm to V_DD"] RPU2["R_PU 4.7 kohm to V_DD"] A["Sensor A
Addr 0x68"] B["RTC
Addr 0x51"] C["EEPROM
Addr 0x50"] D["LCD I2C
Addr 0x27"] MCU_SDA --- RPU1 MCU_SCL --- RPU2 MCU_SDA --- A MCU_SDA --- B MCU_SDA --- C MCU_SDA --- D MCU_SCL --- A MCU_SCL --- B MCU_SCL --- C MCU_SCL --- D ``` > **Operating principle:** All SDA and SCL lines are wired-AND. The MCU addresses exactly one device at a time using the 7-bit address field in the I2C frame. Pull-ups are mandatory because devices have open-drain outputs. ## 4.5 Motor Driver (L298N) Interfacing Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR MCU["MCU
PWM + DIR GPIO"] L298N["L298N
H-Bridge Motor Driver"] MOTOR["DC Motor
6 to 12 V"] PSU["External PSU
7 to 12 V"] MCU -- PWM --> L298N MCU -- DIR --> L298N PSU --> L298N L298N --> MOTOR ``` > **Operating principle:** The MCU cannot drive a motor directly (motors need 100s of mA to Amps). The L298N driver accepts low-power logic signals from the MCU and switches the high-current motor supply. PWM controls speed, GPIO direction controls rotation. ## 4.6 Signal Flow of a Sensor-to-MCU Analog Read ```mermaid flowchart LR PHY["Physical Quantity
Temperature / Light / Pressure"] SENS["Sensor
LM35 / LDR / MPX"] COND["Signal Conditioning
Amplifier + Filter"] VDIV["Voltage Divider
if needed"] ADC["MCU ADC
10 to 12-bit"] PROC["CPU Processing
Scaling + Calibration"] OUT["Digital Reading
Celsius / Lux / kPa"] COMM["UART / I2C / WiFi
Transmit to Gateway"] PHY --> SENS SENS --> COND COND --> VDIV VDIV --> ADC ADC --> PROC PROC --> OUT OUT --> COMM ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Q1]** — *3 marks* > > **Q: Differentiate between Memory-Mapped I/O and Isolated I/O. Which type is used in the 8085 microprocessor?** > > **Model Answer:** > > | Aspect | Memory-Mapped I/O | Isolated I/O | > |--------|-------------------|--------------| > | Address Space | Shared with memory | Separate I/O space | > | Instructions | Normal memory instructions (`MOV`, `LDR`, `STR`) | Special I/O instructions (`IN`, `OUT`) | > | Address Bits Used | Full address bus width (e.g., 16/32 bits) | Limited I/O address bus (8 bits in 8085) | > | Flexibility | High — any memory instruction can access I/O | Limited | > | Examples | ARM Cortex-M, AVR, PIC, RISC-V | **8085, 8086** | > > **Conclusion:** The **8085 microprocessor uses Isolated I/O** because it has dedicated `IN port_addr` and `OUT port_addr` instructions with an 8-bit port address. --- > **[KTU University Exam – Model Q2]** — *3 marks* > > **Q: Why is a current-limiting resistor essential when interfacing an LED to a microcontroller GPIO? What is the typical value?** > > **Model Answer:** > > An LED is a **non-ohmic** (non-linear) device. Once its forward voltage (V_F) is reached, its resistance drops sharply, causing a large uncontrolled current that can **destroy the LED** and **overload the MCU pin**. A series current-limiting resistor restricts the current to a safe, rated value. > > Typical calculation: $R = (V_{DD} - V_{F}) / I_{LED}$. > > For a 3.3 V MCU, red LED (V_F = 1.8 V, I = 15 mA): **R = 100 Ω**, 1/8 W. > > **Typical range used in practice: 100 Ω to 470 Ω.** --- ## 5.2 Part B Question Choice 1 (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Q3A]** — *14 marks (Module 3 focus)* ### Question A **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain how an LED can be interfaced to an 8051/ARM Cortex-M microcontroller GPIO. Derive the expression for the current-limiting resistor. Assume $V_{DD} = 5$ V, $V_{F} = 2$ V, and $I_{LED} = 10$ mA. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the interfacing of a 16×2 LCD (HD44780) in **4-bit mode** with an ARM MCU. List the signals used and explain the initialization sequence. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution — Part (a) **Circuit Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["P1.0 (GPIO)"] R["R = 300 ohm"] L["LED"] G["GND"] P1 --> R --> L --> G ``` **Step 1 — Compute the required resistor:** $$ R = \dfrac{V_{DD} - V_{F}}{I_{LED}} = \dfrac{5 - 2}{10 \text{ mA}} = \dfrac{3}{0.010} = 300 \; \Omega $$ **Step 2 — Power dissipated in the resistor:** $$ P_{R} = I^{2} \cdot R = (0.010)^{2} \cdot 300 = 0.03 \text{ W} = 30 \text{ mW} $$ **Step 3 — Use a standard 1/8 W (125 mW) resistor — comfortably rated.** **Step 4 — Explanation of operation:** When the MCU drives the pin HIGH, current flows through the resistor and LED to ground, illuminating the LED. When the pin is LOW, no current flows, and the LED is OFF. The resistor limits current to a safe 10 mA. --- ### Model Solution — Part (b) **Block Diagram of 4-bit LCD Interface:** ```mermaid flowchart LR MCU["ARM MCU"] D4["D4 (Pin PB0)"] D5["D5 (Pin PB1)"] D6["D6 (Pin PB2)"] D7["D7 (Pin PB3)"] RS["RS (Pin PB4)"] EN["EN (Pin PB5)"] LCD["HD44780 LCD
16x2"] RW["RW to GND
(always write)"] VEE["VEE Contrast
via 10k pot"] MCU --- D4 MCU --- D5 MCU --- D6 MCU --- D7 MCU --- RS MCU --- EN D4 --- LCD D5 --- LCD D6 --- LCD D7 --- LCD RS --- LCD EN --- LCD RW --- LCD VEE --- LCD ``` **Signals used in 4-bit mode:** | Signal | Direction | Function | |--------|-----------|----------| | **RS** | MCU → LCD | 0 = Command, 1 = Data | | **EN** | MCU → LCD | High-to-Low edge latches data | | **D4 – D7** | Bidirectional | 4-bit data bus (only upper nibble used) | | **RW** | MCU → LCD | Tied LOW for write-only mode (saves 1 GPIO) | | **VEE** | Potentiometer | Contrast adjustment | **Initialization sequence (4-bit mode):** 1. Wait ≥ 15 ms after V_DD rises (power-on delay). 2. Send command `0x03` (in 8-bit mode) three times with delays (this is a manufacturer-mandated wake-up sequence). 3. Send command `0x02` to switch to **4-bit mode**. 4. Configure: `0x28` → 4-bit, 2 lines, 5×7 font. 5. Display control: `0x0C` → Display ON, cursor OFF, blink OFF. 6. Clear display: `0x01`. 7. Entry mode: `0x06` → Increment cursor, no shift. 8. LCD is now ready. Every subsequent byte is sent as **two nibbles** (high nibble first, then low nibble), each clocked by EN. > **Why 4-bit mode is preferred in IoT:** It saves **4 GPIO pins** (D0–D3 not used), allowing the MCU to dedicate those pins to sensors or other peripherals — a critical resource in space-constrained IoT designs. --- ## 5.3 Part B Question Choice 2 (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Q3B]** — *14 marks (Module 3 focus)* ### Question B **(a)** Explain the I2C protocol with a neat diagram. List the conditions for **START**, **STOP**, and **ACK** on the bus. How is a slave device addressed? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Interface a DS1307 Real-Time Clock (I2C address `0x68`) to an ARM MCU. Write the sequence of bytes transmitted by the master to **read the seconds register** of the RTC. Explain each byte's significance. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution — Part (a) **I2C Bus Topology:** ```mermaid flowchart LR MASTER["Master
ARM MCU"] SDA["SDA Line
bidirectional"] SCL["SCL Line
master-driven"] RPU_S["R_PU 4.7k to V_DD"] RPU_C["R_PU 4.7k to V_DD"] S1["Slave 1
0x68 (DS1307)"] S2["Slave 2
0x51 (EEPROM)"] S3["Slave 3
0x27 (LCD)"] MASTER --- SDA MASTER --- SCL SDA --- RPU_S SCL --- RPU_C SDA --- S1 SDA --- S2 SDA --- S3 SCL --- S1 SCL --- S2 SCL --- S3 ``` **Key Bus Conditions:** - **START (S):** SDA transitions **HIGH → LOW** while SCL is HIGH. Initiates a transfer. - **STOP (P):** SDA transitions **LOW → HIGH** while SCL is HIGH. Terminates a transfer. - **ACK:** After every byte, the receiver pulls SDA LOW for one SCL clock cycle. - **NACK:** Receiver leaves SDA HIGH (no acknowledge — used to signal end-of-read). **Slave Addressing (7-bit mode):** Each slave has a unique 7-bit address. After the START condition, the master transmits **1 byte = 7-bit address + 1 R/W bit** (total 8 bits). The slave whose address matches ACKs the byte, claiming the bus for that transaction. **Example:** DS1307 has a fixed address of `1101000`. The complete first byte sent by the master to write to it is `11010000` (binary) = `0xD0` (write), and to read from it is `11010001` = `0xD1` (read). --- ### Model Solution — Part (b) **Sequence to Read the DS1307 Seconds Register:** | Step | Byte | Binary | Hex | Direction | Meaning | |------|------|--------|-----|-----------|---------| | 1 | START | — | — | Master generates | Wake up all slaves | | 2 | Address + Write | `11010000` | `0xD0` | Master → Slave | Address DS1307, write mode | | 3 | ACK | — | — | Slave → Master | DS1307 acknowledges | | 4 | Register Pointer | `00000000` | `0x00` | Master → Slave | Point to seconds register | | 5 | ACK | — | — | Slave → Master | DS1307 ACKs | | 6 | Repeated START (Sr) | — | — | Master generates | Reverse direction without releasing bus | | 7 | Address + Read | `11010001` | `0xD1` | Master → Slave | Address DS1307, read mode | | 8 | ACK | — | — | Slave → Master | DS1307 ACKs | | 9 | Seconds Value | `0XXXXXXX` | varies | Slave → Master | DS1307 sends seconds (BCD format) | | 10 | NACK | — | — | Master → Slave | Master NACKs to signal "no more bytes needed" | | 11 | STOP | — | — | Master generates | Release the bus | **Explanation of the BCD seconds byte:** The DS1307 stores time in **Binary-Coded Decimal (BCD)**. So the seconds register value `0x45` means **45 seconds** (not 69 in hex). The MCU must convert BCD → decimal by: - Tens digit = `(value >> 4) & 0x07` (max 5 for seconds) - Ones digit = `value & 0x0F` --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting the pull-up resistor in I2C diagrams** (−2 marks). I2C is an open-drain bus; the diagram *must* show R_PU to V_DD. > 2. **Wrong R/W bit interpretation.** The R/W bit is the **LSB** of the first byte (after the 7-bit address), not a separate byte. > 3. **Confusing START and STOP conditions** — both occur when SCL is HIGH, but the SDA edge direction is opposite. > 4. **Forgetting BCD conversion** in RTC questions. Many students report `0x45` as "45 seconds" which is correct, but `0x59` as "89 seconds" — wrong, it is 59 seconds. > 5. **Omitting the flyback diode** across the relay coil in relay-driver circuits — 1 mark deduction. > 6. **Mixing up sink and source current ratings.** Most MCUs can source *less* current than they can sink. Always check the datasheet. > 7. **Forgetting to specify resistor wattage** in LED calculations — half-mark deduction. > 8. **Not showing the voltage divider** when a sensor output exceeds V_REF of the ADC. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Bullets — Memorize Before Exam:** > > **GPIO Fundamentals** > - GPIO pins have **multiple alternate functions** controlled by a multiplexer. > - Maximum **sink current ≈ 20 mA**, **source current ≈ 20 mA** (datasheet-dependent). > - Total pin current for the entire port is also limited (e.g., 100 mA per port on STM32). > - Always configure pin as input *before* reading to avoid contention. > > **LED Interfacing** > - Formula: $R = (V_{DD} - V_F) / I_F$. > - **Always include a current-limiting resistor** — never connect an LED directly to a pin. > - Typical red V_F = 1.8 V, white/blue V_F = 3.0–3.4 V. > > **Switch / Keypad Debounce** > - Mechanical switches bounce for 5–20 ms. > - Use an **RC filter** (τ ≈ 20 ms) OR **software debounce** with a 50 ms timer. > - 4×4 matrix keypad uses **8 lines** (4 rows + 4 columns) with **scanning** technique. > > **Relay Interfacing** > - MCU cannot drive a relay coil directly — use a **transistor driver** (BC547/2N2222). > - **Flyback diode (1N4007)** across the coil is **mandatory**. > - For AC loads, use an **optocoupler** for galvanic isolation. > > **Motor Interfacing** > - **DC motor:** Use H-bridge (L298N) with PWM for speed and DIR for direction. > - **Stepper motor:** Use dedicated driver (ULN2003, A4988) with step/dir pulses. > - **Servo motor:** Single PWM line, period 20 ms, pulse 1–2 ms controls 0°–180°. > - Motors draw **huge inrush currents** — use a separate supply, not the MCU's 5 V pin. > > **Display Interfacing** > - **16×2 LCD (HD44780):** 4-bit mode uses **6 GPIO lines** (RS, EN, D4–D7). > - **OLED SSD1306:** I2C address `0x3C`, 128×64 pixels, uses `HAL_I2C` or equivalent. > - **7-segment display:** Common-cathode (sink) or common-anode (source) — use BCD-to-7-seg decoder (7447/4511) for multi-digit. > > **Communication Peripherals** > - **UART:** Asynchronous, **baud rate = bits per second**; use 9600/115200 as safe defaults. > - **SPI:** 4 wires, full-duplex, **faster** than I2C; uses chip-select (CS) lines. > - **I2C:** 2 wires, **open-drain + pull-up** mandatory, **7-bit address** + 1 R/W bit. > - **I2C speeds:** Standard 100 kHz, Fast 400 kHz, Fast-Plus 1 MHz, High-Speed 3.4 MHz. > - Always check for **bus collision** in multi-master I2C. > > **ADC/DAC Interfacing** > - **Resolution:** $V_{LSB} = V_{REF} / 2^{n}$. > - **Quantization error:** $\pm V_{LSB} / 2$. > - **Sampling theorem:** Sample at **≥ 2× the highest signal frequency** (Nyquist). > - **Sensor scaling:** Use a voltage divider or op-amp to bring the sensor output into the ADC range. > > **Memory Interfacing** > - 24Cxx **EEPROM** is I2C-based, address range `0x50`–`0x57` (hardware pins A0–A2). > - SD cards use **SPI mode** for MCU interfacing (simple, but slower than SDIO mode). > > **Memory-Mapped vs Isolated I/O** > - **8085** = Isolated I/O (`IN`/`OUT` instructions, 8-bit port address). > - **ARM Cortex-M / AVR / PIC** = Memory-Mapped I/O (peripherals in the memory map). > > **Common KTU One-Liners** > - Pull-up resistor typical value = **10 kΩ**. > - I2C pull-up minimum = **4.7 kΩ** at 5 V, **2.2 kΩ** at 3.3 V. > - LED current limit typical = **10–20 mA**. > - UART default for debugging = **9600, 8N1**. > - I2C default for sensors = **100 kHz standard mode**. > - ADC on most ARM MCUs = **12-bit**, reference = **3.3 V**. > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute Mnemonics:** > - "**SDA down, SCL up**" = **START** (SDA falls while SCL is high). > - "**SDA up, SCL up**" = **STOP** (SDA rises while SCL is high). > - "**4-bit mode saves 4 wires, 6 lines are all you need**" (RS, EN, D4–D7). > - "**I2C = Inter-Integrated Circuit, originally invented by Philips in 1982**."

topics.title: Networking and Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT503 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Networking and Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Networking and Communication in IoT** refers to the layered set of hardware, software, and protocol mechanisms that enable resource-constrained embedded devices (sensors, actuators, microcontrollers) to discover, exchange, synchronize, and route data across heterogeneous physical and data-link media, ultimately delivering application-level payloads to cloud services, edge gateways, or peer devices.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this topic spans the **OSI/TCP-IP reference model mapping to IoT**, **wireless personal/local/wide area network standards** (IEEE 802.15.4, BLE, Wi-Fi, LoRa, NB-IoT), and **application-layer messaging protocols** (MQTT, CoAP, HTTP, AMQP) that operate *over* these physical layers. The defining characteristic is **constrained-node networking** — every protocol decision must respect limited **bandwidth (typically < 250 kbps for many LPWAN links)**, **battery power (often 1–10 mAh coin cells for years of operation)**, and **memory budgets (often < 64 KB RAM)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal System"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of IoT networking as a global postal system.
> - **Physical Layer** = The trucks, roads, and airplanes (the actual radio waves/wires).
> - **MAC/Data-Link Layer** = The post office sorting rules (who speaks when, addressing a street).
> - **Network Layer** = The zip-code routing system (how a letter finds its way from Kerala to Berlin).
> - **Transport Layer** = Registered post with tracking (reliable delivery + acknowledgements).
> - **Application Layer** = The letter's content language (English, Malayalam, or in IoT terms — JSON, CBOR).
>
> A smart agriculture sensor in a remote KTU campus field does not "speak" the same way as your laptop on Wi-Fi; it needs a "translator courier" — gateways and protocol bridges — to deliver the soil-moisture data to the cloud dashboard.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Must Memorize)

| Metric | Value | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| IEEE 802.15.4 (PHY) Bitrate | **250 kbps** (2.4 GHz) | Backbone for ZigBee, 6LoWPAN, Thread |
| BLE 5.0 Data Rate | **2 Mbps** (PHY) | Low-power short-range wearables |
| Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax) | up to **9.6 Gbps** | High-throughput edge nodes |
| LoRa SF range | **SF7 – SF12** | Trade-off: range vs. data rate |
| IPv6 Address Length | **128 bits** | 6LoWPAN header compression necessity |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** When a question mentions "constrained devices", examiners expect you to *justify* the choice of protocol using the metrics above. A 3-mark question asking "Why MQTT over HTTP for IoT?" expects a numeric comparison (header size, persistent connection, QoS levels) — not a vague "MQTT is lightweight."

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The IoT Protocol Stack — Layer-by-Layer Decomposition

The IoT does not invent a new OSI model; it **re-maps the classical TCP/IP stack** onto constrained hardware. The KTU syllabus requires students to understand *every* layer and identify which IoT protocol occupies it.

### 2.1.1 Application Layer (Where Data Becomes Meaning)
- **HTTP / HTTPS** — RESTful, heavy headers (~800 B typical), TCP-based, not designed for sleeping nodes.
- **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** — Publish/Subscribe, broker-mediated, runs over TCP, 2-byte fixed header. QoS levels 0, 1, 2.
- **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)** — RESTful (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE) but over **UDP**, with optional reliability via Confirmable (CON) messages. Header is just **4 bytes**.
- **AMQP** — Enterprise-grade, heavier, used in industrial IoT backbones.
- **DDS (Data Distribution Service)** — Real-time, peer-to-peer, used in autonomous vehicles/robotics.

### 2.1.2 Transport Layer
- **TCP** — Reliable, connection-oriented, handshake overhead (3-way SYN/SYN-ACK/ACK = 3 round trips before data).
- **UDP** — Connectionless, no handshake, ideal for CoAP and quick telemetry bursts.
- **DTLS (Datagram TLS)** — TLS-equivalent security for UDP-based CoAP.

### 2.1.3 Network Layer — IPv6, 6LoWPAN, and RPL
- Native IPv4 is wasteful for tiny packets. **6LoWPAN (RFC 4944, RFC 6282)** compresses the 40-byte IPv6 header down to typically **2–11 bytes** using IPHC (IP Header Compression).
- **RPL (Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks — RFC 6550)** builds a **DODAG (Destination-Oriented Directed Acyclic Graph)** rooted at a border router. Routing metric: **ETX (Expected Transmission Count)**.

### 2.1.4 Adaptation / Mesh Layer
- **Thread**, **ZigBee**, **Z-Wave** form self-healing mesh networks where each node can forward packets for others.

### 2.1.5 MAC / Data-Link Layer
- **IEEE 802.15.4** — CSMA/CA, slotted/unslotted, 16-bit short addresses, 64-bit EUI-64 extended addresses.
- **802.15.1 (Bluetooth Classic)** and **802.15.1 BLE** — Piconet/Scatternet.
- **Wi-Fi 802.11 a/b/g/n/ac/ax** — DCF (Distributed Coordination Function), RTS/CTS optional.

### 2.1.6 Physical Layer
- Sub-GHz ISM bands (868 MHz EU, 915 MHz US, 433 MHz Asia).
- 2.4 GHz Industrial-Scientific-Medical band — globally available, 16 channels (Wi-Fi) / 11–26 channels (802.15.4).

## 2.2 Communication Taxonomy (Range vs. Power)

| Class | Range | Power | Protocols |
|---|---|---|---|
| **NFC / RFID** | < 0.1 m | Passive | ISO 14443, ISO 15693 |
| **PAN / Body** | < 10 m | < 10 mW | BLE, ZigBee, ANT+ |
| **LAN** | < 100 m | < 1 W | Wi-Fi, Thread |
| **LPWAN (wide)** | 2 – 15 km | < 25 mA peak | LoRa, Sigfox, NB-IoT |
| **Cellular IoT** | km-scale | 1–2 W | LTE-M, NB-IoT, 5G mMTC |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Metric Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Path Loss (Free-space, Friis)** | $P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | Predicts RSSI for LoRa link budget design |
| **SNR (dB)** | $\mathrm{SNR}_{\mathrm{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | BLE/Wi-Fi sensitivity threshold (~ −97 dBm BLE) |
| **Shannon Capacity (bps)** | $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Upper bound on IoT link throughput |
| **LoRa Time-on-Air (approximate)** | $T_{\text{air}} = T_{\text{sym}} \cdot (N_{\text{preamble}} + N_{\text{payload}} + 4.25)$ | Battery-life estimation in KTU design problems |
| **6LoWPAN compressed header** | $H_{\text{comp}} = H_{\text{IPv6}} - 38 \text{ bytes (savings)}$ | Justifies IPv6 use in microcontrollers |
| **MQTT Header Size (fixed)** | $H_{\text{MQTT}} = 2 \text{ bytes (min)}$ | Compares with HTTP (~ 800 B) |
| **Duty Cycle Limit (ETSI 868 MHz)** | $D \le 1\%$ | Mandatory design constraint for European deployments |
| **MTU of 802.15.4** | $\mathrm{MTU} = 127 \text{ bytes}$ | Why IPv6 must be fragmented/compressed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule:** In every KTU 2024 calculation question on link budget or duty cycle, write the **units** in the final answer. Examiners award ½ mark strictly for unit correctness in numerical answers.

## 2.4 Why These Protocols Exist — The Engineering "Why"

1. **HTTP failed IoT** because opening a TCP connection for every sensor reading drains battery. MQTT's persistent broker connection amortizes the handshake cost across thousands of messages.
2. **Bluetooth LE introduced "advertisements"** so a sensor can broadcast its presence *without* forming a connection — perfect for a beacon that wakes only 3 times per second.
3. **LoRa uses Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)** — the symbol duration *grows* with Spreading Factor (SF7→SF12), trading bit-rate for **processing gain**, which is why SF12 can reach 15 km line-of-sight but at only 250 bps.
4. **6LoWPAN was necessary** because sending a 40-byte IPv6 header inside an 802.15.4 frame of 127 bytes leaves almost no room for the actual sensor payload.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Friis Free-Space Path Loss (for a LoRa Link-Budget Problem)

A KTU question typically gives transmitter power, antenna gains, frequency, and distance, and asks to compute received power. We must derive and apply **Friis' transmission equation**.

### Given
- Transmit power: $P_t = 14$ dBm (= 25 mW) — typical LoRa SX1276 setting
- Tx antenna gain: $G_t = 2$ dBi
- Rx antenna gain: $G_r = 2$ dBi
- Carrier frequency: $f = 868$ MHz (European ISM band)
- Distance: $d = 5$ km (rural KTU campus deployment)

### Step 1 — Convert dBm to linear Watts
$$P_t \,[\text{W}] = 10^{\frac{P_t \,[\text{dBm}] - 30}{10}}$$

$$P_t = 10^{\frac{14 - 30}{10}} = 10^{-1.6} = 0.02512 \text{ W}$$

### Step 2 — Compute wavelength $\lambda$
$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}{868 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}}$$

$$\lambda = 0.3456 \text{ m}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Friis Transmission Equation
$$P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$$

$$P_r = 0.02512 \cdot 10^{0.2} \cdot 10^{0.2} \cdot \left(\frac{0.3456}{4\pi \cdot 5000}\right)^2$$

$$P_r = 0.02512 \cdot 1.5849 \cdot 1.5849 \cdot \left(\frac{0.3456}{62831.85}\right)^2$$

$$P_r = 0.0631 \cdot \left(5.499 \times 10^{-6}\right)^2$$

$$P_r = 0.0631 \cdot 3.024 \times 10^{-11}$$

$$\boxed{P_r = 1.908 \times 10^{-12} \text{ W} = -117.2 \text{ dBm}}$$

### Step 4 — Check against LoRa receiver sensitivity
For SF12, BW = 125 kHz, LoRa sensitivity is typically **−137 dBm**. Since −117.2 dBm >> −137 dBm (meaning the received signal is **stronger** than the sensitivity floor), the link is **feasible** with a **20 dB link margin**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This 20 dB margin absorbs real-world impairments: vegetation loss, building penetration loss, fading margin, and rain attenuation. Always reserve ≥ 10 dB margin in IoT deployment design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time-on-Air of a LoRa Packet

LoRa's chirp-based modulation makes time-on-air calculation non-trivial. KTU questions may provide this as a design calculation for battery-life estimation.

### Given
- Spreading Factor: $\mathrm{SF} = 9$
- Bandwidth: $\mathrm{BW} = 125$ kHz
- Coding Rate: $\mathrm{CR} = 1$ (i.e., 4/5)
- Preamble symbols: $N_{\text{preamble}} = 8$ (default)
- Payload: $N_{\text{payload}} = 20$ bytes

### Step 1 — Symbol Duration
$$T_{\text{sym}} = \frac{2^{\mathrm{SF}}}{\mathrm{BW}} = \frac{2^9}{125 \times 10^3} = \frac{512}{125000}$$

$$T_{\text{sym}} = 4.096 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 2 — Payload Symbol Count (LoRa formula)
$$N_{\text{payload}} = 8 + \max\!\left(\left\lceil \frac{8N_b - 4\mathrm{SF} + 28 + 16\mathrm{CR} - 20H}{4(\mathrm{SF} - 2\mathrm{DE})} \right\rceil \cdot (\mathrm{CR} + 4),\, 0\right)$$

Where:
- $N_b$ = number of payload bits = $20 \times 8 = 160$ bits
- $H = 0$ (no header enabled)
- $\mathrm{DE} = 0$ (low-data-rate optimization disabled for BW = 125 kHz, SF = 9)

$$8N_b - 4\mathrm{SF} + 28 + 16\mathrm{CR} - 20H = 8(160) - 4(9) + 28 + 16(1) - 0$$

$$= 1280 - 36 + 28 + 16 = 1288$$

$$4(\mathrm{SF} - 2\mathrm{DE}) = 4(9 - 0) = 36$$

$$\frac{1288}{36} = 35.78 \Rightarrow \lceil \cdot \rceil = 36$$

$$N_{\text{payload}} = 8 + 36 \cdot (1 + 4) = 8 + 180 = 188 \text{ symbols}$$

### Step 3 — Total Time-on-Air
$$T_{\text{air}} = T_{\text{sym}} \cdot (N_{\text{preamble}} + N_{\text{payload}} + 4.25)$$

$$T_{\text{air}} = 4.096 \times 10^{-3} \cdot (8 + 188 + 4.25)$$

$$T_{\text{air}} = 4.096 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 200.25$$

$$\boxed{T_{\text{air}} = 0.820 \text{ s}}$$

### Step 4 — Energy consumed (using SX1276 TX current = 90 mA at 14 dBm on 3.3 V)
$$E = V \cdot I \cdot T = 3.3 \cdot 0.090 \cdot 0.820 = 0.2436 \text{ J per packet}$$

If a node transmits once per hour, daily energy = 5.85 J, and a 2400 mAh Li-Ion battery (3.7 V, ~ 31 680 J) would last **~ 14.8 years**. This is why LoRa is preferred for remote KTU campus field-deployed sensors.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Frequently Asked in KTU)

| Parameter | **MQTT** | **CoAP** | **HTTP** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transport | TCP | UDP | TCP |
| Header (typical) | 2 B fixed + variable | 4 B | 200 – 800 B |
| Pattern | Pub/Sub | Request/Response | Request/Response |
| QoS | 0, 1, 2 | CON/NON | None built-in |
| Security | TLS | DTLS | TLS |
| Suitability for sleeping nodes | Excellent (push) | Good | Poor |
| Broker needed | Yes (e.g., Mosquitto) | No (peer-to-peer possible) | No (client-server) |
| KTU exam one-liner | "Lightweight TCP pub/sub" | "REST over UDP" | "Heavy, verbose" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Mantra:** If the question gives a *sleeping battery node* that wakes once per hour to send temperature data — answer **MQTT over TCP with QoS 1** (broker buffers during sleep). If the question describes a *real-time control loop* between two microcontrollers on a mesh — answer **CoAP over UDP with CON messages**.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IoT Protocol Stack — Mermaid Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    app1[Application Layer - MQTT CoAP HTTP]
    app2[Application Layer - AMQP DDS]
    trans1[Transport Layer - TCP]
    trans2[Transport Layer - UDP and DTLS]
    net1[Network Layer - IPv6 RPL]
    adapt1[Adaptation Layer - 6LoWPAN]
    mac1[MAC Layer - IEEE 802 15 4]
    mac2[MAC Layer - BLE]
    mac3[MAC Layer - Wi-Fi 802 11]
    mac4[MAC Layer - LoRa CSS]
    phy1[Physical - 2 4 GHz SubGHz]
    cloudA[Cloud Server]
    cloudB[Mobile App]
    gateway[Edge Gateway]
    sensor1[Temperature Sensor]
    sensor2[Soil Moisture Sensor]
    sensor3[BLE Wearable]
    sensor4[LoRa Field Sensor]

    sensor1 --> mac1
    sensor2 --> mac1
    sensor3 --> mac2
    sensor4 --> mac4
    mac1 --> adapt1
    adapt1 --> net1
    net1 --> trans1
    net1 --> trans2
    trans1 --> app1
    trans2 --> app1
    mac2 --> app1
    mac4 --> app2
    mac3 --> app1
    phy1 --> mac1
    phy1 --> mac2
    phy1 --> mac3
    phy1 --> mac4
    app1 --> gateway
    app2 --> gateway
    gateway --> cloudA
    gateway --> cloudB
```

## 4.2 MQTT Publish/Subscribe — Broker-Mediated Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pub1[Publisher - Temperature Sensor]
    pub2[Publisher - Motion Sensor]
    broker[MQTT Broker - Mosquitto]
    sub1[Subscriber - Mobile App]
    sub2[Subscriber - Cloud Dashboard]
    sub3[Subscriber - Actuator Node]

    pub1 -- publish topic sensor temp --> broker
    pub2 -- publish topic sensor motion --> broker
    broker -- push topic sensor temp --> sub1
    broker -- push topic sensor temp --> sub2
    broker -- push topic sensor motion --> sub3
    sub1 -- subscribe topic sensor temp --> broker
    sub2 -- subscribe topic sensor plus --> broker
    sub3 -- subscribe topic sensor motion --> broker
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this diagram carefully:** Notice that **Publishers and Subscribers never know each other**. The broker is the only mandatory always-on node. This is the *decoupled asynchronous* property that makes MQTT ideal for sleeping IoT nodes.

## 4.3 6LoWPAN Header Compression Flow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ipv6["Full IPv6 Header - 40 bytes"]
    iphc["6LoWPAN IPHC Compression - 2 to 11 bytes"]
    mesh["Mesh Delivery Header - optional"]
    frag["Fragmentation Header - 8 bytes per frag"]
    mac["IEEE 802 15 4 MAC - 127 byte MTU"]
    radio["868 MHz or 2 4 GHz Radio"]

    ipv6 --> iphc
    iphc --> mesh
    mesh --> frag
    frag --> mac
    mac --> radio
```

## 4.4 LoRaWAN Network Architecture (Star-of-Stars)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ed1[End Device - Soil Sensor]
    ed2[End Device - Water Meter]
    ed3[End Device - GPS Tracker]
    gw1[Gateway 1 - KTU Campus]
    gw2[Gateway 2 - Hostel Roof]
    ns[Network Server - ChirpStack]
    appS[Application Server]
    cust[End User Dashboard]

    ed1 -.LoRa radio.-> gw1
    ed2 -.LoRa radio.-> gw1
    ed2 -.LoRa radio.-> gw2
    ed3 -.LoRa radio.-> gw2
    gw1 -->|IP backhaul| ns
    gw2 -->|IP backhaul| ns
    ns --> appS
    appS --> cust
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The LoRaWAN topology is a **Star-of-Stars**, *not* a mesh. End devices do *not* forward each other's packets. This is a deliberate trade-off — longer battery life, simpler firmware, at the cost of needing more gateways to ensure coverage overlap.
