## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the internal architecture of the ESP8266 SoC used in NodeMCU. With the help of a block diagram, describe the role of the Tensilica L106 core, SRAM, Flash, and Wi-Fi radio.

**Model Solution:**

The ESP8266 is a **System-on-Chip (SoC)** designed by Espressif Systems that integrates a 32-bit microcontroller with a 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi radio. Its architecture is built around the following key blocks:

1. **Tensilica L106 Diamond Standard 106Micro Core:**
   - 32-bit RISC processor, supports up to **160 MHz** clock.
   - Handles instruction fetch, decode, execute, and memory access.
   - Includes hardware multipliers and a debug interface.

2. **Memory Subsystem:**
   - **SRAM (160 KB):** Split into **IRAM** (instruction, ~64 KB) and **DRAM** (data, ~96 KB). Used for stack, heap, and Wi-Fi protocol buffers.
   - **Flash (up to 4 MB):** Connected via internal SPI bus. Stores firmware, user sketch, SPIFFS, and OTA update slots.

3. **Wi-Fi Radio:**
   - Implements **IEEE 802.11 b/g/n** at 2.4 GHz.
   - Supports **STA**, **AP**, and **STA+AP** modes.
   - Built-in **PCB trace antenna** + optional U.FL connector.
   - TX power up to **+20 dBm** (range ~90 m open air).

4. **Peripherals:**
   - **1× 10-bit SAR ADC** (single channel, A0).
   - **2× UART**, **2× SPI** (one for flash, one for user), **1× I²C** (software-emulated).
   - **PWM** on all GPIO pins (software-emulated via timer interrupts).
   - **GPIO multiplexer** routes peripheral signals to physical pins.

5. **Power Management Unit:**
   - Internal LDO regulators generate 1.2 V (for core) and 3.3 V (for I/O).
   - Supports **deep sleep** (~20 µA) and **modem sleep** modes for IoT power optimization.

*(Award 2 marks for diagram, 3 marks for block description, 2 marks for clock/peripheral specifications.)*

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** With a neat pin-out diagram, explain the GPIO mapping of NodeMCU. Why are pins D3, D4, D6, D7, and D8 called "boot-sensitive" pins?

**Model Solution:**

The NodeMCU v3 board exposes **17 usable GPIO pins** (D0–D8, plus RX, TX, A0, SD2, SD3). However, the **silkscreen labels (D0–D8)** do not match the **internal ESP8266 GPIO numbers**. The correct mapping is:

| Silkscreen | Internal GPIO |
|---|---|
| D0 | GPIO16 |
| D1 | GPIO5 |
| D2 | GPIO4 |
| D3 | GPIO0 |
| D4 | GPIO2 |
| D5 | GPIO14 |
| D6 | GPIO12 |
| D7 | GPIO13 |
| D8 | GPIO15 |
| TX | GPIO1 |
| RX | GPIO3 |
| A0 | ADC0 |

**Boot-Sensitive Pins Explanation:**

During power-on, the ESP8266 reads the logic levels of **GPIO0, GPIO2, GPIO15, GPIO12** to determine the **boot mode**:

- **GPIO15 = LOW** AND **GPIO0 = HIGH** → Normal flash boot (run sketch).
- **GPIO15 = LOW** AND **GPIO0 = LOW** → Flash mode (accept new upload).
- **GPIO2 must be floating or HIGH** at boot.
- **GPIO12 must be LOW** at boot for normal operation (else 1.8 V flash voltage issue).

If a user connects a **relay module or push-button** to D3 (GPIO0) or D4 (GPIO2) and pulls it LOW at boot, **the board will not boot at all** — it enters flash programming mode and waits indefinitely. This is why these pins are called "boot-sensitive."

**Safe pins for general use:** D0, D1, D2, D5, D7, RX, TX, A0.

*(Award 2 marks for mapping table, 3 marks for boot-mode explanation, 2 marks for "safe pins" conclusion.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Compare the architecture of NodeMCU (ESP8266) with that of a traditional 8051 microcontroller in terms of bit-width, clock speed, on-chip peripherals, memory, and connectivity.

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | NodeMCU (ESP8266) | 8051 (e.g., AT89C51) |
|---|---|---|
| **Bit-width** | 32-bit | 8-bit |
| **Clock Speed** | 80–160 MHz | 12 MHz (typical) |
| **On-chip RAM** | 160 KB | 128–256 bytes |
| **Flash Memory** | 4 MB | 4–64 KB |
| **Wi-Fi / Connectivity** | **Built-in 802.11 b/g/n** | **None** (requires external) |
| **ADC** | 1 × 10-bit | None (8051 has no internal ADC) |
| **GPIOs** | 17 usable | 32 (4 ports) |
| **Operating Voltage** | 3.3 V | 5 V |
| **Power Modes** | Active, Modem Sleep, Deep Sleep (~20 µA) | Idle, Power-down |
| **Instruction Set** | RISC (Tensilica Xtensa) | CISC (8051 ISA) |
| **Typical Application** | IoT, Wi-Fi nodes, cloud sensors | Industrial control, consumer electronics |

**Key inferences:**
1. NodeMCU has **~10–13× more processing power** (clock speed) and **~1000× more RAM** than a classic 8051.
2. NodeMCU's **integrated Wi-Fi** makes it natively suitable for IoT; the 8051 requires external modules (e.g., ESP-01 + UART), adding cost, complexity, and power consumption.
3. The 8051 retains advantages in **real-time deterministic control** and **5 V sensor compatibility** — which is why it is still taught in KTU's lower semesters.
4. NodeMCU is **32-bit**, allowing direct handling of floating-point numbers and large data buffers (e.g., JSON payloads for cloud APIs) — a key requirement for modern IoT.

*(Award 2 marks for the comparison table, 3 marks for analytical inferences, 2 marks for application context.)*

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** A soil moisture sensor outputs a voltage proportional to moisture content. The NodeMCU reads the ADC value as **$D_{ADC} = 614$** on its 10-bit ADC with a 3.3 V reference. Calculate:
(i) The actual sensor voltage.
(ii) The moisture percentage if the dry sensor reads 0 V and the wet sensor reads 3.0 V.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Sensor Voltage Calculation:**

$$V_{sensor} = \left( \dfrac{D_{ADC}}{2^N - 1} \right) \times V_{ref}$$

$$V_{sensor} = \left( \dfrac{614}{1023} \right) \times 3.3$$

$$\dfrac{614}{1023} = 0.6002$$

$$V_{sensor} = 0.6002 \times 3.3 = 1.981 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{sensor} \approx 1.98 \text{ V}}$$

**(ii) Moisture Percentage Calculation:**

Using the linear interpolation formula for percentage:

$$\% \text{ Moisture} = \left( \dfrac{V_{sensor} - V_{dry}}{V_{wet} - V_{dry}} \right) \times 100$$

Substituting values ($V_{dry} = 0$ V, $V_{wet} = 3.0$ V):

$$\% \text{ Moisture} = \left( \dfrac{1.981 - 0}{3.0 - 0} \right) \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Moisture} = \left( \dfrac{1.981}{3.0} \right) \times 100 = 0.6603 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\% \text{ Moisture} \approx 66.03 \%}$$

**Interpretation:** The soil is **moderately to highly moist**. If the threshold for irrigation is < 40 %, irrigation is **not** required.

*(Award 3 marks for part (i), 3 marks for part (ii) setup, 1 mark for final answer with units.)*

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in NodeMCU Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **ADC Formula Error:** Using **1024** as divisor instead of **1023** for a 10-bit ADC. (Lose 1 mark per occurrence.)
> 2. **Silkscreen vs GPIO Confusion:** Writing `digitalWrite(16, HIGH)` instead of `digitalWrite(D0, HIGH)` or `digitalWrite(GPIO16, HIGH)`. (Lose 0.5 mark per pin.)
> 3. **Voltage Mismatch:** Connecting a 5 V relay directly to a GPIO pin. The ESP8266 will be **permanently damaged** in seconds. (Lose 2 marks + viva penalty.)
> 4. **Forgetting the LSB/Full-Scale:** Forgetting to subtract 1 from $2^N$ in the ADC formula. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 5. **Boot-Sensitive Pin Misuse:** Driving GPIO0, GPIO2, GPIO15, GPIO12 LOW at boot — the board will not start. (Lose 2 marks + lab demo fail.)
> 6. **Mixing up A0:** Treating **A0** as a digital pin — it is **analog input only** (no digital output capability).
> 7. **Wrong clock speed:** Saying 16 MHz (Arduino Uno spec) for NodeMCU — the correct answer is **80 MHz default, 160 MHz boost**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — NodeMCU & IoT Significance**

### Core Definitions
- ☐ NodeMCU = **Open-source dev kit** built on the **ESP8266 SoC**.
- ☐ ESP8266 = **32-bit SoC** with **integrated Wi-Fi (802.11 b/g/n)**.
- ☐ CPU = **Tensilica L106**, 32-bit RISC, **80 MHz default, 160 MHz boost**.

### Hardware Specs to Memorize
- ☐ Operating voltage: **3.3 V** (NOT 5 V tolerant).
- ☐ ADC: **1 channel, 10-bit** (0–3.3 V → 0–1023).
- ☐ GPIO: **17 usable pins**.
- ☐ Flash: **4 MB** (typical v3).
- ☐ Wi-Fi: **2.4 GHz**, 802.11 b/g/n, range ~**90 m open air**.

### Key Formulas
- ☐ $V_{in} = \dfrac{D_{ADC}}{1023} \times 3.3$ (ADC voltage conversion)
- ☐ $P = V \times I = 3.3 \times 0.070 \approx 0.23 \text{ W}$ (power consumption)
- ☐ Deep-sleep current: **~20 µA** (vs. 70 mA active).

### Pin Mapping Essentials
- ☐ D0 → GPIO16, D1 → GPIO5, D2 → GPIO4 (safe pins).
- ☐ D3 (GPIO0), D4 (GPIO2), D6 (GPIO12), D7 (GPIO13), D8 (GPIO15) = **boot-sensitive**.
- ☐ A0 = **analog-only**, no digital output.

### IoT Significance
- ☐ Bridges **perception layer** (sensors) to **application layer** (cloud).
- ☐ Built-in Wi-Fi eliminates need for external modules.
- ☐ Arduino IDE compatible → easy C/C++ programming.
- ☐ Deep sleep mode extends battery life by **~600×**.

### Common Pitfalls
- ☐ Never connect 5 V to a GPIO pin.
- ☐ Never pull GPIO0, GPIO2, GPIO15 LOW at boot.
- ☐ Always use `D0`–`D8` labels or explicit `GPIOx` numbers, not bare numbers.

### One-Line Mantra for the Exam
> *"NodeMCU = Arduino simplicity + ESP8266 Wi-Fi power + ₹200 cost + 3.3 V discipline."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Architecture and Features of NodeMCU
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT503 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Architecture and Features of NodeMCU

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **NodeMCU** is an **open-source IoT (Internet of Things) hardware and firmware development platform** built around the **ESP8266 Wi-Fi System-on-Chip (SoC)** manufactured by Espressif Systems. It is designed to facilitate rapid prototyping of connected devices by integrating a **Tensilica L106 32-bit RISC microprocessor core**, on-chip SRAM/ROM, a full TCP/IP networking stack, and a multi-channel ADC into a single low-cost, breadboard-friendly module.

The term "NodeMCU" historically referred to the **firmware (Lua-based scripting runtime)**, but in modern KTU parlance and IoT industry practice, the name has been widely adopted to refer to the entire **development board** (often the NodeMCU v3 / V3 LoLin variant, which uses the ESP-12E module).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Swiss Army Knife of IoT Prototyping"**
> 
> Think of NodeMCU as a **pocket-sized Swiss Army knife for the Internet of Things**:
> - The **Tensilica processor core** is the **knife blade** (the primary working tool that performs operations).
> - The **on-chip Wi-Fi radio** is the **corkscrew** (a special-purpose tool that allows the device to "pop into" wireless networks — a feature most plain microcontrollers don't have).
> - The **GPIO pins** are the **other fold-out tools** (screwdrivers, scissors — each has a dedicated external function).
> - The **USB-to-Serial interface (CH340/CP2102)** is the **handle** that lets you plug the whole kit into a PC and power it.
> 
> Just as a Swiss Army knife bundles many tools into one compact handle, NodeMCU bundles **a processor + Wi-Fi + memory + analog/digital I/O** into a single **$2–$3 board** — eliminating the need to buy and wire separate Wi-Fi modules, ADC chips, and microcontrollers.

## 1.3 What Makes NodeMCU Special for IoT?

| Parameter | Value / Standard Metric |
|---|---|
| **Core SoC** | **ESP8266EX** (Espressif Systems) |
| **CPU Architecture** | **Tensilica L106 32-bit RISC** (Xtensa Diamond Standard 106Micro) |
| **Operating Voltage** | **3.3 V** (logic); **5 V** via USB input |
| **Clock Speed** | **80 MHz (default)** — overclockable to **160 MHz** |
| **On-chip SRAM** | **160 KB** (usable) |
| **On-chip ROM** | **~64 KB** (bootloader + SDK) |
| **External Flash** | **4 MB (typical, ESP-12E)** |
| **Wi-Fi Standard** | **802.11 b/g/n** (2.4 GHz) |
| **Operating Temperature** | **−40 °C to +125 °C** |
| **Board Variants** | **v0.9, v1.0, v2.0, v3 (LoLin)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Node" + "MCU"?** The "Node" implies a **network node** (a thing on the internet), and "MCU" stands for **MicroController Unit**. Together, **NodeMCU = a microcontroller that natively speaks Wi-Fi**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Architecture Breakdown

## 2.1 Block-Level Architecture of the ESP8266EX SoC

The ESP8266EX is the **silicon heart** of the NodeMCU board. Its internal architecture is a tightly integrated set of subsystems:

### 2.1.1 The CPU Subsystem

> The CPU is a **Tensilica L106 32-bit RISC processor** — a low-power, deterministic core optimized for embedded DSP and IoT workloads. It has:
> - **32-bit ALU, register file, and instruction set** (Xtensa ISA).
> - **Single-issue, in-order pipeline** — so it executes one instruction per cycle (CPI ≈ 1 for non-branch).
> - **16-bit instructions** (compact code density) — crucial for fitting firmware into on-chip ROM.
> - **Hardware multiplier and divider** for fast arithmetic.
> - **JTAG debug interface** for boundary-scan and in-circuit emulation.

**Key Idea:** The L106 is a "compute engine" that fetches instructions from external flash, executes them, and orchestrates all the on-chip peripherals (Wi-Fi, timers, GPIO, etc.).

### 2.1.2 Memory Organization

```
+------------------+--------------------+---------------------------+
| Memory Block     | Size (typical)     | Purpose                   |
+------------------+--------------------+---------------------------+
| ROM (on-chip)    | ~64 KB             | Bootloader, low-level SDK |
| IRAM (on-chip)   | ~64 KB             | Time-critical ISR code    |
| DRAM (on-chip)   | ~96 KB             | Data + heap for user code |
| SPI Flash (ext.) | 1–16 MB (typ. 4MB) | User program + file system|
+------------------+--------------------+---------------------------+
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The ESP8266 uses a **Harvard-like memory architecture at the cache level** but presents a unified address space to the firmware. There is **no user-accessible external RAM bus** — the only external memory is **SPI flash**, accessed through a dedicated SPI controller.

### 2.1.3 Wi-Fi Radio Subsystem

The radio block implements a **complete 2.4 GHz 802.11 b/g/n transceiver** with:
- Integrated **PA (Power Amplifier)**, **LNA (Low Noise Amplifier)**, and **balun** (no external antenna matching required for the PCB trace antenna).
- **WPA/WPA2** security protocols.
- **Station (STA), Access Point (AP), and STA+AP** modes.
- The TCP/IP stack and Wi-Fi MAC are implemented mostly in **firmware (in ROM)** to save flash space.

### 2.1.4 Peripherals & Interfaces

NodeMCU exposes a rich set of peripherals — see the formula/cheat sheet below.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Peripherals Cheat Sheet

| Interface / Peripheral | Quantity | Key Specifications |
|---|---|---|
| **GPIO** | **17 usable pins** (D0–D8, D9–D10, SD2–SD3, SD0–SD1, A0) | 3.3 V logic; **max source/sink = 12 mA per pin** |
| **ADC** | **1 × 10-bit SAR ADC** | On pin **A0**; range **0–1.0 V** (not 3.3 V!) |
| **PWM** | **All GPIO** support software PWM | Default freq ≈ **1 kHz**, duty 0–100 % |
| **UART** | **2 × UART** (UART0, UART1) | UART0 used for USB-serial; UART1 TX-only by default |
| **I²C (TWI)** | **1 × software I²C** | 100 kHz / 400 kHz; any 2 GPIOs |
| **SPI** | **2 × SPI** (HSPI, shared with flash) | Up to **80 MHz** clock |
| **I²S** | **1 × I²S** | For digital audio |
| **Timers** | **2 × hardware timers** | FRC1, NMI |
| **Watchdog** | **1 × WDT** | Reset on hang |
| **Deep-Sleep Current** | **≈ 10 µA** | Wake via GPIO or timer |
| **Operating Frequency** | **80 / 160 MHz** | Configurable at boot |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where NodeMCU is used in industry:**
> - **Smart Agriculture** — soil moisture sensors → NodeMCU → Wi-Fi → cloud dashboard.
> - **Home Automation** — MQTT-controlled relays for lights/fans.
> - **Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN)** — battery-powered temperature/humidity nodes with deep-sleep cycles.
> - **Educational Toolkits** — KTU labs, NEP 2020 skill courses, and B.Tech final-year projects.
> - **Rapid Prototyping of IoT endpoints** that later migrate to ESP32 for production.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Architectural Analysis & Logical Derivation

## 3.1 Boot Sequence — The "Why" and "How" of Powering Up

Understanding the boot process is a **frequently asked topic in KTU exams**. Let us derive it step by step:

### Step 1: Power-On Reset (POR)

When **3.3 V** is applied to the chip (or **5 V** via USB and regulated down), the internal **reset circuit** holds the CPU in reset for a few milliseconds. This ensures the **1.0 V core voltage LDO** is stable.

### Step 2: Boot-ROM Takes Control

The **on-chip ROM bootloader** begins executing from address **0x0000_0000**. The ROM is **non-writable** and contains the first-stage bootloader plus the SDK's Wi-Fi/TCP-IP libraries.

### Step 3: Read Boot Mode from Strapping Pins

The ROM samples **3 GPIO pins (GPIO0, GPIO2, GPIO15)** to decide the boot mode:

$$
\text{Boot Mode} = f(\text{GPIO15}, \text{GPIO2}, \text{GPIO0})
$$

| GPIO15 | GPIO2 | GPIO0 | Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | 1 | **Normal boot from flash** |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | **Download mode (flashing firmware)** |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | **SDIO mode** (used by ESP32) |
| 0 | 0 | 0 | **UART download** |

> On the NodeMCU v3 board, **GPIO0 is tied to the FLASH button** — pressing it during reset forces download mode.

### Step 4: Load User Program from SPI Flash

The ROM reads a **boot image header** from flash at offset **0x0000_0000**, which contains:
- **Magic number** (0xE9)
- **Number of segments** (typically 2–3)
- **Entry point address** (where execution jumps after loading)
- **Checksum** for integrity

### Step 5: Segment Loading into IRAM / DRAM

Each segment is copied from SPI flash into either **IRAM (instruction RAM)** or **DRAM (data RAM)** based on its flags. This explains why ESP8266 programs are called "**images**" — they are loaded segment-by-segment like a mini-OS.

### Step 6: Jump to User `app_main()`

Once all segments are loaded, the ROM performs a `JMP` to the entry point, and the **user firmware's `setup()` / `app_main()`** takes over.

## 3.2 Clock Derivation — From Crystal to CPU

The NodeMCU uses a **40 MHz crystal oscillator (XTAL)**. The CPU can run at either **80 MHz or 160 MHz** by configuring the **PLL (Phase-Locked Loop)**:

$$
f_{\text{CPU}} = f_{\text{XTAL}} \times N_{\text{PLL}}
$$

where $N_{\text{PLL}}$ is the PLL multiplier.

**Step 1:** XTAL oscillates at $f_{\text{XTAL}} = 40 \text{ MHz}$ (a common, low-cost frequency).

**Step 2:** The on-chip PLL multiplies this by a programmable integer ratio:
- $N_{\text{PLL}} = 2 \Rightarrow f_{\text{CPU}} = 80 \text{ MHz}$ (default, lower power).
- $N_{\text{PLL}} = 4 \Rightarrow f_{\text{CPU}} = 160 \text{ MHz}$ (high-performance mode).

**Step 3:** The Wi-Fi baseband uses a **fractional-N synthesizer** derived from the same 40 MHz XTAL to generate the 2.4 GHz carrier, ensuring both subsystems are phase-coherent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** The 80/160 MHz selection is a **power-vs-performance trade-off**. For battery-powered deep-sleep wake-ups, run at 80 MHz to halve dynamic power; for high-throughput MQTT publishing, switch to 160 MHz.

## 3.3 Power Consumption Derivation

The total active current of the ESP8266 is approximately:

$$
I_{\text{total}} \approx I_{\text{CPU}} + I_{\text{Wi-Fi TX}} + I_{\text{Wi-Fi RX}} + I_{\text{peripherals}}
$$

Plugging in typical values (TX at +20 dBm):

$$
I_{\text{total}} \approx 15 \text{ mA} + 170 \text{ mA} + 50 \text{ mA} + 5 \text{ mA} \approx 240 \text{ mA (peak TX)}
$$

> The **peak TX current (~240 mA)** is why the NodeMCU's onboard **AMS1117-3.3 LDO** must be capable of sourcing at least **300 mA** — a common cause of "Wi-Fi reset" bugs in poorly designed boards.

## 3.4 ADC Voltage Conversion Derivation

The ESP8266's ADC is **10-bit** with a **0–1.0 V input range** (NOT 3.3 V):

$$
D_{\text{out}} = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{\text{in}}}{V_{\text{ref}}} \times 2^{10} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{\text{in}}}{1.0} \times 1024 \right\rfloor
$$

**Reverse** (to recover input voltage from a digital reading):

$$
V_{\text{in}} = \frac{D_{\text{out}}}{1024} \times 1.0 \text{ V}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU exam pitfall:** Students often write the ADC range as **0–3.3 V**. The correct range is **0–1.0 V** on the ESP8266 — exceeding it permanently damages the pin. For 3.3 V sensors, use a **voltage divider** to scale down to ≤1.0 V.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram of NodeMCU (ESP8266EX)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pwr[USB 5V Input / Vin] --> ldo[AMS1117 3.3V LDO]
    ldo --> soc[ESP8266EX SoC]
    ldo --> gpiopin[GPIO Pins 3.3V]
    usb[USB Micro] --> uart[CH340 USB-to-UART]
    uart -->|UART0 TX RX| soc
    soc -->|GPIO0 FLASH btn| flash[Flash Button]
    soc -->|SPI bus| xflash[SPI Flash 4MB]
    soc --> radio[2.4 GHz Wi-Fi Radio]
    soc --> adc[A0 ADC 10-bit]
    soc --> gpio[17 GPIO Pins]
    soc --> pwm[PWM x software]
    soc --> i2c[Software I2C]
    soc --> spi2[HSPI]
    soc --> timer[HW Timers x2]
    soc --> wdt[Watchdog Timer]
    radio --> antenna[PCB Trace Antenna]
```

## 4.2 Boot Sequence Flow (Logic Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power On Reset POR] --> B[ROM Bootloader Starts]
    B --> C{Sample Strapping Pins GPIO0 GPIO2 GPIO15}
    C -->|GPIO0 0| D[UART Download Mode]
    C -->|GPIO0 1| E[Normal SPI Flash Boot]
    D --> F[Wait for Host to Send Firmware]
    E --> G[Read Boot Header from Flash]
    G --> H{Header Valid}
    H -->|No| I[ROM Messages Wait]
    H -->|Yes| J[Load Segments to IRAM DRAM]
    J --> K[Jump to User app_main]
    K --> L[User Firmware Running]
```

## 4.3 Pinout Reference (NodeMCU v3 LoLin)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LHS[Left Side Pins]
        direction TB
        A0pin[A0 Analog Input]
        RSV[GND]
        VU[VU 5V from USB]
        V3[3V3 3.3V regulated]
        VU2[VU]
        SD3[SD3 GPIO10]
        SD2[SD2 GPIO9]
        SD1[SD1 MOSI]
        CMD[CMD CS]
        SD0[SD0 MISO]
        CLK[CLK SCLK]
        GND2[GND]
        D8[D8 GPIO15]
        D7[D7 GPIO13]
        D6[D6 GPIO12]
        D5[D5 GPIO14]
        GND3[GND]
        D4[D4 GPIO2 Built-in LED]
        D3[D3 GPIO0 FLASH btn]
        D2[D2 GPIO4]
        D1[D1 GPIO5]
        D0[D0 GPIO16]
    end
```

## 4.4 Power & Clock Domain Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    x[X1 40 MHz Crystal] --> pll[PLL Multiplier N]
    pll --> cpu[CPU 80 or 160 MHz]
    pll --> wbm[Wi-Fi Baseband]
    pll --> peri[Peripheral Bus]
    rcc[Power Management Unit] --> cpu
    rcc --> wbm
    rcc --> peri
    usb5v[USB 5V] --> ldo3v3[AMS1117 LDO]
    ldo3v3 --> rcc
    ldo3v3 --> io[3.3V I/O Rail]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagrams are **Mermaid block-level functional representations** of the NodeMCU/ESP8266 architecture — they map signal and power flow rather than physical PCB traces, which is the standard pedagogical approach in KTU 2024 Scheme lecture notes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**List any six features of the NodeMCU development board.**

**Model Answer:**
1. Based on **ESP8266EX** SoC with **Tensilica L106 32-bit RISC CPU**.
2. On-board **2.4 GHz 802.11 b/g/n Wi-Fi** with WPA/WPA2 security.
3. **17 GPIO pins**, all supporting **PWM**, **I²C**, **SPI**, and **1-wire**.
4. **One 10-bit ADC** on pin **A0** (input range 0–1.0 V).
5. **4 MB external SPI flash** for user program and file system.
6. **USB-to-UART (CH340/CP2102)** for easy programming and serial debugging.
7. Operating voltage **3.3 V**, input voltage **5 V** via micro-USB.
8. Clock speed **80 MHz** (default), configurable to **160 MHz**.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model]**
**Differentiate between NodeMCU and Arduino Uno in terms of CPU, Wi-Fi, and operating voltage.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | NodeMCU (ESP8266) | Arduino Uno (ATmega328P) |
|---|---|---|
| **CPU** | 32-bit Tensilica L106, 80–160 MHz | 8-bit AVR, 16 MHz |
| **Wi-Fi** | **Built-in 802.11 b/g/n** | **Not available** (needs shield) |
| **Operating Voltage** | **3.3 V** | **5 V** |
| **Flash** | 4 MB (external SPI) | 32 KB (on-chip) |
| **RAM** | ~160 KB | 2 KB |
| **ADC** | 1 × 10-bit (0–1.0 V) | 6 × 10-bit (0–5 V) |
| **Cost** | ~$3 | ~$25 (board + Wi-Fi shield) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU ESE Model — Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **internal architecture of the ESP8266EX SoC** used in NodeMCU with a neat block diagram. Highlight the role of the **Tensilica L106 CPU**, **on-chip memory**, and **Wi-Fi radio subsystem**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **boot sequence** of NodeMCU in detail, explaining the role of **strapping pins** and the **SPI flash image header**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — (a) ESP8266EX Internal Architecture

**1. CPU Core — Tensilica L106 (2 marks):**
The ESP8266EX is built around the **Tensilica L106 32-bit RISC processor**, which uses the **Xtensa ISA**. It is a **single-issue, in-order pipeline** capable of executing one instruction per clock cycle. It contains a **32-bit ALU**, **hardware multiplier/divider**, and supports up to **160 MHz** operation, providing roughly **160 MIPS** of processing power — more than 10× the throughput of an Arduino Uno.

**2. On-Chip Memory (2 marks):**
- **ROM (~64 KB):** Bootloader and low-level Wi-Fi/TCP-IP libraries.
- **IRAM (~64 KB):** For time-critical ISR code loaded from SPI flash.
- **DRAM (~96 KB):** Data RAM, heap, and stack.
There is **no external RAM bus**; the only external memory is **SPI flash (1–16 MB, typically 4 MB)** that stores the user program and a wear-leveled file system.

**3. Wi-Fi Radio Subsystem (2 marks):**
The on-chip radio implements a complete **2.4 GHz 802.11 b/g/n transceiver** with integrated **PA, LNA, balun, and PCB trace antenna**. The Wi-Fi MAC and full **TCP/IP stack** (with support for TCP, UDP, HTTP, MQTT, DNS, DHCP) are implemented mostly in **ROM firmware** to conserve flash space. It supports **STA, AP, and STA+AP** modes.

**4. Peripherals (1 mark):**
- **17 GPIO** (multi-function), **1× 10-bit ADC** (0–1.0 V), **2× UART**, **HSPI**, **software I²C**, **software PWM**, **I²S**, **2× hardware timers**, and **watchdog timer**.

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to SECTION 4.1 — High-Level Block Diagram of NodeMCU.)*

---

### Model Solution — (b) Boot Sequence

**Step 1 — Power-On Reset (1 mark):** When 3.3 V is applied, the internal reset circuit holds the CPU in reset until the **1.0 V core LDO** is stable.

**Step 2 — ROM Bootloader (1 mark):** Execution begins at address **0x0000_0000** in on-chip ROM. The ROM contains the **first-stage bootloader** and a minimal SDK.

**Step 3 — Strapping Pin Sampling (2 marks):** The ROM reads **3 GPIO pins (GPIO0, GPIO2, GPIO15)** to determine the boot mode:

| GPIO15 | GPIO2 | GPIO0 | Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1 | 1 | Normal flash boot |
| 0 | 1 | 0 | UART download (flashing) |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | SDIO mode |
| 0 | 0 | 0 | Reserved |

On the NodeMCU v3 board, the **FLASH button** is wired to GPIO0 — holding it during reset enables **download mode** for firmware upload.

**Step 4 — SPI Flash Header Read (1.5 marks):** The ROM reads the **boot image header** from external SPI flash at offset **0x0000_0000**. The header contains:
- **Magic byte (0xE9)** for validation.
- **Number of memory segments** (typically 2–3).
- **Entry point address** (jump target after loading).
- **Per-segment load addresses, sizes, and flags** (IRAM vs DRAM).
- **Checksum** for integrity verification.

**Step 5 — Segment Loading (1 mark):** Each segment is copied from SPI flash into either **IRAM** (executable code) or **DRAM** (initialized data) based on its flags. This is why ESP8266 firmware is structured as a **memory image** rather than a flat binary.

**Step 6 — Jump to User Code (0.5 marks):** The ROM performs a `JMP` to the **entry point address**, transferring control to the user firmware's `app_main()` (or Arduino's `setup()`). The Wi-Fi stack is then initialized, and the user program begins execution.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **pinout of NodeMCU v3**. List the functions of pins **D0–D8, A0, VIN, 3V3, GND, and FLASH button**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **power management features** of NodeMCU including **active mode, modem-sleep, light-sleep, and deep-sleep modes**. Mention typical current consumption in each mode. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer Summary — Question B (b) Power Management

| Mode | Current | Wake-up Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Active (TX)** | **170–240 mA** | Always running |
| **Modem-Sleep** | **15–70 mA** | Wi-Fi off between DTIM beacons |
| **Light-Sleep** | **0.5–1 mA** | Timer / GPIO / Wi-Fi |
| **Deep-Sleep** | **~10 µA** | Timer or **GPIO16 (D0)** to RST |
| **Hibernation** | **~0.5 µA** | External signal only |

**Deep-Sleep Wake-up Logic:** The RTC timer counts down and pulls **GPIO16 (D0)** HIGH, which is connected to **RESET**, causing a full chip reset that re-runs `setup()`.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. ❌ Writing **ADC range as 0–3.3 V** — correct is **0–1.0 V**.
> 2. ❌ Confusing **GPIO numbering** (ESP8266 internal GPIO numbers vs. silkscreen D-numbers). Example: Silkscreen **D4 = GPIO2**, **D3 = GPIO0**.
> 3. ❌ Forgetting that **GPIO16 (D0)** is special — it has **no interrupt capability** and is used **only for deep-sleep wake-up**.
> 4. ❌ Stating that NodeMCU has **hardware I²C** — it uses **bit-banged software I²C** on any two GPIOs.
> 5. ❌ Saying the CPU runs at **"16 MHz like Arduino"** — the correct default is **80 MHz**, not Arduino's 16 MHz.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ✅ **Rapid Revision Checklist — Architecture & Features of NodeMCU**
> 
> - **SoC:** ESP8266EX (Espressif Systems).
> - **CPU:** Tensilica L106, **32-bit RISC**, **80 MHz default / 160 MHz max**.
> - **Memory:** ~64 KB ROM, ~160 KB SRAM on-chip, **4 MB SPI flash** external.
> - **Wi-Fi:** **802.11 b/g/n, 2.4 GHz**, with **WPA2** security; STA + AP modes.
> - **GPIO:** **17 pins**, all 3.3 V; max **12 mA per pin**; **no 5 V tolerance**.
> - **ADC:** **1 × 10-bit**, on **A0**, range **0–1.0 V only** (use voltage divider for higher voltages).
> - **PWM:** **Software**, available on all GPIO; default ~1 kHz.
> - **UART:** **2** (UART0 = USB-serial, UART1 = TX-only by default).
> - **I²C / SPI / I²S:** **Bit-banged I²C**, **HSPI**, **I²S** for audio.
> - **Boot pins:** **GPIO0, GPIO2, GPIO15** determine boot mode.
> - **Deep-Sleep:** **~10 µA**, wake-up via **GPIO16 (D0) → RESET** or timer.
> - **Programming:** Arduino IDE, MicroPython, Lua, ESP-IDF (C/C++).
> - **USB-UART chip:** **CH340** (most v3 boards) or **CP2102** (v1/v2 boards).
> - **KTU Mantra:** "NodeMCU = Arduino + Wi-Fi at $3, runs at 80 MHz, **3.3 V logic only**, ADC is **0–1.0 V**."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: NodeMCU Hardware Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT503 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** NodeMCU Hardware Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4: NodeMCU Hardware Components

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📌 Formal Academic Definition

**NodeMCU** is an **open-source IoT development platform** that integrates the **Espressif ESP8266 Wi-Fi System-on-Chip (SoC)** with a complete hardware development board. It is essentially a low-cost, low-power microcontroller unit (MCU) with built-in Wi-Fi capability, designed to bridge physical devices with wireless networks in IoT ecosystems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> NodeMCU is a firmware and development kit based on the **ESP8266 Wi-Fi SoC**, featuring a **Tensilica L106 32-bit RISC processor**, integrated **802.11 b/g/n Wi-Fi transceiver**, and a rich set of **General-Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) pins** with support for multiple communication protocols.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of NodeMCU as a **"Swiss Army Knife for IoT"**:
- The **ESP8266 chip** is the **brain** (like a tiny computer)
- The **Wi-Fi module** is its **mouth and ears** (allowing it to talk to the internet wirelessly)
- The **GPIO pins** are its **fingers** (allowing it to touch and control sensors/motors)
- The **USB interface** is its **power cable and conversation line** to your laptop
- The **voltage regulator** is its **stomach** (safely converting food/electricity into usable energy)

So just as a person can sense the world, think, and communicate, NodeMCU can read sensors, process data, and send it to the cloud — all in a board smaller than a credit card.

### 🔑 Key Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| **Operating Voltage** | **3.3 V DC** |
| **Input Voltage (via USB)** | **5 V DC** |
| **CPU** | **Tensilica L106 32-bit RISC** |
| **Clock Speed** | **80 MHz (default) / 160 MHz (boost)** |
| **Wi-Fi Standard** | **802.11 b/g/n (2.4 GHz)** |
| **Flash Memory** | **4 MB (typical)** |
| **SRAM** | **64 KB (instruction) + 96 KB (data)** |
| **GPIOs** | **17 digital pins (usable)** |
| **ADC** | **1 channel, 10-bit resolution (0–1 V)** |
| **Operating Temperature** | **−40 °C to +125 °C** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **ADC pin (A0)** on NodeMCU accepts only **0–1 V**, NOT the full 3.3 V — this is a critical exam point often missed by students.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🔍 Block-Level Architecture of NodeMCU

The NodeMCU board is built around the **ESP8266EX SoC**, with several supporting hardware blocks:

#### 🧩 A. ESP8266 SoC (The Heart)
- Manufactured by **Espressif Systems**
- Integrates a **Tensilica L106 Diamond Standard 106Micro** (32-bit RISC processor)
- Contains:
  - **CPU core**
  - **SRAM**
  - **Wi-Fi MAC/BB/RF/PA/LNA**
  - **Power management unit (PMU)**
  - **Onboard clock generation**

#### 🧩 B. Flash Memory (External Storage)
- Typically **4 MB SPI Flash** (e.g., Winbond W25Q32)
- Stores:
  - Firmware
  - User programs (Lua scripts / Arduino sketches)
  - Configuration data
- Connected via **SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface)**

#### 🧩 C. Voltage Regulator
- **AMS1117-3.3V** low-dropout (LDO) regulator
- Steps down **USB 5 V → 3.3 V** for ESP8266 and logic
- Can source up to **1 A**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ESP8266 is **NOT 5 V tolerant**. Applying 5 V to any GPIO pin will permanently damage the chip. This is a frequent KTU exam question.

#### 🧩 D. USB-to-Serial Converter
- **CH340 or CP2102** chip
- Converts **USB (asynchronous serial) ↔ UART (TTL serial)**
- Enables programming and serial debugging via PC

#### 🧩 E. Reset & Flash Buttons
- **RST button** → Resets the ESP8266 (warm restart)
- **FLASH button** → Pulls GPIO0 LOW to enter **flash programming mode**

#### 🧩 F. LED Indicators
- **Blue LED** → Connected to **GPIO2** (active LOW)
- **Red LED** → Power indicator (always ON when powered)

### 📊 NodeMCU Pin Mapping (KTU High-Yield)

| NodeMCU Label | ESP8266 GPIO | Special Function | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **D0** | GPIO16 | Wake from deep sleep | No PWM, No I2C |
| **D1** | GPIO5 | I2C SCL | PWM capable |
| **D2** | GPIO4 | I2C SDA | PWM capable |
| **D3** | GPIO0 | Flash mode (LOW at boot) | PWM capable |
| **D4** | GPIO2 | Onboard LED | PWM capable, HIGH at boot |
| **D5** | GPIO14 | SPI CLK | PWM capable |
| **D6** | GPIO12 | SPI MISO | PWM capable |
| **D7** | GPIO13 | SPI MOSI | PWM capable |
| **D8** | GPIO15 | SPI CS | Must be LOW at boot |
| **TX** | GPIO1 | UART TX | Used during boot |
| **RX** | GPIO3 | UART RX | Used during boot |
| **A0** | ADC0 | Analog Input | 0–1 V range only |
| **G** | GND | Ground | — |
| **VU** | — | 5 V (USB passthrough) | Not regulated |
| **3V** | — | 3.3 V regulated | From AMS1117 |

### 📐 KTU Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Value | Description |
|---|---|---|
| ADC Resolution | $\text{Resolution} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | $n = 10$, $V_{ref} = 1.0\text{ V}$ |
| ADC Digital Value | $D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times 1023 \right\rfloor$ | Output range: 0–1023 |
| PWM Duty Cycle | $D_{\%} = \dfrac{t_{on}}{T} \times 100$ | Frequency range: 1 Hz–1 kHz |
| PWM Frequency | $f_{PWM} = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Default ≈ 1 kHz |
| Voltage Divider Scaling | $V_{out} = V_{in} \times \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Used to scale 5 V sensors to 1 V |
| Power Consumption (Active TX) | $P \approx 240\text{ mA} \times 3.3\text{ V} = 0.79\text{ W}$ | Peak during Wi-Fi transmission |
| Deep Sleep Current | $I \approx 10\text{ μA}$ | ESP8266 sleep mode |

> [!NOTE]
> To interface a **5 V analog sensor** with NodeMCU's **1 V ADC**, use a voltage divider with $R_1 = 4 \times R_2$. For example, $R_1 = 80\text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_2 = 20\text{ k}\Omega$.

### 🏗️ Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Smart Agriculture:** Soil moisture sensors (analog) → A0 → Cloud
- **Home Automation:** Relays (digital) → D1/D2 → Wi-Fi control
- **Wearables:** Deep sleep mode → Battery life extension to months
- **Industrial IoT:** MQTT over Wi-Fi → Cloud dashboards

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🧮 Derivation 1: ADC Conversion (Analog to Digital)

The **10-bit ADC** of NodeMCU converts an analog voltage (0–1 V) into a digital value (0–1023).

**Given:**
- $V_{ref} = 1.0\text{ V}$ (internal reference of ESP8266)
- Resolution = 10 bits $\Rightarrow 2^{10} = 1024$ discrete levels

**Step 1:** General ADC formula
$$
D = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times (2^n - 1)\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Substituting $V_{ref} = 1.0\text{ V}$, $n = 10$
$$
D = \text{round}\!\left(\frac{V_{in}}{1.0} \times 1023\right)
$$

**Step 3:** For $V_{in} = 0.5\text{ V}$
$$
D = \text{round}(0.5 \times 1023) = \text{round}(511.5) = 512
$$

**Step 4:** For $V_{in} = 0.25\text{ V}$
$$
D = \text{round}(0.25 \times 1023) = \text{round}(255.75) = 256
$$

**Step 5:** Resolution (smallest detectable voltage step)
$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{1.0}{1024} \approx 0.977\text{ mV}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ADC of ESP8266 has a **maximum input of 1.0 V**. For sensors with higher output (e.g., 5 V), a **voltage divider** is mandatory.

---

### 🧮 Derivation 2: Voltage Divider for 5 V Sensor Interfacing

**Problem:** A sensor outputs 0–5 V. We need to scale it to 0–1 V for NodeMCU's A0.

**Step 1:** Set the required scaling ratio
$$
k = \frac{V_{out,max}}{V_{in,max}} = \frac{1\text{ V}}{5\text{ V}} = 0.2
$$

**Step 2:** Standard voltage divider equation
$$
V_{out} = V_{in} \times \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

**Step 3:** For the ratio to equal 0.2,
$$
\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 0.2
$$

**Step 4:** Solve by assuming $R_2 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$
$$
R_1 + R_2 = \frac{R_2}{0.2} = \frac{10\text{ k}\Omega}{0.2} = 50\text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Step 5:** Therefore
$$
R_1 = 50\text{ k}\Omega - 10\text{ k}\Omega = 40\text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Result:** Use $R_1 = 40\text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_2 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ to scale any 0–5 V signal to 0–1 V.

---

### 🧮 Derivation 3: Power Consumption Estimation

**Step 1:** ESP8266 active mode current during Wi-Fi TX ≈ **170–240 mA** (peak)

**Step 2:** Active power at 3.3 V
$$
P_{active} = V \times I = 3.3 \times 0.24 = 0.792\text{ W}
$$

**Step 3:** Deep sleep current ≈ **10 μA**
$$
P_{sleep} = 3.3 \times 10 \times 10^{-6} = 33\text{ μW}
$$

**Step 4:** Average power (with 1% duty cycle)
$$
P_{avg} = 0.01 \times 0.792 + 0.99 \times 33 \times 10^{-6} \approx 7.95\text{ mW}
$$

**Step 5:** Battery life on a 2000 mAh, 3.7 V Li-Po cell
$$
E_{batt} = 3.7 \times 2000 \times 10^{-3} = 7.4\text{ Wh}
$$
$$
T = \frac{E_{batt}}{P_{avg}} = \frac{7.4}{7.95 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 930\text{ hours} \approx 38\text{ days}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This is why **deep sleep** is critical in battery-powered IoT nodes — it can extend battery life by **100× or more**.

---

### 🧮 Derivation 4: PWM Signal for LED Brightness Control

**Step 1:** NodeMCU PWM range = 0 to 1023 (8-bit × scale = 10-bit in Arduino framework)

**Step 2:** Duty cycle for `analogWrite(pin, 512)`
$$
D_{\%} = \frac{512}{1023} \times 100 \approx 50.05\%
$$

**Step 3:** PWM period (default ≈ 1 ms)
$$
T = 1\text{ ms} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f = 1\text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 4:** On-time
$$
t_{on} = D_{\%} \times T = 0.5005 \times 1\text{ ms} \approx 0.5\text{ ms}
$$

---

### 🧮 Analytical Matrix: NodeMCU vs Arduino Uno (KTU Comparison Table)

| Feature | NodeMCU (ESP8266) | Arduino Uno (ATmega328P) |
|---|---|---|
| **CPU** | Tensilica L106 32-bit | AVR 8-bit |
| **Clock Speed** | 80–160 MHz | 16 MHz |
| **Operating Voltage** | 3.3 V | 5 V |
| **Wi-Fi** | Built-in 802.11 b/g/n | None (needs shield) |
| **Flash Memory** | 4 MB | 32 KB |
| **RAM** | ~160 KB | 2 KB |
| **GPIO** | 17 | 14 |
| **ADC** | 1 × 10-bit (0–1 V) | 6 × 10-bit (0–5 V) |
| **Price (approx.)** | ₹150–250 | ₹400–500 |
| **Best For** | IoT / Wireless | Standalone / Hobby |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 🖼️ Diagram 1: NodeMCU Block Architecture (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[USB Power 5V Input] --> B[AMS1117 Voltage Regulator 3.3V]
    A --> C[CH340 USB to UART Bridge]
    C --> D[ESP8266EX SoC]
    B --> D
    D --> E[Tensilica L106 CPU 80 to 160 MHz]
    D --> F[Wi-Fi 802.11 b/g/n Radio]
    D --> G[SRAM 160 KB]
    D --> H[SPI Bus]
    H --> I[External Flash 4 MB]
    D --> J[GPIO Bank 17 Pins]
    J --> K[GPIO0 to GPIO16]
    D --> L[ADC 10-bit 0 to 1V]
    D --> M[PWM Outputs]
    D --> N[UART TX RX]
    D --> O[I2C SDA SCL]
    D --> P[SPI CLK MISO MOSI CS]
    Q[Reset Button RST] --> D
    R[Flash Button GPIO0] --> D
    S[Blue LED GPIO2] --> J
    T[Red LED Power] --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of the NodeMCU board. The ESP8266EX SoC is the central controller that orchestrates all peripherals through internal buses.

---

### 🖼️ Diagram 2: Power Distribution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PWR[USB 5V from PC or Adapter] --> VU[VU Pin 5V Direct]
    PWR --> REG[AMS1117 3.3V LDO Regulator]
    REG --> V33[3V Pin 3.3V Regulated Output]
    V33 --> ESP[ESP8266 SoC Core]
    V33 --> GPIO[GPIO Logic Levels]
    V33 --> ADC[ADC Reference]
    V33 --> FLASH[External SPI Flash]
```

---

### 🖼️ Diagram 3: Pin Functionality Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DigitalPins
        D0[GPIO16 D0 Wake]
        D1[GPIO5 D1 I2C SCL]
        D2[GPIO4 D2 I2C SDA]
        D3[GPIO0 D3 Flash Mode]
        D4[GPIO2 D4 Onboard LED]
        D5[GPIO14 D5 SPI CLK]
        D6[GPIO12 D6 SPI MISO]
        D7[GPIO13 D7 SPI MOSI]
        D8[GPIO15 D8 SPI CS]
    end
    subgraph Communication
        TX[GPIO1 TX UART Transmit]
        RX[GPIO3 RX UART Receive]
    end
    subgraph Analog
        A0P[ADC0 A0 Analog Input 0 to 1V]
    end
    subgraph Power
        VUP[VU 5V Passthrough]
        V3P[3V 3.3V Regulated]
        GNDP[GND Ground]
    end
```

---

### 🖼️ Diagram 4: Boot Mode Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Power On or Reset] --> B{GPIO0 State}
    B -->|HIGH| C[Normal Boot Run User Program]
    B -->|LOW FLASH Button Held| D[Flash Mode UART Download]
    C --> E[Execute from SPI Flash]
    D --> F[Wait for Firmware via UART]
    F --> G[Receive New Firmware]
    G --> E
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> To **upload a new program**, hold the **FLASH button**, press **RST**, then release FLASH. This pulls GPIO0 LOW during reset to enter **UART download mode**.

---

### 🖼️ Diagram 5: Complete NodeMCU Interfacing with Sensors (IoT Scenario)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENS1[DHT22 Temperature Sensor] --> D4[GPIO2 D4 Digital]
    SENS2[Soil Moisture Analog Sensor] --> DIV[Voltage Divider 40k 10k]
    DIV --> A0[ADC0 A0 Analog]
    LED1[LED Indicator] --> D1[GPIO5 D1 PWM]
    RELAY[Relay Module 5V] --> D2[GPIO4 D2 Digital]
    OLED[OLED Display SSD1306] --> D5[D5 SCL] & D6[D6 SDA]
    MCU[NodeMCU ESP8266] --> ROUTER[Wi-Fi Router 2.4 GHz]
    ROUTER --> CLOUD[IoT Cloud MQTT or HTTP]
    CLOUD --> DASH[Mobile Dashboard]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]
**List any six hardware components of the NodeMCU development board and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (6 points):**

1. **ESP8266 SoC** – Main processor with built-in Wi-Fi; runs user code.
2. **AMS1117 Voltage Regulator** – Steps down USB 5 V to 3.3 V for the board.
3. **CH340 USB-to-UART Converter** – Allows PC communication and programming.
4. **External SPI Flash (4 MB)** – Stores firmware and user programs.
5. **GPIO Pins (D0–D8, TX, RX, A0)** – Interface for sensors and actuators.
6. **Reset and Flash Buttons** – Used for resetting the board and entering flash mode.
7. **LED Indicators** – Blue LED (GPIO2) shows status; Red LED shows power.

*(Any six well-explained points fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

#### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]
**Why is the NodeMCU ADC pin (A0) not directly 5 V tolerant? How do you interface a 5 V analog sensor with it?**

**Model Answer:**

The NodeMCU's ADC pin operates on an internal reference voltage of **1.0 V**, meaning the maximum allowable input is **1 V**. Applying 5 V directly would saturate and damage the ADC.

**Solution:** Use a **voltage divider** to scale down 5 V → 1 V.

$$
V_{out} = V_{in} \times \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

For 5:1 scaling, choose $R_1 = 40\text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_2 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$.

**Connection:**
- Sensor output → $R_1$ → A0 pin → $R_2$ → GND

---

### 📝 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **ANY ONE** of the following.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **hardware architecture of NodeMCU**. List all major on-board components and their functions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **boot modes of ESP8266/NodeMCU** and describe how to upload a new program. How do the RST and FLASH buttons function? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A (a):

**NodeMCU Hardware Architecture Block Diagram:**

```
USB 5V → AMS1117 → 3.3V → ESP8266 SoC
                            ├── Tensilica L106 CPU
                            ├── Wi-Fi 802.11 b/g/n
                            ├── SRAM
                            ├── GPIO Bank
                            ├── ADC (10-bit)
                            ├── PWM, I2C, SPI, UART
                            └── SPI Bus → External 4MB Flash
USB 5V → CH340 → ESP8266 UART
```

**On-Board Components & Functions:**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **ESP8266 SoC** | Main controller with CPU, Wi-Fi, memory |
| **Tensilica L106** | 32-bit RISC processor core (80/160 MHz) |
| **External SPI Flash** | Stores program code and data (4 MB) |
| **AMS1117 Regulator** | Converts 5 V to 3.3 V |
| **CH340/CP2102** | USB-to-serial bridge for programming |
| **GPIO Pins** | 17 digital I/O pins for interfacing |
| **ADC (A0)** | 10-bit analog input (0–1 V) |
| **Onboard Blue LED** | Connected to GPIO2 (active LOW) |
| **RST Button** | Hardware reset |
| **FLASH Button** | Enters programming mode (pulls GPIO0 LOW) |

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A (b):

**Boot Modes of ESP8266:**

| GPIO0 State | GPIO2 State | GPIO15 State | Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| HIGH | HIGH | LOW | **Normal Flash Boot** |
| LOW | — | — | **UART Download Mode** |

**Steps to Upload a New Program:**

1. Install **Arduino IDE** and add the **ESP8266 board package**.
2. Select the correct board: `NodeMCU 1.0 (ESP-12E Module)`.
3. Select the correct **COM port**.
4. Connect NodeMCU via **USB cable**.
5. (If needed) Hold the **FLASH button**, press **RST**, then release FLASH.
6. Click the **Upload** button in Arduino IDE.
7. The CH340 chip transmits the compiled binary via UART.
8. ESP8266 writes the new firmware to **SPI Flash** and reboots.

**RST Button:** Generates a hardware reset signal → ESP8266 restarts from flash.

**FLASH Button:** Pulls GPIO0 to LOW at boot → ESP8266 enters UART download mode (waits for new firmware).

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice – KTU 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the **pin mapping of NodeMCU** with reference to ESP8266 GPIOs. Identify which pins are safe to use and which must be avoided during boot. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A soil moisture sensor outputs 0–5 V analog. Interface it with NodeMCU's A0 pin. Design a suitable voltage divider and calculate the digital ADC value when the sensor outputs 3.5 V. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B (a):

**Detailed Pin Mapping:**

| NodeMCU Label | ESP GPIO | Safe to Use? | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| D0 | GPIO16 | ✅ Safe | No boot restrictions |
| D1 | GPIO5 | ✅ Safe | I2C SCL |
| D2 | GPIO4 | ✅ Safe | I2C SDA |
| D3 | GPIO0 | ⚠️ Avoid during boot | Must be HIGH at boot |
| D4 | GPIO2 | ⚠️ Avoid during boot | Must be HIGH at boot |
| D5 | GPIO14 | ✅ Safe | SPI CLK |
| D6 | GPIO12 | ✅ Safe | SPI MISO |
| D7 | GPIO13 | ✅ Safe | SPI MOSI |
| D8 | GPIO15 | ⚠️ Avoid during boot | Must be LOW at boot |
| TX | GPIO1 | ⚠️ Avoid during boot | Used for boot serial output |
| RX | GPIO3 | ⚠️ Avoid during boot | Used for boot serial input |
| A0 | ADC0 | ✅ Safe | Analog only, 0–1 V |

**Pins to avoid during boot:** D3 (GPIO0), D4 (GPIO2), D8 (GPIO15), TX (GPIO1), RX (GPIO3).

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B (b):

**Voltage Divider Design:**

To scale 0–5 V to 0–1 V, the divider ratio must be **1:5**.

$$
\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{1}{5}
$$

Choosing $R_2 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$:
$$
R_1 + R_2 = 5 \times 10 = 50\text{ k}\Omega
$$
$$
\boxed{R_1 = 40\text{ k}\Omega,\ R_2 = 10\text{ k}\Omega}
$$

**Connection:** Sensor +ve → R1 (40 kΩ) → A0 → R2 (10 kΩ) → GND

**Calculation for Sensor Output = 3.5 V:**

**Step 1:** Voltage at A0
$$
V_{A0} = 3.5 \times \frac{10}{40 + 10} = 3.5 \times 0.2 = 0.7\text{ V}
$$

**Step 2:** ADC digital value
$$
D = \left\lfloor \frac{0.7}{1.0} \times 1023 \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 716.1 \right\rfloor = 716
$$

**Result:** The ADC will read **716** when the sensor outputs 3.5 V.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting ADC voltage range** — Writing that A0 accepts 0–3.3 V (WRONG — it's 0–1 V).
> 2. **Confusing VU with 3V** — VU is unregulated 5 V from USB; 3V is regulated 3.3 V.
> 3. **Boot pin issues** — Using D3 (GPIO0), D4 (GPIO2), D8 (GPIO15), TX, RX as output without pull-up/down resistors causes boot failures.
> 4. **Forgetting voltage divider** — Connecting 5 V sensors directly to A0 damages the chip.
> 5. **Wrong clock speed** — Stating 16 MHz instead of 80/160 MHz for ESP8266.
> 6. **No mention of CH340/CP2102** — Examiners expect the USB-serial converter to be discussed.
> 7. **Confusing NodeMCU firmware with Arduino** — NodeMCU originally used Lua; the ESP8266 Arduino core is a third-party port.

---

### 🧾 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ NodeMCU = **ESP8266 SoC + Development Board** with Wi-Fi.
- ✅ CPU: **Tensilica L106 32-bit RISC**, 80–160 MHz.
- ✅ Operating Voltage: **3.3 V (NOT 5 V tolerant)**.
- ✅ Wi-Fi: **802.11 b/g/n, 2.4 GHz**, integrated.
- ✅ Flash: **4 MB external SPI Flash**.
- ✅ USB bridge: **CH340 or CP2102** for programming.
- ✅ Voltage regulator: **AMS1117-3.3V** LDO.
- ✅ ADC: **1 channel, 10-bit, 0–1 V only** — use voltage divider for higher voltages.
- ✅ GPIO mapping: D0–D8, TX, RX, A0 — know the ESP8266 internal GPIO numbers.
- ✅ Boot pins to avoid: **GPIO0 (D3), GPIO2 (D4), GPIO15 (D8), GPIO1 (TX), GPIO3 (RX)**.
- ✅ Deep sleep current: **~10 μA** — excellent for battery IoT nodes.
- ✅ PWM range: **0–1023**, default frequency ~1 kHz.
- ✅ Flash button function: **GPIO0 LOW → UART download mode**.
- ✅ RST button function: **Hardware reset → restart from flash**.
- ✅ Onboard blue LED: **GPIO2, active LOW**.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for Pin Avoidance at Boot:** "**D3, D4, D8, TX, RX**" → remember as **"3-4-8-T-R"** — these five pins have special boot-time constraints.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**NodeMCU v1.0** is an **open-source, low-cost, Wi-Fi enabled IoT development board** built around the **Espressif ESP8266 (specifically the ESP-12E module)** system-on-chip (SoC). It integrates a **32-bit Tensilica L106 RISC processor**, on-chip SRAM, a 2.4 GHz IEEE 802.11 b/g/n radio, and a full TCP/IP stack into a single, breadboard-friendly form factor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCGT503 / Module 4):**
> The 2024 Scheme explicitly classifies NodeMCU as a **Wi-Fi-SoC based microcontroller platform** used to bridge the **"Device–Edge–Cloud"** architecture of IoT. The board is the de-facto hardware reference for studying **networked embedded firmware development** under the course outcomes **CO4 (IoT Hardware Platforms)** and **CO5 (Edge Computing & Prototyping)**.

The board derives its name from:
- **Node** → Node.js (the original firmware scripting environment was Lua/Node.js-style event-driven)
- **MCU** → Microcontroller Unit

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Swiss Army Knife of IoT Prototyping"**
>
> Imagine you are a chef who wants to cook a meal that **automatically sends a notification to your phone** when the food is ready. A traditional Arduino would be a *stove* — powerful for cooking, but it cannot call your phone. A NodeMCU is a *stove with a built-in Wi-Fi antenna and a smartphone* combined into one tiny gadget. It can:
> - **Sense** (like a thermometer — ADC pin),
> - **Decide** (like a chef — Tensilica CPU),
> - **Communicate** (like a phone — Wi-Fi radio),
> - **Act** (like a kitchen timer — GPIO/PWM pin).
>
> In one board, you get **a microcontroller + a wireless network interface**, eliminating the need for a separate Wi-Fi shield (which Arduinos require).

## 1.3 Key Physical & Performance Metrics (Bold Highlights)

- **Core Processor:** Tensilica Xtensa L106 32-bit RISC @ **80 MHz** (overclockable to **160 MHz**)
- **Operating Voltage:** **3.3 V** (logic-level tolerant I/O)
- **Input Voltage (VIN):** **7 V – 12 V DC** (via onboard LDO / micro-USB)
- **On-board Flash:** **4 MB** (typical for v1.0 with ESP-12E)
- **SRAM:** **160 KB** (user-available ≈ 50 KB after Wi-Fi stack)
- **GPIO Pins:** **17** usable (out of 30 physical pads)
- **ADC:** **1 × 10-bit SAR ADC** (0 V – 1.0 V range on TOUT pin)
- **Wi-Fi Standard:** **IEEE 802.11 b/g/n @ 2.4 GHz**
- **USB-to-UART Bridge:** **CP2102** (NodeMCU v1.0) or **CH340** (v3 clones)
- **PCB Dimensions:** **≈ 49 mm × 24.5 mm**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Reminder for KTU Exams:**
> The **ADC range is 0 V – 1.0 V**, NOT 0 V – 3.3 V. A voltage divider is mandatory for any sensor output > 1.0 V. Examiners frequently deduct **2 marks** for confusing this with Arduino's 0–5 V ADC.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture Breakdown (Top-Down)

### Layer 1 — Radio / PHY Layer
- Transceiver: **2.4 GHz, 802.11 b/g/n**
- TX Power: **+19.5 dBm** (b mode), **+16 dBm** (n mode)
- RX Sensitivity: **−98 dBm**
- Antenna: **PCB trace antenna (meandered monopole)**
- Security: **WPA/WPA2, WEP, TKIP, AES**

### Layer 2 — Network / Protocol Stack
- **TCP/IP v4** (full stack on-chip)
- **UDP, HTTP, MQTT, CoAP** (application layer, firmware-side)
- Station (STA), SoftAP, and **STA + AP** hybrid modes

### Layer 3 — SoC Subsystems
- **CPU:** Tensilica L106 (32-bit, little-endian, 16-bit instructions)
- **Memory:** Unified 160 KB SRAM, 4 MB SPI Flash (external)
- **Peripherals:** UART × 2, SPI × 2, I²C × 1, I²S × 1, 10-bit ADC, PWM (software), GPIO × 17
- **RTC:** Internal, supports deep-sleep wake-up

### Layer 4 — Firmware Layer
- Default firmware: **NodeMCU Lua interpreter** (eLua)
- Arduino IDE compatible via **esp8266 core for Arduino**
- Other toolchains: **MicroPython, ESP-IDF (Espressif native), PlatformIO**

## 2.2 NodeMCU v1.0 Pinout — KTU Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> **The "D" prefix is the Arduino-style GPIO number used in code**, while the "G" or unmarked numbers refer to the **internal ESP8266 GPIO numbers**. Confusing these is the #1 mistake in KTU board-exams.

| Board Label | ESP GPIO | Function | Safe Usage Limit |
|-------------|----------|----------|------------------|
| D0 | GPIO16 | Wake from deep-sleep, no I2C, no PWM, no interrupt | 3.3 V / 12 mA |
| D1 | GPIO5 | I²C SCL, PWM | 3.3 V |
| D2 | GPIO4 | I²C SDA, PWM | 3.3 V |
| D3 | GPIO0 | Flash button, PWM | 3.3 V |
| D4 | GPIO2 | On-board LED (active LOW), PWM, SDA (alt) | 3.3 V |
| D5 | GPIO14 | SPI CLK, PWM | 3.3 V |
| D6 | GPIO12 | SPI MISO, PWM | 3.3 V |
| D7 | GPIO13 | SPI MOSI, PWM | 3.3 V |
| D8 | GPIO15 | SPI SS, PWM | 3.3 V |
| TX | GPIO1 | UART0 TXD (used for programming) | 3.3 V |
| RX | GPIO3 | UART0 RXD (used for programming) | 3.3 V |
| A0 | ADC0 | **10-bit ADC, 0–1.0 V only** | **Max 1.0 V input** |
| SD2 / SD3 | GPIO9 / GPIO10 | Reserved for flash (cannot be used) | — |
| VU | — | Direct 5 V from USB (bypasses LDO) | 5 V |
| VIN | — | External DC input | 7–12 V |
| GND | — | Common ground (3 pins available) | 0 V |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `|` in all table cells to preserve Markdown rendering.** This rule applies even to mathematical delimiters.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit | Boundary Condition |
|---------|----------------|------|--------------------|
| ADC Resolution | $$V_{LSB} \;=\; \frac{V_{REF}}{2^{n}} \;=\; \frac{1.0 \text{ V}}{2^{10}}$$ | Volts / step | $n = 10$ bits, $V_{REF} = 1.0$ V |
| Quantized ADC Output | $$V_{in} \;=\; D \cdot V_{LSB} \;=\; \frac{D}{1024}$$ | Volts | $D \in \mathbb{Z} \mid 0 \le D \le 1023$ |
| Voltage Divider (for >1 V sensors) | $$V_{out} \;=\; V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$ | Volts | Choose $R_2 \vert (R_1 + R_2) = 1 \vert 3$ for 3.3 V → 0.825 V |
| PWM Duty Cycle | $$D\% \;=\; \frac{t_{on}}{T_{period}} \times 100$$ | Percent | $0\% \le D \le 100\%$ |
| PWM Average Voltage | $$V_{avg} \;=\; D\% \times V_{DD}$$ | Volts | $V_{DD} = 3.3$ V |
| Power Consumption (Active TX) | $$P_{avg} \;=\; I_{avg} \times 3.3 \text{ V}$$ | Watts | $I_{avg} \approx 170$ mA peak |
| Deep-Sleep Current | $$Q_{battery} \;=\; I_{sleep} \times t_{sleep} \;=\; 10\mu A \cdot t$$ | Coulombs | $I_{sleep} \approx 10\ \mu A$ |
| Battery Lifetime | $$t_{life} \;=\; \frac{C_{batt}(mAh)}{I_{avg}(mA)} \times 0.7$$ | Hours | 0.7 = derating factor |
| Wi-Fi RSSI → Distance (Friis) | $$P_r \;=\; P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$$ | Watts | Free-space LOS only |
| I²C Clock Frequency | $$f_{SCL} \le 400 \text{ kHz}$$ (Fast Mode) | Hz | NodeMCU default $\approx 100$ kHz |
| Flash Memory Address | $$A_{byte} \;=\; N_{block} \times 4\text{KB} + N_{sector} \times 4\text{KB} + N_{page} \times 256$$ | Bytes | SPI Flash: 4 MB = 4096 KB |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Application Box:**
> - **Smart Agriculture:** Soil-moisture sensor (analog) → A0 → NodeMCU → Wi-Fi → ThingSpeak cloud → SMS alert to farmer.
> - **Home Automation:** DHT11 (digital) → D2 → NodeMCU → MQTT broker (HiveMQ) → Mobile app toggle.
> - **Predictive Maintenance:** Vibration sensor (analog) → voltage divider → A0 → NodeMCU → AWS IoT → Grafana dashboard.
> - **Wearable Health Bands:** Heart-rate (PPG) → I²C → D1/D2 → NodeMCU → BLE/Wi-Fi gateway → Hospital cloud.

These four verticals correspond to the **CO4–CO5 KTU mapping** and are **frequently asked in Module 4 exam questions** in the form of "Design an IoT system using NodeMCU to monitor ______."

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Derivation #1: ADC Resolution of NodeMCU**

**Problem Statement (Typical 7-Mark KTU Question):**
A temperature sensor (LM35) outputs **10 mV/°C** and is connected to NodeMCU's A0 pin through a voltage divider. The measured ADC digital value is **D = 312**. Calculate the actual sensor voltage and the corresponding temperature.

**Step 1 — Identify the ADC parameters.**
The NodeMCU's 10-bit ADC uses an internal reference voltage.

$$V_{REF} \;=\; 1.0 \text{ V}, \qquad n \;=\; 10 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Least Significant Bit (LSB) voltage.**
This is the smallest voltage step the ADC can distinguish.

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{LSB} \;=\; \frac{V_{REF}}{2^{n}}
\;=\; \frac{1.0 \text{ V}}{2^{10}}
\;=\; \frac{1.0 \text{ V}}{1024}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the numerical value.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{LSB} \;=\; 0.0009765625 \text{ V}
\;\approx\; 0.977 \text{ mV per LSB step}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert the digital count to input voltage.**
The general ADC relation: $V_{in} = D \cdot V_{LSB}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{in} \;=\; D \cdot V_{LSB}
\;=\; 312 \times 0.0009765625 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{in} \;=\; 0.3046875 \text{ V}
\;\approx\; 0.305 \text{ V} \;=\; 304.69 \text{ mV}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Convert sensor voltage to temperature.**
LM35 transfer function: $V = 10 \text{ mV}/°C \times T$.

$$\begin{aligned}
T \;=\; \frac{V_{in}}{10 \text{ mV}/°C}
\;=\; \frac{304.69 \text{ mV}}{10 \text{ mV}/°C}
\;=\; 30.469 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Final Answer & Sanity Check.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T \;\approx\; 30.47 \text{ °C} \quad (\text{rounded to 2 decimal places})
\end{aligned}$$

> Sanity check: 30 °C is within the LM35's rated operating range (−55 °C to +150 °C) and the ADC reading (312/1024 ≈ 30.5%) is well below the saturation limit of 1.0 V. ✓

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Derivation #2: Voltage Divider Design for 3.3 V Sensor**

**Problem Statement:**
A sensor outputs **3.3 V** at full scale. Design a resistor divider so the output ≤ 1.0 V (NodeMCU ADC maximum).

**Step 1 — Apply the divider equation.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{out} \;=\; V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Set target ratio.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} \;=\; \frac{1.0 \text{ V}}{3.3 \text{ V}} \;\approx\; 0.303
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Pick a standard value for $R_1$ and solve for $R_2$.**

Let $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$.

$$\begin{aligned}
0.303 \;\approx\; \frac{R_2}{10\,\text{k}\Omega + R_2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-multiply and solve.**

$$\begin{aligned}
0.303 \times (10\,\text{k}\Omega + R_2) \;=\; R_2
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
3.03 \text{ k}\Omega + 0.303 R_2 \;=\; R_2
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
3.03 \text{ k}\Omega \;=\; 0.697 R_2
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
R_2 \;=\; \frac{3.03 \text{ k}\Omega}{0.697}
\;\approx\; 4.347 \text{ k}\Omega
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Round to nearest E12 standard value.**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_2 \;\approx\; 3.9 \text{ k}\Omega \quad \text{(E12 standard)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Verify the new output voltage.**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{out} \;=\; 3.3 \text{ V} \cdot \frac{3.9\,\text{k}\Omega}{10\,\text{k}\Omega + 3.9\,\text{k}\Omega}
\;=\; 3.3 \text{ V} \cdot 0.2804
\;=\; 0.925 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

This is within the 1.0 V ADC limit. ✓

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Derivation #3: Deep-Sleep Battery Lifetime**

**Problem Statement:**
A NodeMCU is powered by a **1000 mAh Li-Po battery**. The board spends **99% of the time in deep-sleep** (10 µA) and **1% active** (average 80 mA during Wi-Fi transmission). Estimate the operational lifetime.

**Step 1 — Calculate the average current.**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{avg} \;=\; 0.99 \times 10\,\mu A + 0.01 \times 80\,000\,\mu A
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{avg} \;=\; 9.9\,\mu A + 800\,\mu A
\;=\; 809.9\,\mu A
\;\approx\; 0.81 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the derated battery formula.**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{life} \;=\; \frac{C_{batt}}{I_{avg}} \times 0.7
\;=\; \frac{1000 \text{ mAh}}{0.81 \text{ mA}} \times 0.7
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{life} \;\approx\; 1234.6 \text{ h} \times 0.7
\;\approx\; 864 \text{ h}
\;\approx\; 36 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** With aggressive deep-sleep duty cycling, a NodeMCU can run for **over a month** on a single 1000 mAh cell — a critical design metric for **battery-powered IoT sensor nodes**.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NodeMCU v1.0 — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pwrVin["VIN 7-12V DC"] --> ldo["AMS1117 LDO 3.3V Regulator"]
    pwrUSB["Micro USB 5V"] --> cdc["CP2102 USB-to-UART Bridge"]
    pwrUSB --> ldo
    cdc --> uart0["UART0 TXD RXD"]
    uart0 --> esp["ESP8266 ESP-12E SoC"]
    ldo --> esp
    esp --> cpu["Tensilica L106 32-bit CPU 80 MHz"]
    esp --> sram["160 KB SRAM"]
    esp --> flash["4 MB SPI Flash"]
    esp --> radio["2.4 GHz 802.11 b/g/n Radio"]
    esp --> gpio["17x GPIO Multiplexer"]
    esp --> adc["10-bit SAR ADC 0 to 1.0V"]
    gpio --> pinD1["D1 GPIO5 I2C SCL"]
    gpio --> pinD2["D2 GPIO4 I2C SDA"]
    gpio --> pinD5["D5 GPIO14 SPI CLK"]
    gpio --> pinTX["TX GPIO1 UART"]
    gpio --> pinRX["RX GPIO3 UART"]
    adc --> pinA0["A0 ADC0"]
    radio --> ant["PCB Trace Antenna"]
    radio --> cloud["Wi-Fi Router to Cloud"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Notes:**
> - The `cdc` node represents the **CP2102** USB bridge which converts USB signals to UART for programming the ESP-12E.
> - The `ldo` node is the **AMS1117-3.3** voltage regulator that steps down VIN/USB 5 V to the required 3.3 V rail.
> - The `ant` node represents the **on-board PCB meandered antenna** etched directly into the FR4 substrate — no external antenna is required for typical indoor use.

## 4.2 NodeMCU Programming & Boot Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1["Apply Power 3.3V or USB"] --> check1{"GPIO0 State at Boot"}
    check1 -- "LOW" --> modeFlash["Flash Mode Programmer Connected"]
    check1 -- "HIGH" --> modeRun["Normal Execution Mode"]
    modeRun --> init["Initialize CPU and SPI Flash"]
    init --> loadFW["Load Firmware from Flash 0x00000"]
    loadFW --> runLua["Run Lua Interpreter OR Arduino Sketch"]
    runLua --> wifiConn["Connect to Wi-Fi AP via Station Mode"]
    wifiConn --> loop["Main Loop: Read Sensors Transmit via MQTT HTTP"]
    loop --> deepSleep{"Timer Expired"}
    deepSleep -- "Yes" --> sleep["Enter Deep-Sleep 10 uA"]
    deepSleep -- "No" --> loop
    sleep --> wake["Wake via RTC or GPIO16 Trigger"]
    wake --> loop
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph pwrBlock["Power Block"]
        vVin["VIN 7-12V"]
        v3v3["3V3 Pin Direct from LDO"]
        vGND["GND x3 Common"]
        vVU["VU 5V from USB bypass"]
    end
    subgraph analogBlock["Analog Block"]
        aA0["A0 ADC 10-bit 0-1.0V"]
    end
    subgraph digitalBlock["Digital GPIO Block"]
        dD0["D0 GPIO16"]
        dD1["D1 GPIO5"]
        dD2["D2 GPIO4"]
        dD3["D3 GPIO0"]
        dD4["D4 GPIO2 LED"]
        dD5["D5 GPIO14"]
        dD6["D6 GPIO12"]
        dD7["D7 GPIO13"]
        dD8["D8 GPIO15"]
    end
    subgraph uartBlock["UART Block"]
        tTX["TX GPIO1"]
        rRX["RX GPIO3"]
    end
    pwrBlock --> espCore["ESP-12E Module"]
    analogBlock --> espCore
    digitalBlock --> espCore
    uartBlock --> espCore
    espCore --> wifiOut["2.4 GHz RF Output"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Penalty Zone 1 (2 marks):** Writing ADC range as "0–3.3 V" instead of "0–1.0 V". Memorize the **1.0 V reference**.
> - **Penalty Zone 2 (2 marks):** Confusing board labels (`D1`) with ESP GPIO numbers (`GPIO5`). Examiners will mark this as a "conceptual error."
> - **Penalty Zone 3 (3 marks):** Forgetting the **voltage divider** requirement when interfacing analog sensors > 1.0 V.
> - **Penalty Zone 4 (2 marks):** Not mentioning the **CP2102/CH340 USB-to-UART bridge** when explaining the programming flow.
> - **Penalty Zone 5 (1 mark):** Spelling NodeMCU as "Node MCU," "Node-MCU," or "node MCU" — the **camel-case form "NodeMCU"** is the only accepted convention.

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Processor:** Tensilica L106 32-bit RISC CPU @ **80 MHz** (1/2 mark)
2. **Wireless:** 2.4 GHz IEEE **802.11 b/g/n** Wi-Fi with on-board PCB antenna (1/2 mark)
3. **Operating Voltage:** **3.3 V** logic level; **7–12 V** VIN input (1/2 mark)
4. **Memory:** **160 KB SRAM** + **4 MB SPI Flash** (1/2 mark)
5. **GPIO:** 17 usable digital I/O pins supporting **PWM, I²C, SPI, UART, 1-Wire** (1/2 mark)
6. **ADC:** One **10-bit SAR ADC** on pin A0 with **0–1.0 V** input range (1/2 mark)
7. **USB Interface:** **CP2102** USB-to-UART bridge for programming and serial monitor (1/2 mark)
