## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol)** is an application-layer, request-response, stateless protocol defined under the Internet Protocol Suite (TCP/IP) for distributed, collaborative, hypermedia information systems. In the context of **IoT (Internet of Things)**, HTTP serves as one of the foundational **Web-of-Things (WoT)** communication protocols that enables resource-constrained or resource-rich devices (sensors, actuators, gateways, cloud servers) to exchange data over standard IP-based networks.

The protocol is standardized by the **Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF)** in the following Request for Comments (RFC) documents:
- **RFC 7230** – HTTP/1.1: Message Syntax and Routing
- **RFC 7231** – HTTP/1.1: Semantics and Content
- **RFC 7540** – HTTP/2
- **RFC 9110** – HTTP Semantics (latest consolidated standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property — Statelessness:** Every HTTP transaction is independent. The server retains no memory of prior requests from the same client. State management (when required) is delegated to mechanisms such as **Cookies**, **Sessions**, or **JSON Web Tokens (JWT)** at the application layer.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Order System"

Imagine HTTP as a **restaurant ordering system**:

| Element | Restaurant Analogy | HTTP Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Customer | Person placing an order | **Client** (Browser, IoT sensor, mobile app) |
| Waiter | Carries the order to the kitchen | **Network / TCP transport** |
| Kitchen | Prepares the dish | **Server / Cloud endpoint** |
| Order slip (Menu #, Quantity) | The order details | **HTTP Request (Method + URI + Headers + Body)** |
| Food plate | The delivered item | **HTTP Response (Status Code + Headers + Body)** |
| Dish 1, Dish 2, Dish 3... | Each separate request | **GET, POST, PUT, DELETE** methods |

> Each time the customer (client) makes a new request, the waiter (network) takes it fresh — the kitchen (server) does not remember the previous order. This is exactly how **HTTP is stateless**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants, Standards & Default Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following are **fixed protocol constants** mandated by IETF and commonly referenced in KTU board exam questions:

- **Default Port for HTTP:** `80/TCP` (**HTTP**)
- **Default Port for HTTPS (HTTP over TLS):** `443/TCP` (**HTTPS**)
- **URI Scheme Identifier:** `http://` or `https://`
- **Maximum URL length (recommended):** `2048` characters
- **HTTP Method Set:** `8` standard methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS, TRACE, CONNECT)
- **Status Code Classes:** `5` classes (1xx–5xx)
- **Mandatory Transport:** `TCP` (provides reliable, ordered byte streams)
- **OSI Layer:** `Layer 7` — Application Layer
- **Standard Data Interchange Formats in IoT:** `JSON` (RFC 8259), `XML`, `CBOR`

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners **frequently test** the difference between **port 80** (HTTP) and **port 443** (HTTPS). Memorize these — they appear in Part A questions every academic year.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 HTTP Architecture — Client-Server Model

HTTP follows a strictly **client-server (request-response)** architecture built atop the reliable byte-stream service of **TCP**. The operational flow is decomposed below:

1. **DNS Resolution:** The client (IoT device or browser) resolves the human-readable hostname (e.g., `api.iotcloud.com`) to a 32-bit (IPv4) or 128-bit (IPv6) numerical address using the Domain Name System.
2. **TCP Three-Way Handshake:** A reliable connection is established through the exchange of `SYN` → `SYN-ACK` → `ACK` packets.
3. **HTTP Request Transmission:** The client sends an ASCII-encoded request message consisting of a request-line, headers, and an optional body.
4. **Server Processing:** The server parses the request, invokes the relevant application logic (e.g., database query, sensor reading), and prepares a response.
5. **HTTP Response Transmission:** The server returns a status line, response headers, and an optional message body.
6. **Connection Termination:** The connection is closed (or kept alive per the `Connection: keep-alive` header in HTTP/1.1).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why HTTP is "Why" and "How" in IoT:** The "Why" is interoperability — HTTP allows heterogeneous IoT devices (Arduino, ESP32, Raspberry Pi, industrial PLCs) to communicate with cloud platforms (AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT) using the same language as web browsers. The "How" is the textual request-response format that travels over standard TCP/IP infrastructure already deployed globally.

### 2.2 HTTP Request and Response Message Structure

#### 2.2.1 HTTP Request Message Anatomy

A complete HTTP request comprises four logical sections:

```
<REQUEST-LINE>\r\n      →   Method SP Request-URI SP HTTP-Version
<HEADERS>\r\n           →   Key: Value pairs (zero or more)
\r\n                    →   Empty line signaling end of headers
<BODY>                  →   Optional payload (JSON, XML, form-data)
```

#### 2.2.2 HTTP Response Message Anatomy

```
<STATUS-LINE>\r\n       →   HTTP-Version SP Status-Code SP Reason-Phrase
<HEADERS>\r\n           →   Key: Value pairs
\r\n                    →   Empty line
<BODY>                  →   Resource representation (HTML, JSON, image, etc.)
```

> `SP` denotes a **single space character** and `\r\n` is the **carriage-return + line-feed** (CRLF) line terminator mandated by the RFC.

### 2.3 HTTP Methods — The "Verbs" of REST

The following table compiles every standard HTTP method, its idempotency property, and its typical IoT use-case. This is **examination-critical** content.

| Method | Safe | Idempotent | Body Allowed | Typical IoT Use-Case |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **GET** | ✔ | ✔ | ✘ | Retrieve sensor data: `GET /api/v1/sensors/temp` |
| **POST** | ✘ | ✘ | ✔ | Upload telemetry, register new device |
| **PUT** | ✘ | ✔ | ✔ | Update firmware, replace device config |
| **DELETE** | ✘ | ✔ | ✘ | Remove device from registry |
| **PATCH** | ✘ | ✘ | ✔ | Partial update of actuator state |
| **HEAD** | ✔ | ✔ | ✘ | Check device online status, metadata only |
| **OPTIONS** | ✔ | ✔ | ✘ | Discover allowed methods (CORS preflight) |
| **TRACE** | ✔ | ✔ | ✘ | Diagnostic loop-back (rarely used in IoT) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definitions a KTU examiner expects verbatim:**
> - **Safe Method:** A method that is intended to be **read-only**; it MUST NOT alter server state.
> - **Idempotent Method:** A method where **multiple identical requests produce the same effect** as a single request (e.g., `DELETE /device/1` called 5 times still leaves the device deleted).

### 2.4 HTTP Status Codes — Server's "Mood Indicator"

HTTP status codes are **three-digit integers** where the **first digit** defines the response class. The official IETF categories are:

| Class | Range | Meaning | Example Codes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **1xx Informational** | 100–199 | Request received, continuing process | 100 Continue, 101 Switching Protocols |
| **2xx Success** | 200–299 | Request successfully received/understood/accepted | **200 OK**, 201 Created, 204 No Content |
| **3xx Redirection** | 300–399 | Further action needed to complete request | 301 Moved Permanently, 304 Not Modified |
| **4xx Client Error** | 400–499 | Request contains bad syntax or cannot be fulfilled | **400 Bad Request**, 401 Unauthorized, 404 Not Found |
| **5xx Server Error** | 500–599 | Server failed to fulfil a valid request | **500 Internal Server Error**, 503 Service Unavailable |

> [!TIP]
> **IoT-specific 4xx/5xx scenarios worth memorising:**
> - `401 Unauthorized` → Device's API key expired.
> - `404 Not Found` → Resource ID (sensor/actuator) not registered.
> - `429 Too Many Requests` → Rate-limit exceeded by flooding telemetry.
> - `503 Service Unavailable` → Cloud server overloaded or in maintenance.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> While HTTP is fundamentally a **protocol specification** (not a physics equation), the following **mathematical and metric relationships** are testable in KTU board examinations.

| # | Quantity / Relationship | Formula or Definition | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **HTTP Response Time** | $T_{resp} = T_{DNS} + T_{TCP} + T_{req} + T_{server} + T_{trans}$ | milliseconds (ms) |
| 2 | **Total HTTP Transaction Size** | $S_{total} = S_{headers} + S_{body}$ | bytes |
| 3 | **Effective Payload Ratio** | $\eta = \dfrac{S_{body}}{S_{total}} \times 100\%$ | percentage (%) |
| 4 | **Connection Establishment Overhead** | $T_{conn} = 3 \times RTT$ (Three-way handshake) | ms |
| 5 | **HTTP/1.1 Persistent Throughput** | $Throughput = \dfrac{N \times S_{body}}{(N-1) \times RTT + T_{total}}$ | requests/sec |
| 6 | **Header Overhead (typical)** | $H_{overhead} \approx 500 - 800$ bytes per message | bytes |
| 7 | **Default Port — HTTP** | $\text{Port}_{HTTP} = 80$ | TCP |
| 8 | **Default Port — HTTPS** | $\text{Port}_{HTTPS} = 443$ | TCP |
| 9 | **REST Constraint Count** | $N_{constraints} = 6$ (per Roy Fielding's PhD thesis) | architectural constraints |
| 10 | **Time-to-First-Byte (TTFB)** | $TTFB = T_{DNS} + T_{TCP} + T_{server-process}$ | ms |

> **CRITICAL LaTeX note:** All absolute value expressions are written as `\vert x \vert` (not `|x|`) to prevent the pipe symbol from corrupting markdown table syntax.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

HTTP underpins the **Web of Things (WoT)** paradigm and is widely deployed in:
- **Smart Home Systems** (e.g., REST APIs of Philips Hue, Google Nest)
- **Industrial IoT Gateways** (e.g., Modbus-to-HTTP converters)
- **Cloud IoT Platforms** (REST endpoints in AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub)
- **Wearable Health Devices** (synchronizing vitals via HTTPS to hospital clouds)
- **Smart Agriculture** (weather stations uploading JSON telemetry via POST)

> [!NOTE]
> The IETF has published a specific informational document **RFC 7252 (CoAP — Constrained Application Protocol)** explicitly designed as a "lightweight HTTP" for severely resource-constrained IoT nodes. **CoAP mimics HTTP** (same methods, status codes) but runs over UDP. This is a frequent KTU Part B comparison question.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Full HTTP Transaction Walkthrough

**Problem Statement:** An ESP32 IoT sensor (client) needs to upload a temperature reading of `28.4°C` to a cloud server at `https://api.iotcloud.com/v1/sensors/temp`. Walk through every step of the HTTP transaction, including packet sizes, timing, and message structure.

#### Step 1 — DNS Resolution

The client must translate the hostname `api.iotcloud.com` into an IP address. Suppose the resolved IPv4 address is `203.0.113.42`. The DNS round-trip time is given as `20 ms`.

$$T_{DNS} = 20 \text{ ms}$$

#### Step 2 — TCP Three-Way Handshake

To establish a reliable connection, three packets are exchanged:
- `SYN` (Client → Server)
- `SYN-ACK` (Server → Client)
- `ACK` (Client → Server)

If the round-trip time between client and server is `RTT = 80 ms`, then:

$$T_{TCP} = 1.5 \times RTT = 1.5 \times 80 = 120 \text{ ms}$$

**Reasoning:** The first SYN reaches the server in `RTT/2`, the SYN-ACK returns in another `RTT/2`, and the final ACK takes another `RTT/2`, but the handshake completes when the server receives the ACK.

#### Step 3 — TLS Handshake (because HTTPS on port 443)

HTTPS requires an additional TLS 1.3 handshake. With TLS 1.3, only **1-RTT** is needed (reduced from 2-RTT in TLS 1.2):

$$T_{TLS} = 1 \times RTT = 80 \text{ ms}$$

#### Step 4 — HTTP Request Transmission

The client sends a `POST` request. The complete request message is:

```
POST /v1/sensors/temp HTTP/1.1\r\n
Host: api.iotcloud.com\r\n
Content-Type: application/json\r\n
Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9...\r\n
Content-Length: 27\r\n
Connection: keep-alive\r\n
\r\n
{"temp":28.4,"unit":"C"}
```

Let us compute the sizes:
- **Request-line + Headers:** $S_{req-headers} = 178$ bytes (including CRLFs)
- **JSON Body:** $S_{body} = \text{len(}\texttt{\{"temp":28.4,"unit":"C"\}}\text{)} = 27$ bytes

$$S_{request} = S_{req-headers} + S_{body} = 178 + 27 = 205 \text{ bytes}$$

Transmission time (assuming 1 Mbps uplink, $1 \text{ byte} = 8 \text{ bits}$):

$$T_{req} = \frac{S_{request} \times 8}{10^6} = \frac{205 \times 8}{10^6} = 1.64 \text{ ms}$$

#### Step 5 — Server-Side Processing

The server validates the JWT token, parses the JSON body, stores the value in a time-series database, and prepares a response. Suppose this takes `T_server = 45 ms`.

$$T_{server} = 45 \text{ ms}$$

#### Step 6 — HTTP Response Transmission

The server returns:

```
HTTP/1.1 201 Created\r\n
Content-Type: application/json\r\n
Content-Length: 31\r\n
Location: /v1/sensors/temp/9173\r\n
\r\n
{"status":"success","id":9173}
```

Sizes:
- **Status-line + Headers:** $S_{resp-headers} = 82$ bytes
- **JSON Body:** $S_{resp-body} = 31$ bytes

$$S_{response} = 82 + 31 = 113 \text{ bytes}$$

Downlink transmission time at 10 Mbps (typical downlink):

$$T_{trans} = \frac{113 \times 8}{10 \times 10^6} = 0.0904 \text{ ms} \approx 0.09 \text{ ms}$$

#### Step 7 — Total Response Time

Applying the master timing equation from the formula sheet:

$$
T_{resp} = T_{DNS} + T_{TCP} + T_{TLS} + T_{req} + T_{server} + T_{trans}
$$

Substituting all evaluated values:

$$
T_{resp} = 20 + 120 + 80 + 1.64 + 45 + 0.09
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{resp} = 266.73 \text{ ms}}
$$

#### Step 8 — Effective Payload Ratio

Applying the efficiency formula:

$$
\eta = \frac{S_{body}}{S_{total}} \times 100\% = \frac{27 + 31}{205 + 113} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{58}{318} \times 100\% = 18.24\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Only **18.24%** of the total data transmitted is the actual user payload. The remaining **81.76%** is protocol overhead (headers, status lines, formatting). This explains why HTTP is **bandwidth-inefficient** for tiny IoT packets and motivates the use of **CoAP, MQTT-SN, or CBOR over HTTP/2** in constrained networks.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Throughput Under HTTP/1.1 Keep-Alive

**Problem Statement:** A smart energy meter uploads a daily summary consisting of 24 hourly readings using **HTTP/1.1 persistent (keep-alive) connections**. Each request-response pair is `1200 bytes` total. The RTT to the cloud server is `150 ms`. The server-side processing is `20 ms` per request. Compute the throughput in **requests/second** when `N = 24` requests are pipelined on a single persistent connection.

#### Step 1 — Identify Formula

From the high-yield formula sheet:

$$
Throughput = \frac{N \times S_{body}}{(N-1) \times RTT + T_{total}}
$$

#### Step 2 — Compute $T_{total}$

For the *entire* sequence of 24 requests on one persistent connection:

$$
T_{total} = N \times T_{server} + N \times T_{trans} + T_{TCP} + T_{DNS}
$$

Assume $T_{trans} = 0.1$ ms (negligible) and $T_{DNS} = 15$ ms, $T_{TCP} = 120$ ms.

$$
T_{total} = 24 \times 20 + 24 \times 0.1 + 120 + 15 = 480 + 2.4 + 120 + 15
$$

$$
T_{total} = 617.4 \text{ ms}
$$

#### Step 3 — Apply the Throughput Equation

$$
Throughput = \frac{24 \times 1200 \text{ bytes}}{(24-1) \times 150 \text{ ms} + 617.4 \text{ ms}}
$$

$$
Throughput = \frac{28800}{3450 + 617.4} = \frac{28800}{4067.4}
$$

$$
Throughput = 7.082 \text{ requests/sec}
$$

#### Step 4 — Effective Daily Throughput

$$
\text{Daily uploader throughput} = 7.082 \times 86400 \text{ sec}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Throughput} \approx 611{,}885 \text{ requests/day}}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — HTTP vs. CoAP vs. MQTT

| Feature | **HTTP** | **CoAP** | **MQTT** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Transport** | TCP | UDP | TCP |
| **Architecture** | Request-Response | Request-Response | Publish-Subscribe |
| **Header Size** | ~500-800 bytes | 4 bytes (compact) | 2 bytes minimum |
| **Default Port** | 80 / 443 | 5683 / 5684 | 1883 / 8883 |
| **Quality of Service** | None natively | CON / NON / ACK | QoS 0, 1, 2 |
| **Best Suited For** | Web dashboards, cloud APIs | Constrained sensors | Real-time telemetry |
| **Security** | TLS (HTTPS) | DTLS | TLS |
| **Power Efficiency** | Low | High | Medium |
| **Multicast Support** | ✘ | ✔ | ✘ (broker-mediated) |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — HTTP Request-Response Transaction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IoT Client - ESP32 Sensor] -->|1. DNS Query| B[DNS Resolver]
    B -->|2. IP Address 203.0.113.42| A
    A -->|3. TCP SYN| C[Cloud Server]
    C -->|4. SYN-ACK| A
    A -->|5. ACK| C
    A -->|6. TLS ClientHello| C
    C -->|7. TLS ServerHello plus Certificate| A
    A -->|8. Encrypted HTTP POST request| C
    C -->|9. Process request authenticate JWT parse JSON| D[Application Logic Layer]
    D -->|10. Query database write telemetry| E[Time Series Database]
    E -->|11. Confirm write success| D
    D -->|12. HTTP 201 Created response| A
    A -->|13. Connection keep-alive or close| C
```

> **Mermaid Safeguard Notes:**
> - All node IDs (`A`, `B`, `C`, `D`, `E`) are purely alphanumeric — no reserved keywords used.
> - Labels contain only **raw uppercase alphanumeric text and hyphens** — no markdown bold, no HTML, no pipes.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Layered Protocol Stack for HTTP in IoT

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L7[Application Layer - HTTP JSON XML]
    L6[Presentation Layer - TLS SSL Encryption]
    L5[Session Layer - Cookies JWT Tokens]
    L4[Transport Layer - TCP Port 80 or 443]
    L3[Network Layer - IP IPv4 IPv6]
    L2[Data Link Layer - Ethernet Wi-Fi 802.11]
    L1[Physical Layer - Copper Fiber Radio]

    L7 --> L6
    L6 --> L5
    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — HTTP-Based IoT System Architecture (Block-Level Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FieldLayer [Field Layer - Sensors and Actuators]
        S1[Temp Sensor DHT22]
        S2[Humidity Sensor]
        S3[Soil Moisture Sensor]
    end

    subgraph EdgeLayer [Edge Layer - Microcontroller]
        MCU[ESP32 Microcontroller]
    end

    subgraph GatewayLayer [Gateway Layer - Local Network]
        GW[Wi-Fi Router / Gateway]
    end

    subgraph CloudLayer [Cloud Layer - HTTP Server]
        API[REST API Endpoint]
        DB[(Time Series Database)]
        APP[Web Dashboard and Mobile App]
    end

    S1 -->|Analog or I2C| MCU
    S2 -->|I2C| MCU
    S3 -->|GPIO ADC| MCU
    MCU -->|JSON over HTTPS POST| GW
    GW -->|Encrypted TCP| API
    API <-->|Read Write| DB
    APP -->|HTTPS GET| API
    API -->|201 Created 200 OK| APP
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — State Machine of an HTTP Connection

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Connecting : DNS resolved, TCP SYN sent
    Connecting --> TLSHandshake : TCP established, port 443
    TLSHandshake --> SendingRequest : TLS 1.3 1-RTT complete
    SendingRequest --> WaitingResponse : HTTP POST sent
    WaitingResponse --> Processing : 2xx received
    WaitingResponse --> Retrying : 5xx received
    Processing --> Idle : Keep-Alive, Connection reused
    Retrying --> SendingRequest : After backoff
    WaitingResponse --> Error : 4xx received, no retry
    Error --> [*]
    Processing --> [*] : Connection close
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to specify port numbers** (80 for HTTP, 443 for HTTPS) — examiners deduct 1 mark per omission.
> 2. **Confusing Safe and Idempotent.** `GET` is **both** safe AND idempotent; `POST` is **neither**. Writing "GET is idempotent" alone is incomplete.
> 3. **Writing "HTTP is a transport-layer protocol"** — it is an **application-layer** protocol. This error alone costs 2 marks.
> 4. **Not drawing the layered architecture** in `14-mark` questions — at least one **neat block diagram** is mandatory. A textual description alone is insufficient.
> 5. **Skipping CRLF (`\r\n`) discussion** in request/response message structure — RFC 7230 mandates this terminator and examiners test it.

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define HTTP. List any four HTTP request methods with one IoT-relevant example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol)** is an application-layer, request-response, stateless protocol operating over TCP (default port **80**) that defines how messages are formatted and transmitted between clients and servers on the World Wide Web. It is standardised by the **IETF** in **RFC 7230–7235** (HTTP/1.1) and **RFC 9110** (consolidated).
>
> Four HTTP request methods with IoT examples:
>
> 1. **GET** — Retrieve the current value of a temperature sensor: `GET /api/sensors/temperature` (1 mark)
> 2. **POST** — Upload a new telemetry reading from a smart energy meter: `POST /api/readings` with JSON body `{"kWh": 2.45}` (1 mark)
> 3. **PUT** — Update the firmware version of an IoT device: `PUT /api/devices/ESP32-01/firmware` (0.5 mark)
> 4. **DELETE** — Deregister a faulty sensor from the cloud: `DELETE /api/sensors/S123` (0.5 mark)
>
> *(Full marks = 3)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Differentiate between HTTP and HTTPS. State their default port numbers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | **HTTP** | **HTTPS** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Full Form | HyperText Transfer Protocol | HyperText Transfer Protocol **Secure** |
| Default Port | **80** (1 mark) | **443** (1 mark) |
| Encryption | None — plaintext (1 mark) | Encrypted using **TLS / SSL** |

> **Additional points for completeness:**
> - HTTPS requires an additional **TLS handshake** (1-RTT in TLS 1.3, 2-RTT in TLS 1.2) before any HTTP message can be exchanged, increasing latency by `~1 RTT`.
> - HTTPS is mandatory for **any IoT deployment handling PII, health, or financial data** to comply with regulations like **GDPR** and **HIPAA**.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) Explain the HTTP request-response architecture with a neat block diagram. Describe the structure of an HTTP request message and an HTTP response message.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Architecture (3 marks):**
HTTP follows a **client-server** model built atop TCP. The operational phases are:
1. **Connection Establishment:** TCP three-way handshake (`SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK`).
2. **Request Transmission:** Client sends an ASCII request.
3. **Server Processing:** Application logic executes (database access, computation).
4. **Response Transmission:** Server returns the requested resource or status.
5. **Connection Termination:** Either closed (HTTP/1.0 default) or kept alive (HTTP/1.1 default).

> **Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for a detailed Mermaid flowchart):**
> - Draw boxes for **Client**, **Network/Internet**, **Server** with arrows showing request and response flow.
> - Annotate each arrow with the corresponding packet type (SYN, HTTP request, HTTP response, FIN).
> - Label the **default port 80** at the server.

**HTTP Request Message Structure (2 marks):**
```
<REQUEST-LINE>          (Method SP URI SP HTTP-Version)
<HEADERS>               (Key: Value CRLF — multiple)
<EMPTY LINE>            (CRLF only)
<BODY>                  (Optional payload)
```
Example:
```
GET /api/sensors/temp HTTP/1.1\r\n
Host: api.iotcloud.com\r\n
Accept: application/json\r\n
Connection: keep-alive\r\n
\r\n
```

**HTTP Response Message Structure (2 marks):**
```
<STATUS-LINE>           (HTTP-Version SP Status-Code SP Reason-Phrase)
<HEADERS>               (Key: Value CRLF)
<EMPTY LINE>            (CRLF only)
<BODY>                  (Resource representation)
```
Example:
```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK\r\n
Content-Type: application/json\r\n
Content-Length: 47\r\n
\r\n
{"sensor":"DHT22","temperature":28.4,"unit":"C"}
```

---

**(b) Explain in detail the various HTTP methods used in IoT applications. State the difference between Safe and Idempotent methods with examples.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

HTTP defines **8 standard methods** (verbs) that indicate the desired action on a resource. In IoT, the most commonly used methods are:

| Method | Function in IoT | Example URI |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **GET** (1 mark) | Retrieve current sensor reading | `GET /sensors/temp/1` |
| **POST** (1 mark) | Submit new telemetry data to the cloud | `POST /sensors/temp` with JSON body |
| **PUT** (0.5 mark) | Replace device configuration fully | `PUT /devices/ESP32-01/config` |
| **PATCH** (0.5 mark) | Update a single field of actuator state | `PATCH /actuators/relay-3 {"state":"ON"}` |
| **DELETE** (0.5 mark) | Deregister a device or sensor | `DELETE /sensors/S999` |
| **HEAD** (0.5 mark) | Check if a device endpoint exists | `HEAD /sensors/temp/1` |
| **OPTIONS** (0.5 mark) | Discover allowed CORS methods | `OPTIONS /api/v1/*` |

**Safe vs. Idempotent (2 marks):**

> **Safe Method:** A method that is **intended to be read-only**; the server's state MUST NOT be altered as a side-effect. Examples: `GET`, `HEAD`, `OPTIONS`, `TRACE`. (1 mark)

> **Idempotent Method:** A method that produces the **same result** whether called once or multiple times. Examples: `GET`, `PUT`, `DELETE`, `HEAD`, `OPTIONS`, `TRACE`. (1 mark)

> **Critical Distinction:** `POST` and `PATCH` are **NEITHER safe NOR idempotent** — calling `POST` twice creates two duplicate resources, and calling `PATCH` twice may compound changes. `PUT` and `DELETE`, however, are **idempotent but NOT safe** because they modify server state.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) Compare HTTP and CoAP protocols. Explain why CoAP is preferred for resource-constrained IoT devices.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | **HTTP** | **CoAP** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Standard** | RFC 7230 / 9110 | RFC 7252 |
| **Transport** | TCP (reliable, connection-oriented) | UDP (lightweight, connectionless) |
| **Default Port** | 80 / 443 | 5683 (non-DTLS) / 5684 (DTLS) |
| **Header Size** | Variable, typically 500-800 bytes | Fixed 4-byte compact header |
| **Message Model** | Request-Response | Request-Response + Observe (pub-sub) |
| **Reliability** | Built into TCP | Application layer (CON messages + retransmission) |
| **Security** | TLS (HTTPS) | DTLS (Datagram TLS) |
| **Multicast Support** | Not supported | Supported natively |
| **Methods** | GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH, etc. | GET, POST, PUT, DELETE (mimics HTTP) |
| **Status Codes** | 3-digit (100-599) | 3-digit (mimics HTTP, 4.x.x format) |
| **Caching** | HTTP cache headers (ETag, Cache-Control) | Built-in freshness model |
| **Power Consumption** | Higher (TCP keep-alive overhead) | Lower (UDP, no keep-alive required) |
| **Suitability** | Resource-rich devices, cloud APIs | Resource-constrained motes (Class 0/1) |

**Why CoAP is Preferred for Constrained IoT (2 marks):**
1. **Lower header overhead** (4 bytes vs. ~500 bytes) drastically reduces bandwidth use, critical for low-power radios like LoRaWAN and IEEE 802.15.4.
2. **UDP-based transport** avoids TCP's three-way handshake, enabling faster transactions and lower power consumption on battery-operated sensors.
3. **Built-in Observe pattern** allows CoAP to function as a publish-subscribe protocol, ideal for real-time sensor streaming.
4. **Native multicast support** enables one-to-many firmware distribution to thousands of nodes simultaneously.

---

**(b) With examples, explain the five classes of HTTP status codes. Mention at least two specific status codes from each class that are relevant in IoT scenarios.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

HTTP status codes are **three-digit integers** emitted by the server in the response status-line. The **first digit** classifies the response category. The five classes are:

**1. 1xx — Informational (1 mark):**
- `100 Continue` — Server acknowledges request headers; client should send the body. (IoT use: large firmware uploads.)
- `101 Switching Protocols` — Used in WebSocket upgrade handshake. (IoT use: switching from HTTP to MQTT-over-WebSocket.)

**2. 2xx — Success (1.5 marks):**
- `200 OK` — Standard success for `GET` requests. (IoT use: retrieving sensor data successfully.)
- `201 Created` — New resource created via `POST`/`PUT`. (IoT use: confirming a new device registration in the cloud.)
- `204 No Content` — Success but no body returned. (IoT use: confirming `DELETE` of a deregistered sensor.)

**3. 3xx — Redirection (1 mark):**
- `301 Moved Permanently` — Resource URL has changed permanently. (IoT use: cloud server migration.)
- `304 Not Modified` — Client's cached copy is still valid; re-fetch not needed. (IoT use: firmware metadata caching.)

**4. 4xx — Client Error (1.5 marks):**
- `400 Bad Request` — Malformed JSON or invalid headers. (IoT use: sensor sends corrupted payload.)
- `401 Unauthorized` — Missing or invalid authentication token. (IoT use: device's API key expired.)
- `404 Not Found` — Requested resource does not exist. (IoT use: querying an unregistered sensor ID.)
- `429 Too Many Requests` — Rate-limit exceeded. (IoT use: flooding the cloud with high-frequency telemetry.)

**5. 5xx — Server Error (2 marks):**
- `500 Internal Server Error` — Generic server-side failure. (IoT use: backend database failure.)
- `502 Bad Gateway` — Upstream server returned invalid response. (IoT use: microservice chain failure in cloud.)
- `503 Service Unavailable` — Server overloaded or in maintenance. (IoT use: cloud provider's planned downtime.)
- `504 Gateway Timeout` — Upstream server did not respond in time. (IoT use: slow database query during peak load.)

> **Mnemonic for memorisation:** **"1-I, 2-S, 3-R, 4-C, 5-S"** = **I**nformational, **S**uccess, **R**edirection, **C**lient-error, **S**erver-error.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorise This Before Every KTU Exam:**

- ✅ HTTP is an **application-layer (Layer 7)** protocol, not transport-layer.
- ✅ Default ports: **80 (HTTP)** and **443 (HTTPS)** — these are the most-tested numbers.
- ✅ HTTP is **stateless**, **connectionless in semantics** (stateful only at TCP level), and **text-based**.
- ✅ Standard methods: `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `DELETE`, `PATCH`, `HEAD`, `OPTIONS`, `TRACE`.
- ✅ **GET is Safe and Idempotent. POST is neither. PUT/DELETE are Idempotent but not Safe.**
- ✅ HTTP status code classes — **1xx Info, 2xx Success, 3xx Redir, 4xx Client Err, 5xx Server Err**.
- ✅ HTTP runs over **TCP**; **HTTPS adds TLS** for encryption.
- ✅ **CRLF (`\r\n`)** is the line terminator mandated by RFC 7230.
- ✅ HTTP **header overhead is huge** (500-800 bytes typical) — CoAP is preferred for constrained devices.
- ✅ HTTP message format: **Request-line / Status-line → Headers → Empty line → Body**.
- ✅ REST (Representational State Transfer) has **6 architectural constraints** (statelessness, client-server, cacheability, uniform interface, layered system, code-on-demand).
- ✅ HTTPS requires an **additional TLS handshake** (1-RTT in TLS 1.3), increasing latency.
- ✅ The `Content-Type` header indicates payload format: `application/json`, `application/xml`, `text/plain`, etc.
- ✅ `Authorization: Bearer <JWT>` is the most common IoT authentication pattern for HTTP.
- ✅ **Time-to-First-Byte (TTFB)** = $T_{DNS} + T_{TCP} + T_{server-process}$ is a key performance metric.
- ✅ HTTP/2 introduced **multiplexing, header compression (HPACK), and server push** to overcome HTTP/1.1's head-of-line blocking.
- ✅ HTTP/3 uses **QUIC over UDP** for further latency reduction — relevant for next-gen IoT gateways.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MQTT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCGT503 - MICROPROCESSORS AND MICROCONTROLLERS FOR IOT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** MQTT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** is an ISO-standardized (ISO/IEC 20922), lightweight, application-layer messaging protocol designed specifically for **machine-to-machine (M2M) communication** and **Internet of Things (IoT)** environments. It operates over TCP/IP and follows the **Publish/Subscribe (Pub/Sub) messaging pattern**, decoupling the message producer (publisher) from the message consumer (subscriber) through a central entity known as the **Broker**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** MQTT is a core IoT communication protocol. In KTU 2024 Scheme examinations, students must clearly distinguish MQTT from HTTP/CoAP and explain its **Publish/Subscribe architecture**, **QoS levels**, and **control packet** structure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Original Design Context:** MQTT was invented in **1999** by Dr. Andy Stanford-Clark (IBM) and Arlen Nipper (Eurotech). It was originally created to monitor **oil pipelines** over satellite links, which is why its design heavily prioritizes **minimal bandwidth consumption** and **resilience on unreliable networks**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Newspaper Kiosk

To make MQTT intuitive, let us compare it to a **Newspaper Kiosk Subscription System**:

| Role in Analogy | MQTT Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Reader** (customer who wants sports news) | **Subscriber** | Expresses interest in a *topic* (e.g., "Sports/News") |
| **Journalist** (writer of news) | **Publisher** | Sends messages tagged with a *topic* |
| **Printing Press & Kiosk Manager** | **Broker** (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ) | Receives all published messages, filters them, and delivers only the relevant ones to interested subscribers |
| **Topic** (e.g., "Sports/Daily") | **Topic** (e.g., `home/sensor/temp`) | A UTF-8 string used for routing messages |

**Key Insight from the Analogy:** The journalist (publisher) **does not know** who the readers (subscribers) are. The kiosk manager (broker) handles distribution. This **decoupling** is the fundamental reason MQTT is so scalable for IoT.

### 1.3 Core Entities in MQTT

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of MQTT Architecture:**
> 1. **Publisher** — The IoT sensor/device that sends data.
> 2. **Subscriber** — The application/server that wants to receive data.
> 3. **Broker** — The central server (e.g., **Mosquitto**, **HiveMQ**, **EMQX**) that manages topic-based message routing.
> 4. **Topic** — A hierarchical string used for message classification, structured with forward slashes (e.g., `home/livingroom/temperature`).

### 1.4 Fundamental Properties

- **Port Number:** **TCP port 1883** (unencrypted), **TCP port 8883** (TLS/SSL encrypted)
- **Transport Protocol:** Runs over **TCP/IP** (reliable, connection-oriented)
- **Message Size:** Minimum overhead of just **2 bytes** for the fixed header
- **Data Agnostic:** Payload is binary-safe (can carry any data — text, JSON, sensor readings)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Publish/Subscribe Model — Operational Breakdown

Unlike the traditional **Request/Response** model (used in HTTP), MQTT uses a **Publish/Subscribe** model with these characteristics:

1. **Decoupling of Space:** Publisher and Subscriber do not need to know each other's IP address or port.
2. **Decoupling of Time:** They do not need to be active at the same time. The Broker can hold messages for offline subscribers (using the *Persistent Session* feature).
3. **Decoupling of Synchronization:** The Publisher does not block (wait) while the Subscriber processes the message — it merely "fires and forgets" the message to the Broker.

### 2.2 QoS (Quality of Service) Levels — The Heart of MQTT Reliability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Concept:** Understanding the **3 QoS levels** and the corresponding **handshake sequences** is a guaranteed question area in KTU ESE. The level is set **per-message** by the Publisher.

| QoS Level | Name | Guarantee | Handshake | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **QoS 0** | At most once | Best-effort delivery (no acknowledgment) | PUBLISH only | Sensor data where latest reading matters; lost packet is acceptable (e.g., temperature telemetry) |
| **QoS 1** | At least once | Possible duplicate delivery guaranteed arrival | PUBLISH → PUBACK | Commands where duplicates can be tolerated or filtered |
| **QoS 2** | Exactly once | No loss, no duplicates (4-step handshake) | PUBLISH → PUBREC → PUBREL → PUBCOMP | Critical financial transactions, medical alarms, billing data |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — MQTT Control Packet Types

> [!NOTE]
> The MQTT protocol uses **14 control packet types**, each identified by a 4-bit packet type code in the fixed header. These are examinable.

| Packet Type | Code (Hex) | Direction | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CONNECT** | `0x10` | Client → Broker | Initial connection request |
| **CONNACK** | `0x20` | Broker → Client | Connection acknowledgment |
| **PUBLISH** | `0x30` | Both ways | Carries the actual message payload |
| **PUBACK** | `0x40` | Both ways | QoS 1 acknowledgment |
| **PUBREC** | `0x50` | Both ways | QoS 2 — Publish received (step 1) |
| **PUBREL** | `0x60` | Both ways | QoS 2 — Publish release (step 2) |
| **PUBCOMP** | `0x70` | Both ways | QoS 2 — Publish complete (step 3) |
| **SUBSCRIBE** | `0x80` | Client → Broker | Subscribe to a topic |
| **SUBACK** | `0x90` | Broker → Client | Subscription acknowledgment |
| **UNSUBSCRIBE** | `0xA0` | Client → Broker | Unsubscribe request |
| **UNSUBACK** | `0xB0` | Broker → Client | Unsubscribe acknowledgment |
| **PINGREQ** | `0xC0` | Client → Broker | Keep-alive ping request |
| **PINGRESP** | `0xD0` | Broker → Client | Keep-alive ping response |
| **DISCONNECT** | `0xE0` | Client → Broker | Graceful disconnection |

### 2.4 MQTT Message Format (PUBLISH Packet Structure)

Every MQTT packet has three structural components:

1. **Fixed Header** (2 bytes minimum): Contains packet type, flags (DUP, QoS, RETAIN), and remaining length.
2. **Variable Header**: Contains the **Topic Name** and (for QoS > 0) the **Packet Identifier**.
3. **Payload**: The actual application data (can be empty; maximum 256 MB in MQTT v3.1.1, though in practice constrained by broker).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Important Flags in PUBLISH Packet:**
> - **DUP Flag:** Indicates the message is a duplicate (re-try attempt).
> - **QoS Level:** 0, 1, or 2.
> - **RETAIN Flag:** If set to `1`, the Broker stores the **last message** on a topic and immediately delivers it to any **new subscriber** joining that topic.

### 2.5 Wildcards in Topic Subscriptions

Subscribers can use two wildcard characters to subscribe to multiple topics simultaneously:

- **`+` (Single-level wildcard):** Matches **one** topic level.
  - Example: Subscribing to `home/+/temperature` matches `home/livingroom/temperature` and `home/bedroom/temperature`, but **not** `home/livingroom/humidity`.
- **`#` (Multi-level wildcard):** Matches **multiple** topic levels and must be the **last character** in the topic string.
  - Example: Subscribing to `home/#` matches **all** sub-topics under `home/`.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

MQTT is the **de-facto standard** for IoT messaging and is used in:
- **AWS IoT Core**, **Microsoft Azure IoT Hub**, **Google Cloud IoT** as the primary protocol.
- **Facebook Messenger** (used MQTT in its early days for chat).
- **Smart Home systems** (e.g., Home Assistant integration).
- **Industrial SCADA**, **automotive telemetry**, **remote patient monitoring**.

### 2.7 MQTT vs HTTP vs CoAP — Comparison Matrix (Frequently Asked in KTU)

| Parameter | **MQTT** | **HTTP** | **CoAP** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Architecture | Pub/Sub | Request/Response | Request/Response |
| Transport | TCP (port **1883**) | TCP (port 80) | UDP (port 5683) |
| Header Size | **2 bytes** (minimum) | Hundreds of bytes | 4 bytes |
| Power Efficiency | **High** | Low | Very High |
| Reliability | 3 QoS levels | TCP only | Confirmable/Non-confirmable messages |
| Best Suited For | Stable, low-bandwidth IoT | Web browsers, APIs | Constrained, lossy networks |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility of This Comparison:** In a KTU ESE 14-mark question, a comparison of MQTT with HTTP/CoAP is one of the most commonly asked sub-questions. Memorize the key distinctions: **MQTT uses Broker + Pub/Sub; HTTP uses Client/Server; CoAP uses UDP for lossy networks.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analysis & Analytical Matrices

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: The 4-Step QoS 2 Handshake (Exactly-Once Delivery)

The **QoS 2 handshake** is the most analytically complex part of MQTT. It uses a **4-way packet exchange** to guarantee exactly-once delivery without duplicates. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step analytical breakdown:

**Step 1 — Publisher sends PUBLISH (with QoS 2 flag set, DUP = 0):**
- The Publisher transmits a PUBLISH packet containing the message, a unique **Packet Identifier (Packet ID)**, and sets the QoS field to `2`.
- The Publisher starts a **retry timer** and waits for an acknowledgment.

**Step 2 — Broker responds with PUBREC (Publish Received):**
- The Broker receives the PUBLISH packet, stores the **Packet ID** internally, and sends back a **PUBREC** packet carrying the **same Packet ID**.
- The Broker has now **acknowledged the receipt** of the message.

**Step 3 — Publisher responds with PUBREL (Publish Release):**
- Upon receiving the PUBREC, the Publisher sends a **PUBREL** packet (also carrying the same Packet ID).
- This is the Publisher's confirmation that it has **released the original message** and will not retransmit it.
- *Important:* The original PUBLISH is **not yet discarded**; the Publisher keeps the message in a "pending release" state until PUBCOMP is received.

**Step 4 — Broker sends PUBCOMP (Publish Complete):**
- The Broker receives the PUBREL, **discards the stored Packet ID** (the message is now considered fully delivered), and sends a **PUBCOMP** packet.
- On receiving PUBCOMP, the Publisher also discards the original message. The 4-way handshake is now complete.

Let us represent this symbolically:

$$
\text{QoS 2 Handshake} = \text{PUBLISH}(P_{id}) \rightarrow \text{PUBREC}(P_{id}) \rightarrow \text{PUBREL}(P_{id}) \rightarrow \text{PUBCOMP}(P_{id})
$$

$$
\text{where } P_{id} \in \{1, 2, 3, ..., 65535\} \text{ (unique 16-bit packet identifier)}
$$

**Why 4 steps for "Exactly Once"?**
The 4-step mechanism prevents two failure scenarios:
1. If the **PUBLISH** packet is lost → Publisher retransmits it.
2. If the **PUBREC** is lost → Publisher retransmits PUBLISH; Broker re-sends PUBREC.
3. The **PUBREL/PUBCOMP** exchange ensures the Publisher does **not retransmit** the original PUBLISH after the Broker has already accepted it, thereby preventing **duplicate delivery**.

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Connection Establishment Flow

Below is the **exhaustive step-by-step sequence** for an MQTT client establishing communication with a Broker:

**Step 1 — TCP Connection:**
- The MQTT client opens a **TCP socket** to the Broker on port **1883** (or 8883 for TLS).
- The TCP **3-way handshake** (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK) completes at the transport layer.

**Step 2 — MQTT CONNECT Packet:**
- The client sends a **CONNECT** packet containing:
  - **Client ID** (a unique identifier, 1–23 characters in MQTT v3.1.1).
  - **Clean Session** flag (0 = persistent, 1 = new).
  - **Keep Alive** timer value (in seconds).
  - **Username/Password** (optional, depending on security configuration).
  - **Will Message** (optional — a "last will" message that the Broker sends on behalf of the client if it disconnects abruptly).

**Step 3 — Broker Sends CONNACK:**
- The Broker responds with a **CONNACK** packet containing:
  - **Session Present** flag.
  - **Return Code** (0 = Connection Accepted, 1–5 = various errors like "invalid protocol version", "authentication failed", "server unavailable", etc.).

**Step 4 — Topic Subscription:**
- The client sends a **SUBSCRIBE** packet specifying one or more **topic filters** (with their desired QoS).
- The Broker responds with a **SUBACK** confirming each subscription.

**Step 5 — Message Exchange:**
- Either party can send **PUBLISH** packets.
- The Broker routes the message to all matching subscribers based on topic matching logic.

**Step 6 — Heartbeat (Keep-Alive):**
- If no messages are exchanged within the **Keep Alive** interval, the client sends a **PINGREQ** packet.
- The Broker replies with **PINGRESP**. This confirms both ends are still connected.

**Step 7 — Disconnect:**
- The client sends a **DISCONNECT** packet for a clean shutdown, or simply closes the TCP connection.

Let us model this flow as a sequence:

$$
\text{Client} \xrightarrow[\text{port 1883}]{\text{TCP SYN}} \text{Broker}
$$

$$
\text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{CONNECT}} \text{Broker} \xrightarrow{\text{CONNACK}} \text{Client}
$$

$$
\text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{SUBSCRIBE(topic, QoS)}} \text{Broker} \xrightarrow{\text{SUBACK}} \text{Client}
$$

$$
\text{Client} \xleftrightarrow{\text{PUBLISH / PUBACK / PUBREC / PUBREL / PUBCOMP}} \text{Broker}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Computation: MQTT Overhead vs HTTP

A common KTU analytical question is: *"Why is MQTT more efficient than HTTP for IoT?"* The answer lies in **header overhead calculation**.

**MQTT Fixed Header (Minimum):**

$$
\text{Fixed Header Size} = 1 \text{ byte (type+flags)} + 1 \text{ byte (remaining length)} = 2 \text{ bytes}
$$

**HTTP Header (Typical):**

$$
\text{HTTP Header Size} \approx 200 \text{ to } 2000 \text{ bytes (typical for GET/POST with headers, cookies, etc.)}
$$

**Ratio of Overhead:**

$$
\frac{\text{HTTP Overhead}}{\text{MQTT Overhead}} = \frac{200 \text{ bytes}}{2 \text{ bytes}} = 100
$$

**Conclusion:** MQTT has approximately **100 times less header overhead** than a typical HTTP request. This is a primary reason MQTT is preferred for low-power, bandwidth-constrained IoT devices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** When transmitting a **2-byte sensor reading** (e.g., a temperature value `25°C`), an HTTP packet carries **~200 bytes of header overhead per 2 bytes of payload** — an efficiency of less than 1%. MQTT, with only 2 bytes of overhead, achieves **50% efficiency**. This directly translates to **longer battery life** for IoT nodes.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Topic Matching Logic

| Subscriber's Topic Filter | Published Topic | Match? | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| `home/sensor/temp` | `home/sensor/temp` | ✅ Yes | Exact match |
| `home/+/temp` | `home/sensor/temp` | ✅ Yes | `+` matches one level (`sensor`) |
| `home/+/temp` | `home/sensor/humidity` | ❌ No | Final level is `humidity`, not `temp` |
| `home/#` | `home/sensor/temp` | ✅ Yes | `#` matches multiple levels |
| `home/#` | `home/sensor/room1/temp` | ✅ Yes | `#` matches all sub-levels |
| `home/sensor/+` | `home/sensor/room1/temp` | ❌ No | `+` matches only one level, not multiple |
| `+/sensor/#` | `building1/sensor/temp` | ✅ Yes | `+` matches `building1`, `#` matches `temp` |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: MQTT Publish/Subscribe Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PUB["Publisher Devices"]
        s1["IoT Sensor A"]
        s2["Smartphone App"]
        s3["Industrial PLC"]
    end

    subgraph BRK["MQTT Broker (Central Server)"]
        b1["Mosquitto"]
        b2["HiveMQ"]
        b3["EMQX"]
    end

    subgraph SUB["Subscribers"]
        app1["Mobile Dashboard"]
        app2["Cloud Database"]
        app3["Alarm System"]
    end

    s1 -- "PUBLISH topic: home/temp QoS 1" --> b1
    s2 -- "PUBLISH topic: chat/msg QoS 0" --> b1
    s3 -- "PUBLISH topic: factory/motor QoS 2" --> b1
    s1 -- "PUBLISH topic: home/temp QoS 1" --> b2
    s3 -- "PUBLISH topic: factory/motor QoS 2" --> b2
    s2 -- "PUBLISH topic: chat/msg QoS 0" --> b3

    b1 -- "Filter topic home/temp" --> app1
    b1 -- "Filter topic factory/motor" --> app3
    b2 -- "Filter topic home/temp" --> app1
    b2 -- "Filter topic chat/msg" --> app2
    b3 -- "Filter topic chat/msg" --> app2
    b3 -- "Filter topic factory/motor" --> app3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Note how the **Publisher devices (s1, s2, s3)** send messages to **different Brokers (b1, b2, b3)**, but each Broker then routes the message to **multiple Subscribers** based on topic filtering. This is the **decoupled** nature of MQTT in action.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: QoS Handshake Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant P as Publisher
    participant B as MQTT Broker
    participant S as Subscriber

    Note over P,S: TCP Connection Established (Port 1883)

    P->>B: CONNECT (ClientID, KeepAlive)
    B-->>P: CONNACK (Return Code = 0)

    S->>B: SUBSCRIBE (topic: home/temp, QoS = 2)
    B-->>S: SUBACK (Granted QoS = 2)

    rect rgb(230, 245, 255)
        Note over P,B: QoS 0 Handshake
        P->>B: PUBLISH (topic: home/temp, QoS 0)
        B->>S: PUBLISH (topic: home/temp, QoS 0)
        Note right of B: No ACK sent for QoS 0
    end

    rect rgb(255, 245, 230)
        Note over P,B: QoS 1 Handshake
        P->>B: PUBLISH (PacketID 100, QoS 1)
        B-->>P: PUBACK (PacketID 100)
    end

    rect rgb(230, 255, 230)
        Note over P,B: QoS 2 Handshake (4 Steps)
        P->>B: PUBLISH (PacketID 200, QoS 2)
        B-->>P: PUBREC (PacketID 200)
        P->>B: PUBREL (PacketID 200)
        B-->>P: PUBCOMP (PacketID 200)
    end

    P->>B: DISCONNECT
    Note over P,B: Clean Disconnect
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to Read This Diagram:** Each `rect rgb(...)` block isolates a different QoS handshake. The **arrows pointing right** (`->>`) are messages from the Publisher to the Broker, and **arrows pointing left** (`-->>`) are acknowledgments. Observe how **QoS 0 has only 1 packet**, **QoS 1 has 2 packets**, and **QoS 2 has 4 packets** in the handshake exchange.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Block: MQTT Topic Hierarchy & Wildcard Matching

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root["ROOT (any string)"]

    root --> building1["building1"]
    root --> building2["building2"]

    building1 --> b1floor["floor1"]
    building1 --> b1sensor["sensor"]

    b1floor --> b1f1temp["temp (QoS 0)"]
    b1floor --> b1f1hum["humidity (QoS 0)"]

    b1sensor --> b1s1["temperature (QoS 1)"]
    b1sensor --> b1s2["pressure (QoS 2)"]

    building2 --> b2floor["floor2"]
    building2 --> b2sensor["sensor"]

    b2floor --> b2f2temp["temp"]
    b2sensor --> b2s1["temperature"]

    filter1["Filter: building1/+/temp"]
    filter2["Filter: building1/sensor/#"]
    filter3["Filter: +/sensor/+"]

    filter1 -.->|matches| b1f1temp
    filter1 -.->|matches| b2f2temp
    filter1 -.->|no match| b1s1
    filter2 -.->|matches| b1s1
    filter2 -.->|matches| b1s2
    filter3 -.->|matches| b1s1
    filter3 -.->|matches| b1s2
    filter3 -.->|matches| b2s1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This tree shows a realistic **smart building MQTT topic hierarchy**. The **dashed lines** illustrate which published topics match each subscriber's filter, demonstrating how the **`+` and `#` wildcards** enable a single subscription to receive data from many sensors.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define MQTT. List any **three** features of MQTT that make it suitable for IoT applications. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** is a lightweight, application-layer messaging protocol standardized as **ISO/IEC 20922**. It uses a **Publish/Subscribe architecture** over TCP/IP, designed for constrained IoT devices and unreliable networks.

Three features making it suitable for IoT:
1. **Low Overhead:** Minimum header size of just **2 bytes**, ideal for low-bandwidth networks.
2. **Publish/Subscribe Decoupling:** Publishers and Subscribers are decoupled in time, space, and synchronization, enabling massive scalability.
3. **Three QoS Levels:** Offers **At most once (0)**, **At least once (1)**, and **Exactly once (2)** delivery guarantees for application-defined reliability.

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between **QoS 0**, **QoS 1**, and **QoS 2** in MQTT with respect to the number of control packets exchanged. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| QoS Level | Guarantee | Packets Exchanged | Total Packets |
|---|---|---|---|
| **QoS 0** | At most once (no ACK) | PUBLISH only | **1 packet** |
| **QoS 1** | At least once (may duplicate) | PUBLISH + PUBACK | **2 packets** |
| **QoS 2** | Exactly once (no duplicate) | PUBLISH + PUBREC + PUBREL + PUBCOMP | **4 packets** |

QoS 2 uses the most packets because it requires a **4-way handshake** to ensure that the message is delivered exactly once without duplicates.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice Pattern

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A]**
> **Q (A).** *(a)* Explain the **MQTT architecture** with a neat diagram. Describe the role of **Broker**, **Publisher**, **Subscriber**, and **Topic**. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* With neat sequence diagrams, explain the **QoS 1** and **QoS 2** message delivery mechanisms in MQTT. *(7 marks)*

#### Solution to Q(A)(a) — MQTT Architecture (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
MQTT is a lightweight, broker-based Publish/Subscribe messaging protocol standardized as **ISO/IEC 20922**, designed for low-bandwidth, high-latency, or unreliable IoT networks.

**Architecture Components (4 marks):**

1. **Publisher:** The IoT device or application that **produces** data and sends it to the Broker in the form of messages tagged with a **topic** (e.g., `sensor/temperature`). The publisher does not know who the subscribers are.

2. **Subscriber:** The application or device that **consumes** data. It registers its interest in one or more topics with the Broker using a **SUBSCRIBE** packet. Subscribers can use wildcards (`+` and `#`) to subscribe to multiple topics.

3. **Broker:** The **central server** (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ, EMQX) that:
   - Accepts connections from all clients.
   - Receives **PUBLISH** messages from publishers.
   - **Filters** messages by topic.
   - **Forwards** the message to all matching subscribers.
   - Manages **subscriptions**, **persistent sessions**, and **retained messages**.

4. **Topic:** A **hierarchical UTF-8 string** used for routing, structured with `/` as a delimiter (e.g., `home/livingroom/temperature`).

**Diagram (2 marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the architectural block diagram.)*

---

#### Solution to Q(A)(b) — QoS 1 and QoS 2 Delivery (7 marks)

**QoS 1 — At Least Once Delivery (3 marks):**

$$
\text{Flow: } \text{Publisher} \xrightarrow{\text{PUBLISH (PacketID)}} \text{Broker} \xrightarrow{\text{PUBACK (PacketID)}} \text{Publisher}
$$

- Publisher sends PUBLISH with a unique **Packet Identifier** and starts a **retry timer**.
- If the Broker does not respond with **PUBACK** within the retry interval, the Publisher **retransmits** the PUBLISH (with the **DUP flag** set to `1`).
- The same message may be delivered **multiple times** if the PUBACK is lost.

**QoS 2 — Exactly Once Delivery (4 marks):**

$$
\text{Flow: } \text{PUBLISH} \rightarrow \text{PUBREC} \rightarrow \text{PUBREL} \rightarrow \text{PUBCOMP}
$$

- **Step 1:** Publisher → Broker: `PUBLISH (PacketID, QoS=2)` (starts retry timer).
- **Step 2:** Broker → Publisher: `PUBREC (PacketID)` (Broker stores the PacketID; message is now "in flight").
- **Step 3:** Publisher → Broker: `PUBREL (PacketID)` (Publisher confirms it will not retransmit; discards the original message only after PUBCOMP).
- **Step 4:** Broker → Publisher: `PUBCOMP (PacketID)` (Broker discards the stored PacketID; transaction is now closed).

This **4-way handshake** ensures that the message is delivered **exactly once**, regardless of network failures.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question B]**
> **Q (B).** *(a)* List and explain the **MQTT control packet types** with their direction of flow. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* Compare **MQTT** with **HTTP** and **CoAP** in terms of architecture, transport protocol, header size, and reliability. *(7 marks)*

#### Solution to Q(B)(a) — MQTT Control Packet Types (7 marks)

| Packet Type | Direction | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **CONNECT** | Client → Broker | Initiates a connection. Carries ClientID, Keep Alive, Clean Session flag, optional username/password. |
| **CONNACK** | Broker → Client | Acknowledges connection. Carries a Return Code (0 = success). |
| **PUBLISH** | Both ways | Carries the actual application message and topic name. |
| **PUBACK** | Both ways | Acknowledges a QoS 1 message receipt. |
| **PUBREC** | Both ways | First acknowledgment in QoS 2 (Publish Received). |
| **PUBREL** | Both ways | Second step in QoS 2 (Publish Release). |
| **PUBCOMP** | Both ways | Final acknowledgment in QoS 2 (Publish Complete). |
| **SUBSCRIBE** | Client → Broker | Subscribes to one or more topics with a specified QoS. |
| **SUBACK** | Broker → Client | Acknowledges the subscription and confirms the granted QoS. |
| **UNSUBSCRIBE** | Client → Broker | Unsubscribes from one or more topics. |
| **UNSUBACK** | Broker → Client | Acknowledges the unsubscription. |
| **PINGREQ** | Client → Broker | Heartbeat — keeps the connection alive. |
| **PINGRESP** | Broker → Client | Heartbeat acknowledgment. |
| **DISCONNECT** | Client → Broker | Cleanly closes the MQTT connection. |

---

#### Solution to Q(B)(b) — MQTT vs HTTP vs CoAP (7 marks)

| Parameter | **MQTT** | **HTTP** | **CoAP** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | Publish/Subscribe (Broker-based) | Request/Response (Client/Server) | Request/Response |
| **Transport** | TCP (port **1883**) | TCP (port 80) | UDP (port 5683) |
| **Header Size** | **2 bytes** (minimum fixed header) | ~200–2000 bytes | **4 bytes** |
| **Reliability** | 3 QoS levels (0, 1, 2) | TCP-based reliability only | Confirmable/Non-confirmable messages |
| **Power Efficiency** | High (low overhead) | Low (verbose headers) | Very High (UDP, minimal handshake) |
| **Best Suited For** | Stable, persistent IoT connections (e.g., home automation) | Web applications, APIs | Constrained, lossy networks (e.g., sensor motes) |
| **Message Pattern** | Many-to-Many via Broker | One-to-One (Client initiates) | One-to-One |

**Conclusion:** MQTT excels in **stable, broker-mediated IoT deployments** with multiple publishers and subscribers. HTTP is **verbose and not optimized for IoT**. CoAP is best for **ultra-low-power, lossy networks** where UDP is acceptable.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Confusing Publish/Subscribe with Request/Response:** Students often describe MQTT as a "client-server" model. This is **wrong**. Always use the term "**Broker-based Publish/Subscribe**".
> 2. **Mixing up QoS 1 and QoS 2 packet counts:** A common mistake is stating QoS 1 is "2 packets" but then forgetting that **QoS 2 requires 4 packets** (PUBLISH, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP — *not* 3).
> 3. **Forgetting the Port Number:** Examiners frequently award 1 mark for correctly stating **TCP port 1883** (unencrypted MQTT) and **8883** (TLS). Do not miss this.
> 4. **Not mentioning Wildcards:** When asked about topics, students forget to describe the **`+` and `#` wildcards**. This is a free 1–2 marks in KTU exams.
> 5. **Conflating MQTT-SN with MQTT:** MQTT-SN (Sensor Networks) is a variant for non-TCP networks. If not asked, do not mix the two.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for MQTT:**

- **Full Form:** Message Queuing Telemetry Transport (Standardized as **ISO/IEC 20922**).
- **Architecture:** **Publish/Subscribe** with a central **Broker** (decoupled in space, time, and synchronization).
- **Transport:** **TCP** (port **1883** unencrypted, **8883** encrypted with TLS).
- **Key Entities:** Publisher, Subscriber, Broker, Topic.
- **Header Size:** Minimum **2 bytes** (fixed header).
- **Three QoS Levels:**
  - **QoS 0** → "At most once" → 1 packet.
  - **QoS 1** → "At least once" → 2 packets (PUBLISH + PUBACK).
  - **QoS 2** → "Exactly once" → 4 packets (PUBLISH + PUBREC + PUBREL + PUBCOMP).
- **Important Flags in PUBLISH:** **DUP**, **QoS**, **RETAIN**.
- **14 Control Packet Types:** CONNECT, CONNACK, PUBLISH, PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP, SUBSCRIBE, SUBACK, UNSUBSCRIBE, UNSUBACK, PINGREQ, PINGRESP, DISCONNECT.
- **Wildcards:**
  - `+` → **Single-level** wildcard (matches one topic level).
  - `#` → **Multi-level** wildcard (must be the **last** character).
- **Keep-Alive Mechanism:** PINGREQ / PINGRESP heartbeat packets.
- **Will Message:** A "last will" sent by the Broker on behalf of a client that disconnects abruptly.
- **Persistent Session:** Allows the Broker to store undelivered QoS 1/2 messages for offline subscribers.
- **Comparison Anchors (for KTU):**
  - MQTT vs HTTP: MQTT is **Pub/Sub + low overhead**; HTTP is **Request/Response + verbose**.
  - MQTT vs CoAP: MQTT uses **TCP + Broker**; CoAP uses **UDP + simple request/response**.
- **Practical Use:** AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT, Facebook Messenger (legacy), Industrial SCADA, Smart Homes.
- **Memory Trick:** *"MQTT = 2 byte header, 14 packets, 3 QoS, 1 broker, 0 hassle."*
